topics.title: Fixed-Width Integer Data Types in C99
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixed-Width Integer Data Types in C99

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **C99** standard of the C programming language, the header file **`<stdint.h>`** defines a set of **Fixed-Width Integer Data Types**. These are typedef-name aliases that specify an integer type occupying *exactly* a predetermined number of bits (8, 16, 32, or 64) regardless of the underlying processor architecture or compiler implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For ARM Cortex-M embedded programming, these types are *non-negotiable*. Hardware register maps in ARM devices demand exact bit-widths, and `<stdint.h>` provides the *only* portable guarantee that `int32_t` is precisely 32 bits on every conformant C99 platform.

The families defined in `<stdint.h>` are:

1. **Exact-width integer types** — `int8_t`, `int16_t`, `int32_t`, `int64_t` and their `u` (unsigned) counterparts.
2. **Minimum-width integer types** — `int_leastN_t`, `uint_leastN_t`.
3. **Fastest minimum-width integer types** — `int_fastN_t`, `uint_fastN_t`.
4. **Integer types capable of holding object pointers** — `intptr_t`, `uintptr_t`.
5. **Greatest-width integer types** — `intmax_t`, `uintmax_t`.
6. **Limit macros** — `INTN_MIN`, `INTN_MAX`, `UINTN_MAX`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine shipping a **palletized box of screws**. If you simply label the box "a few screws," the receiver cannot be sure whether they got 10 or 1,000. The **Fixed-Width Integer Types** are like a shipping invoice that *explicitly declares* "exactly 1,000 screws, each 3 mm." The receiver (the compiler and the ARM hardware) knows **precisely** how many bytes of memory to reserve and how the bits will be laid out — no guesswork.

### 1.3 Why Was C99 Forced to Add This?

Before C99, the C language only guaranteed that:

- `short int` ≥ 16 bits
- `int` ≥ 16 bits
- `long int` ≥ 32 bits
- `long long int` ≥ 64 bits

But **no exact bit-width was guaranteed**. On some obscure platforms `int` was 16-bit, on others 32-bit, on rare DSPs even 24-bit. Embedded engineers and kernel developers revolted, and C99 introduced the `<stdint.h>` header.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for ARM Cortex-M:** On virtually every modern ARM toolchain (ARM CC, GCC-arm-none-eabi, IAR), `sizeof(int32_t) == 4` bytes and `sizeof(int8_t) == 1` byte — but `<stdint.h>` is what makes this *legally portable*, not just empirically true on ARM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Families Explained

#### Family 1 — Exact-Width Types

- **Signed:** `int8_t`, `int16_t`, `int32_t`, `int64_t`
- **Unsigned:** `uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`, `uint64_t`
- These are provided **only if the implementation has an integer type of exactly N bits using two's complement representation (no padding bits, no trap representations).**
- On ARM, all 8/16/32-bit exact types exist. `int64_t` also exists on Cortex-A (64-bit capable).

#### Family 2 — Minimum-Width Types (`int_leastN_t`)

- Guarantees **at least N bits**, but the compiler may use a larger type if it is faster.
- Example: `int_least8_t` may internally resolve to `int` (32 bits) on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M.
- Useful when **minimizing memory footprint** is critical.

#### Family 3 — Fastest Minimum-Width Types (`int_fastN_t`)

- Guarantees the **fastest type** with at least N bits.
- `int_fast8_t` is almost always `int` on a 32-bit platform because operating on 32-bit registers is faster than masking 8-bit values.

#### Family 4 — Pointer-Holding Integer Types

- `intptr_t` and `uintptr_t` are guaranteed to be wide enough to hold a **void pointer** without loss.
- On ARM Cortex-M (32-bit), `uintptr_t` is typically `unsigned int` (32 bits).
- Crucial for **casting addresses to integers** (e.g., device register access in memory-mapped I/O).

#### Family 5 — Greatest-Width Types

- `intmax_t` and `uintmax_t` are the widest signed/unsigned integer types supported.
- On ARM, this is `long long int` (typically 64 bits).

### 2.2 Limit Macros (from `<stdint.h>`)

For each type, C99 provides macros defined using `#define`. The `printf`/`scanf` format specifiers require matching macros like `PRId32` (from `<inttypes.h>`) for portability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| **Category** | **Type Name** | **Width (bits)** | **Range (Signed)** | **Range (Unsigned)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exact | int8_t / uint8_t | 8 | $-2^{7}$ to $2^{7}-1$ | $0$ to $2^{8}-1$ |
| Exact | int16_t / uint16_t | 16 | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ | $0$ to $2^{16}-1$ |
| Exact | int32_t / uint32_t | 32 | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | $0$ to $2^{32}-1$ |
| Exact | int64_t / uint64_t | 64 | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | $0$ to $2^{64}-1$ |
| Pointer | intptr_t / uintptr_t | Arch-dependent | Holds void * | Holds void * |
| Max | intmax_t / uintmax_t | ≥ 64 | Platform max | Platform max |

> [!NOTE]
> **Range Formula:** For an unsigned N-bit type, range is $\left[0,\ 2^{N}-1\right]$. For a signed two's-complement N-bit type, range is $\left[-2^{N-1},\ 2^{N-1}-1\right]$.

### 2.4 Why ARM Programmers Cannot Live Without This

- **Memory-Mapped I/O** in ARM Cortex-M requires exact 32-bit register reads/writes. A `uint32_t *` cast on a peripheral address is only guaranteed safe because of `<stdint.h>`.
- **CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard)** headers from ARM use `uint32_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint8_t` *exclusively* for register definitions.
- **Cross-compiler portability**: Code using `<stdint.h>` compiles identically on GCC, ARM Compiler 6, IAR, and Keil.
- **RTOS kernels** (FreeRTOS, Zephyr) expose task stack sizes as `UBaseType_t` (typedef of `unsigned long`) — but internally use fixed-width types for atomic flags.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Range for `int32_t` / `uint32_t`

The C99 standard mandates that fixed-width types use **two's complement** encoding (no sign bit, no padding, no trap representations). We will derive the full mathematical range for the most exam-relevant case — `int32_t` and `uint32_t`.

**Step 1 — Total Bit Combinations**

For an N-bit type, the number of unique bit patterns is:

$$N_{\text{patterns}} = 2^{N}$$

For N = 32:

$$N_{\text{patterns}} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$$

**Step 2 — Unsigned Range (`uint32_t`)**

Each bit pattern represents a unique non-negative integer from $0$ to $2^{N}-1$.

$$\text{Range}(\texttt{uint32\_t}) = \left[0,\ 2^{32}-1\right] = \left[0,\ 4{,}294{,}967{,}295\right]$$

**Step 3 — Signed Two's Complement Range (`int32_t`)**

Two's complement splits the $2^{N}$ patterns into two halves:

- The **most significant bit (MSB)** is the sign bit (1 = negative).
- The remaining $N-1$ bits encode magnitude.

The number of negative patterns is:

$$N_{\text{negative}} = 2^{N-1} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

The number of non-negative patterns (including zero) is:

$$N_{\text{nonneg}} = 2^{N-1} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

**Step 4 — Negative Magnitude Mapping**

In two's complement, the most negative value is $-2^{N-1}$ (bit pattern `0x80000000`). The most positive value is $2^{N-1}-1$ (bit pattern `0x7FFFFFFF`).

The one extra negative value exists because zero occupies the "positive zero" slot, leaving an asymmetric count. Therefore:

$$\text{Range}(\texttt{int32\_t}) = \left[-2^{31},\ 2^{31}-1\right] = \left[-2{,}147{,}483{,}648,\ +2{,}147{,}483{,}647\right]$$

**Step 5 — Asymmetry Proof**

The total count of representable values:

$$\left(2^{31} - (-2^{31})\right) + 1 = 2^{32} \ \checkmark$$

This matches Step 1, confirming the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation of Overflow Behaviour (KTU Favourite)

When a `uint32_t` exceeds $2^{32}-1$, C99 mandates **modular wrap-around** (defined behaviour, not undefined).

$$\texttt{x} = \texttt{UINT32\_MAX} + 1$$

$$= (2^{32}-1) + 1$$

$$= 2^{32}$$

$$\equiv 0 \pmod{2^{32}}$$

The resulting value of `x` is **0**, not garbage.

For signed overflow, the result remains implementation-defined in C99 (became undefined in C++ but C99 left it implementation-defined). On ARM, it wraps modulo $2^{32}$ with two's complement, matching the hardware.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Family

| **Engineering Scenario** | **Recommended Type** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| GPIO pin read on STM32 | `uint32_t` | 32-bit port register, need exact width. |
| ADC 12-bit sample storage | `uint16_t` | 12-bit fits in 16 bits, no waste. |
| RTOS tick counter | `uint_fast32_t` | Speed > memory; compiler picks register-optimal. |
| Memory-constrained sensor array | `int_least8_t` | Compiler may use `int` if faster; minimum 8 bits guaranteed. |
| Casting `(void *)` to integer | `uintptr_t` | Only type that round-trips a pointer losslessly. |
| Largest guaranteed integer | `intmax_t` | For printf-family portability with `%jd`. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — `<stdint.h>` Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["stdint.h C99 Header"]
    fam1["Exact-Width Family"]
    fam2["Minimum-Width Family"]
    fam3["Fastest Minimum-Width Family"]
    fam4["Pointer-Holding Family"]
    fam5["Greatest-Width Family"]
    lim["Limit Macros INTN_MIN INTN_MAX UINTN_MAX"]
    fmt["inttypes.h Format Strings PRId32 PRIu64 etc"]

    root --> fam1
    root --> fam2
    root --> fam3
    root --> fam4
    root --> fam5
    root --> lim

    fam1 --> s8["int8_t  uint8_t"]
    fam1 --> s16["int16_t  uint16_t"]
    fam1 --> s32["int32_t  uint32_t"]
    fam1 --> s64["int64_t  uint64_t"]

    fam2 --> m8["int_least8_t  uint_least8_t"]
    fam2 --> m16["int_least16_t  uint_least16_t"]
    fam2 --> m32["int_least32_t  uint_least32_t"]
    fam2 --> m64["int_least64_t  uint_least64_t"]

    fam3 --> f8["int_fast8_t  uint_fast8_t"]
    fam3 --> f16["int_fast16_t  uint_fast16_t"]
    fam3 --> f32["int_fast32_t  uint_fast32_t"]
    fam3 --> f64["int_fast64_t  uint_fast64_t"]

    fam4 --> ptr["intptr_t  uintptr_t"]
    fam5 --> mx["intmax_t  uintmax_t"]

    root -.companion.-> fmt
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Fixed-Width Types in ARM Cortex-M Register Access

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["C Source Code"]
    B["Include stdint.h"]
    C["Declare uint32_t regPtr"]
    D["Cast to volatile uint32 t pointer"]
    E["ARM Cortex M Peripheral Register 32 bits"]
    F["Memory Mapped I/O Address 0x40000000"]
    G["Hardware Read Write Bus AHB APB"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -.result.-> H["Predictable Hardware Behavior"]
```

### 4.3 Interpretation

- The first diagram maps the **complete taxonomy** of C99 `<stdint.h>` types — a frequent 14-mark exam question.
- The second diagram shows the **engineering flow** of how a `uint32_t` ends up driving ARM hardware — illustrating *why* exact width matters in memory-mapped I/O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When a question says "explain the role of `<stdint.h>` in ARM programming," your answer must mention: (1) exact bit-width guarantee, (2) portability across ARM toolchains (GCC, ARM CC, IAR), and (3) compatibility with CMSIS register definitions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define the C99 fixed-width integer types. Why were they introduced, and which header file provides them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
C99 introduced **Fixed-Width Integer Types** in the header file **`<stdint.h>`** to guarantee that variables occupy *exactly* a specified number of bits (8, 16, 32, or 64) regardless of the compiler or platform. The exact-width family includes `int8_t`, `int16_t`, `int32_t`, `int64_t` and their unsigned `uint*_t` counterparts. They were introduced because pre-C99 types like `int` and `long` had only minimum-width guarantees (e.g., `int` ≥ 16 bits), which caused portability failures in low-level systems programming, networking, and embedded firmware where exact bit-layouts are mandatory. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between `int_least32_t` and `int_fast32_t`. In which ARM programming scenario would each be preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `int_least32_t` | `int_fast32_t` |
|---|---|---|
| **Guarantee** | At least 32 bits | At least 32 bits, but compiler picks the **fastest** underlying type |
| **Memory** | Minimized | May use a wider type (e.g., 64-bit) if faster |
| **Use case** | Sensor arrays with thousands of samples where memory is critical | RTOS tick counters, inner loops where speed dominates |

For ARM Cortex-M register access, both work, but `uint_fast8_t` is often used in tight polling loops for faster bit-mask operations on the natural 32-bit ARM register width. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the five families of integer types defined in the C99 `<stdint.h>` header. For each family, give one example use-case in ARM Cortex-M programming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the full numerical range of `int32_t` and `uint32_t`. Show every algebraic step and explain the asymmetry between the negative and positive extremes in two's complement representation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Exact-width family** — `int8_t`, `int16_t`, `int32_t`, `int64_t`, `uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`, `uint64_t`. These provide a guarantee of *exactly* N bits. **ARM use case:** Declaring `volatile uint32_t *GPIOA_ODR = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014;` to write a 32-bit output data register on an STM32. **(2 marks)**

2. **Minimum-width family** — `int_leastN_t`, `uint_leastN_t`. At least N bits, no more. **ARM use case:** Storing 12-bit ADC samples in `uint_least16_t` to save RAM in a 10,000-sample buffer. **(1 mark)**

3. **Fastest minimum-width family** — `int_fastN_t`, `uint_fastN_t`. **ARM use case:** RTOS tick counters using `uint_fast32_t` so the compiler may map it to the native 32-bit ARM register for fastest increment. **(1 mark)**

4. **Pointer-holding family** — `intptr_t`, `uintptr_t`. **ARM use case:** Converting a function pointer or address `0x08000000` (Flash base) to `uintptr_t` for storage in a non-volatile backup register. **(1 mark)**

5. **Greatest-width family** — `intmax_t`, `uintmax_t`. **ARM use case:** Performing intermediate 64-bit multiplications of two 32-bit sensor values to avoid overflow before scaling. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Step 1: An N-bit type has $2^{N}$ unique bit patterns. For N = 32: **(1 mark)**

$$2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$$

Step 2: For `uint32_t`, every pattern maps to a non-negative integer: **(1 mark)**

$$0 \le \texttt{uint32\_t} \le 2^{32}-1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295$$

Step 3: For two's complement `int32_t`, the MSB is the sign bit. Splitting $2^{32}$ patterns: **(1 mark)**

$$\text{Non-negative patterns} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

$$\text{Negative patterns} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

Step 4: The non-negative range covers $[0, 2^{31}-1]$ because one slot is occupied by zero. The negative range covers $[-2^{31}, -1]$ because two's complement represents the most negative value as $-2^{N-1}$ (bit pattern `0x80000000`). **(2 marks)**

Step 5: Combining: **(1 mark)**

$$\text{Range}(\texttt{int32\_t}) = \left[-2^{31},\ 2^{31}-1\right] = \left[-2{,}147{,}483{,}648,\ +2{,}147{,}483{,}647\right]$$

Step 6: The **asymmetry** exists because zero consumes one slot from the positive side, leaving one extra negative slot. This is intrinsic to two's complement and is *not* a bug. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, illustrate the role of `<stdint.h>` in enabling portable ARM Cortex-M memory-mapped I/O programming. Discuss why `volatile uint32_t *` is the canonical register-access pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the limit macros `INT8_MIN`, `INT8_MAX`, `UINT8_MAX`, `INT32_MIN`, `INT32_MAX`. Compute the exact decimal values for all five and explain the practical consequence of choosing `uint8_t` versus `int` to count a 300-element loop index. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The block diagram (refer Section 4.2) shows the chain: **C source → include `<stdint.h>` → declare `uint32_t` → cast to `volatile uint32_t *` → ARM peripheral register → AHB/APB bus → hardware effect.** **(2 marks)**

`volatile` is mandatory because the compiler must not optimize away reads/writes to the pointer — the hardware register can change outside the CPU's control (e.g., a status flag set by an interrupt). Without `volatile`, the compiler may cache a read in a register, missing hardware updates. **(2 marks)**

`uint32_t` is mandatory because the ARM Cortex-M memory map defines peripheral registers at fixed 32-bit boundaries, and the load/store instructions (`LDR`, `STR`) operate on 32-bit values. Using `int` would be dangerous because `int` could legally be 16 bits on a hypothetical compiler, breaking the access. **(2 marks)**

This combination is *portable* because any C99-conformant toolchain — GCC-arm-none-eabi, ARM CC 6, IAR — provides identical semantics. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The macros are defined in `<stdint.h>` as:

$$\texttt{INT8\_MIN} = -2^{7} = -128$$

$$\texttt{INT8\_MAX} = 2^{7}-1 = +127$$

$$\texttt{UINT8\_MAX} = 2^{8}-1 = 255$$

$$\texttt{INT32\_MIN} = -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

$$\texttt{INT32\_MAX} = 2^{31}-1 = +2{,}147{,}483{,}647$$

**(3 marks for all five values)**

**Loop-counting consequence:** A `for (uint8_t i = 0; i < 300; i++)` loop is a **logic bug** on most platforms. When `i` reaches 255, the next increment overflows to 0 (wrap-around is defined for unsigned), and the loop never terminates — it loops forever. Using `int` (≥ 16 bits) or `uint16_t` correctly handles 300 iterations. This is a classic KTU exam trap question. **(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating `int_leastN_t` with `int_fastN_t`.** Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the "fastest" qualifier.
> 2. **Forgetting the `<inttypes.h>` companion header** for `printf` format specifiers (`PRId32`, `PRIu64`).
> 3. **Stating that `int` is always 32 bits on ARM.** It is empirically 32 bits on Cortex-M/A, but C99 only guarantees ≥ 16 bits. The standard requires `<stdint.h>` for exactness.
> 4. **Missing the `volatile` keyword** in the register-access question — a single missed word costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Asymmetry of two's complement:** Students often claim the range is "$\pm 2^{31}$" — it is *not*; the positive side is one less.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`<stdint.h>`** is the C99 header providing exact-width types. **(Must remember the header name verbatim.)**
- **Five families:** Exact-width, minimum-width, fastest, pointer-holding, greatest-width. **(Frequently asked in Part A.)**
- **Range formula:** Unsigned N-bit = $\left[0,\ 2^{N}-1\right]$; Signed N-bit = $\left[-2^{N-1},\ 2^{N-1}-1\right]$. **(Frequently asked in Part B derivations.)**
- **Limit macros:** `INTN_MIN`, `INTN_MAX`, `UINTN_MAX` — for N ∈ {8, 16, 32, 64}.
- **`<inttypes.h>`** is the companion header for portable `printf`/`scanf` format specifiers like `PRId32`.
- **ARM Cortex-M register access** mandates `volatile uint32_t *` — exactness from `<stdint.h>`, volatility from `volatile`, and pointer-width from the architecture.
- **Two's complement asymmetry** is a result of zero consuming one slot on the positive side; the negative side gets one extra value.
- **Unsigned overflow** is *defined* (modular wrap) in C99; **signed overflow** is *implementation-defined* (typically wraps on ARM).
- **`int_leastN_t`** minimizes memory; **`int_fastN_t`** maximizes speed — choose based on whether the application is memory-bound or compute-bound.
- **`uintptr_t`** is the only integer type that losslessly round-trips a `void *` — required for storing addresses in integer-typed data structures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boolean Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boolean Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boolean Type (C99 / C11 Standard for ARM Embedded Programming):**
> A **Boolean** is a fundamental scalar data type that can store exactly one of two logical values: **true (1)** or **false (0)**. In the ARM C/C++ extension, `_Bool` is the keyword introduced by the **C99** ISO/IEC 9899:1999 standard, while the `<stdbool.h>` header provides the convenient aliases `bool`, `true`, and `false` for human-readable source code.

In ARM Cortex-M / Cortex-A systems, the Boolean type is mapped to the smallest addressable unit in memory:
- In **C99 (`_Bool`)**: Occupies at least **1 byte (8 bits)** of storage (guaranteed by the standard, even though only **1 bit** is logically required).
- In **C++ (`bool`)**: Also occupies **1 byte** typically, although the ARM AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) does not specify a fixed width for it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEECT524):** Understanding the Boolean type is the foundation for ARM's **conditional execution** model, the `CMP`/`TST` flag-setting instructions, and the **If-Then (IT)** blocks introduced in the ARMv7-M (Thumb-2) architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Boolean** like a **light switch** in your house:
- There are **only two possible states**: ON (true / 1) or OFF (false / 0).
- It does not matter *how bright* the light is — it is either glowing or it is not.
- In ARM, the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register) flags** behave exactly like an array of these light switches. Each flag (N, Z, C, V) is a Boolean that records the result of the previous arithmetic operation, and a single Boolean (e.g., the **Z flag**) often decides whether the CPU takes a branch or falls through to the next instruction.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum storage size (`_Bool`) | **1 byte (8 bits)** | C99 minimum guarantee |
| Logical states | **2** (0, 1) | Binary truth domain |
| Boolean algebra constants | **0 (False)**, **1 (True)** | Identity elements for OR and AND |
| ARM CPSR flag size | **1 bit each** | N, Z, C, V flags |
| `bool` header file | **`<stdbool.h>`** | C99 convenience header |

> [!TIP]
> A common student pitfall is assuming a Boolean only consumes 1 bit. While logically a Boolean is 1 bit, the C standard mandates **at least 1 byte** of storage because memory is byte-addressable in ARM — the CPU cannot read or write a single bit directly without using **bit-banding** (Cortex-M3/M4) or **bitwise masking**.

---

## 1.4 Boolean Type in the ARM Ecosystem

The Boolean type bridges two worlds in PEECT524:

1. **High-level C programming** (application layer) — where you write `if (sensor == HIGH)`.
2. **Low-level ARM assembly / hardware** — where a `CMP` instruction sets the **Z (Zero) flag** in `CPSR`, and a subsequent `BEQ` (Branch if Equal) consumes that Boolean flag to alter control flow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Representation in ARM

The Boolean type is stored in **little-endian** format (default for ARM Cortex), meaning the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** is at the lowest memory address. When `_Bool` is **0**, every bit in the byte is 0. When `_Bool` is **1**, the byte becomes `0x01` (only the LSB is set).

| Logical Value | Hex Byte | Binary Representation | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|
| `false` | `0x00` | `0000 0000` | **0** |
| `true`  | `0x01` | `0000 0001` | **1** |

> [!NOTE]
> If a C program assigns any **non-zero** integer to a `_Bool` variable, the compiler **normalises** it to `0x01` (true). This is a one-way projection: any non-zero value collapses to logical true.

## 2.2 Boolean Algebra Laws (KTU High-Yield)

Boolean algebra governs every conditional branch, mask, and flag check in ARM. The following identities are essential for the KTU exam:

### 2.2.1 Identity Laws
$$A \cdot 1 = A \quad \text{(AND with 1 is identity)}$$

$$A + 0 = A \quad \text{(OR with 0 is identity)}$$

### 2.2.2 Complement Laws
$$A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$$

$$A + \overline{A} = 1$$

### 2.2.3 Dominance Laws
$$A \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(useful for clearing bits via AND with 0)}$$

$$A + 1 = 1 \quad \text{(useful for setting bits via OR with 1)}$$

### 2.2.4 De Morgan's Theorems (Critical for ARM Bit Manipulation)
$$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$$

$$\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$$

### 2.2.5 Idempotent Law
$$A + A = A \quad \text{(redundant flag checks collapse)}$$

$$A \cdot A = A$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Boolean Expression | ARM Instruction | Truth-Table Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logical AND | $A \land B$ | `AND Rd, Rn, Rm` | True only when **both** are 1 |
| Logical OR  | $A \lor B$ | `ORR Rd, Rn, Rm` | False only when **both** are 0 |
| Logical XOR | $A \oplus B$ | `EOR Rd, Rn, Rm` | True when inputs **differ** |
| Logical NOT | $\overline{A}$ | `MVN Rd, Rm` | Inverts all 32 bits |
| Bit Test | $A \land B$ | `TST Rn, Rm` | Sets Z flag if AND result is 0 |
| Equality Compare | $A = B$ | `CMP Rn, Rm` + `BEQ` | Z flag = 1 if equal |
| Less Than | $A < B$ | `CMP` + `BLT` | N != V |
| Greater Than | $A > B$ | `CMP` + `BGT` | Z=0 AND N=V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Constraint Notice:** The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` and `\lor` for clean LaTeX rendering inside markdown tables.

## 2.4 Why Boolean Matters in ARM Engineering

- **Control Flow:** Every `if`, `while`, and `for` compiles down to a Boolean comparison in ARM assembly.
- **Status Flags:** The `APSR` (Application Program Status Register) in Cortex-M is fundamentally a 5-bit Boolean register: **N, Z, C, V, Q**.
- **Bitwise I/O Control:** In embedded ARM, registers like `GPIOA->ODR` are manipulated using Boolean masks (`&`, `\vert`, `^`) to set/clear individual pins.
- **Conditional Execution (IT blocks):** The `ITE` (If-Then-Else) instruction in Thumb-2 can conditionally execute up to 4 subsequent instructions based on a single Boolean flag — saving branch penalties.
- **Power Efficiency:** Boolean logic gates (NAND, NOR) form the physical foundation of CMOS transistors in every ARM chip fabricated by ARM Ltd. licensees.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Footprint of a Boolean Array

A common KTU question asks: *"What is the total memory consumed by an array of N Booleans on an ARM Cortex-M3 system?"*

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Let $N$ be the number of Boolean elements, and $S_{bool}$ be the size of a single `_Bool` in bytes.

By the C99 standard:
$$S_{bool} = 1 \text{ byte (minimum)}$$

The total memory footprint $M_{total}$ in bytes is:
$$M_{total} = N \times S_{bool} = N \times 1 = N \text{ bytes}$$

In bits, this becomes:
$$M_{total}^{bits} = N \times 8 \text{ bits}$$

### Worked Numerical Example 1:

**Problem:** Compute the memory required to store an array of 1024 Booleans representing the status of 1024 sensor channels on a Cortex-M4 (STM32F407).

**Solution:**

Given $N = 1024$ and $S_{bool} = 1$ byte:

$$M_{total} = 1024 \times 1 = 1024 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to kilobytes (using the IEC binary standard $1 \text{ KiB} = 1024$ bytes):
$$M_{total} = \frac{1024}{1024} = 1 \text{ KiB}$$

In bits:
$$M_{total}^{bits} = 1024 \times 8 = 8192 \text{ bits}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If a student answers "128 bytes" by mistakenly assuming 1 bit per Boolean, they lose marks. The C standard guarantees **byte-addressable** storage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Boolean Normalisation Rule

When an integer is assigned to `_Bool`, the compiler applies the **normalisation function** $f: \mathbb{Z} \to \{0, 1\}$:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x = 0 \\ 1 & \text{if } x \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### Algebraic Proof of Normalisation:

Let $x \in \mathbb{Z}$. Define the normalised Boolean value $b$ as:
$$b = (x \neq 0) \;\text{?}\; 1 : 0$$

This can be expressed using the C expression:
$$b = !!x$$

The double-negation operator works because:
- If $x = 0$, then $!x = 1$, and $!!x = !1 = 0$. ✓
- If $x = 5$, then $!x = 0$, and $!!x = !0 = 1$. ✓
- If $x = -3$, then $!x = 0$, and $!!x = !0 = 1$. ✓

### Worked Numerical Example 2:

**Problem:** What value does a `_Bool` variable hold after executing `flag = 42;` in an ARM C program?

**Solution:**

Input: $x = 42$.

Since $42 \neq 0$:
$$f(42) = 1$$

The byte stored in memory becomes:
$$\text{Memory byte} = 0x01 = \underbrace{0000\;0000}_{\text{padded zeros}}\;1_{\text{LSB}}$$

**Verification:** The LSB is set to 1; all higher bits are zero — this is the canonical C99 representation of `true`.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Truth Table Construction for an ARM Comparator

The ARM `CMP` instruction performs internal subtraction: $Rn - Rm$, and sets the **Z (Zero) flag** based on the Boolean result:
$$Z = (Rn == Rm)$$

This is equivalent to:
$$Z = (Rn - Rm == 0)$$

### Analytical Matrix — Full Truth Table for `CMP R0, R1`

| R0 (Decimal) | R0 (Binary) | R1 (Decimal) | R1 (Binary) | R0 - R1 (Dec) | R0 - R1 (Bin) | **Z flag** | **N flag** | **C flag (unsigned borrow)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 0101 | 5 | 0101 | 0 | 0000 | **1** | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 0101 | 3 | 0011 | 2 | 0010 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 3 | 0011 | 5 | 0101 | -2 | 1110 | 0 | **1** | 0 |
| 0 | 0000 | 0 | 0000 | 0 | 0000 | **1** | 0 | 1 |
| 7 | 0111 | 7 | 0111 | 0 | 0000 | **1** | 0 | 1 |
| 8 | 1000 | 1 | 0001 | 7 | 0111 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Z flag is the **most-used Boolean** in ARM control flow. `BEQ` (Branch if Equal) executes when `Z == 1`, and `BNE` (Branch if Not Equal) executes when `Z == 0`.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bitwise Masking Using Boolean Algebra

**Problem:** Set bit 5 of register `R0` without affecting any other bit, using only Boolean operations.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Construct a mask that has 1 only at position 5:
$$M_{set} = 0x20 = \underbrace{0010\;0000}_{2^5}$$

**Step 2:** Apply OR (since $A + 1 = 1$ and $A + 0 = A$):
$$R0_{new} = R0_{old} \lor M_{set} = R0_{old} \lor 0x20$$

**Step 3:** Verification at bit 5: $A_5 \lor 1 = 1$ (Dominance Law). At all other bits $i \neq 5$: $A_i \lor 0 = A_i$ (Identity Law). ✓

### Companion Operation: Clear bit 5

**Step 1:** Construct an inverted mask (all 1s except bit 5):
$$M_{clr} = \overline{0x20} = 0xDF = 1101\;1111$$

**Step 2:** Apply AND (since $A \cdot 1 = A$ and $A \cdot 0 = 0$):
$$R0_{new} = R0_{old} \land M_{clr} = R0_{old} \land 0xDF$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boolean Type Lifecycle in ARM

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a Boolean value flows from C source code down to the ARM hardware transistor level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["C Source: bool flag = true"] --> B["Compiler: _Bool type, 1 byte"]
    B --> C["Memory Map: 0x20000000 = 0x01"]
    C --> D["CMP / TST instruction"]
    D --> E["ALU performs Boolean op"]
    E --> F["APSR / CPSR flags N Z C V"]
    F --> G{"Z flag Boolean = 1 or 0?"}
    G -- Z=1 --> H["BEQ taken, branch executed"]
    G -- Z=0 --> I["BNE taken, fall through"]
    H --> J["Next pipeline stage"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["CMOS NAND/NOR gates implement Boolean logic"]
```

## 4.2 Boolean Algebraic Operation Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    Op1["AND Operation"] --> Bit1["Mask bit to 0"]
    Op2["OR Operation"] --> Bit2["Mask bit to 1"]
    Op3["EOR XOR Operation"] --> Bit3["Toggle bit"]
    Op4["MVN NOT Operation"] --> Bit4["Invert all bits"]
    Bit1 --> ARM1["BIC Rd, Rn, Rm"]
    Bit2 --> ARM2["ORR Rd, Rn, Rm"]
    Bit3 --> ARM3["EOR Rd, Rn, Rm"]
    Bit4 --> ARM4["MVN Rd, Rm"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Boolean in ARM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FetchStage["Fetch Stage"]
        F1["Instruction Memory"]
    end
    FetchStage --> DecodeStage
    subgraph DecodeStage["Decode Stage"]
        D1["Instruction Decoder"]
        D2["CPSR Flag Register"]
    end
    DecodeStage --> ExecuteStage
    subgraph ExecuteStage["Execute Stage"]
        E1["32-bit ALU"]
        E2["Boolean Logic Unit"]
        E3["Barrel Shifter"]
    end
    ExecuteStage --> WriteBackStage
    subgraph WriteBackStage["Write-Back Stage"]
        W1["Register File Update"]
        W2["APSR Flag Update"]
    end
    WriteBackStage --> Result["Result Bus to Memory"]
```

## 4.4 Truth Table to ARM Branch Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TT["Boolean Condition Result"] --> Decision{"Is result TRUE or FALSE?"}
    Decision -- TRUE / 1 --> BranchA["ARM executes conditional path"]
    Decision -- FALSE / 0 --> BranchB["ARM skips or inverts path"]
    BranchA --> Action1["Execute THEN block"]
    BranchB --> Action2["Execute ELSE block"]
    Action1 --> Exit["Pipeline continues"]
    Action2 --> Exit
```

---
