The total struct size is rounded up to a multiple of 4 bytes. **Three Implementation-Defined Aspects (2 marks):** 1. **Allocation direction:** Whether fields are packed from MSB or LSB within a storage unit. 2. **Boundary crossing:** Whether a field is allowed to straddle two adjacent storage units. 3. **Storage unit size:** The size of the underlying allocation unit (4 bytes for ARM, but may differ on other architectures). 4. **Signedness of plain `int` bit fields:** Whether plain `int` bit fields are treated as signed or unsigned. 5. **Padding of zero-width fields:** How zero-width unnamed bit fields affect alignment. *(Any three earn 2 marks.)* ### Part (b) — UART\_CR Bit-Field Struct Definition (7 marks) **C Definition (4 marks):** ```c typedef struct { unsigned int RX_INT_EN : 1; /* bit 0 */ unsigned int TX_INT_EN : 1; /* bit 1 */ unsigned int RX_EN : 1; /* bit 2 */ unsigned int TX_EN : 1; /* bit 3 */ unsigned int STOP_2BIT : 1; /* bit 4 */ unsigned int PARITY_EN : 1; /* bit 5 */ unsigned int PARITY_EVEN : 1; /* bit 6 */ unsigned int LOOPBACK : 1; /* bit 7 */ unsigned int DIV : 8; /* bits 15:8 */ unsigned int RESERVED : 16; /* bits 31:16 */ } UART_CR_bits; #define UART_CR ((volatile UART_CR_bits *)0x40001000U) ``` **Atomic Set of TX_EN (1.5 marks):** ```c UART_CR->TX_EN = 1; ``` The compiler emits a read–modify–write sequence: it reads the 32-bit word, ORs the value `0x08` (i.e., `1 << 3`), and writes it back. Because the pointer is `volatile`, the compiler will not optimize away the read and write. **Atomic Clear of RX_EN (1.5 marks):** ```c UART_CR->RX_EN = 0; ``` The compiler reads the word, ANDs it with the bitmask `~0x04` (i.e., `~(1 << 2)`), and writes it back. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `volatile`** on the peripheral pointer — the compiler will optimize the read-modify-write away, leading to silent hardware failure. Always declare hardware registers as `volatile`. > 2. **Wrong field order** in the struct — placing TX_EN before RX_EN when the hardware layout is opposite. **Always cross-check with the datasheet's bit-position table.** > 3. **Using signed `int` for bit fields** — undefined behaviour on overflow and surprise sign-extension. Prefer `unsigned int`. > 4. **Assuming portable bit-field layout** — the order is **implementation-defined**. For 100% portable code, use explicit masking and shifting rather than bit fields. > 5. **Forgetting the `(b × 4)` term in the alias formula** — losing 1 full mark in bit-band calculations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bit Field** = a struct member of declared bit width; used to map C variables onto hardware register bits. - **Default ARM storage unit** = 32 bits (one `unsigned int`). - **Allocation direction on little-endian ARM** = LSB → MSB. - **Layout is implementation-defined** — verify with `offsetof()` or by inspecting the generated assembly. - **Anonymous zero-width bit field** = forces alignment to the next storage unit (padding). - **You cannot take the address** of a bit field (`&` is illegal). - **Always use `volatile`** for memory-mapped peripheral register pointers. - **Bit-Band regions** (Cortex-M3/M4) are `0x20000000`–`0x200FFFFF` (SRAM) and `0x40000000`–`0x400FFFFF` (Peripherals). - **Bit-Band alias regions** are `0x22000000`–`0x23FFFFFF` (SRAM alias) and `0x42000000`–`0x43FFFFFF` (Peripheral alias). - **Canonical alias address formula:** $$\text{Alias\_Addr} = \text{Alias\_Base} + (A - \text{Region\_Base}) \times 32 + b \times 4$$ - **Bit-band gives 32-bit atomic single-bit access** — interrupt-safe and multicore-safe. - **Bit-band is NOT present** in Cortex-M0 / M0+ — those cores must use read-modify-write or the BSRR/BRR register pattern. - **CMSIS convention** = all peripheral structs use bit fields in the natural LSB-first order matching the silicon bit positions. - **Three things that make bit-field code fail in production:** missing `volatile`, wrong endianness assumption, and non-portable layout assumption across compilers.

topics.title: Casting Address of an Object
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting Address of an Object

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Casting the address of an object** is the process of explicitly converting a pointer (or reference) of one object type into a pointer (or reference) of a different type using type-cast operators. In the context of the C++ programming language used for ARM-based embedded development, this operation instructs the compiler to reinterpret the underlying memory address of an object as a different data type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEECT524):**
> Every object in C++ occupies a unique memory address returned by the address-of operator `&`. Casting this address changes only the *compiler's interpretation* of the bytes stored at that location, not the actual bit pattern.

The four C++ cast operators relevant to this module are:

1. `static_cast<T>(expr)` — Compile-time checked, "sensible" conversions.
2. `reinterpret_cast<T>(expr)` — Low-level, bitwise reinterpretation of the address.
3. `const_cast<T>(expr)` — Adds or removes `const`/`volatile` qualifiers.
4. `dynamic_cast<T>(expr)` — Runtime-checked conversion in polymorphic class hierarchies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "House Number" Model**

Imagine every house on a street has a unique **house number** (this is the *address*). The actual house might be a residential bungalow, a shop, or a factory. If the post office suddenly decides to deliver "industrial machinery" mail to a house number that was originally a "residential bungalow," the **address itself does not change** — only the **postal department's interpretation** of what is at that number changes.

- The **house number** = the memory address (e.g., `0x20000004`).
- The **building type** = the data type (e.g., `int`, `float`, `GPIO_Reg*`).
- **Casting** = the post office deciding to treat the building as a *different* type for delivery purposes.

In ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, addresses like `0x40023800` (RCC base) are *cast* to `volatile uint32_t*` so that writing to `*(uint32_t*)0x40023800` flips actual hardware bits.

### 1.3 Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics

- **Address size on ARM Cortex-M**: **32 bits (4 bytes)**.
- **Alignment requirement**: A cast that produces a misaligned pointer results in a **HardFault** on Cortex-M3/M4.
- **NULL address**: `0x00000000` (or simply `0`) is the universal "no object" sentinel after a failed cast.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Cast Operators — Structured Logic

#### 2.1.1 `static_cast`
- Performed entirely at **compile time**.
- Used for conversions that the compiler can justify logically (e.g., `int` → `float`, derived-class pointer → base-class pointer — *upcasting*).
- **Does NOT** perform runtime type checking.

#### 2.1.2 `reinterpret_cast`
- The most "dangerous" cast — reinterprets the **bit pattern** of the address.
- Used for low-level operations: hardware register access, casting between unrelated pointer types (e.g., `int*` ↔ `char*`).
- **No safety net** — the programmer is fully responsible for correctness.

#### 2.1.3 `const_cast`
- The **only** cast that can add or remove `const` or `volatile` qualifiers.
- Used to call legacy non-const APIs with const data.

#### 2.1.4 `dynamic_cast`
- Used for **downcasting** (base → derived) in polymorphic class hierarchies.
- Performs a **runtime type check** (RTTI) and returns `nullptr` (for pointers) or throws `std::bad_cast` (for references) on failure.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need to cast addresses?**
> 1. **Hardware register access** — MMIO addresses must be cast to volatile pointers.
> 2. **Polymorphism** — Storing derived-class objects as base-class pointers and safely retrieving them.
> 3. **Data serialization** — Treating a struct as a byte array for network/storage transmission.
> 4. **Inheritance hierarchies** — Upcasting and downcasting along class trees.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Cast Operator | Compile / Runtime | Safety Level | Typical Use Case | Failure Behavior |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| `static_cast` | Compile-time | Medium | Numeric conversions, upcasting | Compile error if invalid |
| `reinterpret_cast` | Compile-time | **Low** | Pointer ↔ unrelated pointer, MMIO | Undefined behavior at runtime |
| `const_cast` | Compile-time | Medium | Add/remove `const` / `volatile` | Undefined behavior if misused |
| `dynamic_cast` | **Runtime** | **High** | Downcasting in polymorphic trees | Returns `nullptr` / throws |

### 2.4 Pointer Arithmetic After Cast

When you cast a pointer `p` of type `T1*` to `T2*`, subsequent pointer arithmetic uses the size of `T2`:

$$p_{T2} = p_{T1} + n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad p_{T2} = p_{T1} + n \times \vert T2 \vert$$

where $\vert T2 \vert$ is `sizeof(T2)` in bytes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded firmware**: Casting `0x40000000` to `(volatile uint32_t*)` to toggle GPIO pins on STM32.
- **Device drivers in Linux**: Casting between `void*` and `struct file*` in kernel callbacks.
- **Game engines**: Casting between interface pointers (e.g., `IEntity*` to `IPlayer*`) using `dynamic_cast`.
- **Network stacks**: Casting raw byte buffers to protocol headers (`reinterpret_cast<TCPHeader*>(buffer)`).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — C-Style vs C++ Cast

Consider an `int` variable located at memory address `0x20000100`:

```cpp
int sensorValue = 0x1234ABCD;   // Located at 0x20000100 (little-endian on ARM)
```

**Step 1 — Old C-style cast:**
```cpp
char* pByte = (char*)&sensorValue;   // Permissive, compiler emits a warning in modern C++
```

**Step 2 — Equivalent modern C++ cast:**
```cpp
char* pByte = reinterpret_cast<char*>(&sensorValue);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of Step 1 → Step 2:**
> The C-style cast `(char*)&x` is actually a sequence of: `const_cast` → `static_cast` → `reinterpret_cast`. The C++ version makes the intent explicit and the compiler can flag the dangerous parts separately.

**Step 3 — Reading individual bytes on little-endian ARM:**
```cpp
uint8_t b0 = pByte[0];   // 0xCD  (least significant byte)
uint8_t b1 = pByte[1];   // 0xAB
uint8_t b2 = pByte[2];   // 0x34
uint8_t b3 = pByte[3];   // 0x12  (most significant byte)
```

The pointer arithmetic $pByte[1] = pByte + 1 \times \vert \text{char} \vert = pByte + 1$ advances by **1 byte** because `sizeof(char) = 1`.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Upcasting and Downcasting

```cpp
class Animal  { public: virtual void speak() {} };
class Dog : public Animal {
public:
    void speak() override {}
    void fetch() { /* dog-specific */ }
};
```

**Step A — Upcasting (implicit, no cast needed):**
```cpp
Dog* pDog   = new Dog();
Animal* pAn = pDog;     // Safe, implicit upcast
```

**Step B — Downcasting with `static_cast` (NO runtime check):**
```cpp
Dog* pDog2 = static_cast<Dog*>(pAn);  // Unchecked
```

**Step C — Downcasting with `dynamic_cast` (runtime check):**
```cpp
Dog* pDog3 = dynamic_cast<Dog*>(pAn);
if (pDog3 != nullptr) {
    pDog3->fetch();   // Safe — guaranteed Dog
} else {
    // Not a Dog — handle gracefully
}
```

**The pointer-offset derivation for a downcast:**

If a `Dog` object starts at address $A_{\text{Dog}}$, and `Animal` is the *first* base sub-object, then:

$$A_{\text{Animal}} = A_{\text{Dog}} + 0$$

because in standard C++ layout, the base class sub-object is at offset **0**. Therefore:

$$p_{\text{Dog}} = \text{static\_cast}\langle \text{Dog}^* \rangle (p_{\text{Animal}}) = p_{\text{Animal}}$$

(no arithmetic performed by the cast in this single-inheritance case).

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Memory-Mapped I/O on ARM (Most KTU-Relevant)

The STM32F407 GPIOA output data register (ODR) lives at `0x40020014`:

```cpp
#define GPIOA_ODR  (*(volatile uint32_t*)0x40020014U)
```

**Step 1 — Define the address as an integer literal:**
$$A_{\text{ODR}} = \text{0x40020014}_{16} = 1073871892_{10}$$

**Step 2 — Cast the address to a volatile pointer:**
```cpp
volatile uint32_t* pODR = (volatile uint32_t*)0x40020014U;
```

**Step 3 — Dereference to read/write the register:**
```cpp
*pODR |= (1U << 5);    // Set bit 5 (PA5 LED on many STM32 boards)
```

> [!WARNING]
> The `volatile` qualifier is **NOT** part of the cast itself. It is a separate type qualifier that tells the compiler the value can change at any time, preventing the optimizer from caching it in a register. Without `volatile`, the LED may appear to "freeze" because the compiler thinks the variable never changes.

**Step 4 — Memory write transaction on the bus:**
$$ \text{CPU} \xrightarrow{\text{STR instruction}} \text{AHB bus} \xrightarrow{\text{0x40020014}} \text{GPIOA peripheral} $$

The cast itself does not generate any machine instructions on ARM — it only affects **how the compiler interprets the address** in subsequent load (`LDR`) and store (`STR`) instructions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Cast

| Scenario | Recommended Cast | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Hardware register MMIO | `reinterpret_cast` (or C-style) | Unrelated integer ↔ pointer conversion |
| `int` → `float` | `static_cast` | Logical numeric promotion |
| Remove `const` from a parameter | `const_cast` | Only operator allowed to drop `const` |
| `Base*` → `Derived*` (polymorphic) | `dynamic_cast` | Runtime safety check |
| `void*` → specific `T*` | `static_cast` | Reversible, well-defined |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct Cast

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to cast an address?] --> B{Conversion is logical and checked at compile time?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use static_cast]
    B -- No --> D{Polymorphic downcast in class hierarchy?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use dynamic_cast]
    D -- No --> F{Need to add or remove const or volatile?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use const_cast]
    F -- No --> H[Use reinterpret_cast for low level bit reinterpretation]
    H --> I[Confirm alignment and endianness]
    C --> J[Compile and verify]
    E --> J
    G --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Done]
```

### 4.2 Memory Layout — Object Address and Cast View

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RAM["ARM SRAM 0x20000000"]
        A1["0x20000100:  CD"]
        A2["0x20000101:  AB"]
        A3["0x20000102:  34"]
        A4["0x20000103:  12"]
    end
    P1["int* p"] -- "reinterpret_cast" --> P2["char* pByte"]
    P1 -- points to 4 bytes --> A1
    P2 -- points to 1 byte --> A1
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cast in the Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code with Cast] --> LEX[Lexer and Parser]
    LEX --> SEM[Semantic Analyzer]
    SEM --> CASTNODE[Cast Expression Node]
    CASTNODE -- static_cast --> T1[Type checked at compile time]
    CASTNODE -- reinterpret_cast --> T2[Bit pattern reinterpreted]
    CASTNODE -- const_cast --> T3[Qualifier adjusted]
    CASTNODE -- dynamic_cast --> T4[RTTI lookup at runtime]
    T1 --> IR[LLVM IR or ARM IR]
    T2 --> IR
    T3 --> IR
    T4 --> IR
    IR --> CODEGEN[ARM Code Generator]
    CODEGEN --> LDRSTR[LDR and STR Instructions]
```

### 4.4 Cast Hierarchy Safety Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCAST["dynamic_cast — Highest Safety, Runtime Checked"] --> SCAST["static_cast — Compile Time, Logical Conversion"]
    SCAST --> CCAST["const_cast — Const and Volatile Adjustment"]
    CCAST --> RCAST["reinterpret_cast — Lowest Safety, Bit Reinterpretation"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "casting the address of an object" in C++. List the four C++ cast operators and state one typical use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Casting the address of an object is the explicit conversion of a pointer (or reference) of one type to another using type-cast operators. The four C++ cast operators are:
1. `static_cast` — for compile-time logical conversions (e.g., numeric promotion).
2. `reinterpret_cast` — for low-level bitwise reinterpretation (e.g., MMIO in ARM).
3. `const_cast` — to add or remove `const` / `volatile` qualifiers.
4. `dynamic_cast` — for safe runtime-checked downcasting in polymorphic class hierarchies.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four operators with one use case each, or proportionate distribution.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the role of the `volatile` qualifier when casting a memory-mapped hardware register address on an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `volatile` qualifier instructs the compiler that the value at the given memory address may change at any time outside the program's control. When an address such as `0x40020014` is cast to `volatile uint32_t*`, the compiler is forced to generate a fresh `LDR` or `STR` instruction for every access, preventing it from optimizing away or caching the read/write in a CPU register. This is essential for correct hardware register interaction on ARM Cortex-M devices.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between `static_cast`, `dynamic_cast`, and `reinterpret_cast` with one illustrative code example for each. Discuss why C++ provides four cast operators instead of a single C-style cast. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller, write a short C++ program that defines a memory-mapped GPIO register using an address cast. Explain the significance of `volatile` and the role of pointer arithmetic in your code. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The three cast operators differ as follows:

| Cast | Timing | Safety | Use Case |
|------|--------|--------|----------|
| `static_cast` | Compile | Medium | `int x = static_cast<int>(3.14);` |
| `dynamic_cast` | Runtime | High | `Derived* d = dynamic_cast<Derived*>(basePtr);` |
| `reinterpret_cast` | Compile | Low | `char* p = reinterpret_cast<char*>(&x);` |

C++ provides four cast operators because each has a **narrow, well-defined purpose**, allowing the compiler to detect programmer errors at compile time. A single C-style cast silently performs a chain of conversions, making code harder to audit and debug — especially dangerous in embedded ARM systems where miscasts can lead to HardFaults.

**(b)** Sample program:
```cpp
// STM32F407 GPIOA ODR at 0x40020014
volatile uint32_t* const GPIOA_ODR = reinterpret_cast<volatile uint32_t*>(0x40020014U);

void ledOn()  { *GPIOA_ODR |=  (1U << 5); }   // Set  PA5
void ledOff() { *GPIOA_ODR &= ~(1U << 5); }   // Reset PA5
```

`volatile` ensures every read/write goes to the actual peripheral. Pointer arithmetic is not used here because we only dereference a single register; if we cast to a struct of registers, then `regPtr[1]` would correctly jump by `sizeof(register)`.

*(Award full 7 marks for each sub-part if all key points — cast differences, safety reasoning, volatile role, ARM-specific example — are present.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** What is object slicing? Demonstrate with a C++ program how improper downcasting can be avoided using `dynamic_cast`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain memory-mapped I/O on an ARM processor. How is the address of a peripheral register cast to a pointer? Why must the `volatile` qualifier be used? Provide a code snippet. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Object slicing** occurs when a derived-class object is copied into a base-class object, causing the derived-specific members to be "sliced off." Example demonstrating safe downcast:

```cpp
class Shape  { public: virtual void draw() {} };
class Circle : public Shape {
public:
    void draw() override {}
    double radius() { return 1.0; }
};

Shape* s = new Circle();
Circle* c = dynamic_cast<Circle*>(s);   // Safe runtime check
if (c) c->radius();
```

Using `dynamic_cast` returns `nullptr` if the actual object is not a `Circle`, preventing undefined behavior.

**(b)** Memory-mapped I/O (MMIO) maps peripheral registers to specific memory addresses. On ARM Cortex-M, the register is accessed by casting the address to a volatile pointer:

```cpp
#define RCC_AHB1ENR (*(volatile uint32_t*)0x40023830U)
#define GPIOAEN     (1U << 0)

void enableGPIOA() {
    RCC_AHB1ENR |= GPIOAEN;     // Turn on clock for GPIOA peripheral
}
```

`volatile` prevents the compiler from optimizing away the write, ensuring the peripheral actually receives the clock-enable command.

*(Allocate 7 marks per sub-part. Deduct 1–2 marks if code lacks `volatile` or if cast type is not justified.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `volatile`** on MMIO pointers — leads to "ghost writes" where the compiler optimizes away the store. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using C-style casts in C++ code** when the question explicitly asks for C++ cast operators — partial credit only. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing upcasting with downcasting** — upcasting is implicit and requires NO cast; downcasting requires `dynamic_cast` (safe) or `static_cast` (unchecked).
> 4. **Wrong cast for `void*` conversion** — must be `static_cast`, not `reinterpret_cast`, for compiler safety.
> 5. **Not stating ARM-specific justification** — examiners reward mention of `LDR`/`STR` semantics and little-endian byte order.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Casting an address** changes the *type interpretation* of memory, not the *bit pattern*.
- **Four C++ casts**: `static_cast`, `reinterpret_cast`, `const_cast`, `dynamic_cast`.
- **`reinterpret_cast`** is the go-to for ARM memory-mapped register access.
- **`dynamic_cast`** is the only *runtime-checked* cast and is mandatory for safe polymorphic downcasting.
- **`const_cast`** is the only cast that can drop `const` or `volatile` qualifiers.
- **`volatile`** is **not** a cast — it is a type qualifier that disables compiler optimizations on the pointed-to memory.
- On ARM Cortex-M, a cast producing a **misaligned pointer** will trigger a **HardFault** at runtime.
- Pointer arithmetic after a cast advances by `sizeof(new_type)`, not by 1 byte.
- ARM Cortex-M is **little-endian** by default — the least significant byte is at the lowest address.
- The cast itself emits **no machine instructions** on ARM; it only modifies the type metadata used by subsequent `LDR` and `STR` operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **union** in C is a user-defined data type (derived data type) that allows the storage of **different data types in the same memory location**. Unlike a structure, where each member is assigned a **distinct memory location**, a union allocates a **single shared memory region** whose size equals the size of its **largest member**. All members of a union therefore **share the same starting address** (i.e., they overlap in memory), and only **one member can hold a valid value at any given time**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-Style):** A union is a derived data type in C, declared using the keyword `union`, which provides a single storage location that can be interpreted as any one of its several member types, but never all simultaneously. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a **single physical parking slot** in a busy IT park. Depending on the time of day, that single slot may hold: - A **two-wheeler** in the morning rush (small data, e.g., `char`), - A **car** in the afternoon (medium data, e.g., `int`), - A **truck** at night (large data, e.g., `double`). The **parking slot itself is fixed in size** (determined by the largest vehicle that will ever use it — the `double` truck), and **only one vehicle occupies it at a time**. This is exactly how a union works in C: **one shared memory block**, sized for the biggest member, holding **only one active interpretation** at a time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** A union is a **memory-overlap** mechanism — it is the *C language's* way of saying *"interpret the same bytes in multiple ways."* This is a critical primitive in ARM Cortex-M firmware for things like **memory-mapped peripheral register access** and **protocol frame parsing**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions - The **size of a union** is mathematically defined as: $$\text{sizeof(union)} = \max(\text{sizeof}(m_1), \text{sizeof}(m_2), \ldots, \text{sizeof}(m_n)) + \text{internal padding (alignment)}$$ - For ARM Cortex-M (32-bit) targets, the typical alignment of a union is **4 bytes**, and member offsets are computed relative to the **base address**. - The keyword `union` introduces a union type; the `sizeof` operator returns its size in **bytes** (`size_t`, defined in ``). > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In PEECT524 (ARM Architecture & Programming), the union is taught as a *low-level C construct* that directly maps to the **flat 32-bit memory model** of the ARM processor. Mastering unions is essential for writing efficient firmware, device drivers, and protocol parsers on LPC1768 / STM32 / Tiva-C class microcontrollers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" of Unions A union works on three foundational principles, each of which must be fully understood for KTU-level questions: **Principle 1: Shared Starting Address (Memory Overlap)** - When the compiler lays out a union, it allocates a single contiguous block of memory. - The **address of the union variable = the address of every member**. - Mathematically: `&u == &u.member1 == &u.member2 == ... == &u.memberN`. - **Why?** Because the union is *one* storage location; the compiler simply reinterprets the same bytes through different type lenses. **Principle 2: Size = Size of the Largest Member (plus padding)** - The compiler must guarantee that the union can hold the **largest** of its members safely. - Padding may be added for **alignment constraints** dictated by the ARM ABI (AAPCS — ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard). - For 32-bit ARM, members are usually aligned to their natural boundary (1, 2, or 4 bytes). **Principle 3: Mutual Exclusion of Member Access (Single Active Value)** - Writing to one member **overwrites the bytes** of any previously stored member. - Reading from a different member than the one most recently written yields **type-punning** behavior (legal in C, except for strict-aliasing edge cases). - **Why care?** Because the embedded systems engineer who wrote to `u.intVal` but reads `u.floatVal` will see **garbage** or unexpected values. ### 2.2 Syntax of Union Declaration ```c // Method 1: Declare union type, then variables union Data { int i; float f; char str[20]; }; union Data d1, d2; // Method 2: Declare variables directly at type definition union Data { int i; float f; char str[20]; } d1, d2; // Method 3: Anonymous union (C11 / GNU extension, widely used in ARM firmware) union { uint32_t raw; struct { uint32_t ENABLE : 1; uint32_t MODE : 3; uint32_t RESERVED: 28; } bits; }; ``` ### 2.3 Union vs. Structure — The Critical Distinction This is one of the **most frequently asked distinctions** in KTU university exams. A clear comparison is mandatory: | Feature | `struct` | `union` | |---|---|---| | Memory layout | Each member has a **separate, sequential** memory location | All members **share the same starting address** | | Total size | Sum of (size of each member + padding) | Size of the **largest member** + padding | | Active members | **All members** can hold valid values simultaneously | **Only one member** holds a valid value at a time | | Address equality | `&s.m1 != &s.m2` (in general) | `&u.m1 == &u.m2 == &u` | | Keyword | `struct` | `union` | | Typical use | Logical grouping of related data (e.g., GPIO config: pin, port, direction) | Variant data, memory-mapped registers, protocol unions, type punning | | ARM firmware analogy | A `GPIO_InitTypeDef` with 4 fields | A union over a 32-bit peripheral register's bit-fields and raw word | ### 2.4 Accessing Union Members Members are accessed using the **dot (`.`) operator** for direct variables and the **arrow (`->`) operator** for pointer-to-union variables — exactly like structures. ```c union Data d; d.i = 42; // write through the 'int' lens printf("%d", d.i); // read through the 'int' lens d.f = 3.14f; // overwrites the bytes — 'i' is now invalid ``` ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formulas for Unions** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Size of a union | $\text{sizeof}(U) = \max_{i=1..n}\text{sizeof}(m_i) + P$ | $P$ = alignment padding (bytes) | | 2 | Address of any member | $\&U.m_i = \&U$ | All members share base address | | 3 | Offset of member $m_i$ | $\text{offset}(m_i) = 0$ | Always 0 in a flat union | | 4 | Padding in 32-bit ARM | $P = (4 - (\max \text{size} \bmod 4)) \bmod 4$ | To align union to 4-byte boundary | | 5 | Total accessible memory | $M = \text{sizeof}(U)$ bytes | Equal to largest member's allocation | | 6 | Number of overlapping members | Unbounded, but only **1 active** at a time | Compiler-enforced convention, not enforced by type system | | 7 | Bit-field union (C99+) | $\sum (\text{bitfield widths}) \le 32$ (for ARM word) | Useful for SFR (Special Function Register) access | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in ARM Systems The union is **not a theoretical construct** — it is a **workhorse** in ARM Cortex-M firmware. Five canonical use cases: 1. **Memory-Mapped Peripheral Registers (SFR Access):** The `UART0->DR` register in LPC1768 can be read as a single 32-bit raw word *or* decomposed into 8-bit data/flag fields via a bit-field union. 2. **Variant Data in Protocol Parsers:** Parsing a CAN bus frame where the payload could be an `int`, `float`, or structured message — a union over the payload bytes is the canonical pattern. 3. **Endianness Detection and Byte Manipulation:** Unions are the standard C trick to test whether the target is little-endian (ARM default) or big-endian. 4. **Type-Punning for Floating-Point Bit Inspection:** Inspecting the IEEE-754 bits of a `float` to debug numerical issues. 5. **Tagged Unions for Safe Variant Storage:** Combining a `union` with an `enum` tag to build type-safe variant data (Rust's `enum` is essentially this concept). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Worked Memory-Layout Derivations This is the **highest-weight KTU concept**: given a union declaration, you must be able to derive the memory map on an ARM Cortex-M (32-bit, little-endian, 4-byte aligned) target. --- #### **Worked Example 1: Basic Union Size Calculation** **Declaration:** ```c union U1 { char c; // 1 byte int i; // 4 bytes double d; // 8 bytes (note: on some ARM targets, double = 8 bytes) } u1; ``` **Step 1 — Identify the largest member's size:** $$\text{sizeof}(c) = 1, \quad \text{sizeof}(i) = 4, \quad \text{sizeof}(d) = 8$$ **Step 2 — Compute the raw minimum size:** $$S_{\min} = \max(1, 4, 8) = 8 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Apply ARM AAPCS alignment padding (align to 4 bytes):** $$P = (4 - (8 \bmod 4)) \bmod 4 = (4 - 0) \bmod 4 = 0$$ **Step 4 — Final size:** $$\text{sizeof}(U1) = 8 + 0 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** `sizeof(U1) = 8`. All three members (`c`, `i`, `d`) start at offset `0` from the base address of `u1`. --- #### **Worked Example 2: Union with Non-Power-of-Two Members** **Declaration:** ```c union U2 { char buf[5]; // 5 bytes int i; // 4 bytes } u2; ``` **Step 1 — Largest member's size:** $$S_{\min} = \max(5, 4) = 5 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2 — Alignment padding to 4-byte boundary:** $$P = (4 - (5 \bmod 4)) \bmod 4 = (4 - 1) \bmod 4 = 3 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Final size:** $$\text{sizeof}(U2) = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$ --- #### **Worked Example 3: Union with Bit-Fields (ARM SFR Pattern)** **Declaration:** ```c union ControlReg { uint32_t raw; // 4 bytes, full 32-bit access struct { uint32_t ENABLE : 1; // bit 0 uint32_t MODE : 2; // bits 1-2 uint32_t DIR : 1; // bit 3 uint32_t RESERVED: 28;// bits 4-31 } bits; } ctrl; ``` **Step 1 — Size of `raw`:** $$\text{sizeof}(\text{raw}) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2 — Size of the anonymous bit-field struct:** $$\text{sizeof}(\text{bits}) = \lceil (1+2+1+28)/8 \rceil = \lceil 32/8 \rceil = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Union size:** $$\text{sizeof}(\text{ControlReg}) = \max(4, 4) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Practical use (write a value, then read individual bits):** ```c ctrl.raw = 0x00000005; // set MODE=2 (binary 10) and ENABLE=1 (binary 1) → 0b0101 = 5 printf("ENABLE = %u\n", ctrl.bits.ENABLE); // prints 1 printf("MODE = %u\n", ctrl.bits.MODE); // prints 2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This bit-field union pattern is the **gold standard** for STM32 / LPC1768 peripheral programming — it appears in vendor header files (CMSIS, libopencm3) and is a guaranteed KTU exam question. --- #### **Worked Example 4: Endianness Detection via Union** **Derivation:** The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 is **little-endian by default**, meaning the LSB of a multi-byte value is stored at the lowest address. ```c union EndianTest { uint32_t i; uint8_t c[4]; } et; et.i = 0x12345678; // Memory layout (address low → high): 0x78, 0x56, 0x34, 0x12 (little-endian) if (et.c[0] == 0x78) printf("Little-Endian\n"); // ← ARM Cortex-M prints this else printf("Big-Endian\n"); ``` **Step 1 — Write to integer lens:** `et.i = 0x12345678` writes bytes `78 56 34 12` into memory at offsets `0, 1, 2, 3` respectively (little-endian). **Step 2 — Read through byte lens:** `et.c[0]` reads the byte at offset 0, which is `0x78`. **Step 3 — Test:** `et.c[0] == 0x78` is **true** → system is **little-endian**. --- #### **Worked Example 5: Union of Structures (Nested Composition)** ```c union Payload { struct { uint16_t voltage; uint16_t current; } analog; struct { uint8_t cmd; uint8_t id; uint8_t crc; } digital; uint8_t raw[4]; } p; ``` **Step 1 — Size of each member:** - `analog`: `2 + 2 = 4` bytes (no padding needed, naturally aligned) - `digital`: `1 + 1 + 1 = 3` bytes, padded to 4 bytes for alignment - `raw[4]`: 4 bytes **Step 2 — Union size:** $$\text{sizeof}(Payload) = \max(4, 4, 4) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Practical usage:** Writing `p.analog = {3300, 150}` is equivalent to writing the bytes `0x0C, 0xE4, 0x00, 0x96` (little-endian representation of 3300 and 150) into `p.raw[0..3]`. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: Memory Layout — `struct` vs `union` This is the **most-asked KTU diagram**. It visually demonstrates the fundamental memory-model difference between a structure and a union. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph STRUCT["struct S { char a; int b; char c; } — sizeof = 12 bytes"] S1["Offset 0: a (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding"] S2["Offset 4: b (4 bytes)"] S3["Offset 8: c (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding"] end subgraph UNION["union U { char a; int b; char c; } — sizeof = 4 bytes"] U1["Offset 0..3: shared memory
a, b, c all live here
only one valid at a time"] end STRUCT --- UNION ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Bit-Field Union for ARM Peripheral Register ```mermaid graph LR RAW["ctrl.raw
32 bits
(single ARM word access)"] RAW --> BIT0["bit 0: ENABLE"] RAW --> BIT12["bits 1-2: MODE"] RAW --> BIT3["bit 3: DIR"] RAW --> BIT4_31["bits 4-31: RESERVED"] style RAW fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style BIT0 fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333 style BIT12 fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333 style BIT3 fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333 style BIT4_31 fill:#D5A8D5,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Tag-Discriminated Variant (Tagged Union Pattern) This is the **production-quality pattern** used in real ARM firmware to safely store one-of-N data variants. ```mermaid graph TB TOP["Tagged Variant: SensorReading"] TOP --> TAG["enum Tag { ANALOG, DIGITAL, ERROR } tag"] TOP --> VAL["union {
struct { int voltage; int current; } analog;
struct { int id; bool state; } digital;
int errorCode;
} value"] style TOP fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style TAG fill:#A8C5E8,stroke:#333 style VAL fill:#C8E8A8,stroke:#333 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The `tag` field tells the program **which member of the union is currently valid**. - The `value` union stores the actual data. - This is the pattern used to implement **safe variant types** in embedded C — equivalent to Rust's `enum` or Haskell's `Either`. ### 4.4 Diagram 4: Endianness Test via Union (Memory Byte Layout) ```mermaid graph LR VAL["et.i = 0x12345678"] VAL --> B0["Address +0: 0x78 (LSB)"] VAL --> B1["Address +1: 0x56"] VAL --> B2["Address +2: 0x34"] VAL --> B3["Address +3: 0x12 (MSB)"] B0 -.-> TEST["et.c[0] == 0x78 → Little-Endian"] style VAL fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333 style B0 fill:#FFA8A8,stroke:#333 style B1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style B2 fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333 style B3 fill:#A8C5E8,stroke:#333 style TEST fill:#C8E8A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.5 Diagram 5: Union Member Overlap — Conceptual View ```mermaid graph TB MEM["Union Storage Block (size = max member)"] MEM --> A["Member 1: int i → interpreted as int"] MEM --> B["Member 2: float f → interpreted as float"] MEM --> C["Member 3: char s[20] → interpreted as string"] style MEM fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A fill:#A8C5E8,stroke:#333 style B fill:#C8E8A8,stroke:#333 style C fill:#D5A8D5,stroke:#333 ``` **Interpretation:** All three arrows point to the **same memory block** — only the interpretation lens changes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *Define a union in C. How does it differ from a structure in terms of memory allocation?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **union** is a user-defined data type in C declared using the keyword `union`, in which all members **share the same memory location**. The size of a union is equal to the size of its **largest member**, whereas a structure allocates **separate, sequential memory** for each of its members. Therefore, in a union, only **one member can hold a meaningful value at a time**, while in a structure, all members can be active simultaneously. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *What is the output of the following C program? Justify your answer.* ```c #include union Test { int i; char c[4]; }; int main() { union Test t; t.i = 0x41424344; printf("%c %c %c %c\n", t.c[0], t.c[1], t.c[2], t.c[3]); return 0; } ``` *(Assume little-endian ARM target.)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Since the target is **little-endian** (standard ARM Cortex-M), the integer `0x41424344` is stored in memory as bytes `44 43 42 41` at offsets 0, 1, 2, 3 respectively. Reading through the `c[4]` lens gives characters `'D'`, `'C'`, `'B'`, `'A'`. **Output:** `D C B A` --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of a **union** in C with a suitable example. Discuss how memory is allocated to a union, and clearly distinguish it from a structure with a memory-layout diagram. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider the following union declaration on an ARM Cortex-M (32-bit, little-endian, 4-byte aligned) target. Compute the size of the union, draw its memory map, and explain the bit-field access pattern. ```c union Status { uint8_t raw8; uint32_t raw32; struct { uint32_t READY : 1; uint32_t ERROR : 1; uint32_t MODE : 2; uint32_t VECTOR : 4; uint32_t RESERVED: 24; } bits; }; ``` --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — Concept and Memory Layout (7 marks)** A **union** is a derived data type in C that allows storing different types of data in the **same memory location**. Declared with the keyword `union`, it differs from a `struct` in two key ways: 1. **Memory Allocation:** A union is allocated a single block of memory whose size equals that of its **largest member** (plus alignment padding). All members **start at the same offset (0)** from the base address. 2. **Active Member:** Only **one member** of a union can hold a valid value at a time. Writing to one member overwrites the bytes of any other. **Example:** ```c union Number { int i; float f; char s[8]; } n; ``` Here, `sizeof(n) = 8` bytes (size of `char[8]` is the largest). **Memory-Layout Diagram:** ```mermaid graph TB S["struct S { int i; float f; } — sizeof = 8 bytes"] S --> A["Offset 0..3: i (4 bytes)"] S --> B["Offset 4..7: f (4 bytes)"] U["union U { int i; float f; } — sizeof = 4 bytes"] U --> C["Offset 0..3: shared — i OR f (4 bytes)"] style S fill:#A8C5E8,stroke:#333 style U fill:#C8E8A8,stroke:#333 style A fill:#FFD580 style B fill:#FFD580 style C fill:#FFA8A8 ``` --- #### **Part (b) — Size, Memory Map, and Bit-Field Access (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Member sizes:** - `raw8`: 1 byte - `raw32`: 4 bytes - `bits` (anonymous struct of bit-fields): $\lceil (1+1+2+4+24)/8 \rceil = \lceil 32/8 \rceil = 4$ bytes **Step 2 — Largest member:** $$S_{\min} = \max(1, 4, 4) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Alignment padding (already multiple of 4):** $$P = 0 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Final size:** $$\boxed{\text{sizeof}(\text{Status}) = 4 \text{ bytes}}$$ **Memory Map (4 bytes, little-endian):** ```mermaid graph LR ADDR["Address Offset"] ADDR --> O0["+0: byte 0 (LSB)"] ADDR --> O1["+1: byte 1"] ADDR --> O2["+2: byte 2"] ADDR --> O3["+3: byte 3 (MSB)"] O0 --> B0["bits 0-7: READY(0), ERROR(1), MODE(2-3), VECTOR(4-7)"] O1 --> B1["bits 8-15: VECTOR continues (lower 4 bits)"] O2 --> B2["bits 16-23: RESERVED"] O3 --> B3["bits 24-31: RESERVED"] style ADDR fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Bit-Field Access Pattern:** ```c union Status s; s.raw32 = 0x00000043; // 0x43 = 0b0100_0011 // bit 0 (READY) = 1 // bit 1 (ERROR) = 1 // bits 2-3 (MODE) = 0 // bits 4-7 (VECTOR) = 4 (=0b0100) printf("READY=%u ERROR=%u MODE=%u VECTOR=%u\n", s.bits.READY, s.bits.ERROR, s.bits.MODE, s.bits.VECTOR); // Output: READY=1 ERROR=1 MODE=0 VECTOR=4 ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of **bit-fields in a union** as used in ARM Cortex-M firmware to access memory-mapped peripheral registers. Provide a general C code template for a 32-bit control register with at least three named bit-fields. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Write a C program using a **union** to detect whether the underlying ARM target is **little-endian or big-endian** at runtime. Explain the memory-byte layout of a 32-bit integer `0x12345678` on both endianness modes, and state which mode the standard ARM Cortex-M uses. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — Bit-Field Union Template for ARM SFRs (7 marks)** In ARM Cortex-M firmware, peripheral registers are **32-bit memory-mapped words**. Often the engineer needs to set or clear individual bits (e.g., enable a UART, configure a timer mode). Rather than manually computing bit masks, a **bit-field union** allows the same 32-bit word to be accessed either as a raw value or as a structured set of named fields. **Generic Template:** ```c typedef union { uint32_t RAW; // whole-register access struct { uint32_t FIELD_A : 8; // bits 0-7 uint32_t FIELD_B : 4; // bits 8-11 uint32_t FIELD_C : 4; // bits 12-15 uint32_t FIELD_D : 16; // bits 16-31 } BITS; } REG_t; ``` **Example — UART Control Register on LPC1768:** ```c typedef union { uint32_t RAW; struct { uint32_t UARTEN : 1; // bit 0 — UART enable uint32_t RESERVED0 : 7; // bits 1-7 uint32_t WLENGTH : 2; // bits 8-9 — word length uint32_t RESERVED1 : 22; // bits 10-31 } BITS; } U0LCR_t; #define U0LCR (*(volatile U0LCR_t *)0x4000C00C) U0LCR_t reg; reg.BITS.UARTEN = 1; // set the enable bit reg.BITS.WLENGTH = 3; // set 8-bit character length U0LCR.RAW = reg.RAW; // write to hardware register ``` **Advantages:** - **Readability** — field names replace magic bit masks. - **Atomicity** — single 32-bit write, no read-modify-write race. - **Self-documenting** — register map is visible in the C source. --- #### **Part (b) — Endianness Detection via Union (7 marks)** **C Program:** ```c #include #include union EndianProbe { uint32_t word; uint8_t bytes[4]; }; int main(void) { union EndianProbe p; p.word = 0x12345678; printf("Byte 0 = 0x%02X\n", p.bytes[0]); printf("Byte 1 = 0x%02X\n", p.bytes[1]); printf("Byte 2 = 0x%02X\n", p.bytes[2]); printf("Byte 3 = 0x%02X\n", p.bytes[3]); if (p.bytes[0] == 0x78) { printf("System is LITTLE-ENDIAN\n"); } else { printf("System is BIG-ENDIAN\n"); } return 0; } ``` **Memory Layout Comparison:** | Address Offset | Little-Endian (ARM Cortex-M) | Big-Endian (legacy ARM, network order) | |---|---|---| | +0 (lowest) | `0x78` (LSB) | `0x12` (MSB) | | +1 | `0x56` | `0x34` | | +2 | `0x34` | `0x56` | | +3 (highest) | `0x12` (MSB) | `0x78` (LSB) | **Memory Map Diagram:** ```mermaid graph LR LE["Little-Endian (ARM Cortex-M)"] LE --> A1["+0: 0x78"] LE --> A2["+1: 0x56"] LE --> A3["+2: 0x34"] LE --> A4["+3: 0x12"] BE["Big-Endian"] BE --> B1["+0: 0x12"] BE --> B2["+1: 0x34"] BE --> B3["+2: 0x56"] BE --> B4["+3: 0x78"] style LE fill:#C8E8A8,stroke:#333 style BE fill:#FFA8A8,stroke:#333 ``` **Conclusion:** On a **standard ARM Cortex-M3/M4** (e.g., LPC1768, STM32F4, Tiva-C), the byte at the lowest address is the **LSB (`0x78`)**, confirming **little-endian** operation. The union is the cleanest C idiom for runtime endianness detection. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting alignment padding** — `sizeof(union)` is *not* always `max(sizeof members)`; on 32-bit ARM, the union is rounded up to a 4-byte boundary. (–1 mark per occurrence.) > 2. **Confusing `struct` and `union` semantics** — stating that *all union members can be active simultaneously* is a 0-mark answer for the "difference" question. > 3. **Ignoring endianness in bit-field questions** — on ARM Cortex-M, multi-byte values are little-endian. Forgetting this in the `0x41424344` → "DCBA" question costs the byte-order mark. > 4. **Omitting the `volatile` qualifier** — when using a union to model memory-mapped SFRs, the variable must be `volatile` to prevent the compiler from optimizing out hardware reads/writes. Examiners often dock 1 mark for this. > 5. **Writing to one member and reading another without justification** — explain *type-punning* and acknowledge the strict-aliasing implication (in GCC on ARM, this is permitted). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Unions"** - **Definition:** A union is a C derived data type where **all members share the same memory location** (offset 0 from base). - **Size Rule:** $\text{sizeof}(union) = \max(\text{sizeof of all members}) + \text{alignment padding}$. - **Active Member Rule:** Only **one member** holds a valid value at a time; writing to one member overwrites others. - **Address Rule:** `&u == &u.member1 == &u.member2 == ...` (all equal). - **Keyword:** `union` (NOT `struct`). - **Member Access:** `.` for direct variables, `->` for pointers to union — same as struct. - **Anonymous Union:** Allowed under C11 / GNU extensions; common in ARM firmware headers. - **Bit-Field Union Pattern:** Standard idiom for ARM SFR (Special Function Register) access — combines `uint32_t` raw view with a bit-field struct view in one 32-bit word. - **Endianness Test:** The classic C trick for runtime endianness detection — relies on overlapping a `uint32_t` and a `uint8_t[4]` via union. - **Tagged Union (Variant Type):** Union + `enum` tag = type-safe variant storage; standard pattern in protocol parsers and command frames. - **ARM Cortex-M Default:** **Little-endian**, 32-bit word size, 4-byte natural alignment. - **Padding on 32-bit ARM:** Unions are padded to the next multiple of 4 bytes. - **Compiler Flag:** Use `__attribute__((packed))` (GCC) to disable padding only when absolutely necessary — uncommon for unions in ARM. - **Type-Punning:** Reading a union member of a different type than the one most recently written is *type-punning*; legal in C (and in GCC for ARM) but undefined under strict C99 aliasing. - **Distinguish from `struct` in 3 keywords:** *shared*, *overlap*, *largest-size*. - **Memory-Saving Advantage:** A union can reduce RAM footprint when only one variant of a payload is ever live — critical in resource-constrained embedded targets. - **Volatile for SFR:** Always declare SFR-mapped union pointers as `volatile` to ensure every access translates to a real load/store instruction.

topics.title: Review of Computer Organization: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Computer Organization: Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Computer Memory

In the context of computer organization, **Memory** refers to the fundamental hardware subsystem responsible for the **storage, retrieval, and management of binary data** (instructions and operands) required for the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to execute programs. Memory is organized as a linear array of addressable storage locations, where each location is uniquely identified by a binary address delivered via the **address bus**, with data transferred over the **data bus**, and read/write operations controlled by dedicated **control signals** (e.g., $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{CS}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** Memory is a sequential storage medium structured as an indexed array of *bytes* or *words*, where indexing is achieved through binary addressing. The CPU interacts with memory exclusively through three bus groups: **Address Bus** (unidirectional, specifies location), **Data Bus** (bidirectional, carries payload), and **Control Bus** (carries timing/command signals like *Memory Read*, *Memory Write*, and *IO/Memory Select*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Library of Alexandria (Revisited)

To build immediate intuition, let us map computer memory to a real-world **Post Office Postal System**:

- Imagine the **Post Office** is the **Memory Unit**.
- Every **P.O. Box number** is a unique **Memory Address** (e.g., Box 0x1A3F).
- Every **letter stored inside** a box is the **Data/Instruction** (8-bit byte, 16-bit half-word, 32-bit word).
- The **clerk who retrieves a box** upon request is the **Memory Controller / Address Decoder**.
- The **shelf where boxes are kept close to the clerk** (frequently used letters) is the **Cache Memory**.
- The **main warehouse in the basement** (all letters) is the **Primary/Main Memory (RAM)**.
- The **archival storage facility 50 km away** (rarely accessed historical records) is the **Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)**.

When you request a letter, the clerk first checks the nearby shelf (**Cache hit**). If not there (**Cache miss**), the clerk walks to the basement (**Main Memory access**). If still not found, a vehicle is dispatched to the archival facility (**Page fault, secondary storage access**). The faster, more expensive storage sits closest to the CPU — this is the **Memory Hierarchy Principle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Takeaway:** The fundamental goal of memory organization is to provide the CPU with the **illusion of a large, fast, and cheap memory** by intelligently managing a hierarchy of storage technologies with trade-offs between **speed (access time)**, **capacity**, and **cost per bit**.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Memory Parameters

Every memory technology in computer organization is characterized by three orthogonal engineering trade-offs:

1. **Access Time / Latency:** The time delay between the initiation of a memory request and the delivery of the data. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** for RAM, **microseconds ($\mu s$)** for Flash, and **milliseconds (ms)** for magnetic disks.
2. **Capacity / Density:** The total number of bits that can be stored. Measured in bits, Bytes, KB, MB, GB, or TB. Governed by the physical cell area of each technology.
3. **Cost per Bit:** The economic figure of merit, measured in **cents per megabit (¢/Mbit)** or **dollars per gigabyte (\$/GB)**.

| Parameter | Register | Cache (SRAM) | Main Memory (DRAM) | SSD (Flash) | HDD (Magnetic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Access Time | $\leq 1$ ns | 1–10 ns | 50–100 ns | 50–500 $\mu s$ | 5–15 ms |
| Capacity | Bytes (128 B) | KB–MB (32 KB) | GB (8 GB) | TB (1 TB) | TB (4 TB) |
| Cost / Bit | Very High | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Volatility | Volatile | Volatile | Volatile | Non-Volatile | Non-Volatile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatility** distinguishes memory that loses its contents upon power removal (**volatile**: SRAM, DRAM, Registers) from memory that retains data permanently (**non-volatile**: ROM, Flash, HDD, SSD). The CPU working area is volatile, while the boot firmware and long-term storage are non-volatile.

## 1.4 Classification of Computer Memory (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus partitions memory into two primary branches with a clear functional demarcation:

- **Primary Memory (Main Memory):** Directly addressable by the CPU, operates at processor speed (or with minimal wait states), holds the currently executing program and its data. Subdivided into **RAM (Random Access Memory)** — both **SRAM** and **DRAM** — and **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** family.
- **Secondary Memory (Auxiliary Storage):** Not directly addressable by the CPU; accessed via I/O controllers and DMA. Used for permanent mass storage. Examples: **Hard Disk Drives (HDD)**, **Solid State Drives (SSD)**, **Optical Discs**, and **Magnetic Tapes**.

A third operational layer — **Cache Memory** — sits between the CPU registers and primary memory, while **Virtual Memory** is a software-managed abstraction that uses secondary storage to simulate a larger primary memory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

Modern computer systems employ a **layered memory hierarchy** based on two empirical principles:

- **Principle of Locality (Temporal):** If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.
- **Principle of Locality (Spatial):** If a memory location is referenced, nearby locations are likely to be referenced next.

These principles justify placing smaller, faster memories near the CPU to act as buffers for the larger, slower memories below them. As we descend the hierarchy, **capacity increases, cost per bit decreases, and access time increases**.

### Layer-wise Operational Breakdown

**Layer 1 — CPU Registers (Internal to Processor Core):**
- The fastest storage elements, fabricated using the same CMOS process as the ALU.
- Access time is effectively **one clock cycle** (sub-nanosecond for GHz processors).
- Limited in number (typically 16–32 general-purpose registers in ARM, e.g., **R0–R12**, plus special registers **R13 (SP)**, **R14 (LR)**, **R15 (PC)**).
- Managed by the compiler (register allocation) and hardware (register renaming in out-of-order execution).
- No address is issued; registers are accessed by name/identifier embedded in the instruction.

**Layer 2 — Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3):**
- Built from **SRAM (Static RAM)** cells — typically 6 transistors per bit, requiring no refresh.
- L1 cache is **per-core** and split into **I-cache (instructions)** and **D-cache (data)** in modern ARM Cortex designs.
- L2 cache may be per-core or shared; L3 (if present) is usually shared across all cores.
- Operates on the principle of **block transfer (cache lines, typically 32–128 bytes)**: when one byte is requested, the entire line containing it is fetched from main memory.
- Managed transparently by hardware (the cache controller); the programmer is unaware of its existence except through performance measurements.

**Layer 3 — Main Memory (Primary Storage):**
- Implemented using **DRAM (Dynamic RAM)** — typically 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit.
- The capacitor leaks charge and must be **refreshed every ~64 ms**, hence the term "dynamic."
- Provides the **flat, linear address space** visible to the CPU.
- Connected to the CPU via a parallel bus (legacy) or high-speed serial point-to-point links (modern DDR4/DDR5 with on-die memory controllers).

**Layer 4 — Secondary Storage (Mass Storage):**
- Persistent storage holding the file system, applications, and data not currently in RAM.
- HDDs use **rotating magnetic platters** with a read/write head on an actuator arm; access involves **seek time + rotational latency + transfer time**.
- SSDs use **NAND Flash** memory organized into pages (4–16 KB) and blocks (256–512 pages); access is purely electronic but suffers from **write amplification** and **wear leveling**.

**Layer 5 — Tertiary / Archival Storage:**
- Magnetic tapes, optical media (Blu-ray), and cloud archives. Used for backup and disaster recovery; access times are in **seconds to minutes**.

## 2.2 RAM Technologies: SRAM vs. DRAM (Detailed Comparison)

The two dominant RAM technologies used in computer organization are SRAM and DRAM, each with distinct cell structures, performance characteristics, and applications.

### SRAM (Static RAM) — The 6T Cell

The classical SRAM cell uses **6 MOSFETs** arranged as two cross-coupled inverters (forming a bistable latch) with two access transistors. The cell holds its state as long as power is applied — **no refresh required**. Reading is non-destructive and fast. The cell area is large ($\approx 100 F^2$, where $F$ is the minimum feature size), making SRAM expensive and low-density.

### DRAM (Dynamic RAM) — The 1T1C Cell

The DRAM cell uses **1 transistor + 1 capacitor**. The charge stored on the capacitor represents one bit. Because the capacitor leaks charge through the access transistor's subthreshold leakage, the cell must be **periodically read and rewritten (refreshed)**. The cell area is small ($\approx 6 F^2$), enabling high density. However, reading a DRAM cell is **destructive** — the charge is shared with the sense amplifier, so the data must be immediately written back.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is DRAM used for main memory and SRAM for cache?** It is a direct consequence of the **density vs. speed trade-off**. A 1 GB main memory built from 6T SRAM cells would be physically enormous, power-hungry, and prohibitively expensive. The refresh overhead of DRAM is acceptable for main memory because the memory controller can pipeline refresh operations during otherwise idle bus cycles. Cache memory, however, must support **single-cycle, no-wait-state access** for the CPU, which DRAM cannot deliver.

| Feature | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Structure | 6 Transistors (6T) | 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor (1T1C) |
| Density | Low | High (4–8× denser) |
| Cost per Bit | High | Low (5–10× cheaper) |
| Access Time | 1–10 ns | 50–100 ns |
| Refresh | Not required | Required every ~64 ms |
| Power Consumption (Static) | Higher (continuous current) | Lower (in standby) |
| Typical Application | Cache, Registers | Main Memory |
| Read Operation | Non-destructive | Destructive (write-back needed) |

## 2.3 ROM Family (Non-Volatile Read-Only Memory)

ROM is a class of non-volatile memory used to store firmware and boot code. The KTU syllabus expects familiarity with the ROM evolution:

- **ROM (Mask ROM):** Data is physically encoded during chip fabrication using a custom mask. Cheapest per unit at high volume, but **cannot be updated**.
- **PROM (Programmable ROM):** Uses fusible links; the user can program it **once** using a PROM programmer that burns selected fuses. Cannot be erased.
- **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Uses a **floating-gate MOSFET** cell; erased by **exposing the chip to ultraviolet light** for 20–30 minutes through a quartz window. Can be reprogrammed multiple times.
- **EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):** Erased and reprogrammed **byte-by-byte** using electrical signals. Slower write cycles (ms) but highly convenient. Limited endurance ($\sim 10^6$ write cycles).
- **Flash Memory:** A descendant of EEPROM that erases in **blocks (sectors)** rather than byte-by-byte, enabling much faster bulk operations. Divided into **NOR Flash** (random access, used for BIOS/boot code) and **NAND Flash** (high density, sequential access, used for SSDs and memory cards).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula required for solving Module 1 memory problems in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination:

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = 2^n \times W$ | Total memory capacity with $n$ address lines and $W$-bit data width | Bits. $C$ in bits, $W$ in bits/word |
| 2 | $N = 2^n$ | Number of uniquely addressable memory locations | Locations. $n$ = address bus width |
| 3 | $M_{chips} = \left\lceil \dfrac{2^n \times W_{req}}{2^{n_i} \times W_i} \right\rceil$ | Number of memory chips needed to construct a larger memory | Integer (round up). $n$ = total addr, $W_{req}$ = desired word size, $n_i$ = per-chip addr, $W_i$ = per-chip data width |
| 4 | $A_{ratio} = \dfrac{2^{n_{req}}}{2^{n_i}}$ | Address ratio — how many chips are needed in parallel for one address group | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $T_{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$ | Average Memory Access Time (single-level cache) | $H$ = hit ratio, $T_{hit}$ = cache access time, $T_{miss}$ = main memory access time |
| 6 | $T_{AMAT,2} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot (T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{MM})$ | Two-level cache AMAT (sequential lookup) | Times in ns |
| 7 | $H_{overall} = 1 - (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2)$ | Combined hit rate for parallel (inclusive) caches | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $C_{effective} = 2^{n_{logical}}$ | Virtual/logical address space size | Bits/Bytes |
| 9 | $T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | Secondary storage access time decomposition | Milliseconds |
| 10 | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{B}{R}$ | Data transfer time, $B$ = bytes to transfer, $R$ = transfer rate | Seconds |
| 11 | $P_{avg} = H \cdot P_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot P_{miss}$ | Average power consumption of hierarchical memory | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** The ceiling operator $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is **mandatory** when calculating the number of memory chips. A student who writes 2.3 chips instead of 3 chips loses 1 mark. The vertical bar for absolute value must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid markdown table corruption.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Memory organization principles are not academic abstractions — they directly determine the performance of every computing device from a $5$ microcontroller to a $200$ million data center server:

- **Smartphone Performance:** The **LPDDR5** RAM in a modern smartphone is DRAM, but with aggressive low-power modes and wide I/O (16n prefetch) to balance energy efficiency with bandwidth.
- **Database Servers:** A database server's query throughput is bottlenecked by **cache hit rates**; engineers tune **page sizes, prefetch algorithms, and cache replacement policies** (LRU, LFU, ARC) to maximize the hit ratio $H$.
- **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M):** ARM Cortex-M0/M0+ microcontrollers have **no cache** and use **single-cycle SRAM** as the working memory because deterministic real-time response is more critical than throughput.
- **Operating Systems:** Virtual memory management (paging, segmentation) is the software layer that exploits the memory hierarchy to give each process the illusion of a private, contiguous address space.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Capacity from Address and Data Bus Widths

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):** A computer system has a **20-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. Calculate the total addressable memory in bytes.

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — Identify the number of addressable locations.**
The address bus has $n = 20$ bits. Each unique binary pattern on these 20 lines corresponds to one addressable memory location. The total number of locations is:

$$N = 2^n = 2^{20}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$$

Thus, there are $1{,}048{,}576$ distinct addressable locations.

**Step 3 — Identify the size of each location.**
The data bus width is $W = 16$ bits. By KTU convention, when the data bus width is 16 bits, each address points to a **half-word (16 bits)**, unless the problem explicitly specifies word boundaries.

**Step 4 — Compute total capacity in bits.**

$$C_{bits} = N \times W = 2^{20} \times 16 = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 16 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bytes.**
Since $1$ byte $= 8$ bits, we divide:

$$C_{bytes} = \dfrac{C_{bits}}{8} = \dfrac{16{,}777{,}216}{8} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 20-bit address bus with a 16-bit data bus can address **2 MB** of memory. If the data bus were 8 bits instead, the capacity would still be 2 MB in **byte-addressable** terminology, but the bus would require two consecutive reads to fetch a 16-bit word.

## 3.2 Derivation: Memory Chip Interleaving and Address Decoding

**Problem Statement:** Design a memory system with **64 KB of RAM** using **16 KB × 8-bit chips**. Calculate the number of chips required and the address decoding scheme.

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — Express the desired capacity in the same unit as the chip.**

$$C_{desired} = 64 \text{ KB} = 64 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}$$

$$C_{chip} = 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 131{,}072 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the address ratio (how many chips per "word group").**

$$A_{ratio} = \dfrac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 4$$

Four chips must operate in parallel to fill the 32-bit data bus (since each chip is 8 bits wide and 4 chips $\times$ 8 bits = 32 bits). Wait — re-evaluating: since each chip is $16 \text{ KB} \times 8 \text{ bit}$, and the desired word size is not specified, we assume **byte-wide organization**. In that case, the total number of chips needed is:

$$M_{chips} = \dfrac{C_{desired}}{C_{chip}} = \dfrac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the address lines required per chip.**

Each chip is $16 \text{ KB}$, so it has $16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384 = 2^{14}$ locations. Thus, each chip requires $n_i = 14$ address lines.

**Step 4 — Determine the total address lines required for the full memory.**

The full memory is $64 \text{ KB} = 2^{16}$ locations, so $n = 16$ address lines are required.

**Step 5 — Identify the high-order address lines used for chip select (decoding).**

The high-order lines beyond the 14 lines used by each chip are:

$$n_{select} = n - n_i = 16 - 14 = 2 \text{ lines (A}_{15}\text{, A}_{14}\text{)}$$

A **2-to-4 decoder** is used. The four outputs of the decoder activate the **Chip Select ($\overline{CS}$)** inputs of the four memory chips in a mutually exclusive manner. The resulting address map is:

| A₁₅ | A₁₄ | Selected Chip | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Chip 0 | 0000H – 3FFFH |
| 0 | 1 | Chip 1 | 4000H – 7FFFH |
| 1 | 0 | Chip 2 | 8000H – BFFFH |
| 1 | 1 | Chip 3 | C000H – FFFFH |

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with Two-Level Cache

**Problem Statement:** A computer has an **L1 cache** with hit rate $H_1 = 0.9$ and access time $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, and an **L2 cache** with hit rate $H_2 = 0.95$ and access time $T_{L2} = 10$ ns. Main memory access time is $T_{MM} = 100$ ns. Calculate the AMAT.

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — State the AMAT formula for a two-level cache hierarchy.**

The CPU first consults L1. On an L1 miss, it consults L2. On an L2 miss, it consults main memory. The hierarchical AMAT is:

$$T_{AMAT} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot \big[ T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{MM} \big]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the miss rates.**

$$1 - H_1 = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1$$
$$1 - H_2 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner bracket (L2 access + possible main memory access).**

$$T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{MM} = 10 + (0.05)(100) = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the L1 miss rate and add the L1 access time.**

$$T_{AMAT} = 1 + (0.1)(15) = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5 \text{ ns}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The AMAT is **2.5 ns**, only **1.5 ns longer than a single L1 access**, despite main memory being **100× slower**. This demonstrates the profound performance impact of even a moderate cache hit rate. If $H_1$ dropped to 0.5, AMAT would jump to $1 + 0.5 \times 15 = 8.5$ ns — a 3.4× slowdown.

## 3.4 Derivation: Cache Mapping — Set-Associative Address Partitioning

**Problem Statement:** A cache has **16 KB capacity**, **64-byte line size**, and is **4-way set-associative**. The CPU issues a **32-bit address**. Determine the sizes of the **Tag**, **Set Index**, and **Block Offset** fields.

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — Compute the block offset size.**
The line size (block size) is $B = 64$ bytes $= 2^6$ bytes. Byte addressing requires $\log_2(B) = 6$ bits to uniquely select a byte within a block. Hence:

$$\text{Block Offset} = 6 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of sets in the cache.**
A 4-way set-associative cache partitions its lines into sets, with 4 lines per set. The number of lines in the cache is:

$$L = \dfrac{C}{B} = \dfrac{16{,}384}{64} = 256 \text{ lines}$$

The number of sets is:

$$S = \dfrac{L}{\text{ways}} = \dfrac{256}{4} = 64 \text{ sets} = 2^6 \text{ sets}$$

Hence:

$$\text{Set Index} = \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tag size.**
The address is 32 bits total. The fields must sum to 32:

$$\text{Tag} = 32 - \text{Set Index} - \text{Block Offset} = 32 - 6 - 6 = 20 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The 32-bit physical address partitions as: **Tag (20 bits) | Set Index (6 bits) | Block Offset (6 bits)**. Each cache line stores 20 tag bits + a valid bit + a dirty bit + 64 bytes of data. This 4-way associative organization provides 4 candidate slots per set, allowing hardware to choose which line to evict (typically via LRU policy), dramatically reducing conflict misses compared to direct-mapped caches.

## 3.5 Derivation: Secondary Storage Access Time

**Problem Statement:** A hard disk has an **average seek time of 8 ms**, a **rotational speed of 7200 RPM**, and a **transfer rate of 100 MB/s**. Calculate the total access time to read a **4 KB file**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — Compute the average rotational latency.**
One full rotation takes $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{7200} = 8.33$ ms. On average, the read head must wait **half a rotation** for the desired sector to arrive under the head:

$$T_{rot,avg} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{8.33}{2} = 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the data transfer time.**

$$T_{transfer} = \dfrac{B}{R} = \dfrac{4 \text{ KB}}{100 \text{ MB/s}} = \dfrac{4 \times 1024 \text{ B}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ B/s}} = \dfrac{4096}{10^8} = 40.96 \text{ \mu s} \approx 0.041 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all components.**

$$T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rot,avg} + T_{transfer} = 8 + 4.17 + 0.041 = 12.211 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** For small files, the **transfer time is negligible** compared to the mechanical seek and rotational delays. This is why operating systems aggressively cache file system metadata and why SSDs (which eliminate mechanical delays) provide such dramatic speedups for small random reads.
