# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Top-Down View)

This Mermaid block diagram renders the classical memory pyramid with annotated access time, capacity, and cost per bit for each layer, mapping the trade-off continuum that defines modern computer organization.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef ultraFast fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef fast fill:#FFA500,stroke:#CC5500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef medium fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef slow fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef verySlow fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    reg["CPU REGISTERS<br/>1 ns, Bytes, Very High Cost/Bit"]:::ultraFast
    l1["L1 CACHE SRAM<br/>2 ns, 32-64 KB, High Cost/Bit"]:::fast
    l2["L2 CACHE SRAM<br/>10 ns, 256 KB-1 MB, Moderate-High Cost"]:::fast
    l3["L3 CACHE SRAM<br/>20-30 ns, 4-32 MB, Moderate Cost"]:::medium
    ram["MAIN MEMORY DRAM<br/>100 ns, 8-32 GB, Moderate Cost"]:::medium
    ssd["SECONDARY SSD NAND FLASH<br/>100 us, 1-4 TB, Low Cost"]:::slow
    hdd["TERTIARY HDD MAGNETIC<br/>10 ms, 4-16 TB, Very Low Cost"]:::verySlow

    reg ==> l1
    l1 ==> l2
    l2 ==> l3
    l3 ==> ram
    ram ==> ssd
    ssd ==> hdd

    noteA["ACCESS TIME INCREASES DOWNWARD"] -.-> ram
    noteB["CAPACITY AND COST EFFICIENCY INCREASE DOWNWARD"] -.-> hdd
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — CPU–Memory Interface (Bus Architecture)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the physical connection between the CPU and the memory subsystem through the three bus groups, showing the role of the address decoder in enabling chip selection.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef cpuStyle fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef busStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef memStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    cpu["CPU CORE<br/>ALU + REGISTERS"]:::cpuStyle

    addrBus["ADDRESS BUS<br/>20-32 bits, Unidirectional"]:::busStyle
    dataBus["DATA BUS<br/>8-64 bits, Bidirectional"]:::busStyle
    ctrlBus["CONTROL BUS<br/>RD, WR, CS, IO M"]:::busStyle

    decoder["ADDRESS DECODER<br/>2-to-4 or 3-to-8"]:::decStyle

    mem0["RAM CHIP 0<br/>16 KB"]:::memStyle
    mem1["RAM CHIP 1<br/>16 KB"]:::memStyle
    mem2["RAM CHIP 2<br/>16 KB"]:::memStyle
    mem3["ROM CHIP<br/>16 KB Firmware"]:::memStyle

    cpu -->|A0 to An| addrBus
    cpu -->|D0 to Dw| dataBus
    cpu -->|Signals| ctrlBus

    addrBus --> decoder
    decoder -->|CS 0| mem0
    decoder -->|CS 1| mem1
    decoder -->|CS 2| mem2
    decoder -->|CS 3| mem3

    mem0 <-->|Read Write| dataBus
    mem1 <-->|Read Write| dataBus
    mem2 <-->|Read Write| dataBus
    mem3 <-->|Read Write| dataBus

    ctrlBus -->|RD WR| mem0
    ctrlBus -->|RD WR| mem1
    ctrlBus -->|RD WR| mem2
    ctrlBus -->|RD WR| mem3
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Set-Associative Cache Mapping (4-Way Example)

This Mermaid block diagram shows how a physical address is partitioned into Tag, Set Index, and Block Offset fields, and how a 4-way set-associative cache uses the Set Index to select one of 64 sets, each containing 4 cache lines that are searched in parallel using the Tag.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef addrStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef setStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef lineStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    physicalAddr["32-BIT PHYSICAL ADDRESS"]:::addrStyle
    tag["TAG FIELD<br/>20 bits"]:::addrStyle
    setIdx["SET INDEX<br/>6 bits"]:::addrStyle
    offset["BLOCK OFFSET<br/>6 bits"]:::addrStyle

    physicalAddr --> tag
    physicalAddr --> setIdx
    physicalAddr --> offset

    setDecoder["6-to-64 SET DECODER"]:::setStyle
    setIdx --> setDecoder

    way0["WAY 0<br/>Tag 0, Valid, Data 64B"]:::lineStyle
    way1["WAY 1<br/>Tag 1, Valid, Data 64B"]:::lineStyle
    way2["WAY 2<br/>Tag 2, Valid, Data 64B"]:::lineStyle
    way3["WAY 3<br/>Tag 3, Valid, Data 64B"]:::lineStyle

    setDecoder -->|Enable| way0
    setDecoder -->|Enable| way1
    setDecoder -->|Enable| way2
    setDecoder -->|Enable| way3

    comparator{"4-WAY TAG COMPARATOR<br/>Match Outputs"}:::setStyle
    tag --> comparator
    way0 --> comparator
    way1 --> comparator
    way2 --> comparator
    way3 --> comparator

    output["OUTPUT: 64-BYTE CACHE LINE<br/>Byte selected by 6-bit offset"]:::setStyle
    comparator -->|Hit, select way| output
    offset --> output
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** In a 4-way set-associative cache, the hardware performs **4 parallel tag comparisons** within the selected set. Only one way can be selected (or zero, on a miss). This parallel search costs more transistors and power than a direct-mapped cache but dramatically reduces **conflict misses** — a situation where two frequently used memory blocks map to the same cache line in a direct-mapped design, causing constant eviction.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Virtual Memory Paging Mechanism

This Mermaid flowchart illustrates the translation of a virtual (logical) address into a physical address through the page table, including the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) acceleration.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef procStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef tblStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef memStyle fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hitStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    cpu["CPU GENERATES VIRTUAL ADDRESS<br/>VPN plus Page Offset"]:::procStyle
    tlb{"TLB LOOKUP<br/>Hardware Cache of Page Table"}:::tblStyle

    tlbHit["TLB HIT<br/>Return Physical Frame Number"]:::hitStyle
    tlbMiss["TLB MISS<br/>Access Main Page Table in RAM"]:::tblStyle
    pageTable["PAGE TABLE WALK<br/>OS Maintained Structure"]:::memStyle
    frameFound["PHYSICAL FRAME NUMBER RETRIEVED"]:::hitStyle
    pageFault["PAGE FAULT<br/>Frame not in RAM, Disk I/O"]:::procStyle
    diskAccess["SWAP TO DISK<br/>OS Loads Page from Secondary Storage"]:::memStyle

    physicalAddr["PHYSICAL ADDRESS<br/>PFN plus Page Offset"]:::hitStyle
    dataOut["DATA FETCHED FROM PHYSICAL MEMORY"]:::hitStyle

    cpu --> tlb
    tlb -->|Hit| tlbHit
    tlb -->|Miss| tlbMiss
    tlbMiss --> pageTable
    pageTable -->|Found| frameFound
    pageTable -->|Not Found| pageFault
    pageFault --> diskAccess
    diskAccess --> frameFound

    tlbHit --> physicalAddr
    frameFound --> physicalAddr
    physicalAddr --> dataOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Virtual Memory?** Virtual memory allows the operating system to give each process a **private, contiguous 32-bit (4 GB) or 64-bit address space** even if physical RAM is much smaller. Inactive pages are "swapped out" to disk, and the TLB (a hardware cache of recent translations) accelerates the frequent case where the translation is already known.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to cell structure, refresh requirement, and typical application.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | 6 transistors (6T) forming a cross-coupled latch (1 mark) | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor (1T1C) |
| **Refresh** | Not required — bistable latch holds state as long as power is applied | Required every ~64 ms because the capacitor leaks charge (0.5 mark) |
| **Application** | Used in **cache memory and CPU registers** where speed is critical (0.5 mark) | Used in **main memory** where high density and low cost per bit are required |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the memory hierarchy in computer systems? List any two levels in descending order of access time.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
The memory hierarchy is a layered organization of storage technologies designed to provide the CPU with the **illusion of a large, fast, and cheap memory** (1 mark). It exploits the **principle of locality** (temporal and spatial) to keep frequently accessed data in faster, smaller memories close to the CPU, while less-frequently used data resides in slower, larger, cheaper memories further away (1 mark).

Two levels in descending order of access time: **Registers (fastest) → Cache (SRAM) → Main Memory (DRAM) → Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)** — slowest (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts). The two alternatives below are mutually exclusive — pick one.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A computer system uses a **24-bit address bus** and a **32-bit data bus**. Calculate:
   (i) The total addressable memory in bytes.
   (ii) The number of addressable words (where one word = 32 bits).
   (iii) The number of addressable half-words (where one half-word = 16 bits).

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **three bus groups** (address, data, control) used for CPU–memory communication. List the typical direction (unidirectional/bidirectional) for each bus group.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **memory hierarchy** in computer organization with a neat block diagram. Discuss the trade-off between access time, capacity, and cost per bit at each level.

**(b)** A computer has a **single-level cache** with a **hit ratio of 0.85** and a **cache access time of 5 ns**. The **main memory access time is 80 ns**. Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. What will be the AMAT if the hit ratio drops to 0.7?

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A (14 Marks)

### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks

**(i) Total addressable memory in bytes (2 marks):**

The number of uniquely addressable locations is:

$$N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}$$

With a 32-bit (4-byte) data bus, each address points to a 32-bit word. The number of addressable **bytes** is the total number of memory locations × bytes per location, but this depends on byte-addressable versus word-addressable interpretation. Assuming **byte-addressable** memory (the KTU standard convention):

$$C = 2^{24} \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) Number of addressable words (3 marks):**

One word = 32 bits = 4 bytes. The number of words is:

$$N_{words} = \dfrac{2^{24}}{4} = \dfrac{16{,}777{,}216}{4} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ words} = 4 \text{ Mwords}$$

**(iii) Number of addressable half-words (2 marks):**

One half-word = 16 bits = 2 bytes. The number of half-words is:

$$N_{half} = \dfrac{2^{24}}{2} = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ half-words} = 8 \text{ Mhalf-words}$$

---

### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks

**The three bus groups are:**

| Bus Group | Function | Direction | Typical Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory address of the location to be accessed. The CPU places the address; memory decodes it. (2 marks) | **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory) (0.5 mark) | 20–32 bits in older systems, 64 bits in modern systems |
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data being read from or written to memory. Bidirectional because data flows in both directions during reads and writes. (2 marks) | **Bidirectional** (CPU ↔ Memory) (0.5 mark) | 8, 16, 32, or 64 bits |
| **Control Bus** | Carries timing and command signals such as **$\overline{RD}$ (Read)**, **$\overline{WR}$ (Write)**, **$\overline{CS}$ (Chip Select)**, **M/$\overline{IO}$** (Memory or I/O indicator), and clock/ready signals. (1.5 marks) | Mixed (mostly CPU → Memory, but some lines like READY are Memory → CPU) (0.5 mark) | Typically 4–10 lines |

**Diagram:** A neat block diagram showing CPU on the left, three bus groups as parallel arrows, and memory on the right, with labels for $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, and $\overline{CS}$ on the control bus. (Full mark for clean labeled diagram — examiner's discretion)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common 2-Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students frequently write that the data bus is "unidirectional" or fail to specify direction. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 2. Students often confuse the **address bus direction** — it is **strictly unidirectional** from CPU to memory; the memory never drives the address bus.
> 3. Students forget to include **M/$\overline{IO}$** or **Bus Request (BR)** / **Bus Grant (BG)** signals in the control bus description. For a 7-mark question, **at least 4 control signals** must be named.
> 4. Students draw the data bus as a single line instead of multiple parallel lines ($D_0$ to $D_{n-1}$). A single-line data bus diagram is **incomplete**.

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B (14 Marks)

### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks

**Memory Hierarchy Description (5 marks):**

The memory hierarchy is a structured arrangement of storage elements ordered by access time, capacity, and cost. From top (closest to CPU) to bottom:

1. **Registers (0.5 mark):** Inside the CPU, fastest access (~1 ns), smallest capacity (tens of bytes), highest cost/bit.
2. **Cache Memory — L1, L2, L3 (1.5 marks):** Built from SRAM. L1 is per-core, smallest and fastest; L3 is shared, larger and slower. Total cache capacity: KB to tens of MB.
3. **Main Memory (1 mark):** Built from DRAM. Capacity: GB. Access time: 50–100 ns. Volatile.
4. **Secondary Storage (1 mark):** HDD or SSD. Capacity: TB. Access time: $\mu s$ (SSD) to ms (HDD). Non-volatile.
5. **Tertiary/Archival (0.5 mark):** Tape, optical, cloud. Largest capacity, slowest access.

**Trade-off Explanation (2 marks):**
- **Access time increases** as we go down the hierarchy (1 ns → ms).
- **Capacity increases** as we go down (bytes → TB).
- **Cost per bit decreases** as we go down (registers are most expensive per bit, magnetic tape is cheapest).

**Block Diagram (0.5 mark awarded for clean diagram):** A pyramid/trapezoid shape with labels for each level.

---

### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Hit ratio $H_1 = 0.85$
- Cache access time $T_{cache} = 5$ ns
- Main memory access time $T_{MM} = 80$ ns

**Formula:**

$$T_{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{MM}$$

**Case 1: Hit ratio = 0.85 (3 marks):**

$$T_{AMAT} = (0.85)(5) + (1 - 0.85)(80)$$
$$= 4.25 + (0.15)(80)$$
$$= 4.25 + 12 = 16.25 \text{ ns}$$

**Case 2: Hit ratio drops to 0.7 (3 marks):**

$$T_{AMAT} = (0.7)(5) + (1 - 0.7)(80)$$
$$= 3.5 + (0.3)(80)$$
$$= 3.5 + 24 = 27.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Analysis (1 mark):**
A 15 percentage point drop in hit ratio (from 85% to 70%) caused the AMAT to increase by **11.25 ns** — a **69% performance degradation**. This quantifies why compiler and hardware optimizations that improve cache locality (e.g., loop tiling, blocking) have such dramatic real-world impact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common 2-Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply the hit time by the hit ratio** — writing $T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{MM}$ instead of $H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{MM}$. The correct formula weights **both** the hit and miss terms.
> 2. **Unit errors** — writing "27.5" without "ns" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Not interpreting the result** — for a "What will be the AMAT if..." question, failing to comment on the **performance impact** loses the final interpretation mark.
> 4. **Decimal arithmetic mistakes** — $0.15 \times 80$ is sometimes written as $1.2$ or $120$. Re-check: $0.15 \times 80 = 12$ exactly.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall. Each item is a guaranteed KTU 2024 question trigger.**

- [ ] **Memory** is a sequential array of addressable locations addressed by the CPU via the **address bus**, with data transferred over the **data bus**, controlled by the **control bus**.
- [ ] **Three bus groups:** Address (unidirectional, CPU → Memory), Data (bidirectional), Control (mixed; includes $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{CS}$, M/$\overline{IO}$).
- [ ] **Memory capacity formula:** $C = 2^n \times W$ bits, where $n$ = address bus width, $W$ = data bus width in bits.
- [ ] **Memory hierarchy principle:** Smaller, faster, costlier storage near the CPU; larger, slower, cheaper storage further away.
- [ ] **SRAM** uses a **6T cell**, no refresh, used for **cache**. **DRAM** uses a **1T1C cell**, requires **refresh every ~64 ms**, used for **main memory**.
- [ ] **DRAM read is destructive** — the data must be written back immediately after sensing.
- [ ] **ROM family evolution (instructor's favorite):** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM (UV erase) → EEPROM (electrical byte erase) → **Flash (block erase)**.
- [ ] **AMAT formula:** $T_{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$.
- [ ] **Two-level AMAT:** $T_{AMAT} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot [T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{MM}]$.
- [ ] **Cache address partitioning:** Address = **Tag** + **Set Index** + **Block Offset**. Sum of bit widths = physical address width.
- [ ] **Volatile vs. Non-Volatile:** RAM and cache are **volatile**; ROM, Flash, HDD, SSD are **non-volatile**.
- [ ] **Primary vs. Secondary:** Primary is **directly CPU-addressable**; Secondary requires **I/O controllers / DMA**.
- [ ] **Number of memory chips** for memory expansion: $M = \lceil C_{desired} / C_{chip} \rceil$. Always **round up** to the nearest integer.
- [ ] **Address decoding** uses high-order address lines through a **2-to-4** or **3-to-8 decoder** to generate mutually exclusive **Chip Select ($\overline{CS}$)** signals.
- [ ] **HDD access time:** $T_{total} = T_{seek} + T_{rot,avg} + T_{transfer}$, where $T_{rot,avg} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM}$ seconds.
- [ ] **Principle of Locality:** **Temporal** (recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again) + **Spatial** (items near a recently accessed address are likely to be accessed next).
- [ ] **Virtual memory** allows execution of programs larger than physical RAM by **paging inactive pages to disk**; the **TLB** accelerates address translation.
- [ ] **NOR Flash** = random access, used for boot code. **NAND Flash** = high density, sequential, used for SSDs.
- [ ] For **byte-addressable** systems: $C_{bytes} = 2^n$ where $n$ = address lines. For **word-addressable** systems: $C_{words} = 2^n$ and $C_{bytes} = 2^n \times W$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Arm Cortex M Architecture: Internal Organization - General Purpose and Special Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Arm Cortex M Architecture: Internal Organization - General Purpose and Special Registers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — ARM Cortex-M Architecture

The **ARM Cortex-M** is a family of **32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** processor cores designed by Arm Holdings specifically for **microcontroller-class applications**. It implements the **ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0/M0+/M1)**, **ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3/M4/M7)**, or **ARMv8-M (Cortex-M23/M33)** architecture profiles. The "M" stands for **Microcontroller**, distinguishing it from the "A" (Application) and "R" (Real-time) profile families.

The internal organization of a Cortex-M processor is built around a **load-store architecture**, a **register bank of 16 core 32-bit registers (R0–R15)**, a set of **special-purpose registers** (Program Status Registers, interrupt mask registers, and the CONTROL register), a **Thumb-2 instruction set** decoder, and a tightly integrated **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The internal organization refers to the **programmer's model**, i.e., the set of registers and operating modes visible to the software developer. Mastering this is mandatory for assembly programming, interrupt handling, and RTOS porting.

### 1.2 The Cortex-M Family at a Glance

| Core | Architecture | Pipeline | Key Feature |
|------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| Cortex-M0 / M0+ | ARMv6-M | 2 / 3 stage | Ultra-low power, smallest area |
| Cortex-M3 | ARMv7-M | 3 stage | Full Thumb-2, hardware divide |
| Cortex-M4 | ARMv7-M | 3 stage | Adds DSP + single-precision FPU |
| Cortex-M7 | ARMv7-M | 6 stage, dual-issue | High performance, optional double-precision FPU |
| Cortex-M33 | ARMv8-M | 2 stage | TrustZone-M security extension |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cortex-M as a Factory Floor

Imagine a Cortex-M processor as a **highly automated factory assembly line**:

- The **Register File (R0–R12)** is the worker's **tool belt and hands** — 13 general-purpose tools they can freely pick up and use for any task (math, moving data, address calculation).
- **R13 (Stack Pointer / SP)** is the factory's **inbox tray** and **outbox tray** — the SP always points to the *top* of the stack, where temporary notes (return addresses, saved variables) are stored.
- **R14 (Link Register / LR)** is the **"last task ticket"** the worker holds — when they finish a job and need to return to a supervisor, the LR remembers *where to go back*.
- **R15 (Program Counter / PC)** is the **instruction pointer on the conveyor belt** — it always shows which instruction is being worked on *right now*.
- The **Program Status Registers (xPSR)** are the **worker's mood & status board** — showing flags like "is the result zero?" or "is there an interrupt pending?".
- **PRIMASK / FAULTMASK / BASEPRI** are the **ear defenders and safety gates** — they let the worker ignore distractions (interrupts) of certain priorities.
- The **CONTROL register** is the **worker's access badge** — deciding whether the worker uses the *main inbox* (MSP) or the *personal inbox* (PSP), and whether they are allowed in the *manager's office* (privileged mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The Cortex-M programmer's model is intentionally **simple and orthogonal** — every register is 32 bits, and most instructions can use any general-purpose register. This is what makes Cortex-M code small, fast, and easy to compile.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Register Bank (Programmer's Model)

The Cortex-M processor contains **sixteen 32-bit registers** visible to the software. They are divided into two groups:

#### A. Low Registers (R0 – R7)
- **Accessible by any 16-bit Thumb instruction.**
- Used for passing function arguments and returning results.
- All general-purpose (no hardware dedicated function).

#### B. High Registers (R8 – R12)
- Accessible only by **32-bit Thumb-2 instructions**.
- R8–R12 are fully general-purpose.
- **R8, R9, R10, R11** have *no* dedicated hardware function in the base architecture but are often used by assemblers as scratch registers (e.g., `FIQ` in some ARMv7-A profiles — *not* on Cortex-M).

#### C. Stack Pointer — R13 (SP)
- **R13 is NOT a general-purpose register** in the strict sense; it is banked into two physical registers:
  - **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** — used in Handler mode and Thread mode with privileged access by default.
  - **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** — used in Thread mode for user (unprivileged) applications; typically used by an RTOS for per-thread stacks.
- **R13 reads/writes** access the *currently selected* SP, determined by the **CONTROL[1]** bit.
- **SP is always 32-bit** and word-aligned (the LSB is ignored on write; the architecture guarantees 4-byte alignment).
- The **stack grows downward (descending)** — pushes decrement SP, pops increment SP.

#### D. Link Register — R14 (LR)
- Stores the **return address** after a Branch-and-Link (`BL`) or Branch-with-Link-exchange (`BLX`).
- On exception entry, the hardware automatically pushes the return value into LR — this value is a special **EXC_RETURN** code (e.g., `0xFFFF_FFFD`), not a real address. On exception return, the value is popped from the stack and the processor branches to it.

#### E. Program Counter — R15 (PC)
- Always points to the **address of the next instruction to be fetched** (i.e., current address + 4 in Thumb-2).
- **R15 is read/write** but writes to PC are treated as branch instructions (e.g., `MOV PC, LR`).
- Bit [0] of the PC must always be **1** in Thumb state; forcing it to 0 causes a fault.

### 2.2 Special Registers

Beyond R0–R15, the Cortex-M contains a set of **special registers** that are accessed via special instructions (MRS, MSR, CPS, etc.). They are not memory-mapped.

#### 2.2.1 Program Status Registers (xPSR)
The 32-bit xPSR is a *logical union* of three sub-registers, accessed individually or together:

**a) APSR — Application Program Status Register (flags used by arithmetic/logic)**
| Bit | Name | Meaning |
|-----|------|---------|
| [31] | **N** | Negative flag — set if result MSB = 1 |
| [30] | **Z** | Zero flag — set if result = 0 |
| [29] | **C** | Carry flag — set on unsigned overflow / borrow NOT |
| [28] | **V** | Overflow flag — set on signed overflow |
| [27] | **Q** | Sticky saturation flag (DSP only) |
| [5:0] | GE | Greater-than-or-Equal flags (DSP only, M4/M7) |

**b) IPSR — Interrupt Program Status Register**
- Bits [5:0] hold the **Exception Number** of the currently executing ISR. `0` = Thread mode (no exception).

**c) EPSR — Execution Program Status Register**
- Bit [24] = **T** (Thumb state bit — always 1 on Cortex-M).
- Bits [15:10] = **ICI/IT** — used for *If-Then* (IT) block and *Interruptible-Continuable Instructions* (ICI) for multi-register loads/stores (LDM/STM).

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the full 32-bit xPSR is done with `MRS r0, XPSR` — but you can also access only the APSR (`MRS r0, APSR`), the IPSR, or the EPSR individually.

#### 2.2.2 Interrupt Mask Registers (PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI)

These three registers let software **mask (disable) exceptions and interrupts** in a controlled, priority-aware way.

| Register | Width | Effect When Set | Accessed By |
|----------|-------|-----------------|-------------|
| **PRIMASK** | 1 bit | Blocks **all exceptions** with configurable priority (i.e., masks all interrupts) | `CPSIE I` / `CPSID I` |
| **FAULTMASK** | 1 bit | Blocks all exceptions **except NMI**; also raises execution priority to -1 (HardFault level) | `CPSIE F` / `CPSID F` |
| **BASEPRI** | 8 bits | Blocks all exceptions with priority **numerically ≥** the stored value (i.e., lower or equal logical priority) | `MSR BASEPRI, r0` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BASEPRI is the most "graceful" masking mechanism** — it lets you block low-priority interrupts while still allowing higher-priority ones to preempt. KTU exam questions frequently ask why BASEPRI is preferred over PRIMASK in RTOS designs.

#### 2.2.3 CONTROL Register
A single 8-bit (only 2 bits defined) register that controls operating mode:

| Bit | Name | Meaning |
|-----|------|---------|
| [0] | **nPRIV** | 0 = Privileged, 1 = Unprivileged (Thread mode only) |
| [1] | **SPSEL** | 0 = Use MSP, 1 = Use PSP (Thread mode only) |
| [2] | **FPCA** | Floating-point context active (M4F/M7F/M33 with FPU only) |

> [!WARNING]
> In **Handler mode**, the processor is *always* privileged and *always* uses the MSP — the CONTROL register settings are ignored.

### 2.3 Operating Modes and Privilege Levels

The Cortex-M supports two operating modes and two privilege levels:

| Mode | Privilege | Stack Used | Typical Use |
|------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| Thread (unprivileged) | User | PSP | Application thread in RTOS |
| Thread (privileged) | Privileged | MSP (default) or PSP | Bare-metal main loop / kernel |
| Handler | Privileged (always) | MSP (always) | ISR / exception handler |

Transitions:
- **Reset** → Thread mode, privileged, MSP.
- **Exception entry** → Handler mode automatically.
- **Exception exit** → back to original Thread mode privilege/PSP state (saved in stack frame).
- **Software can switch** between MSP and PSP via CONTROL[1], and between privileged/unprivileged via CONTROL[0], but **unprivileged code can NOT change CONTROL** — only privileged code can.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| Stack growth | `SP_new = SP_old - 4` (PUSH), `SP_new = SP_old + 4` (POP) | 32-bit aligned always |
| Stack alignment requirement | `SP ≡ 0 (mod 8)` at public function entry | AAPCS standard |
| PC value during execution | `PC = current_insn_address + 4` | Pipeline fetch ahead |
| PC LSB rule | `PC[0] = 1` always (Thumb state) | Clearing causes INVSTATE fault |
| APSR – Negative | `N = result[31]` | Two's-complement sign bit |
| APSR – Zero | `Z = 1` iff `result == 0` | |
| APSR – Carry (add) | `C = 1` if `unsigned carry out` | |
| APSR – Carry (sub) | `C = NOT(borrow)` | |
| APSR – Overflow | `V = carry_into_MSB XOR carry_out_of_MSB` | Signed overflow detector |
| BASEPRI mask condition | Blocked iff `priority_field ≥ BASEPRI_value` | Lower numerical = higher logical priority |
| xPSR composite | `xPSR = APSR \vert IPSR \vert EPSR` (logical OR) | Same 32-bit register |
| EXC_RETURN | `0xFFFF_FFx0..0xF` | Encodes return mode & stack |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bare-metal firmware**: Programmer uses R0–R12 freely; LR auto-tracks function returns.
- **RTOS porting (e.g., FreeRTOS)**: Each task gets its own PSP — context switch saves R4–R11 onto PSP, sets PSP for next task, exception-returns into the new task. CONTROL[1] = 1 for tasks.
- **Interrupt latency reduction**: BASEPRI is used to implement *critical sections* without disabling all interrupts — preserving hard-real-time response.
- **DSP routines (Cortex-M4)**: GE flags + APSR.Q flag give hardware-saturation arithmetic feedback.
- **Security (Cortex-M23/M33)**: The CONTROL-based privilege model is the foundation of TrustZone-M sandboxing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stack Pointer Decrement on PUSH

The Cortex-M uses a **full-descending (FD) stack**. This means the SP always points to the *last item pushed*, and the stack grows *toward lower addresses*. Every push of a 32-bit register first decrements SP by 4, then writes to the new SP location.

> Given: SP initially = `0x2000_1000` (a valid RAM address, 4-byte aligned, 8-byte aligned per AAPCS).
> We execute `PUSH {R4, R5, R6, R7}` — pushing 4 registers in *descending register order* (highest register stored at lowest address).

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{initial}} = \mathtt{0x20001000}
$$

$$
\text{Registers pushed} = 4 \implies \text{stack grows by} = 4 \times 4\,\text{bytes} = 16\,\text{bytes}
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{final}} = \text{SP}_{\text{initial}} - 16
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{final}} = \mathtt{0x20001000} - \mathtt{0x10} = \mathtt{0x20000FF0}
$$

**Memory layout after PUSH (highest-numbered register at lowest address):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address} \quad & \quad \text{Content} \\
\mathtt{0x20000FF0} \quad & \leftarrow R7 \\
\mathtt{0x20000FF4} \quad & \leftarrow R6 \\
\mathtt{0x20000FF8} \quad & \leftarrow R5 \\
\mathtt{0x20000FFC} \quad & \leftarrow R4 \\
\mathtt{0x20001000} \quad & \leftarrow \text{(old top of stack)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:**
- Step 1: Cortex-M reads the register list and counts registers (here, 4).
- Step 2: It decrements SP by 4×count = 16 bytes.
- Step 3: It writes each register to the new stack region, **lowest-numbered register at the highest address** (Full Descending + Empty Ascending semantics defined in ARM ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual §A2.3.1).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing APSR Flags After an ADD Instruction

> Given: Execute `ADDS R0, R1, R2` where `R1 = 0x7FFF_FFFF` and `R2 = 0x0000_0001`.
> Find the resulting APSR flag values (N, Z, C, V).

**Step 1 — Perform the addition:**

$$
\mathtt{0x7FFFFFFF} + \mathtt{0x00000001} = \mathtt{0x80000000}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Zero flag:**

$$
Z = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if result} = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Since } \mathtt{0x80000000} \neq 0, \quad Z = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Negative flag:**

$$
N = \text{result}[31] = \mathtt{0x80000000}[31] = 1
$$

$$
N = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Carry flag (unsigned):**

For ADD, C = 1 if the unsigned addition produces a carry out of bit 31.

$$
\mathtt{0x7FFFFFFF} + \mathtt{0x00000001} = \mathtt{0x80000000}, \quad \text{no carry out}
$$

$$
C = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Overflow flag (signed):**

For ADD, V = 1 if both operands have the *same sign* and the result has the *opposite sign* (i.e., signed overflow).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operand 1 sign} & = \text{0x7FFFFFFF}[31] = 0 \quad (\text{positive}) \\
\text{Operand 2 sign} & = \text{0x00000001}[31] = 0 \quad (\text{positive}) \\
\text{Result sign} & = \text{0x80000000}[31] = 1 \quad (\text{negative})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Same sign operands, opposite sign result} \implies V = 1
$$

**Result:**

$$
\text{APSR} = N:1,\; Z:0,\; C:0,\; V:1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{APSR[31:28]} = \mathtt{0b1010} = \mathtt{0xA}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — EXC_RETURN Encoding

On exception entry, the Cortex-M hardware writes a special value into LR — this is **EXC_RETURN**. It is the value the processor will use to return from the handler. The 32-bit value looks like `0xFFFF_FFx0` where the bottom 5 bits encode the return behavior.

| Bit | Name | Meaning |
|-----|------|---------|
| [31:4] | Prefix | Always `0xFFFF_FF` (bits 31:4 = `0xFFFFFF`) |
| [3] | — | Reserved (must be 1) |
| [2] | SPSEL | 0 = Return to MSP, 1 = Return to PSP |
| [1] | — | Reserved |
| [0] | Mode | 1 = Return to Thread mode, 0 = Return to Handler mode |

> Given: An interrupt fires in Thread mode using the PSP. The hardware writes EXC_RETURN = `0xFFFF_FFFD`. Identify the return configuration.

**Step 1 — Compare to the template `0xFFFF_FFx0`:**

$$
\mathtt{0xFFFF\_FFFD}\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{bottom nibble} = \mathtt{0xD} = \mathtt{1101}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Decode bit fields:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bit 3} &= 1 \quad (\text{reserved, correct}) \\
\text{bit 2} &= 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Return to PSP} \\
\text{bit 1} &= 0 \quad (\text{reserved}) \\
\text{bit 0} &= 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Return to Thread mode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Upon `BX LR` / exception return, the processor restores R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR from the **PSP** stack frame and resumes execution in **Thread mode**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Register Access Permission

The following matrix summarizes *which instructions can access which registers* (a high-yield KTU question topic):

| Instruction Width | Can Access R0–R7 | Can Access R8–R12 | Can Access SP (R13) | Can Access LR (R14) | Can Access PC (R15) |
|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 16-bit Thumb (e.g., `MOV`) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (except as destination = branch) |
| 32-bit Thumb-2 (e.g., `MOV.W`) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (limited) | ✅ (limited) | ✅ (writes = branch) |
| MRS / MSR | — | — | ✅ (MSP/PSP) | — | — (read-only IPSR/PSR fields) |

> [!NOTE]
> This restriction is **why** 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions were introduced — they removed the 16-bit Thumb limitation and made registers R8–R12 freely usable in compiled C code.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cortex-M Programmer's Model — Register Bank Overview

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CoreRegs["Core 32-bit Registers (Visible to Software)"]
        R0["R0 - Argument 1 / Return"]
        R1["R1 - Argument 2"]
        R2["R2 - Argument 3"]
        R3["R3 - Argument 4"]
        R4["R4 - General Purpose"]
        R5["R5 - General Purpose"]
        R6["R6 - General Purpose"]
        R7["R7 - General Purpose"]
        R8["R8 - General Purpose (Thumb-2)"]
        R9["R9 - General Purpose (Thumb-2)"]
        R10["R10 - General Purpose (Thumb-2)"]
        R11["R11 - General Purpose (Thumb-2)"]
        R12["R12 - Intra-procedure Scratch"]
        R13["R13 - SP (Banked)"]
        R14["R14 - LR (Link Register)"]
        R15["R15 - PC (Program Counter)"]
    end

    subgraph SPBank["SP Banking (R13)"]
        MSP["MSP - Main Stack Pointer (Handler/Privileged)"]
        PSP["PSP - Process Stack Pointer (RTOS Threads)"]
    end

    subgraph SpecialRegs["Special Registers (via MRS/MSR)"]
        xPSR["xPSR = APSR + IPSR + EPSR"]
        PRIMASK["PRIMASK - 1-bit mask"]
        FAULTMASK["FAULTMASK - 1-bit mask"]
        BASEPRI["BASEPRI - 8-bit threshold"]
        CONTROL["CONTROL - nPRIV, SPSEL, FPCA"]
    end

    R13 --> MSP
    R13 --> PSP
    CONTROL -. "SPSEL bit selects" .-> MSP
    CONTROL -. "SPSEL bit selects" .-> PSP

    xPSR --> APSR["APSR - N, Z, C, V, Q, GE"]
    xPSR --> IPSR["IPSR - Exception Number"]
    xPSR --> EPSR["EPSR - T bit, IT/ICI"]
```

### 4.2 APSR Bit-Field Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph APSR["APSR - 32 bits"]
        B31["31: N (Negative)"]
        B30["30: Z (Zero)"]
        B29["29: C (Carry)"]
        B28["28: V (Overflow)"]
        B27["27: Q (Sticky Sat)"]
        B19_16["19-16: GE[3:0] (DSP)"]
        B0_5["5-0: Reserved"]
    end
    B31 --- B30 --- B29 --- B28 --- B27 --- B19_16 --- B0_5
```

### 4.3 Operating Mode & Privilege State Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    RESET(["Reset"]) --> THREAD_PRIV["Thread Mode\nPrivileged\nMSP (default)"]
    THREAD_PRIV -- "Set CONTROL[0]=1\n(privileged code only)" --> THREAD_UNPRIV["Thread Mode\nUnprivileged\nMSP or PSP"]
    THREAD_PRIV -- "Set CONTROL[1]=1" --> THREAD_PRIV_PSP["Thread Mode\nPrivileged\nPSP"]
    THREAD_UNPRIV -- "Set CONTROL[1]=1" --> THREAD_UNPRIV_PSP["Thread Mode\nUnprivileged\nPSP"]

    THREAD_PRIV -- "Exception fires" --> HANDLER["Handler Mode\nALWAYS Privileged\nALWAYS MSP"]
    THREAD_PRIV_PSP -- "Exception fires" --> HANDLER
    THREAD_UNPRIV -- "Exception fires" --> HANDLER
    THREAD_UNPRIV_PSP -- "Exception fires" --> HANDLER

    HANDLER -- "Exception return\n(EXC_RETURN)" --> THREAD_PRIV
    HANDLER -- "Exception return to PSP" --> THREAD_PRIV_PSP

    style HANDLER fill:#ffe5e5
    style THREAD_PRIV fill:#e5f5ff
    style THREAD_UNPRIV fill:#fff5e5
    style THREAD_PRIV_PSP fill:#e5ffe5
    style THREAD_UNPRIV_PSP fill:#f0e5ff
```

### 4.4 Exception Stack Frame Layout (Hardware-Pushed on Entry)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Frame["Stack Frame on Exception Entry (8 words = 32 bytes)"]
        OFS0["Higher Address\n(Prior SP)"]
        W0["xPSR"]
        W1["PC (return address)"]
        W2["LR"]
        W3["R12"]
        W4["R3"]
        W5["R2"]
        W6["R1"]
        W7["R0"]
        OFS7["Lower Address\n(New SP)"]
    end
    OFS7 --- W7 --- W6 --- W5 --- W4 --- W3 --- W2 --- W1 --- W0 --- OFS0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **8-word hardware stack frame** is automatically pushed on exception entry in Cortex-M3/M4/M7 — you do NOT code this yourself. The order is *descending* in the stack: xPSR is at the highest address, R0 at the lowest. If FPU is used, the lazy stacking feature may add 17 more words, but only when CONTROL.FPCA = 1.

### 4.5 Interrupt Masking Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Exception Request Arrives"] --> B{"FAULTMASK = 1?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Blocked (except NMI)"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"PRIMASK = 1?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Blocked (all configurable)"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"BASEPRI \u2265 exception priority?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Blocked"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Accepted \u2192 NVIC arbitrates"]
    H --> I{"Higher priority\npending?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Preempt current handler"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Enter exception handler"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the general-purpose registers available in the Cortex-M processor and explain the special functions of R13, R14, and R15.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Cortex-M processor has **16 core 32-bit registers: R0–R15**.
- **R0–R12** are general-purpose, used for data manipulation and argument passing (R0–R3 for AAPCS).
- **R13 is the Stack Pointer (SP)** — banked into MSP and PSP; points to top of stack; stack grows downward.
- **R14 is the Link Register (LR)** — holds the return address after subroutine calls (BL/BLX); on exception entry, holds EXC_RETURN.
- **R15 is the Program Counter (PC)** — points to the next instruction to be fetched (current + 4); bit 0 is always 1 (Thumb state). *(3 marks: 1 for listing, 1 for R13/R14, 1 for R15.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the role of the PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, and BASEPRI registers in interrupt handling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **PRIMASK (1-bit):** When set to 1, blocks all exceptions with configurable priority (i.e., all interrupts). Set/cleared by `CPSID I` / `CPSIE I`.
- **FAULTMASK (1-bit):** When set to 1, blocks all exceptions **except NMI**; also raises execution priority to -1. Used in fault handlers to prevent escalation.
- **BASEPRI (8-bit):** Masks all exceptions whose priority is **numerically greater than or equal to** the stored value. Allows fine-grained critical sections while keeping high-priority interrupts responsive. *(3 marks: 1 for each register.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q A(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]**
*With neat diagrams, explain the internal organization of the ARM Cortex-M processor register file. Distinguish between general-purpose registers and special-purpose registers. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Cortex-M programmer's model consists of 16 core 32-bit registers (R0–R15) and a small set of special-purpose registers accessed via special instructions (MRS/MSR).

**General-Purpose Registers (3 marks):**
- R0–R7: Low registers, accessible by all 16-bit and 32-bit Thumb/Thumb-2 instructions. Used as scratch and for AAPCS argument passing.
- R8–R12: High registers, accessible only by 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. R8–R12 are general-purpose; R12 is conventionally used as a scratch register within subroutines (intra-procedure-call scratch register).

**Special-Purpose Registers (3 marks):**
- R13 / SP: Banked into MSP (privileged/handler) and PSP (RTOS threads). Selected by CONTROL[1]. Always 32-bit aligned, stack grows downward.
- R14 / LR: Stores return address for BL/BLX. On exception entry, holds EXC_RETURN.
- R15 / PC: Points to next instruction (current + 4). Bit 0 must be 1.
- xPSR: 32-bit composite of APSR (N, Z, C, V, Q, GE), IPSR (current exception number), and EPSR (T-bit, IT/ICI state).
- PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI: Interrupt-mask registers for critical sections.
- CONTROL: Defines privilege (nPRIV), stack selection (SPSEL), and FPU state (FPCA).

*(Refer to the diagrams in SECTION 4.1 and 4.3 for the visual.)*

---

**Q A(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, adapted]**
*Explain the two operating modes (Thread and Handler) and the two privilege levels (Privileged and Unprivileged) in Cortex-M. How does the CONTROL register manage mode transitions? State the restrictions. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Operating Modes (2 marks):**
- **Thread mode:** The normal execution mode after reset; used for application code. Can be privileged or unprivileged.
- **Handler mode:** Entered automatically on exception/interrupt; used for ISRs. **Always privileged**, **always uses MSP**.

**Privilege Levels (2 marks):**
- **Privileged:** Full access to all system control registers, NVIC, memory (subject to MPU if enabled). Default for reset and Handler mode.
- **Unprivileged:** Restricted access — cannot disable interrupts, cannot change CONTROL, cannot access some system registers. Used to sandbox application code in RTOS or TrustZone.

**CONTROL Register Role (2 marks):**
- **CONTROL[0] (nPRIV):** 0 = privileged, 1 = unprivileged. Effective only in Thread mode.
- **CONTROL[1] (SPSEL):** 0 = use MSP, 1 = use PSP. Effective only in Thread mode.
- **CONTROL[2] (FPCA):** On FPU cores, indicates FP context is active — affects lazy stacking.

**Restrictions (1 mark):**
- Unprivileged code **cannot write to CONTROL** — any attempt causes a fault.
- Handler mode **ignores CONTROL** — it is always privileged and uses MSP.
- Switches between MSP/PSP are only meaningful in Thread mode.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q B(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, adapted]**
*Explain the structure of the xPSR register in Cortex-M. List and define all flag bits in the APSR. Demonstrate with a worked example how the N, Z, C, V flags are set after `SUBS R0, R1, R2` where R1 = 0x0000_0000 and R2 = 0x0000_0001. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**xPSR Structure (2 marks):**
xPSR is a 32-bit register logically composed of three sub-registers read/written independently:
- **APSR** (Application PSR): bits 31:27 — N, Z, C, V, Q; bits 19:16 — GE[3:0].
- **IPSR** (Interrupt PSR): bits 5:0 — current exception number (0 = Thread mode).
- **EPSR** (Execution PSR): bit 24 — T (Thumb state, always 1); bits 15:10 — ICI/IT for multi-register ops and IT blocks.

**APSR Flags (2 marks):**
- **N** (bit 31): Negative — equals result[31].
- **Z** (bit 30): Zero — 1 if result = 0.
- **C** (bit 29): Carry — for SUB, C = NOT(borrow) = 1 if no borrow.
- **V** (bit 28): Overflow — 1 on signed overflow.
- **Q** (bit 27): Sticky saturation (DSP only).
- **GE[3:0]**: Greater-than-or-Equal flags (M4/M7 DSP only).

**Worked Example (3 marks):** `SUBS R0, R1, R2`, R1 = `0x00000000`, R2 = `0x00000001`.

$$
R0 = \mathtt{0x00000000} - \mathtt{0x00000001} = \mathtt{0xFFFFFFFF}
$$

**N flag:** `R0[31] = 1` → **N = 1**
**Z flag:** `R0 ≠ 0` → **Z = 0**
**C flag (SUB):** Unsigned borrow occurs (0 < 1), so `C = NOT(borrow) = 0` → **C = 0**
**V flag (SUB):** Operands are `+` and `+`, result is `−` → signed overflow → **V = 0**
*(Actually, for SUB, V = 1 when operand signs differ AND result sign differs from first operand. Here op1=+, op2=+, result=−, so V = 0.)*

$$
\text{APSR} = N:1,\; Z:0,\; C:0,\; V:0
$$

---

**Q B(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, adapted]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the hardware stack frame pushed by the Cortex-M processor on exception entry. What is EXC_RETURN? Decode the value 0xFFFF_FFF1 and explain the resulting behavior. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Hardware Stack Frame (3 marks):**
On exception entry, Cortex-M3/M4/M7 automatically push 8 words (32 bytes) onto the selected stack (MSP by default). In *descending* order of address:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Highest address} &\leftarrow \text{xPSR} \\
&\leftarrow \text{PC (return address)} \\
&\leftarrow \text{LR} \\
&\leftarrow \text{R12} \\
&\leftarrow \text{R3} \\
&\leftarrow \text{R2} \\
&\leftarrow \text{R1} \\
\text{Lowest address} &\leftarrow \text{R0 (new SP points here)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **deterministic and atomic** — no instruction sequence in software can be interrupted mid-way, giving Cortex-M its hard-real-time interrupt latency.

**EXC_RETURN (2 marks):**
EXC_RETURN is a special value written into **LR** by hardware on exception entry. When the handler issues `BX LR` (or returns via `EXC_RETURN`-aware instruction), the processor interprets LR as a *command*, not an address, and uses it to:
1. Restore the 8-word frame from the stack.
2. Switch back from Handler to Thread mode.
3. Choose the correct stack pointer (MSP or PSP).

**Decoding 0xFFFF_FFF1 (2 marks):**
Template: `0xFFFF_FFx0`. Bottom nibble = `0x1` = `0001₂`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 3} &= 0 \quad (\text{reserved, VIOLATION — invalid encoding!}) \\
\text{Bit 2} &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Return to MSP} \\
\text{Bit 1} &= 0 \quad (\text{reserved}) \\
\text{Bit 0} &= 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Return to Thread mode}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The encoding `0xFFFF_FFF1` is **technically reserved/invalid** because bit 3 must be 1. Students often overlook this and only report bits 0 and 2. **The correct EXC_RETURN values are 0xFFFF_FFF1 (Thread/MSP), 0xFFFF_FFF9 (Thread/PSP), and 0xFFFF_FFFD (Handler/MSP)**. The bit-3 rule is a common 1-mark trap in KTU papers. Always check bit 3 = 1.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting R13 is banked** into MSP and PSP — many answers treat SP as a single register. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Conflating LR's two roles** — return address (BL) vs. EXC_RETURN (exceptions). These are mutually exclusive at any given moment. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Confusing CONTROL[0] privilege with mode** — Handler mode is *always* privileged, regardless of CONTROL[0]. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 4. **Sign error on V flag for SUB** — V = 1 in SUB when operand signs *differ* and result sign differs from op1. *Loss: 1 mark in numerical questions.*
> 5. **Ignoring the T bit (EPSR[24])** — this bit is always 1 in Cortex-M and *cannot* be cleared by software. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **16 core 32-bit registers** in the Cortex-M programmer's model: R0–R12 (general-purpose), R13 (SP, banked), R14 (LR), R15 (PC).
- **R0–R3** = AAPCS argument/return registers. **R12** = intra-procedure scratch.
- **R13 (SP)** is banked: **MSP** for Handler / privileged-Thread; **PSP** for RTOS task stacks. Selected by **CONTROL[1]**.
- **R14 (LR)** holds the return address for BL/BLX, but on exception entry, it holds **EXC_RETURN** (e.g., `0xFFFF_FFFD`).
- **R15 (PC)** is `current + 4` in Thumb-2; **bit 0 must be 1** (Thumb state indicator).
- **xPSR** = APSR (N, Z, C, V, Q, GE) ∪ IPSR (exception#) ∪ EPSR (T, IT/ICI).
- **APSR flag rules:** N = result[31]; Z = (result == 0); C (ADD) = carry-out; C (SUB) = NOT(borrow); V = signed overflow detection.
- **Mask registers:** **PRIMASK** = block all interrupts; **FAULTMASK** = block all but NMI; **BASEPRI** = block ≤ a given threshold (graceful, priority-aware).
- **Modes:** **Thread** (normal/app) and **Handler** (ISR). **Handler mode is always privileged and uses MSP.**
- **Privilege:** **Privileged** (full access) and **Unprivileged** (sandboxed). Unprivileged code cannot modify CONTROL.
- **CONTROL bits:** [0] nPRIV, [1] SPSEL, [2] FPCA (FPU only).
- **Stack:** full-descending, 32-bit aligned, 8-byte aligned at public function entry (AAPCS).
- **Hardware exception stack frame** = 8 words (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) — automatic, atomic, no extra instructions needed.
- **EXC_RETURN format** = `0xFFFF_FFx0`; bit 3 = 1 (must); bit 2 = SPSEL; bit 0 = return-to-Thread.
- **Memory-mapped vs special:** R0–R15 are *core* registers; xPSR/mask/CONTROL are *special* registers accessed via MRS/MSR — not memory-mapped.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instruction Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the instruction processing into a series of independent stages, each stage performing a specific portion of the instruction cycle and passing the partially processed instruction to the next stage in a synchronized manner.

In the context of the **ARM architecture**, instruction pipelining enables the processor to fetch one instruction while simultaneously decoding a previously fetched instruction, executing a third, and storing the result of a fourth — all within a single clock cycle framework. The **ARM7TDMI** processor, for example, uses a classic **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute), while the **ARM9** uses a **5-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute → Memory → Writeback), and modern Cortex-A series processors employ **13+ stage superpipelined and superscalar** designs.

### Key Terminology

- **Pipeline Stage**: A logical subdivision of the instruction execution process that performs a well-defined function in one clock cycle.
- **Pipeline Register**: Hardware latches placed between stages to isolate them temporally and hold the intermediate results.
- **Throughput**: The number of instructions completed per unit of time (measured in **Instructions Per Cycle - IPC**).
- **Latency**: The time taken for a single instruction to traverse the entire pipeline from start to finish.
- **Speedup Factor (S)**: The ratio of execution time without pipelining to execution time with pipelining.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Laundry Analogy** (Patterson & Hennessy) — Four students share a dorm with one washer, one dryer, and one folder. Doing 4 loads of laundry sequentially takes 8 hours (each load = wash 30 min + dry 40 min + fold 20 min = 90 min). Doing them **pipelined** takes only 3.5 hours. While load 2 is drying, load 1 is being folded, and load 3 is being washed — all in parallel stages.

**Imagine a factory assembly line:** Instead of one worker building an entire car from start to finish (which would take days), the car moves along a conveyor belt. Station 1 fits the chassis, Station 2 adds the engine, Station 3 installs wheels, Station 4 paints it. At any given moment, **four different cars are at four different stations**. The same principle applies to a processor: while one instruction is being **executed**, the next is being **decoded**, and the one after that is being **fetched**. This dramatically increases the rate at which instructions complete.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Pipeline Depth (k)**: Number of stages (typical ARM7 = **3**, ARM9 = **5**, Cortex-A8 = **13**).
- **Clock Period (T)**: Time per stage, governed by the **slowest stage** in the pipeline.
- **Pipeline Fill Time**: Equal to $k \cdot T$ cycles.
- **Pipeline Drain Time**: Equal to $k \cdot T$ cycles after the last instruction enters.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**: Without pipelining = **5** for a 5-stage pipeline; with ideal pipelining = **1** (steady state).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Pipeline (ARM9 / Generic RISC Model)

A canonical RISC pipeline consists of the following five stages:

| Stage | Name | Function |
|:------|:-----|:---------|
| **IF** | Instruction Fetch | Read instruction from instruction memory using PC; update PC to PC + 4 |
| **ID** | Instruction Decode | Decode opcode, read register file, sign-extend immediate values |
| **EX** | Execute | ALU performs arithmetic/logic operation, computes branch target, evaluates condition codes |
| **MEM** | Memory Access | Load/Store instructions access data memory; ALU results simply pass through |
| **WB** | Write Back | Write result back to the register file |

### Pipeline Registers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Between each pair of adjacent stages, a **pipeline register** holds the intermediate data. These registers are the "boundaries" of each stage. In a 5-stage pipeline, we have **four** sets of pipeline registers: **IF/ID**, **ID/EX**, **EX/MEM**, and **MEM/WB**.

## 2.2 Performance Metrics — Derivation Philosophy

The **'Why'** behind pipelining lies in the fundamental observation: a single instruction must pass through all $k$ stages, but a *new* instruction can enter the pipeline every single clock cycle (in an ideal world). This means:

- **Latency per instruction** remains roughly $k \cdot T$ (each instruction still takes the same time from start to finish).
- **Throughput** increases because multiple instructions are in-flight simultaneously.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they are the core of every KTU numerical on pipelining.**

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|:----------|:--------|:------------|:------|
| **Sequential Execution Time** | $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T$ | $n$ instructions, $k$ stages, cycle time $T$ | Seconds |
| **Pipelined Execution Time** | $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$ | Includes fill + drain overhead | Seconds |
| **Speedup Factor (S)** | $S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | Ratio of improvement | Dimensionless |
| **Maximum Theoretical Speedup** | $S_{max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$ | As $n$ grows large | Dimensionless |
| **Pipeline Throughput** | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T}$ | Instructions per second | IPS |
| **Pipeline Efficiency ($\eta$)** | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Fraction of ideal speedup achieved | Dimensionless |
| **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI)** | $CPI_{pipe} = \dfrac{k + n - 1}{n} = 1 + \dfrac{k-1}{n}$ | Average CPI in pipelined mode | Cycles |
| **Ideal CPI** | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | Steady-state, no hazards | Cycles |
| **Stall Penalty** | $\text{Penalty} = \text{Stall Cycles} \times T$ | Cost of pipeline bubbles | Seconds |
| **Actual Speedup with Stalls** | $S_{actual} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1 + s) \cdot T}$ | $s$ = total stall cycles | Dimensionless |

> **Critical Substitution Rule for Tables:** When using the expression $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value of $x$), write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ using `\lvert` and `\rvert` in $\LaTeX$ to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Pipeline Hazards — The "How" of Pipeline Failures

Hazards are situations that prevent the next instruction from executing in the immediately following clock cycle, thereby **stalling the pipeline** and reducing effective throughput.

### 2.4.1 Structural Hazards (Resource Conflicts)

Occur when **hardware resources are insufficient** to support all combinations of instructions in the pipeline simultaneously.

- **Example**: A single memory port serving both instruction fetch (IF) and data access (MEM). When a Load instruction reaches MEM in cycle 4, and a new instruction enters IF in cycle 5, both want the memory.
- **Solution**: Duplicate resources (Harvard architecture: separate I-cache and D-cache), or stall the pipeline for one cycle.

### 2.4.2 Data Hazards (Data Dependency Conflicts)

Occur when instructions depend on the results of previous instructions still in the pipeline. Three subtypes:

| Type | Full Form | Description | Example |
|:-----|:----------|:------------|:--------|
| **RAW** | Read After Write | True data dependency — instruction needs the result of a previous instruction still in flight | `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `SUB R4, R1, R5` |
| **WAR** | Write After Read | Anti-dependency — write happens too early before prior read | Less common in in-order pipelines |
| **WAW** | Write After Write | Output dependency — two instructions write to the same register | Common in floating-point pipelines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAW is the most common hazard in scalar in-order pipelines** and the one KTU questions focus on. Solutions include **operand forwarding (bypassing)**, **pipeline stalls (bubbles)**, and **compiler scheduling**.

### 2.4.3 Control Hazards (Branch Hazards)

Occur due to **branches and jumps** — the pipeline cannot determine the next PC until the branch outcome is known (typically in the EX stage for ARM's conditional execution).

- **Branch Penalty**: Typically **2 cycles** in a 5-stage pipeline (flush IF and ID stages).
- **Solutions**: Branch prediction, delayed branching, branch target buffer (BTB), speculative execution.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Instruction pipelining is the **backbone of every modern CPU**, from embedded microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M) to high-performance server processors (Apple M-series, Intel Core). It enables the GHz-scale clock frequencies we rely on by breaking critical paths into shorter stages, allowing higher clock rates. **Performance engineers, compiler designers, and embedded systems developers** must understand pipelining to:
- Write branch-light, dependency-free code for hot loops.
- Schedule instructions to avoid RAW hazards.
- Predict pipeline stalls to meet real-time deadlines in **RTOS environments**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speedup Factor of a k-Stage Pipeline

**Given:** A processor with $k$ pipeline stages, executing $n$ instructions. Each stage takes exactly one clock cycle of duration $T$.

### Step 1: Calculate Unpipelined (Sequential) Execution Time

In a non-pipelined processor, each instruction must complete **all $k$ stages** before the next one begins. So the time for $n$ instructions is:

$$
T_{seq} = n \times k \times T
$$

**Logic:** $n$ instructions $\times$ $k$ stages per instruction $\times$ $T$ seconds per stage.

### Step 2: Calculate Pipelined Execution Time

In a pipelined processor, the first instruction takes $k \cdot T$ to complete (pipeline fill). After that, one new instruction **completes every clock cycle** (steady state). The remaining $n - 1$ instructions each take an additional $T$ to finish:

$$
T_{pipe} = k \cdot T + (n - 1) \cdot T
$$

Combining the terms:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T
$$

**Logic:** Total time = (fill time) + (drain time) = (one full pipeline traversal) + (one cycle per remaining instruction).

### Step 3: Compute the Speedup Factor

By definition, speedup is the ratio of sequential time to pipelined time:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T}
$$

Canceling the $T$ terms (they are common to both numerator and denominator):

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}}
$$

### Step 4: Verify the Asymptotic Limit

As $n \to \infty$ (very long program), the constant $k$ in the denominator becomes negligible:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n} = k
$$

**Interpretation:** The theoretical maximum speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline is exactly $k$ (achieved only for infinitely long programs with no hazards).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Throughput

**Throughput** is the number of instructions completed per unit of time:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Total Instructions}}{\text{Total Time}} = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T}
$$

To convert to instructions per cycle (IPC), divide by the number of cycles $(k + n - 1)$:

$$
\text{IPC} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}
$$

In the steady state ($n \gg k$): $\text{IPC} \to 1$ instruction per cycle, which is the ideal CPI = 1 for a scalar pipelined processor.

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Question:** A processor has a 5-stage pipeline. The clock period is $T = 2$ ns. Compute the speedup, throughput, and efficiency for $n = 100$ instructions. Compare with $n = 1000$.

### Step 1: Sequential Time

$$
T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T = 100 \times 5 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 2: Pipelined Time

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T = (5 + 100 - 1) \times 2 = 104 \times 2 = 208 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 3: Speedup

$$
S = \dfrac{1000}{208} = 4.808
$$

### Step 4: Efficiency

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{4.808}{5} = 0.9616 = 96.16\%
$$

### Step 5: Throughput

$$
\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{100}{208 \text{ ns}} = 0.481 \text{ instructions/ns} \approx 481 \text{ MIPS}
$$

### Step 6: Repeat for n = 1000

$$
T_{seq} = 1000 \times 5 \times 2 = 10000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pipe} = (5 + 1000 - 1) \times 2 = 1004 \times 2 = 2008 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{10000}{2008} = 4.980
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{4.980}{5} = 99.60\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As $n$ increases from 100 to 1000, the speedup moves from 4.808 toward the theoretical limit of 5. This confirms the asymptotic behavior: $\lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$.

## 3.4 Speedup with Pipeline Stalls (Hazard Penalty)

**Given:** A 5-stage pipeline with a 2-cycle branch penalty. Out of every 10 instructions, 2 are branches. Effective $n$ = 100.

**Total stall cycles:** Each branch causes 2 wasted cycles.

$$
s = (\text{branches}) \times (\text{penalty}) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ stall cycles}
$$

$$
S_{actual} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1 + s} = \dfrac{100 \times 5}{5 + 99 + 40} = \dfrac{500}{144} = 3.472
$$

This is significantly less than the ideal 4.808, illustrating the **heavy cost of control hazards**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Pipeline Depth (k) | n = 100 (S) | n = 1000 (S) | n = 10,000 (S) | Hazard Cost Impact |
|:-------------------|:------------|:-------------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 3 | 2.91 | 2.99 | 2.999 | Low |
| 5 | 4.81 | 4.98 | 4.998 | Medium |
| 7 | 6.71 | 6.97 | 6.999 | High |
| 13 | 12.27 | 12.91 | 12.99 | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipeline Stage Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IF: Instruction Fetch] -->|IF/ID Register| B[ID: Instruction Decode]
    B -->|ID/EX Register| C[EX: Execute ALU]
    C -->|EX/MEM Register| D[MEM: Memory Access]
    D -->|MEM/WB Register| E[WB: Write Back]
    E --> F([Result to Register File])
    C -->|Branch Target| G[Update PC]
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (Mermaid Gantt Chart)

```mermaid
gantt
    title 5-Stage Pipeline Timing Diagram (n = 7 instructions)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section I1 Instruction 1
    IF1 :a1, 0, 1
    ID1 :a2, 1, 1
    EX1 :a3, 2, 1
    MEM1 :a4, 3, 1
    WB1 :a5, 4, 1
    section I2 Instruction 2
    IF2 :b1, 1, 1
    ID2 :b2, 2, 1
    EX2 :b3, 3, 1
    MEM2 :b4, 4, 1
    WB2 :b5, 5, 1
    section I3 Instruction 3
    IF3 :c1, 2, 1
    ID3 :c2, 3, 1
    EX3 :c3, 4, 1
    MEM3 :c4, 5, 1
    WB3 :c5, 6, 1
    section I4 Instruction 4
    IF4 :d1, 3, 1
    ID4 :d2, 4, 1
    EX4 :d3, 5, 1
    MEM4 :d4, 6, 1
    WB4 :d5, 7, 1
```

**Interpretation:** At cycle 5, five different instructions are simultaneously active in five different stages — true parallelism in time.

## 4.3 Hazard Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Pipeline Hazard Detected] --> H1{Type of Conflict?}
    H1 -->|Hardware Resource Clash| SH[Structural Hazard]
    H1 -->|Data Dependency| DH[Data Hazard]
    H1 -->|Branch / Jump| CH[Control Hazard]
    SH --> SH1[Solution: Duplicate HW or Stall]
    DH --> DH1{Dependency Type?}
    DH1 -->|RAW True Dependency| DH2[Forwarding or Stall]
    DH1 -->|WAR Anti Dependency| DH3[Rename Registers]
    DH1 -->|WAW Output Dependency| DH4[Rename Registers]
    CH --> CH1[Solution: Prediction or Delay Slot]
```

## 4.4 Data Hazard — RAW Dependency Visualization

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IF as IF Stage
    participant ID as ID Stage
    participant EX as EX Stage
    participant MEM as MEM Stage
    participant WB as WB Stage
    Note over IF,WB: I1: ADD R1, R2, R3
    Note over IF,WB: I2: SUB R4, R1, R5 (depends on R1)
    IF->>ID: Cycle 1: Fetch I1
    ID->>EX: Cycle 2: Decode I1, Fetch I2
    EX->>MEM: Cycle 3: Compute I1, Decode I2
    MEM->>WB: Cycle 4: Memory I1, Compute I2 (needs R1!)
    Note over EX,MEM: RAW HAZARD - R1 not yet written
    WB-->>EX: Forward R1 result via bypass
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Define instruction pipelining. List the three main types of pipeline hazards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Instruction pipelining is a technique of implementing instruction-level parallelism wherein the execution of multiple instructions is overlapped by dividing the instruction processing into a sequence of independent stages. Each stage completes one part of the instruction, and multiple instructions are processed concurrently in different stages, with one instruction completing per clock cycle in the steady state.

**Three Hazards (1 mark):**
1. **Structural Hazard** — Resource conflict when hardware cannot support all active instructions.
2. **Data Hazard** — When an instruction depends on the result of a previous in-flight instruction.
3. **Control Hazard** — Caused by branches and jumps where the next PC is not yet known.

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: A 4-stage pipeline processes 50 instructions. The clock period is 1 ns. Calculate the speedup factor and pipeline efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Given:** $k = 4$ stages, $n = 50$ instructions, $T = 1$ ns.

**Step 1: Sequential time** (0.5 mark)

$$
T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T = 50 \times 4 \times 1 = 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Pipelined time** (1 mark)

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T = (4 + 50 - 1) \times 1 = 53 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Speedup and Efficiency** (1.5 marks)

$$
S = \dfrac{200}{53} = 3.77
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{3.77}{4} = 0.9425 = 94.25\%
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a **5-stage instruction pipeline** used in RISC processors. Discuss the function of each stage and the role of pipeline registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a **5-stage pipeline** with a **clock period of 2 ns**. An application has **500 instructions**, of which **25% are branch instructions** causing a **2-cycle stall** each. Compute:
  (i) The total execution time with pipelining.
  (ii) The actual speedup factor over non-pipelined execution.
  (iii) The pipeline efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A pipeline flow diagram (similar to SECTION 4.1) showing IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB with pipeline registers between each stage.

**Explanation of each stage (4 marks, 1 mark per stage except pipeline registers):**

1. **IF (Instruction Fetch)**: The instruction at the address pointed to by the Program Counter (PC) is fetched from instruction memory. The PC is incremented by 4 (for ARM, 32-bit instructions) to point to the next sequential instruction. Branch target calculation also occurs here in some designs.

2. **ID (Instruction Decode)**: The fetched instruction is decoded to determine the operation type, source operands, and destination. The register file is read to obtain operand values. Immediate values are sign-extended.

3. **EX (Execute)**: The ALU performs the arithmetic, logical, or comparison operation. For memory access instructions, the effective address is calculated. For branches, the branch condition is evaluated and the target address is computed.

4. **MEM (Memory Access)**: Load instructions read data from data memory at the address calculated in EX. Store instructions write data to data memory. ALU-only instructions bypass this stage.

5. **WB (Write Back)**: The result of the ALU operation or data loaded from memory is written back to the destination register in the register file.

**Pipeline registers (1 mark):** The IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB registers hold the control signals, data, and intermediate results passed from one stage to the next. They decouple the stages, allowing each to operate independently in a single clock cycle, and ensure pipeline synchronization.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $k = 5$, $T = 2$ ns, $n = 500$, Branch fraction = 25%, Branch penalty = 2 cycles.

**Step 1: Calculate number of stalls** (2 marks)

$$
\text{Branches} = 0.25 \times 500 = 125
$$

$$
\text{Total stall cycles} \; s = 125 \times 2 = 250
$$

**Step 2: Pipelined execution time** (2 marks)

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1 + s) \cdot T = (5 + 500 - 1 + 250) \times 2 = 754 \times 2 = 1508 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Sequential time and actual speedup** (2 marks)

$$
T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T = 500 \times 5 \times 2 = 5000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
S_{actual} = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{5000}{1508} = 3.316
$$

**Step 4: Pipeline efficiency** (1 mark)

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{k} = \dfrac{3.316}{5} = 0.6632 = 66.32\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of data hazards** (RAW, WAR, WAW) in instruction pipelining with suitable examples. Discuss how **operand forwarding** eliminates the RAW hazard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **non-pipelined processor** that takes **5 clock cycles per instruction** at a **clock rate of 100 MHz**. A pipelined version of the same processor has **5 stages** and runs at the **same clock rate**. How many instructions must be executed to achieve a speedup of **at least 4**? Also calculate the throughput in MIPS for $n = 1000$ instructions in the pipelined version. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Three data hazards (4.5 marks, 1.5 each):**

1. **RAW (Read After Write) — True Data Dependency:**
Instruction $I_2$ tries to read a source register before a prior instruction $I_1$ has written its result to it. This is the **most common hazard** in scalar in-order pipelines.

   Example:
   ```
   I1: ADD R1, R2, R3    ; R1 = R2 + R3
   I2: SUB R4, R1, R5    ; needs R1 written by I1
   ```
   If I2 reads R1 before I1 completes the WB stage, it gets a stale value.

2. **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependency:**
Instruction $I_2$ writes to a register that a prior instruction $I_1$ still needs to read. In an in-order pipeline, this can occur only when write-back happens in an early stage (e.g., WB before EX of a later instruction) or with out-of-order execution.

   Example:
   ```
   I1: SUB R4, R1, R5
   I2: ADD R1, R2, R3    ; writes R1, but I1 may not have read it yet
   ```

3. **WAW (Write After Write) — Output Dependency:**
Two instructions write to the same destination register. The pipeline must ensure that the final value written is from the logically last instruction.

   Example:
   ```
   I1: MUL R1, R2, R3    ; writes R1 (slow operation)
   I2: ADD R1, R4, R5    ; writes R1 (fast operation)
   ```
   If I2 completes WB before I1, the register file ends up with the wrong value.

**Operand forwarding (2.5 marks):**

Operand forwarding (or bypassing) is a hardware technique that **routes the result of an ALU operation directly from the EX/MEM or MEM/WB pipeline register back to the ALU input latches**, eliminating the need to wait for the WB stage. This allows a dependent instruction to receive the needed operand in the **EX stage itself**, avoiding a stall.

Example revisited:
```
I1: ADD R1, R2, R3   (EX at cycle 3, result ready in EX/MEM register)
I2: SUB R4, R1, R5   (EX at cycle 4 — receives R1 via forwarding path)
```
A forwarding path from EX/MEM → ALU input is established, so the result is used **one cycle earlier** than waiting for the WB stage.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Non-pipelined CPI = 5, Pipelined stages $k = 5$, Clock rate = 100 MHz, $T = 10$ ns. Required $S \geq 4$.

**Step 1: Set up the speedup equation** (2 marks)

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} \geq 4
$$

**Step 2: Solve for n** (3 marks)

$$
5n \geq 4(k + n - 1) = 4(5 + n - 1) = 4n + 16
$$

$$
5n - 4n \geq 16
$$

$$
n \geq 16
$$

So a **minimum of 16 instructions** is needed to achieve $S \geq 4$.

Verification: $S = (16 \times 5) / (5 + 15) = 80 / 20 = 4.0$ ✓

**Step 3: Throughput for n = 1000** (2 marks)

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T = (5 + 1000 - 1) \times 10 \text{ ns} = 10040 \text{ ns} = 10.04 \; \mu s
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{1000}{10.04 \; \mu s} = 99.6 \text{ MIPS}
$$

Alternatively, $\text{Throughput} = (1/T) \times \text{IPC} = 100 \text{ MHz} \times (1000/1004) = 99.6 \text{ MIPS}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong pipelined time formula**: Students often write $T_{pipe} = n \cdot T$ instead of $(k + n - 1) \cdot T$. The pipeline must first **fill** ($k$ cycles) before steady-state throughput is achieved. **Lose 1-2 marks** if omitted.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add stall cycles**: When branch/data hazards are given, you MUST add the stall cycles $s$ to $(k + n - 1)$. Many students compute ideal pipelined time and ignore hazards. **Lose 2-3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing speedup with efficiency**: Speedup $S$ is a ratio (unitless), while efficiency $\eta$ is $S/k$ (a fraction or percentage). The examiner explicitly looks for the **correct symbol and unit** in the final answer.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the $k$ term in denominator**: When $n$ is small, the $(k - 1)$ term is significant. Always carry it through — do NOT approximate $k + n - 1 \approx n$.
>
> 5. **Mixing up hazards**: WAR and WAW are not common in simple in-order scalar pipelines. If asked for "the most common hazard," answer **RAW (true data dependency)**, not just "data hazard."
>
> 6. **Not labeling diagrams**: When drawing pipeline timing diagrams in Part B, label **both** the cycle numbers and the instruction stages. Unlabeled diagrams receive **0 marks** in KTU evaluation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this before every KTU exam:**

- [x] **Pipeline Definition**: Overlapping execution of multiple instructions by partitioning into independent stages.

- [x] **Canonical 5 Stages**: **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB** (memorize in order).

- [x] **Pipeline Registers**: 4 sets: IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB.

- [x] **Core Formulas** (must be memorized verbatim):
  - $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T$
  - $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1 + s) \cdot T$
  - $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1 + s}$
  - $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k}$, $\text{IPC} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1 + s}$

- [x] **Asymptotic Speedup**: $S_{max} = k$ as $n \to \infty$.

- [x] **Three Hazards**:
  - **Structural** → Resource conflict → Solution: duplicate hardware.
  - **Data** → RAW / WAR / WAW → Solution: forwarding, stalls, register renaming.
  - **Control** → Branches → Solution: prediction, delay slots, BTB.

- [x] **Operand Forwarding**: Routes ALU result from EX/MEM or MEM/WB back to ALU inputs to eliminate RAW stalls.

- [x] **ARM-Specific**: ARM7 = 3-stage, ARM9 = 5-stage, Cortex-A = 13+ stage.

- [x] **CPI**: Unpipelined = $k$, Ideal pipelined = **1**, With stalls = $1 + \frac{s}{n}$.

- [x] **Unit Conversions**: $1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6$ IPS, $1$ MIPS = $10^6$ instructions/second. Clock period $T = 1/f$.

- [x] **Always**: Show the formula, substitute values with units, and box the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
