The address is decremented **before** each store. - Start: `Rn − 4N` - End: `Rn − 4` - Writeback: `Rn ← Rn − 4N` **Memory Layout Diagram (3 registers, `R0`, `R1`, `R2`):** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IA2["STMIA Rn"] IAa["Rn+0: R0"] --> IAb["Rn+4: R1"] --> IAc["Rn+8: R2"] end subgraph IB2["STMIB Rn"] IBa["Rn+4: R0"] --> IBb["Rn+8: R1"] --> IBc["Rn+12: R2"] end subgraph DA2["STMDA Rn"] DAa["Rn-8: R0"] --> DAb["Rn-4: R1"] --> DAc["Rn+0: R2"] end subgraph DB2["STMDB Rn"] DBa["Rn-12: R0"] --> DBb["Rn-8: R1"] --> DBc["Rn-4: R2"] end ``` **PUSH Implementation:** The `PUSH` mnemonic is a **syntactic alias** defined as: $$\text{PUSH} \;\; \{reglist\} \;\;\equiv\;\; \text{STMDB} \;\; SP!, \;\; \{reglist\}$$ This decrements the stack pointer first (Full Descending stack convention) and stores registers in descending memory order. It is the canonical **function prologue** instruction in ARM AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard). *(7 marks: 1 mark per variant description, 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for PUSH alias explanation.)* --- #### (b) Given `R4 = 0x80000010` and `R5 = 0x0000ABCD`, evaluate the following sequence of instructions and determine the final values of `R4`, `R5`, and the contents of memory locations `0x80000010` to `0x80000013`. (7 marks) ``` STRB R5, [R4], #1 STRH R5, [R4, #2]! STR R5, [R4] ``` **Model Solution:** **Initial state:** `R4 = 0x80000010`, `R5 = 0x0000ABCD`. --- **Instruction 1: `STRB R5, [R4], #1`** - `Rd = R5`, `Rn = R4`, post-indexed, byte transfer. - Effective address (post-indexed): $EA = R_n = 0x80000010$ - Byte written: $\text{Mem}[0x80000010] \leftarrow (R5 \;\&\; \texttt{0xFF}) = 0xCD$ - Writeback (mandatory): $R4 \leftarrow R4 + 1 = 0x80000011$ **State after Instruction 1:** - `R4 = 0x80000011` - `R5 = 0x0000ABCD` - `Mem[0x80000010] = 0xCD` --- **Instruction 2: `STRH R5, [R4, #2]!`** - `Rd = R5`, `Rn = R4`, pre-indexed with writeback, halfword transfer. - Compute EA: $EA = R4 + 2 = 0x80000011 + 0x02 = 0x80000013$ - Writeback: $R4 \leftarrow R4 + 2 = 0x80000013$ - Halfword written: $\text{Mem}[0x80000013] \leftarrow 0xCD$ and $\text{Mem}[0x80000014] \leftarrow 0xAB$ (little-endian, LSB at lower address). **State after Instruction 2:** - `R4 = 0x80000013` - `R5 = 0x0000ABCD` - `Mem[0x80000010] = 0xCD` (unchanged from Instruction 1) - `Mem[0x80000013] = 0xCD`, `Mem[0x80000014] = 0xAB` --- **Instruction 3: `STR R5, [R4]`** - `Rd = R5`, `Rn = R4`, pre-indexed **without** writeback (no `!`), word transfer. - Compute EA: $EA = R4 + 0 = 0x80000013$ - Word written (little-endian): - $\text{Mem}[0x80000013] \leftarrow 0xCD$ - $\text{Mem}[0x80000014] \leftarrow 0xAB` - $\text{Mem}[0x80000015] \leftarrow 0x00$ - $\text{Mem}[0x80000016] \leftarrow 0x00` - No writeback: `R4` remains unchanged. **Final State after all three instructions:** - `R4 = 0x80000013` - `R5 = 0x0000ABCD` (unchanged throughout) - Memory at requested range `0x80000010` to `0x80000013`: - `Mem[0x80000010] = 0xCD` (set by `STRB`) - `Mem[0x80000011] = 0xCD` (set by `STRH` LSB) — *Wait, correction:* the halfword at `0x80000013` overwrites only `0x80000013` and `0x80000014`. So `Mem[0x80000011]` retains its prior value (initially undefined, or whatever was there). - `Mem[0x80000012] =` unchanged from initial memory. - `Mem[0x80000013] = 0xCD` (last byte of the final word store). > [!NOTE] > The requested range `0x80000010` to `0x80000013` is most directly affected by Instructions 1 and 3, yielding: > - `0x80000010 = 0xCD` > - `0x80000011 =` indeterminate (not written in this sequence) > - `0x80000012 =` indeterminate > - `0x80000013 = 0xCD` (overwritten by both `STRH` and `STR`) *(7 marks: 2 marks per instruction step, 1 mark for final state synthesis.)* --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Store Instruction Questions:** > 1. **Endianness Reversal:** Forgetting to swap byte order between little- and big-endian layouts — examiners specifically look for this. > 2. **Writeback in Post-Indexed:** Some students write `STR R0, [R1]` and claim there is no writeback. **Post-indexed ALWAYS writes back** — there is no syntactic option to disable it. > 3. **Pre vs. Post Confusion:** Mixing up the timing of the address computation. Use the mnemonic: **"Pre = prepare first, post = postpone the update."** > 4. **Alignment Faults:** Forgetting that `STR` requires word-aligned addresses and `STRH` requires halfword-aligned addresses. An unaligned access triggers a `Data Abort` exception in ARMv7+ with alignment checking enabled. > 5. **Source vs. Destination Confusion:** In `STR`, the register *before* the comma is the **source** (data), and the bracketed expression is the **destination** (address). The reverse applies to `LDR`. > 6. **Forgetting the `B` Flag in `STM`:** The base register `Rn` for `STM` should generally be a **word-aligned** address; otherwise behaviour is UNPREDICTABLE on some ARM cores. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Store instructions transfer data FROM a register TO memory.** The source register is `Rd`; the destination address is computed from the base register `Rn` and an offset. - The three addressing modes are **Pre-indexed (no writeback)**, **Pre-indexed with writeback (`!`)**, and **Post-indexed (always writeback)**. - Formulae: Pre-indexed uses $EA = R_n + \text{offset}$; Post-indexed uses $EA = R_n$ and then updates $R_n$. - Transfer width is selected by the suffix: `STR` = **Word (32 bits)**, `STRH` = **Halfword (16 bits)**, `STRB` = **Byte (8 bits)**, `STRD` = **Doubleword (64 bits)**. - **Endianness is critical:** Little-endian (E=0) stores LSB at the lowest address; big-endian (E=1) stores MSB at the lowest address. - **Alignment requirements:** Word access needs `EA ≡ 0 (mod 4)`; halfword access needs `EA ≡ 0 (mod 2)`. - The `T` suffix (e.g., `STRT`, `STRBT`) forces a **User-mode (unprivileged) memory access**, regardless of the current privilege level. - The `STM` instruction stores **multiple registers** to memory; the four variants (`IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB`) differ in the direction and timing of address progression. - The `PUSH` mnemonic is an alias for `STMDB SP!, {reglist}`, and `POP` is an alias for `LDMIA SP!, {reglist}`. - **Scaled register offsets** (e.g., `[Rn, Rm, LSL #2]`) leverage ARM's barrel shifter to compute array indices in a single instruction. - The **writeback `!`** suffix applies to all pre-indexed instructions; in post-indexed, writeback is implicit and cannot be omitted. - Always check the **condition code** (`{cond}`) — conditional store instructions only execute if the CPSR flags satisfy the condition.

topics.title: Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly** is the systematic process of translating high-level language (HLL) statements written in C into their equivalent low-level ARM7/ARM Cortex assembly language representations. An assignment statement in C of the general form: $$ \text{LHS (variable)} \; \texttt{=} \; \text{RHS (expression)} $$ is decomposed into a sequence of ARM assembly instructions that: 1. Evaluate the right-hand side (RHS) expression using registers. 2. Store the computed result into the memory location corresponding to the left-hand side (LHS) variable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEECT524, Module 2):** > *"Translation of C assignment statements to ARM assembly using LDR, STR, MOV, and the LDR pseudo-instruction. Covers global, local, and parameter variables, immediate values, and indirect memory access."* The canonical ARM data-movement instructions required for this translation are: | Instruction | Description | |-------------|-------------| | `LDR` | **Load Register** – Read 32-bit word from memory into a register | | `STR` | **Store Register** – Write 32-bit word from a register into memory | | `MOV` | **Move** – Copy a value between registers or load an immediate | | `LDR Rd, =label` | **LDR Pseudo-Instruction** – Load a 32-bit constant or address into a register | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Moving Company" Analogy:** > > Think of a C assignment like writing a postcard. The **variable on the LHS** is the *destination mailbox* (a labeled box in memory). The **expression on the RHS** is the *message to be written*. > > - The **MOV** instruction is like the postal worker writing a quick note on the postcard before delivery. > - The **LDR** instruction is like the worker *fetching an already-typed letter* from another mailbox and bringing it to the destination. > - The **STR** instruction is the act of *placing the final letter into the destination mailbox*. > - The **LDR pseudo-instruction (=)** is a special request to the postal service to print a brand-new address label for a mailbox it has never seen before. > > Without these three actions, the message can never reach its destination! ### 1.3 Why This Matters (Physical/Engineering Reality) In embedded systems (the core domain of ARM), compiled C directly maps to these assembly sequences. Understanding this mapping allows engineers to: - **Debug** optimized firmware when source-level debugging fails. - **Optimize** critical inner loops by hand-coding assembly. - **Verify** that the compiler is producing efficient code (especially for interrupt service routines). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard ARM Register Convention Used Throughout:** > - **R0–R12:** General-purpose working registers > - **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer > - **R14 (LR):** Link Register > - **R15 (PC):** Program Counter > - **Condition Flags:** N, Z, C, V (in CPSR) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fundamental Translation Rules When converting a C assignment statement to ARM assembly, follow this **3-Phase Translation Protocol**: 1. **Phase 1 — Address Loading:** Load the memory address of every variable referenced in the statement into a register using `LDR Rd, =var`. 2. **Phase 2 — RHS Evaluation:** Compute the RHS expression using registers, `MOV` for constants, `LDR` for variables. 3. **Phase 3 — Result Storage:** Store the final register value into the memory address of the LHS variable using `STR`. ### 2.2 Categorization of C Variables in ARM ARM distinguishes between three types of variables, each with its own addressing strategy: | Variable Type | Lifetime | Storage | Addressing in Assembly | |---------------|----------|---------|------------------------| | **Global** | Entire program | Static RAM (data segment) | Direct via `LDR Rd, =var` + `LDR/STR` | | **Local** | Function body | Stack frame (SP-relative) | Indirect via `LDR Rd, [SP, #offset]` | | **Parameter** | Function call | Stack frame / Registers (R0–R3) | Direct in register or `LDR Rd, [SP, #offset]` | | **Constant** | Compile time | Code segment (literal pool) | `LDR Rd, =const` (pseudo-instruction) | ### 2.3 Instruction Format Cheat Sheet The general ARM instruction format is: $$ \texttt{\{\} Rd, Rn, } $$ For the assignment translation, the key addressing modes are: $$ \texttt{LDR Rd, [Rn, \#offset]} \quad \text{— Pre-indexed (no writeback)} $$ $$ \texttt{LDR Rd, [Rn, \#offset]!} \quad \text{— Pre-indexed with writeback} $$ $$ \texttt{LDR Rd, [Rn], \#offset} \quad \text{— Post-indexed with writeback} $$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | ARM Assembly Construct | Example | |---------|------------------------|---------| | Load a variable's **address** | `LDR Rn, =var` | `LDR R1, =a` | | **Read** variable's value | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` | `LDR R0, [R1]` | | **Write** register into variable | `STR Rd, [Rn]` | `STR R0, [R1]` | | **Move** an 8-bit immediate | `MOV Rd, #imm` | `MOV R0, #5` | | **Move** a 32-bit constant | `LDR Rd, =imm` | `LDR R0, =0x12345678` | | **Indirect** (pointer dereference) | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` | `LDR R0, [R1]` | | **Stack** local access | `LDR Rd, [SP, #offset]` | `LDR R0, [SP, #4]` | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):** > > - `LDR Rd, =X` → **Pseudo-instruction** (assembler expands it; loads a constant or address). The literal `=` sign is mandatory. > - `LDR Rd, [X]` → **Real instruction** (loads from memory pointed to by X). Brackets denote indirection. > > These two have **completely different meanings** and KTU exams frequently test this! ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This conversion is the foundation of: - **Compiler back-ends** (the code generator phase of GCC for ARM targets). - **Reverse engineering** tools like Ghidra/IDA Pro that reconstruct C from assembly. - **Bare-metal embedded development** for ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (STM32, LPC, etc.) where startup code in assembly interfaces with C `main()`. ### 2.6 The Memory Map (Logical View) Variables declared in C are placed by the linker in a logical memory map: | Segment | Address Range | Contains | |---------|---------------|----------| | Code (Text) | `0x00000000` | Machine instructions, literal pool | | Read-Only Data | `0x0001XXXX` | `const` variables, string literals | | Initialized Data | `0x20000000` | Initialized globals | | Zero-Initialized (BSS) | `0x2001XXXX` | Uninitialized globals | | Heap | Grows upward | `malloc`/`free` | | Stack | Grows downward (high addr) | Local variables, parameters | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Case 1: Simple Constant Assignment **C Statement:** ```c int a = 0x25; ``` **Translation Logic:** 1. We need to reserve a 32-bit memory location and label it `a`. 2. The literal value `0x25` is a 32-bit constant. 3. It does **not fit** in an 8-bit rotated immediate, so we must use the `LDR` pseudo-instruction. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm LDR R1, =a ; R1 ← address of variable a LDR R0, =0x25 ; R0 ← constant 0x25 STR R0, [R1] ; memory[a] ← R0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not `MOV R0, #0x25`?** > ARM's `MOV` with an immediate operand only supports an **8-bit value rotated by an even number of bits**. The value `0x25` does fit here, but for general constants (e.g., `0x12345678`), `MOV` cannot represent them and `LDR Rd, =const` is mandatory. ### 3.2 Case 2: Variable-to-Variable Assignment **C Statement:** ```c a = b; ``` **Translation Logic:** 1. Load the address of `b` into R1. 2. Read the value of `b` from memory into R0 using `[R1]`. 3. Load the address of `a` into R2. 4. Store the value from R0 into memory location of `a`. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm LDR R1, =b ; R1 ← &b LDR R0, [R1] ; R0 ← b LDR R2, =a ; R2 ← &a STR R0, [R2] ; a ← R0 ``` ### 3.3 Case 3: Arithmetic Expression Assignment **C Statement:** ```c a = b + c - d; ``` **Translation Logic:** 1. Load all three variable addresses. 2. Load their values. 3. Perform the arithmetic in the CPU. 4. Store the result. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm LDR R4, =b LDR R0, [R4] ; R0 ← b LDR R5, =c LDR R1, [R5] ; R1 ← c LDR R6, =d LDR R2, [R6] ; R2 ← d ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 ← b + c SUB R0, R0, R2 ; R0 ← b + c - d LDR R3, =a STR R0, [R3] ; a ← R0 ``` ### 3.4 Case 4: Local Variable in a Function **C Code:** ```c void foo(void) { int x, y, z; x = 10; y = x + 2; z = y; } ``` **Translation Logic (Stack-Frame Based):** Within a function, local variables are placed on the stack. The compiler allocates a **stack frame** by decrementing SP. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm foo SUB SP, SP, #12 ; Reserve 12 bytes (3 ints × 4) ; Assume: [SP,#0]=x, [SP,#4]=y, [SP,#8]=z MOV R0, #10 STR R0, [SP, #0] ; x = 10 LDR R0, [SP, #0] ; R0 = x ADD R0, R0, #2 ; R0 = x + 2 STR R0, [SP, #4] ; y = x + 2 LDR R0, [SP, #4] STR R0, [SP, #8] ; z = y ADD SP, SP, #12 ; Deallocate stack frame MOV PC, LR ; Return ``` > [!NOTE] > **Memory Frame Visualization:** | Address Offset | Holds | Variable | |----------------|-------|----------| | `[SP+8]` | unused | — | | `[SP+4]` | y | int y | | `[SP+0]` | x | int x | | `[SP-4]` | z | int z | ### 3.5 Case 5: Pointer / Indirect Access **C Statement:** ```c *p = 5; ``` **Translation Logic:** 1. R1 already contains the pointer `p` (an address). 2. Write the constant `5` directly into `[R1]`. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm MOV R0, #5 STR R0, [R1] ; *p = 5 ``` ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which LDR Do I Use?" | Situation | Correct Instruction | Reason | |-----------|---------------------|--------| | Load a **constant** that fits in 8 bits | `MOV Rd, #imm` | Single instruction, no memory fetch | | Load a **large constant** (>8 bits) | `LDR Rd, =const` | Assembler creates a literal pool entry | | Load an **address of a variable** | `LDR Rd, =var` | Assembler substitutes the symbol's address | | Read a **variable's value** | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` | Indirect memory read | | Store to a **variable** | `STR Rd, [Rn]` | Indirect memory write | | Read a **stack-relative local** | `LDR Rd, [SP, #off]` | Indexed addressing using SP | ### 3.7 Complete Translation Example (Exam-Style) **C Source:** ```c int g; // global variable int main(void) { g = 100; g = g + 25; return 0; } ``` **Step-by-Step Translation:** 1. `g = 100;` → Load address of g, load constant 100, store. 2. `g = g + 25;` → Load address, read g, add 25, store. **ARM Assembly:** ```armasm AREA |.text|, CODE, READONLY ENTRY EXPORT main main LDR R1, =g MOV R0, #100 STR R0, [R1] ; g = 100 LDR R0, [R1] ; R0 = g ADD R0, R0, #25 ; R0 = g + 25 STR R0, [R1] ; g = g + 25 MOV R0, #0 BX LR ; return 0 AREA |.data|, DATA, READWRITE g DCD 0 ; 32-bit word initialized to 0 END ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Translation Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[C Assignment Statement] --> B{Identify LHS Variable} A --> C{Identify RHS Expression} B --> D[Load Address: LDR Rn, =var] C --> E{Expression Type?} E --> F[Constant] E --> G[Variable] E --> H[Arithmetic] F --> F1[MOV Rd, #imm OR LDR Rd, =const] G --> G1[LDR Rd, [Rn]] H --> H1[Multiple LDR + ADD/SUB/MUL] F1 --> I[Store Result: STR Rd, [Rn]] G1 --> I H1 --> I I --> J[Final ARM Assembly] D --> J ``` ### 4.2 Register–Memory Interaction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU[ARM CPU Core] R0[R0] R1[R1] R2[R2] R3[R3] SP[SP / R13] end subgraph MEM[Memory] A[Variable a] B[Variable b] C[Variable c] STK[Stack Frame: x, y, z] end R1 -- LDR Rd, =a --> A R0 -- LDR Rd, [R1] <-- A R0 -- STR Rd, [R2] --> A R0 -- LDR Rd, [SP, #off] <--> STK A -.Address.-> R1 B -.Address.-> R2 ``` ### 4.3 Three-Phase Translation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Phase 1: Address Loading
LDR Rn, =variable] --> P2[Phase 2: RHS Evaluation
LDR Rd, [Rn] / MOV / arithmetic] P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Result Storage
STR Rd, [Rn]] P3 --> OUT[Final Assembly Code] ``` ### 4.4 Pseudo vs Real LDR — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Need to load a value] --> Q1{Is it a constant or address?} Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use LDR Rd, =X
PSEUDO-INSTRUCTION] R1 --> R2[Assembler places X in literal pool
and generates real LDR from PC] Q1 -- No, need memory contents --> S1[Use LDR Rd, [Rn]
REAL INSTRUCTION] S1 --> S2[Hardware reads memory at address in Rn] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:** > The single most common mistake in KTU answer scripts is writing `LDR R0, b` (forgetting the `=`) when the address of `b` is needed, or writing `LDR R0, =b` when the *value* of `b` is required. Use this diagram as a mental checkpoint before writing any `LDR`. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** Differentiate between the ARM `LDR` instruction and the `LDR` pseudo-instruction with one example each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `LDR Rd, =label` (Pseudo) | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` (Real) | |--------|---------------------------|------------------------| | Meaning | Loads a **32-bit constant or address** into Rd | Loads a **32-bit value from memory** pointed to by Rn | | Expansion | Assembler expands to a PC-relative load from the literal pool | Encoded as a single machine instruction | | Brackets | None (no `[ ]`) | Square brackets `[ ]` indicate indirection | | Example | `LDR R1, =SUM` | `LDR R0, [R1]` | In the pseudo form, the assembler stores the constant/address in a literal pool near the code and generates a PC-relative load. In the real form, the CPU performs an indirect memory read using the address held in `Rn`. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** What is the role of the `STR` instruction in converting a C assignment statement to ARM assembly? **Model Answer:** The `STR` (**Store Register**) instruction writes the contents of a CPU register into a memory location. In the context of C-to-ARM translation, `STR` is the **final step** of any assignment statement. Once the RHS expression has been evaluated in a register, `STR Rd, [Rn]` deposits the result into the memory address of the LHS variable held in `Rn`. Without `STR`, the computed result would remain only in the register and the C variable would not be updated. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting the `=` in `LDR Rd, =var`** when an address is required → loses 2 marks immediately. > - Using `MOV Rd, #large_const` for constants >255 (or rotated forms) → instruction is invalid; examiner deducts 1 mark and shows correct usage. > - For **local variables**, students often write `LDR R0, =x` instead of `LDR R0, [SP, #offset]` → loses 2–3 marks. > - **Forgetting to update SP** when allocating/deallocating a stack frame → the function corrupts memory, but students rarely get penalized for this in KTU unless explicitly asked. > - **Confusing store-back order** when the LHS and RHS share a variable (e.g., `a = a + 1`) — must read BEFORE writing. --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Convert the following C program to equivalent ARM assembly: ```c int a, b, c; void main(void) { a = 10; b = 20; c = a + b; } ``` **Model Solution:** ```armasm AREA |.text|, CODE, READONLY ENTRY EXPORT main main LDR R1, =a MOV R0, #10 STR R0, [R1] ; a = 10 LDR R2, =b MOV R0, #20 STR R0, [R2] ; b = 20 LDR R1, =a LDR R0, [R1] ; R0 = a LDR R2, =b LDR R3, [R2] ; R3 = b ADD R0, R0, R3 ; R0 = a + b LDR R4, =c STR R0, [R4] ; c = a + b MOV PC, LR END ``` | Line | C Statement | Assembly Constructs | |------|-------------|---------------------| | 1–3 | `a = 10;` | Load address of a, MOV constant, STR | | 4–6 | `b = 20;` | Load address of b, MOV constant, STR | | 7–12 | `c = a + b;` | Load both, ADD, store result | **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the role of the literal pool in ARM assembly with reference to the `LDR` pseudo-instruction. **Model Solution:** When the assembler encounters `LDR Rd, =constant` or `LDR Rd, =symbol`, it cannot encode the 32-bit value directly into a single ARM instruction (since ARM instructions are fixed 32 bits). The assembler therefore: 1. Places the constant in a **literal pool** — a region of memory within the code segment, typically placed at the end of a subroutine or at a 4 KB boundary. 2. Replaces the pseudo-instruction with a real **PC-relative load**: `LDR Rd, [PC, #offset]`, where the offset points to the literal-pool entry. 3. At runtime, the CPU loads the 32-bit constant from the literal pool into `Rd`. **Example:** ```armasm LDR R0, =0x12345678 ``` is expanded by the assembler to: ```armasm LDR R0, [PC, #offset_to_literal] ... ; Literal pool: DCD 0x12345678 ``` This mechanism allows ARM to load any 32-bit constant using a single source-level pseudo-instruction, while preserving the fixed-length 32-bit instruction encoding. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] Convert the following C function to ARM assembly, clearly showing stack-frame allocation: ```c int add(int a, int b) { int s; s = a + b; return s; } ``` **Model Solution:** ARM APCS (ARM Procedure Call Standard) passes the first 4 parameters in `R0–R3` and returns the result in `R0`. ```armasm AREA |.text|, CODE, READONLY EXPORT add add SUB SP, SP, #4 ; Reserve 4 bytes for local 's' STR LR, [SP, #0] ; Save LR (optional but good practice) ; R0 = a (1st param), R1 = b (2nd param) ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = a + b STR R0, [SP, #0] ; s = a + b (or skip; not strictly needed) LDR LR, [SP, #0] ADD SP, SP, #4 ; Deallocate stack BX LR ; Return (R0 holds result) END ``` **Explanation of constructs:** - `SUB SP, SP, #4` allocates 4 bytes for the local variable `s`. - Parameters arrive in R0 and R1 per the AAPCS. - The return value is left in R0 (sum). - `ADD SP, SP, #4` restores the stack pointer. - `BX LR` returns to the caller. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between the following pairs of instructions, with a worked example for each: (i) `LDR R0, =0x200` versus `MOV R0, #0x200` (ii) `LDR R0, [R1]` versus `STR R0, [R1]` **Model Solution:** **(i) `LDR R0, =0x200` vs `MOV R0, #0x200`** - `MOV R0, #0x200` is a **real ARM instruction** that puts the immediate 0x200 into R0. ARM allows 8-bit values rotated by an even number of positions; 0x200 = 0x02 rotated left by 8 → valid. The CPU executes this in a single cycle using the rotated-immediate hardware. - `LDR R0, =0x200` is a **pseudo-instruction**. The assembler stores `0x200` in a literal pool and replaces the line with a PC-relative `LDR`. This form is required for any 32-bit constant (e.g., 0x12345678) that cannot be represented as a rotated 8-bit value. **Worked example:** Suppose the C statement is `int x = 0x12345678;`. `MOV R0, #0x12345678` is **invalid** (assembler error). The only legal form is `LDR R0, =0x12345678`, which the assembler expands using a literal pool. **(ii) `LDR R0, [R1]` vs `STR R0, [R1]`** - `LDR R0, [R1]` reads a 32-bit word from the memory address held in R1 and writes it into R0. It is a **memory-to-register** transfer. - `STR R0, [R1]` reads R0 and writes it into the memory address held in R1. It is a **register-to-memory** transfer. **Worked example:** Converting `a = b;`: ```armasm LDR R1, =b ; R1 holds address of b LDR R0, [R1] ; LDR: memory[b] → R0 LDR R2, =a ; R2 holds address of a STR R0, [R2] ; STR: R0 → memory[a] ``` Here the `LDR` fetches b's value into a register, and the `STR` writes the register value into a. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Three phases** of C-to-ARM conversion: **Load address → Evaluate RHS → Store result**. - [x] `LDR Rd, =var` → **Pseudo-instruction** for loading **addresses or 32-bit constants** (uses literal pool). - [x] `LDR Rd, [Rn]` → **Real instruction** for **reading from memory**. - [x] `STR Rd, [Rn]` → **Real instruction** for **writing to memory**. - [x] `MOV Rd, #imm` → **Real instruction** for **small immediates** (8-bit rotated). - [x] **Global variables** → `LDR Rn, =var` then `LDR/STR [Rn]`. - [x] **Local variables** → `LDR/STR [SP, #offset]` inside a stack frame; remember to `SUB SP` and `ADD SP` around the function. - [x] **Function parameters** → First four passed in R0–R3 (AAPCS); return value in R0. - [x] **Pointers (`*p`)** → Indirection `LDR/STR [Rn]`; `p` itself lives in a register. - [x] **For combined read/write of same variable** (e.g., `a = a + 1`), always **read first, then write** to avoid corruption. - [x] **Literal pool** is a memory region holding 32-bit constants, accessed via PC-relative addressing. - [x] **Keywords for KTU 2024:** LDR, STR, MOV, pseudo-instruction, literal pool, AAPCS, stack frame, indirect addressing, pre-indexed, post-indexed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** > When a question says "convert to assembly," always show **all three phases explicitly**: (1) address loading with `=`, (2) value fetching, (3) STR the result. Examiners award 2–3 marks just for showing this structured approach, even if a single instruction is wrong.

topics.title: Memory Address Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Memory Address Calculations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition **Memory Address Calculation** in ARM architecture refers to the systematic process of determining the *Effective Address (EA)* of an operand in memory, using one of the seven supported addressing modes. The ARM processor computes the EA by combining a *base register* value with a flexible *offset* (which can be an immediate constant, a register, or a shifted register) prior to, or following, the actual data transfer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on **Indexed Addressing Modes** — specifically *Pre-indexed*, *Post-indexed*, and their *write-back* variants — as these directly map to how compilers generate array/structure access code. Every memory address calculation problem in the ESE will test one of these four indexed forms. The generic ARM address calculation expression is: $$ \text{Effective Address (EA)} = \text{Base Register} \pm \text{Offset} $$ where the **Offset** can be one of three types: - An **unsigned 12-bit immediate** value (range **0 to 4095**) - A **register** value - A **register with optional shift operation** (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) --- ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The GPS Navigation System** > > Imagine you are asking a GPS to find a specific coffee shop: > - The **Base Register** is your *current city* (a known, fixed location). > - The **Offset** is the *distance and direction* from that city to the shop. > - The **Effective Address** is the *final destination coordinates* the GPS computes. > > Just as a GPS supports different ways of describing the route (e.g., "Go 5 km North", "Go 2 blocks past the hospital", "Stop at the next landmark"), ARM supports multiple addressing modes for calculating memory addresses: > > | GPS Instruction Style | ARM Addressing Mode | > |---|---| > | "Plot course first, *then* move to the new city" | **Pre-indexed with write-back** | > | "Use the hospital as a *temporary* marker, but don't update your home city" | **Pre-indexed without write-back** | > | "Walk to the next shop on this street" | **Post-indexed** | > | "Go straight to 5th Avenue" | **Immediate offset** | > > The *base register* is the **anchor point**; the *offset* is the **displacement**; the *effective address* is the **final computed memory location** the processor accesses. The two physical constants to remember are: - **ARM word size = 32 bits = 4 bytes** - **Address increment for sequential access = 4 bytes (for word), 2 bytes (for halfword), 1 byte (for byte)** --- ## Why Memory Address Calculation Matters in ARM ARM is a **Load/Store architecture** — meaning the CPU cannot directly operate on data sitting in memory. Every memory operand must first be *loaded* into a register (via `LDR`/`LDRB`/`LDRH`) or *stored* from a register (via `STR`/`STRB`/`STRH`). Therefore, the address of that memory location must be computed *before* the transfer occurs. This is precisely what **memory address calculation** accomplishes, and it is handled in a dedicated hardware stage of the ARM pipeline. > [!TIP] > **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse the *address calculation* (which determines *where* in memory to read/write) with the *data transfer* (the actual read/write operation). In ARM, these are two distinct steps, and the addressing mode dictates *how* the calculation interacts with the base register update. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## The Seven ARM Addressing Modes — Structured Logic ARM supports **seven** distinct memory address calculation modes. Understanding each requires a structured breakdown: ### Group A: Non-Indexed Modes (Direct / Offset Modes) 1. **Immediate Offset Addressing (Pre-computed, no write-back)** - **Syntax:** `[Rn, #offset]` - **Logic:** The EA is computed as `Rn + offset` *before* the access. The base register `Rn` is **NOT** updated. - **Use Case:** Accessing a fixed structure member (e.g., a fixed field in a struct). 2. **Register Offset Addressing (Pre-computed, no write-back)** - **Syntax:** `[Rn, Rm]` - **Logic:** EA = `Rn + Rm`. `Rn` is unchanged. 3. **Scaled Register Offset Addressing** - **Syntax:** `[Rn, Rm, LSL #n]` - **Logic:** EA = `Rn + (Rm << n)`. Allows flexible byte/halfword/word traversal. ### Group B: Indexed Modes (Write-back Involved) 4. **Pre-Indexed Addressing (with write-back)** - **Syntax:** `[Rn, #offset]!` - **Logic:** EA = `Rn + offset` *first*, then `Rn` is **updated** to this new value. - **Use Case:** Iterating through arrays (like a `for` loop pointer). 5. **Pre-Indexed Addressing (without write-back)** - **Syntax:** `[Rn, #offset]` - **Logic:** EA = `Rn + offset` for the access, but `Rn` remains at its *original* value. - **Use Case:** Random access into a data structure without disturbing the pointer. 6. **Post-Indexed Addressing (auto-update)** - **Syntax:** `[Rn], #offset` - **Logic:** EA = `Rn` (the *original* value of `Rn` is used for the access), *then* `Rn` is updated to `Rn + offset`. - **Use Case:** FIFO queues, stack operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The exclamation mark `!` is the KTU's favorite punctuation.** Whenever you see `!` in an ARM instruction, it signifies **write-back** — meaning the base register is updated with the computed EA. This is the single most-tested concept in address calculation problems. ### Group C: PC-Relative (Special Case) 7. **PC-Relative Addressing** - Used implicitly by `LDR` literal pool loads. EA = `PC + offset`. --- ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Addressing Mode** | **Syntax** | **EA Calculation** | **Base Register After?** | **Typical Use** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Immediate Offset | `[Rn, #imm12]` | EA = Rn + imm12 | **Unchanged** | Read struct field | | Register Offset | `[Rn, Rm]` | EA = Rn + Rm | **Unchanged** | Indexed array traversal | | Scaled Register | `[Rn, Rm, LSL #n]` | EA = Rn + (Rm << n) | **Unchanged** | Stride access | | Pre-indexed (no WB) | `[Rn, #imm12]` | EA = Rn + imm12 | **Unchanged** | One-time read with offset | | Pre-indexed (WB) | `[Rn, #imm12]!` | EA = Rn + imm12 | **Rn ← EA** | Array iteration (`for` loop) | | Post-indexed | `[Rn], #imm12` | EA = Rn, then Rn = Rn + imm12 | **Rn ← Rn + imm12** | Stack / queue traversal | | PC-Relative | `label` / `[PC, #off]` | EA = PC + 8 + off | **PC advances normally** | Literal pool / branch | > [!NOTE] > **Negative offsets** are encoded using two's complement within the 12-bit field. So `#−4` is valid and represented as the unsigned pattern `0xFFC`. ### Real-World Engineering Utility Memory address calculation forms the **microarchitectural backbone** of every C program you write. When you write `array[i]` in C, the ARM compiler emits an `LDR` or `STR` instruction with a *scaled register offset* (`[Rn, Rm, LSL #2]`) to access `i`-th word of the array. The same applies to structure access (`p->field` becomes a pre-indexed load). Mastering these modes is essential for: - **Compiler designers** generating optimal code - **Embedded firmware engineers** writing register-level drivers - **Reverse engineers** decoding stripped binaries # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Worked Example 1: Pre-Indexed with Write-Back (Most Common KTU Problem) > **Problem Statement:** > Given `R5 = 0x00008000`, execute the instruction: > ``` > LDR R6, [R5, #16]! > ``` > Show the *effective address*, the *value loaded into R6*, and the *updated value of R5*. Assume memory at address `0x00008010` contains the value `0x12345678`. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode.** The presence of the `!` operator means **Pre-Indexed Addressing with Write-Back**. **Step 2 — Compute the Effective Address.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R5} + \text{offset} \\ &= \text{0x00008000} + \text{16} \\ &= \text{0x00008000} + \text{0x10} \\ &= \text{0x00008010} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the data transfer.** The processor reads the 32-bit word at address `0x00008010` and loads it into `R6`. $$ \text{R6} \leftarrow \text{Memory[0x00008010]} = \text{0x12345678} $$ **Step 4 — Apply write-back.** Because of the `!`, the base register `R5` is updated with the EA: $$ \text{R5} \leftarrow \text{0x00008010} $$ **Final Result:** | Register | Value Before | Value After | |---|---|---| | R5 | 0x00008000 | **0x00008010** | | R6 | (don't care) | **0x12345678** | --- ## Worked Example 2: Post-Indexed Addressing (The "Tricky" Mode) > **Problem Statement:** > Given `R2 = 0x20000000` and memory at `0x20000000` = `0xAAAAAAAA`, execute: > ``` > LDR R3, [R2], #4 > ``` > Determine the EA, the value of R3, and the new value of R2. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode.** The offset `, #4` appears *after* the brackets, so this is **Post-Indexed Addressing**. **Step 2 — Compute the Effective Address.** In post-indexed mode, the EA is simply the *original* base register value: $$ \text{EA} = \text{R2} = \text{0x20000000} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the data transfer first.** The 32-bit word at `0x20000000` is loaded into `R3`: $$ \text{R3} \leftarrow \text{Memory[0x20000000]} = \text{0xAAAAAAAA} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the post-increment (write-back) AFTER the transfer.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{R2}_{\text{new}} &= \text{R2}_{\text{old}} + 4 \\ &= \text{0x20000000} + \text{0x4} \\ &= \text{0x20000004} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** | Register | Value Before | Value After | |---|---|---| | R2 | 0x20000000 | **0x20000004** | | R3 | (don't care) | **0xAAAAAAAA** | > [!WARNING] > **The Crucial Distinction:** In **pre-indexed**, the new base is computed *before* the load. In **post-indexed**, the load uses the *old* base, and the update happens *after*. Many students swap these two — losing 7 marks instantly on a 14-mark question. --- ## Worked Example 3: Scaled Register Offset (Array Access Simulation) > **Problem Statement:** > Given `R0 = 0x10000000` (base of an int array), `R1 = 3` (index), execute: > ``` > LDR R2, [R0, R1, LSL #2] > ``` > What is the EA, and what value is loaded into R2 if `array[3] = 0xDEADBEEF`? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode.** This is **Scaled Register Offset Addressing** (no write-back since no `!`). **Step 2 — Compute the offset using the shift.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset} &= \text{R1} \ll 2 \\ &= 3 \times 2^2 \\ &= 3 \times 4 \\ &= 12 = \text{0xC} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the Effective Address.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R0} + \text{Offset} \\ &= \text{0x10000000} + \text{0xC} \\ &= \text{0x1000000C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Perform the data transfer.** $$ \text{R2} \leftarrow \text{Memory[0x1000000C]} = \text{0xDEADBEEF} $$ **Step 5 — Note that R0 is NOT modified** (no `!` present). **Final Result:** | Register | Value Before | Value After | |---|---|---| | R0 | 0x10000000 | **0x10000000** (unchanged) | | R1 | 0x00000003 | 0x00000003 (unchanged) | | R2 | (don't care) | **0xDEADBEEF** | --- ## Worked Example 4: STR with Pre-Indexed Write-Back (Stack Emulation) > **Problem Statement:** > Given `SP = 0x40000800` and `R7 = 0xCAFEBABE`, execute: > ``` > STR R7, [SP, #-4]! > ``` > What is the new SP, and where is the value `0xCAFEBABE` stored? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Pre-Indexed with Write-Back (and negative offset).** **Step 2 — Compute the new EA (the negative offset is applied to the base first).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{SP} + (-4) \\ &= \text{0x40000800} - \text{0x4} \\ &= \text{0x400007FC} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Store the value at the EA.** $$ \text{Memory[0x400007FC]} \leftarrow \text{0xCAFEBABE} $$ **Step 4 — Write-back the updated base.** $$ \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{0x400007FC} $$ **Final Result:** This is precisely how a *full descending stack push* is implemented in ARM! SP now points to the newly pushed value. --- ## Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Summary Table of All Examples) | **Example** | **Instruction** | **Mode** | **EA** | **Base Register After** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LDR R6, [R5, #16]!` | Pre-Indexed WB | 0x00008010 | R5 ← 0x00008010 | | 2 | `LDR R3, [R2], #4` | Post-Indexed | 0x20000000 | R2 ← 0x20000004 | | 3 | `LDR R2, [R0, R1, LSL #2]` | Scaled Reg Offset | 0x1000000C | R0 unchanged | | 4 | `STR R7, [SP, #-4]!` | Pre-Indexed WB (neg) | 0x400007FC | SP ← 0x400007FC | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Memory Address Calculation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fetch ARM Instruction e.g. LDR STR] --> B{Is it a memory access instruction?} B -->|No| Z[Route to Data Processing Unit] B -->|Yes| C[Decode Addressing Mode Field] C --> D{Mode Type?} D -->|Pre-Indexed with WB| E[Compute EA equals Rn plus offset] D -->|Pre-Indexed no WB| F[Compute EA equals Rn plus offset] D -->|Post-Indexed| G[EA equals Rn original value] D -->|Register or Scaled| H[Compute EA equals Rn plus Rm shifted] E --> I[Perform Memory Read or Write] F --> I G --> J[Perform Memory Read or Write using old Rn] J --> K[Update Rn to Rn plus offset] I --> L{Write-Back Flag Set?} L -->|Yes| M[Update Rn to computed EA] L -->|No| N[Keep Rn unchanged] M --> O[Continue Pipeline] K --> O N --> O H --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart visualizes the *control logic* inside the ARM address generation unit. Notice how **post-indexed** memory access happens *before* the base register update, while **pre-indexed with write-back** updates the base register *as part of* the address computation step. This timing difference is what gives each mode its unique semantic meaning. --- ## Diagram 2: Memory Layout Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph mem[ARM Memory Address Space] A0[0x00008000
Base Address Rn] A1[0x00008004
offset plus 4] A2[0x00008008
offset plus 8] A3[0x00008010
offset plus 16 EA Target] A4[0x00008014
offset plus 20] end A0 -->|plus 16| A3 A2 -->|plus 8| A3 style A3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the Diagram:** The yellow-highlighted cell at `0x00008010` represents the *Effective Address (EA)* computed by the formula `EA = Rn + offset`. For a `LDR` instruction, the value at this address is fetched into the destination register. For a `STR` instruction, the source register's value is written to this address. --- ## Diagram 3: Comparison of Pre vs Post Indexed Timing ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as ARM Core participant Rn as Base Register Rn participant MEM as Memory Bus Note over CPU: PRE-INDEXED with write-back [Rn, #off]! CPU->>Rn: Read Rn value Rn-->>CPU: Returns Rn_old CPU->>CPU: Compute EA equals Rn_old plus offset CPU->>Rn: Write EA back to Rn (write-back enabled) CPU->>MEM: Access Memory at EA MEM-->>CPU: Returns data Note over CPU: POST-INDEXED [Rn], #off CPU->>Rn: Read Rn value Rn-->>CPU: Returns Rn_old CPU->>MEM: Access Memory at Rn_old MEM-->>CPU: Returns data CPU->>CPU: Compute new Rn equals Rn_old plus offset CPU->>Rn: Write new Rn back ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sequence Diagram Insight:** The `!` operator in pre-indexed mode causes the base register to be updated *before* the memory transaction completes. In post-indexed mode, the memory transaction uses the *original* base value, and the update happens *after* the transaction. This temporal ordering is the essence of the difference. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Pre-indexed addressing and Post-indexed addressing modes in ARM. Give one example instruction for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Pre-Indexed `[Rn, #off]!` | Post-Indexed `[Rn], #off` | |---|---|---| | **EA Computation** | EA = Rn + offset | EA = Rn (no offset added) | | **Base Register** | Updated *before* memory access | Updated *after* memory access | | **Syntax Marker** | `!` at the end | Offset after the brackets | | **Example** | `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` | `LDR R0, [R1], #4` | **Example Use Cases:** - **Pre-indexed:** Iterating through an array using a pointer that moves forward. - **Post-indexed:** Popping values from a stack (load first, then decrement pointer). --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is the role of the `!` symbol in ARM memory address calculation? Explain with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** The `!` symbol, when appended to the addressing mode brackets (e.g., `[Rn, #offset]!`), indicates **write-back**. This means the base register `Rn` is automatically updated with the *Effective Address* computed for the current memory access. **Example:** ``` LDR R0, [R5, #8]! @ R5 = 0x100, then EA = 0x108 @ R0 ← Memory[0x108] @ R5 is updated to 0x108 ``` Without `!` (`LDR R0, [R5, #8]`), `R5` would remain `0x100` and only `R0` would be loaded. --- ## PART B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Q (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the different indexed addressing modes supported by the ARM processor with suitable examples. Compare the behavior of the base register in each case.** **Model Solution:** The ARM processor supports three main indexed addressing modes: 1. **Pre-Indexed without Write-Back** `[Rn, #offset]` - EA = Rn + offset - Rn remains unchanged after the access - Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` — if R1=0x200, access at 0x204, R1 stays 0x200 2. **Pre-Indexed with Write-Back** `[Rn, #offset]!` - EA = Rn + offset - Rn is updated to EA after the address calculation - Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` — if R1=0x200, access at 0x204, R1 becomes 0x204 3. **Post-Indexed** `[Rn], #offset` - EA = Rn (original value) - Rn is updated to Rn + offset *after* the memory access - Example: `LDR R0, [R1], #4` — if R1=0x200, access at 0x200, then R1 becomes 0x204 **Comparison Table:** | Mode | EA Calculation | Order of Update | |---|---|---| | Pre-Indexed (no WB) | Rn + offset | No update | | Pre-Indexed (WB) | Rn + offset | Update *before* access | | Post-Indexed | Rn (no offset) | Update *after* access | **Real-world usage:** Pre-indexed with WB is used in `for` loops, post-indexed in stack/queue operations, and pre-indexed without WB for random access into fixed structures. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]: Consider the following ARM instruction sequence. Compute the Effective Address, the value loaded/stored, and the final value of the base register for each instruction. Given: R4 = 0x40000000, Memory[0x40000000] = 0xA1A1A1A1, Memory[0x40000004] = 0xB2B2B2B2, Memory[0x40000010] = 0xC3C3C3C3.** ``` (i) LDR R0, [R4, #4] (ii) LDR R1, [R4, #4]! (iii) LDR R2, [R4], #4 (iv) STR R3, [R4, #16]! (assume R3 = 0x12345678) ``` **Model Solution:** **(i) `LDR R0, [R4, #4]`** — Pre-Indexed, No Write-Back $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R4} + 4 = \text{0x40000000} + \text{0x4} = \text{0x40000004} \\ \text{R0} &\leftarrow \text{Memory[0x40000004]} = \text{0xB2B2B2B2} \\ \text{R4} &\text{ remains } \text{0x40000000} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) `LDR R1, [R4, #4]!`** — Pre-Indexed, With Write-Back $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R4} + 4 = \text{0x40000004} \\ \text{R1} &\leftarrow \text{Memory[0x40000004]} = \text{0xB2B2B2B2} \\ \text{R4} &\leftarrow \text{0x40000004} \quad \text{(write-back executed)} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) `LDR R2, [R4], #4`** — Post-Indexed $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R4} = \text{0x40000000} \quad \text{(note: not 0x40000004!)} \\ \text{R2} &\leftarrow \text{Memory[0x40000000]} = \text{0xA1A1A1A1} \\ \text{R4} &\leftarrow \text{R4} + 4 = \text{0x40000004} \quad \text{(update after access)} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) `STR R3, [R4, #16]!`** — Pre-Indexed Write-Back (Store) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA} &= \text{R4} + 16 = \text{0x40000000} + \text{0x10} = \text{0x40000010} \\ \text{Memory[0x40000010]} &\leftarrow \text{R3} = \text{0x12345678} \\ \text{R4} &\leftarrow \text{0x40000010} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Register State Summary:** | Register | Final Value | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | R0 | 0xB2B2B2B2 | Loaded from 0x40000004 | | R1 | 0xB2B2B2B2 | Loaded from 0x40000004 | | R2 | 0xA1A1A1A1 | Loaded from 0x40000000 (post-indexed) | | R3 | 0x12345678 | Source for store | | R4 | 0x40000010 | Updated by final write-back | --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]` **Q (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the Scaled Register Offset addressing mode in ARM. How is it used in array access? Demonstrate with an example where R0 = base address, R1 = index, and the array contains 32-bit integers.** **Model Solution:** **Scaled Register Offset Addressing** uses the syntax: ``` [Rn, Rm, LSL #shift] ``` **EA Calculation:** $$ \text{EA} = \text{Rn} + (\text{Rm} \ll \text{shift}) $$ The shift operation is performed in the **barrel shifter** during the address phase, so it does not consume an extra cycle. Common shift values: - `LSL #2` → 4-byte stride (word-sized array) - `LSL #1` → 2-byte stride (halfword array) - `LSL #3` → 8-byte stride (doubleword array) **Array Access Example:** In C: `int array[5]; value = array[i];` ARM assembly: ``` LDR R0, =array @ R0 = base address of array LDR R1, =3 @ R1 = i = 3 LDR R2, [R0, R1, LSL #2] @ EA = R0 + (3 << 2) = R0 + 12 @ R2 = array[3] ``` If R0 = 0x10000000, then EA = 0x1000000C, and `R2` is loaded with the integer stored at that address. **Key Advantage:** This single instruction replaces what would otherwise require a separate multiplication (`array[i] * 4` for word arrays). The barrel shifter's hardware performs the scaling in zero additional clock cycles. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]: A C function uses a stack to store local variables. Translate the following C code snippet into equivalent ARM assembly, showing the memory address calculations involved:** ```c void func() { int a = 10; int b = 20; int sum; sum = a + b; } ``` **Model Solution:** **Translation into ARM assembly** (using full descending stack with `SP` as base): ``` func: PUSH {R4, LR} @ Save callee-saved registers SUB SP, SP, #12 @ Allocate 12 bytes for a, b, sum (3 words) @ Pre-indexed with WB: SP ← SP − 12 MOV R0, #10 STR R0, [SP, #0] @ Store a = 10 at EA = SP+0 @ Pre-indexed, no WB: SP unchanged MOV R0, #20 STR R0, [SP, #4] @ Store b = 20 at EA = SP+4 LDR R1, [SP, #0] @ R1 = a (load from SP+0) LDR R2, [SP, #4] @ R2 = b (load from SP+4) ADD R3, R1, R2 @ R3 = a + b = 30 STR R3, [SP, #8] @ Store sum = 30 at EA = SP+8 ADD SP, SP, #12 @ Deallocate local variables POP {R4, PC} @ Restore and return ``` **Memory Address Calculation Table for the `STR`/`LDR` operations:** | Instruction | Addressing Mode | EA (assuming SP=0x7FFF_FFEC) | Operation | |---|---|---|---| | `SUB SP, SP, #12` | Pre-Indexed WB | 0x7FFF_FFE0 | SP ← 0x7FFF_FFE0 | | `STR R0, [SP, #0]` | Pre-Indexed no WB | 0x7FFF_FFE0 | Mem[EA] ← 10 | | `STR R0, [SP, #4]` | Pre-Indexed no WB | 0x7FFF_FFE4 | Mem[EA] ← 20 | | `STR R3, [SP, #8]` | Pre-Indexed no WB | 0x7FFF_FFE8 | Mem[EA] ← 30 | This demonstrates how **all three indexed modes** work in concert to manage the stack frame. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Confusing Pre and Post Indexed Order** — Most common mistake. In post-indexed, the memory access uses the *original* `Rn`. Writing `EA = Rn + 4` for `LDR R0, [R1], #4` will cost 4 out of 7 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the `!` Update** — If the instruction has `!`, the base register *must* be shown as updated in the final register state. Omitting this is a 2-mark deduction. > > 3. **Sign Error on Negative Offsets** — For `STR R0, [SP, #-4]!`, students often write `EA = SP + 4` instead of `EA = SP − 4`. The `−` sign in front of the immediate is part of the offset and must be respected. > > 4. **Endianness Confusion** — The byte/halfword loads (`LDRB`, `LDRH`) require understanding ARM's default **little-endian** byte ordering. KTU questions occasionally test this. > > 5. **Shift Field Misinterpretation** — For `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]`, students forget to *apply* the shift to R2. The address is `R1 + (R2<<2)`, not `R1 + R2 + 2`. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** > > ✅ **Seven Addressing Modes:** Immediate Offset, Register Offset, Scaled Register, Pre-indexed (no WB), Pre-indexed (WB), Post-indexed, PC-Relative. > > ✅ **The `!` is the Write-Back indicator** — its presence always means "update the base register." > > ✅ **Pre-indexed:** `EA = Rn + offset` computed *first*, then memory access, *then* (if `!`) update Rn. > > ✅ **Post-indexed:** Memory access uses `EA = Rn` (original), *then* update `Rn ← Rn + offset`. > > ✅ **The barrel shifter** computes scaled offsets in **zero extra cycles** — the LSL/LSR/ASR/ROR happens in the address generation phase. > > ✅ **Scaled register mode** is how ARM accesses `int array[i]` (LSL #2), `short array[i]` (LSL #1), and `double array[i]` (LSL #3). > > ✅ **Negative offsets** use two's complement representation within the 12-bit immediate field. > > ✅ **PC-relative addressing** uses `EA = PC + 8 + offset` (the `+8` is because the ARM pipeline has already fetched the next two instructions). > > ✅ **LDR/STR can never access memory directly without first computing an EA** — ARM is strictly Load/Store. > > ✅ **For KTU ESE:** Always present the EA calculation in a clear equation, then show register state changes in a table.

topics.title: Memory Addressing Examples: Translating C Pointer Expressions to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Addressing Examples: Translating C Pointer Expressions to Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Addressing** in ARM architecture refers to the mechanism by which the processor computes the **effective address** of an operand located in main memory, using a combination of a **base register**, an **offset** (immediate or register), and an **addressing mode** (offset, pre-indexed, or post-indexed). When translating C pointer expressions to ARM assembly, the compiler maps high-level pointer operations (dereferencing, arithmetic, array indexing, and structure access) into sequences of `LDR` (Load Register) and `STR` (Store Register) instructions whose effective addresses correspond exactly to the memory locations referenced by the C pointers.

In simpler terms, every time a C program writes `*p` or `array[i]`, the ARM compiler must generate an instruction that **calculates a numeric memory address and either reads from it or writes to it**. The calculation rules are dictated by ARM's flexible second-operand addressing syntax.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Number" Model

Imagine main memory as an enormous **street of houses**, where every house is exactly **1 byte wide** and has a unique numeric address (0, 1, 2, … 4,294,967,295 for a 32-bit system).

| C Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| A pointer variable (`int *p`) | A piece of paper on which you have **written a house number** |
| Dereferencing (`*p`) | **Walking to the house** whose number is on the paper |
| Pointer arithmetic (`p + 1`) | **Adding N to the number on the paper** (taking scaling into account) |
| Array indexing (`a[i]`) | Starting at the first house and **walking i * size_of_element houses** down the street |
| `&x` (address-of) | Writing down the **house number** of variable `x` |
| `NULL` pointer | A paper that says "**no house exists**" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** ARM is a **Load/Store architecture** — arithmetic instructions (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`) operate *only* on CPU registers. To read or write memory, you *must* use `LDR` (Load) or `STR` (Store). Every C pointer dereference compiles into one of these two instructions (or a sequence involving them).

### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024

The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and **cognitive levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze)**. Examiners regularly test whether a student can:

1. Read a short C fragment and produce equivalent ARM assembly.
2. Decode an ARM addressing expression back into C semantics.
3. Compute the **effective address** byte-by-byte for pre/post-indexed modes.
4. Understand **type scaling** (pointer arithmetic must respect element size).

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Pointer Formula (use this as your anchor):**
> $$\text{Effective Address} = \text{Base Register} \pm \text{Offset}$$
> where **Offset = (index} \times \text{element\_size)}$ in bytes. For `int` (4 bytes), index 3 yields offset 12. For `char` (1 byte), index 3 yields offset 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three ARM Memory Addressing Modes

ARM provides **three families** of memory addressing expressions (excluding the plain register-indirect `LDR Rd, [Rn]`):

| Mode | Syntax | When the Address is Computed | When the Base is Updated | Write-Back Form? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Offset** | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | At access time | **Not** updated | Use `!` to enable: `[Rn, #offset]!` |
| **Pre-indexed** | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]!` | **Before** access (offset included) | **Updated** before use | Always write-back |
| **Post-indexed** | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset` | **At** access (no offset yet) | **Updated** after access | Always write-back |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `!` (write-back) suffix is the single most-missed symbol in KTU exams.** In pre-indexed mode `[Rn, #4]!`, the base register `Rn` is *incremented* by 4 *before* the load occurs. In post-indexed `[Rn], #4`, the load uses the *original* `Rn` and the increment happens *afterwards*. These two behave identically *only* for simple loads — they diverge for `STR` when the destination is the same register used in the address.

### 2.2 Generic Effective-Address Equation

For any ARM memory instruction, the **effective address** EA is:

$$
\text{EA} = \text{Base} \;\pm\; \text{Offset}
$$

where:

$$
\text{Offset} = \begin{cases} \text{immediate} & \text{(constant in the instruction, 12-bit unsigned)}\\ \text{register} & \text{(a general-purpose register)}\\ \text{scaled register} & \text{(register, optionally shifted: LSL \#n, LSR \#n, ASR \#n, ROR \#n)} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** The "scaled register offset" form `LDR Rd, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]` is the **direct assembly counterpart of array indexing** `a[i]` when `Rn` points to the array base and `Rm` holds the index, because the `LSL #2` scales the byte offset by **4 (the size of an `int`)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | C Syntax | ARM Assembly | Effective Address | Write-Back? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address-of | `&x` | (implicit, no instruction) | EA of `x` in register | — |
| 2 | Dereference (read) | `r = *p` | `LDR r, [p]` | EA = `p` | No |
| 3 | Dereference (write) | `*p = r` | `STR r, [p]` | EA = `p` | No |
| 4 | Constant offset (read) | `r = *(p+4)` | `LDR r, [p, #4]` | EA = `p + 4` | No |
| 5 | Pre-indexed, write-back | `p += 4; r = *p` | `LDR r, [p, #4]!` | EA = `p + 4` | `p ← p + 4` |
| 6 | Post-indexed | `r = *p; p += 4` | `LDR r, [p], #4` | EA = `p` | `p ← p + 4` |
| 7 | Array indexed | `r = a[i]` | `LDR r, [a, i, LSL #2]` | EA = `a + 4i` | No |
| 8 | Char array | `r = str[i]` | `LDRB r, [str, i]` | EA = `str + i` | No |
| 9 | Struct field | `r = s.field` | `LDR r, [s, #offset]` | EA = `s + field_offset` | No |
| 10 | Double dereference | `r = **pp` | `LDR p, [pp]; LDR r, [p]` | EA = `[pp]`, then EA = `[p]` | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Byte vs Word rule:** Use `LDRB`/`STRB` for `char` (1 byte), `LDRH`/`STRH` for 16-bit `short`, and `LDR`/`STR` for 32-bit `int`/`long`/`float`. The **assembler does not auto-scale** — you must choose the right suffix.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of This Skill |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Firmware (IoT, Automotive ECU)** | Hand-coded assembly drivers for memory-mapped I/O registers; reading `*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE000E010` |
| **OS Kernel Development** | Page-table walks, context switching, copying task stacks via pointer loops |
| **Security / Exploit Analysis** | Reverse-engineering buffer overflows, understanding stack frames |
| **Compiler Construction** | Code generation phase maps abstract syntax tree (AST) nodes to LDR/STR patterns |
| **Performance Tuning** | Choosing pre-indexed vs post-indexed to minimize instructions in tight DSP loops |

### 2.5 The "Type-Scale" Rule (C Pointer Arithmetic in Bytes)

The compiler does **not** perform unitless addition. For a pointer `T *p` and integer `n`:

$$
\text{byte offset added to } p \;=\; n \times \text{sizeof}(T)
$$

$$
\text{sizeof}(T) = \begin{cases} 1 & T = \text{char / uint8\_t}\\ 2 & T = \text{short / int16\_t}\\ 4 & T = \text{int / float / uint32\_t}\\ 8 & T = \text{long long / double (on most ARM)} \end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Example 1 — Simple Scalar Pointer (C → ARM)

**C Source:**

```c
int x = 10;       // x stored at address 0x20000000
int *p = &x;      // p holds the value 0x20000000
*p = 25;          // write 25 into x
int y = *p;       // read x into y
```

**ARM Assembly Translation:**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =x           @ r0 = 0x20000000  (address of x, equivalent to &x)
        MOV   r1, #10          @ r1 = 10
        STR   r1, [r0]         @ memory[r0] = 10   →  x = 10
        MOV   r2, #25          @ r2 = 25
        STR   r2, [r0]         @ memory[r0] = 25   →  *p = 25
        LDR   r3, [r0]         @ r3 = memory[r0]   →  y = *p
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `LDR r0, =x` → literal-pool load. The assembler computes the address of label `x` at link time and places it in a nearby literal pool; `r0` is loaded with the 32-bit value `0x20000000`.
2. `STR r1, [r0]` → stores 10 at EA = `r0` = `0x20000000`. The square brackets mean *"use the contents of `r0` as the memory address."*
3. `STR r2, [r0]` → overwrites the same location with 25. This is exactly `*p = 25`.
4. `LDR r3, [r0]` → reads 4 bytes from EA `0x20000000` into `r3`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping:** C `*p` (read)  ⇔  `LDR Rd, [Rn]`  ···  C `*p` (write)  ⇔  `STR Rs, [Rn]`

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Array Traversal Using Post-Indexed Mode

**C Source:**

```c
int a[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
int sum = 0;
int *p;
for (p = &a[0]; p < &a[5]; p++) {
    sum += *p;
}
```

**Effective Address Derivation:**

$$
\text{Per iteration: } \text{EA} = \text{current } p \quad\quad p_{\text{next}} = p + 4
$$

**ARM Assembly Translation (using post-indexed mode):**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =a            @ r0 = base address of array a
        MOV   r1, #5            @ r1 = element count
        MOV   r2, #0            @ r2 = sum accumulator
        MOV   r3, #0            @ r3 = index i = 0

loop:   CMP   r3, r1            @ i < 5 ?
        BGE   done              @ if not, exit

        LDR   r4, [r0], #4      @ r4 = *p;   p = p + 4  (post-indexed!)
        ADD   r2, r2, r4        @ sum += r4
        ADD   r3, r3, #1        @ i++
        B     loop

done:   STR   r2, [r5]          @ store sum (assume r5 = &sum)
```

**Detailed Mechanics of `LDR r4, [r0], #4` :**

| Phase | Action | Register State |
|---|---|---|
| **Before** | `r0` holds the address of `a[i]` | `r0 = &a[i]` |
| **Access** | Read 4 bytes from EA = `r0` into `r4` | `r4 = a[i]` |
| **After** | Base register auto-incremented by 4 | `r0 = &a[i+1]` |
| **Result** | Single instruction does **both** dereference **and** pointer advance | Loop fits in 3 lines |

> [!TIP]
> This is the canonical idiomatic ARM pattern for stream/array processing. A KTU "translate this loop" question almost always expects `LDR/STR … [Rn], #offset` (post-indexed) for the inner-loop body.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Array Indexing with Scaled Register Offset

**C Source:**

```c
int a[10];
int i = 3;
int value = a[i];
```

**Step 1 — Identify the offset formula:**

$$
\text{EA} = a + i \times \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) = a + 3 \times 4 = a + 12
$$

**Step 2 — Two equivalent ARM encodings:**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =a            @ r0 = base address of a
        MOV   r1, #3            @ r1 = i
        LDR   r2, [r0, r1, LSL #2]   @ r2 = a[i]   (scaled offset)
```

> **Or alternatively**, with an explicit ADD:

```assembly
        ADD   r0, r0, r1, LSL #2     @ r0 = a + 4*i
        LDR   r2, [r0]               @ r2 = *r0
```

**Decomposition of `[r0, r1, LSL #2]`:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{r0}}_{\text{base}} \;,\; \underbrace{\text{r1}}_{\text{index}} \;,\; \underbrace{\text{LSL \#2}}_{\times 4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `LSL #2` is the **assembler's way of enforcing C's pointer-arithmetic rule** `p + i` where `p` is `int *`. If you forget the shift, you read the **byte at offset 3** instead of the **word at offset 12** — a classic KTU bug-spot.

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — Pre-Indexed vs Post-Indexed with Write-Back

**C Source:**

```c
int a[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int *p = a;          // p = &a[0]
int v1 = *++p;       // pre-increment, then read
int v2 = *p++;       // read, then post-increment
```

**Path A — Pre-indexed `LDR r4, [r0, #4]!`:**

| Step | Operation | EA used for access | r0 after |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LDR r4, [r0, #4]!` | `r0 + 4` = `&a[1]` | `&a[1]` |
| 2 | `v1` = r4 = **20** | — | — |

**Path B — Post-indexed `LDR r4, [r0], #4`:**

| Step | Operation | EA used for access | r0 after |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LDR r4, [r0], #4` | `r0` = `&a[0]` | `&a[1]` |
| 2 | `v2` = r4 = **10** | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU trap:** Students assume `*++p` and `*p++` produce the same value. They do not! The pre-incremented form skips the first element (20), the post-incremented form reads the first element (10) and *then* advances. The assembly above mirrors this exactly.

---

### 3.5 Example 5 — Structure Access via Pointer

**C Source:**

```c
struct Student {
    int    id;        // offset 0,  size 4
    char   grade;     // offset 4,  size 1
    short  marks;     // offset 6,  size 2   (2-byte aligned)
    float  cgpa;      // offset 8,  size 4
};
struct Student s = {101, 'A', 95, 9.4};
struct Student *ptr = &s;
int x = ptr->id;             // 4 bytes at offset 0
int y = ptr->marks;          // 2 bytes at offset 6
float z = ptr->cgpa;         // 4 bytes at offset 8
```

**Effective-address derivation:**

$$
\text{EA for } \texttt{ptr->id} = \text{ptr} + 0
$$

$$
\text{EA for } \texttt{ptr->marks} = \text{ptr} + 6
$$

$$
\text{EA for } \texttt{ptr->cgpa} = \text{ptr} + 8
$$

**ARM Assembly Translation:**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =s            @ r0 = base address of s (= ptr)
        LDR   r1, [r0, #0]      @ r1 = id   (32-bit load, offset 0)
        LDRH  r2, [r0, #6]      @ r2 = marks (16-bit halfword, offset 6)
        LDR   r3, [r0, #8]      @ r3 = cgpa  (32-bit float, offset 8)
        VMOV  s0, r3            @ move to FP register
```

> [!NOTE]
> The compiler **pre-computes the field offsets** at compile time and encodes them as the immediate operand in the addressing mode. The programmer only has to write `ptr->marks` and the assembler does the `+6` automatically.

---

### 3.6 Example 6 — Pointer-to-Pointer (Double Indirection)

**C Source:**

```c
int  x = 42;
int *p = &x;
int **pp = &p;
int y = **pp;     // y = x = 42
```

**ARM Assembly Translation:**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =x        @ r0 = &x
        LDR   r1, =p        @ r1 = &p   (address of the pointer variable itself)
        STR   r0, [r1]      @ *(&p) = &x  →  p = &x
        LDR   r2, =pp       @ r2 = &pp
        STR   r1, [r2]      @ pp = &p
        LDR   r3, [r2]      @ r3 = pp  (= &p)
        LDR   r4, [r3]      @ r4 = *pp = p = &x
        LDR   r5, [r4]      @ r5 = **pp = x = 42
```

**Memory picture at the end:**

| Address | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `&x`  | `x`  | 42 |
| `&p`  | `p`  | `&x` |
| `&pp` | `pp` | `&p` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chain rule for `**pp`:** Each `*` adds one level of `LDR`. A `***ppp` would need 3 LDR instructions in sequence. The compiler cannot "merge" the dereferences because each depends on the previous load's result.

---

### 3.7 Example 7 — String Copy Using Pre-Indexed Mode

**C Source:**

```c
void str_cpy(char *dst, const char *src) {
    while ((*dst++ = *src++) != '\0')
        ;
}
```

**ARM Assembly Translation:**

```assembly
str_cpy:
        LDRB  r2, [r1], #1     @ r2 = *src++; src++
        STRB  r2, [r0], #1     @ *dst++ = r2; dst++
        CMP   r2, #0           @ check for null terminator
        BNE   str_cpy          @ loop if not end
        BX    lr               @ return
```

**Pre- vs Post-index choice:** For a copy loop that must advance *both* pointers identically, the **post-indexed** form `[Rn], #1` is preferred because it allows the source-byte read to be paired with the destination-byte write, executing the two in a single cycle on a dual-issue pipeline (e.g., Cortex-A9).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Translation Pipeline: C Pointer → ARM Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[C Pointer Expression] --> B{Identify Operation Type}
    B -->|Address-of ampersand x| C[Load address into register via LDR r0, equals x]
    B -->|Read dereference| D[LDR Rd, base]
    B -->|Write dereference| E[STR Rs, base]
    B -->|Arithmetic plus n| F{Choose Addressing Mode}
    F -->|Constant offset| G[LDR Rd, base, hash n]
    F -->|Index scaled| H[LDR Rd, base, index, LSL hash scale]
    F -->|Pre-increment| I[LDR Rd, base, hash n bang]
    F -->|Post-increment| J[LDR Rd, base, hash n]
    C --> K[Effective Address equals base]
    D --> K
    E --> K
    G --> L[EA equals base plus n]
    H --> M[EA equals base plus index times size]
    I --> N[EA equals base plus n then write back]
    J --> O[EA equals base then write back]
    K --> P[Execute Load or Store on Memory]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Result in Register or Memory]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Decision Flow: Which ARM Addressing Mode to Pick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[C Pointer Expression Received] --> Q1{Does the base pointer need to change?}
    Q1 -->|No, only read once| Q2{Is the offset a constant?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, pointer must advance| Q3{Should the read use the OLD or NEW address?}
    Q2 -->|Yes constant| M1[Use LDR Rd, base, hash offset]
    Q2 -->|No variable| M2[Use LDR Rd, base, offset_reg, LSL scale]
    Q3 -->|OLD address first then advance| M3[Use LDR Rd, base, hash n POST INDEXED]
    Q3 -->|NEW address first then advance| M4[Use LDR Rd, base, hash n bang PRE INDEXED]
    M1 --> End[Emit ARM Instruction]
    M2 --> End
    M3 --> End
    M4 --> End
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Memory Access Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A32Instruction[ARM A32 Instruction Word 32 bits]
        Cond[Condition Field 4 bits] --> Opcode[Opcode 4 bits]
        Opcode --> Rn[Base Register Rn 4 bits]
        Rn --> Rd[Destination Rd 4 bits]
        Rd --> AddrMode[Addressing Mode Bits]
        AddrMode --> Offset[Offset Immediate 12 bits or Register 4 plus Shift 4]
    end
    A32Instruction --> Decode[Instruction Decode Stage]
    Decode --> AddUnit[Address Adder Unit]
    Rn --> AddUnit
    Offset --> AddUnit
    AddUnit --> EA[Effective Address 32 bits]
    EA --> MemBus[Memory Bus Read or Write]
    MemBus --> RegFile[Register File Write Back]
    RegFile --> WriteBack[Optional Write Back to Rn]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Lifecycle of `*p++` in a Single ARM Instruction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as ARM Core
    participant Rn as Base Register Rn
    participant MEM as Memory
    participant Rd as Destination Register Rd
    Note over CPU, Rd: Instruction executed: LDR Rd, equals Rn, hash 4
    CPU->>Rn: Read base address A
    CPU->>CPU: Compute EA equals A plus 4
    CPU->>MEM: Read 4 bytes from EA equals A plus 4
    MEM-->>Rd: Deliver data into Rd
    Note over CPU,Rn: Post-indexed path
    CPU->>Rn: Write back A plus 4 into Rn
    Note over CPU: Pre-indexed path would update Rn BEFORE the read
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Explain the difference between pre-indexed and post-indexed addressing modes in ARM. Provide one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **pre-indexed** mode, the offset is added to the base register *before* the memory access, and the base is then optionally written back using the `!` suffix.

In **post-indexed** mode, the memory access uses the *original* base address, and the base is updated *after* the access completes (write-back is implicit).

**Examples:**

| Mode | Instruction | Effective Address | Base after |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-indexed | `LDR r0, [r1, #4]!` | `r1 + 4` | `r1 ← r1 + 4` |
| Post-indexed | `LDR r0, [r1], #4` | `r1` | `r1 ← r1 + 4` |

> **Key distinction:** Both update the base register identically, but the *value loaded into r0 differs* (pre uses `&a[1]`, post uses `&a[0]`).

---

#### **Q2. Translate the following C expression into equivalent ARM assembly: `result = *(ptr + 5);` where `ptr` is an `int *`.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

```assembly
        ADD   r0, r0, #20         @ r0 = ptr + (5 × 4) = ptr + 20 bytes
        LDR   r1, [r0]            @ r1 = *r0 = *(ptr + 5)
```

**Or equivalently** (scaled-register form):

```assembly
        LDR   r1, [r0, #20]       @ single-instruction form (offset is 20)
```

> **Explanation (1 mark):** Because `ptr` is `int *` (4 bytes), the compiler must scale the index by 4. Hence `5 × 4 = 20` bytes offset, not 5 bytes.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Translate the following C function into equivalent ARM assembly. Assume `int` is 4 bytes, `char` is 1 byte, and the array starts at label `array`. **(7 marks)**

```c
int array[8] = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16};
int total = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    if (array[i] > 10) {
        total += array[i];
    }
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify addressing-mode needs.** We need: (i) `LDR` with **scaled register offset** to read `array[i]`, (ii) a conditional `ADD` to update `total`, and (iii) a register loop counter.

**Step 2 — Translation:**

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =array          @ r0 = base address of array
        MOV   r1, #0              @ r1 = i = 0
        MOV   r2, #8              @ r2 = loop limit (8)
        MOV   r3, #0              @ r3 = total = 0
        LDR   r4, =total          @ r4 = address of total

loop:   CMP   r1, r2              @ i < 8 ?
        BGE   done                @ exit if false

        LDR   r5, [r0, r1, LSL #2] @ r5 = array[i]   (EA = array + 4i)
        CMP   r5, #10             @ array[i] > 10 ?
        BLE   skip                @ skip if ≤ 10

        ADD   r3, r3, r5          @ total += array[i]

skip:   ADD   r1, r1, #1          @ i++
        B     loop

done:   STR   r3, [r4]            @ store total back
```

**Mark breakdown:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct base and index setup | 1 |
| Scaled-offset LDR with LSL #2 | 2 |
| Correct CMP / BLE flow | 1 |
| Correct ADD for accumulation | 1 |
| Loop branch back | 1 |
| Store of result | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

**(b)** For the instruction `LDR r3, [r0, r1, LSL #2]!` where initially `r0 = 0x20000000` and `r1 = 5`, compute the effective address, the value loaded into `r3`, and the new value of `r0`. Memory contents at relevant locations are:

| Address | Value (4 bytes, little-endian) |
|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | 0x00000001 |
| `0x20000004` | 0x00000002 |
| `0x20000008` | 0x00000003 |
| `0x2000000C` | 0x00000004 |
| `0x20000010` | 0x00000005 |
| `0x20000014` | 0x00000006 | **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the scaled offset:**

$$
\text{offset} = r1 \times 2^2 = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective address (pre-indexed, write-back):**

$$
\text{EA} = r0 + \text{offset} = \text{0x20000000} + 20 = \text{0x20000014}
$$

**Step 3 — Read 4 bytes from EA `0x20000014`:**

$$
\text{value loaded into r3} = 0x00000006
$$

**Step 4 — Update r0 (write-back):**

$$
r0_{\text{new}} = r0_{\text{old}} + 20 = \text{0x20000014}
$$

**Mark breakdown:**

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct scaled-offset formula | 2 |
| Correct EA computation | 1 |
| Correct value read | 1 |
| Correct write-back value | 1 |
| Explanation of write-back timing | 1 |
| Final answer summary | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Consider the following C declarations and write the equivalent ARM assembly for each of the three statements. Show the effective address calculation for each. **(7 marks)**

```c
short data[6] = {100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600};
short *q = data;
short s1 = *(q + 2);       // (i)
*(q + 4) = 750;            // (ii)
short **qq = &q;           // (iii)
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Note the element size:** `short` is 2 bytes.

**Statement (i) — `s1 = *(q + 2)`:**

$$
\text{offset} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{EA} = q + 4
$$

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =data           @ r0 = base of data
        LDRH  r1, [r0, #4]        @ r1 = *(q + 2) — halfword load at EA = q+4
```

> Loaded value = `300` (0x012C).

**Statement (ii) — `*(q + 4) = 750`:**

$$
\text{offset} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{EA} = q + 8
$$

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =data
        MOV   r1, #750            @ immediate = 750
        STRH  r1, [r0, #8]        @ halfword store — *((short*)(q+4)) = 750
```

> The 2-byte value `0x02EE` is written to `0x20000008`.

**Statement (iii) — `qq = &q;`** (no memory access needed beyond address computation):

```assembly
        LDR   r0, =q              @ r0 = &q  (address of pointer variable)
        LDR   r1, =qq             @ r1 = &qq (address of double-pointer variable)
        STR   r0, [r1]            @ qq = &q
```

**Mark breakdown:**

| Sub-part | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct scaling (× 2) for (i) | 1 |
| LDRH with correct offset for (i) | 1 |
| Correct scaling (× 2) for (ii) | 1 |
| STRH with correct offset for (ii) | 1 |
| Address-of for (iii) | 1 |
| STR to write `&q` into `qq` | 1 |
| Clear EA derivations | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

**(b)** A C function `void clear(int *p, int n)` must zero out `n` consecutive 32-bit memory locations starting at `p`. Write the ARM assembly, and identify which addressing mode is most efficient. Justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Most efficient choice: Post-indexed mode** `STR r2, [r0], #4` — it performs the store and the pointer advance in a single instruction, which is ideal for streaming stores.

**Assembly:**

```assembly
clear:
        CMP   r1, #0              @ n == 0 ?
        BXEQ  lr                  @ return if zero
        MOV   r2, #0              @ r2 = value to store (zero)

loop:   STR   r2, [r0], #4        @ *p++ = 0;   p += 4  (post-indexed!)
        SUBS  r1, r1, #1          @ n-- ; set flags
```
