The following diagram illustrates the architectural flow of a `PUSH` and `POP` operation, mapping the relationship between the CPU registers, the Stack Pointer, and the RAM region.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    regA["Register File R0 to R12 LR PC"]
    alu["ARM Core Data Path"]
    spReg["Stack Pointer R13 SP"]
    memCtrl["Memory Write and Read Controller"]
    ramHigh["High Address Boundary"]
    ramTop["SP old points here in FD stack"]
    ramDown1["Push 1 Slot Address SP new"]
    ramDown2["Push 2 Slot Address SP new minus 4"]
    ramDown3["Push 3 Slot Address SP new minus 8"]
    ramLow["Low Address Boundary"]

    regA --> alu
    alu --> spReg
    spReg --> memCtrl
    memCtrl --> ramTop
    memCtrl --> ramDown1
    memCtrl --> ramDown2
    memCtrl --> ramDown3
    ramHigh --> ramTop
    ramTop --> ramDown1
    ramDown1 --> ramDown2
    ramDown2 --> ramDown3
    ramDown3 --> ramLow
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows depict the *direction of stack growth* (downward in memory). The CPU core writes register values from the register file into successive 4-byte slots while the SP is decremented.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: PUSH / POP Subroutine Prologue-Epilogue

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant CallerSP as Caller SP
    participant Sub as Subroutine
    participant SubSP as Subroutine SP
    participant RAM as Stack Memory

    Caller->>CallerSP: Read current SP value
    Caller->>Sub: BL subroutine sets LR return address
    Sub->>SubSP: PUSH R4 to R11 and LR
    SubSP->>SubSP: SP new equals SP old minus 32
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R4 at SP new
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R5 at SP new plus 4
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R6 at SP new plus 8
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R7 at SP new plus 12
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R8 at SP new plus 16
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R9 at SP new plus 20
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R10 at SP new plus 24
    SubSP->>RAM: Write R11 at SP new plus 28
    SubSP->>RAM: Write LR at SP new plus 32
    Sub->>Sub: Execute function body
    Sub->>SubSP: POP R4 to R11 and PC
    SubSP->>SubSP: SP new equals SP old plus 32
    SubSP->>RAM: Read back in reverse order
    Sub->>Caller: PC loaded with return address
```

> [!TIP]
> This is the **canonical AAPCS-compliant subroutine frame** for ARM Cortex-M processors. The saved set `R4–R11, LR` and restored set `R4–R11, PC` are exactly what the ARM ABI mandates for public functions.

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Stack Pointer State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateA["State A: SP points to empty stack boundary"]
    stateB["State B: PUSH decrement SP then store"]
    stateC["State C: Stack contains pushed data"]
    stateD["State D: POP read from SP then increment SP"]
    stateE["State E: SP restored to prior level"]

    stateA --> stateB: Execute PUSH Rn
    stateB --> stateC: Memory updated
    stateC --> stateD: Execute POP Rn
    stateD --> stateE: Memory released
    stateE --> stateA: Cycle repeats for next push pop pair
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> Differentiate between a **Full Stack** and an **Empty Stack** in the context of ARM architecture. Which model does the ARM use by default?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Full Stack (1 mark):** The Stack Pointer (SP) points to the location holding the *last item pushed* onto the stack. The next push operation must therefore *decrement* SP (for descending) or *increment* SP (for ascending) *before* writing, so the SP always tracks the topmost valid data.
* **Empty Stack (1 mark):** The Stack Pointer points to the *first free location* in memory — the slot where the *next* item will be written. The push operation writes at SP first, then advances SP.
* **ARM default (1 mark):** The ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) adopts the **Full Descending (FD)** model, in which SP points to the most recent item and the stack grows toward lower memory addresses.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam II]**
> What is the role of the `!` (write-back) suffix in `STMFD SP!, {R0, R1, R2}`? What happens if it is omitted?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Function of `!` (1 mark):** The write-back suffix instructs the ARM core to **update the base register** (here, SP) automatically after the multiple-register transfer completes. Without it, SP would remain at its old value.
* **Effect on stack (1 mark):** With `!`, SP is *pre-decremented* by $3 \times 4 = 12$ bytes, then R0, R1, R2 are stored at SP, SP+4, SP+8 respectively, and SP is left at the new (lower) value. This is essential for correct stack semantics.
* **Consequence of omission (1 mark):** If the `!` is omitted, the registers are written to the correct memory addresses, but **SP is not updated**. Subsequent `POP` or `STMFD` instructions will read from the wrong location, the stack will rapidly become corrupt, and the program will crash or exhibit undefined behaviour.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **four types of stack models** (`FA`, `FD`, `EA`, `ED`) with neat diagrams. Show how the legacy `STM`/`LDM` mnemonics map to the unified `PUSH`/`POP` mnemonics. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Given that the Stack Pointer (`SP`) initially contains the value `0x00008000` and the register contents are `R0 = 0x11111111`, `R1 = 0x22222222`, `R2 = 0x33333333`, trace the execution of the following ARM code segment and show the **memory contents and final SP** after each instruction. **(7 marks)**
>
> ```text
> PUSH   {R0, R1}
> PUSH   {R2}
> POP    {R0}
> POP    {R2, R1}
> ```

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Stack Models — Tabular Explanation (3 marks):**

| Model | SP Points To | Direction of Growth | Operation Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FA — Full Ascending** | Last item pushed | Upward (higher addresses) | `STMFA` = `STMIB` with write-back |
| **FD — Full Descending** | Last item pushed | Downward (lower addresses) | `STMFD` = `STMDB` with write-back; this is the **ARM default** |
| **EA — Empty Ascending** | First free slot | Upward (higher addresses) | `STMEA` = `STMIA` with write-back |
| **ED — Empty Descending** | First free slot | Downward (lower addresses) | `STMED` = `STMDA` with write-back |

**Mapping Unified to Legacy Mnemonics (2 marks):**

| Unified | Legacy Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `PUSH {R0, R1, R2}` | `STMFD SP!, {R0, R1, R2}` |
| `POP {R0, R1, PC}` | `LDMFD SP!, {R0, R1, PC}` |

**Diagrammatic Description (2 marks):** A stack memory diagram showing SP pointing to top, with arrows indicating growth direction. For FD: SP starts at `0x00008000`, after `PUSH {R0}` SP becomes `0x00007FFC` holding `R0`. The stack "grows downward" while the SP "moves down".

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Trace Table:**

| Step | Instruction | SP Before | SP After | Memory Affected | Register After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `PUSH {R0, R1}` | `0x00008000` | `0x00007FF8` | `[0x7FF8]=R0`, `[0x7FFC]=R1` | Unchanged |
| 2 | `PUSH {R2}` | `0x00007FF8` | `0x00007FF4` | `[0x7FF4]=R2` | Unchanged |
| 3 | `POP {R0}` | `0x00007FF4` | `0x00007FF8` | Unchanged | `R0 ← 0x33333333` (read from 0x7FF4) |
| 4 | `POP {R2, R1}` | `0x00007FF8` | `0x00008000` | Unchanged | `R2 ← 0x11111111`, `R1 ← 0x22222222` |

**Key Calculations (3 marks):**

* **Step 1 (1 mark):** $N = 2$ registers; $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 0\text{x}00008000 - (2 \times 4) = 0\text{x}00007FF8$. `R0` stored at `0x7FF8`, `R1` at `0x7FFC`.
* **Step 2 (1 mark):** $N = 1$; $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 0\text{x}00007FF8 - 4 = 0\text{x}00007FF4$. `R2` stored at `0x7FF4`.
* **Step 3 (1 mark):** Reads from SP `0x7FF4` (value `0x33333333`) into R0; then SP incremented by 4 to `0x7FF8`.
* **Step 4 (1 mark):** Reads from `0x7FF8` into R2, then `0x7FFC` into R1; SP becomes `0x00008000` — **stack is fully restored.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation (1 bonus mark):** After the four instructions, the stack is balanced (SP back to `0x00008000`), but the register values have been **rotated** — R0 holds what was in R2, and R1, R2 have been swapped. This is a classic demonstration of LIFO behaviour.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With the help of a register transfer diagram, explain the **operation of `STMFD`** and **operation of `LDMFD`** for the instruction sequence `STMFD SP!, {R4–R7, LR}` and `LDMFD SP!, {R4–R7, PC}`. Assume SP initial = `0x00004000`. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write an **ARM assembly subroutine** that accepts three integers passed in `R0`, `R1`, `R2` and returns their sum in `R0`. The subroutine must follow AAPCS conventions — show the **prologue** (`PUSH`) and **epilogue** (`POP`) explicitly and justify every saved register. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Initial Conditions (1 mark):**
* SP = `0x00004000`
* Registers: R4, R5, R6, R7, LR holding arbitrary values `v4, v5, v6, v7, vLR`.

**Execution of `STMFD SP!, {R4–R7, LR}` (3 marks):**

The `!` triggers pre-decrement write-back. Number of registers $N = 5$ (R4, R5, R6, R7, LR). Total bytes = 20.

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 0\text{x}00004000 - (5 \times 4) = 0\text{x}00003FEC
$$

Storage addresses (ascending register order):

$$
A(R4) = 0\text{x}00003\text{FEC}, \quad A(R5) = 0\text{x}00003\text{FF0}, \quad A(R6) = 0\text{x}00003\text{FF4}
$$

$$
A(R7) = 0\text{x}00003\text{FF8}, \quad A(\text{LR}) = 0\text{x}00003\text{FFC}
$$

**Execution of `LDMFD SP!, {R4–R7, PC}` (3 marks):**

For the LDMFD, the current SP = `0x00003FEC` holds R4 (the *last pushed* in FD sense). Reads in order:

* `R4 ← M[0x00003FEC]`
* `R5 ← M[0x00003FF0]`
* `R6 ← M[0x00003FF4]`
* `R7 ← M[0x00003FF8]`
* `PC ← M[0x00003FFC]` — **this is the saved return address; loading PC causes a branch**, effectively returning from the subroutine.

Final SP = `0x00003FEC + 5 \times 4 = 0x00004000` — balanced.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Assembly Subroutine (5 marks):**

```asm
        AREA    SumThree, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  SumThree          ; make visible to linker

SumThree
        PUSH    {R4, LR}          ; Prologue: save callee-preserved regs (2 marks)
        ; R0, R1, R2 are already the parameters per AAPCS
        MOV     R4, R0            ; copy first operand to R4 (1 mark)
        ADD     R4, R4, R1        ; R4 = R0 + R1
        ADD     R0, R4, R2        ; R0 = (R0 + R1) + R2
        POP     {R4, PC}          ; Epilogue: restore R4 and return (1 mark)
        END
```

**Justification (2 marks):**

* **Why save `LR` (1 mark):** The `BL` instruction used by the caller places the return address in `LR`; if our subroutine calls any nested function or `BL`, that nested call will *overwrite* `LR`. Pushing `LR` preserves the caller's return address across the subroutine body.
* **Why save `R4` (1 mark):** Under AAPCS, registers `R4–R11` are *callee-preserved*. If the subroutine uses `R4` as a scratch register, it must restore the original value before returning; otherwise, the caller's value would be corrupted, violating the ABI.
* **Why use `POP {R4, PC}` (1 mark):** Loading the saved `LR` directly into `PC` performs the subroutine return in a single instruction — more efficient than `MOV PC, LR` and is the standard Cortex-M idiom.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update SP** (omitting `!`): The most common error. Without `!`, SP is unchanged and subsequent stack operations corrupt memory. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Wrong suffix mapping**: Students frequently write `STMFA` or `PUSH` and then claim the SP points to the *next free* slot. ARM is *Full Descending* — not Empty Descending. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Register-order confusion**: In a `STMFD`, *lowest-numbered register goes to the lowest address*. Writing R0 to the highest address is wrong. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Omitting `LR` save in non-leaf functions**: If the subroutine itself contains a `BL` (e.g., to `printf`), `LR` is clobbered. Marks lost: 2.
> 5. **Misaligning SP**: AAPCS requires 8-byte alignment at public interfaces. Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Confusing `IA`/`IB`/`DA`/`DB` with stack suffixes**: `STMFD` is `STMDB` not `STMDA` — both decrement, but the *timing* of write-back differs. Marks lost: 1–2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PEECT524 Module 2: Stack Instructions**

- **Default stack model:** ARM uses a **Full Descending (FD) stack** — SP points to the last used location, and the stack grows toward lower memory.
- **Stack Pointer:** Register `R13` is dedicated to SP. Never use it as a general-purpose register in AAPCS-compliant code.
- **Link Register:** Register `R14` (LR) holds the return address after a `BL` instruction.
- **Two notations:**
   * Unified: `PUSH {reglist}` and `POP {reglist}`.
   * Legacy: `STMFD SP!, {reglist}` and `LDMFD SP!, {reglist}`.
- **Unified form restrictions:** `PUSH` accepts only `R0–R7` plus optional `LR`. `POP` accepts `R0–R7` plus optional `PC`. For other registers, use the legacy `STMFD`/`LDMFD` form.
- **SP arithmetic for PUSH (FD):** $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4N$.
- **SP arithmetic for POP (FD):** $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 4N$.
- **Write-back `!` suffix:** Without it, the base register (SP) is *not* updated, and the stack becomes inconsistent. Always include `!` (or use `PUSH`/`POP` which imply write-back).
- **Register storage order in `STMFD`:** Lowest-numbered register is stored at the lowest address. Operand list order in the assembly source is **irrelevant** — the hardware sorts by register number.
- **AAPCS alignment:** Public functions must enter with SP aligned to an **8-byte boundary**. The `PUSH` of an even number of registers (or any number that is a multiple of 2) preserves this.
- **Subroutine return idiom:** `POP {..., PC}` atomically restores registers *and* branches to the saved return address — preferred over `POP {..., LR}` followed by `MOV PC, LR`.
- **Suffix mapping recap:** `STMFD = STMDB!`, `LDMFD = LDMIA!`, `STMFA = STMIB!`, `LDMFA = LDMDA!`, `STMEA = STMIA!`, `LDMEA = LDMDB!`, `STMED = STMDA!`, `LDMED = LDMIB!`.
- **Common use cases:** Interrupt Service Routines, RTOS context switches, function prologues/epilogues, nested subroutine calls.
- **High-yield KTU topics:** SP arithmetic problems, conversion between unified and legacy mnemonics, trace tables showing memory and SP after a sequence of PUSH/POP, AAPCS-compliant subroutine templates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Processing Instructions: Updating Flags in APSR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Processing Instructions: Updating Flags in APSR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** in the ARM Cortex-M architecture is a special-purpose 32-bit register located within the **System Control Space (SCS)** at address **`0xE000ED00`** that holds the four arithmetic condition flags — **N, Z, C, and V** — used by all conditional branch instructions (e.g., `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, `BLT`) to decide program flow after a comparison or data operation.

In the ARM Cortex-M4 (ARMv7-M architecture), the APSR bit layout is defined as follows:

| Bit  | Name | Meaning |
|:----:|:----:|:--------|
| 31   | **N** | **Negative flag** — Set to bit[31] of the result |
| 30   | **Z** | **Zero flag** — Set when the result is zero |
| 29   | **C** | **Carry flag** — Set on unsigned overflow / no-borrow |
| 28   | **V** | **Overflow flag** — Set on signed overflow |
| 27   | **Q** | **Saturation flag** — Set by SSAT/USAT |
| 26–0 | —    | **Reserved** (read as zero) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **APSR vs. xPSR:** In ARM Cortex-M, APSR is part of a unified `xPSR` register. The IPSR (Interrupt Program Status Register) shares the same address but occupies bits [5:0] and is read-only. The EPSR (Execution PSR) holds the Thumb state bit (T, bit 24).

> [!NOTE]
> **Byte-ordering Note:** The flag bits in the APSR are stored in the *upper nibble* of the most significant byte of the register (bits 31–28). When debugging, single-step through an instruction and observe `xPSR` in the register window to see the flag transitions live.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine the APSR as the **dashboard of a four-dial weighing and measuring instrument** sitting on top of a digital calculator (the ALU).

- Every time the calculator performs an arithmetic or logical operation, the four dials automatically swing to indicate something about the **outcome**:
  - **N-Dial (Sign):** *"Did the result turn negative?"*
  - **Z-Dial (Zero):** *"Did the result become exactly zero?"*
  - **C-Dial (Carry):** *"Did any extra 'spill' digit need to be carried out (or borrowed)?"*
  - **V-Dial (Overflow):** *"Did the result spill over the signed number range?"*

- The user (programmer) can then read these dials and make decisions — like *"if the Z-dial is ON, branch to the equality handler"*.

The **'S' suffix** in instructions (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`) is the **'Update Dashboard' button**. Without it (`ADD`, `SUB`), the calculator works silently, leaving the dials unchanged.

## 1.3 Why Flag Updates Matter

> [!NOTE]
> The ARM instruction set is **load-store** and **conditionally executed**. The entire philosophy of `IF-THEN-ELSE` control flow in ARM assembly is driven by inspecting the flags in APSR. Every high-level construct — `if (a > b)`, `while (x != 0)`, `if ((x & 0x01) == 0)` — compiles down to a data-processing instruction that updates flags, followed by a conditional branch (`Bcc`) that reads those flags.

## 1.4 Default Flag-Updating Instructions

Some instructions *implicitly* update flags — no `S` suffix is needed (and in fact, **cannot** be omitted) because the entire *purpose* of the instruction is to set flags:

| Instruction | Implicit Operation | Flags Updated |
|:-----------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|
| `CMP  Rn, Rm` | `SUBS` with result discarded | **N, Z, C, V** |
| `CMN  Rn, Rm` | `ADDS` with result discarded | **N, Z, C, V** |
| `TST  Rn, Rm` | `ANDS` with result discarded | **N, Z, C** |
| `TEQ  Rn, Rm` | `EORS` with result discarded | **N, Z, C** |

> [!TIP]
> **Compiler Clue:** When you see `CMP` in compiled C code, remember the assembler translates it into an internal `SUBS R0, R0, R0` (with result discarded) just to set the flags. The destination register is *not* written.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instruction Classification by Flag-Update Behavior

In ARM Cortex-M, every data-processing instruction is classified into one of two modes:

1. **Non-flag-updating (default):** Plain `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, etc. The APSR is **untouched**.
2. **Flag-updating (with `S` suffix):** `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`, etc. The APSR is updated based on the result.

The four core flag-setting rules are universal for **arithmetic** operations:

> **General Rule (applies to all arithmetic ops with `S`):**
> - $N \leftarrow \text{result}[31]$
> - $Z \leftarrow 1$ if $\text{result} = 0$, else $0$
> - $C$ and $V$ depend on the specific operation (see table below)

## 2.2 Flag-Update Rule Table (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the V flag:** "**V**otes only when **signs of inputs agree** but **sign of result disagrees**" for ADD. For SUB, the rule is "**V**otes when **signs of inputs disagree**."

| Instruction | N | Z | C | V | Rule for C | Rule for V |
|:------------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-----------|:-----------|
| `ADDS` / `CMN` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | $C = \text{carry-out from bit 31}$ | $V = \overline{\text{op1[31]}} \oplus \text{op2[31]} \, \wedge \, \overline{\text{result[31]}} \oplus \text{op1[31]}$ |
| `SUBS` / `CMP` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | $C = 1$ if $\text{op1} \geq \text{op2}$ (no borrow); else $0$ | $V = \text{op1[31]} \oplus \text{op2[31]} \, \wedge \, \text{result[31]} \oplus \text{op1[31]}$ |
| `RSBS` (NEG) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | $C = 1$ if $\text{op2} \neq 0$ (no borrow); else $0$ | Sign-overflow of $\text{0} - \text{op1}$ |
| `ANDS` / `TST` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `ORRS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `EORS` / `TEQ` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `BICS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `MOVS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `MVNS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| Shifts (`LSLS`, `LSRS`, `ASRS`, `RORS`) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | $C = \text{last bit shifted out}$ | $V$ unchanged |
| `MULS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |
| `SDIVS` / `UDIVS` | ✓ | ✓ | **preserved** | **preserved** | $C$ unchanged | $V$ unchanged |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In automotive ABS controllers, the overflow flag `V` is checked after every wheel-speed subtraction to detect signed-counter wrap-around. In a digital filter, the `Q` flag is monitored for saturation behaviour in fixed-point arithmetic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (ASCII-Safe)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the equations below are written with $\vert$ in place of $\vert$ to remain render-safe.

### Carry flag (C) for ADDS

$$C = \text{CarryOut}\big(\text{op1} + \text{op2}\big)$$

In Boolean terms:

$$C = \text{CarryOut}_{\text{bit31}} = \text{majority}\big(\text{op1[31]},\, \text{op2[31]},\, \overline{\text{result[31]}}\big) \text{ for } \text{op1[31]} = \text{op2[31]} = 1$$

### Carry flag (C) for SUBS

$$C = \overline{\text{Borrow}}_{\text{bit31}} = 1 \iff \text{op1} \geq \text{op2}$$

### Overflow flag (V) for ADDS

$$V = (\text{op1[31]} \wedge \text{op2[31]} \wedge \overline{\text{result[31]}}) \,\vert\, (\overline{\text{op1[31]}} \wedge \overline{\text{op2[31]}} \wedge \text{result[31]})$$

> Read as: "V is set when a positive + positive becomes negative, OR a negative + negative becomes positive."

### Overflow flag (V) for SUBS

$$V = (\text{op1[31]} \wedge \overline{\text{op2[31]}} \wedge \overline{\text{result[31]}}) \,\vert\, (\overline{\text{op1[31]}} \wedge \text{op2[31]} \wedge \text{result[31]})$$

> Read as: "V is set when a positive − negative becomes negative, OR a negative − positive becomes positive."

### General rules (universal)

$$N = \text{result[31]}$$

$$Z = \overline{\text{OR}(\text{result}[31\!:\!0])}$$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Compiler design:** GCC's `-mthumb` backend optimises away unnecessary flag updates by emitting plain (non-S) variants of instructions when the result is consumed without needing a flag check.
- **DSP routines:** In FIR filter kernels, the `Q` flag from `SSAT`/`USAT` is used to detect saturation, which is then converted into a conditional execution to clip output values without branches.
- **Safety-critical code (ISO 26262):** The carry and overflow flags are tested explicitly after every multiply-accumulate in automotive MCUs to guard against 16-bit accumulator wrap-around.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive worked examples** to demonstrate how the four APSR flags are derived from the operands of a data-processing instruction. *No steps are skipped.*

## 3.1 Example 1 — `ADDS R0, R1, R2` with Signed Overflow

**Given:**
$$\text{R1} = 0\text{x}7\text{FFFFFFF}, \quad \text{R2} = 0\text{x}00000001$$

**Step 1 — Perform the binary addition.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R1} &= 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111_2 \\
\text{R2} &= 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001_2 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 1000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \\
&= 0\text{x}80000000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Determine N flag.**

$$\text{result[31]} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 1$$

**Step 3 — Determine Z flag.**

$$\text{result} \neq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; Z = 0$$

**Step 4 — Determine C flag.**

Look at the unsigned carry out of bit 31. Both inputs are non-negative and within unsigned range, so the unsigned sum fits exactly without carry-out:

$$0\text{x}7\text{FFFFFFF} + 0\text{x}00000001 = 0\text{x}80000000 \;<\; 0\text{xFFFFFFFF} \;\Rightarrow\; C = 0$$

**Step 5 — Determine V flag using the V formula for ADDS.**

Both operands are positive (bit 31 = 0), and the result is negative (bit 31 = 1). Apply:

$$V = (\text{op1[31]} \wedge \text{op2[31]} \wedge \overline{\text{result[31]}}) \;\vert\; (\overline{\text{op1[31]}} \wedge \overline{\text{op2[31]}} \wedge \text{result[31]})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= (0 \wedge 0 \wedge 0) \;\vert\; (1 \wedge 1 \wedge 1) \\
  &= 0 \;\vert\; 1 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** `APSR = 0x90000000` (N=1, V=1, with Z=0, C=0). The signed overflow is correctly detected.

## 3.2 Example 2 — `SUBS R0, R1, R2` with Borrow

**Given:**
$$\text{R1} = 0\text{x}00000005, \quad \text{R2} = 0\text{x}00000007$$

**Step 1 — Perform the binary subtraction using two's complement.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R1} - \text{R2} &= 0\text{x}00000005 + \overline{0\text{x}00000007} + 1 \\
&= 0\text{x}00000005 + 0\text{xFFFFFFF8} + 1 \\
&= 0\text{xFFFFFFF8} + 0\text{x}00000006 \\
&= 0\text{xFFFFFFFE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — N flag.**

$$\text{result[31]} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 1$$

**Step 3 — Z flag.**

$$\text{result} = 0\text{xFFFFFFFE} \neq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; Z = 0$$

**Step 4 — C flag (no-borrow rule).**

The C flag is the *inverse* of the borrow. Since $0\text{x}5 < 0\text{x}7$, a borrow occurs:

$$\text{op1} \geq \text{op2} \;?\; \text{No} \;\Rightarrow\; C = 0$$

**Step 5 — V flag (signed overflow for SUB).**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= (\text{op1[31]} \wedge \overline{\text{op2[31]}} \wedge \overline{\text{result[31]}}) \;\vert\; (\overline{\text{op1[31]}} \wedge \text{op2[31]} \wedge \text{result[31]}) \\
  &= (0 \wedge 1 \wedge 0) \;\vert\; (1 \wedge 0 \wedge 1) \\
  &= 0 \;\vert\; 0 \\
  &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** `APSR = 0x80000000` (N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0). The borrow is captured by C=0, and the result is correctly negative.

## 3.3 Example 3 — `ANDS R0, R1, R2` (Logical Operation)

**Given:**
$$\text{R1} = 0\text{xF0F0F0F0}, \quad \text{R2} = 0\text{x0F0F0F0F}$$

**Step 1 — Perform the bitwise AND.**

$$\text{R0} = 0\text{x}00000000$$

**Step 2 — N flag.**

$$\text{result[31]} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 0$$

**Step 3 — Z flag.**

$$\text{result} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; Z = 1$$

**Step 4 — C flag.**

Per the rule for `ANDS`, the carry flag is **preserved** (not modified):

$$C = C_{\text{previous}}$$

**Step 5 — V flag.**

Likewise, the overflow flag is **preserved**:

$$V = V_{\text{previous}}$$

**Result:** If previously APSR was, say, `0x90000000`, then after `ANDS` it becomes `0x40000000` (only Z changes from 0 to 1; C and V stay 0; N stays 1 because result[31] is now 0, so N updates to 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key learning point:** The N and Z flags of logical operations are computed from the *result*, but the C and V flags are *not* updated. This is critical when porting code from one architecture to another.

## 3.4 Example 4 — `CMP R1, R2` (Implicit Flag-Setting)

**Given:** `R1 = 0x12345678`, `R2 = 0x12345678`.

The assembler internally executes `SUBS R0, R1, R2` and discards the result.

$$\text{R0 (discarded)} = 0\text{x}00000000 \;\Rightarrow\; Z = 1, \, N = 0, \, C = 1, \, V = 0$$

A subsequent `BEQ label` would branch, because `BEQ` checks `Z == 1`.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix of Flag Updates

| Example | Operation | Result | N | Z | C | V |
|:-------:|:---------:|:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | `ADDS 0x7FFFFFFF, 0x00000001` | `0x80000000` | **1** | **0** | **0** | **1** |
| 2 | `SUBS 0x00000005, 0x00000007` | `0xFFFFFFFE` | **1** | **0** | **0** | **0** |
| 3 | `ANDS 0xF0F0F0F0, 0x0F0F0F0F` | `0x00000000` | **0** | **1** | *preserved* | *preserved* |
| 4 | `CMP  0x12345678, 0x12345678` | *discarded* | **0** | **1** | **1** | **0** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 APSR Bit Layout (Mermaid Diagram)

The following block-level functional diagram illustrates the APSR register bit-fields and how the various flag-updating instructions feed into them.

```mermaid
graph TD
    APSR["APSR 32 bit Register at 0xE000ED00"]
    APSR --> HighNibble["Bits 31 to 28 Flags"]
    APSR --> LowRest["Bits 27 to 0 Reserved Read as Zero"]
    HighNibble --> Nbit["Bit 31 N Negative Flag"]
    HighNibble --> Zbit["Bit 30 Z Zero Flag"]
    HighNibble --> Cbit["Bit 29 C Carry Flag"]
    HighNibble --> Vbit["Bit 28 V Overflow Flag"]
    Qbit["Bit 27 Q Saturation Flag"] -.-> APSR
    APInstruct["Arithmetic Instructions ADDS SUBS RSBS"] -->|update| Nbit
    APInstruct -->|update| Zbit
    APInstruct -->|update| Cbit
    APInstruct -->|update| Vbit
    LogInstruct["Logical Instructions ANDS ORRS EORS BICS MVNS"] -->|update| Nbit
    LogInstruct -->|update| Zbit
    LogInstruct -->|preserve| Cbit
    LogInstruct -->|preserve| Vbit
    ShiftInstruct["Shift Instructions LSLS LSRS ASRS RORS"] -->|update| Cbit
    SatInstruct["Saturation Instructions SSAT USAT"] -->|update| Qbit
    Branch["Conditional Branch BNE BEQ BGT BLT"] -->|reads| HighNibble
```

## 4.2 Flag-Update Decision Flow

The diagram below captures the **decision logic** the CPU executes after every flag-updating data-processing instruction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Data Processing Instruction with S suffix executes"]
    Start --> Compute["CPU performs 32 bit operation and produces result"]
    Compute --> SetN["Set N to result bit 31"]
    Compute --> SetZ["Set Z to 1 if all result bits are 0 else 0"]
    Compute --> OpType{"Is it an arithmetic instruction?"}
    OpType -->|Yes ADDS SUBS RSBS CMN CMP| SetCV["Compute C from carry out and V from signed overflow formula"]
    OpType -->|No ANDS ORRS EORS BICS| PreserveCV["Preserve C and V flags unchanged"]
    OpType -->|Shift LSLS LSRS ASRS RORS| SetCShift["Set C to the last bit shifted out preserve V"]
    SetCV --> End["Flags written to APSR bits 31 to 28"]
    SetN --> End
    SetZ --> End
    PreserveCV --> End
    SetCShift --> End
    End --> BranchCheck["Next Conditional Branch reads APSR"]
```

## 4.3 Conditional Branch ↔ Flag Mapping (Block View)

| Conditional Branch | Mnemonic | Condition Checked (in terms of APSR flags) |
|:------------------:|:--------:|:-------------------------------------------|
| `BEQ` | Equal | $Z = 1$ |
| `BNE` | Not equal | $Z = 0$ |
| `BCS` / `BHS` | Carry set / unsigned higher or same | $C = 1$ |
| `BCC` / `BLO` | Carry clear / unsigned lower | $C = 0$ |
| `BMI` | Negative | $N = 1$ |
| `BPL` | Positive | $N = 0$ |
| `BVS` | Signed overflow | $V = 1$ |
| `BVC` | No signed overflow | $V = 0$ |
| `BGT` | Signed greater than | $Z = 0 \;\wedge\; N = V$ |
| `BLT` | Signed less than | $N \neq V$ |
| `BGE` | Signed greater than or equal | $N = V$ |
| `BLE` | Signed less than or equal | $Z = 1 \;\vert\; N \neq V$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above table is the **heart of every C `if`-statement** compiled to ARM. The four APSR flags are the *only* state that the CPU retains across a branch decision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: APSR Register Layout `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: List the four arithmetic condition flags stored in the APSR of an ARM Cortex-M processor. State the bit position and the condition under which each flag is set after an `ADDS` instruction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The APSR contains four flags in the upper nibble (bits 31–28):
1. **N (bit 31) — Negative:** Set to the value of bit 31 of the result.
2. **Z (bit 30) — Zero:** Set to 1 if the 32-bit result equals 0; else 0.
3. **C (bit 29) — Carry:** Set to 1 if there is an unsigned carry-out from bit 31 of `op1 + op2`; else 0.
4. **V (bit 28) — Overflow:** Set to 1 if a signed overflow occurs, i.e., two operands of the same sign produce a result of the opposite sign.

*(1 mark for listing the flags with bit positions, 1 mark for N and Z rule, 1 mark for C and V rule of ADDS.)*

### Question 2: Difference Between `ADD` and `ADDS` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between `ADD` and `ADDS` instructions in ARM Cortex-M. In what way does the choice affect the APSR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `ADD Rd, Rn, Rm` performs a 32-bit addition and writes the sum into `Rd`, but the **flags in APSR are NOT modified** — they retain whatever value they held before the instruction. (1 mark)
- `ADDS Rd, Rn, Rm` performs the same addition **and** updates N, Z, C, and V in APSR based on the result. (1 mark)
- Practical consequence: a sequence of arithmetic computations followed by a single conditional branch must use the `S` form on the *last* instruction; otherwise the branch will test stale flags. Conversely, plain `ADD` should be used in the middle of an expression to avoid spuriously disturbing flags that an outer conditional branch depends upon. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**a)** Explain the four condition flags stored in the APSR of an ARM Cortex-M4 processor. With a neat sketch, show the bit positions of each flag and describe the conditions under which they are set by the `ADDS` instruction. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Solve the following: For the operands $R1 = 0\text{x}7FFFFFFE$ and $R2 = 0\text{x}00000002$, determine the resulting APSR flags after executing `ADDS R0, R1, R2`. Show every binary step. Also state which conditional branch instructions would be taken. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**a) APSR Flags (7 marks):**

The **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** is a 32-bit register at address `0xE000ED00`. The four condition flags occupy the upper nibble:

| Bit | Flag | Meaning |
|:---:|:----:|:--------|
| 31  | N | Negative (sign of result) |
| 30  | Z | Zero (result equals 0) |
| 29  | C | Carry (unsigned overflow) |
| 28  | V | Signed overflow |

**Update rules for `ADDS`:**
- $N \leftarrow \text{result[31]}$ (2 marks)
- $Z \leftarrow 1$ if all result bits are zero, else 0 (1 mark)
- $C \leftarrow$ carry out of bit 31 during $\text{op1} + \text{op2}$ (2 marks)
- $V \leftarrow 1$ when $\text{op1[31]} = \text{op2[31]}$ but $\text{result[31]} \neq \text{op1[31]}$ (2 marks)

**b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R1} &= 0\text{x}7\text{FFFFFFE} = 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1110_2 \\
\text{R2} &= 0\text{x}00000002 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0010_2 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 1000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \\
&= 0\text{x}80000000
\end{aligned}$$

(2 marks for binary addition)

- **N flag:** result[31] = 1 → **N = 1** (1 mark)
- **Z flag:** result ≠ 0 → **Z = 0** (1 mark)
- **C flag:** Sum is `0x80000000`, well below `0xFFFFFFFF`; no carry out → **C = 0** (1 mark)
- **V flag:** op1[31] = 0, op2[31] = 0, result[31] = 1. Two positives sum to a negative → **V = 1** (1 mark)

**Conditional branches that would be taken:**
- `BMI` (N=1), `BVS` (V=1) — taken.
- `BEQ`, `BCC`, `BPL`, `BVC` — not taken.
- `BGT` would NOT be taken (since Z must be 0 AND N=V; here N=1, V=1, so N=V holds, but Z=0 too — wait, BGT requires Z=0 AND N=V. Here Z=0 and N=V=1, so **BGT is taken**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently write `C = 1` for `ADDS` whenever the result is negative. **This is wrong.** The C flag is about *unsigned* carry-out, not about the sign of the result. In Example (b) above, $0\text{x}7FFFFFFE + 0\text{x}00000002 = 0\text{x}80000000$, which has NO unsigned carry-out, so C = 0.
> 2. The V flag is often confused with C. Remember: **C is unsigned overflow, V is signed overflow**. They are independent.
> 3. Forgetting that the C flag for `SUBS` is the *inverse* of the borrow: a borrow occurred ⇒ C = 0, NOT 1.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**a)** Compare and contrast the flag-update behaviour of `ADDS`, `ANDS`, and `LSLS` (logical shift left) in ARM Cortex-M. Construct a single table showing exactly which of N, Z, C, V are updated and how. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Consider the following code snippet:
```assembly
MOV   R1, #0x80000000
MOV   R2, #0x80000000
ADDS  R3, R1, R2
```
Determine the contents of R3 and the APSR flags after execution. If the next instruction is `BLT label`, will the branch be taken? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Flag | `ADDS` | `ANDS` | `LSLS` |
|:----:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| **N** | Set to result[31] (1 mark) | Set to result[31] (1 mark) | Set to result[31] (0.5 mark) |
| **Z** | 1 if result = 0 (0.5 mark) | 1 if result = 0 (0.5 mark) | 1 if result = 0 (0.5 mark) |
| **C** | Carry-out of bit 31 (1 mark) | **Preserved** (1 mark) | Last bit shifted out (1 mark) |
| **V** | Signed overflow (1 mark) | **Preserved** (1 mark) | **Preserved** (0.5 mark) |

(remaining 0.5 mark for the "preserve vs update" conceptual distinction)

**b) Worked Calculation (7 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R1} &= \text{R2} = 0\text{x}80000000 = 1000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \\
\text{R3} &= \text{R1} + \text{R2} = 0\text{x}00000000 \;(\text{with carry out discarded into C})
\end{aligned}$$

(2 marks for binary addition and identification of carry-out)

- **R3** = `0x00000000` (1 mark)
- **N** = result[31] = 0 → **N = 0** (0.5 mark)
- **Z** = result == 0 → **Z = 1** (0.5 mark)
- **C** = carry out of bit 31 = 1 (since `0x80000000 + 0x80000000 = 0x100000000`, upper bit carried out) → **C = 1** (1 mark)
- **V** = two negative operands (−2³¹) summed to 0 (positive). Same signs in, different sign out → **V = 1** (1 mark)

**Branch decision (1 mark):** `BLT` is "Branch if Less Than", which is taken when $N \neq V$. Here $N = 0$ and $V = 1$, so $N \neq V$ holds ⇒ **the branch IS taken**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Q.B:**
> 1. Many students forget that for ADD, the carry flag holds the *discarded* bit 32. They write C=0 because the stored 32-bit result is 0. **This is the most common mistake in ARM exams.**
> 2. For `ANDS`, students often write "C = 0" or "C = 1". The correct answer is **C is preserved** (unchanged from whatever it was before). Similarly for V.
> 3. For `LSLS`, C is the bit that was shifted *out* of the operand, not into bit 0.
> 4. In Q.B(b), the final `BLT` branch logic requires `N != V`. Students who compute only Z and forget V will incorrectly conclude the branch is not taken.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section just before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ The **APSR** sits at address `0xE000ED00` and shares storage with the IPSR and EPSR to form the unified `xPSR`.
- ✅ The four **arithmetic condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) are in **bits 31–28** of APSR.
- ✅ The **`S` suffix** is what tells the CPU to update flags. Without it, APSR is **untouched**.
- ✅ **N flag** = sign bit (bit 31) of the result. Applies to *all* `S` instructions.
- ✅ **Z flag** = 1 if the 32-bit result is exactly zero. Applies to *all* `S` instructions.
- ✅ **C flag (arithmetic)** = carry out of bit 31 for ADD; inverse-of-borrow for SUB. **Preserved** for logical ops.
- ✅ **V flag (arithmetic)** = signed overflow. **Preserved** for logical ops.
- ✅ **`CMP`** is `SUBS` with discarded result. **`CMN`** is `ADDS` with discarded result. **`TST`** is `ANDS` with discarded result. **`TEQ`** is `EORS` with discarded result. All four update flags automatically.
- ✅ **`MULS`, `SDIVS`, `UDIVS`** update N and Z only; C and V are preserved.
- ✅ **Shifts** (`LSLS`, `LSRS`, `ASRS`, `RORS` with S) update C to the last bit shifted out and V is preserved.
- ✅ The mnemonic for **V on ADD** is: "pos + pos = neg" or "neg + neg = pos".
- ✅ The mnemonic for **V on SUB** is: "pos − neg = neg" or "neg − pos = pos".
- ✅ **Conditional branches** (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, `BLT`, etc.) read the flags set by the *most recent* flag-updating instruction — there is no separate "compare register."
- ✅ The **Q flag** (bit 27) is set only by the saturation instructions `SSAT` and `USAT`, and is *not* part of the four main condition flags.
- ✅ When in doubt, remember the **golden rule:** *APSCR is updated by S-suffixed instructions and read by conditional branches. It is the only state surviving across a branch decision.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture, **Arithmetic Instructions** form a critical sub-class of the *Data Processing* instruction category. They are responsible for performing mathematical computations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and multiplication-accumulation on values held in CPU registers. ARM arithmetic instructions operate exclusively on **32-bit word-sized operands** loaded into the General-Purpose Registers (GPRs) **R0 through R12**, and may optionally update the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR) condition flags** (`N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`) based on the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PEECT524 Module 2 syllabus, Arithmetic Instructions specifically encompass the **Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, and Multiply-Accumulate (MAC)** families, their flexible second-operand syntax (shifts/rotates), and the impact of the **barrel shifter** on instruction latency and flag generation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Calculator in Your Pocket"

Imagine a high-school math classroom where every student has a tiny pocket calculator. This calculator can **only** work with numbers it can physically hold in its memory slots (the **registers**), and it has exactly **three input slots** — *Slot A* (the destination/Rd), *Slot B* (the source/Rn), and *Slot C* (the operand2, which can be transformed *before* the operation using a built-in "magic shifter" called the **barrel shifter**). 

When a teacher says, *"Take the value in Slot B, shift it left by 2 bits, add the value in Slot A, and tell me whether the result is zero or negative"* — that single sentence describes a complete ARM arithmetic instruction, for example:

```assembly
ADDS   R0, R1, R2, LSL #2      ; R0 = R1 + (R2 << 2), and update flags
```

The **barrel shifter** is the "magic" component that allows the second operand to be modified *for free* in a single cycle, eliminating the need for separate shift instructions before arithmetic operations.

## 1.3 Classification of ARM Arithmetic Instructions

ARM arithmetic instructions can be broadly classified into the following families:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Arithmetic Instruction Families (per ARMv7-A/R Reference Manual):**
> 1. **Addition Instructions** — `ADD`, `ADC`, `ADR`, `RSB`, `RSC`
> 2. **Subtraction Instructions** — `SUB`, `SBC`
> 3. **Multiplication Instructions** — `MUL`, `MLA`
> 4. **Multiply-Long Instructions (64-bit result)** — `UMULL`, `UMLAL`, `SMULL`, `SMLAL`

## 1.4 Instruction Encoding — The Big Picture

Every ARM instruction is encoded into a **fixed 32-bit word**. The standard encoding layout for a data processing (arithmetic) instruction is:

$$
\underbrace{cond}_{[31:28]} \;
\underbrace{00}_{[27:26]} \;
\underbrace{I}_{[25]} \;
\underbrace{opcode}_{[24:21]} \;
\underbrace{S}_{[20]} \;
\underbrace{Rn}_{[19:16]} \;
\underbrace{Rd}_{[15:12]} \;
\underbrace{operand2}_{[11:0]}
$$

- **`cond`** — 4-bit condition code (e.g., `EQ`, `NE`, `GT`)
- **`opcode`** — specifies the operation (e.g., `0100` = ADD, `0010` = SUB, `1001` = MUL)
- **`S`** — when set, updates CPSR flags
- **`Rn`, `Rd`** — source and destination registers
- **`operand2`** — the **flexible second operand** (register + shifter, or 8-bit immediate + rotate)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Syntax of Arithmetic Instructions

The universal ARM assembly syntax for an arithmetic instruction is:

```assembly
<operation>{<cond>}{S}   Rd, Rn, Operand2
```

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `<operation>` | The mnemonic (ADD, SUB, MUL, etc.) |
| `<cond>` | Optional 2-letter condition code (e.g., EQ, NE, LT) |
| `S` | Optional suffix; if present, sets condition flags in CPSR |
| `Rd` | Destination Register (R0–R12) |
| `Rn` | First source Register (R0–R12) |
| `Operand2` | The **flexible second operand** — a register with optional shift, or an immediate constant with optional rotation |

## 2.2 The Flexible Second Operand — Why ARM is Unique

The **barrel shifter** is a dedicated hardware unit inside the ARM datapath that performs shifts and rotates on the second operand *before* it reaches the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU). This means an arithmetic instruction can **pre-process** its operand in a single cycle, completely free of extra latency.

### 2.2.1 The Five Modes of Operand2

| Mode | Syntax | Operation | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | `#imm` | Loads an 8-bit constant rotated right by an even number | Embedding small constants without separate LDR |
| Register | `Rm` | Uses the value of Rm directly | Simple ALU operations |
| Logical Shift Left | `Rm, LSL #n` | `Rm × 2ⁿ` | Fast multiply-by-power-of-two |
| Logical Shift Right | `Rm, LSR #n` | `Rm ÷ 2ⁿ` (unsigned) | Fast divide-by-power-of-two (unsigned) |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | `Rm, ASR #n` | `Rm ÷ 2ⁿ` (signed, sign-preserving) | Fast signed divide |
| Rotate Right | `Rm, ROR #n` | Right rotation by n bits | Bit manipulation, MD5/CRC algorithms |
| Rotate Right Extended | `Rm, RRX` | 33-bit rotate through Carry flag | Multi-word shifts |

## 2.3 Detailed Walkthrough of the Instruction Families

### 2.3.1 ADD — Addition

Performs unsigned or signed (two's complement) addition of two 32-bit values.

```assembly
ADD   Rd, Rn, Operand2     ; Rd = Rn + Operand2
ADDS   Rd, Rn, Operand2    ; Same as above, but updates CPSR flags
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Used in array indexing, pointer arithmetic, hash table addressing, and nearly every high-level language compiled to ARM.

### 2.3.2 ADC — Add with Carry

Adds two operands **plus** the carry flag. This is the workhorse for **multi-word arithmetic** (e.g., adding two 64-bit numbers using two 32-bit registers).

```assembly
ADCS   R3, R4, R5          ; R3 = R4 + R5 + C-flag
```

### 2.3.3 SUB — Subtraction

```assembly
SUB   Rd, Rn, Operand2     ; Rd = Rn - Operand2
SUBS   Rd, Rn, Operand2    ; Same, with flag updates
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The C (Carry) flag in SUB instructions is set to **NOT borrow** (i.e., C=1 if `Rn ≥ Operand2`, no borrow occurred). This is the **inverse** of most other architectures, and is a frequent error point in board exams.

### 2.3.4 SBC — Subtract with Carry

Performs subtraction that includes a borrow (the inverted carry flag). Together with `RSB` (Reverse Subtract), it enables 64-bit subtraction:

```assembly
SBCS   R1, R2, R3          ; R1 = R2 - R3 - NOT(C)
```

### 2.3.5 RSB / RSC — Reverse Subtract

Performs `Rd = Operand2 - Rn` (operand order reversed). Useful for computing *negation*:

```assembly
RSB   Rd, Rn, #0           ; Rd = -Rn  (the canonical negation idiom)
```

### 2.3.6 MUL and MLA — Multiplication (32-bit Result)

ARM's basic multiply produces a **32-bit result** (the lower half of the true product). This is sufficient for most applications and fits the 32-bit datapath.

$$
\text{MUL: } \quad Rd = (Rm \times Rs)_{[31:0]}
$$

$$
\text{MLA: } \quad Rd = (Rm \times Rs + Rn)_{[31:0]}
$$

```assembly
MUL   R0, R1, R2           ; R0 = R1 * R2
MLA   R0, R1, R2, R3       ; R0 = (R1 * R2) + R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Restriction:** In the MUL/MLA encoding, the destination register `Rd` **cannot be the same** as `Rm`. This is a hardware-imposed limitation; otherwise the multiply unit would have a structural hazard.

### 2.3.7 UMULL, UMLAL, SMULL, SMLAL — Long Multiply (64-bit Result)

These instructions produce a full **64-bit result** stored across two registers (`RdLo`, `RdHi`):

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Signed/Unsigned |
|---|---|---|
| `UMULL` | Unsigned Multiply Long | Unsigned |
| `UMLAL` | Unsigned Multiply-Accumulate Long | Unsigned |
| `SMULL` | Signed Multiply Long | Signed (two's complement) |
| `SMLAL` | Signed Multiply-Accumulate Long | Signed |

$$
\text{UMULL: } \quad \{RdHi, RdLo\} = Rm \times Rs \quad \text{(unsigned 64-bit)}
$$

$$
\text{UMLAL: } \quad \{RdHi, RdLo\} = \{RdHi, RdLo\} + (Rm \times Rs) \quad \text{(unsigned 64-bit accumulate)}
$$

## 2.4 Condition Code Suffixes — Conditional Execution

Every ARM instruction can be made conditional by appending a 2-letter code, eliminating branch penalties. The most common ones used in arithmetic are:

| Suffix | Flag Tested | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `EQ` | Z=1 | Equal |
| `NE` | Z=0 | Not Equal |
| `LT` | N≠V | Less Than (signed) |
| `LE` | Z=1 OR N≠V | Less or Equal (signed) |
| `GT` | Z=0 AND N=V | Greater Than (signed) |
| `GE` | N=V | Greater or Equal (signed) |
| `HI` | C=1 AND Z=0 | Higher (unsigned) |
| `LS` | C=0 OR Z=1 | Lower or Same (unsigned) |
| `CC` / `LO` | C=0 | Carry Clear |
| `CS` / `HS` | C=1 | Carry Set |
| `MI` | N=1 | Negative |
| `PL` | N=0 | Positive or Zero |
| `VS` | V=1 | Signed Overflow |
| `VC` | V=0 | No Signed Overflow |
| `AL` | (always) | Always executes (default) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, flag-rule, and encoding constant** that has been historically tested in KTU board examinations for this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| ADD result | `Rd = Rn + Operand2` | Addition |
| SUB result | `Rd = Rn - Operand2` | Subtraction |
| ADC result | `Rd = Rn + Operand2 + C` | Multi-word addition |
| SBC result | `Rd = Rn - Operand2 - NOT(C)` | Multi-word subtraction |
| RSB result | `Rd = Operand2 - Rn` | Negation (`RSB Rd, Rn, #0`) |
| MUL result | `Rd = (Rm × Rs) mod 2³²` | Fast 32-bit multiply |
| MLA result | `Rd = (Rm × Rs + Rn) mod 2³²` | Multiply-accumulate (e.g., dot product) |
| UMULL result | `{RdHi, RdLo} = Rm × Rs` (64-bit unsigned) | Full 64-bit unsigned product |
| SMULL result | `{RdHi, RdLo} = Rm × Rs` (64-bit signed) | Full 64-bit signed product |
| UMLAL result | `{RdHi, RdLo} += Rm × Rs` | 64-bit unsigned MAC |
| LSL shift | `x << n` ≡ `x × 2ⁿ` | Multiply by power of 2 |
| LSR shift | `x >> n` (unsigned) ≡ `⌊x ÷ 2ⁿ⌋` | Unsigned divide by power of 2 |
| ASR shift | `x >>ₛ n` (sign-preserving) | Signed divide by power of 2 |
| Immediate value | `imm = imm8 ROR (2 × rot)` | Encoding an arbitrary 8-bit rotated constant |
| N flag | `N = result[31]` | Negative result |
| Z flag | `Z = 1 if result == 0` | Zero result |
| C flag (ADD/ADC) | `C = carry out of bit 31` | Unsigned overflow |
| C flag (SUB/SBC) | `C = NOT(borrow out of bit 31)` | **Inverted borrow** — common exam trap |
| V flag (ADD) | `V = (Rn[31] == Operand2[31]) AND (Rd[31] ≠ Rn[31])` | Signed overflow |
| V flag (SUB) | `V = (Rn[31] ≠ Operand2[31]) AND (Rd[31] ≠ Rn[31])` | Signed overflow |
| `MUL` restriction | `Rd ≠ Rm` | Hardware constraint |
| Long-mul restriction | `RdHi ≠ RdLo ≠ Rm ≠ Rs` | Hardware constraint (post-ARMv6, partially relaxed) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of ARM Arithmetic Instructions |
|---|---|
| **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** | MLA/MLAL for FIR filters, convolution, FFT butterflies |
| **Cryptography** | Barrett/Montgomery reduction using UMULL/UMLAL on ARMv7 |
| **Image Processing** | SIMD-style summation via MLA loops in 1D/2D convolution kernels |
| **Compilers** | Strength reduction: `x * 5` → `ADD x, x, LSL #2` |
| **Operating Systems** | Address computation, page table walks, TLB update arithmetic |
| **Machine Learning (Edge AI)** | 8-bit quantized MACs emulated via SMULL+ASR sequences |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Flag Calculation for `SUBS R0, R1, R2`

> **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU question):** For the operands `R1 = 0x00000005` and `R2 = 0x00000007`, manually execute `SUBS R0, R1, R2` and determine the values of the N, Z, C, V flags.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Perform the binary subtraction in 32 bits.**

We compute `0x00000005 - 0x00000007`. Since the result is negative, we expect a borrow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decimal: } & 5 - 7 = -2 \\
\text{Hex (32-bit two's complement): } & \text{0x00000005} - \text{0x00000007} \\
& = \text{0xFFFFFFFE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the N flag (Negative).**

The N flag mirrors the most significant bit of the result:

$$
N = \text{result}[31] = 0\text{xFFFFFFFE}[31] = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Z flag (Zero).**

$$
Z = (R0 == 0) = (0\text{xFFFFFFFE} == 0) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the C flag (NOT Borrow — ARM's inverted convention).**

In ARM subtraction, the C flag is set to **1 if NO borrow occurred**, i.e., if `Rn ≥ Operand2`. Since `5 < 7`, a borrow *does* occur, so:

$$
C = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the V flag (Signed Overflow).**

For SUB, overflow occurs if the operands have **different signs** and the result has the **opposite sign** of `Rn`:

$$
V = (\text{sign}(Rn) \neq \text{sign}(Operand2)) \text{ AND } (\text{sign}(Rd) \neq \text{sign}(Rn))
$$

- `sign(R1) = 0` (positive)
- `sign(R2) = 0` (positive)
- Since `sign(R1) == sign(R2)`, the first AND term fails.

$$
V = 0
$$

### 3.1.2 Final Result

$$
\boxed{R0 = 0\text{xFFFFFFFE}, \quad N=1, \ Z=0, \ C=0, \ V=0}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Encoding the Immediate `0xFC00` in an ARM Instruction

> **Problem (Frequent 5–7 mark KTU question):** Show how the ARM assembler encodes the constant `#0xFC00` as a 12-bit Operand2 (8-bit immediate + 4-bit rotation).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express `0xFC00` in binary (only the low 8 bits are needed).**

$$
0\text{xFC00} = 1111\,1100\,0000\,0000_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the rotation needed to bring an 8-bit pattern into the lowest 8 bits.**

The pattern `11111100` (which is `0xFC`) must be rotated **right by 26 bits** so it ends up at bit positions `[11:4]` of the final 32-bit value, then shifted into `[27:8]` of the instruction's Operand2 field. ARM encodes rotation as a multiple of 2:

$$
\text{rot} = 26 / 2 = 13 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{4-bit field} = 0b1101 = 13
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the round-trip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{imm} & = 0\text{xFC} = 0b1111\,1100 \\
\text{rotate right by 26} & = 0\text{xFC} \gg 26 \\
& = 0\text{xFC00} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Final Encoded Form

The 12-bit Operand2 field is:

$$
\boxed{\text{imm8} = 0\text{xFC}, \quad \text{rot}_4 = 13 = 0b1101}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** ARM's clever 8-bit-rotate encoding lets it represent any 32-bit constant that is a multiple/rotated multiple of a byte — a far richer set than the naive 8-bit immediate would suggest, without inflating instruction width.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — MAC Dot Product Using MLA

> **Problem:** Compute the dot product of vectors **A = [2, 3, 4]** and **B = [5, 6, 7]** stored in memory, using ARM MLA instructions. The result must accumulate in `R0`.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Assembly

```assembly
        LDR     R1, =A            ; R1 → base of array A
        LDR     R2, =B            ; R2 → base of array B
        MOV     R0, #0            ; R0 = accumulator = 0
        LDR     R3, [R1]          ; R3 = A[0] = 2
        LDR     R4, [R2]          ; R4 = B[0] = 5
        MLA     R0, R3, R4, R0    ; R0 = 2*5 + 0 = 10
        LDR     R3, [R1, #4]      ; R3 = A[1] = 3
        LDR     R4, [R2, #4]      ; R4 = B[1] = 6
        MLA     R0, R3, R4, R0    ; R0 = 3*6 + 10 = 28
        LDR     R3, [R1, #8]      ; R3 = A[2] = 4
        LDR     R4, [R2, #8]      ; R4 = B[2] = 7
        MLA     R0, R3, R4, R0    ; R0 = 4*7 + 28 = 56
```

### 3.3.2 Numerical Verification

$$
(2 \times 5) + (3 \times 6) + (4 \times 7) = 10 + 18 + 28 = 56
$$

$$
\boxed{R0 = 56}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A single MLA instruction replaces two separate MUL and ADD instructions, halving the cycle count in tight DSP loops. Modern ARM Cortex-M cores can issue an MLA every cycle when the pipeline is kept full.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — 64-bit Addition Using `ADD` + `ADC`

> **Problem:** Add the 64-bit value in `{R1:R0}` to the 64-bit value in `{R3:R2}`, placing the result in `{R5:R4}` (R0,R2,R4 are the low words; R1,R3,R5 are the high words).

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Assembly

```assembly
        ADDS    R4, R0, R2        ; Add low words, set carry if overflow
        ADC     R5, R1, R3        ; Add high words PLUS carry
```

### 3.4.2 Worked Numerical Verification

Let `{R1:R0} = 0x00000002_00000001` and `{R3:R2} = 0x00000001_FFFFFFFF`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 + R2 & : \quad 0\text{x00000001} + 0\text{xFFFFFFFF} = 0\text{x00000000}, \quad C=1 \\
R1 + R3 + C & : \quad 0\text{x00000002} + 0\text{x00000001} + 1 = 0\text{x00000004}, \quad C=0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:** `{R5:R4} = 0x00000004_00000000` (which equals 8,596,014,387,200, consistent with `(2³²+1) + (2³²+2³²−1) = 2·2³² + 2³² = 4·2³²`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always use `ADDS` (with the S suffix) on the *low* word, but plain `ADC` (no S) on the *high* word unless you specifically want to update flags for a subsequent conditional branch. This idiom appears in nearly every KTU ESE question on multi-word arithmetic.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a one-stop reference mapping each instruction to its operand format, the flag-update rule, the hardware constraint, and a typical use case — perfect for last-minute KTU revision.

| Mnemonic | Operands | Operation | Flags Updated? | Hardware Restriction | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Rn + Op2` | Optional via `S` | None | General addition |
| `ADC` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Rn + Op2 + C` | Optional via `S` | None | 64-bit arithmetic |
| `SUB` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Rn - Op2` | Optional via `S` | None | General subtraction |
| `SBC` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Rn - Op2 - !C` | Optional via `S` | None | 64-bit subtraction |
| `RSB` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Op2 - Rn` | Optional via `S` | None | Negation idiom |
| `RSC` | Rd, Rn, Op2 | `Rd = Op2 - Rn - !C` | Optional via `S` | None | 64-bit reverse subtract |
| `MUL` | Rd, Rm, Rs | `Rd = Rm × Rs` | Optional via `S` | `Rd ≠ Rm` | 32-bit multiply |
| `MLA` | Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn | `Rd = Rm × Rs + Rn` | Optional via `S` | `Rd ≠ Rm` | Multiply-accumulate |
| `UMULL` | RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs | `{Hi,Lo} = Rm × Rs` (U) | Optional via `S` | All 4 regs distinct (relaxed) | 64-bit unsigned multiply |
| `UMLAL` | RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs | `{Hi,Lo} += Rm × Rs` (U) | Optional via `S` | All 4 regs distinct | 64-bit unsigned MAC |
| `SMULL` | RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs | `{Hi,Lo} = Rm × Rs` (S) | Optional via `S` | All 4 regs distinct | 64-bit signed multiply |
| `SMLAL` | RdLo, RdHi, Rm, Rs | `{Hi,Lo} += Rm × Rs` (S) | Optional via `S` | All 4 regs distinct | 64-bit signed MAC |
| `ADR` | Rd, label | `Rd = PC ± offset` | No | Within ±4 KB | Position-independent code |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Arithmetic Datapath — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the journey of an arithmetic instruction through the ARM7/TDMI-class datapath, showing exactly where the barrel shifter, ALU, and register file interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Fetch Stage] --> B[Instruction Decode Stage]
    B --> C{Operand2 Source Selector}
    C -->|Immediate| D[8-bit Immediate + 4-bit Rotation]
    C -->|Register| E[Read Register File Rm]
    E --> F[Barrel Shifter Unit]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Shifted Operand2 Output]
    G --> H[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
    B --> I[Read Register File Rn]
    I --> H
    H --> J[32-bit Result]
    J --> K{Update CPSR Flags?}
    K -->|Yes - S bit set| L[CPSR N Z C V Updated]
    K -->|No| M[CPSR Preserved]
    J --> N[Write Back Stage to Rd]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Next Instruction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The barrel shifter is *not* a separate cycle — it operates in parallel with the second register read, so the entire arithmetic instruction (including the shift) completes in a single datapath cycle for non-multiply operations.

## 4.2 MUL/MLA Pipeline vs Long-Multiply Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StandardMUL["Standard 32-bit MUL / MLA"]
        S1[Cycle 1: Issue] --> S2[Cycle 2: Booth Encode]
        S2 --> S3[Cycle 3-4: Partial Product Reduction]
        S3 --> S4[Cycle 5: Final Carry Propagate Add]
        S4 --> S5[Cycle 6: Result to Rd]
    end
    subgraph LongMUL["64-bit UMULL / SMULL"]
        L1[Cycle 1: Issue] --> L2[Cycle 2: Booth Encode]
        L2 --> L3[Cycle 3-5: 32-bit Sub-Products]
        L3 --> L4[Cycle 6-7: Final 64-bit Add]
        L4 --> L5[Cycle 7-8: Result to RdHi and RdLo]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** ARM's `MUL`/`MLA` typically take **2–4 internal cycles** (depending on the implementation), while `UMULL`/`SMULL` take **3–6 cycles** because they must compute and combine 32 sub-products. The compiler pipeline is unaffected because the issue logic interlocks until the result is ready.

## 4.3 Flag-Update Decision Tree for Arithmetic Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Arithmetic Instruction has S bit set?}
    Start -->|No| F1[Flags unchanged in CPSR]
    Start -->|Yes| F2{Operation Type}
    F2 -->|ADD or ADC| F3[Compute N from bit 31]
    F2 -->|SUB or SBC| F4[Compute N from bit 31]
    F2 -->|MUL or MLA| F5[Compute N from bit 31]
    F3 --> G1[Z = result == 0]
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{ADD ADC MUL MLA}
    H1 -->|Yes| I1[C = carry out of bit 31]
    H1 -->|No SUB SBC| I2[C = NOT borrow out of bit 31]
    I1 --> J1[V = signed overflow check]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> End[Write All Four Flags to CPSR]
```

## 4.4 Flexible Operand2 — Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Assembler encounters Operand2] --> P2{Starts with hash?}
    P2 -->|Yes - Immediate| P3[Try to encode as 8-bit rotated]
    P3 --> P4{Has valid rotation?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Encode as imm8 plus 4-bit rot]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Error: literal pool needed, use LDR]
    P2 -->|No - Register| P7{Has shift suffix?}
    P7 -->|No| P8[Use Rm directly]
    P7 -->|Yes LSL| P9[Logical shift left]
    P7 -->|Yes LSR| P10[Logical shift right]
    P7 -->|Yes ASR| P11[Arithmetic shift right]
    P7 -->|Yes ROR| P12[Rotate right]
    P7 -->|Yes RRX| P13[Rotate right extended by Carry]
    P9 --> Q1[Output shifted operand2 to ALU]
    P10 --> Q1
    P11 --> Q1
    P12 --> Q1
    P8 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[ALU performs operation with Rn]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use Case:** This is the diagram most frequently requested in KTU ESE questions. A student who can draw this from memory demonstrates mastery of the assembly syntax resolution process and the encoding constraints of the barrel shifter.
