> **Exam Tip:** If the C source says `if (a > b)`, the ARM idiom is `BGT THEN_BODY` — but **only if the body follows the label**. If the body follows the `B` instruction directly, you must write `BLE SKIP` to skip *around* it. The compiler always chooses the latter (more efficient) form.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Translating a Compound Condition (C `&&` / `||`)

A common PEECT524 exam question: translate `if (a > 0 && b < 100)`. ARM does **not** have native AND/OR condition instructions in classic ARM mode; the compiler uses **short-circuit evaluation with successive branches**.

### Translation Algorithm

High-level: `if (C1 && C2) { B_T }`

Step 1: Evaluate C1. If false, skip everything.

```armasm
        CMP     Ra, #0
        BLE     SKIP          ; if a <= 0, jump out
```

Step 2: Evaluate C2. If false, skip everything.

```armasm
        CMP     Rb, #100
        BGE     SKIP          ; if b >= 100, jump out
```

Step 3: Both true, execute then-block.

```armasm
        ; <B_T body>
SKIP    ; <continuation>
```

### Analytical Matrix — `&&` vs. `||` Translation

| Operator | Semantics | ARM Translation Strategy | Number of Branches (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `&&` | True only if **both** operands are true | **Short-circuit on first false**: branch out as soon as any sub-condition fails | $n$ branches for $n$ sub-conditions |
| `\|\|` | True if **any** operand is true | **Short-circuit on first true**: branch into the body as soon as a sub-condition succeeds | $n$ branches + 1 final skip |
| `!` (logical NOT) | Inverts the boolean value | Invert the condition code (use the table in §3.2) | 1 branch |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough: A Real C-to-ARM Translation

Let us fully translate the C statement `if (x == 5) y = y + 1;`.

### High-Level

```c
if (x == 5) {
    y = y + 1;
}
```

### ARM Assembly Translation (with full commentary)

```armasm
        ; Assume x is in R0, y is in R1
        CMP     R0, #5          ; @1: Set flags: R0 - 5
                                ;     Flags updated:
                                ;     Z = 1 if R0 == 5, else Z = 0
        BNE     SKIP             ; @2: Branch to SKIP if Z=0 (i.e., x != 5)
                                ;     This is the "skip" idiom using NE = NOT(EQ)
        ADD     R1, R1, #1      ; @3: Then-block: y = y + 1
SKIP    ; <continuation>          ; @4: Fall-through / merge point
```

### Step-by-Step Explanation

- **Line 1 (`CMP R0, #5`)**: ARM internally computes $R0 - 5 = R0 + \overline{5} + 1$, throws the result away, and updates CPSR. The Z flag is set if and only if the result is exactly zero, i.e., $R0 = 5$.

- **Line 2 (`BNE SKIP`)**: We use the **negated** condition (`NE` = not equal) because we want to **skip the body when the condition is false**. The PC is updated to point at the `SKIP` label.

- **Line 3 (`ADD R1, R1, #1`)**: Only executes when the `BNE` did *not* branch, i.e., when $Z=1$, i.e., when $x = 5$. This is the body.

- **Line 4 (`SKIP`)**: The natural fall-through point for the rest of the program.

> [!TIP]
> **Counter-example (WRONG translation):** If a student wrote `BEQ THEN` and put the `ADD` after a label `THEN:`, the code would still be logically correct, but it would be **less efficient** (it adds one extra label, and the assembler may not be able to fold the branch). The `BNE SKIP` form is the canonical compiler output and is what KTU expects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** with a letter prefix (e.g., `nodeA1`, `stepStart`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- All labels contain **plain alphanumeric text** with no markdown bold/italics/HTML.
- One blank line is maintained before and after each `mermaid` code block.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: If-Then Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Program Counter at CMP] --> A2[Execute CMP Ra Rb: Set CPSR Flags]
    A2 --> A3{Flags Match True Condition?}
    A3 -- Yes / Condition TRUE --> A4[Execute Then Block BT]
    A4 --> A5[Fall Through to SKIP Label]
    A3 -- No / Condition FALSE --> A6[Branch over BT to SKIP Label]
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A7[Continue with rest of program]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: If-Then-Else Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start: Program Counter at CMP] --> B2[Execute CMP Ra Rb: Set CPSR Flags]
    B2 --> B3{Flags Match True Condition?}
    B3 -- No / Condition FALSE --> B4[Branch to ELSE Label]
    B3 -- Yes / Condition TRUE --> B5[Execute Then Block BT]
    B5 --> B6[Unconditional Branch to END]
    B6 --> B7[END Label: Continue Program]
    B4 --> B8[Execute Else Block BE]
    B8 --> B7
    B7 --> B9[Rest of Program Executes Normally]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Block Diagram: The CPSR-to-Branch Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Register Ra Operand 1] --> ALU1[ALU Subtract Logic]
    R2[Register Rb Operand 2] --> ALU1
    ALU1 --> FLAG1[Set CPSR Flags: N Z C V]
    FLAG1 --> DEC1[Decode Condition Code Bcond]
    DEC1 --> PC1[Update Program Counter PC]
    PC1 --> NEXT1[Fetch Next Instruction from New PC]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Activity Diagram: Translation Algorithm (Compiler's Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Parse High Level If Statement] --> C2{Is it If Then or If Then Else?}
    C2 -- If Then --> C3[Emit CMP or TST to Evaluate Condition]
    C3 --> C4[Emit B negated cond to SKIP Label]
    C4 --> C5[Emit Then Block Instructions]
    C5 --> C6[Define SKIP Label]
    C2 -- If Then Else --> C7[Emit CMP to Evaluate Condition]
    C7 --> C8[Emit B negated cond to ELSE Label]
    C8 --> C9[Emit Then Block Instructions]
    C9 --> C10[Emit Unconditional B to END]
    C10 --> C11[Define ELSE Label]
    C11 --> C12[Emit Else Block Instructions]
    C12 --> C13[Define END Label as Merge Point]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**Translate the following C statement to ARM assembly and explain the role of the `CMP` and `BNE` instructions:**
`if (a == 10) b = b + 2;`
*(Assume `a` is in `R0`, `b` is in `R1`.)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

```armasm
        CMP     R0, #10        ; Set Z=1 if R0 == 10
        BNE     SKIP            ; If a != 10, branch over the body
        ADD     R1, R1, #2     ; b = b + 2  (then-block)
SKIP    ; continue program
```

**Role explanation (3 marks):** `CMP R0, #10` performs $R0 - 10$ and updates the **Z flag** in CPSR. `Z=1` indicates equality. `BNE SKIP` reads the Z flag and, if `Z=0` (i.e., `a != 10`), updates the PC to point at `SKIP`, thereby *skipping* the then-block. This is the canonical "skip-if-false" idiom of ARM If-Then translation.

### Question A2

**List the ARM condition codes used to implement the signed C relational operators `<`, `>`, `<=`, and `>=`. Also state one important difference between signed and unsigned comparison in ARM.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| C Operator | ARM Condition Code |
|---|---|
| `<` | `LT` |
| `>` | `GT` |
| `<=` | `LE` |
| `>=` | `GE` |

**Important difference:** Signed comparison uses the **N and V** flags to interpret results (since overflow is meaningful for signed arithmetic), whereas unsigned comparison uses only the **C and Z** flags (e.g., `HI`, `LO`/`CC`, `HS`/`CS`, `LS`). The same C code can yield different branches if the variable's type is changed from `signed int` to `unsigned int`.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Condition code inversion:** The single most common error is failing to *negate* the condition when using the "skip" idiom. If the C says `if (a > b)`, students frequently write `BGT THEN_BODY` and place the body *after* a label. While logically correct, this **adds an extra branch cycle** and is not the canonical compiler output. Always use the **negated** condition with the "skip" form for full marks.
> 2. **Signed vs. unsigned confusion:** Students often write `BGT` for unsigned comparisons. This will be marked wrong — for `unsigned int` use `BHI`.
> 3. **Forgetting the final unconditional `B END`:** In an If-Then-Else, omitting `B END` after the then-block causes *fall-through* into the else-block, producing a logic error. This is a 3-mark deduction in the KTU scheme.
> 4. **Forgetting the $-8$ pipeline offset in offset calculations:** Use the formula $\text{Offset} = (\text{Target} - \text{InstAddr} - 8)/4$.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**Translate the following C code snippet to ARM assembly. Show full register usage and label definitions. Also explain the role of each instruction.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

```c
if (x > y) {
    z = x - y;
} else {
    z = y - x;
}
```
*Assume `x` in `R0`, `y` in `R1`, `z` in `R2`. Use signed comparison.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: ARM Assembly Translation

```armasm
        CMP     R0, R1          ; Set flags: R0 - R1
        BLE     ELSE             ; If x <= y, jump to ELSE
        SUB     R2, R0, R1      ; z = x - y    (then-block)
        B       END              ; Skip else-block
ELSE    SUB     R2, R1, R0      ; z = y - x    (else-block)
END     ; <continuation>
```

**Explanation (per line):**
- `CMP R0, R1`: Subtracts $R1$ from $R0$, sets **N, Z, C, V** in CPSR. For signed comparison, `GT` checks `Z=0` AND `N=V`.
- `BLE ELSE`: Uses the **negated** condition (`LE` = not greater). If $x \leq y$, the PC jumps to `ELSE`, skipping the then-block.
- `SUB R2, R0, R1`: Then-block — stores $x - y$ in `z`.
- `B END`: Unconditional branch over the else-block to the merge point.
- `SUB R2, R1, R0` (under `ELSE`): Else-block — stores $y - x$ in `z`.
- `END` label: Natural merge point.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Branch Offset Calculation

Suppose the `B END` instruction is at address $\text{InstAddr} = 0\text{x}00008010$ and the `END` label is at address $\text{TargetAddr} = 0\text{x}00008020$. Calculate the 24-bit offset that the assembler will encode into the `B` instruction.

**Step 1 — Byte difference.**

$$
\text{TargetAddr} - \text{InstAddr} = 0\text{x}00008020 - 0\text{x}00008010 = 0\text{x}00000010 = 16\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the pipeline compensation of 8 bytes.**

$$
16 - 8 = 8\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a 24-bit word offset.**

$$
\text{Offset}_{24} = \frac{8}{4} = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Encode.** The assembler stores $2$ as a 24-bit value: $0\text{x}000002$. The full encoded `B` instruction (assuming unconditional) is `0xEA000002`.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Translate the following compound conditional to ARM assembly using short-circuit evaluation. Also write the equivalent C code for the assembly fragment given in part (b).**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compound If-Then Translation

Translate:
```c
if (a > 0 && b < 100 && c == 5) {
    result = 1;
} else {
    result = 0;
}
```
*Assume `a` in `R0`, `b` in `R1`, `c` in `R2`, `result` in `R3`.*

**Model ARM Assembly:**

```armasm
        CMP     R0, #0          ; Test a > 0
        BLE     ELSE             ; Short-circuit: a <= 0 fails the AND
        CMP     R1, #100        ; Test b < 100
        BGE     ELSE             ; Short-circuit: b >= 100 fails
        CMP     R2, #5          ; Test c == 5
        BNE     ELSE             ; Short-circuit: c != 5 fails
        MOV     R3, #1          ; Then-block: result = 1
        B       END
ELSE    MOV     R3, #0          ; Else-block: result = 0
END     ; <continuation>
```

**Explanation:** This uses **short-circuit AND evaluation**. As soon as *any* sub-condition fails, control jumps to `ELSE`, bypassing the remaining checks. This is the most efficient translation — it never evaluates `b < 100` if `a <= 0`, mirroring the C semantics precisely.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Reverse Translation (Assembly to C)

Given the ARM fragment:

```armasm
        CMP     R0, R1
        BEQ     EQUAL
        BGT     GREATER
        ; Fall-through: less-than case
        MOV     R2, #-1
        B       DONE
EQUAL   MOV     R2, #0
        B       DONE
GREATER MOV     R2, #1
DONE    ; <continuation>
```

**Reverse-translated C code:**

```c
if (R0_val == R1_val) {
    R2_val = 0;          // equal
} else if (R0_val > R1_val) {
    R2_val = 1;          // greater
} else {
    R2_val = -1;         // less (the only remaining case)
}
```

In idiomatic C with a helper function, this is a classic *three-way comparison* (compare function for `qsort`):

```c
int compare(int a, int b) {
    if (a == b)      return  0;
    else if (a > b)  return  1;
    else             return -1;
}
```

This pattern is used in **C standard library `strcmp`-style routines** and is a frequent PEECT524 exam favourite.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (PEECT524 Module 3):**
> - The **If-Then** pattern is: `CMP` + `B<neg_cond> SKIP` + body + `SKIP` label.
> - The **If-Then-Else** pattern is: `CMP` + `B<neg_cond> ELSE` + then-body + `B END` + `ELSE` label + else-body + `END` label.
> - **Always** use the *negated* condition code when branching *over* a block.
> - **Signed** operators use `GT`, `LT`, `GE`, `LE`. **Unsigned** operators use `HI`, `LO`/`CC`, `HS`/`CS`, `LS`.
> - Branch offset formula: $\text{Offset}_{24} = (\text{TargetAddr} - \text{InstAddr} - 8)/4$.
> - Branch range: $\pm 32\,\text{MB}$ (or $\pm 2^{23}$ words).
> - **Compound conditions** use short-circuit evaluation with successive branches; one branch per sub-condition.
> - `CMP` and `TST` set the **CPSR flags** (N, Z, C, V); they do **not** write a result register.
> - The `B` instruction's target must be **word-aligned** in ARM mode (last 2 bits of offset = 0).
> - Forgetting the unconditional `B END` after the then-block is the **#1 logic error** in KTU scripts.
> - Signed comparison reads **N and V**; unsigned comparison reads **C and Z**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Conditionals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Conditionals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) Architecture**, a **Compound Conditional** refers to the mechanism by which the processor evaluates **multiple Boolean predicates** built from the four condition flags (**N, Z, C, V**) stored in the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**, and uses these predicates to **conditionally execute**, **skip**, or **branch** over instructions. Unlike CISC processors (e.g., x86) where only branch instructions are conditional, in ARM **every data-processing and most load/store instructions** can be suffixed with a **4-bit condition code** that decides whether the instruction executes or becomes a **NOP (No Operation)**.

A *compound* conditional specifically refers to combining primitive conditions (EQ, NE, CS, CC, MI, PL, VS, VC) into **derived** conditions (HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE) using Boolean operators (AND, OR) over the CPSR flags.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The ARM instruction encoding reserves the **top 4 bits [31:28]** of every instruction word for the **condition field (`cond`)**, giving 16 possible condition codes. This is the single most distinctive feature of the ARM ISA (Instruction Set Architecture).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at the Club Door"

Imagine each ARM instruction as a **person trying to enter a club**. At the door stands a **bouncer** (the **condition checker**) who looks at the **club's current mood flags** (the **CPSR flags**):

- **`N` (Negative flag)** — *Was the last guest sad or happy?* (1 = sad/negative, 0 = happy/positive)
- **`Z` (Zero flag)** — *Did the last guest leave a tip of exactly zero?* (1 = yes, 0 = no)
- **`C` (Carry flag)** — *Did the last transaction require a "carry-over" of money?* (1 = yes)
- **`V` (Overflow flag)** — *Did the last count overflow the safe?* (1 = yes)

A **compound conditional** is like the bouncer's **checklist of moods**:
- "Let him in **only if** the last guest was **not sad AND tipped more than zero**" → this is the **`GT` (Greater Than, signed)** condition.

For **compound** checks, the bouncer applies multiple rules **simultaneously** (Boolean AND/OR). If the rule passes → the instruction enters (executes). If not → it is silently turned away (becomes a NOP).

## 1.3 The CPSR Condition Flags (Standard ARM Metric)

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** contains four condition flags that act as the "state" for all conditional logic:

| Flag | Full Name | Set When... | Cleared When... |
|:----:|:----------|:------------|:----------------|
| **N** | **Negative** | Result of operation is negative (MSB = 1, in two's complement) | Result is positive or zero |
| **Z** | **Zero** | Result of operation is exactly zero | Result is non-zero |
| **C** | **Carry** | Unsigned overflow / no borrow occurs in subtraction | Unsigned underflow / borrow occurs |
| **V** | **Overflow** | Signed overflow occurs (result does not fit in N bits) | No signed overflow occurs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Suffix `'S'` in ARM Mnemonics:** To update the CPSR flags, append **`S`** to a data-processing instruction (e.g., `ADDS r0, r1, r2`). Compare instructions (`CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`) **always** update flags by default, and discard their result.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 16 ARM Condition Codes (The Complete Map)

ARM provides **2⁴ = 16** possible condition codes, but only **15** are practically used (`NV` — Never — is reserved / behaves as unconditional in some ARM versions).

### 2.1.1 Primitive (Flag-Check) Conditions

These check **a single flag** in the CPSR:

| Mnemonic | Name | Encoding (`cond`) | Flag Condition | Boolean Form |
|:--------:|:----:|:-----------------:|:---------------|:-------------|
| `EQ` | Equal | `0000` | **Z = 1** | $Z$ |
| `NE` | Not Equal | `0001` | **Z = 0** | $\overline{Z}$ |
| `CS` / `HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned Higher or Same | `0010` | **C = 1** | $C$ |
| `CC` / `LO` | Carry Clear / Unsigned Lower | `0011` | **C = 0** | $\overline{C}$ |
| `MI` | Minus (Negative) | `0100` | **N = 1** | $N$ |
| `PL` | Plus (Positive or Zero) | `0101` | **N = 0** | $\overline{N}$ |
| `VS` | Overflow Set | `0110` | **V = 1** | $V$ |
| `VC` | Overflow Clear | `0111` | **V = 0** | $\overline{V}$ |
| `AL` | Always (unconditional) | `1110` | **always true** | $1$ |

### 2.1.2 Compound (Derived) Conditions

These are **Boolean combinations** of two or more flags — hence the term "Compound Conditionals":

| Mnemonic | Name | Encoding | Flag Condition | Boolean Form | Signed/Unsigned |
|:--------:|:----:|:--------:|:---------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| `HI` | **H**igher (unsigned) | `1000` | **C = 1 AND Z = 0** | $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ | **Unsigned** |
| `LS` | **L**ower or **S**ame (unsigned) | `1001` | **C = 0 OR Z = 1** | $\overline{C} + Z$ | **Unsigned** |
| `GE` | **G**reater than or **E**qual (signed) | `1010` | **N = V** | $N \oplus V \oplus 1$ | **Signed** |
| `LT` | **L**ess **T**han (signed) | `1011` | **N ≠ V** | $N \oplus V$ | **Signed** |
| `GT` | **G**reater **T**han (signed) | `1100` | **Z = 0 AND N = V** | $\overline{Z} \cdot (N \oplus V \oplus 1)$ | **Signed** |
| `LE` | **L**ess than or **E**qual (signed) | `1101` | **Z = 1 OR N ≠ V** | $Z + (N \oplus V)$ | **Signed** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why encode Boolean logic in hardware?** ARM does not need extra comparator circuits for compound checks like `x > y` (signed). The `CMP` instruction pre-computes `x − y` and sets the four flags. Then the hardware literally performs the **single-instruction-cycle Boolean test** of the condition code, deciding whether to abort the next instruction in the pipeline. This is the **heart of compound conditionals** in ARM.

## 2.2 How Compound Conditionals Are Set — The Compare Family

The **flag-setting instructions** are what *generate* the state for compound conditionals:

$$ \text{CMP} \;\; R_a, R_b \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_a - R_b \;\; (\text{result discarded, flags updated}) $$

$$ \text{CMN} \;\; R_a, R_b \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_a + R_b \;\; (\text{result discarded, flags updated}) $$

$$ \text{TST} \;\; R_a, R_b \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_a \;\text{AND}\; R_b \;\; (\text{result discarded, flags updated}) $$

$$ \text{TEQ} \;\; R_a, R_b \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_a \;\text{EOR}\; R_b \;\; (\text{result discarded, flags updated}) $$

## 2.3 The Condition Code Boolean — Why `GT` = `LE` Negated

For any signed comparison of two's-complement numbers $a$ and $b$ after a `CMP a, b`:

$$ \text{Result} = a - b $$

**Signed Greater Than** means $a > b$ algebraically, which requires:

$$ a - b > 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{no zero result} \;\wedge\; \text{sign matches the overflow flag} $$

In Boolean form: $\text{GT} \Leftrightarrow (Z = 0) \wedge (N = V)$. This is exactly the table entry above. The de Morgan expansion gives `LE = NOT GT`, so the hardware reuses the same comparator.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 2.A — Decision Boundaries for Compound Conditionals

| Operator | Mnemonic | Boolean Expression | Encoding (hex) |
|:--------:|:--------:|:------------------|:--------------:|
| `==` | `EQ` | $Z$ | `0x0` |
| `!=` | `NE` | $\overline{Z}$ | `0x1` |
| `>` (unsigned) | `HI` | $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ | `0x8` |
| `>=` (unsigned) | `CS` | $C$ | `0x2` |
| `<` (unsigned) | `CC` | $\overline{C}$ | `0x3` |
| `<=` (unsigned) | `LS` | $\overline{C} + Z$ | `0x9` |
| `>` (signed) | `GT` | $\overline{Z} \cdot (N = V)$ | `0xC` |
| `>=` (signed) | `GE` | $N = V$ | `0xA` |
| `<` (signed) | `LT` | $N \neq V$ | `0xB` |
| `<=` (signed) | `LE` | $Z + (N \neq V)$ | `0xD` |
| — | `AL` | $1$ | `0xE` |

### Table 2.B — Flag-Setting Instructions for Compound Logic

| Instruction | Operation | Sets Flags? | Discards Rd? | Boolean Probe |
|:-----------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:--------------|
| `CMP Ra, Rb` | $R_a - R_b$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | Equality, magnitude |
| `CMN Ra, Rb` | $R_a + R_b$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | Equality to negative |
| `TST Ra, Rb` | $R_a \;\&\; R_b$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | Bit-mask tests |
| `TEQ Ra, Rb` | $R_a \oplus R_b$ | **Yes** | **Yes** | Bit-equality tests |
| `ADDS Rd, Ra, Rb` | $R_d = R_a + R_b$ | **Yes** | No | General ALU + flag |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bare-metal embedded systems (IoT, automotive ECUs):** Compound conditionals allow **branch-free** control flow, reducing pipeline flushes → lower power, deterministic latency (critical for ISO 26262 functional safety).
- **DSP and signal processing:** Multi-condition filtering (`if (x > threshold && y < max)`) maps to a single `CMP` + conditional instruction.
- **Compiler code generation:** Modern ARM compilers (GCC, ARMCC, LLVM) translate high-level `if/else` and `while` loops into compound conditional sequences to maximize branch prediction efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the `GT` Boolean Condition from First Principles

**Problem:** Prove that, after a `CMP a, b` instruction on two 32-bit signed two's-complement numbers $a$ and $b$, the **Signed Greater Than (`GT`)** condition is exactly equivalent to $(Z = 0) \wedge (N = V)$.

### Step 1 — Define the difference

The `CMP` instruction computes $a - b$ and updates the CPSR flags based on this result.

$$ d = a - b $$

### Step 2 — Express the result bits in terms of $d$

The **Z flag** is set if and only if every bit of $d$ is zero:

$$ Z = 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; d = 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; a = b $$

### Step 3 — The Negative flag $N$

In two's complement, the **MSB (bit 31)** of $d$ is the sign bit. Therefore:

$$ N = 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; d_{31} = 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; d < 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; a < b $$

### Step 4 — The Overflow flag $V$

Signed overflow occurs when the true mathematical result of $a - b$ cannot fit in 32 bits (i.e., it is outside the range $[-2^{31}, \; 2^{31} - 1]$). Equivalently, $V = 1$ iff the sign of $d$ **differs from the sign that arithmetic alone would predict** — which happens iff $a$ and $b$ have different signs.

In Boolean form, the overflow detector for a 32-bit subtraction is:

$$ V = (a_{31} \;\text{XOR}\; b_{31}) \cdot \overline{((a_{31} \;\text{XNOR}\; b_{31}) \cdot d_{31} \;\text{XOR}\; b_{31} \;\text{XOR}\; d_{31})} $$

But for our derivation we only need the **logical** relationship: $V = 1$ iff the apparent sign of $d$ is wrong.

### Step 5 — Determine "True Signed Greater Than"

For $a > b$ (strictly), we require the **true mathematical** sign of $a - b$ to be positive. The **apparent** sign is the MSB of $d$ (the $N$ flag). The **apparent sign is correct** iff $V = 0$.

So, the "true" sign is positive iff:

$$ (N = 0) \;\text{XOR}\; (V = 1) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; N = V $$

Combined with the requirement $d \neq 0$ (i.e., $Z = 0$):

$$ \text{GT} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (Z = 0) \;\wedge\; (N = V) \qquad \blacksquare $$

### Step 6 — Cross-verify with `LE` (the logical negation)

The `LE` (Less Than or Equal) condition is the **logical NOT** of `GT`:

$$ \text{LE} = \overline{\text{GT}} = \overline{(Z = 0) \wedge (N = V)} $$

By **de Morgan's law**:

$$ \text{LE} = (Z = 1) \;\vee\; (N \neq V) $$

This matches the encoding table entry: $Z + (N \oplus V)$. Confirmed.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tracing a Compound Conditional

**High-level code:**

```c
if (x > 100 && y < 50) {
    z = z + 1;
}
```

**ARM assembly translation (compound conditional in action):**

```asm
    CMP   r0, #100         ; r0 = x  →  sets flags for x - 100
    BLE   skip              ; Branch if x <= 100  (LE = NOT GT)
    CMP   r1, #50          ; r1 = y  →  sets flags for y - 50
    BGE   skip              ; Branch if y >= 50   (GE = NOT LT)
    ADD   r2, r2, #1        ; Compound true → execute body
skip:
```

### Step-by-step trace

1. **`CMP r0, #100`** — Subtracts $100$ from $x$. CPSR now holds the four flags describing $x - 100$.
2. **`BLE skip`** — The hardware tests the `LE` condition: $(Z = 1) \vee (N \neq V)$. If true, $x \leq 100$, and the branch is taken. **No further compound logic needed for the first predicate.**
3. **`CMP r1, #50`** — Subtracts $50$ from $y$. CPSR now describes $y - 50$.
4. **`BGE skip`** — Tests `GE` condition: $N = V$. If true, $y \geq 50$, branch to skip. The **logical AND** of the two predicates is implemented via two short-circuited branches.
5. **`ADD r2, r2, #1`** — Reached only if **both** conditions passed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compound Conditional Decoding

For each of the 16 condition codes, the table below shows the **Boolean test** the ARM hardware performs on the CPSR before deciding to execute the instruction:

| `cond` | Mnemonic | CPSR Test | C-equivalent | Pseudo-code Evaluation |
|:------:|:--------:|:----------|:-------------|:-----------------------|
| `0000` | `EQ`  | $Z$ | `==` | `if (Z == 1) exec;` |
| `0001` | `NE`  | $\overline{Z}$ | `!=` | `if (Z == 0) exec;` |
| `0010` | `CS`  | $C$ | `>=u` | `if (C == 1) exec;` |
| `0011` | `CC`  | $\overline{C}$ | `<u` | `if (C == 0) exec;` |
| `0100` | `MI`  | $N$ | `<0` | `if (N == 1) exec;` |
| `0101` | `PL`  | $\overline{N}$ | `>=0` | `if (N == 0) exec;` |
| `0110` | `VS`  | $V$ | `overflow` | `if (V == 1) exec;` |
| `0111` | `VC`  | $\overline{V}$ | `no-overflow` | `if (V == 0) exec;` |
| `1000` | `HI`  | $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ | `>u` | `if (C==1 && Z==0) exec;` |
| `1001` | `LS`  | $\overline{C} + Z$ | `<=u` | `if (C==0 || Z==1) exec;` |
| `1010` | `GE`  | $N = V$ | `>=s` | `if (N==V) exec;` |
| `1011` | `LT`  | $N \neq V$ | `<s` | `if (N!=V) exec;` |
| `1100` | `GT`  | $\overline{Z} \cdot (N = V)$ | `>s` | `if (Z==0 && N==V) exec;` |
| `1101` | `LE`  | $Z + (N \neq V)$ | `<=s` | `if (Z==1 || N!=V) exec;` |
| `1110` | `AL`  | $1$ | always | unconditional |
| `1111` | `NV`  | $0$ | never | reserved (architecturally unpredictable) |

## 3.4 Compound Conditionals via the IT Block (Thumb-2)

In **Thumb-2** (ARM Cortex-M), to make **multiple consecutive instructions** conditional in a compact way, the **`IT` (If-Then)** block is used. The IT instruction encodes up to **4 conditional instructions** in one 16-bit instruction.

**Syntax:**

$$ \text{IT}\{x\{y\{z\}\}\} \;\; \langle \text{first cond} \rangle $$

where each of $x, y, z$ is either **`T`** (Then) or **`E`** (Else), and the body contains 1 to 4 instructions matching.

**Example — compound logic in a single IT block:**

```asm
    CMP   r0, #10
    ITE   GT          ; If-Then-Else: r0 > 10?
    SUBGT r1, r1, #1  ; Then    : r1 = r1 - 1
    ADDLT r1, r1, #2  ; Else    : r1 = r1 + 2
```

The `ITE GT` expands as follows: the next instruction is executed *only if GT*; the following instruction is executed *only if NOT GT* (i.e., `LE`). The two predicates are **logical complements** — a fundamental property of compound conditional blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In ARM state (32-bit ARM instructions), the condition suffix is appended directly (e.g., `ADDEQ`). In Thumb/Thumb-2 state, the **`IT`** block is mandatory because the underlying Thumb instructions do not have a 4-bit condition field.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — How the ARM Condition Checker Decodes a Compound Conditional

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[32-bit Instruction Word Fetched] --> B[Decode bits 31 to 28 as COND]
    B --> C{COND field}
    C -->|1110 AL| D[Execute Unconditionally]
    C -->|Other Code| E[Read CPSR Flags N Z C V]
    E --> F[Apply Boolean Test for that COND]
    F --> G{Test Result}
    G -->|TRUE| H[Instruction Proceeds Through Pipeline]
    G -->|FALSE| I[Instruction Converted to NOP]
    H --> J[Writeback Stage]
    I --> K[Pipeline Advances with No Side Effects]
    J --> L[Next Instruction Fetch]
    K --> L
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Decomposition of a Compound `GT` Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CMP a, b executed] --> B[32-bit Subtractor Computes a minus b]
    B --> C[Result discarded by hardware]
    B --> D[Flags updated in CPSR]
    D --> E[N flag equals MSB of result]
    D --> F[Z flag equals NOR of all result bits]
    D --> G[C flag computed from borrow chain]
    D --> H[V flag computed from signed overflow logic]
    E --> I{Compound Test GT}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Z equals 0 AND N equals V}
    J -->|TRUE| K[Execute Next Instruction]
    J -->|FALSE| L[Skip / NOP Next Instruction]
```

## 4.3 State Machine for the IT Block (Thumb-2 Compound Conditionals)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> IfState: IT x cond instruction
    IfState --> ThenBlock: First body instruction If TRUE
    IfState --> ElseBlock: Else branch triggered
    ThenBlock --> ThenCont: x equals T
    ThenBlock --> End: x equals E then block done
    ElseBlock --> ElseCont: y equals E
    ElseBlock --> End: y equals T then block done
    ThenCont --> ThenCont2: z equals T
    ThenCont --> End: z equals E
    ThenCont2 --> End
    ElseCont --> End
    End --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Compound Conditional Pipeline Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Memory] --> B[Fetch Stage F]
    B --> C[Decode Stage D]
    C --> D{COND Decoder}
    D -->|Pass| E[Execute Stage E]
    D -->|Fail| F[NOP Injector]
    F --> G[Pipeline Stall No Writeback]
    E --> H[Memory Stage M]
    H --> I[Writeback Stage W]
    I --> J[Register File + CPSR Update]
    J --> A
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **COND Decoder** sits between the Decode and Execute stages. For **compound** conditions (e.g., `GT`), this decoder contains a small **Boolean logic gate network** that AND's and OR's the four CPSR flag bits exactly as the encoding table specifies. The output is a single 1-bit enable signal: `1` = execute, `0` = squash to NOP.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Explain the four condition flags in the ARM CPSR. How are they set by the `CMP` instruction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per concept):**

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** of the ARM processor contains four condition flags that record the outcome of the most recent arithmetic or logical operation:

1. **N (Negative) flag** — Set to 1 when the result of an operation has its **Most Significant Bit (MSB) = 1**, indicating a negative value in two's-complement signed representation; cleared to 0 otherwise.

2. **Z (Zero) flag** — Set to 1 when the result of an operation is **exactly zero** (all 32 bits are 0); cleared to 0 when the result is non-zero.

3. **C (Carry) flag** — Set to 1 when an unsigned operation produces a **carry-out** (e.g., unsigned addition overflow) or when a subtraction produces **no borrow**; cleared to 0 in the opposite cases.

4. **V (Overflow) flag** — Set to 1 when a **signed** operation produces a result that **cannot fit in 32 bits** (i.e., the sign of the result is incorrect due to overflow); cleared to 0 otherwise.

The **`CMP Ra, Rb`** instruction performs the internal subtraction $R_a - R_b$, **discards the result** (does not write to any register), and **updates all four CPSR flags** based on this subtraction. This makes it the primary flag-setting instruction for compound conditional logic.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between the `HI` and `GT` condition codes. When would you use each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `HI` (Higher, unsigned) | `GT` (Greater Than, signed) |
|:-------|:-----------------------|:----------------------------|
| Boolean | $C = 1 \;\text{AND}\; Z = 0$ | $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$ |
| Interpretation | $a > b$ in **unsigned** comparison | $a > b$ in **signed (two's-complement)** comparison |
| Example use | `CMP r0, r1` then `BHI label` — branch if `r0 > r1` treating them as unsigned 32-bit values | `CMP r0, r1` then `BGT label` — branch if `r0 > r1` treating them as signed 32-bit values |
| Typical domain | Memory addresses, character codes, bit-masks, array indices | Temperatures, financial balances, deltas, any signed quantity |

**Use `HI`** when comparing **non-negative quantities** like pointer addresses, ASCII values, or hardware register contents. **Use `GT`** whenever the registers may hold **negative values** in two's complement (e.g., sensor readings, signed counters, error codes).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat diagram how ARM's conditional execution mechanism works. Show the role of the 4-bit condition field in the instruction encoding.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write an ARM assembly program to find the **largest of three signed numbers** stored in `r0`, `r1`, `r2`, and place the result in `r3`. Use only compound conditional instructions (no separate compare-then-branch sequences).

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**The 4-bit Condition Field — Bits [31:28] of every ARM instruction:**

| Bits [31:28] | Mnemonic | Meaning |
|:------------:|:--------:|:--------|
| `0000` | `EQ` | Equal |
| `0001` | `NE` | Not Equal |
| ... | ... | ... |
| `1110` | `AL` | Always |
| `1111` | (reserved) | Unpredictable |

**Working Mechanism (5 key points, ~1.4 marks each):**

1. **Encoding:** The top 4 bits of every 32-bit ARM instruction word are reserved for the condition code. For example, `ADD` becomes `ADDEQ`, `ADDNEs`, `ADDGT`, etc. In assembly, the suffix is appended to the mnemonic.

2. **CPSR flags as state:** Before the instruction executes, the CPSR holds the four flags **N, Z, C, V** set by a prior operation (e.g., `CMP`, arithmetic with `S` suffix).

3. **Hardware Boolean test:** During the **Decode stage** of the pipeline, the **Condition Unit** examines the 4-bit `cond` field and performs a Boolean test on the current CPSR flags. For **compound** codes like `GT`, multiple flags are ANDed/ORed (e.g., $\overline{Z} \cdot (N = V)$).

4. **Enable signal:** The output of the Boolean test is a single 1-bit **enable** signal. If TRUE, the instruction proceeds through **Execute → Memory → Writeback** normally. If FALSE, the instruction is **squashed to a NOP** — the pipeline advances, but no register, memory, or flag is modified.

5. **Benefit:** This mechanism **eliminates many short branches**, reducing pipeline flushes and improving performance. A simple 4-instruction `if-else` block can execute **branch-free** by simply changing the condition suffixes of each instruction.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above for the canonical schematic).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Algorithm:** Compare `r0` with `r1` and `r2` using signed `CMP`, then use compound conditional `MOVGT` to conditionally update `r3`.

**Assembly code:**

```asm
    MOV   r3, r0           ; Assume r0 is largest initially
    CMP   r1, r3           ; Compare r1 with current max (r3)
    MOVGT r3, r1           ; If r1 > r3 (signed), update r3 = r1
    CMP   r2, r3           ; Compare r2 with current max (r3)
    MOVGT r3, r2           ; If r2 > r3 (signed), update r3 = r2
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

1. `MOV r3, r0` — Initialize `r3` with `r0`.
2. `CMP r1, r3` — Subtracts `r3` from `r1`, updates flags.
3. `MOVGT r3, r1` — Compound conditional: if `r1 > r3` (signed GT), `r3 ← r1`. Otherwise this instruction becomes a NOP.
4. `CMP r2, r3` — Subtracts `r3` from `r2`, updates flags.
5. `MOVGT r3, r2` — If `r2 > r3` (signed), `r3 ← r2`.

**Why compound conditional is used here (1 mark for explanation):** Using `MOVGT` instead of `CMP` + `BGT` + `MOV` saves 2 instructions (no branch, no branch delay slot, no separate compare-conditional). The single compound `GT` suffix embeds the entire Boolean test.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Set]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a Boolean truth table, derive the condition code for **Signed Less Than (`LT`)** and show that it is the logical negation of **Signed Greater Than or Equal (`GE`)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write an ARM assembly program fragment that implements the following C code **using compound conditionals only** (no `B` branches):

```c
if ((a == b) && (c != d))
    e = e + 5;
else
    e = e - 3;
```

Assume `a` is in `r0`, `b` in `r1`, `c` in `r2`, `d` in `r3`, `e` in `r4`.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Derivation of `LT`:**

For `CMP a, b` (i.e., $a - b$), the `LT` (Signed Less Than) condition must be true when $a < b$ algebraically.

From the derivation in Section 3.1, the result $d = a - b$ is negative iff the true sign of $d$ is negative. The apparent sign is `N`; the apparent sign is wrong iff `V = 1`. So the true sign is negative iff $N \neq V$.

But for **strict** less-than, we must also rule out equality (which would be $Z = 1$). However, if $a = b$, then $d = 0$, hence $N = 0$ and $V = 0$, so $N \neq V$ is **automatically false** in the equality case. Therefore the `LT` condition does **not** need an explicit $Z$ check:

$$ \text{LT} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; N \neq V \;\Longleftrightarrow\; N \oplus V $$

**Derivation of `GE`:**

Signed Greater Than or Equal means $a \geq b$, which is the logical NOT of `LT`:

$$ \text{GE} = \overline{\text{LT}} = \overline{N \oplus V} = N \;\text{XNOR}\; V \;\Longleftrightarrow\; N = V $$

**Truth Table for the four key signed conditions:**

| `N` | `V` | `Z` | `N == V` (GE) | `N != V` (LT) | `Z == 0 AND N == V` (GT) | `Z == 1 OR N != V` (LE) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:------------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | **1 (GE)** | 0 | **1 (GT)** | 0 |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **1 (GE)** | 0 | 0 | **1 (LE)** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **1 (LT)** | 0 | **1 (LE)** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **1 (LT)** | 0 | **1 (LE)** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1 (LT)** | 0 | **1 (LE)** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **1 (LT)** | 0 | **1 (LE)** |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | **1 (GE)** | 0 | **1 (GT)** | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **1 (GE)** | 0 | 0 | **1 (LE)** |

**Verification:** Inspecting the table column-by-column, `GE` is the exact bitwise complement of `LT` in every row. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Strategy:** Use the `IT` block (Thumb-2) or branch-free compound conditionals (ARM state) to implement the `if-else` logic.

**Method 1 — Thumb-2 with IT block (preferred for compactness):**

```asm
    CMP   r0, r1           ; a - b, sets flags
    ITE   EQ               ; If a == b, Then <next>, Else <after next>
    CMPEQ r2, r3           ; If branch: compare c and d (only if a==b)
    CMPNE r2, r3           ; Else branch: compare c and d (only if a!=b)
    ; Note: above is illustrative; cleanest form is:
    CMP   r0, r1           ; a - b
    BNE   else_branch      ; if a != b, go to else
    CMP   r2, r3           ; c - d
    BEQ   else_branch      ; if c == d, go to else (compound: a==b && c!=d fails)
    ADD   r4, r4, #5       ; if block: e = e + 5
    B     end
else_branch:
    SUB   r4, r4, #3       ; else block: e = e - 3
end:
```

**Method 2 — Pure compound conditionals (no branches):**

A **fully branch-free** version can be constructed using `IT` blocks. Below is the canonical Thumb-2 IT-block form for the same logic:

```asm
    CMP   r0, r1           ; flags = a - b
    CMPNE r2, r3           ; useless, but illustrates CMPEQ/CMPNE
    ; The actual branch-free translation uses the ITT/ITE blocks
```

**Cleanest branch-free Thumb-2 solution:**

```asm
    CMP   r0, r1
    ITE   EQ
    CMPEQ r2, r3           ; Execute only if a==b; sets flags for c-d
    CMPNE r2, r3           ; Execute only if a!=b; sets flags for c-d
    ITE   NE
    ADDNE r4, r4, #5       ; Execute if a==b AND c!=d
    SUBEQ r4, r4, #3       ; Execute otherwise (else branch)
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

- The first `CMP r0, r1` tests the predicate `a == b`. The flags are then used by the `ITE EQ` block.
- Inside the first IT block, `CMPEQ` runs **only if** `a == b` (computing `c - d`); `CMPNE` runs **only if** `a != b`.
- The flags after the first IT block are the `c - d` comparison, but **only valid** in the branch where the predicate matched.
- The second `ITE NE` block conditionally executes `ADDNE` (the `if` body) or `SUBEQ` (the `else` body).
- The compound conditional logic `(a == b) && (c != d)` is fully expressed in **two IT blocks** with **zero branches**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `S` suffix:** Students often write `ADD r4, r4, #5` and expect flags to be updated. Only `ADDS` (or compare-class instructions) update CPSR. Marks lost: **1 mark per error**.
> 2. **Mixing signed and unsigned:** Using `HI` for signed data (or `GT` for unsigned) gives **incorrect results** because the Boolean tests differ. Examiner strictly checks this.
> 3. **IT block syntax errors:** In Thumb-2, the IT condition must match the **first body's** condition. Writing `IT GT` followed by `ADDNE` is **illegal** and will trigger an assembler error.
> 4. **Forgetting that `CMP` discards the result:** Students sometimes write `CMP r3, r0, r1` thinking the difference is stored. It is not — only flags are written.
> 5. **Not citing the Boolean form:** KTU 2024 expects the Boolean expression for each compound condition (e.g., `GT = Z=0 AND N=V`). Omitting this loses **2 marks** in Part (a) of any 7-mark question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Compound Conditional** = a Boolean combination of CPSR flags that decides whether an ARM instruction executes or becomes a NOP.
- The **condition field** is the **top 4 bits [31:28]** of every ARM instruction → 16 possible codes.
- **CPSR flags** are **N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow)** — the "state" of the processor.
- **Primitive conditions** (`EQ`, `NE`, `CS`, `CC`, `MI`, `PL`, `VS`, `VC`) check a **single flag**.
- **Compound (derived) conditions** combine flags:
  - `HI` = $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ (unsigned `>`)
  - `LS` = $\overline{C} + Z$ (unsigned `<=`)
  - `GE` = $N = V$ (signed `>=`)
  - `LT` = $N \neq V$ (signed `<`)
  - `GT` = $\overline{Z} \cdot (N = V)$ (signed `>`)
  - `LE` = $Z + (N \neq V)$ (signed `<=`)
- `CMP Ra, Rb` is the workhorse instruction — it computes $R_a - R_b$, **discards the result**, and **updates all four flags**.
- In **ARM state**, append the condition suffix directly: `ADDEQ r0, r1, r2` means "add only if EQ".
- In **Thumb/Thumb-2 state**, use the **`IT` (If-Then)** block to make multiple consecutive instructions conditional.
- **Compound logic** in C (`&&`, `||`) is compiled into a **sequence** of `CMP` + conditional-branch instructions, exploiting the **short-circuit** evaluation.
- The `GT` and `LE` are **logical complements**; `GE` and `LT` are logical complements — use this to derive one from the other.
- **Hardware benefit:** Compound conditionals eliminate many branches → **fewer pipeline flushes**, **less power**, **deterministic timing** (critical in embedded/real-time systems).
- The **Condition Unit** in the Decode stage performs the Boolean test in **one gate delay** — no extra clock cycles are consumed by the conditional check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **ARM Assembly Language Programming**, a *loop* is a programmed sequence of instructions that is **executed repeatedly** until a specified termination condition is satisfied. Looping is achieved using the **Program Counter (PC)** manipulation capabilities of the **branch instructions** (`B`, `BX`, `BL`) in combination with the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** condition flags (`N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`).

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEECT524, Module 3), implementing loops in ARM involves the use of:
- **Conditional branch instructions** (e.g., `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`)
- **Comparison instructions** (`CMP`, `TST`, `CMN`)
- **Loop counter registers** (typically `Rn`)
- **Loop body** — the instructions that execute iteratively
- **Branch-back logic** that alters the PC to the start of the loop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In ARM, loops are NOT constructed using dedicated `LOOP` instructions (as in x86). Instead, they are **manually synthesized** using a combination of `CMP` (or `SUBS`) and a conditional `B` instruction that targets the start of the loop body.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hamster Wheel:** Imagine a hamster running on a wheel in his cage. The hamster will keep running as long as he is on the wheel. He stops only when (1) he gets too tired, or (2) he reaches a specific count of rotations. In ARM assembly, the **loop body** is the hamster running, the **condition check** is his tiredness, and the **counter** is the number of rotations.

A loop essentially boils down to **three logical blocks** that a programmer must explicitly code:
1. **Initialization** — set up the loop counter and starting state.
2. **Condition Check & Branch** — decide whether to continue or exit.
3. **Update** — modify the counter and/or data, then jump back to step 2.

### 1.3 Physical / Architectural Constants

- **ARM Register Width:** **32 bits** (for ARM7/ARM Cortex-A/R series, which are KTU-prescribed).
- **Number of General-Purpose Registers:** **13 (R0–R12)** + **3 special registers (R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC)**.
- **Pipeline Stages (Classical ARM):** **3-stage (Fetch → Decode → Execute)**.
- **Branch Penalty:** **2 cycles** for classical ARM (1 cycle for branch forwarding).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How Loops Work at the Hardware Level

The ARM processor executes instructions in **sequential flow** dictated by the **Program Counter (R15 / PC)**. A loop is created by **modifying the PC** to point back to a previously executed instruction, causing the CPU to **re-fetch and re-execute** a block of code.

The high-level logic is decomposed as follows:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** A general-purpose register (say `R0`) is loaded with the loop limit or counter value using `MOV`, `LDR`, or `LDR immediate`.
- **Step 2 — Loop Body Execution:** The actual processing instructions (e.g., `ADD`, `LDR`, `STR`, `MUL`) are executed. These may also update the loop counter (e.g., `SUBS R0, R0, #1`).
- **Step 3 — Condition Setting:** A comparison instruction (`CMP Rn, Rm` or `SUBS Rd, Rn, #1`) is executed. Crucially, the `S` suffix is used in the update instruction to **set the CPSR flags** automatically.
- **Step 4 — Conditional Branch:** A conditional branch like `BNE loop_start` is executed. If the condition is true, the PC is rewritten with the target address and the loop iterates; otherwise, execution falls through to the next instruction (loop exit).
- **Step 5 — Loop Termination:** The flow continues linearly past the branch instruction once the termination condition becomes true.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why use `SUBS` instead of `SUB`?** The `S` suffix is critical because the **status flags (N, Z, C, V) in the CPSR** must be updated so that the next conditional branch can decide whether to branch.
- **Why not use dedicated loop instructions?** ARM is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture, so complex instructions like x86's `LOOP` or `LOOPE` are intentionally excluded. The **load/store architecture** philosophy requires that the programmer explicitly construct the loop.
- **How is branching implemented?** The branch instruction adds a **signed 24-bit offset (in classic ARM)**, shifted left by 2, to the current PC value. This yields a branch range of **±32 MB**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Branch & Loop Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Operation | Sets Flags? | Used in Loops? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `B` | `B label` | Unconditional branch to `label` | No | Loop back / exit |
| `BL` | `BL label` | Branch with Link (saves return addr in LR) | No | Subroutine loops |
| `BX` | `BX Rn` | Branch to address in `Rn`; switches ARM/Thumb | No | Thumb interworking |
| `CMP` | `CMP Rn, Rm` | Compares `Rn - Rm` | **Yes (always)** | Condition check |
| `SUBS` | `SUBS Rd, Rn, #imm` | Subtract with flag update | **Yes** | Counter decrement |
| `BEQ` | `BEQ label` | Branch if **Z=1** (equal) | No | Loop exit on counter=0 |
| `BNE` | `BNE label` | Branch if **Z=0** (not equal) | No | Loop back while ≠0 |
| `BGT` | `BGT label` | Branch if greater (signed) | No | Signed comparison loops |
| `BLT` | `BLT label` | Branch if less than (signed) | No | Signed comparison loops |
| `BGE` | `BGE label` | Branch if ≥ (signed) | No | Boundary loops |
| `TST` | `TST Rn, Rm` | Bitwise AND, sets flags | **Yes (always)** | Bit-test loops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Loop constructs form the basis of **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** kernels on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, where the **MAC (Multiply-Accumulate)** operation is repeated N times to compute convolution, FFT butterflies, and FIR filter taps. In **embedded automotive ECUs** running on ARM Cortex-R, loops are used for **sensor sampling** at fixed time intervals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Counted Loop (Equivalent to `for(i=5; i>0; i--)`)

> [!NOTE]
> **Counted Loops** are the most common type in assembly. The loop executes a **predetermined number of times** based on an initialized counter.

Let us derive the assembly implementation step-by-step from the high-level C construct:

**High-Level C Construct:**
```c
int sum = 0;
for (int i = 5; i > 0; i--) {
    sum = sum + i;
}
```

**Step-by-Step ARM Assembly Translation:**

**Step 1 — Map variables to registers.** From KTU convention, we use:
- `R4` → counter `i`
- `R5` → accumulator `sum`

**Step 2 — Initialize registers.** Load `i = 5` and `sum = 0`.

```armasm
        MOV     R4, #5          ; R4 (i) = 5
        MOV     R5, #0          ; R5 (sum) = 0
```

**Step 3 — Define the loop label** (loop start address).

```armasm
LOOP
```

**Step 4 — Loop body.** Add the counter value to the sum.

```armasm
        ADD     R5, R5, R4      ; sum = sum + i
```

**Step 5 — Decrement counter AND set flags** (the `S` suffix is critical here).

```armasm
        SUBS    R4, R4, #1      ; i = i - 1, sets Z flag if i becomes 0
```

**Step 6 — Conditional branch back to LOOP** if not equal (i.e., Z=0, i.e., counter not yet zero).

```armasm
        BNE     LOOP            ; if Z=0 (i != 0), branch back to LOOP
```

**Step 7 — Loop exit (fall-through).** When `Z=1` (i.e., `i == 0`), the `BNE` is skipped, and execution continues linearly.

```armasm
        ; sum now holds 1+2+3+4+5 = 15
```

### 3.2 Complete ARM Assembly Program — Counted Loop

Below is a fully runnable ARM assembly snippet for the Keil µVision / ARMulator environment commonly used in KTU labs:

```armasm
        AREA    Loops, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        MOV     R4, #5          ; Initialize counter i = 5
        MOV     R5, #0          ; Initialize sum = 0
        MOV     R6, #0          ; R6 = 0 (used for BNE; alternatively can use immediate 0)

LOOP
        ADD     R5, R5, R4      ; sum = sum + i
        SUBS    R4, R4, #1      ; i = i - 1; update flags
        BNE     LOOP            ; if i != 0, repeat

STOP    B       STOP            ; Infinite loop to halt the program

        END
```

**Line-by-Line Logical Explanation:**

- `MOV R4, #5` → Loads the decimal constant 5 into R4 (immediate value).
- `MOV R5, #0` → Clears the accumulator.
- `LOOP` → A symbolic label that marks the address of `ADD`.
- `ADD R5, R5, R4` → Adds current counter value to sum.
- `SUBS R4, R4, #1` → Decrements counter. The `S` suffix writes the result AND updates the **N, Z, C, V flags** in CPSR. The Z flag is set to 1 when R4 reaches 0.
- `BNE LOOP` → If Z=0, branch back; if Z=1, fall through.
- `B STOP` → Halts the program in an infinite loop.

### 3.3 Mathematical Verification

Let us trace the loop execution register by register to **prove correctness** — this is the KTU examiner's gold-standard method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad & R4 = 5, \quad R5 = 0 + 5 = 5, \quad R4 \rightarrow 4 \\
\text{Iteration 2:} \quad & R4 = 4, \quad R5 = 5 + 4 = 9, \quad R4 \rightarrow 3 \\
\text{Iteration 3:} \quad & R4 = 3, \quad R5 = 9 + 3 = 12, \quad R4 \rightarrow 2 \\
\text{Iteration 4:} \quad & R4 = 2, \quad R5 = 12 + 2 = 14, \quad R4 \rightarrow 1 \\
\text{Iteration 5:} \quad & R4 = 1, \quad R5 = 14 + 1 = 15, \quad R4 \rightarrow 0 \\
\text{Loop Exit:} \quad & Z = 1, \quad BNE \text{ not taken}, \quad R5 = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the mathematical formula:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{5} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

### 3.4 Derived Example — Array Sum Loop (Indexed Loop)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indexed loops** use a **pointer/index** to traverse a memory block. This is the most important pattern in **DSP** and **embedded systems**.

**Task:** Sum the elements of a 5-element integer array stored at memory label `Array`.

```armasm
        AREA    ArraySum, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R7, =Array      ; R7 = address of Array
        MOV     R8, #0          ; R8 = index (i = 0)
        MOV     R9, #5          ; R9 = loop count
        MOV     R10, #0         ; R10 = sum = 0

LOOP
        LDR     R11, [R7, R8, LSL #2]  ; R11 = Array[i] (word-aligned, 4 bytes)
        ADD     R10, R10, R11           ; sum = sum + Array[i]
        ADD     R8, R8, #1              ; i = i + 1
        CMP     R8, R9                  ; Compare i with count
        BNE     LOOP                    ; if i != 5, repeat

STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    Data, DATA, READWRITE
Array   DCD     10, 20, 30, 40, 50      ; 5 integers

        END
```

**Detailed Step-by-Step Explanation:**

- `LDR R7, =Array` → Loads the literal pool address of `Array` into R7. ARM uses a **literal pool** for 32-bit constants.
- `LDR R11, [R7, R8, LSL #2]` → **Pre-indexed addressing**: Reads the word at address `R7 + (R8 × 4)`. The `LSL #2` is a **barrel shifter** operation to multiply index by 4 (word size). This is equivalent to `Array[i]` in C.
- `ADD R8, R8, #1` → Increments the index.
- `CMP R8, R9` → Compares index with loop bound; sets Z flag if equal.
- `BNE LOOP` → Loops back if more elements remain.

**Memory Trace:**

| Iteration | R8 (i) | R11 (Array[i]) | R10 (sum) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 10 | 10 |
| 2 | 1 | 20 | 30 |
| 3 | 2 | 30 | 60 |
| 4 | 3 | 40 | 100 |
| 5 | 4 | 50 | **150** |

### 3.5 Derived Example — While Loop (Sentinel-Controlled Loop)

A `while` loop continues as long as a **condition is TRUE**, with no predetermined count.

**C Equivalent:**
```c
while (R0 != 0) {
    R0 = R0 - 1;
}
```

**ARM Assembly:**

```armasm
        MOV     R0, #10         ; Load initial value

WHILE
        CMP     R0, #0          ; Compare R0 with 0
        BEQ     END_WHILE       ; If R0 == 0, exit loop
        SUBS    R0, R0, #1      ; R0 = R0 - 1 (sets flags)
        B       WHILE           ; Unconditional branch back

END_WHILE
        ; R0 = 0 here
```

**Explanation:** The check is performed **at the top of the loop** (entry-controlled loop). If the initial value of R0 were 0, the loop would not execute even once.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Counted Loop Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start"] --> B["Initialize Counter Rn = N"]
    B --> C["Initialize Accumulator / Variables"]
    C --> D["LOOP: Execute Loop Body"]
    D --> E["Update Counter SUBS Rn, Rn, 1"]
    E --> F{"Condition Check: Z flag?"}
    F -->|"Z=0, continue"| D
    F -->|"Z=1, exit"| G["Loop Exit: Fall Through"]
    G --> H["End / Next Instruction"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — ARM Loop Architecture Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Instruction Memory: LDR R11, R7 R8 LSL 2"] --> B["ARM Decode Stage"]
    B --> C["Register Bank R0 to R12"]
    C --> D["ALU: ADD / SUB / CMP"]
    D --> E["CPSR Flags: N Z C V"]
    E --> F{"Branch Unit: Evaluate Condition"}
    F -->|"Condition TRUE"| G["PC updated: Branch to LOOP label"]
    F -->|"Condition FALSE"| H["PC incremented: Sequential flow"]
    G --> A
    H --> I["Loop Exit / Next Block"]
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Loop Iteration Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Program Counter R15
    participant MEM as Instruction Memory
    participant ALU as ALU + Barrel Shifter
    participant CPSR as CPSR Flags

    PC->>MEM: Fetch ADD R5, R5, R4
    MEM->>ALU: Execute ADD
    PC->>MEM: Fetch SUBS R4, R4, 1
    MEM->>ALU: Execute SUB
    ALU->>CPSR: Update Z=0 if R4 != 0
    PC->>MEM: Fetch BNE LOOP
    MEM->>PC: Evaluate Z flag, branch if Z=0
    Note over PC,MEM: PC now points to LOOP label
    PC->>MEM: Re-fetch loop body
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Branch Instruction to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Loop Control Decision"] --> Q1{"Is count known?"}
    Q1 -->|"YES, use Counted Loop"| Counted["SUBS counter, counter, 1 + BNE label"]
    Q1 -->|"NO, use Conditional Loop"| Cond{"Signed or Unsigned?"}
    Cond -->|"Signed"| Signed["BGT / BLT / BGE / BLE"]
    Cond -->|"Unsigned"| Unsign["BHI / BLS / BHS / BLO"]
    Counted --> Body["Loop Body"]
    Signed --> Body
    Unsign --> Body
    Body --> End["Branch back via B or BNE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain how loops are implemented in ARM assembly language. Why does ARM not have a dedicated `LOOP` instruction?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** In ARM, loops are implemented using **branch instructions** (`B`, `BEQ`, `BNE`, etc.) combined with **comparison** or **flag-setting arithmetic instructions** (`CMP`, `SUBS`).
- **(1 Mark)** A typical counted loop uses a register as a counter, decrements it using `SUBS` (to set the Z flag), and then uses `BNE` to branch back to the loop start.
- **(1 Mark)** ARM is a **RISC architecture**, which avoids complex, multi-cycle instructions like `LOOP` to maintain a streamlined, pipelined, and high-performance design. The RISC philosophy requires that loops be **synthesized** by the programmer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the role of the `S` suffix in instructions like `SUBS R4, R4, #1`? What happens if the `S` is omitted?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The `S` suffix causes the instruction to **update the condition flags (N, Z, C, V)** in the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** based on the result of the operation.
- **(1 Mark)** In a loop, `SUBS` is essential because the **Z flag** must be set when the counter reaches zero, so that the subsequent `BNE` (branch if not equal) can decide whether to continue or exit the loop.
- **(1 Mark)** If `S` is omitted, the flags are NOT updated, the Z flag retains its previous (stale) value, and the branch condition becomes unpredictable, leading to **incorrect loop termination**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer either the full Question A or the full Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Write an ARM assembly program to compute the **sum of first N natural numbers** using a counted loop, where N is stored in memory location `N`. Show the complete program with directives. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Trace the execution of the program for `N = 4`, showing the register values after each iteration. Verify your result mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

```armasm
        AREA    SumN, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R0, =N          ; R0 = address of N
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 = N
        MOV     R2, #0          ; R2 = sum = 0

LOOP
        CMP     R1, #0          ; Compare counter with 0
        BEQ     END_LOOP        ; If R1 == 0, exit
        ADD     R2, R2, R1      ; sum = sum + R1
        SUB     R1, R1, #1      ; R1 = R1 - 1 (no flag update needed since CMP follows)
        B       LOOP            ; Branch back

END_LOOP
        STR     R2, [R0, #4]    ; Store result in next memory word

STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    Data, DATA, READWRITE
N       DCD     4               ; Value of N
RESULT  DCD     0               ; Space for result

        END
```

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- **1 Mark** — Correct `AREA`, `ENTRY`, `EXPORT` directives.
- **2 Marks** — Correct initialization of counter and accumulator.
- **2 Marks** — Correct loop body and decrement.
- **1 Mark** — Correct use of `CMP` + `BEQ` for exit.
- **1 Mark** — Correct storage of result and termination.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Iteration | R1 (counter) | R2 (sum) | Z flag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Before loop | 4 | 0 | — |
| 1 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| 2 | 3 | 7 | 0 |
| 3 | 2 | 9 | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | 10 | 0 |
| Check | 0 → BEQ taken | **10** | **1** |

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{4} i = \frac{4 \times 5}{2} = 10 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- **3 Marks** — Complete iteration trace table with register values.
- **2 Marks** — Identification of Z flag behavior.
- **2 Marks** — Correct mathematical formula and verification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Write an ARM assembly program to find the **largest element** in a 5-element array stored in memory. Use a loop with proper comparison and branch instructions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between a **counted loop** and a **conditional (while) loop** in ARM. Give one example of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

```armasm
        AREA    Largest, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main

__main
        LDR     R4, =Array      ; R4 = base address of Array
        MOV     R5, #5          ; R5 = count = 5
        MOV     R6, #0          ; R6 = index = 0
        LDR     R7, [R4]        ; R7 = largest = Array[0]

LOOP
        ADD     R6, R6, #1      ; i = i + 1
        CMP     R6, R5          ; Compare i with count
        BEQ     END_LOOP        ; If i == 5, exit
        LDR     R8, [R4, R6, LSL #2]  ; R8 = Array[i]
        CMP     R8, R7          ; Compare Array[i] with largest
        BLE     SKIP            ; If Array[i] <= largest, skip
        MOV     R7, R8          ; largest = Array[i]

SKIP
        B       LOOP

END_LOOP
        ; R7 holds the largest element

STOP    B       STOP

        AREA    Data, DATA, READWRITE
Array   DCD     23, 87, 14, 96, 42

        END
```

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- **2 Marks** — Correct array addressing using `[R4, R6, LSL #2]`.
- **2 Marks** — Correct comparison and conditional update.
- **2 Marks** — Correct loop termination logic.
- **1 Mark** — Initialization of `largest` with `Array[0]`.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | Counted Loop | Conditional (While) Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Termination** | Based on a **fixed counter** that decrements to a known value | Based on a **runtime condition** that may be data-dependent |
| **Iterations** | **Known in advance** | **Unknown in advance** |
| **Implementation** | `SUBS` + `BNE` (typical) | `CMP` + `BEQ`/`BNE`/`BGT` etc. at top of loop |
| **Risk** | Always executes at least once (unless checked) | May execute **zero times** if condition is initially false |
| **Example (Sum 1..N)** | `for(i=N; i>0; i--)` with `SUBS` + `BNE` | `while (R0 != 0) { R0--; }` with `CMP` + `BEQ` exit |
| **Use Case** | Array traversal, fixed-iteration DSP kernels | Polling loops, event-driven waiting, data parsing |

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- **3 Marks** — Clear difference between counted and conditional loops.
- **2 Marks** — Correct example of counted loop.
- **2 Marks** — Correct example of while loop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `S` suffix** in `SUBS` is the **#1 reason students lose 2-3 marks**. Without the S suffix, the Z flag is stale and `BNE` behaves unpredictably.
> 2. **Off-by-one errors** in array bounds: students often loop until `R8 > 5` instead of `R8 == 5`, causing array-out-of-bounds access.
> 3. **Not clearing the accumulator**: forgetting `MOV R2, #0` before the loop leads to garbage initial values.
> 4. **Confusing `LSL #2`**: Students forget that word-aligned arrays need index × 4 byte offset.
> 5. **Not declaring `ENTRY` and `EXPORT __main`**: Without these, the linker will throw an error and the program won't run.
> 6. **Using `MOV R0, #large_number`**: ARM immediate values are limited to 8-bit rotated. Use `LDR Rn, =constant` for large values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Implementing Loops in ARM**

- ✅ **Loops in ARM are synthesized**, not built-in. Use `B`, `CMP`, `SUBS`, `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, etc.
- ✅ The **`S` suffix** is **mandatory** on the update instruction to set condition flags.
- ✅ **Three-part structure** of every ARM loop: **(1) Initialization, (2) Body + Update, (3) Branch-back test**.
- ✅ **Counted loops** use a **decrementing counter** with `SUBS` + `BNE`.
- ✅ **Conditional (while) loops** use `CMP` + `BEQ` (or `BNE`) at the **top** of the loop.
- ✅ **Array indexing** uses `[Rn, Rm, LSL #2]` for **word-aligned 32-bit arrays**.
- ✅ **Literal pools** (`LDR Rn, =label`) are required to load 32-bit addresses.
- ✅ The **Z flag** is the most commonly used flag in loop termination logic.
- ✅ Always **initialize the accumulator** (`MOV Rx, #0`) before the loop body.
- ✅ Classical ARM branch range is **±32 MB** (24-bit signed offset × 4).
- ✅ The **`BL` instruction** saves the return address in `LR` (R14) and is used for **subroutine calls**, not regular loops.
- ✅ **Thumb mode** uses a shorter `B` instruction; the concept of looping remains the same, only the encoding differs.
- ✅ Common KTU exam trick: students confuse `BEQ` (branch if Z=1) with `BNE` (branch if Z=0). Memorize: **E** → **E**qual, **NE** → **N**ot **E**qual.
- ✅ Pipeline effect: a branch causes a **2-cycle penalty** in classical ARM (3-stage pipeline) because the fetched instructions after the branch must be flushed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speeding Up Array Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speeding Up Array Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Array Access Acceleration** in ARM architecture refers to the set of hardware-supported techniques and programming strategies used to minimize the average memory access latency when processing contiguous data structures (arrays, buffers, blocks). In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this concept is rooted in exploiting the principles of **spatial locality of reference**, **prefetching mechanisms**, and the ARM-specific **multi-register transfer instructions (LDM/STM)** to ensure that the processor spends the maximum possible time in the execution pipeline rather than stalling on memory fetches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Speeding up array access is not merely a software trick. It is a **hardware-software co-design** issue. The ARM core provides specific addressing modes (pre-indexed, post-indexed, with write-back) and block transfer instructions (LDM, STM) precisely so that the programmer/compiler can amortize address arithmetic over multiple data transfers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Ready):**
> *Speeding up array access is the process of reducing the effective memory access time per element of an array by exploiting spatial locality through bulk transfer instructions, address auto-incrementing, and cache-friendly traversal patterns.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **single cashier at a grocery store** (this is your ARM processor core) and a customer who wants to buy **10 items** that are scattered across the store (this is an array of 10 memory locations).

- **Slow Method (Naive Access):** The cashier walks to item 1, scans it, walks back; then walks to item 2, scans it, walks back. This is equivalent to executing 10 individual `LDR` instructions with separate address calculations — **10 round trips**.
- **Fast Method (Optimized Access):** The cashier brings a **large basket**, walks once, scans all 10 items in a row as he passes them, and brings them back together. This is what **LDM (Load Multiple)** does — **1 round trip, 10 items fetched**.

The "basket" is a set of ARM registers, and the "walking back and forth" represents the cost of address calculation plus memory bus access. The faster method saves both **time** and **energy**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Array Access

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are standard KTU Board-evaluation terms. Commit them to memory verbatim.

- **Memory Access Time ($T_{mem}$):** The time taken to fetch a single word from main memory, typically **50–100 ns** for SDRAM, **1–10 ns** for on-chip SRAM cache.
- **Cache Hit Ratio ($H$):** The fraction of memory references satisfied from cache, defined as $H = \frac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$.
- **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT):** $AMAT = T_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$
- **Speedup Factor ($S$):** $S = \frac{T_{naive}}{T_{optimized}}$ where $T$ denotes the total execution time of the array operation.
- **Spatial Locality Factor:** The propensity of the program to access memory locations that are physically close to recently accessed locations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Standard Array Access is Inefficient

A naive ARM implementation to sum an array of $N$ words typically executes the following operations per element:

1. Compute the address: `ADD R0, R0, #4` (1 cycle for address generation)
2. Load the element: `LDR R1, [R0]` (1 cycle issue + memory latency)
3. Add to accumulator: `ADD R2, R2, R1` (1 cycle)
4. Branch back: `SUBS R3, R3, #1; BNE loop` (2 cycles for branch)

For every **1 useful data word**, the processor performs roughly **3–4 supporting instructions** plus the memory fetch. This leads to severe **instruction fetch bandwidth** wastage and **pipeline stalls**.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Array Access Speedup

### Pillar 1: Block Transfer (LDM / STM)
The ARM instruction set includes the family of **Load Multiple (LDM)** and **Store Multiple (STM)** instructions. A single `LDM` can transfer between 1 and 16 registers to/from consecutive memory locations. This collapses $N$ memory transactions into a single bus request.

### Pillar 2: Auto-Indexed Addressing Modes
ARM provides four addressing modes specifically designed for sequential data structures:
- **Pre-indexed:** `LDR R1, [R0, #4]!` — offset is added, then used, and base is updated.
- **Post-indexed:** `LDR R1, [R0], #4` — base is used as-is, then updated.
- **Pre-indexed without writeback:** `LDR R1, [R0, #4]`
- **Offset (no indexing):** `LDR R1, [R0]`

### Pillar 3: Loop Unrolling
The branch instruction at the end of every loop iteration costs a pipeline flush. By unrolling the loop $k$ times, the number of branches reduces by a factor of $k$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Average Memory Access Time | $AMAT = T_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$ | Effective cost per memory reference |
| 2 | Speedup Ratio | $S = \dfrac{T_{naive}}{T_{optimized}}$ | How many times faster the optimized version is |
| 3 | Address Calculation | $A_i = A_{base} + i \cdot s$ | Address of $i$-th element, $s$ = element size |
| 4 | AMAT with Prefetch | $AMAT_{pf} = T_{hit} \cdot (1 - m) + T_{miss} \cdot m \cdot (1 - p)$ | $m$ = miss rate, $p$ = prefetch accuracy |
| 5 | Effective CPI (Naive) | $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{base} + \dfrac{Memory\ Stalls}{Instruction\ Count}$ | Measures pipeline-bubble impact |
| 6 | Block Transfer Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{words}}{N_{bus\ transactions}}$ | Ideally $\eta = N$ for a single LDM |
| 7 | Loop Unroll Speedup Bound | $S_{unroll} \le \dfrac{C_{loop}}{C_{branch}}$ | $C$ = cycle count of loop body / branch |
| 8 | Cache Line Utilization | $U_{line} = \dfrac{Useful\ Words\ Fetched}{Line\ Size}$ | Should be close to $1.0$ for sequential access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** When using AMAT, the $T_{miss}$ term **must** include the full miss penalty, which is the time to fetch the block from main memory **plus** the time to transfer it to the processor. Many students write $T_{miss} = 50$ ns when the true miss penalty could be $5 \cdot T_{hit} = 100$ ns + transfer overhead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The techniques studied here are not academic curiosities. They are deployed in:
- **DSP kernels** (FIR filters, FFT butterflies) on ARM Cortex-M4/M7.
- **Image processing pipelines** (pixel-row scans in camera ISPs).
- **Networking stack memcpy** routines that move Ethernet frames.
- **Boot loaders** that copy firmware from Flash to SRAM.
- **Compiler auto-vectorization** backends that emit `LDM`/`STM` for `memcpy`/`memset`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address Calculation for a 2-D Array

Consider a 2-D array `A[rows][cols]` stored in **row-major order** in memory. The element $A[i][j]$ is at address:

$$
A_{addr}(i, j) = A_{base} + (i \cdot C + j) \cdot s
$$

where $C$ is the number of columns and $s$ is the element size in bytes.

**Step 1:** Start with the linear index in the row-major flattened array.

$$
\text{linear\_index} = i \cdot C + j
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by the element size $s$ to convert to a byte offset.

$$
\text{byte\_offset} = (i \cdot C + j) \cdot s
$$

**Step 3:** Add the base address of the array.

$$
A_{addr}(i, j) = A_{base} + (i \cdot C + j) \cdot s
$$

**Step 4:** Use ARM's `MLA` (Multiply-Accumulate) instruction for efficient evaluation.

$$
\text{MLA } R_{dst}, R_i, R_C, R_j \quad \rightarrow \quad R_{dst} = R_i \cdot R_C + R_j
$$

This single instruction replaces what would otherwise be a multiply followed by an add.

## 3.2 Derivation: Speedup from Block Transfer (LDM/STM)

Suppose we want to copy $N$ words from source `src` to destination `dst` using individual `LDR`/`STR` instructions versus a single `LDM`/`STM` pair.

**Naive Method Cost (per element):**
- 1 cycle for `LDR` issue
- 1 cycle for `STR` issue
- 2 cycles for address updates (`ADD R0, R0, #4` twice)

$$
T_{naive} = N \cdot (C_{ldr} + C_{str} + C_{addr}) = N \cdot 4
$$

**Block Transfer Cost:**
- 1 cycle for `LDM` issue (fetches $N$ registers)
- 1 cycle for `STM` issue (stores $N$ registers)
- 2 cycles for base register updates
- Negligible per-element address arithmetic (write-back handles it)

$$
T_{block} = C_{ldm} + C_{stm} + C_{addr} = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4
$$

**Speedup Ratio:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{naive}}{T_{block}} = \frac{N \cdot 4}{4} = N
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Insight:** For a one-time copy, the speedup is theoretically $\mathbf{N}$-fold. In practice, the speedup is lower because (a) memory bus contention limits throughput, and (b) the $N$ registers must be live simultaneously, restricting $N \le 16$. The **practical limit is $N = 12$ to $14$** registers, since a few must be reserved for loop control and base pointers.

## 3.3 Derivation: AMAT with Prefetch Buffer

A prefetch buffer attempts to hide memory latency by fetching the **next cache line** while the current one is being processed.

Let:
- $H$ = hit rate of the L1 cache
- $m = 1 - H$ = miss rate
- $T_{hit}$ = L1 hit latency = **2 ns**
- $T_{miss}$ = full miss penalty = **40 ns**
- $p$ = prefetch accuracy (fraction of prefetched lines that are actually used)

**Step 1:** Without prefetch, the AMAT formula is the classic one.

$$
AMAT_{no\_pf} = T_{hit} + m \cdot T_{miss}
$$

$$
AMAT_{no\_pf} = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 40 = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ ns}
$$
