**Step 2:** With a perfect prefetch ($p = 1$), all misses are converted to hits from the prefetch buffer (no main memory stall).

$$
AMAT_{pf,ideal} = T_{hit} + m \cdot (1 - p) \cdot T_{miss}
$$

$$
AMAT_{pf,ideal} = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 0 \cdot 40 = 2 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** With realistic prefetch ($p = 0.7$), 30% of prefetched lines are wasted, but 70% of misses are converted.

$$
AMAT_{pf,real} = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 40 = 2 + 0.6 = 2.6 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the effective speedup.

$$
S_{pf} = \frac{AMAT_{no\_pf}}{AMAT_{pf,real}} = \frac{4.0}{2.6} \approx 1.538
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Loop Unrolling Optimization

Consider a loop that processes 16 array elements, where each iteration takes $T_{body}$ cycles plus $T_{branch}$ cycles for the conditional branch.

**Original Loop:**

$$
T_{orig} = 16 \cdot (T_{body} + T_{branch})
$$

**Unrolled by 4x (4 iterations, 4 elements each):**

$$
T_{unroll} = 4 \cdot T_{body} + 4 \cdot T_{branch} + 3 \cdot T_{adjust}
$$

where $T_{adjust}$ is the small overhead of updating the loop counter by 4 instead of by 1. The savings come from the fact that 3 of the 4 branch overheads are eliminated.

**Numerical Example (assuming $T_{body} = 5$, $T_{branch} = 2$, $T_{adjust} = 1$):**

$$
T_{orig} = 16 \cdot (5 + 2) = 16 \cdot 7 = 112 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
T_{unroll} = 4 \cdot 5 + 4 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 = 20 + 8 + 3 = 31 \text{ cycles for 16 elements}
$$

Extrapolated to 16 elements from the unrolled body rate: $4 \cdot 7.75 = 31$ cycles.

$$
S_{unroll} = \frac{112}{31} \approx 3.61
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works:** The branch overhead is amortized over multiple array elements. The savings diminish as $T_{body} \gg T_{branch}$, so loop unrolling is most effective when the loop body is small — exactly the case for simple array access.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Speedup (Typical) | Code Size Cost | Hardware Requirement | Best Use Case |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| LDM / STM | 3× – 8× | Low | None (always available) | Bulk copy, context switch |
| Pre-indexed addressing | 1.2× – 1.5× | None | None | Sequential scan |
| Loop unrolling (4×) | 1.5× – 2× | 3× – 4× larger | None | Tight DSP loops |
| Cache prefetch (HW) | 1.3× – 2× | None | Prefetch buffer | Streaming data |
| Software prefetch (PLD) | 1.2× – 1.8× | Moderate | Cache support | Linked data structures |
| Combining all | 5× – 12× | High | Cache + multi-issue | Tight inner loops |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optimized Array Access

The following diagram illustrates the data flow when an optimized array access routine executes on an ARM core, showing the interaction between the CPU registers, the LDM/STM bus master, the cache controller, and main memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ARM Core - Register Bank R0-R15] --> B[Address Generation Unit AGU]
    B --> C{Cache Controller L1}
    C -- Cache Hit --> D[Data Returned to Register]
    C -- Cache Miss --> E[DMA / Bus Master Unit]
    E --> F[Main Memory SDRAM]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Cache Line Fill Buffer]
    G --> C
    D --> H[ALU Arithmetic Operation]
    H --> I[Result Written Back to Register]
    I --> J[Loop Counter Update]
    J --> B
    style A fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600
    style C fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#336600
    style D fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#993300
    style E fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#990000
    style F fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#660066
    style G fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#003366
    style H fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#993300
    style I fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
    style J fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Selecting the Right Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Process Array of N elements]) --> Q1{Is the data structure strictly sequential?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is N greater than 16?}
    Q1 -- No --> Random[Use standard LDR/STR with random addressing]
    Q2 -- Yes --> LDM[Use LDM STM with loop unrolling]
    Q2 -- No --> Simple[Use simple LDR/STR with post-increment]
    LDM --> CacheCheck{Cache line size?}
    CacheCheck -- 32 bytes --> Unroll4[Unroll by 4]
    CacheCheck -- 64 bytes --> Unroll8[Unroll by 8]
    Unroll4 --> Done([Optimal Performance])
    Unroll8 --> Done
    Simple --> Done
    Random --> Done
    style Start fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
    style Done fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#336600
    style Random fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#990000
    style Simple fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600
    style LDM fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#993300
    style Unroll4 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#993300
    style Unroll8 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#993300
```

## 4.3 Memory Access Pattern Comparison (Naive vs Optimized)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Naive
        N1[Fetch Addr A0] --> N2[Load Word at A0]
        N2 --> N3[Compute Addr A0+4]
        N3 --> N4[Fetch Addr A0+4]
        N4 --> N5[Load Word at A0+4]
        N5 --> N6[Compute Addr A0+8]
        N6 --> N7[Fetch Addr A0+8]
        N7 --> N8[Load Word at A0+8]
    end
    subgraph Optimized
        O1[Set Base R0 = A0] --> O2[Issue LDMIA R0, R1-R8]
        O2 --> O3[Hardware auto-increments internally]
        O3 --> O4[All 8 words arrive in 1 bus cycle]
    end
    style Naive fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#990000
    style Optimized fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#336600
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain any three addressing modes supported by the ARM processor that are particularly useful for array access. Give the corresponding instruction examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Post-indexed addressing:** The base register holds the address for the current transfer; after the transfer, the base is updated by the offset.
   - Example: `LDR R1, [R0], #4` — loads from address in R0, then R0 = R0 + 4. Useful for sequential scan.

2. **Pre-indexed with writeback:** The offset is added to the base **before** the transfer, and the base is updated.
   - Example: `LDR R1, [R0, #4]!` — R0 = R0 + 4 first, then load. Useful when next iteration's address is known in advance.

3. **Pre-indexed without writeback:** Offset is added to the base, but the original base is preserved.
   - Example: `LDR R1, [R0, #4]` — used when the base pointer must remain pointing to the start of the array.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Standard):** 1 mark per correct addressing mode + example. No credit if the example instruction does not match the stated mode.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by 'spatial locality of reference'? How does it help in speeding up array access in ARM systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Spatial locality of reference** is the property of programs to access memory locations that are physically close to recently accessed locations within a short time window. In array processing, this property is intrinsic because elements $A[i]$ and $A[i+1]$ reside in adjacent memory addresses (separated by exactly the element size).

**How it speeds up array access:**
1. **Cache lines** are typically 32 or 64 bytes wide. When $A[i]$ is fetched, the entire cache line containing $A[i], A[i+1], \ldots, A[i+7]$ is loaded. Subsequent accesses to $A[i+1]$ through $A[i+7]$ become cache hits with near-zero latency.
2. **Prefetch buffers** exploit spatial locality by fetching the next cache line speculatively while the current one is being processed.
3. **LDM/STM** block transfer instructions are designed to transfer entire cache-line-aligned blocks, achieving one-bus-cycle-per-line throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** KTU examiners specifically look for the connection between **spatial locality** and **cache line behavior**. A purely definitional answer without this connection scores at most 2 out of 3.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**a) Explain the different methods used to speed up array access in an ARM-based system. Describe how `LDM` and `STM` instructions contribute to this. (7 marks)**

**b) A system has an L1 cache hit time of 2 ns and a miss penalty of 50 ns. The cache hit rate is 95%. Calculate the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) without and with a perfect prefetch buffer. Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The following methods are used to speed up array access in ARM systems:

**1. Exploiting Spatial Locality (2 marks)**
Arrays are stored contiguously. When one element is accessed, neighbouring elements are very likely to be accessed shortly afterwards. The ARM memory system, particularly the L1 cache, exploits this by fetching an entire **cache line** (typically 32 bytes = 8 words) on a single miss. This amortizes the miss penalty over multiple useful word fetches.

**2. Block Transfer Instructions: LDM and STM (3 marks)**
ARM's Load Multiple (`LDM`) and Store Multiple (`STM`) instructions can transfer up to 16 registers to or from consecutive memory locations in a single instruction. This collapses the loop overhead into one bus request.

There are four variants based on address increment/decrement behavior:
- **IA (Increment After):** `LDMIA R0, {R1-R4}` — loads R1, R2, R3, R4 from [R0], [R0+4], [R0+8], [R0+12]; R0 unchanged.
- **IB (Increment Before):** `LDMIB R0, {R1-R4}` — loads from [R0+4], [R0+8], [R0+12], [R0+16].
- **DA (Decrement After):** `LDMDA R0, {R1-R4}` — for descending stack-like access.
- **DB (Decrement Before):** `LDMDB R0, {R1-R4}` — for full descending stack.

The **writeback** suffix `!` updates the base register after the transfer, enabling seamless pointer advancement.

**3. Auto-Indexed Addressing Modes (1 mark)**
Pre- and post-indexed addressing with writeback allows a single instruction to perform data transfer and pointer update simultaneously, eliminating the need for a separate `ADD` instruction inside the loop.

**4. Loop Unrolling (1 mark)**
The branch at the end of every iteration is a major pipeline hazard. By replicating the loop body $k$ times, the branch overhead is amortized over $k$ elements, yielding near-linear speedup for small loop bodies.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- L1 cache hit time: $T_{hit} = 2$ ns
- Miss penalty: $T_{miss} = 50$ ns
- Hit rate: $H = 0.95$
- Miss rate: $m = 1 - H = 0.05$

**Step 1: AMAT without prefetch (3 marks)**

$$
AMAT_{no\_pf} = T_{hit} + m \cdot T_{miss}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
AMAT_{no\_pf} = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 50
$$

$$
AMAT_{no\_pf} = 2 + 2.5 = 4.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: AMAT with perfect prefetch (3 marks)**

A perfect prefetch converts all compulsory misses into hits because the next line is already in the cache before the processor requests it. Hence the effective miss rate becomes 0.

$$
AMAT_{pf} = T_{hit} + 0 \cdot T_{miss} = T_{hit} = 2 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Comment (1 mark)**

$$
S_{pf} = \frac{AMAT_{no\_pf}}{AMAT_{pf}} = \frac{4.5}{2.0} = 2.25
$$

> **Comment:** With a perfect prefetch buffer, the average memory access time drops by **2.25×**, demonstrating that prefetching is highly effective for streaming workloads like array processing where access patterns are predictable.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**a) With a neat diagram, explain how the LDMIA and STMIA instructions perform block data transfer in ARM. How do they improve the performance of array access over conventional LDR/STR-based loops? (7 marks)**

**b) An ARM processor takes 3 cycles per LDR instruction, 1 cycle per ADD (for address update), and 2 cycles per branch. For copying 32 words of data, compare the cycle count of (i) a simple LDR/STR loop and (ii) a block copy using LDM/STM. Compute the speedup. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Diagram and Operation of LDMIA / STMIA (4 marks):**

Consider copying 4 words from memory address `0x1000` to `0x2000`.

- **Load Phase:** `LDMIA R0!, {R1-R4}` — loads registers R1, R2, R3, R4 from [R0], [R0+4], [R0+8], [R0+12] and increments R0 by 16.
- **Store Phase:** `STMIA R1!, {R1-R4}` — stores the values into [R1], [R1+4], [R1+8], [R1+12] and increments R1 by 16.

```
Memory at 0x1000:   [ W0 ][ W1 ][ W2 ][ W3 ]
                     |||   |||   |||   |||
Registers:          [ R1 ][ R2 ][ R3 ][ R4 ]
                     |||   |||   |||   |||
Memory at 0x2000:   [ W0 ][ W1 ][ W2 ][ W3 ]
```

**Performance Comparison (3 marks):**

| Operation | Conventional Loop (per word) | LDM/STM Block (per 4 words) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Address computation | 1 ADD | 0 (writeback in instruction) |
| Memory read | 1 LDR | 1 LDM (4 words) |
| Memory write | 1 STR | 1 STM (4 words) |
| Branch | 1 BNE | 1 BNE (per 4 words) |
| **Total per word** | **6 cycles** | **1.5 cycles** |

The LDM/STM version is approximately **4× faster** because:
1. Address computation is eliminated (writeback handles it).
2. The branch overhead is amortized over 4 words.
3. Memory bus utilization improves due to burst-mode transfers.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $C_{LDR} = 3$ cycles
- $C_{ADD} = 1$ cycle
- $C_{branch} = 2$ cycles
- $N = 32$ words

**Method 1: Simple LDR/STR Loop (3 marks)**

Per word, the loop executes:
- 1 `ADD` to update source address
- 1 `LDR` to read word
- 1 `ADD` to update dest address
- 1 `STR` to write word
- 1 `SUBS` + 1 `BNE` (counted as $C_{branch} = 2$ cycles, executed once per iteration)

$$
T_{simple} = N \cdot (C_{ADD} + C_{LDR} + C_{ADD} + C_{STR}) + N \cdot C_{branch}
$$

$$
T_{simple} = 32 \cdot (1 + 3 + 1 + 3) + 32 \cdot 2
$$

$$
T_{simple} = 32 \cdot 8 + 32 \cdot 2 = 256 + 64 = 320 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Method 2: Block Copy using LDM/STM (3 marks)**

Assume we unroll to use 4 registers per `LDM`/`STM` pair, so the loop runs $32/4 = 8$ times.

Per outer iteration (4 words):
- 1 `LDM` (cost = $C_{LDR} = 3$ cycles, transfers 4 words)
- 1 `STM` (cost = $C_{STR} = 3$ cycles, transfers 4 words)
- 1 `SUBS` + 1 `BNE` ($C_{branch} = 2$ cycles)

$$
T_{block} = 8 \cdot (3 + 3 + 2) = 8 \cdot 8 = 64 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Speedup Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
S = \frac{T_{simple}}{T_{block}} = \frac{320}{64} = 5
$$

> **Result:** The block copy is **5× faster** than the simple loop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the branch cost in the simple loop:** Many students compute $T_{simple} = 32 \cdot 8 = 256$ cycles, missing the 64-cycle branch overhead. **Deduct 2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Using $C_{STR}$ incorrectly:** Some students equate `STM` to a single `STR` cost. Each `STM` issues $N$ bus cycles internally, but the instruction issue cost is comparable to a single `STR`. **The model solution above uses the simplified convention of $C_{STM} = C_{STR}$.**
> 3. **Not stating the assumption for registers:** A block copy with 4 registers/iteration requires 4 user registers plus 2 base pointers plus 1 loop counter = 7 registers, which is feasible (ARM has 13 general-purpose registers).
> 4. **Confusing IA with IB:** `LDMIA` uses the base as the start address; `LDMIB` uses base+4. Getting this backwards loses 1 mark on the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must-memorize):** *Array access speedup* is the use of hardware-software techniques to reduce per-element memory access time by exploiting **spatial locality**.
- **The Three Pillars:** (1) `LDM`/`STM` block transfer, (2) Auto-indexed addressing (`[Rn], #offset` and `[Rn, #offset]!`), (3) Loop unrolling.
- **The Master Formula:** $AMAT = T_{hit} + m \cdot T_{miss}$ — this is the single most important equation for this topic. Practice substituting values.
- **Speedup Bound:** A single `LDM`/`STM` can transfer up to **16 registers** in one instruction, giving a theoretical upper-bound speedup of $16\times$ over single-word transfers.
- **The four `LDM`/`STM` modes** — IA, IB, DA, DB — correspond to **Increment/Decrement After/Before**. `IA` is the most common for forward array scans; `DB` is used for stack pops.
- **The exclamation mark `!`** in `[Rn, #offset]!` denotes **writeback** of the base register. Without it, the base remains unchanged.
- **Cache line size matters:** A 32-byte cache line holds 8 ARM words. Sequential access to 8 elements results in exactly 1 cache miss — the ideal **cache line utilization** of $1.0$.
- **Prefetch accuracy ($p$):** Even at 70% accuracy, prefetching typically yields a 1.3×–1.8× speedup.
- **Loop unrolling trade-off:** Speedup of 1.5×–2× for 4× unrolling, but code size increases 3×–4×. Use only for tight inner loops.
- **Real-world relevance:** This topic underpins `memcpy`/`memmove` in C standard libraries, DSP kernel optimization, and image processing on ARM Cortex-M/A processors.
- **Common KTU 2024 trap:** Forgetting to convert the element size $s$ to bytes when calculating addresses. Always remember: **$A[i]$ is at $A_{base} + i \cdot s$**, where $s = 4$ for `int`/`word` and $s = 1$ for `char`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Functions: Function Call and Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Functions: Function Call and Return

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function Call and Return** in ARM architecture refers to the standardized mechanism governed by the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** that allows a program to transfer execution control to a subroutine (callee) and later resume execution from the point of invocation, while preserving the architectural state, passing parameters, and returning results through well-defined register and stack conventions.

The ARM procedure call standard is a **hardware-software contract** that ensures:
1. The **Link Register (LR = R14)** captures the return address when a function is invoked.
2. The **Stack Pointer (SP = R13)** is used to preserve the callee's frame and the return address when nested calls occur.
3. The first four arguments are passed in **R0–R3**, and the return value is returned in **R0** (with R1 holding the high half for 64-bit results).
4. Registers **R4–R11** are **caller-saved / variable-preserved** registers that must be preserved across function calls.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term "function call" is synonymous with **subroutine invocation** and **procedure call**. The mechanism is implemented using the **BL (Branch with Link)** instruction, while return is performed using **BX LR** or **POP {PC}**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Phone Call with a Sticky Note" Model

Imagine you are working on a math problem (the **caller**), and you suddenly need a complex calculation that you cannot do mentally. You call an expert friend (the **callee**) to solve it for you.

1. **Before calling**, you write down on a sticky note exactly where you stopped in your work — this sticky note is the **Link Register (LR)**.
2. **You dial the friend's number** — this is the **BL instruction** (Branch with Link).
3. The expert works using their own scratch paper (their **local variables on the stack**).
4. When done, the expert tells you the answer (the **return value in R0**).
5. You look at the sticky note and resume work from exactly where you stopped — this is the **return using BX LR**.

The **sticky note (LR)** is single-layered. If you need to call *another* expert during the conversation (nested calls), you must save the first sticky note in a **filing cabinet (Stack)** before placing a new one — this is **PUSH {LR}**.

---

## 1.3 Why AAPCS Exists — Engineering Justification

| Engineering Need | AAPCS Solution | Real-World Parallel |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Predictable linkage | Fixed LR (R14), PC (R15) | Standardized phone number format |
| Argument passing | R0–R3 (fast registers) | First 4 items carried in hand |
| Local storage | Stack frame at SP-relative addresses | Temporary desk drawer |
| Modular libraries | Caller/Callee register rules | Library lending agreement |
| Nested calls | Stack-based LR save | Stack of sticky notes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Constants (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Link Register:** R14
> - **Stack Pointer:** R13
> - **Program Counter:** R15
> - **Argument Registers:** R0–R3
> - **Return Value Register:** R0 (and R1 for double-word)
> - **Variable-Preserved Registers:** R4–R11

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Lifecycle of an ARM Function Call

Every function call in ARM executes in **two distinct phases**:

### Phase 1: CALL (Caller → Callee)
- The caller places the first four arguments in **R0, R1, R2, R3**.
- The caller executes `BL <function_label>`.
- The CPU automatically writes **PC + 4** into **LR (R14)**.
- The CPU sets **PC = target_address**, transferring control to the callee.

### Phase 2: RETURN (Callee → Caller)
- The callee computes the result and places it in **R0**.
- The callee executes `BX LR` (or `POP {PC}`) to transfer control back to the address stored in LR.
- The caller resumes execution immediately after the BL instruction.

---

## 2.2 The Three Architectural Roles of R14 (LR)

> [!NOTE]
> R14 has **three distinct uses** depending on execution context. This is a frequent KTU exam topic.

| Context | Value in LR | Action |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Normal execution | Garbage / undefined | LR has no meaning |
| After `BL` instruction | Return address (PC + 4) | Used for returning via `BX LR` |
| Exception taken (IRQ, SVC) | Return from exception | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` in exception handler |
| User mode → IRQ | `PC + 4` of interrupted instr | Auto-saved on exception entry |

The general rule is:

$$
LR_{new} = PC_{branch\_target} + 4
$$

This is because ARM uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute), so at the time of executing the BL, the PC has already advanced two instructions beyond the branch target. Hence the return address is `target + 4` for ARM state and `target + 2` for Thumb state.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | KTU Importance |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $LR \leftarrow PC + 4$ | Return address stored in LR on `BL` | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $SP_{new} \leftarrow SP - N$ | Stack grows **downward** (full descending) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Stack End Address | $SP_{initial} - 4N$ (N = PUSHes) | ★★★★ |
| 4 | Arg Passing | $R0, R1, R2, R3 \rightarrow$ args 1–4 | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Return Value | $R0$ (32-bit), $R0\!:\!R1$ (64-bit) | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Preserved Registers | $R4\!-\!R11, R13, R14$ | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Scratch Registers | $R0\!-\!R3, R12$ | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | Stack Alignment | 8-byte aligned at public interface | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Leaf Function | No `BL` inside, so no LR save needed | ★★★★ |
| 10 | Non-Leaf Function | Contains `BL`, so MUST `PUSH {LR}` | ★★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for AAPCS register classification — "Caterpillar-Pin":**
> - **Caller-saved (scratch):** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12 (also IP)
> - **Callee-saved (preserve):** R4–R11, R13 (SP), R14 (LR)

---

## 2.4 Why the ARM Convention Matters in Embedded Engineering

The AAPCS is not merely academic — it enables:

- **Library reusability**: Pre-compiled object files from ARM Ltd. can be linked to user code without recompilation.
- **Predictable interrupt latency**: Since registers are preserved in known places, ISRs can be written with deterministic stack usage.
- **Position-independent code (PIC)**: Stack-relative addressing allows the entire program to be relocated in memory.
- **Compiler optimization**: Compilers like `armcc` and `gcc-arm` rely on these rules to perform register allocation, inlining, and dead-code elimination.

In **real-time embedded systems** (automotive ECUs, IoT devices, medical instrumentation), violating AAPCS causes **stack corruption**, **race conditions**, and **hard-to-debug crashes** — a common KTU viva question.

---

## 2.5 Stack Frame Anatomy

A **stack frame** (also called an *activation record*) is the region of stack memory allocated for a single function invocation. The structure follows the **FD (Full Descending)** AAPCS convention:

$$
\text{Frame Layout} = \underbrace{[\text{High Address}]}_{\text{Previous Frame}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{FP} \rightarrow \text{Saved LR}}_{\text{Callee frame}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Local Vars}}_{\text{Allocated space}} \rightarrow \underbrace{[\text{Low Address} = SP]}_{\text{Stack Top}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Frame Pointer (FP = R11 in ARM)** is optional in ARM AAPCS. Modern code often omits it because SP-relative addressing is sufficient and one less register needs saving.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Return Address Calculation for ARM BL Instruction

The ARM pipeline has 3 stages: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**. When a `BL` instruction is in the **Execute** stage, the PC has the address of the instruction **two ahead** of the BL.

**Step 1:** Define the address of the `BL` instruction.
Let:
$$
A_{BL} = \text{Address of the BL instruction}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the value of PC during execution.
At the Execute stage of `BL`, the PC points to the instruction **two instructions after** the BL (because of fetch + decode prefetch).

$$
PC_{execute} = A_{BL} + 8
$$

**Step 3:** Account for pipeline advance on branch.
On a successful branch, the pipeline is flushed and refilled, so the return address is **PC + 4** (i.e., the instruction immediately after the branch target).

$$
A_{return} = A_{BL} + 4
$$

**Step 4:** Confirm the LR load.
The ARM architecture defines that on a `BL` execution, the LR is loaded with:
$$
LR = PC + 4 = A_{BL} + 4
$$

**Verification example:**
Suppose `BL printf` is at address `0x00008000`.

$$
LR = 0x00008000 + 4 = 0x00008004
$$

After the callee returns via `BX LR`, execution resumes at `0x00008004`. This is correct because `BL` occupies 4 bytes in ARM state, and the instruction immediately following it is at `0x00008004`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stack Pointer Evolution Through a Function Call

Consider the canonical ARM function prologue-epilogue sequence.

**Step 1:** Initial state.
Let the caller invoke a function with SP at some value:
$$
SP_0 = 0x00010000
$$

**Step 2:** Prologue — push LR and any preserved registers.
A typical prologue is:
```
PUSH  {R4, R5, LR}    ; SP decreases by 3 words = 12 bytes
```

The new stack pointer is:
$$
SP_1 = SP_0 - (3 \times 4) = 0x00010000 - 12 = 0x0000FFF4
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract from SP to allocate local variables.
Allocate 8 bytes of local storage:
```
SUB   SP, SP, #8
```

The new stack pointer is:
$$
SP_2 = SP_1 - 8 = 0x0000FFF4 - 0x08 = 0x0000FFEC
$$

**Step 4:** Epilogue — restore SP, then pop saved registers.
```
ADD   SP, SP, #8     ; SP = SP_2 + 8 = 0x0000FFF4
POP   {R4, R5, PC}   ; SP returns to 0x00010000; PC = saved LR
```

**Step 5:** Final restoration.
The final SP value after `POP` is:
$$
SP_{final} = SP_2 + 8 + 12 = 0x00010000 = SP_0
$$

Thus, the caller's stack is fully restored, confirming correct behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **order of restoration in epilogue**: first we deallocate locals (by adding back to SP), then we POP the saved registers **with PC in the same instruction**. This is the idiomatic ARM pattern and saves one instruction compared to separate `POP` + `BX LR`.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Argument Passing Cost Analysis

AAPCS passes the first 4 arguments in registers, and **stack-passes** any additional arguments.

Let there be $N$ arguments in total. The number of **register-passed arguments** is:
$$
N_{reg} = \min(N, 4)
$$

The number of **stack-passed arguments** is:
$$
N_{stack} = \max(0, N - 4)
$$

The total stack cost on the callee side (in bytes) is:
$$
C_{stack} = 4 \cdot N_{stack} + 4 \quad \text{(extra word for saved LR if non-leaf)}
$$

**Example 1:** Function with 3 arguments.
$$
N_{reg} = 3, \quad N_{stack} = 0, \quad C_{stack} = 0
$$
All arguments travel in **R0, R1, R2** with no stack access. This is the **fastest path**.

**Example 2:** Function with 7 arguments.
$$
N_{reg} = 4, \quad N_{stack} = 3, \quad C_{stack} = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$
The 5th, 6th, and 7th arguments must be read from stack offsets `[SP]`, `[SP+4]`, `[SP+8]` in the callee.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why ARM code generators are incentivized to keep function signatures under 4 parameters — the marginal cost grows linearly with stack memory traffic.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Leaf vs Non-Leaf Function Decision Tree

A **leaf function** is a function that performs no further `BL` calls. A **non-leaf function** is one that calls at least one other function. The decision impacts whether LR must be saved on the stack.

| Property | Leaf Function | Non-Leaf Function |
|:---|:---|:---|
| LR save in prologue | Not required | **Required** (`PUSH {LR}`) |
| Epilogue | `BX LR` (direct) | `POP {PC}` (reloads LR into PC) |
| Stack frame cost | 0 extra bytes | ≥ 4 bytes for LR slot |
| Speed | Faster (no stack mem access) | Slower |
| Compiler detection | At -O2/-O3, no LR push | Always emits `PUSH {LR}` |

**Compiler reasoning pseudocode (non-executable, conceptual):**
```
if function_body contains any BL/BLX instruction:
    emit("PUSH {..., LR}")
    emit("POP {..., PC}")
else:
    emit("BX LR")  // LR is still valid, no need to save
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common student mistake is to `PUSH {LR}` even in leaf functions. This is **functionally correct** but wastes 4 bytes of stack and one memory write per call. In KTU exams, full credit is awarded for either form **only if** the matching `BX LR` / `POP {PC}` is used consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Function Call Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant LR as Link Register R14
    participant Callee
    participant SP as Stack R13
    participant R0

    Caller->>R0: Place arg1 in R0
    Caller->>R0: Place arg2 in R1
    Caller->>LR: Execute BL func
    Note over LR: LR = PC + 4
    Caller->>Callee: Branch to func entry
    Callee->>SP: PUSH {R4-R11, LR}
    Callee->>Callee: Execute function body
    Callee->>R0: Move result into R0
    Callee->>SP: POP {R4-R11, PC}
    Note over SP: PC loaded with saved LR
    Callee-->>Caller: Return to LR address
    Caller->>Caller: Resume after BL
```

---

## 4.2 Register Usage Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AAPCS["AAPCS Register Conventions"]
        R0["R0: Arg1 / Return Value"]
        R1["R1: Arg2 / Return High"]
        R2["R2: Arg3"]
        R3["R3: Arg4"]
        R4["R4: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R5["R5: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R6["R6: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R7["R7: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R8["R8: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R9["R9: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R10["R10: Variable (Preserved)"]
        R11["R11: Frame Pointer (FP) optional"]
        R12["R12: Intra-Procedure scratch IP"]
        R13["R13: Stack Pointer SP"]
        R14["R14: Link Register LR"]
        R15["R15: Program Counter PC"]
    end

    R0 --> R3
    R4 --> R11
    R12
    R13 --> R14
    R15
```

---

## 4.3 Stack Frame Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighAddress["HIGH ADDRESS (older frames)"]
        Prev["Previous Frame Args"]
    end

    Prev --> LRslot
    LRslot["Saved LR (R14)"] --> FPslot
    FPslot["Saved FP (R11) optional"] --> Local1
    Local1["Local Variable 1"] --> Local2
    Local2["Local Variable 2"] --> SP

    subgraph LowAddress["LOW ADDRESS (stack top)"]
        SP["SP points here (Top of Stack)"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Full Descending (FD)** AAPCS model used by ARM, the stack pointer points to the **last filled location** and the stack grows toward **lower** addresses. This matches the diagram: SP is at the bottom (lowest address), and the callee's saved LR is at a higher address.

---

## 4.4 Branch with Link Encoding Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BL["BL Instruction (32-bit ARM)"]
        Cond["cond (31:28) = 0b1110 (AL)"]
        F1["1 0 1 1 (27:24)"]
        Imm24["imm24 (23:0): signed offset"]
    end

    Cond --> F1 --> Imm24
    Imm24 -. "PC = PC + 4 + sign_extend_imm24 shift left 2" .-> PCUpdate["New PC value"]
```

The BL instruction uses the bit pattern `cond 1011 imm24`, where:
- **cond** = condition code (e.g., `1110` = always)
- **1011** identifies the Branch-with-Link opcode
- **imm24** = signed 24-bit offset, shifted left by 2 to give a 26-bit signed byte offset

The range of branches is therefore:
$$
\text{Range} = \pm 2^{25} \text{ bytes} = \pm 32 \text{ MB}
$$

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1.** What is the role of the **Link Register (R14)** in the ARM procedure call standard? How is its value set during a `BL` instruction?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Link Register (R14) holds the **return address** of a subroutine call. When the CPU executes a `BL <label>` instruction, the address `PC + 4` (where PC is the address of the instruction two ahead due to the 3-stage pipeline) is automatically written into R14. Upon return, the callee executes `BX LR`, which loads the saved return address into the PC, thereby resuming the caller's execution. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **caller-saved** and **callee-saved** registers in the AAPCS. List all registers in each category.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Caller-saved (scratch) registers:** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12. The caller cannot assume these will retain their values across a `BL` call, so the caller must save them itself if needed after the call. (1.5 marks)
> - **Callee-saved (preserved) registers:** R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, R11, R13 (SP), R14 (LR). The callee must preserve these values — it must save them in its prologue and restore them in its epilogue if it uses them. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Explain the complete mechanism of a **function call and return** in ARM architecture using the **AAPCS** standard. Include a description of register roles, the role of the Link Register, and the stack frame layout. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the ARM assembly code for a function `int sum_array(int *arr, int n)` that computes the sum of an integer array of length `n`. Show clearly the prologue, parameter handling, body, and epilogue. Explain why you chose `POP {..., PC}` over `BX LR` in the epilogue. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> ### Model Solution — Question A
>
> #### Part (a) — Function Call Mechanism
>
> The AAPCS defines a **standardized contract** between caller and callee. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 1: Argument Setup (Caller).** The caller places up to four 32-bit arguments in **R0, R1, R2, R3**. Any additional arguments are pushed onto the stack in **right-to-left order**. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 2: Branch with Link.** The caller executes `BL <func>`. The ARM CPU automatically stores `PC + 4` in **LR (R14)**, where PC refers to the address two instructions past the BL (due to the 3-stage pipeline). The PC is then loaded with the branch target. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Step 3: Callee Prologue.** The callee typically begins with:
> ```
> PUSH  {R4-R11, LR}    ; Save preserved registers and return address
> SUB   SP, SP, #N      ; Allocate N bytes for local variables
> ```
> This creates a **stack frame** in the Full Descending convention. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Step 4: Execution.** The callee performs its computation. It may use R0–R3 freely (scratch registers). If it needs R4–R11, they are already saved on the stack. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 5: Result and Return.** The callee places the return value in **R0** (and **R1** for 64-bit). It then executes:
> ```
> ADD   SP, SP, #N      ; Deallocate locals
> POP   {R4-R11, PC}    ; Restore registers; PC = saved LR
> ```
> The `POP {..., PC}` idiom is preferred over `BX LR` because it **atomically restores all preserved registers and the return address in a single instruction**, saving both code size and one memory access. (1 mark)
>
> #### Part (b) — ARM Assembly for `sum_array`
>
> ```armasm
> sum_array:
>     PUSH  {R4, LR}          ; Prologue: save preserved regs + LR (1 mark)
>     MOV   R4, #0            ; R4 = running sum = 0  (0.5 mark)
>     MOV   R12, #0           ; R12 = loop counter i  (0.5 mark)
> loop:
>     CMP   R12, R1           ; Compare i with n
>     BGE   done              ; if i >= n, exit loop  (0.5 mark)
>     LDR   R2, [R0, R12, LSL #2]  ; R2 = arr[i]    (0.5 mark)
>     ADD   R4, R4, R2        ; sum += arr[i]        (0.5 mark)
>     ADD   R12, R12, #1      ; i++                  (0.5 mark)
>     B     loop              ; repeat               (0.5 mark)
> done:
>     MOV   R0, R4            ; Return value in R0   (0.5 mark)
>     POP   {R4, PC}          ; Epilogue: restore + return (1 mark)
> ```
>
> **Why `POP {R4, PC}` instead of `BX LR`?** (1 mark)
> The function calls no other subroutine (it is a leaf function), so we *could* use `BX LR`. However, we use `POP {R4, PC}` because:
> 1. **R4 was saved**, so we must restore it. By loading PC from the same POP, we combine the restore and the return.
> 2. It is **idiomatic ARM**: a matched `PUSH {R4, LR}` requires a matching `POP {R4, PC}` (or `POP {R4, LR}` + explicit `BX LR`).
> 3. It is **one instruction shorter** than the two-instruction `POP {R4, LR} / BX LR` sequence.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> ### Question B (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **complete stack frame layout** during a nested function call in ARM. Show how LR is preserved across the nested call. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following C code:
> ```c
> int square(int x) { return x * x; }
> int sum_sq(int a, int b) { return square(a) + square(b); }
> int main(void)   { return sum_sq(3, 4); }
> ```
> Write the **equivalent ARM assembly** for the `sum_sq` function, showing explicitly how it passes arguments to `square` and preserves the return address. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> ### Model Solution — Question B
>
> #### Part (a) — Stack Frame Layout
>
> (See the diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the visual layout.) The textual description follows:
>
> When `sum_sq` calls `square`, two stack frames exist simultaneously: the **outer frame** of `sum_sq` and the **inner frame** of `square`. The Link Register of `sum_sq` (its return address back to `main`) was saved on `sum_sq`'s stack frame when it executed its own `PUSH {..., LR}`. When `sum_sq` calls `square` via `BL square`, the LR is overwritten with the return address to `sum_sq` — but since the **outer LR was already saved on the stack**, the nested call is safe. (3 marks)
>
> **Frame layout from high to low address:**
> ```
> [High Address]
>  ├─ sum_sq's saved LR (return to main)   ← saved by PUSH in sum_sq
>  ├─ sum_sq's saved R4–R11 (if any)
>  ├─ Local variables of sum_sq
>  ├─ Stack pointer of sum_sq (FP optional)
>  ├─ square's saved LR (return to sum_sq)  ← saved by PUSH in square
>  ├─ square's saved R4–R11 (if any)
>  └─ SP of square  [Low Address]
> ```
>
> **Key point:** The inner `square` frame is *stacked on top of* the outer `sum_sq` frame. As the stack grows downward, each new frame occupies lower addresses. When `square` returns via `POP {..., PC}`, only its own frame is deallocated, restoring the SP to point back into `sum_sq`'s frame. Then `sum_sq` can return to `main` by popping its own saved LR. (2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram)
>
> #### Part (b) — ARM Assembly for `sum_sq`
>
> ```armasm
> ; int sum_sq(int a, int b)   a in R0, b in R1
> sum_sq:
>     PUSH   {R4, R5, LR}         ; Save preserved regs + return addr (1.5 marks)
>     MOV    R4, R0               ; R4 = a (preserve a) (0.5 mark)
>     MOV    R5, R1               ; R5 = b (preserve b) (0.5 mark)
>
>     ; First call: square(a)
>     MOV    R0, R4               ; R0 = a (argument)  (0.5 mark)
>     BL     square               ; R0 = a*a; LR = return-in-sum_sq (0.5 mark)
>     MOV    R4, R0               ; Save first result in R4 (0.5 mark)
>
>     ; Second call: square(b)
>     MOV    R0, R5               ; R0 = b (argument)  (0.5 mark)
>     BL     square               ; R0 = b*b; LR = return-in-sum_sq (0.5 mark)
>     ADD    R4, R4, R0           ; R4 = a*a + b*b (0.5 mark)
>
>     MOV    R0, R4               ; Return value in R0 (0.5 mark)
>     POP    {R4, R5, PC}         ; Epilogue + return to main (1 mark)
> ```
>
> **Argument passing analysis (1 mark):**
> - `a` arrives in R0, `b` arrives in R1 per AAPCS.
> - We must preserve them across the `BL square` call, because R0 is a scratch register that `square` is free to clobber. We save them in R4 and R5 (callee-saved).
> - The `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}` saves R4, R5, and the LR (return address to `main`). Without saving LR, the second `BL square` would overwrite it, and we could never return to `main`.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Recurring Trends):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to save LR in non-leaf functions** (–2 to –4 marks). If your function contains a `BL`, you **must** `PUSH {..., LR}`. Otherwise the return address is clobbered by the inner BL.
> 2. **Confusing `BX LR` with `POP {PC}`** (–1 mark). `BX LR` is used when LR still holds the correct return address (leaf functions). `POP {PC}` is used when LR was saved on the stack and is being restored.
> 3. **Returning value in wrong register** (–2 marks). The return value **must** be in **R0** for 32-bit, **R0:R1** for 64-bit. Placing it in R4 or a memory location violates AAPCS and breaks inter-module linkage.
> 4. **Mismatched PUSH/POP register lists** (–1 mark). If you PUSH `{R4, R5, LR}`, your POP must list exactly the same set, optionally with PC substituted for LR.
> 5. **Ignoring stack alignment** (–1 mark for -O2 graded code). AAPCS requires 8-byte stack alignment at public function entries. Mismatched alignment causes HardFault on Cortex-M3/M4.
> 6. **Off-by-one in return address** (–2 marks). Returning to `LR` directly is correct in ARM state. In **Thumb state**, the return address is `LR` directly (not `LR - 1`). Many students get confused between ARM and Thumb pipelines.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Function Call and Return**

- ✅ **BL instruction** is the only way to call a subroutine in ARM. It writes `PC + 4` into **LR (R14)**.
- ✅ **Return** is done with `BX LR` (leaf) or `POP {..., PC}` (non-leaf).
- ✅ **R0–R3** are **argument and return** registers (scratch). **R4–R11** are **preserved**.
- ✅ **R12** is the **Intra-Procedure scratch register (IP)** — scratch, do not rely on its value.
- ✅ **R13 = SP** (Stack Pointer), **R14 = LR** (Link Register), **R15 = PC** (Program Counter).
- ✅ The stack is **Full Descending (FD)**: SP points to the last filled location, grows toward lower addresses.
- ✅ **Leaf functions** may use `BX LR` directly; **non-leaf functions** must `PUSH {LR}` and `POP {PC}`.
- ✅ AAPCS mandates **8-byte stack alignment** at all public function entry points.
- ✅ A maximum of **4 arguments** are passed in registers; arguments beyond the fourth are passed on the stack.
- ✅ The **64-bit return value** is split between R0 (low word) and R1 (high word).
- ✅ `POP {..., PC}` is preferred over `POP {..., LR} + BX LR` for code density and atomicity.
- ✅ When a function modifies R4–R11, it **must** save and restore them — failure causes silent data corruption in the caller.
- ✅ The **Frame Pointer (FP = R11)** is optional in AAPCS; modern code often omits it to save stack memory.
- ✅ In **Thumb state**, the return address is just `LR` (no +4 correction), unlike ARM state.
- ✅ Always remember: **AAPCS compliance is mandatory** for inter-module linking and library reusability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory anchor for viva:**
> *"BL saves return in LR, callee saves LR on stack if it calls others, and POP {PC} returns — the entire AAPCS in three operations."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> In the **ARM Architecture**, **Register Usage** refers to the systematic organization, allocation, and operational assignment of the **37 physical 32-bit registers** (R0–R15 + CPSR + banked SPSRs) as defined in the **ARM Programmer's Model**. It dictates how general-purpose registers, special-purpose registers (SP, LR, PC), and the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** are partitioned, banked, and utilized across different processor modes (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System) to execute instructions, manage control flow, pass procedure parameters, and preserve processor state.

From the user's (Application Programmer's) perspective, the ARM core provides **16 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0–R15)** plus the **CPSR**. However, in privileged modes, additional **banked physical registers** become visible, taking the total to **37 registers**, which the hardware uses to maintain multiple execution contexts simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "37 Registers"?**
> Because ARM is a *banked-register architecture*: instead of saving state to memory on every mode switch (a costly operation), ARM hardware swaps in a different physical set of registers. This is a **key performance optimization** you must remember for KTU exams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the ARM register file as a **massive office desk with 37 drawers** (physical registers):

- **You (the User-mode programmer)** can only see and open **16 drawers** (R0–R15) on the top tier of your desk.
- **The CEO (Privileged modes like FIQ, IRQ)** has *additional* dedicated drawers hidden underneath, swapped in when an interrupt fires — so the CEO's work-in-progress is **never mixed up with yours**.
- **The "Inbox" drawer (R13 / SP)** is unique to the current task — it tracks the stack.
- **The "Return Address" drawer (R14 / LR)** remembers where to go back when a function finishes.
- **The "Bookmark" drawer (R15 / PC)** always points to the **next instruction** to execute.
- **The "Status Dashboard" (CPSR)** displays system flags like a car dashboard (N, Z, C, V, I, F, T, Mode bits).

> [!TIP]
> Whenever KTU asks "What is the total number of registers in ARM?" — the answer is **37 in physical hardware**, but **30 are general-purpose (banked) + 6 status registers + 1 CPSR + SPSRs per mode**.

## 1.3 Key Numerical Metrics (Bolded)

- **Total physical registers in ARM core = 37**
- **General-purpose registers visible in User mode = 16 (R0–R15)**
- **Register width = 32 bits**
- **Program Counter (PC) = R15**, always 8 bytes ahead of current instruction in ARM state (2 bytes in Thumb state)
- **Condition flags in CPSR = 4 (N, Z, C, V)**
- **Control bits in CPSR = 8 (I, F, T, M4–M0)**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARM Register Classification (Hierarchical Breakdown)

The ARM register file is structured in **three logical tiers**:

### Tier 1 — Unbanked General-Purpose Registers (Always Visible)

| Register | Mnemonic | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| R0 | — | Argument / Return value / Scratch |
| R1 | — | Argument / Return value (2nd word) / Scratch |
| R2 | — | Argument / Scratch |
| R3 | — | Argument / Scratch |
| R4 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R5 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R6 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R7 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R8 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R9 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R10 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R11 | — | Local variable (must preserve) |
| R12 | IP | Intra-Procedure-call scratch register |
| R13 | SP | Stack Pointer (banked per mode) |
| R14 | LR | Link Register (banked per mode) |
| R15 | PC | Program Counter |

### Tier 2 — Banked Registers (Mode-Specific)

The registers **R8–R14** are physically banked. This means the same *logical* name refers to **different physical storage** depending on the current processor mode.

| Mode | Banked Registers |
|------|------------------|
| FIQ | R8_fiq – R14_fiq (7 registers) |
| IRQ | R13_irq, R14_irq |
| Supervisor (SVC) | R13_svc, R14_svc |
| Abort (ABT) | R13_abt, R14_abt |
| Undefined (UND) | R13_und, R14_und |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** FIQ has **more** banked registers (R8–R14) than other privileged modes. This is **intentional** — it allows the FIQ handler to begin execution *without saving any registers*, achieving the **fastest possible interrupt latency**.

### Tier 3 — Program Status Registers

| Register | Role |
|----------|------|
| **CPSR** | Current Program Status Register — one per core, always reflects current state |
| **SPSR** | Saved Program Status Register — exactly **one per privileged mode** (5 SPSRs total), automatically loaded on exception entry and used to restore CPSR on exception return |

## 2.2 CPSR / SPSR Bit Layout (32 Bits)

The CPSR is divided into **four control fields** and **four condition flags**:

$$
\text{CPSR} = \underbrace{b_{31}\ldots b_{28}}_{\text{Condition Flags}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{b_{27}\ldots b_{8}}_{\text{Reserved}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{b_{7} \ldots b_{0}}_{\text{Control Field}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition Flags (bits 31–28)} &: N, Z, C, V \\
\text{Control Bits (bits 7–0)} &: I, F, T, M_4, M_3, M_2, M_1, M_0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Condition Flag Meanings

| Flag | Bit | Set When |
|------|-----|----------|
| **N (Negative)** | 31 | Result bit[31] = 1 (signed negative) |
| **Z (Zero)** | 30 | Result equals zero |
| **C (Carry)** | 29 | Unsigned overflow / borrow / extend |
| **V (oVerflow)** | 28 | Signed overflow into sign bit |

### Control Bit Meanings

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **I** | IRQ disable | 1 = mask IRQ interrupts |
| **F** | FIQ disable | 1 = mask FIQ interrupts |
| **T** | Thumb state | 0 = ARM (32-bit) state, 1 = Thumb (16-bit) state |
| **M[4:0]** | Mode bits | Encodes current processor mode (see table below) |

### Processor Mode Encoding (M[4:0])

| M[4:0] | Mode | Privileged? |
|--------|------|-------------|
| 10000 | User | No |
| 10001 | FIQ | Yes |
| 10010 | IRQ | Yes |
| 10011 | Supervisor | Yes |
| 10111 | Abort | Yes |
| 11011 | Undefined | Yes |
| 11111 | System | Yes |

## 2.3 The Stack Pointer (R13 / SP) — Detailed Role

- Always **banked** — every privileged mode has its own physical SP.
- SP must be **8-byte aligned** in ARM-ABI (AAPCS).
- Used implicitly by `PUSH`, `POP`, `LDM`, `STM` with `^` suffix.
- On exception entry, hardware **automatically** switches SP to the banked copy of the new mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bank SP?** When an IRQ fires mid-function, the IRQ handler needs a *private* stack to push its own context. If SP were not banked, the handler would corrupt the User-mode stack.

## 2.4 The Link Register (R14 / LR) — Detailed Role

- Holds the **return address** after a subroutine call (`BL` instruction) or exception.
- On `BL subroutine`, the address of `PC + 4` is loaded into LR.
- On exception, hardware loads the address `PC + 4` (or `PC + 8` for IRQ/FIQ) into the banked LR of the new mode.
- Use `MOV PC, LR` or `BX LR` to return.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favorite Trick Question:** "If a `BL` is executed at address `0x00008000`, what value goes into LR?" — Answer: `0x00008004` (PC + 4) because of the ARM pipeline.

## 2.5 The Program Counter (R15 / PC) — Pipeline Behavior

In ARM state, the PC reads as **2 instructions ahead (8 bytes)** of the currently executing instruction because of the 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute).

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address of current instruction} + 8
$$

In Thumb state:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address of current instruction} + 4
$$

Writing to R15 acts as a **branch** (changes program flow), with the low bit selecting ARM/Thumb state.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes |
|---------|-----------------|-------|
| Read PC (ARM) | $\text{PC} = \text{CurrAddr} + 8$ | 3-stage pipeline |
| Read PC (Thumb) | $\text{PC} = \text{CurrAddr} + 4$ | 2-stage pipeline |
| BL return addr | $\text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4$ | At time of `BL` execution |
| SP alignment | $\text{SP} \equiv 0 \pmod{8}$ | AAPCS requirement |
| Branch with state switch | $\text{BX} \rightarrow \text{bit}[0]$ | 0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb |
| Total registers | $37 = 30_{\text{GP}} + 6_{\text{Status}} + 1_{\text{CPSR}}$ | 5 SPSRs + 1 CPSR |
| Banked GP registers | $\text{FIQ} = 7$, $\text{Other priv} = 2$ | R8–R14 |
| CPSR width | **32 bits** | |
| Condition codes | $2^{4} = 16$ | bits 28–31 |
| Mode encoding width | $5 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ states}$ | 7 valid modes |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Register Usage is the **backbone of**:
- **RTOS context switching** (Task state preservation uses SPSR + banked SP/LR)
- **Compiler code generation** (GCC's `-marm` / `-mthumb` mode uses ATPCS to assign registers)
- **Assembly hand-optimization** in embedded firmware (engineers decide which regs hold loop counters, pointers, temporaries)
- **Interrupt latency minimization** (leveraging FIQ's extra banked registers)
- **Bare-metal bootloaders** in IoT/automotive ECUs (initial SP/PC setup from vector table)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why PC reads as "Address + 8" in ARM State

**Context:** The ARM7TDMI uses a 3-stage pipeline.

**Step 1 — Identify the stages.**
The pipeline stages are:
- **F (Fetch):** Instruction memory read
- **D (Decode):** Instruction decoded, registers read
- **E (Execute):** ALU operation, result write-back

**Step 2 — Trace a single instruction at address `A`.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1} &: \text{Fetch instruction at } A \\
\text{Cycle 2} &: \text{Decode instruction at } A, \text{Fetch } A+4 \\
\text{Cycle 3} &: \text{Execute instruction at } A, \text{Decode } A+4, \text{Fetch } A+8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — When the Execute stage is processing the instruction at address `A`, the PC register itself (which always holds the *Fetch* address) is pointing at `A + 8`.**

$$
\therefore \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = A + 8
$$

**Step 4 — Consequence for branches.**

A branch instruction must compute its target and load it into the PC *before* the pipeline has prefetched the next two instructions. The ARM branch unit handles this by flushing the pipeline (2-cycle penalty for non-taken predicted, or 1-cycle for taken branch in later cores).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: BL Instruction Link Value

**Given:** A `BL subroutine` is located at address `0x0000_4000`. Show the value placed in R14 (LR).

**Step 1 — At the moment `BL` is in the Execute stage, PC is in the Fetch stage pointing 2 instructions ahead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{at execute of BL}} &= \text{Address of BL} + 8 \\
&= 0x00004000 + 8 \\
&= 0x00004008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — However, the ARM architecture defines that `BL` stores the *address of the next instruction after BL* (i.e., the instruction following the branch). This equals the Execute-stage address — 4 (since PC at execute = current + 8, the "next instruction" is at current + 4).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LR} &\leftarrow \text{Address of instruction after BL} \\
\text{LR} &\leftarrow 0x00004000 + 4 \\
\text{LR} &\leftarrow 0x00004004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using the PC-read rule.**

From the perspective of the *branch target* (subroutine's first instruction at `0x0000_4020`), when the subroutine reads R15:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read inside subroutine}} = 0x00004020 + 8 = 0x00004028
$$

The LR (`0x00004004`) is therefore exactly the address the subroutine must later return to — but note: it points to the instruction *after* BL, not to the current PC value at read time. This is the **architectural guarantee** that makes function returns work.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mode Bit Update on Exception Entry

**Problem:** The core is in User mode (`M = 0b10000`), executing at address `0x0010_0000`. An IRQ occurs. Show the new CPSR.

**Step 1 — Mode bits M[4:0] become `0b10010` (IRQ).**

$$
\text{M}[4:0] = 10010_2 = 18_{10}
$$

**Step 2 — I bit is set to 1 (to mask further IRQs).**

$$
\text{I} \leftarrow 1
$$

**Step 3 — T bit is unchanged (exceptions take ARM state on classic ARM, but on Cortex-M they are handled in Thumb).**

$$
\text{T} \leftarrow \text{T}_{\text{old}}
$$

**Step 4 — Old CPSR is copied into SPSR_irq.**

$$
\text{SPSR\_irq} \leftarrow \text{CPSR}_{\text{old}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final CPSR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR}_{\text{new}} &= (\text{CondFlags}_{\text{old}}) \,\|\, (0 \ldots 0) \,\|\, (I=1, F=\text{F}_{\text{old}}, T=\text{T}_{\text{old}}, 10010) \\
&= 0x\text{XX}000000 + (I \cdot 0x80) + (F \cdot 0x40) + (T \cdot 0x20) + 0x12
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Register Usage Across Processor Modes

The following matrix shows which **physical** registers are accessible in each mode, illustrating the banked-register design.

| Logical | User | FIQ | IRQ | SVC | ABT | UND | System |
|---------|:----:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:------:|
| R0 | P0 | P0 | P0 | P0 | P0 | P0 | P0 |
| R1 | P1 | P1 | P1 | P1 | P1 | P1 | P1 |
| R2 | P2 | P2 | P2 | P2 | P2 | P2 | P2 |
| R3 | P3 | P3 | P3 | P3 | P3 | P3 | P3 |
| R4 | P4 | P4 | P4 | P4 | P4 | P4 | P4 |
| R5 | P5 | P5 | P5 | P5 | P5 | P5 | P5 |
| R6 | P6 | P6 | P6 | P6 | P6 | P6 | P6 |
| R7 | P7 | P7 | P7 | P7 | P7 | P7 | P7 |
| R8 | P8 | **P8_fiq** | P8 | P8 | P8 | P8 | P8 |
| R9 | P9 | **P9_fiq** | P9 | P9 | P9 | P9 | P9 |
| R10 | P10 | **P10_fiq** | P10 | P10 | P10 | P10 | P10 |
| R11 | P11 | **P11_fiq** | P11 | P11 | P11 | P11 | P11 |
| R12 | P12 | **P12_fiq** | P12 | P12 | P12 | P12 | P12 |
| R13 (SP) | P13 | **P13_fiq** | **P13_irq** | **P13_svc** | **P13_abt** | **P13_und** | P13 |
| R14 (LR) | P14 | **P14_fiq** | **P14_irq** | **P14_svc** | **P14_abt** | **P14_und** | P14 |
| R15 (PC) | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC |
| CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR |
| SPSR | — | SPSR_fiq | SPSR_irq | SPSR_svc | SPSR_abt | SPSR_und | — |

*Bold entries denote banked (mode-private) physical storage.*

## 3.5 Worked Example: ATPCS Parameter Passing

**Question:** A C function `int foo(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e)` is called from ARM assembly. Identify register usage.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

- **Step 1** — `a` is passed in **R0**.
- **Step 2** — `b` is passed in **R1**.
- **Step 3** — `c` is passed in **R2**.
- **Step 4** — `d` is passed in **R3**.
- **Step 5** — The 5th parameter `e` **exceeds the register argument budget** and is pushed onto the stack at `[SP]` before the call. The AAPCS permits only **4 register arguments (R0–R3)**.
- **Step 6** — The return value is placed in **R0** on exit (and **R0+R1** for 64-bit values).
- **Step 7** — Inside `foo`, **R0–R3 and R12** are *caller-saved scratch* — the callee may freely clobber them. **R4–R11** are *callee-saved* — `foo` must preserve them by pushing to the stack and restoring before return.

    M4 --> MODE_DEC["M4-M0 = Mode Encoding"]

    M2 --> MODE_DEC
    M1 --> MODE_DEC

## 4.3 Exception Entry / Exit Flow (Register State Transitions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Mode executing instruction at A"] --> B["Exception occurs e.g. IRQ"]
    B --> C["Hardware saves old CPSR to SPSR_irq"]
    C --> D["Hardware sets CPSR mode bits to 10010 IRQ"]
    D --> E["Set I bit to 1 in CPSR"]
    E --> F["Banked SP_irq and LR_irq become active"]
    F --> G["LR_irq is loaded with return address A+4"]
    G --> H["PC is loaded with vector address 0x18"]
    H --> I["IRQ handler executes using banked SP_irq and LR_irq"]
    I --> J["Handler reads SPSR_irq to get original mode and flags"]
    J --> K["On return: SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4"]
    K --> L["Hardware restores CPSR from SPSR_irq"]
    L --> M["PC resumes at original instruction A"]
    M --> N["User Mode continues with original flags and mode"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**List the 37 registers of the ARM processor and briefly explain the role of the CPSR.**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM processor has **37 physical 32-bit registers**:
- **R0–R12:** General-purpose registers (unbanked, visible in all modes).
- **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer — banked per mode.
- **R14 (LR):** Link Register — banked per mode, holds return addresses.
- **R15 (PC):** Program Counter — points to the next instruction to fetch.
- **1 CPSR (Current Program Status Register):** Single, always reflects the current condition flags (N, Z, C, V) and control bits (I, F, T, M[4:0]).
- **5 SPSRs (Saved PSRs):** One per privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) — saves the CPSR on exception entry.

**Role of CPSR:** The CPSR holds the current **condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) that reflect the result of the most recent arithmetic/logic operation, plus **control bits** — I and F for interrupt masking, T for ARM/Thumb state selection, and M[4:0] for the current processor mode. It is the single source of truth for the processor's *current* execution state.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**What is the role of the Link Register (R14) and the Stack Pointer (R13) in ARM architecture?**

**Model Answer:**

**R14 — Link Register (LR):**
- Holds the **return address** after a `BL` (Branch and Link) instruction or exception.
- After `BL subroutine`, LR contains the address of the instruction immediately following the BL, so the subroutine can return via `MOV PC, LR` or `BX LR`.
- On exception entry, the banked LR of the new mode is automatically loaded with the return address.
- LR is **banked per mode**, so the User-mode LR is preserved when an IRQ handler runs.

**R13 — Stack Pointer (SP):**
- Points to the **top of the current stack** in memory.
- Adjusted automatically by `PUSH`/`POP` and `LDM`/`STM` instructions.
- **Banked per mode** — each privileged mode has its own physical SP, allowing exception handlers to use a private stack without corrupting the User stack.
- Must be **8-byte aligned** as per AAPCS.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution A(a) — ARM Register Organization

**Step 1 — User-visible registers (16 total).**
In any processor mode, the programmer sees R0–R15. R0–R12 are general-purpose scratch and variable registers. R13 (SP) is the Stack Pointer. R14 (LR) is the Link Register. R15 (PC) is the Program Counter.

**Step 2 — Banked registers.**
In privileged modes (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND), additional physical registers replace the user copies of R13 and R14 — these are called **banked registers**. Each privileged mode has its own private R13 and R14. The FIQ mode additionally banks R8 through R14 (7 extra registers).

**Step 3 — Reason for banking.**
Banking enables **fast exception handling** — when an interrupt fires, the hardware switches in the banked SP/LR of the new mode in **zero cycles**, eliminating the need for the handler to immediately save the User-mode SP/LR to memory. The handler can begin real work immediately.

**Step 4 — Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).**

**Step 5 — Total count.** 16 user-visible logical names map onto 30 general-purpose physical registers (because of banking), plus 1 CPSR and 5 SPSRs = **37 physical registers**.

---

### Solution A(b) — CPSR and SPSR

**Step 1 — CPSR overview.**
The CPSR is a single 32-bit register holding the **current** state of the processor. It is accessible in all modes.

**Step 2 — Condition flags (bits 31–28).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{bit 31, set if ALU result is negative} \\
Z &= \text{bit 30, set if ALU result is zero} \\
C &= \text{bit 29, set on unsigned carry/borrow/extend} \\
V &= \text{bit 28, set on signed overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

These flags are tested by conditional branch instructions (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, etc.) and by conditional execution suffixes (e.g., `ADDNE`).

**Step 3 — Control bits (bits 7–0).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \text{bit 7, IRQ interrupt mask} \\
F &= \text{bit 6, FIQ interrupt mask} \\
T &= \text{bit 5, state: 0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb} \\
M[4:0] &= \text{bits 4–0, processor mode encoding}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SPSR.**
There are **5 SPSRs**, one per privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). User mode and System mode do **not** have an SPSR (since they cannot be entered via an exception). On exception entry, the old CPSR is **automatically** copied into the SPSR of the new mode. On exception return (`SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `LDM ..., R0-R3, ^`), the SPSR is **automatically** restored into CPSR.

**Step 5 — Significance.**
The CPSR/SPSR mechanism is what makes ARM's **low-latency exception model** possible — atomic state preservation without software overhead.

---

### Solution B(a) — ATPCS Register Conventions

**Step 1 — What is ATPCS?**
The **ARM-Thumb Procedure Call Standard (ATPCS)** is a convention that defines how registers are used for parameter passing, return values, and register preservation across procedure calls in ARM/Thumb code.

**Step 2 — Parameter passing.**
- The **first 4 word-sized arguments** are passed in **R0, R1, R2, R3**, in left-to-right order.
- Any **5th or later arguments** are passed on the **stack**, pushed right-to-left by the caller before the `BL` call.

**Step 3 — Return value.**
- A 32-bit return value is placed in **R0** by the callee.
- A 64-bit return value is placed in **R0 (low word) and R1 (high word)**.

**Step 4 — Scratch / Caller-saved.**
- **R0–R3** and **R12 (IP)** are *scratch registers* — the callee may clobber them freely without saving.
- The caller must save these in its own stack frame if it needs the values across a call.

**Step 5 — Variable / Callee-saved.**
- **R4–R11** are *variable registers* — the callee must **preserve** them (push to stack on entry, pop on exit) if it uses them.
- This allows the caller to assume R4–R11 retain their values across calls.

**Step 6 — Special registers.**
- **R13 (SP)** — Stack Pointer, must be 8-byte aligned, managed by callee.
- **R14 (LR)** — Link Register, holds return address.
- **R15 (PC)** — Program Counter, used for branching.

**Step 7 — Register summary table.**

| Register | Role | Saved by |
|----------|------|----------|
| R0–R3 | Argument + return | Caller |
| R4–R11 | Local variables | Callee |
| R12 (IP) | Scratch | Caller |
| R13 (SP) | Stack pointer | — |
| R14 (LR) | Link register | — |
| R15 (PC) | Program counter | — |

---

### Solution B(b) — PC Behavior in ARM State

**Step 1 — Pipeline stages.**
Classic ARM (ARM7TDMI) uses a **3-stage pipeline**:
1. **Fetch** — instruction read from memory
2. **Decode** — instruction decoded, source registers read
3. **Execute** — ALU operation, result written back

**Step 2 — Why PC reads as "current + 8".**
When the processor is *executing* an instruction at address `A`:
- That instruction was *fetched* in cycle 1.
- The instruction at `A+4` is being *decoded* in cycle 2.
- The instruction at `A+8` is being *fetched* in cycle 3.
- The PC register (used for sequential fetching) holds the address of the *fetch-stage* instruction, which is `A + 8`.

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = A + 8
$$

**Step 3 — Consequence 1: PC-relative loads.**
The literal pool mechanism uses `LDR R0, [PC, #offset]`. Since the *read* PC is `A+8`, the effective address of the literal is `[A+8+offset]`. Assemblers account for this by automatically computing the correct offset.

**Step 4 — Consequence 2: Branch instructions.**
When a branch is taken, the hardware must **flush** the two prefetched instructions in the pipeline. This causes a **2-cycle branch penalty** (in older ARM cores). Newer cores (Cortex-A series) use branch prediction to reduce this penalty.

**Step 5 — Consequence 3: PC-relative addressing and Thumb state.**
In Thumb state, the pipeline is only 2 stages deep, so:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read in Thumb}} = A + 4
$$

This difference must be accounted for when switching between ARM and Thumb via `BX` or `BLX`.

**Step 6 — Demonstration with a numerical example.**
Suppose an instruction at address `0x8000` executes. Inside the instruction, the assembler places a literal at address `0x800C`. To reach it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective address} &= \text{PC}_{\text{read}} + \text{offset} \\
&= (0x8000 + 8) + \text{offset} \\
&= 0x8008 + \text{offset}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this equal to `0x800C`:

$$
0x8008 + \text{offset} = 0x800C \implies \text{offset} = 4
$$

So the assembler emits `LDR R0, [PC, #4]`. KTU students frequently compute the wrong offset by forgetting the `+8` rule.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "30 registers" with "37 registers":** Many students write "ARM has 30 registers" because the user sees 16 + banking adds 14 (so 30 GP), but they forget the **6 status registers (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs)** → total is **37**.
> 2. **Forgetting the +8 PC rule:** When asked to compute the offset for `LDR Rn, [PC, #imm]`, students compute from the *current* instruction address, not the *PC-read* value. This causes an **off-by-8 error** and full mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing FIQ banked count:** FIQ has **7 banked GP registers** (R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq, R13_fiq, R14_fiq), not 2. Other privileged modes bank only **2** (R13, R14).
> 4. **Writing "CPSR is banked":** CPSR is **not** banked — it is a *single* register for the entire core. It is the **SPSR** that is banked per mode.
> 5. **Mixing up LR content on BL:** LR holds `current_BL_address + 4`, not `+8`. The pipeline effect applies to *reading* PC; the architectural definition of the link is the address *after* the branch.
> 6. **ATPCS confusion:** Students often say "R0–R12 are saved by the caller" — incorrect. R0–R3 and R12 are caller-saved (scratch), while R4–R11 are callee-saved (variable).
> 7. **Mode bit values:** Writing mode bits in decimal (e.g., "Mode = 17") instead of binary (`10001`) loses marks. Always use 5-bit binary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ARM has **37 physical 32-bit registers**: 30 general-purpose (banked), 1 CPSR, 5 SPSRs.
- User-visible: **R0–R15** + CPSR.
- **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**, **R15 = PC** — these three are the special-purpose registers.
- **R8–R14 are banked**; FIQ banks R8–R14 (7 registers), other modes bank R13–R14 (2 registers).
- **PC read = current + 8** in ARM state, **+4** in Thumb state (pipeline artifact).
- **BL** stores `PC + 4` (address of next instruction) into LR.
- **CPSR** is the single current-status register; **SPSR** is one-per-privileged-mode backup.
- **CPSR flags**: N, Z, C, V (bits 31–28) — set by ALU ops, tested by conditional branches.
- **CPSR control bits**: I, F, T, M4–M0 (bits 7–0) — interrupt mask, state, mode.
- **Mode encodings** (M[4:0]): User=10000, FIQ=10001, IRQ=10010, SVC=10011, ABT=10111, UND=11011, System=11111.
- **ATPCS rules**: R0–R3 + R12 = caller-saved (scratch); R4–R11 = callee-saved (variable); first 4 args in R0–R3; return in R0 (R0+R1 for 64-bit).
- **SP must be 8-byte aligned** per AAPCS.
- **Banking purpose**: enables zero-cycle register context for exception handlers — the entire reason ARM is fast in embedded/real-time.
- **On exception**: hardware auto-saves CPSR → SPSR, sets new mode, loads banked LR with return address, loads PC with vector address.
- **On exception return**: `SUBS PC, LR, #4` simultaneously restores PC and CPSR (atomic).
- **BX** with bit[0]=0 → ARM state, =1 → Thumb state.
- **User and System modes share registers**; only System mode is privileged.
- **R12 (IP)**: Intra-Procedure-call scratch register — used by linker veneers during interworking calls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parameter Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parameter Passing** in ARM architecture refers to the standardized mechanism defined by the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** that governs how arguments (parameters) are transferred from a **caller** function to a **callee** function during a procedure call, and how return values are passed back to the caller. According to AAPCS, the first four 32-bit parameters are passed in registers **R0, R1, R2, and R3**, while any additional parameters are pushed onto the **Full Descending Stack (FD)**. The return value is conventionally placed in **R0** (or R0:R1 for 64-bit values).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)** is the binding convention that every compiler (GCC, Keil, ARM CC) targeting ARM/Thumb-2 must follow. Without it, independently compiled object files could not interoperate via shared library linking or function calls.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office Counter"

Imagine you walk into a **post office counter** (the callee function) to ship a package. There are exactly **four service windows** open in front of you — these are registers **R0, R1, R2, R3**. If your shipment requires only **1 to 4 items**, you place them directly on the open counter slots; the clerk (callee) picks them up instantly. If your shipment requires **more than 4 items**, you must place the extras on a **conveyor belt behind you** (the stack), and the clerk retrieves them in order. When the service is done, the clerk hands you back a **single receipt slip** (return value via R0). This is precisely how ARM's AAPCS routes data into and out of functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Quick-Reference:**
> - **Caller** → The function that invokes another function.
> - **Callee** → The function being invoked.
> - **Caller-saved registers** → R0–R3, R12, LR (caller must preserve if needed).
> - **Callee-saved registers** → R4–R11, SP (callee must preserve if used).
> - **Full Descending Stack** → Stack grows toward **lower** memory addresses; SP points to the **last** filled item.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The AAPCS Parameter Passing Rules — Step-by-Step Logic

The AAPCS defines an explicit, deterministic ordering of operations. Understanding the *why* behind each rule is critical for KTU exam answers.

1. **Step 1 — Argument Classification by Size:**
   - For each parameter, the compiler classifies it as a **32-bit word**, **64-bit doubleword**, or larger composite (e.g., struct).
   - 32-bit (or smaller) values are passed in a **single register** (R0–R3).
   - 64-bit values (e.g., `double`, `long long`) consume **two consecutive registers** (R0:R1 or R2:R3).

2. **Step 2 — Register Allocation (First 4 Parameters):**
   - Parameter 1 → **R0**
   - Parameter 2 → **R1**
   - Parameter 3 → **R2**
   - Parameter 4 → **R3**
   - Arguments 5+ are passed on the stack in **right-to-left order** (the last extra argument is pushed first).

3. **Step 3 — Stack Parameter Pushing:**
   - Additional parameters are stored in the **caller's stack frame**.
   - The stack is a **Full Descending (FD)** stack: pushes *decrement* SP, pops *increment* SP.
   - Before the `BL` (Branch with Link) instruction, the caller pushes extra parameters in reverse order.

4. **Step 4 — Function Call via `BL` Instruction:**
   - `BL subroutine` copies current PC + 4 into **LR (R14)** and branches to the subroutine address.
   - The callee may push LR onto the stack if it itself makes further calls (nested calls).

5. **Step 5 — Callee Frame Setup:**
   - The callee typically executes `PUSH {R4–R11, LR}` to preserve callee-saved registers and the return address.
   - It then executes `SUB SP, SP, #local_vars_size` to allocate space for **local variables**.

6. **Step 6 — Return Value Placement:**
   - For a 32-bit return: result is placed in **R0**.
   - For a 64-bit return: result is placed in **R0:R1** (low word in R0, high word in R1).
   - For larger returns (e.g., a struct), the caller allocates a memory buffer and passes a **pointer to the buffer in R0** (effectively transforming the return into an "out-parameter").

7. **Step 7 — Epilogue and Return:**
   - Callee restores callee-saved registers: `POP {R4–R11, PC}` (note: popping into PC performs the return in a single instruction).
   - Stack parameters pushed by the caller are cleaned up by the caller (caller-restored convention, unlike x86 `stdcall`).

### 2.2 Why AAPCS Uses Registers First (The Engineering Rationale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Registers are the **fastest storage** in the CPU — accessing R0 takes **1 clock cycle**, while a stack access (LDR/STR) takes **2–3 cycles** (and may cause a cache miss costing 10s of cycles). AAPCS prioritizes the first 4 parameters in registers because empirical studies on real programs (SPEC benchmarks) show that **>95% of function calls pass 4 or fewer scalar arguments**, so this design optimizes the common case.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

| **Rule ID** | **Scenario** | **AAPCS Mandate** | **Notes** |
|-------------|--------------|-------------------|-----------|
| P-01 | Parameters 1–4 (32-bit each) | R0, R1, R2, R3 respectively | Order is **positional**, left-to-right |
| P-02 | Parameter 5+ | Pushed on **Full Descending Stack** | Pushed in **right-to-left** order |
| P-03 | 64-bit parameter (e.g., `double`) | Occupies **2 consecutive registers** | R0:R1 or R2:R3 |
| P-04 | 32-bit return value | Returned in **R0** | Caller reads from R0 after return |
| P-05 | 64-bit return value | Returned in **R0:R1** | Low word in R0, high word in R1 |
| P-06 | Large struct return (>8 bytes) | Caller passes pointer in R0 | Struct written to caller's memory |
| P-07 | Stack growth direction | **Full Descending** (toward lower addr) | `PUSH` decrements SP; `POP` increments SP |
| P-08 | Caller-saved regs | **R0–R3, R12, LR** | If caller needs them after the call, it must save them itself |
| P-09 | Callee-saved regs | **R4–R11, SP** | If callee uses them, it must save & restore them |
| P-10 | Function invocation | `BL label` instruction | Sets LR = PC + 4, then branches |
| P-11 | Nested call support | Callee pushes LR onto stack | `PUSH {..., LR}` in prologue |
| P-12 | Function return | `POP {..., PC}` or `BX LR` | Popping directly into PC returns and restores SP simultaneously |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parameter passing rules underpin **every** ARM binary: from the C library `printf()` (which uses R0 for format string, R1–R3 for first 3 args, stack for rest), to **RTOS context switches** (where the scheduler must save R0–R3 along with PC), to **inter-procedure optimization** in compilers (link-time register allocation).
