Mastering AAPCS is non-negotiable for embedded systems engineers, OS kernel developers, and reverse engineers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Tracing Parameter Passing for a 5-Argument Function Call

**Problem Statement:**
Trace the parameter passing mechanism (per AAPCS) when the following C function is called from an ARM assembly program:

```c
int add5(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e);
int result = add5(10, 20, 30, 40, 50);
```

**Given Parameters (5 total — exceeds the 4-register limit):**
- Param 1 = 10
- Param 2 = 20
- Param 3 = 30
- Param 4 = 40
- Param 5 = 50 (excess → goes to stack)

**Step-by-Step Solution (Explicit Derivation):**

**Step 1 — Register Loading (Parameters 1 through 4):**

The caller loads the first four 32-bit integers directly into R0, R1, R2, and R3:

```
MOV R0, #10       ; R0 ← 10  (Param 1: a)
MOV R1, #20       ; R1 ← 20  (Param 2: b)
MOV R2, #30       ; R2 ← 30  (Param 3: c)
MOV R3, #40       ; R3 ← 40  (Param 4: d)
```

**Step 2 — Stack Push (Parameter 5, in Right-to-Left Order):**

Since 50 is the 5th argument and the stack must be loaded in **reverse order** (so that parameter 5 is at the lowest address — the first item the callee will read via [SP, #0] after the link register is pushed):

```
; Push Param 5 (50) onto Full Descending Stack
; Full Descending: SP decrements first, then stores
SUB  SP, SP, #4    ; SP = SP - 4   (allocate 4 bytes)
STR  R4, [SP]      ; [SP] ← 50     (store at new SP)
; (Assuming R4 holds 50; alternatively: LDR R4, =50; STR R4, [SP])
```

**Mathematical/Memory Address Representation:**

$$\text{New SP} = \text{Old SP} - 4$$

$$\text{Memory}[SP_{\text{new}}] = 50$$

**Step 3 — Branch with Link (BL Instruction):**

```
BL add5            ; LR ← PC + 4,  PC ← address of add5
```

**Step 4 — Callee Prologue (Stack Frame Setup):**

Upon entry to `add5`, the callee must preserve LR (so it can return correctly after its own internal work) and any callee-saved registers it intends to use:

```
add5:
    PUSH {R4–R11, LR}   ; Save callee-saved regs + return address
                         ; SP now points to saved LR
```

**Step 5 — Callee Reads Parameters:**

The callee accesses R0–R3 directly. To read parameter 5 (50), it loads from the stack. Since the caller pushed one item, and the callee pushed 9 items (R4–R11 + LR = 9 × 4 = 36 bytes), the offset to find Param 5 is:

$$\text{Offset to Param 5} = (9 \times 4) + 4 = 40 \text{ bytes from current SP}$$

Wait — let me re-derive this more carefully using AAPCS offsets.

**Re-derivation of stack offset for parameter 5:**

After the caller's `SUB SP, SP, #4` and `STR`, SP = SP_caller − 4, and `[SP] = 50`.

After the `BL` instruction, SP is unchanged (BL doesn't touch SP).

In the callee, after `PUSH {R4–R11, LR}` (9 registers × 4 bytes = 36 bytes):
$$SP_{\text{callee}} = SP_{\text{caller}} - 4 - 36 = SP_{\text{caller}} - 40$$

So to access parameter 5, the callee uses:

```
LDR R5, [SP, #40]    ; R5 ← 50  (Param 5: e)
```

**Verification:**
$$[SP_{\text{callee}} + 40] = [SP_{\text{caller}} - 40 + 40] = [SP_{\text{caller}}]$$

This correctly resolves to where 50 was stored. ✓

**Step 6 — Computation and Return:**

Suppose the callee computes `a + b + c + d + e = 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 = 150`:

```
ADD R0, R0, R1    ; R0 = a + b = 30
ADD R0, R0, R2    ; R0 = 30 + c = 60
ADD R0, R0, R3    ; R0 = 60 + d = 100
ADD R0, R0, R5    ; R0 = 100 + e = 150
```

**Step 7 — Epilogue and Return:**

```
POP {R4–R11, PC}   ; Restore callee-saved regs; PC ← LR (return)
```

**Step 8 — Caller Cleanup:**

After the call returns, the caller must remove parameter 5 from the stack (caller-restored convention):

```
ADD SP, SP, #4    ; Deallocate Param 5's slot
```

**Final Result:** R0 = 150 ✓

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: 64-bit Parameter Passing Derivation

**Problem Statement:**
Trace the AAPCS handling of a 64-bit `double` argument:

```c
double foo(double x, int y);
double result = foo(3.14, 42);
```

**Derivation:**

The `double` is 8 bytes and occupies R0:R1 (low 32 bits in R0, high 32 bits in R1). Since the next parameter `y` (32-bit) would normally be in R2, R2 is used for `y`. The sequence is:

```
; Load 3.14 into R0:R1 (assuming R0 = low word, R1 = high word)
MOV  R0, #0x51EB851F    ; Low 32 bits of 3.14 in IEEE 754
MOV  R1, #0x40091EB8    ; High 32 bits of 3.14 in IEEE 754
MOV  R2, #42            ; y in R2
BL   foo
; After return: R0:R1 holds the 64-bit double result
```

**Memory Layout Rule for 64-bit Values:**

$$R0 = \text{low word} \quad \text{and} \quad R1 = \text{high word (little-endian assumed)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Where Does This Parameter Go?"

This matrix is a high-yield visual summary of AAPCS dispatching logic for KTU exams:

| **# of Params** | **Param Sizes** | **Register Assignments** | **Stack Items** | **Total Stack Bytes Used by Args** |
|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | All 32-bit | R0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | All 32-bit | R0, R1, R2, R3 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | All 32-bit | R0, R1, R2, R3 | 1 | 4 |
| 8 | All 32-bit | R0, R1, R2, R3 | 4 | 16 |
| 2 | One 64-bit | R0:R1, R2 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | One 64-bit + one 32-bit | R0:R1, R2 | 0 (R3 unused, no alignment) | 0 |
| 4 | Two 64-bit | R0:R1, R2:R3 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | One 64-bit + three 32-bit | R0:R1, R2, R3 | 1 | 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VFP/NEON note:** If the hardware has a **Vector Floating-Point (VFP)** unit, floating-point scalars are actually passed in **S0–S15** (single-precision) or **D0–D7** (double-precision) registers, *not* in R0–R3. The integer registers (R0–R3) remain reserved for the first 4 *integer* or *memory* arguments. This is a frequent KTU exam trap!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Parameter Passing Flow (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *flow of control and data* from a caller's stack/register file into the callee, and the return path back to the caller. This is the architecture-level view requested by AAPCS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller Function Start] --> B[Load Param1 into R0]
    B --> C[Load Param2 into R1]
    C --> D[Load Param3 into R2]
    D --> E[Load Param4 into R3]
    E --> F{More than 4 Params?}
    F -- No --> H[Execute BL CalleeName]
    F -- Yes --> G[Push Param5 to ParamN on Full Descending Stack in Reverse Order]
    G --> H
    H --> I[LR receives Return Address]
    I --> J[Callee Entry Point]
    J --> K[Prologue: PUSH CalleeSavedRegs and LR]
    K --> L[Allocate Local Variables using SUB SP]
    L --> M[Callee Reads R0 to R3 as First Four Params]
    M --> N[Callee Reads Extra Params from Stack via SP Offsets]
    N --> O[Callee Performs Computation]
    O --> P[Place 32-bit Return Value in R0]
    O --> Q[Place 64-bit Return Value in R0 and R1]
    P --> R[Epilogue: POP CalleeSavedRegs and PC for Return]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Caller Receives R0 as Return Value]
    S --> T[Caller Cleans up Stack Parameters: ADD SP]
    T --> U[Caller Continues Execution]
```

### 4.2 Register Allocation Schematic for a 6-Parameter Call

This schematic shows the **state of R0–R3, the stack, and LR** immediately *before* the `BL` instruction executes, for a function `func(P1, P2, P3, P4, P5, P6)`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R0Block["R0 = P1 (32-bit)"]
        R0X[4 bytes holding P1]
    end
    subgraph R1Block["R1 = P2 (32-bit)"]
        R1X[4 bytes holding P2]
    end
    subgraph R2Block["R2 = P3 (32-bit)"]
        R2X[4 bytes holding P3]
    end
    subgraph R3Block["R3 = P4 (32-bit)"]
        R3X[4 bytes holding P4]
    end
    subgraph LRBlock["LR = Return Address"]
        LRX[Set by BL instruction]
    end
    subgraph StackBlock["Full Descending Stack (High to Low Address)"]
        S1["[OldSP] = (caller's saved data)"]
        S2["[OldSP-4] = P6 (pushed first, rightmost param)"]
        S3["[OldSP-8] = P5 (pushed second)"]
        S4["SP points here after both pushes"]
    end
    R0Block --> JUMP[BL func]
    R1Block --> JUMP
    R2Block --> JUMP
    R3Block --> JUMP
    JUMP --> LRBlock
    JUMP --> StackBlock
```

### 4.3 Stack Frame Layout Diagram (Before/After Callee Prologue)

This diagram depicts how the stack frame evolves when the callee saves its working environment. It is essential for understanding how local variables and parameters coexist in memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before["Before Callee Prologue (Just After BL)"]
        B1["Higher address: P6 = 50"]
        B2["Lower: P5 = 40"]
        B3["SP = points to P5 (last pushed by caller)"]
    end
    subgraph After["After Callee PUSH and SUB SP"]
        A1["Higher: P6 = 50"]
        A2["P5 = 40"]
        A3["Saved R11"]
        A4["Saved R10"]
        A5["Saved R9"]
        A6["Saved R8"]
        A7["Saved R7"]
        A8["Saved R6"]
        A9["Saved R5"]
        A10["Saved R4"]
        A11["Saved LR (return address)"]
        A12["Local variable space (after SUB SP, SP, #N)"]
        A13["SP = points to top of local space"]
    end
    Before --> After
```

### 4.4 AAPCS Decision Tree (Flowchart)

This flowchart encapsulates the *complete* AAPCS decision logic in a single navigable view. Use it to answer any "where does this parameter go?" question in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Function Call Encountered]) --> Q1{How many parameters?}
    Q1 -- 0 --> BL[Execute BL Instruction]
    Q1 -- 1 --> OneP[Load into R0]
    OneP --> BL
    Q1 -- 2 --> TwoP[Load into R0 and R1]
    TwoP --> BL
    Q1 -- 3 --> ThreeP[Load into R0, R1, R2]
    ThreeP --> BL
    Q1 -- 4 --> FourP[Load into R0, R1, R2, R3]
    FourP --> BL
    Q1 -- 5+ --> FivePlus[Load first 4 into R0 to R3]
    FivePlus --> StackPush[Push remaining parameters onto Full Descending Stack in Right to Left Order]
    StackPush --> BL
    BL --> Sub[Callee Begins: PUSH Callee Saved Registers and LR]
    Sub --> LocalAlloc[Allocate Local Variable Space: SUB SP, SP, #N]
    LocalAlloc --> Exec[Execute Function Body: Read Params from R0 to R3 and Stack]
    Exec --> RetVal[Place Return Value in R0 or R0 R1]
    RetVal --> Epilogue[Epilogue: POP into PC to Return]
    Epilogue([Caller Resumes])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
**State the rules defined by AAPCS for passing the first four parameters to a function in ARM. What happens to additional parameters beyond the fourth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, the first four 32-bit parameters are passed in the **core registers R0, R1, R2, and R3** respectively. The mapping is **positional**:
- 1st parameter → R0
- 2nd parameter → R1
- 3rd parameter → R2
- 4th parameter → R3

If a parameter is **64 bits** (e.g., `double`, `long long`), it occupies **two consecutive registers** (e.g., R0:R1).

For the **5th and subsequent parameters**, AAPCS mandates that they be passed on the **Full Descending Stack** (FD stack). The caller pushes them in **right-to-left order** (i.e., the last extra argument is pushed first) before issuing the `BL` instruction. The stack grows toward lower memory addresses, so `PUSH` decrements SP, and `POP` increments SP.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain how a 32-bit and a 64-bit return value are returned to the caller in AAPCS. What is the role of the LR register in a function call?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In AAPCS, the **return value** is placed by the callee in the **core registers** before the function returns:
- For a **32-bit return value** (e.g., `int`, `char`, `float`): The result is placed in **R0**.
- For a **64-bit return value** (e.g., `double`, `long long`, 64-bit pointer): The result is placed in **R0:R1** (low word in R0, high word in R1, assuming little-endian).
- For **larger structs**, the caller pre-allocates a memory buffer and passes a **pointer to it in R0**; the callee writes the struct to that memory and returns the pointer (or void).

The **LR (Link Register, R14)** plays a critical role: when the `BL subroutine` instruction is executed, the CPU **automatically stores the return address (PC + 4)** in LR and then branches to the subroutine. To return, the callee either executes `BX LR` (branch to LR) or, more efficiently, pops LR's saved copy directly into PC using `POP {..., PC}`. For nested calls, the callee must first push LR onto the stack to preserve the original return address.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most students lose 2–3 marks** by confusing the **direction of stack growth** — ARM uses a **Full Descending** stack (grows toward lower addresses), NOT an ascending stack. PUSH decrements SP.
> 2. **Forgetting that the caller cleans up stack arguments** is a common 2-mark error. ARM uses **caller-restored** cleanup (unlike x86 `stdcall`). After the callee returns, the caller must `ADD SP, SP, #N`.
> 3. **Mixing up callee-saved vs caller-saved registers** loses 1–2 marks. Memorize: R0–R3, R12, LR = **caller-saved**; R4–R11, SP = **callee-saved**.
> 4. **Ignoring 64-bit alignment rules** for stack parameters is another 1-mark pitfall. 64-bit values on the stack must be **8-byte aligned**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** in detail. Describe the rules for passing **up to four 32-bit parameters** and how the **Full Descending Stack** is used for additional parameters. Illustrate with a code example for a function call `sum(a, b, c, d, e)`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **caller-saved** and **callee-saved** registers in AAPCS. Why is this distinction necessary? Show a sample assembly code segment that demonstrates the **preservation of callee-saved registers** during a nested function call. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — AAPCS Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** is a standardized calling convention that ensures interoperability between separately compiled ARM assembly and C modules. Its primary rules for parameter passing are:

**Rule 1 — First Four 32-bit Parameters Use Registers:**
The first four 32-bit parameters are passed in registers **R0, R1, R2, and R3** in left-to-right order. This is a deliberate design choice to leverage the speed of register access (1 cycle) over memory access (2–3+ cycles).

**Rule 2 — Stack Used for Parameter 5+:**
The 5th parameter and beyond are placed on the **Full Descending Stack** by the caller. The stack grows toward lower memory addresses. Arguments are pushed in **right-to-left order** so that the leftmost extra argument is at the highest address (closest to SP after all pushes).

**Rule 3 — Stack Cleanup by Caller:**
After the callee returns, the caller is responsible for removing the pushed stack arguments (this is the **caller-restored** convention).

**Code Example for `sum(a, b, c, d, e)` where a=10, b=20, c=30, d=40, e=50:**

```assembly
    AREA    SumExample, CODE, READONLY
    ENTRY
    EXPORT  main

main PROC
    ; Load first 4 parameters into R0-R3
    MOV   R0, #10           ; a = 10 in R0
    MOV   R1, #20           ; b = 20 in R1
    MOV   R2, #30           ; c = 30 in R2
    MOV   R3, #40           ; d = 40 in R3

    ; Push 5th parameter (e=50) onto Full Descending Stack
    MOV   R4, #50
    SUB   SP, SP, #4        ; SP = SP - 4 (allocate 4 bytes)
    STR   R4, [SP]          ; [SP] = 50 (e)

    BL    sum               ; Call sum; LR = PC+4

    ; After return, R0 holds the sum
    ; Caller cleans up stack argument
    ADD   SP, SP, #4        ; Deallocate e's slot

    ; R0 now contains the result (10+20+30+40+50 = 150)
    B     .                 ; Infinite loop / end

sum PROC
    PUSH  {R4, R5, LR}      ; Prologue: save callee-saved regs + LR
    ; Read e from stack. Offset = 3 saved regs * 4 = 12 bytes
    LDR   R5, [SP, #12]     ; R5 = e (50)
    ADD   R0, R0, R1        ; R0 = a + b
    ADD   R0, R0, R2        ; R0 += c
    ADD   R0, R0, R3        ; R0 += d
    ADD   R0, R0, R5        ; R0 += e; R0 = 150
    POP   {R4, R5, PC}      ; Epilogue: restore regs and return
    ENDP
    END
```

**Memory State Immediately Before `BL sum` (High to Low Address):**
| Address | Content |
|---------|---------|
| Higher | (caller's saved data) |
| [SP+8] | (uninitialized) |
| [SP+4] | (uninitialized) |
| **[SP]** | **50 (Param 5: e)** ← SP points here |

**(b) — Caller-Saved vs Callee-Saved Registers (7 marks):**

The distinction between caller-saved and callee-saved registers exists to **define responsibility for preservation**, avoiding redundant saves in deeply nested call chains and enabling compiler optimizations.

| Register Type | List | Preservation Responsibility |
|---------------|------|------------------------------|
| **Caller-saved** | R0–R3, R12, LR | **Caller** must save them before the call if it still needs their values afterward |
| **Callee-saved** | R4–R11, SP | **Callee** must save them at entry and restore them at exit if it uses them |

**Why is this distinction necessary?**
1. **Efficiency:** It avoids unnecessary register saves. If a register is rarely needed across calls, the caller can simply not save it. Conversely, registers used for long-lived data (like loop counters in R4–R11) are guaranteed to survive calls.
2. **Modularity:** It allows the compiler to allocate registers independently in each function without coordinating with all possible callers.
3. **Predictability:** It provides a contract — programmers can trust that R4–R11 retain their values across function calls (e.g., R11 is often used as the **frame pointer**).

**Sample Assembly Demonstrating Preservation During Nested Call:**

```assembly
outer_func PROC
    PUSH   {R4-R8, LR}          ; Save callee-saved regs and LR
    MOV    R4, #100             ; R4 holds long-lived data
    MOV    R5, #200             ; R5 holds long-lived data
    ; ... prepare R0-R3 for inner_func call ...
    MOV    R0, #1
    MOV    R1, #2
    BL     inner_func           ; Call inner_func
    ; After return, R4 and R5 are GUARANTEED unchanged
    ; because inner_func preserved them
    ADD    R4, R4, R0           ; Safe to use R4 after the call
    POP    {R4-R8, PC}          ; Restore and return
    ENDP

inner_func PROC
    PUSH   {R4, LR}             ; Save what inner_func will USE
    ; ... work that may trash R4 ...
    MOV    R4, #999             ; Clobber R4 freely
    ; ... compute result in R0 ...
    POP    {R4, PC}             ; Restore R4 and return
    ENDP
```

In this example, `outer_func` does not need to save R4/R5 before calling `inner_func` because `inner_func` is contractually obligated to preserve them.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **complete stack frame layout** for an ARM function call involving **3 register parameters** and **2 stack parameters**. Show the state of memory **before** the `BL` instruction, **after** the callee's prologue, and **after** the callee's epilogue. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **64-bit parameters and return values** are handled in AAPCS. Provide a code example showing the passing of a `double` value as a parameter and its return to the caller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Stack Frame Layout (7 marks):**

Assume a function call `compute(P1, P2, P3, P4_stack, P5_stack)` where P1, P2, P3 are 32-bit ints and P4_stack, P5_stack are 32-bit ints pushed onto the stack.

**Phase 1 — Before BL (Caller State):**

```
Higher Memory Address
+----------------------+
| Caller's local data  |
+----------------------+
| (caller's saved regs)|  ← if any
+----------------------+
| P5_stack (rightmost) |  ← [SP + 4]
+----------------------+
| P4_stack             |  ← [SP]  (SP points here)
+----------------------+
Lower Memory Address

Register State:
  R0 = P1, R1 = P2, R2 = P3, R3 = (unused)
  LR = (will be set by BL)
```

**Phase 2 — After Callee's Prologue `PUSH {R4-R8, LR}` and `SUB SP, SP, #8`:**

```
Higher Memory Address
+----------------------+
| Caller's local data  |
+----------------------+
| P5_stack (rightmost) |  ← [SP + 24]
+----------------------+
| P4_stack             |  ← [SP + 20]
+----------------------+
| Saved R8             |  ← [SP + 16]
+----------------------+
| Saved R7             |  ← [SP + 12]
+----------------------+
| Saved R6             |  ← [SP + 8]
+----------------------+
| Saved R5             |  ← [SP + 4]
+----------------------+
| Saved R4             |  ← [SP]  (LR was saved just before R4 conceptually)
+----------------------+
| Saved LR             |  ← [SP - 4] (before SUB SP)
+----------------------+
| Local var 1          |  ← [SP - 4] (after SUB SP, SP, #8)
+----------------------+
| Local var 2          |  ← [SP - 8]  ← SP now points here
+----------------------+
Lower Memory Address
```

The callee reads P4_stack at offset `[SP, #20]` and P5_stack at `[SP, #24]`.

**Phase 3 — After Callee's Epilogue `POP {R4-R8, PC}`:**

```
Higher Memory Address
+----------------------+
| P5_stack             |  ← still in memory (caller's job to clean)
+----------------------+
| P4_stack             |  ← still in memory
+----------------------+
Lower Memory Address
```

The callee-saved registers are restored, PC receives LR's saved value, and SP returns to the point right after P4_stack. The caller then executes `ADD SP, SP, #8` to remove P4_stack and P5_stack from the stack.

**(b) — 64-bit Parameter & Return Handling (7 marks):**

A 64-bit value (e.g., `double`, `long long`) consumes **two consecutive 32-bit registers** in AAPCS. The low 32 bits are placed in the **even-numbered** register and the high 32 bits in the **odd-numbered** register. For example, a 64-bit first parameter occupies **R0:R1**.

**Code Example: Passing a `double` Parameter and Returning a `double`:**

```c
double addDouble(double a, double b) {
    return a + b;
}

double result = addDouble(3.14, 2.71);
```

**Corresponding ARM Assembly:**

```assembly
    AREA    DoubleExample, CODE, READONLY
    ENTRY
    EXPORT  _start

_start
    ; Load 3.14 into R0:R1 (low word in R0, high word in R1)
    MOV   R0, #0x51EB851F    ; Low 32 bits of 3.14 (IEEE 754)
    MOV   R1, #0x40091EB8    ; High 32 bits of 3.14

    ; Load 2.71 into R2:R3
    MOV   R2, #0x70A3D70A    ; Low 32 bits of 2.71
    MOV   R3, #0x4005AC4C    ; High 32 bits of 2.71

    BL    addDouble          ; Call addDouble

    ; After return, R0:R1 holds the 64-bit double result
    B     .                  ; End

addDouble PROC
    PUSH  {R4, LR}           ; Prologue
    ; a is in R0:R1, b is in R2:R3
    ; For 64-bit addition, we need to add the low and high words
    ; separately and handle carry. Simplified here:
    ADD   R0, R0, R2         ; Low word:  a.low + b.low
    ADC   R1, R1, R3         ; High word: a.high + b.high + carry
    ; Result in R0:R1
    POP   {R4, PC}           ; Epilogue and return
    ENDP
    END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key rules for 64-bit values:**
> 1. They occupy **two consecutive registers** (low word first).
> 2. When passed on the stack, they must be **8-byte aligned**.
> 3. If using the **VFP coprocessor**, doubles are passed in **D0–D7** instead of R0–R3.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **AAPCS = ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard** — the universal calling convention for ARM.
- ✅ **First 4 parameters** (32-bit) → **R0, R1, R2, R3** in that order.
- ✅ **5th parameter onwards** → pushed onto the **Full Descending Stack** in **right-to-left order**.
- ✅ **64-bit parameter** → occupies **2 consecutive registers** (e.g., R0:R1), low word in the even register.
- ✅ **32-bit return value** → in **R0**.
- ✅ **64-bit return value** → in **R0:R1** (low in R0, high in R1).
- ✅ **Large struct return** → caller passes a pointer in R0; callee writes to the buffer.
- ✅ **Stack direction** → **Full Descending** (grows toward lower addresses; PUSH decrements SP, POP increments SP).
- ✅ **Caller-saved registers** → **R0–R3, R12, LR** — caller must save if needed.
- ✅ **Callee-saved registers** → **R4–R11, SP** — callee must save and restore if used.
- ✅ **Function call** → `BL label` sets LR = PC + 4, then branches.
- ✅ **Function return** → `POP {..., PC}` is the most efficient method (restores SP and returns in one instruction).
- ✅ **Stack cleanup** → **caller restores** the stack arguments after the call returns.
- ✅ **LR preservation in nested calls** → callee must `PUSH {..., LR}` if it makes further calls.
- ✅ **VFP note** → floating-point scalars use **S0–S15** or **D0–D7**, not R0–R3, when VFP is enabled.
- ✅ **Stack alignment** → 64-bit stack parameters must be **8-byte aligned**.
- ✅ **Frame pointer** → R11 is conventionally used as the frame pointer in debug builds.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Return Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Return Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In ARM Architecture, **Return Values** refer to the mechanism by which a function (or subroutine) communicates its computed result back to the calling function. According to the **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)**, return values are passed through dedicated general-purpose registers rather than via the stack, ensuring maximum efficiency in register-rich RISC architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAPCS Standard:** In the **32-bit ARM (AArch32)** state, return values of up to a **32-bit** integer are placed in register **R0**. For **64-bit** integer return values, **R0 holds the lower 32 bits** and **R1 holds the upper 32 bits**. In the **64-bit AArch64** state, register **X0** is used (with W0 for 32-bit).

### Register Allocation for Return Values

| Architecture | 32-bit Return | 64-bit Return | Up to 4 Words |
|--------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| **AArch32 (ARMv7)** | **R0** | **R0:R1** | **R0–R3** |
| **AArch64 (ARMv8)** | **W0 (X0 lower 32)** | **X0** | **X0–X3** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Courier Service:**
> Imagine you send a courier to pick up a package from a warehouse. The courier (the **callee function**) brings back the package (the **return value**) and hands it to you (the **caller function**) in a standardized box. In ARM, this "standardized box" is always **R0** (or **X0** in 64-bit mode). If the package is too big for one box, additional boxes (**R1**, **R2**, **R3**) are used in a predefined order. This convention ensures the caller always knows where to look for the result without searching through memory — just like a courier always knows which hand to use to hand over the package.

## 1.3 Physical & Architectural Constants

- **No. of Return Registers (AArch32):** **4** (R0, R1, R2, R3)
- **No. of Return Registers (AArch64):** **8** (X0–X7)
- **Composite Returns:** Structures larger than **4 words** use memory pointed to by an implicit pointer passed in **R0/X0** (caller-allocated buffer)
- **Floating Point Returns:** **S0/D0/V0** in VFP register file
- **Standard Mandate:** All registers except **R0–R3, R12, LR, SP** are **callee-saved** (must be preserved)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under AAPCS, if a function returns a structure larger than 4 words, the *caller* allocates memory, places a pointer to that memory in **R0** before the call, and the *callee* writes the result into that memory location.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The return value mechanism in ARM follows a strict sequence:

**Step 1 — Caller Setup:**
- The caller places function arguments in **R0–R3** (AArch32) or **X0–X7** (AArch64).
- For large composite returns, the caller allocates a buffer in its own stack frame and places the buffer address in **R0/X0**.
- The caller saves the **Link Register (LR)** if needed (via `BL` or `PUSH {LR}`).

**Step 2 — Branch with Link (`BL` instruction):**
- The processor jumps to the callee and stores the return address in **LR (R14)**.
- Program flow enters the callee.

**Step 3 — Callee Execution:**
- The callee preserves any **callee-saved registers** it modifies (R4–R11, SP, LR).
- The callee performs its computation and prepares the result.

**Step 4 — Result Placement:**
- The result is placed in the designated return register(s) **BEFORE** returning.
- For integers ≤ 32 bits → **R0** (or **W0**)
- For 64-bit integers → **R0:R1** (or **X0**)
- For floating-point → **S0/D0**

**Step 5 — Return Instruction:**
- `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` returns control to the caller.
- The caller reads the return value from the conventional register.

## 2.2 Why This Convention? — The "Why" Behind AAPCS

- **Performance:** RISC architectures have abundant registers; using them avoids costly memory accesses.
- **Predictability:** A fixed contract allows the compiler to optimize aggressively.
- **Pipeline Efficiency:** The CPU can dispatch return-value usage immediately after the function returns, with no memory stall.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Return Type | Size (bits) | AArch32 Register | AArch64 Register | Notes |
|-------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|-------|
| Integer / Pointer | **32** | **R0** | **W0** | Most common case |
| Long Integer | **64** | **R0:R1** | **X0** | R0 = low, R1 = high |
| Float | **32** | **S0** (VFP) | **S0** | IEEE 754 single |
| Double | **64** | **D0** (VFP) | **D0** | IEEE 754 double |
| Small Struct (≤ 4 words) | **≤ 128** | **R0–R3** | **X0–X3** | Packed sequentially |
| Large Struct (> 4 words) | **> 128** | **Memory via R0** | **Memory via X0** | Caller-allocated buffer |
| 128-bit SIMD | **128** | **Q0 / D0:D1** | **V0** | NEON return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** If the return value is a **128-bit (16-byte) structure**, it is still returned in registers **R0–R3 (AArch32)** / **X0–X3 (AArch64)** because AAPCS guarantees up to a "doubleword-aligned 4-word" register return.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems Firmware:** Function return optimization is critical in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, where deterministic cycle counts are essential.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Register-based returns reduce latency in tight computational loops.
- **Operating System Kernels:** AAPCS compliance is mandatory for system calls (SVC) and kernel/user-mode transitions.
- **Compiler Design:** GCC's ARM backend and LLVM both implement AAPCS for ABI compatibility.
- **IoT & Edge Devices:** Reduced memory access directly translates to lower power consumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — 32-bit Integer Return (AArch32)

**Problem:** Write an ARM assembly function that returns the square of an integer passed as argument, following AAPCS.

### Caller Code

```assembly
        MOV     R0, #5            @ Argument: n = 5
        BL      SQUARE            @ Call the function
        @ Result is now in R0
```

### Callee Code

```assembly
SQUARE  PUSH    {LR}              @ Preserve return address
        MUL     R0, R0, R0        @ R0 = R0 * R0  (square)
        MOV     PC, LR            @ Return (R0 holds result)
```

### Mathematical Representation

The function $f(n) = n^2$ is computed as:

$$
f(n) = n \times n
$$

After the `BL SQUARE` instruction, the result resides in R0:

$$
R0_{\text{post-call}} = n^2 = 5^2 = 25
$$

**Step-by-step analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-call state:} \quad & R0 = 5, \quad LR = \text{return\_address} \\
\text{After MUL instruction:} \quad & R0 \leftarrow R0 \times R0 = 5 \times 5 = 25 \\
\text{After MOV PC, LR:} \quad & PC \leftarrow LR, \quad R0 = 25 \text{ (preserved)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 64-bit Integer Return (AArch32)

**Problem:** Multiply two 32-bit numbers to produce a 64-bit result.

### Derivation of the Multiplication

Given two unsigned 32-bit numbers $a$ and $b$ in R0 and R1 respectively, the 64-bit product $p = a \times b$ is split as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= a \times b \\
p_{\text{low32}} &= (a \times b) \mod 2^{32} \\
p_{\text{high32}} &= \lfloor (a \times b) / 2^{32} \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

### Assembly Implementation

```assembly
        UMULL   R0, R1, R0, R1     @ R0 = low 32 bits, R1 = high 32 bits
        BX      LR                 @ Return with 64-bit result in R0:R1
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

Suppose $a = 0x00010000$ and $b = 0x00010000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a \times b &= 0x00010000 \times 0x00010000 \\
           &= 2^{16} \times 2^{16} = 2^{32} \\
           &= 0x0000000100000000
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the register state after `UMULL` becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0_{\text{low32}} &= 0x00000000 \\
R1_{\text{high32}} &= 0x00000001
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the AAPCS convention: **R0 = low word, R1 = high word**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — AArch64 (64-bit) Return

```assembly
square64:
        MUL     X0, X0, X0          @ X0 = X0 * X0 (full 64-bit multiply)
        RET                        @ Return to caller, result in X0
```

Mathematically:

$$
X0_{\text{result}} = X0_{\text{input}} \times X0_{\text{input}} = n^2
$$

For $n = 0x1\,0000\,0000$ (i.e., $2^{32}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X0_{\text{result}} &= (2^{32})^2 = 2^{64} \\
                   &= 0x1\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the 64-bit result fits naturally in **X0** without needing a second register, unlike the AArch32 case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Return Types

$$
\text{Return Strategy} =
\begin{cases}
R0 / W0, & \text{if size} \leq 32 \text{ bits (integer/pointer)} \\
R0:R1 / X0, & \text{if size} = 64 \text{ bits (long/pointer)} \\
R0 \to R3 / X0 \to X3, & \text{if size} \leq 128 \text{ bits (small struct)} \\
\text{Memory via } R0 / X0, & \text{if size} > 128 \text{ bits (large struct)} \\
S0 \text{ or } D0, & \text{if floating-point return} \\
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.5 Composite Type Return — Stack Memory Mechanism

For structures exceeding 4 words (16 bytes), the steps are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Caller allocates buffer of size } S \text{ bytes on its stack} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Caller stores buffer address in } R0 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Caller issues BL to callee} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Callee writes result into memory}[R0] \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Callee returns; caller reads from its own buffer}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The buffer is owned by the caller, ensuring the callee never has to allocate or deallocate memory — a clean ownership model that prevents leaks in low-level code.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Return Value Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller Function] -->|Step 1: Place arguments in R0-R3| B[Execute BL instruction]
    B -->|Step 2: Save return address in LR| C[Callee Function Entry]
    C -->|Step 3: PUSH callee-saved registers| D[Callee Stack Frame Setup]
    D -->|Step 4: Perform computation| E[Computation Logic]
    E -->|Step 5: Place result in R0 or R0:R1| F[Result Register]
    F -->|Step 6: POP and BX LR| G[Return to Caller]
    G -->|Step 7: Read result from R0| H[Caller Consumes Return Value]
    H -->|Step 8: R4-R11 restored if needed| I[Execution Continues]
```

## 4.2 AAPCS Register Roles

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Caller_Side
        CA[Caller Stack Frame]
        CB[Caller Buffer for large struct]
    end
    subgraph Register_File
        R0[R0 - Arg0 / Return Value]
        R1[R1 - Arg1 / Return High 32]
        R2[R2 - Arg2]
        R3[R3 - Arg3]
        R4[R4 - Callee Saved]
        R5[R5 - Callee Saved]
        R6[R6 - Callee Saved]
        R7[R7 - Callee Saved]
        R8[R8 - Callee Saved]
        R9[R9 - Callee Saved]
        R10[R10 - Callee Saved]
        R11[R11 - Frame Pointer]
        R12[R12 - IP Scratch]
        R13[R13 - SP Stack Pointer]
        R14[R14 - LR Link Register]
        R15[R15 - PC Program Counter]
    end
    CA -->|Allocates| CB
    CB -->|Address passed| R0
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Return Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Function needs to return value] --> Q1{What is the data type?}
    Q1 -->|32-bit int/ptr| P1[Place in R0 or W0]
    Q1 -->|64-bit int| P2[R0:R1 or X0]
    Q1 -->|float| P3[S0]
    Q1 -->|double| P4[D0]
    Q1 -->|struct <= 16 bytes| P5[R0 to R3 or X0 to X3]
    Q1 -->|struct > 16 bytes| P6[Caller allocates buffer, pointer in R0]
    P6 --> P6a[Callee writes to memory]
    P1 --> End[Return via BX LR or RET]
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
    P6a --> End
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout for Large Struct Return

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Caller_Stack
        Dir1[Lower Address]
        Buf[Result Buffer 16 bytes]
        Dir2[Higher Address]
    end
    Buf -->|R0 points here| Callee
    Callee -->|Writes struct fields| Buf
    Buf -->|Caller reads after return| Use[Use Returned Data]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: According to AAPCS, where is a 32-bit integer return value placed in ARM architecture? What about a 64-bit integer?**

**Model Answer:**

According to the ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS):
- A **32-bit integer** (or pointer) return value is placed in register **R0** in AArch32, or **W0** (lower 32 bits of X0) in AArch64.
- A **64-bit integer** return value is placed in **R0:R1** in AArch32 (R0 holds the lower 32 bits, R1 holds the upper 32 bits), or in the single 64-bit register **X0** in AArch64.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**R0** is the **R**eturn **R**egister" — always the first place a caller looks after a function returns.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: How does AAPCS handle the return of a structure larger than 4 words (16 bytes)?**

**Model Answer:**

For structures larger than 16 bytes, AAPCS mandates a **caller-allocated buffer** mechanism:
1. The **caller** allocates a memory buffer in its own stack frame large enough to hold the result.
2. The caller places the **address of this buffer** in **R0** (AArch32) or **X0** (AArch64) before issuing the `BL` call.
3. The **callee** treats this implicit pointer as its destination and writes the structure directly into the caller's buffer.
4. After the call returns, the caller reads the data from its own local buffer.

This approach keeps memory ownership clean and avoids dynamic allocation in low-level code.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ARM AAPCS convention for return values, covering integer types (32-bit and 64-bit), floating-point types, and small composite structures. Include the register allocations for both AArch32 and AArch64.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an ARM assembly subroutine that takes a 32-bit unsigned integer N in R0 and returns the value of N × (N+1) / 2 (i.e., the sum 1+2+...+N) as a 64-bit result. Show how the AAPCS convention is followed for the 64-bit return.

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — AAPCS Return Value Convention [7 Marks]

The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** defines a uniform contract for function returns to enable interoperability between separately compiled modules.

**Integer Return Values:**

For **32-bit integers**, the return value resides in **R0** (AArch32) or **W0** (AArch64). This is the most common case for `int`, `char`, short, pointer, and enumeration types.

For **64-bit integers** (`long long` or `doubleword`), in AArch32 the result occupies **R0:R1**, where **R0 holds the lower 32 bits** and **R1 holds the upper 32 bits**. In AArch64, the full 64-bit result fits in **X0**.

**Floating-Point Return Values:**

Floating-point results are returned in the VFP/NEON register file rather than general-purpose registers. A 32-bit `float` returns in **S0**; a 64-bit `double` returns in **D0** (which is an alias for the lower 64 bits of **Q0**). This separation prevents floating-point data from corrupting integer pipelines and allows SIMD optimization.

**Small Composite Structures:**

A structure (or `struct`/`union`) of size **≤ 16 bytes** is returned in registers **R0–R3** (AArch32) or **X0–X3** (AArch64), packed in little-endian word order. For example, a 12-byte struct occupies R0, R1, R2.

| Data Type | AArch32 Register | AArch64 Register |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| 32-bit integer | R0 | W0 |
| 64-bit integer | R0:R1 | X0 |
| float | S0 | S0 |
| double | D0 | D0 |
| Small struct (≤16 B) | R0–R3 | X0–X3 |

[7 marks — full marks awarded for: register table, AArch32 vs AArch64 distinction, FP register separation, composite rule]

#### Part (b) — Sum 1+2+...+N with 64-bit return [7 Marks]

**Problem:** Compute $S = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ for unsigned 32-bit N, returning a 64-bit result.

**Algorithm:**
1. Compute $T = N \times (N+1)$ as a 64-bit value (using `UMULL`).
2. Divide T by 2 to get the sum.

**Assembly Code:**

```assembly
        AREA    SumFormula, CODE, READONLY
        EXPORT  compute_sum

compute_sum
        PUSH    {LR}                  @ Preserve return address
        ADD     R1, R0, #1            @ R1 = N + 1
        UMULL   R0, R1, R0, R1        @ R0:R1 = N * (N+1)  [64-bit result]
        LSR     R0, R0, #1            @ R0 = R0 / 2 (lower half)
        ADC     R1, R1, #0            @ R1 = R1 + carry from LSR
        POP     {PC}                  @ Return; result in R0:R1

        END
```

**Step-by-step derivation for N = 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= N \times (N+1) = 5 \times 6 = 30 \\
\text{64-bit representation:} \quad & 30 = 0x0000001E \quad \Rightarrow \quad R0 = 0x0000001E,\; R1 = 0x00000000 \\
\text{After LSR R0, #1:} \quad & R0 = 0x0000000F = 15 \\
\text{Carry from LSR} &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R1 \text{ unchanged} = 0 \\
\text{Final 64-bit result:} \quad & R0:R1 = 0x00000000\,0000000F = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $S = 1+2+3+4+5 = 15$ ✓

**AAPCS Compliance:**
- 64-bit result correctly placed in **R0:R1** (R0 = low, R1 = high).
- **LR** preserved via `PUSH` and restored via `POP {PC}`.
- No callee-saved registers (R4–R11) are modified, so no additional saving is required.

[7 marks — full marks for: correct use of `UMULL`, proper R0:R1 split, AAPCS compliance, working code, verification]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the mechanism used in AAPCS for returning structures larger than 4 words. Draw a memory diagram showing the caller stack, the buffer, and the register passing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an ARM assembly function `big_struct_func` that returns a structure of 5 words (20 bytes). Write the complete caller and callee code, showing how the caller allocates the buffer, passes the pointer, and the callee writes the result.

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Large Structure Return Mechanism [7 Marks]

When a structure exceeds 16 bytes (4 words), it is too large to fit in the general-purpose return registers R0–R3. AAPCS therefore employs a **caller-allocated, callee-populated** memory mechanism.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Caller Buffer Allocation:** The caller reserves a contiguous block of memory in its own stack frame, with size at least equal to the structure size (often rounded up for alignment).

2. **Pointer Passing:** The address of this buffer is loaded into **R0** (AArch32) or **X0** (AArch64) — overwriting whatever argument would normally be in R0. If actual arguments exist, they are shifted to R1–R3/X1–X3.

3. **Implicit Contract:** The function signature is silently augmented: the first "argument" is now a hidden output pointer.

4. **Callee Writes Result:** Inside the callee, the code treats R0/X0 as a memory pointer and uses `STR` instructions to write the structure fields.

5. **Return:** On return, the caller already has direct access to the populated buffer.

**Memory Diagram (Caller Stack):**

```
Higher Address
+-------------------+
|  Saved LR         |
+-------------------+
|  Saved R4–R11     |   <- Caller's saved registers
+-------------------+
|  Return Buffer    |   <- 20 bytes (struct size)
|  [Word 0]         |   R0 points HERE before BL
|  [Word 1]         |
|  [Word 2]         |
|  [Word 3]         |
|  [Word 4]         |
+-------------------+
|  Other locals     |
+-------------------+
Lower Address (SP points here after PUSH)
```

[7 marks — full marks for: clear description of 5 steps, explicit mention of caller ownership, correct register usage, drawn or described diagram]

#### Part (b) — Implementation of 5-Word Struct Return [7 Marks]

**Problem:** `big_struct_func` returns a 5-word (20-byte) structure.

**Caller Code:**

```assembly
        AREA    CallerCode, CODE, READONLY
        IMPORT  big_struct_func

        LDR     R13, =StackTop        @ Initialize SP
        SUB     SP, SP, #24           @ Reserve 24 bytes (20 + 4 alignment)
        MOV     R0, SP                @ R0 = address of result buffer
        BL      big_struct_func       @ Call function (struct ptr in R0)
        @ After return, the 5-word struct is at [SP]
        LDR     R4, [SP, #0]          @ Read word 0
        LDR     R5, [SP, #4]          @ Read word 1
        LDR     R6, [SP, #8]          @ Read word 2
        LDR     R7, [SP, #12]         @ Read word 3
        LDR     R8, [SP, #16]         @ Read word 4

        ADD     SP, SP, #24           @ Clean up buffer
        ...
StackTop  DCD   0x40001000
```

**Callee Code:**

```assembly
        AREA    CalleeCode, CODE, READONLY
        EXPORT  big_struct_func

big_struct_func
        PUSH    {R4-R11, LR}          @ Save callee-saved registers
        @ R0 = pointer to result buffer
        MOV     R4, #0x11111111
        MOV     R5, #0x22222222
        MOV     R6, #0x33333333
        MOV     R7, #0x44444444
        MOV     R8, #0x55555555
        STR     R4, [R0, #0]          @ Write word 0 to buffer
        STR     R5, [R0, #4]          @ Write word 1
        STR     R6, [R0, #8]          @ Write word 2
        STR     R7, [R0, #12]         @ Write word 3
        STR     R8, [R0, #16]         @ Write word 4
        POP     {R4-R11, PC}          @ Return
```

**Explanation of AAPCS Compliance:**

- The **caller** owns the buffer at `[SP]` and passes its address in **R0**.
- The **callee** writes 5 words sequentially into the buffer at offsets 0, 4, 8, 12, 16.
- All **callee-saved registers** (R4–R11) are properly preserved using `PUSH`/`POP`.
- **LR** is preserved via `POP {..., PC}` which simultaneously restores and returns.
- No global state is corrupted — the function is re-entrant and safe for nested calls.

[7 marks — full marks for: complete caller code, complete callee code, explicit AAPCS rules followed, correct buffer offset math, register preservation]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting LR preservation** in leaf vs non-leaf functions. A non-leaf function (one that calls other functions) MUST save LR via `PUSH {LR}` because the nested `BL` will overwrite it. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Wrong byte order** when writing multi-word structs. AAPCS mandates **little-endian** word layout; swapping high/low causes silent logic errors worth 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing R0 with W0/X0** in mixed ARM/Thumb contexts. In Thumb-2, R0 is still R0, but in AArch64 the distinction between W0 (32-bit zero-extended) and X0 (64-bit) is critical.
> 4. **Buffer size miscalculation** — using `SUB SP, #20` instead of `#24` causes 4-byte alignment violations and stack corruption. AAPCS requires 8-byte stack alignment.
> 5. **Forgetting callee-saved registers** R4–R11 — modifying them without saving destroys the caller's state and is an immediate 3-mark deduction in KTU grading.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **R0 is the primary return register** for 32-bit values in AArch32; **W0/X0** in AArch64.
- **R0:R1 (or X0 alone)** is used for 64-bit integer returns, with **R0 = low word, R1 = high word**.
- **S0/D0** are used for floating-point returns in the VFP/NEON register file.
- **Structures ≤ 16 bytes** are returned in **R0–R3** (AArch32) or **X0–X3** (AArch64).
- **Structures > 16 bytes** use a **caller-allocated buffer**, with the pointer passed in **R0/X0**.
- **AAPCS** mandates that **R0–R3, R12, LR, SP** are **caller-saved** (scratch); **R4–R11** are **callee-saved**.
- **LR (R14)** must be saved explicitly using `PUSH {LR}` in any non-leaf function.
- **Stack alignment** must be maintained at **8-byte boundaries** at all public function entry/exit points.
- **Return instructions** are `BX LR` (AArch32), `RET` (AArch64), or `POP {PC}` for combined restore-and-return.
- **AAPCS compliance** is a contract between compiler, linker, and programmer — violating it causes silent data corruption, not crashes, making bugs extremely hard to debug.
- **High-yield for KTU:** Questions frequently test the **R0:R1 split** for 64-bit returns and the **caller-allocated buffer** mechanism for large structs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporary Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temporary Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Temporary Variable (Definition as per KTU 2024 Scheme / AAPCS Standard):**  
> A *temporary variable* in ARM assembly programming is a **short-lived storage location** — typically a CPU **register** or a **stack-allocated slot** — that is used to hold an **intermediate result** during the evaluation of an expression, the execution of a function, or the execution of a single instruction. Temporary variables exist only for the duration of their immediate computational use and are discarded (or overwritten) once their value has been consumed.

In the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, temporary variables are most commonly associated with the **caller-saved (volatile / scratch) registers** — namely **r0, r1, r2, r3**, and **r12 (IP)**. These registers are explicitly designated as *temporary* because:

- Their contents are **not preserved across a function call** (i.e., the called function is *not* obligated to save/restore them).
- They are typically used to pass **function arguments (r0–r3)**, return **results (r0)**, and act as **intra-procedure scratch space (r12)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuitive Explanation — The "Sticky Note on a Desk" Analogy:**  
> Imagine you are solving a long arithmetic problem on paper. You write the intermediate result of the first multiplication on a small **sticky note** placed on the corner of your desk — you do not file it in a cabinet, you do not give it a permanent label, and you know you will **throw it away** as soon as the final answer is computed.  
>  
> That sticky note is your **temporary variable**.  
>  
> In ARM, a CPU **register** is exactly that sticky note: ultra-fast, immediately accessible, and meant to hold a *throwaway* value during a calculation. The **stack frame** acts as a secondary, slightly slower sticky note — used when there are too many simultaneous values to fit in registers alone.

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Number of temporary (scratch) registers in ARM AAPCS:** **5** (r0, r1, r2, r3, r12).
- **Number of general-purpose registers in ARM:** **13** visible user registers (r0–r12).
- **Register access time:** ~**1 CPU clock cycle** (negligible latency).
- **Stack access time:** **2–3+ CPU clock cycles** (memory latency).
- **Standard temporary slot size on stack:** **32 bits (4 bytes)** in ARM state, **64 bits (8 bytes)** in Thumb-2 state for 64-bit temporaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests students on the **classification of registers (temporary vs. variable)**, the **lifetime of temporary variables**, and the **compiler's strategy for allocating temporaries** in expressions and function prologues/epilogues.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Temporary Variables Are Needed

When the ARM compiler (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc` or Keil ARMCC) translates a high-level expression such as:

```c
int y = (a * b) + (c - d);
```

into ARM assembly, it must:

1. Compute `a * b` and store the intermediate result somewhere → **Temp1**.
2. Compute `c - d` and store the intermediate result somewhere → **Temp2**.
3. Add `Temp1 + Temp2` and place the final result in `y`.

If there is not enough register space, intermediate results must be **spilled to the stack**. Each spilled value is a **stack-allocated temporary variable**.

## 2.2 Categories of Temporary Variables

| # | Category | Storage Location | Lifetime | Preserved Across Call? |
|---|----------|------------------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Register Temporary** | r0–r3, r12 (scratch regs) | One expression / basic block | **NO** (caller-saved) |
| 2 | **Stack Temporary (Spill)** | Stack frame (push/pop) | One expression / basic block | **NO** (caller-saved) |
| 3 | **Loop Temporary** | r4–r11 (named callee-saved) | Entire loop duration | **YES** (callee-saved) |
| 4 | **Function Argument Temporary** | r0–r3 | Function call | **NO** (caller-saved) |

## 2.3 The AAPCS Register Classification

The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** partitions the 16 core registers into three classes — this is a high-yield KTU exam topic:

| Register(s) | AAPCS Name | Role | Temporary? |
|-------------|------------|------|------------|
| **r0** | **a1** (argument/result) | First argument / return value | ✅ **Temporary** |
| **r1, r2, r3** | **a2, a3, a4** | Arguments 2, 3, 4 | ✅ **Temporary** |
| **r4, r5, r6, r7, r8** | **v1–v5** | Variable registers | ❌ Callee-saved |
| **r9** | **v6 / SB / TR** | Platform-specific | ❌ Callee-saved |
| **r10, r11** | **v7, v8 / FP** | Variable / Frame Pointer | ❌ Callee-saved |
| **r12** | **IP** | Intra-Procedure-call scratch | ✅ **Temporary** |
| **r13** | **SP** | Stack Pointer | ❌ Reserved |
| **r14** | **LR** | Link Register | ❌ Reserved |
| **r15** | **PC** | Program Counter | ❌ Reserved |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Total "Temporary" registers per AAPCS: r0, r1, r2, r3, r12 → 5 registers.**  
> The remaining 8 visible registers (r4–r11) are the **"Variable" registers** used to hold long-lived values.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> All high-yield formulas and rules for Temporary Variables:

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N_{temp} = 5$ | Number of AAPCS temporary registers (r0–r3, r12) |
| 2 | $N_{var} = 8$ | Number of AAPCS variable registers (r4–r11) |
| 3 | $T_{reg} \approx 1 \text{ cycle}$ | Register access time |
| 4 | $T_{stack} \approx 2 \text{ to } 5 \text{ cycles}$ | Stack (LDR/STR) access time |
| 5 | $\text{Spill cost} = T_{stack} - T_{reg}$ | Performance penalty of a stack temporary |
| 6 | $N_{args} \le 4$ | Maximum arguments passed in registers (ARM 32-bit) |
| 7 | $R_{result} = r0$ | Return value register (a temporary) |
| 8 | $\text{Spill overhead} = \text{STR} + \text{LDR} = 2 \text{ mem ops}$ | Each stack temporary requires save + load |

> **Real-World Utility:** In high-performance embedded systems (automotive ECUs, mobile SoCs, IoT devices), minimising temporary variable spills is critical. Compiler optimisation flags like `-O2` and `-O3` aggressively **reuse temporary registers** to reduce stack pressure and improve inner-loop performance. Engineers profiling real-time systems must understand temporaries to interpret assembly dumps and stack-usage reports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Temporary Variables in an Expression

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Yield KTU Question Type:** Translate a C expression to ARM assembly and identify temporary variables.

**Source C code:**

```c
int32_t a = 5, b = 3, c = 8, d = 2;
int32_t result = (a + b) * (c - d);
```

### Step 1 — Identify sub-expressions

We have **two sub-expressions** whose results must be temporarily stored:

$$
T_1 = a + b
$$
$$
T_2 = c - d
$$
$$
\text{result} = T_1 \times T_2
$$

### Step 2 — Allocate temporaries to registers

The compiler first attempts to map temporaries to AAPCS scratch registers:

| Temporary | Variable | Mapped Register | Reason |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|--------|
| $T_1$ | a, b | **r0, r1** | Scratch regs free |
| $T_2$ | c, d | **r2, r3** | Scratch regs free |

### Step 3 — Generate ARM assembly (no spills needed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{MOV} \quad r0, \#5 \quad &&\text{; load } a \text{ into r0} \\
&\text{MOV} \quad r1, \#3 \quad &&\text{; load } b \text{ into r1} \\
&\text{ADD} \quad r0, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; } T_1 = r0 + r1 \text{ (computed in r0)} \\
&\text{MOV} \quad r2, \#8 \quad &&\text{; load } c \text{ into r2} \\
&\text{MOV} \quad r3, \#2 \quad &&\text{; load } d \text{ into r3} \\
&\text{SUB} \quad r2, r2, r3 \quad &&\text{; } T_2 = r2 - r3 \text{ (computed in r2)} \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r0, r0, r2 \quad &&\text{; result = } T_1 \times T_2 \\
&\text{STR} \quad r0, \text{[result]} \quad &&\text{; store final value}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Now force a stack spill

Suppose we have **3 simultaneous temporaries** in a more complex expression:

```c
int32_t result = (a + b) * (c - d) + (e * f);
```

There are now **three** sub-results: $T_1, T_2, T_3$. But only **5** scratch registers are available (r0–r3, r12), and we also need a register to hold the final accumulation. The compiler will likely **spill one temporary to the stack**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{LDR} \quad r0, =a \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r1, =b \\
&\text{ADD} \quad r12, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; } T_1 = a + b \text{ in r12} \\
&\text{STR} \quad r12, \text{[SP, \#-4]!} \quad &&\text{; } \color{red}{\text{SPILL } T_1 \text{ to stack (temporary variable!)}} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r0, =c \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r1, =d \\
&\text{SUB} \quad r0, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; } T_2 = c - d \text{ in r0} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r12, \text{[SP]}, \#4 \quad &&\text{; reload } T_1 \text{ from stack} \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r0, r0, r12 \quad &&\text{; } T_1 \times T_2 \\
&\text{...}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The stack slot `[SP, #-4]!` is a **stack-allocated temporary variable**. It is created, written to, read from, and discarded all within a few instructions. This is what KTU examiners mean by "temporary variable" in assembly code.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When Are Temporaries Created?

| Condition | Type of Temporary Created | Example |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------|
| Sub-expression of size $> 1$ operand | Register temporary | `a + b` requires loading both `a` and `b` |
| Expression depth $> $ available scratch regs | Stack temporary (spill) | 6-operand expression → 1 stack slot |
| Function call argument passing | Argument temporary | `foo(a, b, c, d)` uses r0–r3 |
| Return value handling | Return temporary | `r0` holds result of `return x;` |
| Loop index initialization | Loop temporary (callee-saved) | `for(int i=0; i<n; i++)` → r4 |
| Conditional evaluation | Condition-code temporary | `CMP r0, r1` sets N, Z, C, V flags |

## 3.3 Cost Derivation for a Spilled Temporary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{spill}} &= \underbrace{1 \times \text{STR}}_{\text{save to stack}} + \underbrace{1 \times \text{LDR}}_{\text{reload from stack}} \\
&= T_{STR} + T_{LDR} \\
&\approx 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cycles per temporary (cache miss adds } 10{-}20 \text{ more)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with a register temporary:
$$
\text{Cost}_{reg} \approx 0 \text{ extra cycles (already in register)}
$$

Hence, the **performance penalty ratio**:
$$
\text{Penalty Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cost}_{spill}}{\text{Cost}_{reg}} \to \infty \text{ (per instruction)}
$$

> This is why **optimising compilers (gcc -O2/-O3)** perform *register allocation* and *liveness analysis* to **minimise temporary spills** — a topic KTU frequently tests under the CO "Apply optimisation techniques to ARM code."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AAPCS Register Partition (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ARM_REGS["ARM Core Register File r0 to r15"]
        direction TB
        subgraph TEMP_GROUP["Temporary Registers Caller-Saved Scratch"]
            R0["r0 a1 Argument1 ReturnValue"]
            R1["r1 a2 Argument2"]
            R2["r2 a3 Argument3"]
            R3["r3 a4 Argument4"]
            R12["r12 IP Intra Procedure scratch"]
        end
        subgraph VAR_GROUP["Variable Registers Callee-Saved Long-Lived"]
            R4["r4 v1"]
            R5["r5 v2"]
            R6["r6 v3"]
            R7["r7 v4"]
            R8["r8 v5"]
            R9["r9 v6 Platform Specific"]
            R10["r10 v7"]
            R11["r11 v8 Frame Pointer"]
        end
        subgraph SYS_GROUP["System Registers Reserved"]
            R13["r13 SP Stack Pointer"]
            R14["r14 LR Link Register"]
            R15["r15 PC Program Counter"]
        end
    end
    TEMP_GROUP -. "Volatile across calls" .- VAR_GROUP
    VAR_GROUP -. "Preserved across calls" .- SYS_GROUP
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The diagram above maps the **complete AAPCS register classification**, which is the foundation for understanding where temporary variables live during program execution. **Memorise this partition** for KTU viva and written exams.

## 4.2 Lifetime of a Temporary Variable (Liveness Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as C Source Code
    participant CMP as ARM Compiler
    participant REG as Scratch Register
    participant STK as Stack Frame
    C->>CMP: int y = a + b
    CMP->>CMP: Parse AST
    CMP->>REG: Allocate r0, r1 for a, b
    CMP->>REG: ADD r0, r0, r1 (T1 created)
    Note over REG: T1 LIVE in r0
    CMP->>CMP: Use T1 in next expression
    Note over REG: T1 DEAD, register freed
    alt Spill Required
        CMP->>STK: STR r0, [SP, #-4]!
        Note over STK: T1 LIVE in stack slot
        CMP->>STK: LDR r0, [SP], #4
        Note over STK: T1 DEAD, slot freed
    end
    CMP-->>C: Final machine code emitted
```

> [!NOTE]  
> This sequence diagram illustrates the **lifecycle of a temporary variable** — from creation (allocation), through its live range (in-use), to its death (freed/spilled). The `LIVE → DEAD` interval is what the compiler tracks in its **liveness analysis** pass.

## 4.3 Stack Spill Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Level Expression] --> B[Compiler Frontend AST]
    B --> C{Liveness Analysis}
    C -->|Free registers exist| D[Allocate to Scratch Reg r0 r1 r2 r3 r12]
    C -->|Register Pressure High| E[Spill to Stack Frame]
    D --> F[Emit ADD SUB MUL Instructions]
    E --> G[Emit STR PUSH]
    G --> H[Emit LDR POP after use]
    F --> I[Final ARM Machine Code]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Execute on ARM Core]
```

---
