<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a temporary variable in ARM assembly programming? Give two examples of registers classified as temporary under AAPCS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **temporary variable** is a short-lived storage location used to hold intermediate values during expression evaluation or function execution. It is allocated in a CPU **register** or a **stack slot**, and its lifetime spans only a small region of code.

Under the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, the following registers are classified as temporary (caller-saved):

- **r0 (a1)** — argument 1 / return value
- **r1, r2, r3 (a2, a3, a4)** — arguments 2, 3, 4
- **r12 (IP)** — intra-procedure scratch

These registers are not preserved across a function call, making them ideal for holding short-lived intermediate results.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 2 marks examples)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between register temporaries and stack temporaries. When is the compiler forced to use a stack temporary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Register Temporary | Stack Temporary |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Storage** | CPU register (r0–r3, r12) | Stack memory slot |
| **Access time** | ~1 cycle | 2–5+ cycles |
| **Allocation** | Direct, by compiler | Spilled when registers exhausted |
| **Lifetime** | One expression | One expression |

The compiler is **forced to use a stack temporary (spill)** when the number of simultaneously live intermediate values exceeds the number of available scratch registers. This situation is called **register pressure**. For example, in a deeply nested expression like `((a+b)*(c-d)) + ((e*f)/(g-h))`, there may be up to 4–5 live temporaries, forcing the compiler to spill at least one to the stack.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for difference table, 1 mark for "register pressure" concept)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **AAPCS register classification** for ARM with a neat block diagram. Highlight which registers are designated as **temporary** and which as **variable** registers. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Translate the following C code into **ARM assembly**, and **identify all temporary variables** (both register and stack-based) used by the compiler:

```c
int32_t compute(int32_t a, int32_t b, int32_t c, int32_t d) {
    int32_t t1 = a * b;
    int32_t t2 = c + d;
    int32_t t3 = t1 - t2;
    return t3 * 2;
}
```

Assume arguments arrive in r0–r3 and the result must be returned in r0. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]**

**AAPCS Register Classification:**

The ARM Procedure Call Standard divides the **13 user-visible registers (r0–r12)** plus the system registers (r13–r15) into three functional groups:

**1. Temporary / Argument / Result Registers (Scratch / Caller-Saved):**  *(2 marks)*

- **r0 (a1):** Holds the **first argument** on entry to a function and the **return value** on exit.
- **r1, r2, r3 (a2, a3, a4):** Hold **arguments 2, 3, and 4**.
- **r12 (IP):** The **Intra-Procedure-call scratch register**, used as a temporary between function calls. Often used by linkers and veneers for indirect calls.

These **5 registers** are the *only* registers whose values a function is **not required to preserve** across a call.

**2. Variable / Callee-Saved Registers:** *(2 marks)*

- **r4, r5, r6, r7, r8 (v1–v5)**
- **r9 (v6 / SB / TR)** — Platform-specific. May be a static base, thread register, or general variable.
- **r10 (v7)** — General variable.
- **r11 (v8 / FP)** — Typically used as the **Frame Pointer** for backtracing the stack.

These **8 registers** are **preserved across function calls** — if a function uses them, it must save and restore them in the prologue/epilogue.

**3. System / Reserved Registers:** *(1 mark)*

- **r13 (SP):** Stack Pointer
- **r14 (LR):** Link Register (return address)
- **r15 (PC):** Program Counter

**Block Diagram:** *(2 marks — see Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid diagram)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify temporaries** *(1 mark)*

| Temporary | Purpose | Lifetime |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| $T_1$ | `a * b` | One instruction pair |
| $T_2$ | `c + d` | One instruction pair |
| $T_3$ | `t1 - t2` | One instruction pair |
| $T_4$ | `t3 * 2` | One instruction (final result) |

**Step 2 — Allocation strategy** *(1 mark)*

Since the function has only **4 active temporaries at any time** and there are **5 scratch registers** available, **no stack spills are required** — all temporaries are **register temporaries**.

**Step 3 — ARM assembly** *(5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{compute:} \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r12, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; } T_1 = a * b \text{ (temp in r12)} \\
&\text{ADD} \quad r0, r2, r3 \quad &&\text{; } T_2 = c + d \text{ (temp in r0)} \\
&\text{SUB} \quad r0, r12, r0 \quad &&\text{; } T_3 = t1 - t2 \text{ (temp in r0)} \\
&\text{MOV} \quad r1, \#2 \quad &&\text{; } T_4_{const} = 2 \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r0, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; result = } T_3 * 2 \text{ (final)} \\
&\text{BX} \quad lr \quad &&\text{; return to caller (result in r0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Temporary Variable Identification Summary** *(1 mark)*

- $T_1$ (in **r12**) — register temporary
- $T_2$ (in **r0**) — register temporary
- $T_3$ (in **r0**) — register temporary (overwrites $T_2$)
- $T_4$ (in **r0**) — register temporary (overwrites $T_3$)

**No stack spills were required** because the number of live temporaries (4) never exceeded the available scratch registers (5).

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is **register pressure**? Explain with an example how it forces the compiler to create **stack-allocated temporary variables**. Show the resulting assembly with proper stack operations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A C function is given below. Identify all temporary variables and determine **whether each one is stored in a register or on the stack**. Justify your answer. *(7 marks)*

```c
int32_t foo(int32_t a, int32_t b, int32_t c, int32_t d, int32_t e) {
    int32_t x = a + b + c + d + e;
    int32_t y = a * b * c;
    return x - y;
}
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]**

**Definition of Register Pressure** *(2 marks):*

**Register pressure** is the condition in which the number of *simultaneously live* temporary values (intermediate results, live variables) in a program region **exceeds the number of available scratch registers** (5 in ARM AAPCS). When this happens, the compiler must **spill** one or more temporaries to the **stack frame**, increasing memory traffic and reducing performance.

**Example showing forced stack spill** *(5 marks):*

Consider:

```c
int32_t z = ((a * b) + (c * d)) * ((e * f) - (g * h));
```

**Live temporaries at peak:** $T_1 = a \cdot b$, $T_2 = c \cdot d$, $T_3 = e \cdot f$, $T_4 = g \cdot h$ — **four** live temporaries. But we also need registers to hold `a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h` (eight source operands). The compiler must spill at least one temporary to the stack:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PUSH} \quad \{r4\} \quad &&\text{; save callee-saved r4} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r0, [a] \quad &&\text{; load a} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r1, [b] \quad &&\text{; load b} \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r0, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; T1 = a*b in r0} \\
&\text{STR} \quad r0, [SP, \#-4]! \quad &&\color{red}{\text{; SPILL T1 to stack (stack temp!)}} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r0, [c] \quad &&\text{; load c} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r1, [d] \quad &&\text{; load d} \\
&\text{MUL} \quad r0, r0, r1 \quad &&\text{; T2 = c*d in r0} \\
&\text{LDR} \quad r12, [SP], \#4 \quad &&\text{; reload T1 from stack} \\
&\text{ADD} \quad r0, r0, r12 \quad &&\text{; T12 = T1 + T2} \\
&\text{...} \\
&\text{POP} \quad \{r4\} \quad &&\text{; restore r4}
\end{aligned}
$$

The stack slot accessed via `STR r0, [SP, #-4]!` and `LDR r12, [SP], #4` is a **stack-allocated temporary variable**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify temporaries** *(1 mark)*

| # | Temporary | Expression | Status |
|---|-----------|------------|--------|
| $T_1$ | $a+b$ | partial sum | Live until end of `x` line |
| $T_2$ | $(a+b)+c$ | partial sum | Live until end of `x` line |
| $T_3$ | $T_2 + d$ | partial sum | Live until end of `x` line |
| $T_4$ | $x + e$ (final x) | stored in stack | Long-lived |
| $T_5$ | $a * b$ | partial product | Live |
| $T_6$ | $T_5 * c$ (final y) | stored in stack | Long-lived |
| $T_7$ | $x - y$ (return) | result | Returned in r0 |

**Step 2 — Argument passing limitation** *(2 marks)*

Note that the function has **5 arguments**, but **AAPCS allows only 4** in registers (r0–r3). Therefore, the **5th argument `e` MUST be passed on the stack** — this is the first temporary.

**Step 3 — Allocation map** *(3 marks)*

| Temporary | Location | Reason |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| `e` (arg 5) | **[SP+0]** | Spilled argument (5th arg) |
| $T_1, T_2, T_3$ | **r12, r0, r1** | Scratch registers |
| $T_4$ (`x`) | **[SP+4]** | Long-lived; needs callee-saved → stack |
| $T_5$ (`a*b`) | **r2** | Scratch register |
| $T_6$ (`y`) | **[SP+8]** | Long-lived; needs callee-saved → stack |
| $T_7$ | **r0** | Return register |

**Step 4 — Conclusion** *(1 mark)*

The function uses **3 stack-allocated temporaries** (5th argument + `x` + `y`) because of (i) AAPCS's 4-argument limit and (ii) the need for long-lived storage that survives across the multiple `MUL`/`ADD` operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `r12` with a variable register** — `r12` (IP) is a **temporary scratch register**, NOT a callee-saved variable register. Many students lose 1–2 marks by misclassifying it.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 4-argument AAPCS limit** — When a C function has $>4$ arguments, the extras are **spilled to the stack** and become *stack temporaries*. Failing to identify this costs full marks in part (b).
> 
> 3. **Not mentioning "register pressure"** — When asked *why* a temporary is on the stack, students often just say "no register available" without using the term **register pressure**, which is the precise KTU-syllabus terminology.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the liveness interval** — Examiners expect students to comment on *when* a temporary is created and *when* it dies. A complete answer must mention **lifetime / liveness range**.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up `r13 (SP)`, `r14 (LR)`, `r15 (PC)`** — These are system registers, **not** temporaries or variables. They are reserved.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Temporary Variables**
> 
> - ⭐ **Definition:** A temporary variable is a **short-lived intermediate storage** location — usually a **register** or a **stack slot** — used during expression evaluation or function execution.
> - ⭐ **AAPCS temporary registers (5 total):** **r0, r1, r2, r3, r12**. These are **caller-saved (volatile)**.
> - ⭐ **AAPCS variable registers (8 total):** **r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11**. These are **callee-saved (non-volatile)**.
> - ⭐ **System registers (reserved):** **r13 (SP), r14 (LR), r15 (PC)** — not used as temporaries.
> - ⭐ **Register Pressure** is the situation that forces **stack spills** → creating **stack-allocated temporary variables**.
> - ⭐ **AAPCS argument limit:** Only **4 arguments** can be passed in registers (r0–r3); the 5th and beyond are passed on the **stack** (these are also temporaries).
> - ⭐ **Return value register:** **r0 (a1)** — it is itself a temporary.
> - ⭐ **Intra-Procedure scratch:** **r12 (IP)** is used by the linker for veneers (e.g., BL → long-branch). User code can also use it as a temporary.
> - ⭐ **Liveness analysis** determines the **birth and death** of every temporary in a function.
> - ⭐ **Stack temporary spill cost:** Each spill = **1 STR + 1 LDR ≈ 2–4 extra cycles** vs. 0 cycles for a register temporary.
> - ⭐ **Compiler flags** like `-O2`, `-O3`, `-Os` use **register allocation algorithms** (e.g., **graph colouring**) to minimise temporary spills.
> - ⭐ **Thumb-2 code** (common in modern ARM Cortex-M) uses the **same AAPCS** for temporary variable classification.
> - ⭐ **Frame Pointer (r11/FP)** is *not* a temporary; it is used to walk the stack during debugging and exception handling.
> - ⭐ **Watch out for `r12`** — it is the **most-misclassified** register in KTU exams.
> - ⭐ **Key phrase to use in answers:** *"Register pressure forced the compiler to spill the temporary to the stack frame."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preserving Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preserving Registers # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In ARM Architecture, **register preservation** refers to the disciplined act of saving the contents of a processor's general-purpose registers onto the **stack** (a region of memory pointed to by the **Stack Pointer, R13/SP**) before a subroutine modifies them, and restoring those contents back to the same registers after the subroutine completes. The rules governing *which* registers must be saved and *who* is responsible for saving them are codified in the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, which is a binding subset of the ABI (Application Binary Interface) for the ARM architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **AAPCS Categorization of Registers (ARM 32-bit):** > - **Caller-saved (Volatile / Scratch):** R0–R3, R12, R14 (LR) — the *caller* must assume these are destroyed across a `BL` call. > - **Callee-saved (Non-volatile / Preserved):** R4–R11, R13 (SP) — the *callee* must preserve the values it sees on entry. The principal assembly instructions used to preserve registers in ARMv7-M / Cortex-M (Thumb-2) and unified ARM syntax are: | Instruction | Mnemonic Expansion | Direction | |---|---|---| | `PUSH {reg_list}` | Store Multiple, Full Descending, before (pre-indexed write-back) | SP ↓ | | `POP {reg_list}` | Load Multiple, Full Descending, after (post-indexed write-back) | SP ↑ | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Shared Office Workspace:** > Imagine you and three colleagues share one desk (the CPU with its small set of registers). You are the *caller* function, and a colleague is the *callee* function who is about to use the desk. > - The little sticky notes on the desk (R0–R3) are meant for **quick, throwaway messages** — anyone can scribble over them. That's why they are *caller-saved*. > - The important files in the drawer (R4–R11) belong to whoever is currently using the desk. If your colleague needs to use a file from your drawer, they must first **photocopy it (PUSH it onto the stack)** and then **put the original back when they're done (POP it back)**. That's *callee-saved* discipline. > - If the colleague forgets to return the file, your workflow breaks the moment you return — the *calling function* finds its register corrupted. In short: **preserving registers is the contract that lets independent functions be safely composed**, the same way a polite house-guest leaves the kitchen as clean as they found it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 2024 scheme explicitly covers *Register Preservation*, *AAPCS*, *Stack Frames*, and *PUSH/POP semantics*. Mastery of these guarantees marks in 14-mark procedure-call questions. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" — Motivation for Register Preservation ARM provides only **16 general-purpose registers** (R0–R15), but a real C program may nest dozens of function calls (e.g., `main() → foo() → bar() → baz()`). If every function simply overwrote registers without saving them, the outermost `main()` would find its variables destroyed the moment any subroutine returns. Register preservation solves this using the **main memory (RAM)** as overflow storage, accessed through the stack. ## 2.2 The "How" — The Full Descending Stack Model ARM uses a **Full Descending (FD) Stack** by AAPCS convention: - **Full:** The Stack Pointer (SP) points to the **last item pushed** (the most recently filled slot), not to the next empty slot. - **Descending:** The stack grows **toward lower memory addresses** as items are pushed. Therefore, every `PUSH` operation: 1. Decrements SP by `(N × 4)` bytes, where $N$ is the number of registers in the list. 2. Stores the registers at increasing addresses starting from the new SP. ## 2.3 PUSH and POP — Encoded Semantics `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}` and `POP {R4, R5, PC}` are the canonical pair. Notice that the **return address is saved in LR (R14) by the `BL` instruction**, so a leaf subroutine must `PUSH {..., LR}` to survive nested calls, and the matching `POP {..., PC}` automatically performs the function return by loading PC from the saved LR. > [!NOTE] > **Endianness Note:** AAPCS mandates **little-endian** byte ordering for stack-stored words. Register R4 = `0x12345678` is stored at address `SP` as bytes `78 56 34 12` (low byte at low address). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Bytes consumed per register on stack | $B = 4$ | Bytes (32-bit ARM) | | Total stack delta on PUSH | $\Delta_{SP} = -N \times 4$ | Bytes (negative = grows down) | | Total stack delta on POP | $\Delta_{SP} = +N \times 4$ | Bytes (positive = shrinks up) | | Net effect of matched PUSH/POP | $\sum \Delta_{SP} = 0$ | Stack must balance to the byte | | New SP after PUSH | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - (N \times 4)$ | Address arithmetic | | Address of $k$-th pushed reg (0-indexed) | $addr(R_k) = SP_{new} + k \times 4$ | $k = 0 \Rightarrow$ lowest reg in list | | Load-effective for POP | $R_k \leftarrow M[SP_{old} + k \times 4]$ | then $SP \leftarrow SP_{old} + N \times 4$ | | AAPCS preservation responsibility | Callee saves R4–R11, SP | Caller saves R0–R3, R12, LR (if needed later) | | Standard frame restore for return | `POP {..., PC}` | Auto-loads LR → PC (atomically) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule:** The number of registers in the `PUSH` list **must exactly equal** the number in the matching `POP` list (in reverse order is allowed but not required by the assembler — it is required by *you*). Otherwise the stack pointer drifts and the program crashes at the next function return. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Register preservation is not an academic exercise — it underpins every real-time embedded system built on Cortex-M microcontrollers: from a BLDC motor controller (`main() → PID_Compute() → ADC_Read() → DMA_Handler()`) to the secure-boot chain of an IoT device. Debugging stack corruption (a `HardFault` in Cortex-M) almost always traces back to **mismatched PUSH/POP lists** or **forgotten callee-saved register restoration**. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PUSH Operation on a Full Descending Stack Consider the instruction `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}`. Let the initial stack pointer value be $SP_{old}$. We want to determine the new stack pointer $SP_{new}$ and the memory layout after execution. **Step 1 — Count registers.** The register list $\{R4, R5, LR\}$ contains $N = 3$ registers. Each register is $B = 4$ bytes wide on 32-bit ARM. **Step 2 — Compute total stack delta.** $$ \Delta_{SP} = -N \times B = -(3 \times 4) = -12 \text{ bytes} $$ The negative sign reflects the descending nature of the stack. **Step 3 — Update the stack pointer.** $$ SP_{new} = SP_{old} + \Delta_{SP} = SP_{old} - 12 $$ **Step 4 — Determine per-register store addresses.** AAPCS mandates that registers are stored at *consecutive ascending addresses* starting from $SP_{new}$, and that the **lowest-numbered register goes to the lowest address**. $$ \begin{aligned} addr(R4) &= SP_{new} + 0 \times 4 = SP_{old} - 12 \\ addr(R5) &= SP_{new} + 1 \times 4 = SP_{old} - 8 \\ addr(LR) &= SP_{new} + 2 \times 4 = SP_{old} - 4 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify SP-after-PUSH points to top of stack.** Since $SP_{new} = addr(R4)$, and R4 was stored last, $SP_{new}$ correctly points to the **last filled slot** — satisfying the "Full" stack definition. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation: POP Operation (Matching Restoration) The matching instruction is `POP {R4, R5, PC}`. We must recover the same register values from the same memory locations. **Step 1 — Identify the read addresses.** Reads use the *current* SP (which equals $SP_{new}$ from the PUSH). $$ \begin{aligned} R4 \leftarrow M[SP_{new} + 0] &= M[SP_{old} - 12] \\ R5 \leftarrow M[SP_{new} + 4] &= M[SP_{old} - 8] \\ PC \leftarrow M[SP_{new} + 8] &= M[SP_{old} - 4] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Update the stack pointer.** $$ SP_{final} = SP_{new} + N \times B = (SP_{old} - 12) + 12 = SP_{old} $$ **Step 3 — Net stack effect.** $$ SP_{final} - SP_{old} = 0 $$ The stack is exactly balanced. ✓ **Step 4 — Verify return semantics.** Writing the saved LR value directly into PC (R15) causes the processor to branch to the caller's return address, effectively executing a return from subroutine — a single instruction both restores state *and* returns control. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example Suppose $SP_{old} = \text{0x2000\_1000}$ (a typical Cortex-M SRAM start). Execute `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}` followed by `POP {R4, R5, PC}`. **After PUSH:** $$ SP_{new} = 0x2000\_1000 - 0xC = 0x2000\_0FF4 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} M[0x2000\_0FF4] &= R4_{old} \\ M[0x2000\_0FF8] &= R5_{old} \\ M[0x2000\_0FFC] &= LR_{old} \end{aligned} $$ **After POP:** $$ SP_{final} = 0x2000\_0FF4 + 0xC = 0x2000\_1000 $$ $$ PC = LR_{old} \Rightarrow \text{branch back to caller} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Who Saves What? (AAPCS Decision Table) | Register | Classification | Saved By | Why? | |---|---|---|---| | R0 | Argument / Return | Caller (if needed later) | Scratch register, may be overwritten by callee | | R1 | Argument / Return | Caller (if needed later) | Same as R0 | | R2 | Argument | Caller (if needed later) | Argument passing slot | | R3 | Argument | Caller (if needed later) | Argument passing slot | | R4 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R5 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R6 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R7 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R8 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R9 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R10 | Local variable | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R11 | Frame Pointer (FP) | **Callee** | AAPCS-mandated preservation | | R12 (IP) | Intra-procedure-call scratch | Caller (if needed later) | Used by linker veneers | | R13 (SP) | Stack Pointer | **Callee** (implicitly) | Stack discipline enforces this | | R14 (LR) | Link Register | Caller (if subroutine calls another) | Holds return address | | R15 (PC) | Program Counter | — | Not preserved; it's the executing instruction pointer | ## 3.5 Derivation: Stack Frame Setup with Frame Pointer For nested procedures or debuggers, a **frame pointer** (FP = R11) is typically used. The pattern is: **Prologue:** $$ \begin{aligned} PUSH \{R11, LR\} &\Rightarrow SP \leftarrow SP - 8 \\ MOV \, R11, SP &\Rightarrow R11 \text{ now points to saved R11} \\ PUSH \{R4-R10\} &\Rightarrow SP \leftarrow SP - 28 \end{aligned} $$ **Epilogue (mirror order):** $$ \begin{aligned} POP \{R4-R10\} &\Rightarrow \text{restore 7 callee-saved regs} \\ MOV \, SP, R11 &\Rightarrow \text{tear down local frame atomically} \\ POP \{R11, PC\} &\Rightarrow \text{restore FP and return} \end{aligned} $$ The `MOV SP, R11` is the **tear-down safeguard**: it guarantees the stack is collapsed to the caller's frame regardless of how many bytes the callee's locals consumed, which is why compiled C code on ARM is robust against variable-sized stack frames (e.g., `alloca`). --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Procedure Call Flow with Register Preservation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Caller Function main
R4=0xAAAA active"] --> B["Execute BL subroutine
LR ← return_address
PC ← subroutine"] B --> C["Subroutine Entry
Inspect R4: needs it for caller"] C --> D["PUSH {R4, LR}
SP -= 8 bytes
M[SP] = R4
M[SP+4] = LR"] D --> E["Subroutine Body
R4 modified freely
SP untouched"] E --> F["POP {R4, PC}
R4 ← M[SP]
PC ← M[SP+4]
SP += 8 bytes"] F --> G["Return to Caller
main resumes with R4 = 0xAAAA"] G --> H["Stack Balanced: SP unchanged"] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333 style C fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#333 style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style E fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333 style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style G fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333 style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Stack Memory Layout Diagram (Full Descending) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STACK["MEMORY ADDRESS SPACE (Lower = Lower Address)"] TOP["0x2000_1000 SP_old BEFORE PUSH"] S1["0x2000_0FFC M[SP_old-4] ← LR saved here (highest addr)"] S2["0x2000_0FF8 M[SP_old-8] ← R5 saved here"] S3["0x2000_0FF4 M[SP_old-12] ← R4 saved here (lowest addr)"] SP_NOW["0x2000_0FF4 SP_new AFTER PUSH points here"] BOTTOM["0x2000_0FF0 Free stack space below"] end TOP -.->|PUSH decrements 12 bytes| SP_NOW S1 -.->|POP reads, then SP increments back| TOP style TOP fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333 style S1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333 style S2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333 style S3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333 style SP_NOW fill:#fecaca,stroke:#333 style BOTTOM fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 AAPCS Register Classification Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CALLER["CALLER-SAVED (Volatile)"] A1["R0 — Arg/Return"] A2["R1 — Arg/Return"] A3["R2 — Arg"] A4["R3 — Arg"] A5["R12 — IP scratch"] A6["R14 — LR (return)"] end subgraph CALLEE["CALLEE-SAVED (Preserved)"] B1["R4"] B2["R5"] B3["R6"] B4["R7"] B5["R8"] B6["R9"] B7["R10"] B8["R11 — FP"] B9["R13 — SP"] end CALLER -.->|Caller responsibility| NOTE1["Save BEFORE BL if needed later"] CALLEE -.->|Callee responsibility| NOTE2["Save in PROLOGUE, restore in EPILOGUE"] style NOTE1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 style NOTE2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Differentiate between caller-saved and callee-saved registers in the ARM AAPCS. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** According to the ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS), registers are divided based on **who is responsible for preserving** their values across a procedure call. - **Caller-saved (Volatile) registers:** The *calling function* must save these if it needs their values after the call. They may be freely overwritten by the callee. **Examples: R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, R14 (LR).** - **Callee-saved (Non-volatile) registers:** The *called function* must preserve the values of these registers as they were on entry, and restore them before returning. **Examples: R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, R11, R13 (SP).** This convention enables independent compilation of functions and guarantees a stable execution environment for the caller. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **What is the function of the `PUSH` and `POP` instructions in ARM? Why must they be balanced?** **Model Answer:** `PUSH {reg_list}` stores the specified registers onto the **Full Descending Stack** pointed to by R13 (SP). It first decrements SP by `4 × N` bytes (where N is the number of registers), then stores registers at ascending memory addresses starting from the new SP. Conversely, `POP {reg_list}` reads registers from the stack and increments SP back by `4 × N` bytes. `PUSH` and `POP` **must be balanced** (same number of registers, in any order) so that the stack pointer returns to its original value after the subroutine. An imbalance causes **stack drift**, which corrupts the return address on the next `POP {..., PC}` and typically results in a `HardFault` (Cortex-M) or undefined behavior. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the AAPCS register usage convention with a clear classification table. Identify which registers must be preserved by the callee and which must be saved by the caller before a `BL` instruction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** is the standard that defines the contract between separately compiled functions, ensuring correct inter-procedure communication on ARM processors. **Classification of Registers:** | Category | Registers | Preservation Responsibility | |---|---|---| | Argument / Return | R0 – R3 | Caller (save before `BL` if needed after) | | Intra-procedure scratch | R12 (IP) | Caller (save before `BL` if needed after) | | Link Register | R14 (LR) | Caller (save before any further `BL`) | | Local variables | R4 – R11 | **Callee (must save and restore)** | | Stack Pointer | R13 (SP) | **Callee (implicitly preserved by stack discipline)** | | Program Counter | R15 (PC) | Not preserved (control flow) | **Why the split?** R0–R3 and R12 are conventionally used for **argument passing and fast scratch** operations, so the compiler can freely clobber them within any function for efficiency. R4–R11, however, are typically used to hold **long-lived local variables** that must survive across calls to other functions — hence the callee must save them. **Callee's responsibility:** Before modifying R4–R11, the callee must `PUSH` them in the prologue and `POP` them in the epilogue (with `POP {..., PC}` to also return). **Caller's responsibility:** Before calling another function via `BL`, the caller must `PUSH` any R0–R3, R12, or LR values it needs after the call returns. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Consider the following ARM subroutine. Trace the state of R4, R5, LR, and SP before and after the subroutine executes, assuming the initial SP is `0x2000_2000`. ```assembly SUBROUTINE PUSH {R4, R5, LR} MOV R4, #0x10 MOV R5, #0x20 ADD R4, R4, R5 POP {R4, R5, PC} ``` Identify the value in R4, the value in R5, and SP after the `POP` instruction. Show the stack memory contents. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Before subroutine entry (assume caller did `BL SUBROUTINE`):** - LR = return address (say `0x0000_0108`, the instruction after `BL`). - R4, R5 = whatever the caller had (let's call them $R4_c$, $R5_c$). - SP = `0x2000_2000`. **Step 1 — `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}`:** $N = 3$ registers, $N \times 4 = 12$ bytes. $$ SP_{new} = 0x2000\_2000 - 12 = 0x2000\_1FF4 $$ Stack memory after PUSH: | Address | Contents | |---|---| | `0x2000_1FF4` | $R4_c$ | | `0x2000_1FF8` | $R5_c$ | | `0x2000_1FFC` | LR = `0x0000_0108` | **Step 2 — `MOV R4, #0x10`:** R4 ← `0x10`. **Step 3 — `MOV R5, #0x20`:** R5 ← `0x20`. **Step 4 — `ADD R4, R4, R5`:** R4 ← `0x10 + 0x20 = 0x30`. **Step 5 — `POP {R4, R5, PC}`:** SP currently = `0x2000_1FF4`. - R4 ← `M[0x2000_1FF4]` = $R4_c$ (caller's original R4 restored) - R5 ← `M[0x2000_1FF8]` = $R5_c$ (caller's original R5 restored) - PC ← `M[0x2000_1FFC]` = `0x0000_0108` (return to caller) **Final state after POP:** - **R4 = $R4_c$** (caller's value, NOT `0x30` — the addition result is *discarded*) - **R5 = $R5_c$** (caller's value) - **SP = `0x2000_2000`** (restored exactly) - **PC = `0x0000_0108`** (execution continues at the caller) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subtle Insight:** This subroutine demonstrates that any *modifications* made to callee-saved registers inside a procedure are **invisible to the caller** because they are popped back to their original saved values. To return a computed result, the function must place it in **R0** (the AAPCS return register) *before* the `POP` — for example: `MOV R0, R4` then `POP {R4, R5, PC}`. **(7 marks)** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** What is a **stack frame**? With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a typical ARM stack frame including the saved frame pointer, link register, and local storage area. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **stack frame** (also called an *activation record*) is a region of memory allocated on the stack for a single procedure invocation. It contains the procedure's saved context and local variables. **Structure of a typical ARM stack frame (growing downward):** | Stack Address (High → Low) | Contents | |---|---| | Caller's frame | (Caller's data) | | Saved LR | Return address of the caller | | Saved FP (R11) | Caller's frame pointer | | Local variables | R4 – R10 (callee-saved scratch for locals) | | Sub-spilled temporaries | R0 – R3, R12 (if callee needs to preserve them) | | SP → | (Top of stack) | **Construction (Prologue) sequence:** 1. `PUSH {R11, LR}` — Save frame pointer and return address. 2. `MOV R11, SP` — Establish new frame pointer at the bottom of the caller's frame. 3. `PUSH {R4–R10}` — Save callee-saved registers this function will use. 4. `SUB SP, SP, #frame_size` — Allocate space for local variables / outgoing arguments. **Destruction (Epilogue) sequence (in reverse):** 1. `ADD SP, SP, #frame_size` (or `MOV SP, R11`) — Deallocate locals. 2. `POP {R4–R10}` — Restore callee-saved registers. 3. `POP {R11, PC}` — Restore frame pointer and return. **Why FP (R11) is used:** It provides a *stable reference point* to the caller's frame that does not move even if the callee pushes/pops many times. This is essential for debuggers to walk the call stack, and for accessing spilled arguments at fixed offsets from FP. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** A nested C function call `main() → funcA() → funcB()` is compiled to ARM assembly. Identify all the register-preservation actions that must occur at each boundary, assuming `funcB` modifies R4–R7. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Boundary 1: `main()` calls `funcA()` via `BL funcA`.** - The compiler emits `BL funcA`, which sets `LR = return_to_main` and jumps to `funcA`. - If `main` needs R0–R3, R12, or LR after the call, it must have `PUSH`ed them in its own prologue. - `funcA` is the callee. Inside its prologue, since it might use R4–R7 (callee-saved), it must `PUSH {R4, R5, R6, R7, LR}` (LR is saved because `funcA` will in turn call `funcB`). - `funcA` then freely modifies R4–R7 to hold its own local variables. **Boundary 2: `funcA()` calls `funcB()` via `BL funcB`.** - LR is set to `return_to_funcA`. - `funcA` does *not* need to save R4–R7 here — they are callee-saved, and `funcB` must preserve them if it uses them. - `funcB`'s prologue: `PUSH {R4, R5, R6, R7, LR}` — saving the values `funcA` carefully preserved. - `funcB` may modify R4–R7 for its own work. **Boundary 3: `funcB()` returns to `funcA()`.** - `funcB`'s epilogue: `POP {R4, R5, R6, R7, PC}` — restoring R4–R7 to the values `funcA` left them in, and returning to `funcA` by writing LR into PC. **Boundary 4: `funcA()` returns to `main()`.** - `funcA`'s epilogue: `POP {R4, R5, R6, R7, PC}` — restoring R4–R7 to the values `main` had, and returning to `main`. **Summary Table:** | Boundary | Caller | Callee | Callee's PUSH | Callee's POP | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | main | funcA | `{R4, R5, R6, R7, LR}` | `{R4, R5, R6, R7, PC}` | | 2 | funcA | funcB | `{R4, R5, R6, R7, LR}` | `{R4, R5, R6, R7, PC}` | > [!NOTE] > The four `PUSH`/`POP` pairs (one per function) must be **individually balanced**. If `funcA` `PUSH`es R4–R7 but `POP`s only R4–R6, the stack pointer will be 4 bytes off, and `main` will return to the wrong address — a classic `HardFault` source on Cortex-M. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting LR in the PUSH list of a non-leaf function.** A function that itself calls another MUST save LR. The matching `POP` must end with `PC` (not `LR`) to actually return. Losing this: **−3 to −5 marks**. > 2. **Mismatched PUSH/POP register counts.** Even if you "remember" to save, popping the wrong number causes stack drift. Examiner will mark it as fatal logic error: **−2 to −4 marks**. > 3. **Confusing "caller-saved" with "callee-saved" directionality.** Students often say "R4 is saved by the caller" — this is wrong. R4–R11 are saved by the **callee** because the caller assumes they survive. Examiner allocates **−2 marks** for this conceptual inversion. > 4. **Writing `POP {R4, R5, LR}`** instead of `POP {R4, R5, PC}` at the very end of a subroutine. The `PC` version atomically restores LR into PC and triggers the return. Using `LR` just loads the value back without branching. Examiner deducts **−3 marks**. > 5. **Ignoring the SP restoration in the answer.** When asked "trace SP," students often show the PUSH delta but forget to add the POP delta back. Examiner deducts **−1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Preserving Registers (Module 3)** > - ✅ **AAPCS** = ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard; defines register preservation rules. > - ✅ **Caller-saved:** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, R14 (LR) — caller saves if needed. > - ✅ **Callee-saved:** R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, R11, R13 (SP) — callee saves and restores. > - ✅ **Stack type:** Full Descending (FD) — SP points to the last item pushed, stack grows to lower addresses. > - ✅ **`PUSH {reg_list}`** = STMDB / pre-decrement write-back store. > - ✅ **`POP {reg_list}`** = LDMIA / post-increment load. > - ✅ **Stack delta on PUSH of N registers:** $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 4N$. > - ✅ **Stack delta on POP of N registers:** $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 4N$. > - ✅ **Net effect of matched PUSH/POP:** Stack pointer unchanged; stack balanced. > - ✅ **Return idiom:** `POP {..., PC}` — loads saved LR into PC, automatically returning. > - ✅ **Non-leaf function MUST PUSH LR** (because it will itself call another function). > - ✅ **Frame Pointer (R11):** Set in prologue via `MOV R11, SP`; used for stable base address within a function. > - ✅ **Little-endian:** AAPCS-mandated; low-order byte of a word is at the lowest address. > - ✅ **Balanced PUSH/POP is non-negotiable** — imbalance = stack corruption = `HardFault`. > - ✅ **Return value goes in R0**, not in any callee-saved register. > - ✅ **Memory addresses of pushed registers (lowest reg at lowest address):** $addr(R_k) = SP_{new} + k \cdot 4$.

topics.title: Interrupts and Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupts and Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Exceptions?

In the ARM architectural framework, an **exception** is any event that causes the processor to suspend the normal sequential execution of instructions and transfer control to a dedicated software handler. The ARM Architecture Reference Model treats every asynchronous external signal and synchronous internal fault as a unified class of "exceptions."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An *exception* in ARM is defined as any condition that alters the normal program flow. The ARM7TDMI core recognizes **7 categories** of exceptions, each occupying a fixed 4-byte slot in the **Exception Vector Table** located at memory address `0x00000000`.

## 1.2 What are Interrupts?

An **interrupt** is a specific *subclass* of exception that is generated by an **external hardware peripheral** to request processor service. In ARM terminology, the two hardware interrupt sources are:

- **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** — Normal priority interrupt, maskable via the `I` bit in CPSR.
- **FIQ (Fast Interrupt Request)** — Higher priority, used for time-critical, low-latency servicing, maskable via the `F` bit in CPSR.

## 1.3 Interrupts vs Exceptions — The Critical Distinction

| Term | Scope | Source | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exception** | Broad (all events) | Hardware + Software | Reset, SWI, Aborts, IRQ, FIQ |
| **Interrupt** | Narrow (hardware requests) | External peripherals | Timer tick, UART RX, GPIO edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *"All Interrupts are Exceptions, but NOT all Exceptions are Interrupts."* Software Interrupt (SWI/SVC) and Aborts are exceptions but **not** interrupts.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Worker

Imagine you are an office worker (the **CPU**) typing a report (executing the **main program**). Suddenly, three things can happen:

1. **Phone rings (IRQ):** You pause, note the page number (save state), answer the call (ISR), then return to the exact word you left off.
2. **Boss storms in (FIQ):** The phone call is cut short — this is more urgent. You handle it immediately.
3. **You spill coffee on the keyboard (Data Abort):** This is a *fault* caused by your current action — a synchronous exception that needs the OS to intervene.

The **exception vector table** is like the **sticky notes on your desk** telling you the exact desk (memory address) where you must go for each type of interruption.

## 1.5 Physical & Performance Constants (Bold for Recall)

- **Vector Table Base Address** = `0x00000000` (standard ARM7/ARM9)
- **Vector Table Relocation Register** = `0xFFFF0000` (high-vector variant on ARM720T/ARM920T)
- **ARM7TDMI IRQ Latency** ≈ **30 clock cycles** (worst case)
- **ARM7TDMI FIQ Latency** ≈ **6 clock cycles** (worst case)
- **Vector Spacing** = **4 bytes** (ARM state) or **4 bytes with zero upper halfword** for Thumb
- **Number of Exception Vectors** = **7 active + 1 reserved** = 8 entries × 4 bytes = **32 bytes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven ARM Exceptions — Authoritative Classification

The ARM7TDMI core (used in KTU labs on LPC2148) defines exactly **7 exception sources**. The following is the definitive KTU board-reference table:

| Vector Offset | Exception | Mode on Entry | Priority | Vector Address (Low) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | **Reset** | Supervisor (`SVC`) | 1 (Highest) | `0x00000000` |
| `0x04` | **Undefined Instruction** | Undefined (`UND`) | 6 (Lowest) | `0x00000004` |
| `0x08` | **Software Interrupt (SWI)** | Supervisor (`SVC`) | 6 | `0x00000008` |
| `0x0C` | **Prefetch Abort** | Abort (`ABT`) | 5 | `0x0000000C` |
| `0x10` | **Data Abort** | Abort (`ABT`) | 2 | `0x00000010` |
| `0x14` | **Reserved** | — | — | `0x00000014` |
| `0x18` | **IRQ** | IRQ (`IRQ`) | 4 | `0x00000018` |
| `0x1C` | **FIQ** | FIQ (`FIQ`) | 3 | `0x0000001C` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Order (High → Low):** Reset → Data Abort → FIQ → IRQ → Prefetch Abort → SWI/Undefined. Reset is non-maskable and trumps everything.

## 2.2 Processor Modes vs Exceptions — The Banked Register Concept

Each exception (except User/System) enters the processor into a **dedicated privileged mode** with its own **banked register set** (R8–R14 physical, plus SPSR). This is ARM's elegant mechanism to provide *zero-latency register context switching*.

| Mode | Code (CPSR[4:0]) | Banked Registers | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| User | `10000` | None | Normal application code |
| FIQ | `10001` | R8–R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq | Fast interrupt |
| IRQ | `10010` | R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq | General interrupt |
| Supervisor | `10011` | R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc | Reset & SWI |
| Abort | `10111` | R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt | Memory access failures |
| Undefined | `11011` | R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und | Undefined instructions |
| System | `11111` | None (uses User regs) | Privileged OS tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **FIQ is special:** It has **5 extra banked registers** (R8_fiq to R12_fiq) compared to other modes. This means an FIQ handler can run completely without saving any registers, achieving the minimum possible latency — the very reason FIQ exists.

## 2.3 The Exception Vector Table — Architectural Skeleton

The vector table is an array of **branch instructions** (typically `B <handler>`) located at the bottom of memory. The CPU's hardware automatically reads the instruction at the appropriate vector offset when an exception is recognized.

**High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat Sheet):**

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Vector Table Size | $\text{N}_{\text{vec}} \times 4$ | Bytes |
| Exception Vector Address | $V_{addr} = V_{base} + 4 \cdot k$ | Bytes |
| IRQ Worst-Case Latency (ARM7) | $T_{IRQ} \approx 30 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| FIQ Worst-Case Latency (ARM7) | $T_{FIQ} \approx 6 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| Total Exception Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{recog} + T_{branch}$ | Seconds |
| CPSR-to-SPSR Copy Time | $1$ clock cycle | Cycles |
| Vectored IRQ Latency (VIC) | $T_{VIC} = T_{sync} + 3 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| Interrupt Nesting Depth Limit | $\text{N}_{\text{nest}} = \text{STACK}_{\text{depth}}$ | Levels |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT use the vertical bar `|` symbol for absolute value inside markdown tables — it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX contexts (e.g., $\vert V_{addr} \vert$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control):** FIQ handles crank-shaft timing (sub-µs deadline), IRQ handles CAN bus messages.
- **Medical Pacemakers:** Reset exception = hardware watchdog, FIQ = ventricular sensing, IRQ = telemetry.
- **Mobile Baseband:** Data Abort exception = virtual memory page fault; SWI = syscall to OS.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Prefetch Abort = ECC single-bit error correction entry point.

## 2.5 The CPSR — Control Hub of Exceptions

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is the 32-bit register that the ARM CPU consults to decide if an exception is permitted.

**CPSR Bit Layout (Critical for KTU):**

| Bit | Name | Function in Exception Context |
|---|---|---|
| `[31]` | N | Negative flag (preserved) |
| `[30]` | Z | Zero flag (preserved) |
| `[29]` | C | Carry flag (preserved) |
| `[28]` | V | Overflow flag (preserved) |
| `[27:8]` | — | Reserved / execution state |
| `[7]` | I | **1 = IRQ disabled** |
| `[6]` | F | **1 = FIQ disabled** |
| `[5]` | T | Thumb state (preserved) |
| `[4:0]` | Mode | New mode bits on exception |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mechanism:** On exception entry, hardware copies the entire CPSR into the banked **SPSR** of the new mode. The `I` and `F` bits in the new CPSR are set to **mask further interrupts** (preventing stack corruption during handler execution). On return (`MOVS PC, LR` or `SUBS PC, LR, #4`), SPSR is copied back to CPSR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Exception Entry Sequence (ARM7TDMI)

The ARM hardware performs the following **7-step procedure atomically** when an exception is recognized. This is the most heavily tested KTU derivation.

**Step 1 — Save Next Instruction Address**

The value saved in `R14_<mode>` (the banked Link Register) depends on the exception type, because different exceptions must resume at different offsets.

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{new}} = \text{PC}_{\text{at\_exception}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{type}}
$$

Where the offsets are derived from the ARM pipeline (3-stage: Fetch → Decode → Execute):

| Exception | Offset | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | None (SPSR not loaded) | PC loses meaning |
| Data Abort | $+8$ | Aborted instruction must be re-executed |
| IRQ | $+4$ | Normal pipeline advance |
| FIQ | $+4$ | Normal pipeline advance |
| Prefetch Abort | $+4$ | Faulted instruction must be re-executed |
| SWI | $0$ | SWI must be re-executed (or skipped, per design) |
| Undefined | $+4$ | Undefined instruction must be re-tried |

**Step 2 — Copy CPSR to SPSR**

$$
\text{SPSR}_{\text{new\_mode}} \leftarrow \text{CPSR}_{\text{old}}
$$

This single-cycle copy preserves the flags, control bits, and mode of the *interrupted* program.

**Step 3 — Force New Processor Mode**

The mode bits `[4:0]` of CPSR are updated to the exception-specific mode code from the table in Section 2.2.

$$
\text{CPSR}[4:0] \leftarrow \text{Mode}_{\text{new}}
$$

**Step 4 — Disable Further Interrupts**

To prevent nested-exception stack corruption, ARM automatically masks the new mode's own interrupt class and the higher-priority one(s):

$$
\text{CPSR}[6] \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(Disable FIQ — set on all exceptions except Reset)}
$$
$$
\text{CPSR}[7] \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(Disable IRQ — set on all exceptions except Reset)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When an IRQ exception occurs, the `I` bit is set (IRQ disabled), but the `F` bit is **also** set in some ARM cores. This prevents FIQ from being acknowledged during IRQ handling — design choice for deterministic latency.

**Step 5 — Load PC with Vector Address**

The hardware forces the PC to the appropriate vector table entry:

$$
\text{PC} \leftarrow 0x00000000 + \text{Offset}_{\text{vector}}
$$

For example, on IRQ: $\text{PC} = 0x00000018$. The instruction at that address (typically `B IRQ_Handler`) is then fetched.

**Step 6 — Banked SP Setup (Software Responsibility)**

Although hardware sets R13 to the banked SP, the SP value is **uninitialized**. The handler's first instructions must explicitly load SP:

$$
\text{R13}_{\text{new}} \leftarrow \text{Stack\_Top}_{\text{mode}}
$$

**Step 7 — Save User Registers (Software Responsibility)**

The handler must push all *non-banked* registers it will use onto the stack pointed to by the new R13:

$$
\text{STMFD} \quad \text{R13!}, \quad \{R0-R12, R14\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without the `STMFD` push, the handler will corrupt the interrupted program's R0–R12 and R14. This is the **#1 KTU board exam mark-loser** scenario.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Exception Exit Sequence (The `SUBS` Trick)

Returning from an exception uses a clever mechanism that simultaneously **restores CPSR, returns PC, AND skips the faulting instruction** if needed.

**Case 1: Returning from IRQ / FIQ / Prefetch Abort / Undefined**

$$
\text{SUBS} \quad \text{PC}, \text{LR}, \#4
$$

**Step-by-step atomic actions executed by `SUBS PC, LR, #4`:**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} \leftarrow \text{LR}_{\text{old}} - 4
$$
$$
\text{CPSR} \leftarrow \text{SPSR}_{\text{old}}
$$

**Case 2: Returning from SWI**

For SWI, the OS handler typically wants to skip the SWI instruction (since it's been serviced) and return to the instruction *after* SWI:

$$
\text{MOVS} \quad \text{PC}, \text{LR}
$$

This restores `PC = LR` (which is `PC_at_SWI + 4`) and copies SPSR → CPSR atomically.

**Case 3: Returning from Data Abort**

A data-aborted instruction must be **re-executed** (the faulting page has been paged in). Therefore:

$$
\text{SUBS} \quad \text{PC}, \text{LR}, \#8
$$

This sets `PC = LR - 8 = PC_at_abort - 8 + 8 = PC_at_abort`, exactly re-fetching the faulting load/store.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vector Table Construction

**Problem (KTU Style):** An LPC2148 system uses the high-vector variant at `0xFFFF0000`. Calculate the address of each vector and the size of the table.

**Given:**

$$
V_{base} = 0xFFFF0000
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
V_{reset} = V_{base} + 0 \times 4 = 0xFFFF0000
$$
$$
V_{und} = V_{base} + 1 \times 4 = 0xFFFF0004
$$
$$
V_{swi} = V_{base} + 2 \times 4 = 0xFFFF0008
$$
$$
V_{pabt} = V_{base} + 3 \times 4 = 0xFFFF000C
$$
$$
V_{dabt} = V_{base} + 4 \times 4 = 0xFFFF0010
$$
$$
V_{irq} = V_{base} + 6 \times 4 = 0xFFFF0018
$$
$$
V_{fiq} = V_{base} + 7 \times 4 = 0xFFFF001C
$$

$$
\text{Table Size} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

## 3.4 Interrupt Latency Calculation (Quantitative)

**Problem:** An ARM7TDMI system runs at `60 MHz`. An IRQ fires. What is the **maximum** time before the first instruction of the ISR executes?

**Given:**

$$
f_{clk} = 60 \text{ MHz} \implies T_{clk} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^{6}} = 16.67 \text{ ns}
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
T_{IRQ,\text{max}} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = 30 \times 16.67 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{IRQ,\text{max}} = 500 \text{ ns}
$$

**For FIQ:**

$$
T_{FIQ,\text{max}} = 6 \times 16.67 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why FIQ is reserved for *hard real-time* events like motor control loops where the deadline is under 1 µs. IRQ is used for "soft" peripherals like UART buffers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    E --> E1["B PrefetchAbort_Handler"]
    F --> F1["B DataAbort_Handler"]
    H --> H1["B IRQ_Handler"]
    I --> I1["B FIQ_Handler"]

    H1 --> H2["SAVE R0-R12, R14 TO STACK"]
    H2 --> H3["READ VICVECADDR FOR ISR ADDR"]
    H3 --> H4["BRANCH TO ISR"]
    H4 --> H5["RESTORE REGISTERS"]
```

## 4.2 Exception Entry/Exit Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as ARM7TDMI Core
    participant CPSR as CPSR Register
    participant LR14 as R14 Banked
    participant SPSR as SPSR Banked
    participant VEC as Vector Table
    participant STK as Mode Stack

    Note over CPU: NORMAL EXECUTION
    CPU->>CPSR: Read current mode bits and I/F flags

    Note over CPU: EXCEPTION SIGNAL DETECTED
    CPU->>CPSR: Save CPSR to SPSR of new mode
    CPU->>LR14: R14_mode = PC + Offset
    CPU->>CPSR: Force new mode bits, Set I=1, Set F=1
    CPU->>VEC: PC = Vector[Offset]
    CPU->>STK: Load banked SP (software)
    CPU->>STK: STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, R14}
    CPU->>CPU: Execute Handler Body
    CPU->>STK: LDMFD SP!, {R0-R12, PC}
    Note over CPU,STK: SUBS PC, LR, #4 atomically restores CPSR from SPSR
```

## 4.3 Exception Priority Resolution Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Exception Pending"] --> Q1{"Is it RESET?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> RST["Acknowledge Reset, Highest Priority, Non-maskable"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Is it DATA ABORT?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> DA["Acknowledge Data Abort, Priority 2"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Is it FIQ AND F=0?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> FIQ["Acknowledge FIQ, Priority 3"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Is it IRQ AND I=0?"}
    Q4 -- YES --> IRQ["Acknowledge IRQ, Priority 4"]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{"Is it Prefetch Abort?"}
    Q5 -- YES --> PA["Acknowledge Prefetch Abort, Priority 5"]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6{"SWI or UNDEF?"}
    Q6 -- YES --> SU["Acknowledge SWI or Undef, Priority 6"]
    Q6 -- NO --> NONE["No Exception, Continue Normal Flow"]

    style RST fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style DA fill:#e26b0a,color:#ffffff
    style FIQ fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an Interrupt and an Exception in the ARM architecture. Give one example of each that is *not* an interrupt.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **exception** is a general term for any event that causes a change in the normal program flow of the processor. It includes both hardware-generated and software-generated events. An **interrupt** is a *subtype* of exception generated by external hardware to request CPU service.

Key differentiators:

1. **Scope:** Exceptions include interrupts + faults + software calls. Interrupts are hardware-only.
2. **Source:** Exceptions originate from both inside (synchronous) and outside (asynchronous) the CPU. Interrupts are always asynchronous external events.
3. **Masking:** All exceptions can be selectively masked; IRQ via CPSR[7], FIQ via CPSR[6].

**Non-interrupt exceptions (must mention for full marks):**
- **Software Interrupt (SWI/SVC)** — A synchronous exception invoked by executing an `SVC` instruction in user code to request OS service.
- **Data Abort** — A synchronous fault raised when a load/store accesses a memory address with invalid permissions or no translation entry.
- **Undefined Instruction** — Triggered when the decoder encounters an opcode not in the ARM instruction set.
- **Reset** — The highest priority, non-maskable exception that occurs at power-on or watchdog timeout.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Exception = Interrupt" and lose 1 mark. Always state the **superset-subset** relationship explicitly: *"All interrupts are exceptions, but not all exceptions are interrupts."*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the Link Register (R14) and SPSR in ARM exception handling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Link Register (R14)** is used to **store the return address** when the processor enters an exception. Each privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) has its own **banked** copy of R14. When an exception occurs, hardware writes `PC + offset` into the banked R14 of the new mode. The offset varies: `+4` for IRQ/FIQ, `+8` for Data Abort, `0` for SWI.

The **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR)** is used to **preserve the CPSR of the interrupted program**. Each privileged mode (except User/System) has its own SPSR. On exception entry, hardware copies CPSR → SPSR. On exception exit, the atomic instruction `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (or similar) restores SPSR → CPSR simultaneously with PC update.

**Working together:** R14 holds the address to return to; SPSR holds the flags/mode to restore. The atomic `SUBS PC, LR, #4` instruction performs both restorations in a single non-interruptible step, preventing state corruption.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **ARM Exception Vector Table** for ARM7TDMI. List all 7 exceptions with their vector addresses, modes entered, and priorities. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe in detail the **steps executed by ARM hardware** when an IRQ exception occurs. Include the changes to CPSR, R14, R13, and SPSR. (7 Marks)

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Exception Vector Table (7 Marks)

The ARM Exception Vector Table is a set of 32 bytes (8 slots × 4 bytes) located at memory address `0x00000000` in the standard ARM7/ARM9 configuration. The CPU hardware automatically reads the instruction at the appropriate vector offset whenever an exception is recognized.

**Complete Table:**

| Vector Address | Exception | Mode on Entry | Mode Code (CPSR[4:0]) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `0x00000000` | Reset | Supervisor | `10011` | 1 (Highest) |
| `0x00000004` | Undefined Instruction | Undefined | `11011` | 6 (Lowest) |
| `0x00000008` | Software Interrupt (SWI) | Supervisor | `10011` | 6 |
| `0x0000000C` | Prefetch Abort | Abort | `10111` | 5 |
| `0x00000010` | Data Abort | Abort | `10111` | 2 |
| `0x00000014` | Reserved | — | — | — |
| `0x00000018` | IRQ | IRQ | `10010` | 4 |
| `0x0000001C` | FIQ | FIQ | `10001` | 3 |

**Vectored Interrupt Controller (VIC) Enhancement:** The LPC2148 incorporates a VIC peripheral that allows the IRQ vector at `0x00000018` to be a single branch that reads the address of the highest-priority enabled interrupt from the **VICVECADDR** register, then jumps directly to that ISR. This is called **vectored IRQ** and reduces the IRQ entry overhead significantly.

**Key Properties for KTU:**

1. Each vector slot holds a **branch instruction**, not the handler itself. The handler is placed elsewhere in memory.
2. In the **Thumb** state, the vector slots must contain the **LSB set** (e.g., `0x1A` for handler at `0x19`) to indicate Thumb execution.
3. The vector table can be **remapped** to `0xFFFF0000` on ARM720T/ARM920T cores using the CP15 control register.

---

#### Part (b) — IRQ Exception Entry Steps (7 Marks)

When the ARM7TDMI core detects an IRQ signal (and the `I` bit in CPSR is `0`), it performs the following **7-step sequence**:

**Step 1 — Complete Current Instruction**

The CPU finishes the instruction currently in the Execute stage of the 3-stage pipeline. No instruction is left half-done.

**Step 2 — Save Return Address in R14_irq**

$$
\text{R14}_{irq} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4
$$

The `+4` accounts for the pipeline: the next instruction to execute (when we return) is 2 instructions ahead of the one currently executing, so the link register holds `PC + 4`.

**Step 3 — Copy CPSR to SPSR_irq**

$$
\text{SPSR}_{irq} \leftarrow \text{CPSR}_{old}
$$

This single-cycle copy preserves the condition flags (N, Z, C, V), control bits (I, F, T), and mode bits of the interrupted program.

**Step 4 — Force Processor into IRQ Mode**

$$
\text{CPSR}[4:0] \leftarrow 0b10010 \quad \text{(IRQ mode code)}
$$

The banked registers R13_irq, R14_irq, and SPSR_irq become the active set.

**Step 5 — Mask Further Interrupts**

$$
\text{CPSR}[7] \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(IRQ disabled)}
$$
$$
\text{CPSR}[6] \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(FIQ also disabled during IRQ — design choice)}
$$

This prevents **nested interrupt corruption** of the SPSR_irq register.

**Step 6 — Set PC to IRQ Vector**

$$
\text{PC} \leftarrow 0x00000018
$$

The instruction at the IRQ vector is fetched. Typically this is:

```assembly
B       IRQ_Handler
```

**Step 7 — Software Stack Setup (Handler Prologue)**

The handler must explicitly load the IRQ stack pointer and save the non-banked user registers:

```assembly
IRQ_Handler
    SUB     LR, LR, #4          ; Adjust LR for correct return
    STMFD   SP!, {R0-R12, LR}   ; Save working registers
    ; ... read VICVECADDR, branch to ISR ...
    LDMFD   SP!, {R0-R12, PC}^  ; Atomic restore, '^' copies SPSR to CPSR
```

**Return Mechanism:**

The `LDMFD ... {PC}^` instruction performs **three actions atomically**:
1. Loads R0–R12 from the stack.
2. Loads PC with the saved LR (which is `PC_at_IRQ + 4 - 4 = PC_at_IRQ`), resuming the interrupted program.
3. Copies SPSR_irq back to CPSR, restoring flags and mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Top Mark Loser:** Forgetting to subtract 4 from LR in the IRQ prologue. If you use `MOVS PC, LR` instead of `SUBS PC, LR, #4`, the processor will re-execute the instruction at the IRQ vector itself, causing an **infinite loop**. The board examiner awards **0 marks** for return mechanism if this is wrong.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **banked registers** in ARM. With a neat diagram, show how many banked registers each exception mode has and why FIQ is "faster" than IRQ. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An ARM7TDMI system has a clock of `50 MHz`. Calculate the worst-case latency for IRQ and FIQ interrupts. Justify why FIQ is preferred for time-critical applications. (7 Marks)

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Banked Registers & FIQ Speed Advantage (7 Marks)

**Definition:** *Banked registers* are **physically separate copies** of a register (R8–R14) that exist for specific processor modes. When the processor switches to that mode, the banked copy becomes the active register, and the User-mode copy is automatically preserved without any software push/pop.

**Banked Register Count by Mode:**

| Mode | R8 | R9 | R10 | R11 | R12 | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| FIQ | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| IRQ | — | — | — | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Supervisor | — | — | — | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Abort | — | — | — | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Undefined | — | — | — | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |

**Key Observation:** FIQ has **5 additional banked registers** (R8_fiq through R12_fiq) compared to all other privileged modes.

**Why FIQ is Faster:**

1. **No Register Saving Required:** A typical C-language FIQ handler written in assembly can use R8–R12 without pushing them onto the stack, because the banked copies are *already isolated* from User-mode R8–R12. This saves approximately **5 × S + 5 × L = 10 memory cycles** of context save/restore.

2. **Last in Vector Table:** The FIQ vector is at the **last** position (`0x1C`) in the vector table. This means a FIQ handler can be placed **immediately at the vector address** (i.e., the handler code starts at `0x1C`) without needing a branch instruction at the vector. This eliminates one branch and saves **2–3 pipeline cycles**.

3. **Higher Priority:** FIQ cannot be interrupted by IRQ (the `F` bit is set automatically). An FIQ can preempt an IRQ handler, but not vice versa. This ensures deterministic latency.

4. **Maskable Separately:** The `F` bit (CPSR[6]) and `I` bit (CPSR[7]) allow independent control, enabling nested interrupt policies where IRQ is enabled inside FIQ if desired.

**Practical Impact (for KTU numerical):** On ARM7TDMI, a well-written FIQ handler in assembly can achieve **end-to-end latency under 12 cycles** (including entry), whereas an IRQ handler needs at least 30 cycles plus context save overhead.

---

#### Part (b) — Interrupt Latency Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$
f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Derivation of Clock Period:**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds} = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

**IRQ Worst-Case Latency:**

The ARM7TDMI Technical Reference Manual specifies the worst-case IRQ latency as the time for the longest instruction (an LDM with 16 registers = 32 cycles) to complete, plus the exception entry sequence:

$$
T_{IRQ,\text{max}} = (N_{\text{longest\_instr}} + N_{\text{entry}}) \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{IRQ,\text{max}} = (32 + 3) \times 20 \text{ ns} = 35 \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{IRQ,\text{max}} = 700 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For examination purposes, KTU accepts the canonical figure:** $T_{IRQ,\text{max}} \approx 30 \times T_{clk} = 600 \text{ ns}$. Use the 30-cycle figure unless the question explicitly states the longest instruction.

**FIQ Worst-Case Latency:**

FIQ is recognized at the **Execute stage** of the pipeline, not the Memory stage, because ARM's FIQ signal is sampled earlier:

$$
T_{FIQ,\text{max}} = (N_{\text{longest\_instr}} + 2) \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{FIQ,\text{max}} = (32 + 2) \times 20 \text{ ns} = 34 \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{FIQ,\text{max}} = 680 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(pessimistic with longest instruction)}
$$

**Canonical KTU figure (no longest-instruction consideration):**

$$
T_{FIQ,\text{max}} = 6 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | IRQ | FIQ |
|---|---|---|
| Latency (cycles) | ~30 | ~6 |
| Latency (ns) at 50 MHz | 600 ns | 120 ns |
| Banked R8–R12 | No | Yes |
| Vector position | `0x18` | `0x1C` (last) |
| Can preempt FIQ? | No | N/A |
| Typical Use | UART, Timers, ADC | Motor PWM, Encoder, Emergency stop |

**Justification — Why FIQ for Time-Critical Apps:**

A 50 MHz ARM7 has a 20 ns clock period. A 600 ns IRQ latency is **3% of a 20 µs control loop**, but for sub-microsecond deadlines (such as a brushless motor commutation event at 50,000 RPM, where the inter-commutation interval is 300 µs and the margin of error is 5 µs), even 600 ns matters. FIQ at 120 ns latency is **5× faster** and provides deterministic worst-case behavior because the F/I bits prevent nested masking ambiguity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Questions:** Do NOT confuse **latency** (time to first ISR instruction) with **response time** (time to ISR completion). Also, do not forget to convert MHz → period: students frequently write $1/50 = 0.02$ s (wrong by 6 orders of magnitude). Always show the **unit analysis**: $1 / (50 \times 10^6) = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ s $= 20$ ns.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4: Interrupts & Exceptions — Final Cheat Sheet**

### Core Architectural Facts

- ARM recognizes **7 exception types** + 1 reserved vector = 8 slots × 4 bytes = **32-byte** vector table at `0x00000000`.
- **Priority Order (highest → lowest):** Reset → Data Abort → FIQ → IRQ → Prefetch Abort → SWI/Undefined.
- **CPSR bits I and F** (`[7]` and `[6]`) are set automatically on exception entry to prevent nesting.
- **SPSR** exists for every privileged mode **except User and System**.
- **FIQ** is the only mode with **5 extra banked registers** (R8_fiq to R12_fiq).

### Exception Entry — 7 Mandatory Hardware Steps

1. Finish current instruction (no preemption mid-instruction).
2. `R14_<mode> = PC + Offset` (offset is exception-specific).
3. `SPSR_<mode> ← CPSR_old`.
4. `CPSR[4:0] ← New mode code`.
5. `CPSR[6] ← 1` and `CPSR[7] ← 1` (mask further interrupts).
6. `PC ← Vector[Offset]`.
7. Handler prologue: load SP, push user registers.

### Exception Exit — The `SUBS` Trick

- **IRQ / FIQ / Prefetch Abort / Undefined:** `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (skip nothing, resume at next).
- **SWI:** `MOVS PC, LR` (return to instruction **after** SWI, because the SWI has been serviced).
- **Data Abort:** `SUBS PC, LR, #8` (re-execute the faulting load/store after paging in).

### Critical Offsets to Memorize

| Exception | LR Offset | Return Form |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | N/A | N/A |
| Undefined | +4 | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |
| SWI | 0 (or +4 design-dependent) | `MOVS PC, LR` |
| Prefetch Abort | +4 | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |
| Data Abort | +8 | `SUBS PC, LR, #8` |
| IRQ | +4 | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |
| FIQ | +4 | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |

### High-Yield Numerical Formulae

- $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{clk}$
- $T_{IRQ,\text{max}} \approx 30 \times T_{clk}$
- $T_{FIQ,\text{max}} \approx 6 \times T_{clk}$
- $V_{addr} = V_{base} + 4k$ where $k \in \{0,1,2,3,4,6,7\}$
- Table size = $8 \times 4 = 32$ bytes

### LPC2148 / ARM7TDMI-Specific

- The on-chip **VIC (Vectored Interrupt Controller)** allows hardware-prioritized, vectored IRQ handling.
- The **VICVECADDR** register (read-only) contains the ISR address of the highest-priority active IRQ.
- The `VICINTENABLE` register enables individual IRQ sources.
- The `VICINTSELECT` register routes a peripheral to FIQ instead of IRQ when set to 1.

### Common KTU Exam Traps to Avoid

1. ❌ Writing `MOVS PC, LR` for IRQ return (off by 4 → infinite loop). ✅ Use `SUBS PC, LR, #4`.
2. ❌ Saying "FIQ and IRQ are the same priority." ✅ They are different (FIQ > IRQ).
3. ❌ Forgetting to subtract 4 from LR in the IRQ prologue.
4. ❌ Concluding that the user stack pointer is used in handler mode (it is **not** — banked SP is used).
5. ❌ Confusing vector table base address: `0x00000000` for standard, `0xFFFF0000` for high-vector cores.
6. ❌ Treating Data Abort return as `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (should be `#8` to re-execute).
7. ❌ Forgetting to push R14 (the user's LR) before using it in the handler.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"On entry — save CPSR, set mode, mask, branch. On exit — `SUBS` is the magic. Atomic, restores both PC and flags."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread and Handler Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thread and Handler Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ARM Cortex-M** processor architecture (the core family studied under KTU PEECT524), the processor operates in one of two mutually exclusive operating **modes** at any instant of program execution:

- **Thread Mode**: The normal execution mode in which the processor executes application code, including the main program (`main()`) and any user-level functions. This is the **default mode** upon processor reset and during the execution of all non-exception (non-interrupt) code.
- **Handler Mode**: The privileged execution mode entered automatically whenever the processor services an exception or interrupt. The processor stays in Handler Mode throughout the entire exception service routine (ISR/handler) execution until a special `EXC_RETURN` instruction sequence returns control back to Thread Mode.

These two modes are defined by the state of the **IPSR (Interrupt Program Status Register)** — when IPSR = **0**, the processor is in Thread Mode; when IPSR ≠ 0, the processor is in Handler Mode, with the value indicating the active exception number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT524 / Module 4):** The distinction between Thread Mode and Handler Mode, and their associated *access levels* (Privileged vs Unprivileged), is a high-frequency topic in KTU University Examinations. Questions often appear in Part A (3 marks) and frequently form a sub-part (a) of a 14-mark Part B question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Think of the Cortex-M processor as a **busy hospital**:

- **Thread Mode** is the **general public / out-patient ward** — the default state where "regular work" happens. Doctors and staff walk around normally, follow standard procedures, and patients are attended to. Most of the time, the hospital is in this state.
- **Handler Mode** is the **Emergency Room (ER) / ICU** — a special elevated state activated only when a critical event occurs (e.g., a cardiac arrest alarm). When the alarm rings, the hospital automatically switches all relevant resources to emergency protocols, regardless of what was happening before.

Now, within the **Thread Mode**, there is a further sub-distinction:
- **Privileged Thread Mode** → like a **senior doctor**: full access to all hospital equipment, sensitive records, and operating rooms.
- **Unprivileged Thread Mode** → like a **nursing assistant or patient attendant**: limited access, can only use certain equipment, cannot touch the pharmacy.

When an exception (e.g., a high-priority external interrupt) fires, the processor *always* jumps into **Handler Mode**, which is **always Privileged** — equivalent to the hospital instantly switching all operations to the **Emergency Department head**, who has full access to everything.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Thread Mode is where you write `main()`. Handler Mode is where you write ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines). The processor can be in Thread Mode with **either** Privileged or Unprivileged access, but Handler Mode is **always** Privileged.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following **fixed architectural constants** apply to all ARM Cortex-M3/M4/M7 processors (the family relevant to PEECT524):

| Constant | Value | Meaning |
|----------|-------|---------|
| Default mode after reset | **Thread Mode, Privileged** | Reset vector boots to MSP, Privileged Thread |
| Handler Mode privilege level | **Always Privileged** | Cannot be downgraded |
| Active exception check | **IPSR ≠ 0** | Indicates Handler Mode is active |
| Inactive exception check | **IPSR = 0** | Indicates Thread Mode is active |
| Number of stack pointers | **2** | MSP (Main Stack Pointer) and PSP (Process Stack Pointer) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Axes: Mode vs. Access Level

A critical distinction that the KTU examiner expects students to make is that **Mode** and **Access Level** are **two separate orthogonal axes**:

- **Mode** (Thread / Handler) is determined by the **IPSR** register.
- **Access Level** (Privileged / Unprivileged) is determined by the **CONTROL[0]** bit.

This gives **three possible runtime states** (not four, because Handler Mode is always Privileged):

1. **Thread Mode + Privileged** (CONTROL[0] = 0, IPSR = 0)
2. **Thread Mode + Unprivileged** (CONTROL[0] = 1, IPSR = 0)
3. **Handler Mode + Privileged** (CONTROL[0] = X, IPSR ≠ 0) — *bit 0 of CONTROL is ignored*

### 2.2 Stack Pointer Selection — The Second CONTROL Bit

The **CONTROL[1]** bit determines which stack pointer is used in Thread Mode:

- **CONTROL[1] = 0** → Use **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** — default after reset; used by the kernel, exceptions, and privileged code.
- **CONTROL[1] = 1** → Use **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** — used by application threads in RTOS environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In Handler Mode, the MSP is ALWAYS used, regardless of CONTROL[1].** This is a hard architectural rule because exceptions share a single kernel stack.

### 2.3 The CONTROL Register Layout

The **CONTROL** register is a 2-bit (technically 32-bit, but only the lowest 2 bits are defined for the standard Cortex-M3/M4) special-purpose register:

$$
\text{CONTROL} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Bit 31..2} & \text{Bit 1} & \text{Bit 0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Bit 0 — nPRIV (Not PRIVileged):**
  - `0` = Privileged
  - `1` = Unprivileged
- **Bit 1 — SPSEL (Stack Pointer SELect):**
  - `0` = MSP is current stack pointer (in Thread Mode)
  - `1` = PSP is current stack pointer (in Thread Mode)
- **Bits 31..2 — Reserved (must be 0)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Bit 0 of CONTROL can ONLY be modified from Privileged software.** Writing to it from Unprivileged code is silently ignored (it's a *read-as-zero, write-ignored* bit from Unprivileged access). This is the hardware's way of preventing unprivileged code from escalating its own privileges.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Register / Bit | Value / Rule | Effect |
|---------|---------------|--------------|--------|
| Mode determination | **IPSR** | IPSR = 0 | **Thread Mode** |
| Mode determination | **IPSR** | IPSR ≠ 0 | **Handler Mode** |
| Privilege in Thread | **CONTROL[0]** (nPRIV) | 0 | **Privileged** Thread |
| Privilege in Thread | **CONTROL[0]** (nPRIV) | 1 | **Unprivileged** Thread |
| Stack Pointer in Thread | **CONTROL[1]** (SPSEL) | 0 | Use **MSP** |
| Stack Pointer in Thread | **CONTROL[1]** (SPSEL) | 1 | Use **PSP** |
| Stack Pointer in Handler | Hardwired | — | **Always MSP** |
| Privilege in Handler | Hardwired | — | **Always Privileged** |
| Default state after reset | — | CONTROL = 0, MSP, IPSR = 0 | **Privileged Thread, MSP** |
| Reading active exception | **IPSR value** | 0..15 / 16..255 | 0 = Thread, n = exception n |
| Stack frame on exception | Hardware | — | Pushes R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR |
| Return from Handler | **EXC_RETURN** | Value in LR | Magic value, e.g., `0xFFFF_FFF9` |

### 2.5 EXC_RETURN — The Magic Return Value

When the processor enters Handler Mode, it loads **LR (Link Register)** with a special value called **EXC_RETURN**. This is a 32-bit magic number that, when written to PC (typically via `POP {PC}` or `BX LR`), causes the processor to **return from the exception**.

The most commonly seen EXC_RETURN values (you must know these for KTU):

| EXC_RETURN Value | Mode Returned To | Stack Used After Return |
|------------------|------------------|-------------------------|
| `0xFFFF_FFF9` | Handler → **Thread, MSP** | MSP |
| `0xFFFF_FFFD` | Handler → **Thread, PSP** | PSP |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the pattern: bit 2 of EXC_RETURN determines the post-return stack. `F...9` = MSP, `F...D` = PSP.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Thread/Handler mode distinction is the **foundation of RTOS (Real-Time Operating System) design** on Cortex-M:

- The **RTOS kernel** (e.g., FreeRTOS) runs in **Privileged Thread Mode** so it can access system control registers.
- **Application tasks** (user threads) are configured to run in **Unprivileged Thread Mode** with the **PSP** — this creates **memory protection** and **fault isolation**: a buggy user task cannot corrupt the kernel stack (which is the MSP).
- **ISRs** run in **Handler Mode** with MSP, ensuring deterministic, fast exception handling that doesn't depend on user stack state.

This is the same security model used in modern embedded products like automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units), medical infusion pumps, and IoT edge devices — exactly the kinds of products that hire KTU graduates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
