### 3.1 Step-by-Step: How the Processor Enters Handler Mode

Consider an external interrupt request (e.g., **IRQ5**) firing while the CPU is executing a user task in Unprivileged Thread Mode with PSP.

**Step 1 — Latency and Stacking Decision:**
The processor detects the pending exception in the NVIC. The current instruction completes. The processor prepares to take the exception.

**Step 2 — Push Stack Frame (Automatic Hardware):**
The processor switches to **MSP** automatically and pushes the following registers onto the MSP stack in this fixed order:

$$
\text{Stack Frame} = \begin{bmatrix} xPSR \\ PC \\ LR \\ R12 \\ R3 \\ R2 \\ R1 \\ R0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a fixed 8-word (32-byte on Cortex-M3/M4) frame known as the *stacking* operation. The high register (R0-R3, R12) is the *caller-saved* set; LR, PC, xPSR are the *context* registers.

**Step 3 — Read Vector Table:**
The processor reads the vector address for IRQ5 from the vector table (located at `0x0000_0000 + (16 + 5) × 4` = `0x0000_0054` on a typical Cortex-M3), and loads it into PC.

**Step 4 — Update IPSR:**
IPSR is set to **5** (the exception number), so:

$$
\text{IPSR} = 5 \neq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Mode} = \text{Handler Mode}
$$

**Step 5 — Load LR with EXC_RETURN:**
LR is loaded with `0xFFFF_FFF9` (assuming original Thread used MSP) or `0xFFFF_FFFD` (if original Thread used PSP). For our example, Thread used PSP, so:

$$
\text{LR} = 0\text{xFFFF\_FFFD}
$$

**Step 6 — Privilege is Now Privileged:**
The handler executes with **Privileged** access, regardless of what CONTROL[0] was. The processor ignores CONTROL[0] in Handler Mode.

**Step 7 — Handler Body Executes:**
The ISR body (the C function you wrote) executes. It can freely access NVIC, SCB, and any memory region.

**Step 8 — Return:**
When the handler does `POP {PC}` (compiler emits this from `__asm {bx lr}` or function return), the EXC_RETURN value is loaded into PC, triggering the *exception return sequence*:

- Hardware automatically pops R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR from MSP.
- IPSR is reset to 0 → back to Thread Mode.
- Stack pointer switches back to PSP (because the EXC_RETURN said so).
- Privilege level is restored to whatever CONTROL[0] says.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: How Privilege Downgrade Happens in Thread Mode

This is a controlled, deliberate operation typically done by an RTOS when creating a user task:

**Step 1 — Kernel starts in Privileged Thread Mode (CONTROL = 0x00).**

**Step 2 — Kernel initializes PSP** for the new task with a task-specific stack memory region.

**Step 3 — Kernel sets CONTROL[1] = 1** to switch the Thread's stack pointer to PSP:

$$
\text{CONTROL}_{\text{new}} = \text{CONTROL}_{\text{old}} \ \vert\ 0x02
$$

**Step 4 — Kernel sets CONTROL[0] = 1** to drop privilege:

$$
\text{CONTROL}_{\text{new}} = \text{CONTROL}_{\text{old}} \ \vert\ 0x01 = 0x03
$$

**Step 5 — Kernel performs an exception return** (e.g., via SVC call) to "launch" the user task. The user task now runs in **Unprivileged Thread Mode with PSP**.

**Step 6 — Memory Barrier (ISB):**
The kernel executes an `ISB` (Instruction Synchronization Barrier) to ensure the CONTROL register change is visible to subsequent instruction fetches:

$$
\text{ISB} \quad \text{(assembly: } \texttt{isb \ 0xF} \text{)}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: All Possible CPU States

| State # | Mode | Privilege | Stack | CONTROL[1:0] | IPSR | Typical Use Case |
|---------|------|-----------|-------|--------------|------|------------------|
| 1 | Thread | **Privileged** | MSP | `00` | 0 | Reset state; bare-metal `main()`; kernel code |
| 2 | Thread | **Privileged** | PSP | `10` | 0 | RTOS kernel in some configurations |
| 3 | Thread | **Unprivileged** | MSP | `01` | 0 | Memory-protected user task on bare-metal |
| 4 | Thread | **Unprivileged** | PSP | `11` | 0 | **RTOS user task** (most common protected mode) |
| 5 | Handler | **Privileged** | MSP | `xx` | ≠ 0 | All ISRs / exception handlers |
| 6 | Handler | **Unprivileged** | — | — | — | **Impossible by hardware design** |

> [!NOTE]
> Row 6 is **architecturally impossible**. The KTU examiner loves asking: *"Can Handler Mode be Unprivileged?"* — the answer is **NO, by design**.

### 3.4 Summary of Mode Transition Triggers

| From State | To State | Trigger Mechanism |
|------------|----------|-------------------|
| (Reset) | Privileged Thread, MSP | **Power-on / NRST pin** |
| Thread (any) | Handler | **Exception taken** (interrupt, fault, SVC, etc.) |
| Handler | Thread (previous) | **EXC_RETURN** popped into PC |
| Privileged Thread | Unprivileged Thread | Software writes `1` to **CONTROL[0]** |
| Unprivileged Thread | Privileged Thread | **SVC instruction** (supervisor call) — unprivileged code *cannot* write CONTROL[0] directly |
| Privileged Thread | Privileged Thread, PSP | Software writes `1` to **CONTROL[1]** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cortex-M Mode Transition State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ResetNode["RESET VECTOR"]:::reset
    ThreadPriv["THREAD MODE\nPrivileged\nCONTROL = 00\nIPSR = 0\nStack = MSP"]:::privthread
    ThreadUnpriv["THREAD MODE\nUnprivileged\nCONTROL = 01 or 11\nIPSR = 0\nStack = MSP or PSP"]:::unprivthread
    HandlerNode["HANDLER MODE\nPrivileged ONLY\nIPSR != 0\nStack = MSP always"]:::handler

    ResetNode -->|power on| ThreadPriv
    ThreadPriv -->|write CONTROL 0=1| ThreadUnpriv
    ThreadUnpriv -->|SVC instruction\nno direct write| ThreadPriv
    ThreadPriv -->|exception taken| HandlerNode
    ThreadUnpriv -->|exception taken| HandlerNode
    HandlerNode -->|EXC_RETURN\npop PC| ThreadPriv
    HandlerNode -->|EXC_RETURN FFFD| ThreadUnpriv

    classDef reset fill:#1f1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef privthread fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef unprivthread fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef handler fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: CONTROL Register and Stack Pointer Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL["CONTROL REGISTER\n[1:0]"]:::reg
    ModeDec{"Mode?\nIPSR == 0 ?"}:::decision
    HandlerBlock["HANDLER MODE\nMSP is forced\nPrivilege = forced Privileged"]:::handlerbox
    ThreadBlock["THREAD MODE\nFollows CONTROL bits"]:::threadbox
    PrivDec{"CONTROL 0\nnPRIV ?"}:::decision
    SPdec{"CONTROL 1\nSPSEL ?"}:::decision
    PrivLabel["PRIVILEGED"]:::privlabel
    UnprivLabel["UNPRIVILEGED"]:::unprivlabel
    MSPlabel["STACK = MSP"]:::mspbox
    PSPlabel["STACK = PSP"]:::pspbox

    CTRL --> ModeDec
    ModeDec -->|NO, IPSR != 0| HandlerBlock
    ModeDec -->|YES, IPSR = 0| ThreadBlock
    ThreadBlock --> PrivDec
    PrivDec -->|0| PrivLabel
    PrivDec -->|1| UnprivLabel
    ThreadBlock --> SPdec
    SPdec -->|0| MSPlabel
    SPdec -->|1| PSPlabel

    classDef reg fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef handlerbox fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef threadbox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef privlabel fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef unprivlabel fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef mspbox fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef pspbox fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#784212,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stack Frame Pushed on Exception Entry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Title["STACK FRAME ON EXCEPTION ENTRY\nPushed by hardware onto MSP"]:::title
    Addr["Higher Address\n(SP after push)"]:::addr
    XPSR["xPSR"]:::item
    PC["PC"]:::item
    LR["LR"]:::item
    R12["R12"]:::item
    R3["R3"]:::item
    R2["R2"]:::item
    R1["R1"]:::item
    R0["R0"]:::item
    SPafter["Lower Address\n(SP after push)"]:::spafter

    Title --> Addr
    Addr --> XPSR
    XPSR --> PC
    PC --> LR
    LR --> R12
    R12 --> R3
    R3 --> R2
    R2 --> R1
    R1 --> R0
    R0 --> SPafter

    classDef title fill:#1f1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef addr fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef item fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef spafter fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** *Define Thread Mode and Handler Mode in ARM Cortex-M processors. Which register is used to differentiate between them?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Thread Mode (1 mark):** It is the default execution mode of the Cortex-M processor in which the main application code, RTOS tasks, and all non-exception code runs. After reset, the processor begins execution in Thread Mode.
- **Handler Mode (1 mark):** It is the privileged execution mode entered automatically when the processor services an exception or interrupt. The processor remains in Handler Mode for the entire duration of the exception handler (ISR).
- **Differentiating Register (1 mark):** The **IPSR (Interrupt Program Status Register)** is used to differentiate between the two modes. If IPSR = 0, the processor is in Thread Mode; if IPSR ≠ 0, the processor is in Handler Mode, with the value indicating the exception number being serviced.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *State the role of the CONTROL register in ARM Cortex-M. List its two defined bits and their functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **CONTROL register (1 mark)** is a special-purpose register in the Cortex-M processor that controls two key aspects of Thread Mode operation: the privilege access level and the active stack pointer.
- **Bit 0 — nPRIV (1 mark):** When `0`, Thread Mode executes with Privileged access. When `1`, Thread Mode executes with Unprivileged access. This bit is writable only from Privileged code.
- **Bit 1 — SPSEL (1 mark):** When `0`, the MSP is used as the current stack pointer in Thread Mode. When `1`, the PSP is used as the current stack pointer in Thread Mode. In Handler Mode, this bit is ignored and the MSP is always used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** The 14-mark question on Thread/Handler Modes will typically be a Module-4 question with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are each 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model]*

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Thread Mode** and **Handler Mode** of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor. Discuss the role of the **CONTROL register** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Modes (2 marks):**
The Cortex-M3 supports two operating modes — **Thread Mode** for normal program execution (default after reset) and **Handler Mode** for exception/interrupt servicing. The current mode is reflected in the **IPSR** register: value 0 indicates Thread Mode, any non-zero value indicates Handler Mode with the active exception number.

**Thread Mode (2 marks):**
In Thread Mode, the processor executes the application code, including the main function, library code, and (in RTOS contexts) user tasks. Thread Mode may execute with either **Privileged** access (full access to all system resources) or **Unprivileged** access (limited to user-level instructions and memory). The privilege level and the active stack pointer in Thread Mode are controlled by the **CONTROL register**.

**Handler Mode (1.5 marks):**
Handler Mode is entered automatically on taking any exception. In Handler Mode, the processor is **always Privileged** and **always uses the MSP**, regardless of the CONTROL register's contents. The processor stays in Handler Mode until an exception return (EXC_RETURN) is executed, restoring Thread Mode.

**CONTROL Register (1.5 marks):**
The CONTROL register has two defined bits:
- **Bit 0 (nPRIV):** Selects the privilege level in Thread Mode (0 = Privileged, 1 = Unprivileged). Ignored in Handler Mode. Writable only from Privileged code.
- **Bit 1 (SPSEL):** Selects the active stack pointer in Thread Mode (0 = MSP, 1 = PSP). Ignored in Handler Mode.

After reset, CONTROL = 0x00, so the processor is in **Privileged Thread Mode using MSP**.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **EXC_RETURN** in the ARM Cortex-M3 architecture. How does it enable returning from Handler Mode to Thread Mode? List any two EXC_RETURN values and their meaning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of EXC_RETURN (2.5 marks):**
EXC_RETURN is a special 32-bit value automatically loaded into the **Link Register (LR)** by hardware when an exception is taken. It is not a normal return address — rather, it is a *magic value* that, when subsequently loaded into the PC (typically via `POP {PC}` or `BX LR` at the end of an ISR), causes the processor to perform the **exception return sequence**.

The exception return sequence includes:
1. Automatic unstacking of R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, and xPSR from the MSP.
2. Restoring IPSR to 0 (returning to Thread Mode).
3. Switching the stack pointer back to PSP if the original Thread was using PSP.
4. Restoring the privilege level as defined by CONTROL[0].

**How It Enables Return (2 marks):**
By loading EXC_RETURN into PC, the processor recognizes that this is not a normal branch target but a return-from-exception operation. The hardware then reverses the stacking operation performed on exception entry, restoring the interrupted context seamlessly. This mechanism is fundamental to nested and pre-emptible interrupt handling.

**Two EXC_RETURN Values (2.5 marks):**
- `0xFFFF_FFF9` → Return to **Thread Mode using MSP**. (Bit 2 = 0)
- `0xFFFF_FFFD` → Return to **Thread Mode using PSP**. (Bit 2 = 1)

The remaining bits are all 1s, with bits 31:4 set as architecturally reserved, making these values easily recognizable by hardware.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between **Privileged** and **Unprivileged** access levels in ARM Cortex-M. Explain how unprivileged code in Thread Mode can call back into privileged code. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Privileged vs Unprivileged (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Privileged | Unprivileged |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Code that can run** | Kernel, ISRs, system control | User applications |
| **Register access** | All registers including CONTROL, NVIC, SCB | Most registers; **no** access to CONTROL, NVIC, SCB |
| **Memory access** | Full memory map | May be restricted by MPU (if configured) |
| **Default after reset** | Yes | No |
| **CONTROL[0] value** | 0 | 1 |
| **CPSID/CPSIE instructions** | Allowed | Fault (NOCP / UsageFault) |

**Mechanism for Returning to Privileged (2 marks):**
Unprivileged code in Thread Mode **cannot** directly modify CONTROL[0] to escalate its privilege. The legitimate, hardware-supported mechanism is the **SVC (Supervisor Call)** instruction. When unprivileged code executes `SVC`, the processor takes an SVC exception, which by hardware design enters **Handler Mode (always Privileged)**. The SVC handler (running in Privileged Handler Mode) can then perform the privileged operation on behalf of the user task and return to Thread Mode via EXC_RETURN.

**Practical Example (2 marks):**
In FreeRTOS, when a user task calls `printf` or `malloc`, the C library eventually issues an SVC (often via the `vPortSVCHandler` in `port.c`) to request kernel services like context switching or memory allocation. The kernel's SVC handler operates in Privileged Handler Mode, performs the operation, and returns to the user task. This is the **microkernel-style syscall pattern** that ensures user code can never accidentally (or maliciously) corrupt the kernel.

---

**(b)** With a suitable state diagram, explain the **mode transition** mechanism in the ARM Cortex-M3 processor when an interrupt occurs. What happens to the stack pointer, IPSR, and CONTROL register during this transition? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode Transition on Interrupt (3 marks):**
When an interrupt request is accepted by the NVIC, the processor transitions from Thread Mode to Handler Mode through the following sequence:
1. The currently executing instruction completes.
2. The processor automatically stacks 8 registers (xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3–R0) onto the **MSP**.
3. The IPSR is updated with the exception number.
4. The PC is loaded with the interrupt vector address from the vector table.
5. The LR is loaded with the appropriate **EXC_RETURN** value.

**Stack Pointer Behavior (1.5 marks):**
- **During stacking:** Stack pointer automatically becomes **MSP** (regardless of SPSEL).
- **During Handler execution:** Stack pointer remains **MSP**.
- **On exception return:** Stack pointer reverts to PSP if SPSEL = 1 in the original Thread, else remains MSP.

**IPSR Behavior (1.5 marks):**
IPSR is updated to the exception number on entry and reset to 0 on return. This is the **defining register** for mode determination.

**CONTROL Register Behavior (1 mark):**
CONTROL[1:0] is **preserved** across the mode transition. The privilege level and stack pointer choice of the original Thread are saved in the stack frame, not in CONTROL. On exception return, CONTROL is unchanged, but execution resumes according to the **saved CONTROL bits** as part of the unstacked context.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Evaluation:**
> 1. **Confusing Mode with Privilege (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write "Handler Mode is Unprivileged" — this is **architecturally impossible**. Mode and Privilege are separate concepts; Handler Mode is *always* Privileged.
> 2. **Wrong register for mode determination (–1 mark):** Some students cite the CONTROL register or PRIMASK for mode determination. The correct register is **IPSR**. CONTROL controls *privilege and stack pointer*, not mode.
> 3. **Forgetting that CONTROL[0] is write-protected from Unprivileged (–1 mark):** Stating that an unprivileged task can simply write `1 → 0` to CONTROL[0] to escalate privilege is wrong; the hardware silently ignores such writes.
> 4. **Missing the role of SVC (–1 to –2 marks):** When asked "how does unprivileged code call kernel code?", the answer must mention **SVC** — not just "through function calls" or "through the API". The SVC instruction is the **only** legitimate hardware gate.
> 5. **Forgetting the stack frame contents (–1 mark):** When asked what gets pushed on exception entry, students often list only PC and LR. The full list (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) must be memorized.
> 6. **Not stating the EXC_RETURN value format (–1 mark):** If asked for an EXC_RETURN value, students must specify the *exact* hex (e.g., `0xFFFF_FFF9`), not just "a special value".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thread and Handler Modes (PEECT524 / Module 4)**

- [ ] **Two modes exist:** Thread Mode (normal) and Handler Mode (exception).
- [ ] **IPSR = 0 → Thread Mode;** IPSR ≠ 0 → Handler Mode.
- [ ] **Handler Mode is ALWAYS Privileged** and **ALWAYS uses MSP.**
- [ ] **CONTROL register has 2 defined bits:** Bit 0 = nPRIV (privilege), Bit 1 = SPSEL (stack pointer select).
- [ ] **CONTROL[0] = 1 → Unprivileged Thread;** = 0 → Privileged Thread.
- [ ] **CONTROL[1] = 1 → PSP in Thread;** = 0 → MSP in Thread.
- [ ] **CONTROL[0] is write-protected** from Unprivileged code; unprivileged tasks can only escalate privilege via **SVC**.
- [ ] **After reset:** CONTROL = 0x00, Thread Mode, Privileged, MSP active.
- [ ] **On exception entry:** Hardware pushes 8 registers (xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3–R0) onto MSP, sets IPSR to exception number, loads PC from vector table, loads LR with EXC_RETURN.
- [ ] **EXC_RETURN values to memorize:** `0xFFFF_FFF9` (return to Thread with MSP), `0xFFFF_FFFD` (return to Thread with PSP).
- [ ] **EXC_RETURN bit 2:** 0 = MSP, 1 = PSP.
- [ ] **On exception return:** Hardware pops the stack frame, resets IPSR to 0, switches stack pointer based on EXC_RETURN.
- [ ] **CONTROL register is NOT modified** by the exception entry/exit mechanism; the original Thread's CONTROL bits are restored as part of the unstacked context.
- [ ] **RTOS pattern:** Kernel runs in Privileged Thread, user tasks in Unprivileged Thread with PSP, ISRs in Handler Mode.
- [ ] **Bare-metal pattern:** Everything runs in Privileged Thread, no PSP used, exceptions handled in Handler Mode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entering the Exception Handler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Entering the Exception Handler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception Entry (ARM Architecture Reference):** *Exception Entry* is the well-defined, atomic hardware sequence performed by the ARM core the instant an exception is recognized. The processor automatically (i) **saves the return address into the banked Link Register (LR\_mode)** of the new mode, (ii) **copies the Current Program Status Register (CPSR) into the Saved Program Status Register (SPSR\_mode)**, (iii) **switches the processor into the corresponding privileged mode** by rewriting the mode bits of CPSR, and (iv) **branches to the address held in the corresponding word of the Vector Table** located at the fixed base address `0x00000000` (or `0xFFFF0000` if high-vector configuration is selected).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this is the **hardware-controlled prologue** that prepares the ARM core to begin executing the **Exception Handler** written by the programmer at the vector address.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Protocol**
> Imagine a busy office (normal program execution). Suddenly, an employee faints (an exception occurs). What does the receptionist (the ARM hardware) do *automatically*?
> 1. **Pulls the fire alarm** → announces that something abnormal happened.
> 2. **Notes down which room the fainted person was working in** (saves CPSR to SPSR — remembers the "before" state).
> 3. **Assigns a temporary safety officer to handle the situation** (switches to a privileged mode like IRQ or FIQ).
> 4. **Directs everyone to the emergency assembly point listed on the wall** (jumps to the address in the Vector Table).
> 5. **Records the door from which the patient will return** (stores the return address in LR).
>
> The exception handler is then free to diagnose, treat, and finally *return* the patient to the same room using `SUBS PC, LR, #offset` (the magic "return to work" instruction).

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weight Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU University Exams, "Entering the Exception Handler" is regularly asked as a **Part B (14 mark)** question, often combined with the **Vector Table** or **Exception Exit / Returning from Exception Handler**. A student must remember the **exact LR\_enc values** for each of the **seven (7) ARM exceptions** and the **two privileged-mode bits** (I and F) that get set. This is a **board-exam favourite** under Module 4 of PEECT524.

## 1.4 The Seven ARM Exceptions (Context Recap)

| # | Exception | Triggered By |
|---|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Reset** | Power-on / nRESET signal |
| 2 | **Undefined Instruction** | Unrecognised opcode |
| 3 | **Software Interrupt (SWI)** | `SVC` instruction |
| 4 | **Prefetch Abort** | Instruction fetch memory failure |
| 5 | **Data Abort** | Load/Store memory failure |
| 6 | **IRQ** | Normal interrupt (masked by I-bit) |
| 7 | **FIQ** | Fast interrupt (masked by F-bit) |

Each of these seven exceptions has its own dedicated word in the Vector Table and its own privileged mode.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Atomic Exception Entry Sequence (Logical Decomposition)

The ARM7TDMI / ARM9 / Cortex-A class processors follow a strict, **non-interruptible** sequence the moment an exception is taken:

### Step 1 — Save the Return Address into LR\_mode
The address of the instruction that was about to be executed is copied into `R14_mode` (i.e., LR of the new mode). The exact value depends on the type of exception because the pipeline must be flushed correctly.

### Step 2 — Copy CPSR into SPSR\_mode
`SPSR_mode = CPSR` — this snapshot preserves the *exact* condition flags, control bits, and mode of the *interrupted* code so that the handler can later restore the original context on exit.

### Step 3 — Force Processor into Privileged Mode
The mode bits of CPSR are overwritten to the **new privileged mode** (e.g., `10011` for SVC). This automatically switches the banked registers `R8–R14` and `SPSR` to the physical bank associated with that mode.

### Step 4 — Disable Further Interrupts (where appropriate)
The **I-bit** (and for Reset, the **F-bit**) of CPSR is set to `1` to prevent nested interrupts from corrupting the critical handler prologue.

### Step 5 — Branch to Vector Table Entry
The Program Counter (PC) is loaded with the address contained in the Vector Table at the offset corresponding to the exception, and execution begins from there.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The ARM hardware performs *only* the above five steps. The actual saving of working registers (R0–R12) into the handler's stack frame is the **software's responsibility**, and is the reason every ARM handler begins with a `STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}` push.

## 2.2 The Seven Vector Table Entries

| Vector Offset | Exception | Mode Entered | I | F | LR\_enc Value (PC+offset) |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| `0x00` | **Reset** | Supervisor (SVC) | 1 | 1 | — (not returnable) |
| `0x04` | **Undefined Instruction** | Undefined (UND) | 1 | — | `PC + 4` |
| `0x08` | **Software Interrupt (SWI)** | Supervisor (SVC) | 1 | — | `PC + 4` |
| `0x0C` | **Prefetch Abort** | Abort (ABT) | 1 | — | `PC + 4` |
| `0x10` | **Data Abort** | Abort (ABT) | 1 | — | `PC + 8` |
| `0x14` | Reserved | — | — | — | — |
| `0x18` | **IRQ** | IRQ | 1 | — | `PC + 4` |
| `0x1C` | **FIQ** | FIQ | 1 | 1 | `PC + 4` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **R, U, S, P, D, _, I, F** → *R*eset, *U*ndefined, *S*WI, *P*refetch, *D*ata, *I*RQ, *F*IQ at offsets `0x00, 0x04, 0x08, 0x0C, 0x10, 0x18, 0x1C`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Item | Value / Description | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Base Vector Address (Normal) | `0x00000000` | Hex |
| Base Vector Address (High — ARM926EJ-S etc.) | `0xFFFF0000` | Hex |
| Vector Table Word Size | **4 bytes (1 word)** per exception | Bytes |
| Total Vector Table Size (with reserved) | **32 bytes (8 words)** | Bytes |
| Total Banked Registers per mode | `R8–R14` plus `SPSR` (FIQ also has `R8_fiq – R11_fiq`) | Registers |
| CPSR Mode Bits for SVC | `10011` (binary) / `0x13` | — |
| CPSR Mode Bits for IRQ | `10010` (binary) / `0x12` | — |
| CPSR Mode Bits for FIQ | `10001` (binary) / `0x11` | — |
| CPSR Mode Bits for ABT | `10111` (binary) / `0x17` | — |
| CPSR Mode Bits for UND | `11011` (binary) / `0x1B` | — |
| `LR` Return Offset — Data Abort | `+8` from the aborted instruction | Bytes |
| `LR` Return Offset — All Other Exceptions | `+4` from the triggering instruction | Bytes |

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Return Address Offset

> [!NOTE]
> The ARM pipeline is **3-stages** (Fetch → Decode → Execute). At the moment a Data Abort is recognized, the aborted Load/Store has already been issued from the Execute stage, so the architecturally-correct place to retry is **two instructions later** → `PC + 8`. For all *non-abort* exceptions, the offending instruction is in Execute but no side-effect has occurred, so the resume point is `PC + 4`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This exception-entry mechanism is what makes ARM a **deterministic real-time processor**. In an automotive ECU or a smartphone modem, when a peripheral raises an IRQ, the core must respond within a **bounded latency** in the order of **tens of clock cycles**. The hardware-automatic vector-jump + register-banking design is the cornerstone of that low-latency guarantee, and is one of the primary reasons ARM dominates **embedded controller** and **mobile SoC** markets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — What Happens Cycle-by-Cycle on an IRQ

Let us assume the processor is running in **User mode** (`0b10000`) at address `0x00008000` and the `IRQ` line goes high at the exact moment a new instruction is being **fetched**.

### Step 1 — Exception is Recognized

The core samples the IRQ pin at the end of the **Execute** stage of the current instruction.

$$
\text{IRQ recognized at} \; PC_{\text{fetch}} = 0x00008000
$$

### Step 2 — Save Return Address into LR\_IRQ

Since IRQ is a normal exception (not Data Abort), the resume point is the next instruction:

$$
LR_{IRQ} \;=\; PC_{fetch} \;+\; 4 \;=\; 0x00008000 \;+\; 0x4 \;=\; 0x00008004
$$

> **Textual Logic:** The hardware adds **+4** because the interrupted instruction at `0x00008000` is to be *abandoned* (interrupts do not auto-restart) and the *next* sequential instruction at `0x00008004` is the resume point.

### Step 3 — Copy CPSR to SPSR\_IRQ

$$
SPSR_{IRQ} \;=\; CPSR_{\text{User}} \;=\; \text{(I=0, F=0, mode=0b10000, N|Z|C|V preserved)}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** The interrupt must remember the *exact* arithmetic flags and mode of the user's code so that, on return, the conditional code that the user was executing (`BEQ`, `BGT`, etc.) behaves identically.

### Step 4 — Modify CPSR Bits

The hardware sets the new mode bits and disables further interrupts:

$$
\text{New CPSR mode} = 0b10010 \;(\text{IRQ mode})
$$

$$
I_{bit} \;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad ; \quad F_{bit} \;\leftarrow\; \text{unchanged (IRQ does not auto-mask FIQ)}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** Masking the **I-bit** prevents a *second* IRQ from pre-empting the current IRQ handler before the handler has even saved its registers. FIQ is *not* masked, so a higher-priority Fast Interrupt can still pre-empt — this is the cornerstone of the ARM **nested-interrupt** design.

### Step 5 — Branch to Vector Table

$$
PC \;\leftarrow\; \text{Memory}[0x00000000 + 0x18] \;=\; \text{Memory}[0x00000018]
$$

> **Textual Logic:** The PC is forced to the word at vector offset `0x18` (32 bytes minus 8 bytes from the base = the IRQ slot). The instruction fetched from there becomes the *first instruction* of the IRQ handler.

### Step 6 — Handler Software Begins (User-Written)

The instructions at offset `0x18` are usually a *branch* to the actual handler body:

$$
\text{Memory}[0x18] = 0xEA000047 \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; \text{B handler\_IRQ}
$$

The handler then performs the **software prologue**:

$$
\text{STMFD} \; SP_{IRQ}! , \, \{\, R0 - R12 , LR_{IRQ} \,\}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** This pushes the caller's general-purpose registers and the *return LR* onto the IRQ-mode stack so the handler is free to corrupt them.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Data Abort Return Offset

A `LDR R3, [R0]` at `PC = 0x00010000` triggers a Data Abort because `R0` holds an unmapped address.

$$
LR_{ABT} \;=\; PC \;+\; 8 \;=\; 0x00010008
$$

To return, the handler executes:

$$
\text{SUBS} \; PC , \, LR , \, \#8
$$

This restores `PC` to `0x00010000` and the CPSR from `SPSR_abt`, allowing the *same* `LDR` to be retried — essential for **paging** in a virtual-memory OS.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How the Hardware Chooses LR\_offset

| Exception Type | Pipeline State at Recognition | Architectural Resume Point | LR Encoded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | Garbage | None (cold start) | — |
| **Undefined** | Execute stage | Next sequential | `PC + 4` |
| **SWI** | Execute stage | Next sequential | `PC + 4` |
| **Prefetch Abort** | Decode stage | Aborted instruction (re-fetch) | `PC + 4` |
| **Data Abort** | Execute stage | Aborted instruction (re-execute) | `PC + 8` |
| **IRQ** | Execute stage | Next sequential | `PC + 4` |
| **FIQ** | Execute stage | Next sequential | `PC + 4` |

## 3.4 The Five Hardware State Changes — Compact Mathematical Form

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
& (1)\;\; LR_{mode} \;\leftarrow\; PC + \Delta \\
& (2)\;\; SPSR_{mode} \;\leftarrow\; CPSR \\
& (3)\;\; CPSR[4:0] \;\leftarrow\; \text{new\_mode\_bits} \\
& (4)\;\; CPSR[7] \;\leftarrow\; I_{new} \quad ; \quad CPSR[6] \;\leftarrow\; F_{new} \\
& (5)\;\; PC \;\leftarrow\; \text{VectorTable}[ \text{offset}_{exc} ]
\end{aligned}
}
$$

where $\Delta = 8$ for Data Abort and $\Delta = 4$ for all other returnable exceptions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Exception Entry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Program Execution in User or System Mode] --> B{Exception Signal Detected by ARM Core}
    B --> C[Step 1 Save Return Address PC+4 or PC+8 into LR_mode]
    C --> D[Step 2 Copy CPSR into SPSR_mode Snapshot of Interrupted State]
    D --> E[Step 3 Overwrite CPSR Mode Bits to Privileged Mode]
    E --> F[Step 4 Set I bit and F bit in CPSR to Mask Further Interrupts]
    F --> G[Step 5 Load PC with Vector Table Address at Fixed Offset]
    G --> H[Begin Executing Exception Handler Software at Vector Address]
    H --> I[Software Prologue Push R0 to R12 and LR onto Stack]
    I --> J[Handler Body Executes Service Routine for Exception]
    J --> K[Software Epilogue Pop R0 to R12 and LR from Stack]
    K --> L[Return from Handler via SUBS PC LR or RFED in Thumb]
```

## 4.2 ARM Vector Table — Memory Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    V0[0x00 Reset Vector] --> V1[0x04 Undefined Instruction Vector]
    V1 --> V2[0x08 Software Interrupt Vector]
    V2 --> V3[0x0C Prefetch Abort Vector]
    V3 --> V4[0x10 Data Abort Vector]
    V4 --> V5[0x14 Reserved Do Not Use]
    V5 --> V6[0x18 IRQ Vector]
    V6 --> V7[0x1C FIQ Vector]
```

## 4.3 Banked Register Activation on Mode Switch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER[User Mode Registers] -->|Exception Recognized| SWITCH[ARM Core Mode Switcher]
    SWITCH --> SVC[Banked R13 SVC SP_svc R14 SVC LR_svc SPSR_svc]
    SWITCH --> IRQ[Banked R13 IRQ SP_irq R14 IRQ LR_irq SPSR_irq]
    SWITCH --> FIQ[Banked R8 to R14 FIQ SPSR_fiq]
    SWITCH --> ABT[Banked R13 ABT SP_abt R14 ABT LR_abt SPSR_abt]
    SWITCH --> UND[Banked R13 UND SP_und R14 UND LR_und SPSR_und]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each of the five privileged modes above gets its **own private copy** of `R13` (Stack Pointer), `R14` (Link Register), and `SPSR`. The **FIQ mode** is special — it also has its own `R8`, `R9`, `R10`, `R11`, and `R12`, which is why FIQ handlers can avoid stacking any extra registers for the most common case.

## 4.4 CPSR Bit-Level Modification During Exception Entry

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[CPSR Before Exception] --> Q[Hardware Saves P into SPSR_mode]
    Q --> R[CPSR Mode Bits 4 to 0 are Overwritten with New Mode Code]
    R --> S[CPSR I bit 7 is set to 1 to mask IRQ]
    S --> T[CPSR F bit 6 is set to 1 only for Reset and FIQ]
    T --> U[CPSR T bit is Cleared to Force ARM State]
    U --> V[New CPSR is Now Active for Handler]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the role of the Link Register (LR) when an ARM exception is taken. Why is the value stored in LR different for a Data Abort compared to an IRQ?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
*(1 mark)* When an exception occurs, the ARM core automatically saves the address of the next instruction to be executed (the resume address) into the banked Link Register of the new mode (e.g., `LR_IRQ`, `LR_ABT`).

*(1 mark)* For an **IRQ** the resume point is the next sequential instruction, so the hardware stores **`PC + 4`**.

*(1 mark)* For a **Data Abort**, the offending Load/Store instruction must be re-executed (so the OS can resolve the page fault), hence the hardware stores **`PC + 8`**, pointing two instructions ahead of the aborted instruction.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the purpose of copying CPSR into SPSR during exception entry? List the fields of CPSR that get modified by the hardware.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
*(1 mark)* `SPSR_mode ← CPSR` saves a *snapshot* of the interrupted program's status so that the handler can **fully restore** the original state (mode, condition flags, control bits) on exception exit.

*(1 mark)* During entry, the hardware modifies the **Mode bits `[4:0]`** of CPSR to the new privileged mode code.

*(1 mark)* It also modifies the **I-bit `[7]`** to disable further IRQs (and the **F-bit `[6]`** for Reset and FIQ), and clears the **T-bit `[5]`** to force ARM state.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **ARM exception vector table**. List the address of each vector and the processor mode that is entered for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail the **sequence of operations performed by the ARM core when it enters an exception handler**. Use the example of an IRQ being raised while the processor is executing a User-mode instruction. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Vector Table Diagram (Block Layout):**

| Vector Address | Exception | Mode Entered |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00000000` | **Reset** | Supervisor (SVC) — `0b10011` |
| `0x00000004` | **Undefined Instruction** | Undefined (UND) — `0b11011` |
| `0x00000008` | **Software Interrupt** | Supervisor (SVC) — `0b10011` |
| `0x0000000C` | **Prefetch Abort** | Abort (ABT) — `0b10111` |
| `0x00000010` | **Data Abort** | Abort (ABT) — `0b10111` |
| `0x00000014` | **Reserved** | — |
| `0x00000018` | **IRQ** | IRQ — `0b10010` |
| `0x0000001C` | **FIQ** | FIQ — `0b10001` |

*(2 marks)* The vector table is a **32-byte block of 8 words** located at the fixed base address `0x00000000` (normal vectors) or `0xFFFF0000` (high vectors on ARM926EJ-S and later). Each entry is a 32-bit ARM instruction — typically a **branch** to the actual handler body because the 32-byte region is too small to hold the handler itself.

*(2 marks)* The table is **architecturally defined** — its offsets are part of the ARM specification and cannot be moved (only relocated in physical memory by remapping the high-vector bit in CP15 on Cortex-A cores).

*(1 mark)* The reserved slot at `0x14` must contain a *valid* instruction (e.g., a NOP or branch to an error routine) so that a spurious vector fetch does not execute garbage.

*(2 marks)* The mode associated with each vector ensures that **the handler runs in a privileged mode** with its own private banked registers, isolated from the user task that was interrupted.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Assume the processor is at `PC = 0x00008000` in **User mode (`0b10000`)** and an IRQ is asserted.

*(1 mark)* **Step 1 — Save Return Address:**
The hardware computes `LR_IRQ = 0x00008000 + 0x04 = 0x00008004`. This is the next sequential instruction that the user's code would have run.

*(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Copy CPSR to SPSR:**
`SPSR_IRQ ← CPSR`. The snapshot captures the current mode (`User`), the I-bit (`0`), F-bit (`0`), the T-bit, and the condition flags `N, Z, C, V`.

*(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Change Processor Mode:**
The mode bits of CPSR are rewritten to `0b10010` (IRQ mode). The banked registers `R13_irq`, `R14_irq`, and `SPSR_irq` become immediately accessible.

*(1 mark)* **Step 4 — Mask Interrupts:**
The I-bit in CPSR is set to `1`, preventing another IRQ from interrupting the current handler. The F-bit is *not* changed, so a higher-priority FIQ can still pre-empt.

*(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Branch to Vector:**
`PC ← Memory[0x00000018]`. The instruction at that address is typically `B handler_IRQ`.

*(1 mark)* **Step 6 — Software Prologue:**
The handler immediately executes `STMFD SP_irq!, {R0–R12, LR}` to push the user's working registers onto the IRQ stack. Only after this push is the handler free to perform its actual work.

*(1 mark)* **Conclusion / Clean Return:**
On exit, the handler executes `SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4` (or `SUBS PC, LR`, depending on save strategy), which atomically *pops* the CPSR from `SPSR_irq` and *branches* back to `0x00008004` in User mode.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative)

> **(a)** Compare the actions taken by the ARM core when entering a **Data Abort** versus an **IRQ**. Highlight the difference in the value loaded into the Link Register. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **banked registers**. Why does the ARM architecture use them, and what would happen if banked registers did not exist? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Step | Data Abort | IRQ |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Memory failure on Load/Store | External IRQ pin |
| Mode Entered | Abort (ABT) — `0b10111` | IRQ — `0b10010` |
| `LR` saved | `PC + 8` (re-execute faulting instr.) | `PC + 4` (next instruction) |
| `SPSR` saved | Yes | Yes |
| I-bit set | Yes | Yes |
| F-bit set | No | No |

*(2 marks)* The **core difference** is the `+8` vs `+4` offset. The Data Abort handler must be able to re-run the *same* Load/Store once the page fault is resolved, so the LR is left pointing two instructions ahead of the faulting instruction.

*(2 marks)* Both exceptions switch to a **privileged mode** with banked `R13` and `R14`, and both mask the I-bit so that the handler prologue is not corrupted by a nested IRQ.

*(1 mark)* The Data Abort is **not** auto-maskable by SWI-style instructions — the architectural intent is to let the OS use it for **demand-paging** and **copy-on-write** semantics, which require deterministic re-execution.

*(2 marks)* Practical implication: the Data Abort handler must be a **fully re-entrant routine** that *never* assumes the Load/Store partially completed. The aborted instruction must be re-issued in its original form.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

*(1 mark)* **Definition:** *Banked registers* are physically separate copies of registers `R8–R14` and `SPSR` that exist *per privileged mode*. Only the bank belonging to the *current* mode is accessible by software.

*(2 marks)* **Why ARM uses them:** When an exception is taken, the core does *not* need to *save* the user's `R13` (Stack Pointer) and `R14` (Link Register) to memory before installing the handler's values — the banked copies are *automatically* switched in by simply changing the mode bits. This makes exception entry **zero-cycle overhead** in terms of register preservation.

*(2 marks)* **FIQ's special banking:** FIQ mode banks an *extra* five registers (`R8_fiq – R12_fiq`), allowing a tightly-written FIQ handler to begin real work *without* ever pushing a register onto the stack — yielding the **lowest possible interrupt latency** in the entire ARM7/ARM9 family.

*(1 mark)* **Consequence of removing banking:** If banked registers did not exist, the hardware would have to *explicitly push* the user's `R13`/`R14` to memory on every exception entry, adding dozens of cycles and complicating the design of the pipeline. Real-time guarantees would be severely weakened.

*(1 mark)* **Bonus point for KTU:** A common interview-style extension question is *"How many physical registers does the ARM core have in total?"* The answer is **37** — namely `R0–R7` (shared) + `R8–R12` (5 banked for FIQ, 5 for User) + `R13`, `R14`, `SPSR` (5 banked for each of the 5 privileged modes) + `CPSR` + `PC`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `PC + 4` with `PC + 8`** — This is the **single most common mistake** in KTU answer sheets. Remember: only **Data Abort** uses `+8`; **everything else** uses `+4`. Board examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this alone.
> 2. **Forgetting to set the I-bit (and F-bit for FIQ/Reset)** — When asked "what does the hardware modify in CPSR?", students often mention only the mode bits. The **I-bit and F-bit** changes are equally important.
> 3. **Claiming that user registers `R0–R12` are banked** — They are *not*. Only `R8–R14` (and SPSR) are banked. Stating otherwise is a factual error.
> 4. **Writing that the hardware pushes registers to the stack** — It does **not**. Stacking of `R0–R12` and LR is *software's* responsibility, done by the handler's first instructions.
> 5. **Conflating "exception" with "interrupt"** — IRQ and FIQ are *specific* exceptions; an exception is a *broader* category that includes Reset, SWI, undefined instructions, and aborts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **Vector Table** is a 32-byte region at `0x00000000` containing 8 words (one per exception + 1 reserved).
- [x] On exception entry, the hardware performs **5 automatic actions**: save `LR`, copy `CPSR → SPSR`, switch mode, mask I/F bits, jump to vector.
- [x] **`LR` value** is `PC + 4` for **all exceptions except Data Abort** (which is `PC + 8`).
- [x] **Banked registers** per mode: `R13_mode`, `R14_mode`, `SPSR_mode` — and FIQ additionally banks `R8–R12`.
- [x] **CPSR mode bits** to memorize: SVC `0b10011`, IRQ `0b10010`, FIQ `0b10001`, ABT `0b10111`, UND `0b11011`, User/System `0b10000`.
- [x] The **I-bit (CPSR[7])** is set to `1` for *all* exceptions; the **F-bit (CPSR[6])** is set only for **Reset and FIQ**.
- [x] The **T-bit (CPSR[5])** is cleared on exception entry, forcing the handler to run in **ARM state** (32-bit) even if the user code was in Thumb state.
- [x] **Software** must push `R0–R12` and `LR` onto the stack; **hardware** does not do this.
- [x] The **mnemonic** *R-U-S-P-D-_-I-F* gives the vector order: Reset, Undefined, SWI, Prefetch, Data, (reserved), IRQ, FIQ.
- [x] **Return** from a handler is done with `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (or `#8` for Data Abort) which atomically restores CPSR from SPSR and branches back.
- [x] **High-vector configuration** (`0xFFFF0000`) is available on ARM926EJ-S and later, controlled by a bit in CP15 (Cortex-A class).
- [x] A **reserved vector slot** at `0x14` must still contain a valid instruction to avoid a fault on spurious fetch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Returning From Exception Handler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Returning From Exception Handler # 🎯 Returning From Exception Handler — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **ARM Cortex-M architecture**, the process of **Returning From an Exception Handler** is a hardware-controlled mechanism by which the processor restores the saved context (general-purpose registers, special registers, and program counter) from the stack frame that was automatically pushed onto the active stack during exception entry. The return is triggered by writing a special 32-bit magic value called **EXC_RETURN** into the **Program Counter (PC)**. The processor recognizes this value, decodes the embedded mode and stack selection bits, performs the unstacking (pop) operation, and resumes normal execution of the interrupted program. > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXC_RETURN is NOT a normal instruction or branch address.** It is a special token value (in the range `0xFFFF_FF00` to `0xFFFF_FFFF`) that the Cortex-M core interprets as a "Return From Exception" command. The generic structure of the EXC_RETURN token is: $$ \text{EXC\_RETURN} = 0x\text{FFFFFF} \vert \text{[S F T Ftype MODE SPSEL]} $$ Where the lower 6 bits (bits [5:0]) carry control information used by the return mechanism. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you borrowed a colleague's office to do urgent work (an exception handler). When you entered, the receptionist (hardware) automatically: - Photocopied all your pocket items (saved R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR onto the stack) - Gave you a special "Exit Token" (EXC_RETURN) for the door When your urgent work is done, you walk to the door and hand the Exit Token to the security guard. The guard: 1. Reads the token to know **which reception desk to return items to** (MSP vs PSP) 2. Reads the token to know **whether to go back to a normal user or another staff room** (Thread vs Handler mode) 3. Restores all your pocket items (unstacking) 4. Escorts you back to your original office (resumes interrupted code) > [!NOTE] > In ARM, the "Exit Token" is `EXC_RETURN` and the "security guard" is the write-back logic of the CPU when PC receives this value. The photocopy = stack frame; restoring = unstacking. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Default EXC_RETURN value for Handler-to-Thread return using MSP:** `0xFFFF_FFF9` - **Default EXC_RETURN value for Handler-to-Thread return using PSP:** `0xFFFF_FFFD` - **Default EXC_RETURN value for Handler-to-Handler (tail-chaining) using MSP:** `0xFFFF_FFF1` - **Stack frame size (without FPU):** **8 words (32 bytes)** - **Stack frame size (with lazy FPU stacking):** **26 words (104 bytes)** - **Saved registers in basic frame:** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR - **EXC_RETURN bit positions used:** bit 2 (SPSEL), bit 3 (MODE), bit 4 (FTYPE), bit 5 (F), bit 6 (S) --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently tests EXC_RETURN bit meanings, stack pointer selection logic, and the unstacking sequence. **A 7-mark question on EXC_RETURN format is highly likely** in the 2024 Scheme ESE. # 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Stepwise Theory ### A. The EXC_RETURN Token — Anatomy The Cortex-M core loads EXC_RETURN into the **Link Register (LR / R14)** automatically during exception entry. The handler then ends with a typical `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` instruction — and the magic value in LR drives the return. $$ \text{LR} = \text{EXC\_RETURN} \quad \text{on entry to every exception handler} $$ When PC is written with EXC_RETURN (e.g., via `BX LR`), the core performs these steps **atomically** (non-interruptible by higher-priority exceptions): 1. **Decode SPSEL (bit 2):** Select the stack pointer to unstack from. 2. **Decode MODE (bit 3):** Decide if the destination mode is Thread or Handler. 3. **Decode S bit (bit 6):** Decide if FP state (S0–S15, FPSCR) must also be restored. 4. **Decode F bit (bit 5):** Indicates whether FP context was stacked. 5. **Perform the unstacking operation** (reverse of the automatic stacking at entry). 6. **Update APSR flags from restored xPSR.** 7. **Resume execution at restored PC.** --- ### B. Bit-by-Bit Meaning of EXC_RETURN (Lower 6 Bits) | Bit Position | Name | Value 0 | Value 1 | |---|---|---|---| | Bit 2 | **SPSEL** | Return to **MSP** | Return to **PSP** | | Bit 3 | **MODE** | Return to **Handler mode** | Return to **Thread mode** | | Bit 4 | **FTYPE** | Standard FP frame (16 registers) | Extended FP frame (32 registers) | | Bit 5 | **F** | FP state NOT on stack | FP state WAS on stack | | Bit 6 | **S** | FP state was NOT saved | FP state WAS saved (lazy stacking) | | Bits 31:7 | **Constant** | Always `0x1FFFFFF` | Always `0x1FFFFFF` | > [!NOTE] > Bits 31:28 are forced to `0xF` to make all valid EXC_RETURN values a legal Thumb2 instruction (the architecture guarantees that any value with bits 31:28 = `1111` is decoded as a wide Thumb branch — preventing accidental EXC_RETURN triggering from random data). --- ### C. Unstacking Order (Reverse of Stacking) The stack frame pushed on entry is popped in **exact reverse order** to reconstruct the original state: $$ \boxed{ \begin{array}{l} \text{Top of stack} \rightarrow \text{xPSR} \quad (\text{lowest address}) \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{PC} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{LR} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{R12} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{R3} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{R2} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{R1} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack} \rightarrow {}}\text{R0} \quad (\text{highest address}) \end{array} } $$ The pop sequence is therefore: - Pop **xPSR** → restored, IPSR cleared - Pop **PC** → next instruction to execute - Pop **LR** → link register restored - Pop **R12** → scratch register restored - Pop **R3, R2, R1, R0** → argument/scratch registers restored --- ### D. Stacking vs Unstacking — Symmetric Mirror | Operation | Direction | Effect on SP | |---|---|---| | Exception Entry | Pushes 8 words down | SP decreases by **32 bytes** | | Exception Return | Pops 8 words up | SP increases by **32 bytes** | $$ \Delta\text{SP} = \pm 8 \times 4 = \pm 32 \text{ bytes (basic frame)} $$ With FPU, the frame expands to 26 words (lazy) or 18 words (non-lazy) — the SP delta adjusts accordingly. --- ### E. Tail-Chaining vs Standard Return If the EXC_RETURN has **MODE = 0** (bit 3 cleared), the processor does **NOT** return to Thread mode but instead **enters another pending exception's handler** — this is **tail-chaining**. > [!IMPORTANT] > Tail-chaining skips the unstacking + re-stacking round-trip, achieving a 6-cycle entry latency instead of 30+ cycles. This is the **"Late-Arrive + Tail-Chain" optimization** in the NVIC. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | EXC_RETURN constant upper bits | `0xFFFF_FF00` $\vert$ bits[5:0] | — | Bits 31:28 must be `0xF` | | EXC_RETURN to Thread/MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF9` | — | Decimal $-7$ | | EXC_RETURN to Thread/PSP | `0xFFFF_FFFD` | — | Decimal $-3$ | | EXC_RETURN to Handler/MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF1` | — | Decimal $-15$ | | SPSEL field | bit 2 of EXC_RETURN | — | 0 = MSP, 1 = PSP | | MODE field | bit 3 of EXC_RETURN | — | 0 = Handler, 1 = Thread | | S field | bit 6 of EXC_RETURN | — | FPU state saved | | Basic stack frame size | $8 \text{ words} \times 4$ bytes | **bytes** | 32 bytes per entry | | FPU full frame size | $26 \text{ words} \times 4$ bytes | **bytes** | 104 bytes per entry | | Number of registers restored | **8 core registers** | count | R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR | | SP delta on return | $+32$ (basic) | bytes | Mirror of entry | | Tail-chain entry latency | **6 cycles** | clock cycles | Skips stacking | | Standard entry latency | **12 cycles** | clock cycles | Full stacking | | Late-arrival savings | **12 cycles** | clock cycles | Avoids stacking twice | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bare-metal embedded systems** (sensor nodes, motor controllers): exception return enables deterministic interrupt response. - **RTOS context switching** (FreeRTOS, Keil RTX): PSP is used for Thread mode, MSP for Handler — context switch uses EXC_RETURN semantics. - **Safety-critical automotive (ISO 26262)**: tail-chaining and lazy FPU stacking reduce interrupt latency below **1 µs**, essential for CAN/LIN bus handlers. - **IoT low-power devices (Cortex-M0+)**: efficient exception return is the **single biggest factor** in average current consumption. # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — EXC_RETURN Bit Decoding Logic ### Given A Cortex-M4 processor takes an interrupt while executing Thread mode code using the **Process Stack Pointer (PSP)**. The exception handler completes and executes `BX LR`. ### To Find The bit-level state transitions during exception return. ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation **Step 1 — On Exception Entry, LR is loaded with EXC_RETURN by the hardware:** $$ \text{LR} = 0xFFFF\_FF\text{[6-bit field]} \quad \text{...(eq. 1)} $$ **Step 2 — The kernel (handler) preserves LR by pushing it onto the stack first, but the original LR is captured in the stacked frame:** $$ \text{Memory}[\text{SP}+0x18] \leftarrow \text{original LR} \quad \text{...(eq. 2)} $$ **Step 3 — On `BX LR`, the PC receives `EXC_RETURN`. The decode logic in the processor inspects bits [5:0]:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{bit 2 (SPSEL)} &: 0 \rightarrow \text{use MSP}, \quad 1 \rightarrow \text{use PSP} \\ \text{bit 3 (MODE)} &: 0 \rightarrow \text{enter Handler}, \quad 1 \rightarrow \text{enter Thread} \\ \text{bit 4 (FTYPE)} &: 0 \rightarrow \text{16 FP regs}, \quad 1 \rightarrow \text{32 FP regs} \\ \text{bit 5 (F)} &: 0 \rightarrow \text{no FP stack}, \quad 1 \rightarrow \text{FP stack present} \\ \text{bit 6 (S)} &: 0 \rightarrow \text{no FP state}, \quad 1 \rightarrow \text{FP state present} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verify the value `0xFFFF_FFFD` (return to Thread using PSP):** $$ \begin{aligned} 0xFFFF\_FFFD &= 0b\underbrace{1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111}_{\text{bits 31:6}}\ \underbrace{1111\ 01}_{\text{bits 5:0}} \\ \text{bit 2} &= 1 \Rightarrow \text{PSP} \\ \text{bit 3} &= 1 \Rightarrow \text{Thread mode} \\ \text{All other bits} &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{No FP context} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Unstack operation is triggered. The SP (selected by SPSEL = PSP) increments by 32 bytes:** $$ \text{PSP}_{\text{new}} = \text{PSP}_{\text{old}} + 32 \quad \text{bytes} \quad \text{...(eq. 3)} $$ **Step 6 — Program counter receives the value stored at the original top-of-stack PC location, and execution resumes there.** --- ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Stack Frame Size Calculation ### Problem A Cortex-M4F system (with FPU) is in a high-performance DSP loop. An IRQ occurs mid-computation. The handler uses lazy FPU stacking. Calculate: (a) Total stack bytes consumed per exception entry (b) Number of registers saved (c) SP delta on return ### Solution **Part (a) — Total stack size:** For Cortex-M4F with **lazy stacking** (default configuration), the FPU registers S0–S15 and FPSCR are pushed **lazily** (only when an FP instruction executes inside the handler). The initial frame size on entry is: $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Core registers} &= 8 \text{ words} \\ \text{FP registers (S0–S15)} &= 16 \text{ words} \\ \text{FPSCR} &= 1 \text{ word} \\ \text{Total} &= 8 + 16 + 1 = 25 \text{ words} \end{aligned} } $$ Adding the integrated initial dummy word and a reserved slot, the full lazy frame can be **26 words = 104 bytes** in some configurations. For exam purposes: $$ \text{Stack used} = 25 \times 4 = 100 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{...(eq. 4)} $$ **Part (b) — Number of registers saved:** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \text{8 core registers} + \text{16 FP regs} + \text{1 FPSCR} \\ R &= 25 \text{ registers} \quad \text{...(eq. 5)} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — SP delta on return:** $$ \Delta\text{SP}_{\text{return}} = +100 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{...(eq. 6)} $$ This mirrors the entry delta exactly, restoring the PSP to its pre-exception value. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Return Path Selection | Handler Type | Stack Used | EXC_RETURN Value | Final Mode | Stack Restored To | |---|---|---|---|---| | Interrupt w/ MSP | MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF9` | Thread | MSP | | Interrupt w/ PSP | PSP | `0xFFFF_FFFD` | Thread | PSP | | Tail-chain to next IRQ | MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF1` | Handler | MSP (no unstack) | | FPU lazy stack active | PSP | `0xFFFF_FFED` | Thread | PSP + FP regs | | NMI | MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF9` | Thread | MSP | | HardFault | MSP | `0xFFFF_FFF9` | Thread | MSP | > [!NOTE] > The EXC_RETURN value is **always** `0xFFFF_FFxx` form because bits 31:4 must be `1`s (resembling a Thumb2 instruction prefix `1111`), ensuring the BX interpreter can decode it. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Exception Return Flow The following Mermaid block diagram traces the full exception return mechanism, from the handler's `BX LR` instruction to the restored Thread mode execution. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Handler body executes] --> B[Handler ends with BX LR or POP PC] B --> C[PC receives EXC_RETURN value] C --> D{Decode bit 2 SPSEL} D -->|SPSEL = 0| E1[Select MSP for unstacking] D -->|SPSEL = 1| E2[Select PSP for unstacking] E1 --> F{Decode bit 3 MODE} E2 --> F F -->|MODE = 0| G1[Target = Handler mode - Tail Chain] F -->|MODE = 1| G2[Target = Thread mode - Normal Return] G1 --> H1[NVIC picks next pending exception] G2 --> H2[Pop xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0] H1 --> H2 H2 --> I{Decode bit 6 S - FPU present?} I -->|S = 0| J1[Restore complete - Continue Thread code] I -->|S = 1| J2[Also unstack S0 to S15 and FPSCR] J2 --> J1 H1 --> K1[Skip unstack - Direct handler switch] K1 --> J1 J1 --> L[APSR flags updated from restored xPSR] L --> M[PC starts fetching from restored address] M --> N[Interrupted program resumes] ``` --- ## 4.2 Memory Layout Diagram — Stack Frame on Return ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stack_Frame["Stack Frame (low → high address)"] direction TB w0["xPSR
0x1C"] w1["PC
0x18"] w2["LR
0x14"] w3["R12
0x10"] w4["R3
0x0C"] w5["R2
0x08"] w6["R1
0x04"] w7["R0
0x00"] end A[SP at entry
points here] -.-> w7 B[After 8-word push
SP moves down] -.-> w0 C[On return
SP pops up] -.-> w0 ``` --- ## 4.3 State Machine — Return Path Decision ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> HandlerActive HandlerActive --> DecodeExcReturn: BX LR / POP PC DecodeExcReturn --> CheckFPU: S bit CheckFPU --> PopFPU: S = 1 CheckFPU --> PopCore: S = 0 PopFPU --> PopCore PopCore --> CheckMode: bit 3 CheckMode --> TailChain: MODE = 0 CheckMode --> ThreadReturn: MODE = 1 TailChain --> HandlerActive: Next pending IRQ ThreadReturn --> [*]: Resumed Thread code ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above emphasize the **decision flow** rather than physical pin connections — this is the KTU-expected style for architecture questions. # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define EXC_RETURN. List any two valid EXC_RETURN values and their meanings.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Model Answer:** **EXC_RETURN** is a special 32-bit magic value (of the form `0xFFFF_FFxx`) automatically loaded into the **Link Register (LR / R14)** by the Cortex-M core on exception entry. When this value is written to the **Program Counter (PC)**, the processor triggers a hardware-controlled exception return sequence that unstacks the saved context and resumes execution of the previously interrupted code. **Two valid EXC_RETURN values:** 1. **`0xFFFF_FFF9`** — Return from Handler to **Thread mode**, using the **Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** for unstacking. Bits: SPSEL=0, MODE=1. 2. **`0xFFFF_FFFD`** — Return from Handler to **Thread mode**, using the **Process Stack Pointer (PSP)** for unstacking. Bits: SPSEL=1, MODE=1. --- ### **Q2. List the registers restored from the stack during an exception return in ARM Cortex-M.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **Model Answer:** The following **8 registers** are restored (popped) from the stack in reverse order on exception return: 1. **xPSR** (with IPSR field cleared) 2. **PC** (Program Counter) 3. **LR** (Link Register / R14) 4. **R12** 5. **R3** 6. **R2** 7. **R1** 8. **R0** In systems with FPU and lazy stacking enabled, **S0–S15** and **FPSCR** are additionally unstacked. --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat diagram the EXC_RETURN mechanism in ARM Cortex-M. Describe how bits [5:0] of EXC_RETURN are decoded by the processor.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Model]** #### Model Solution: **EXC_RETURN Mechanism:** When any exception (IRQ, NMI, HardFault, SVCall, etc.) is accepted by the Cortex-M core, the hardware automatically: 1. **Stacks** the following 8 registers onto the active stack: `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0`. 2. **Loads LR with a special EXC_RETURN value** of the form `0xFFFF_FFxx`. 3. **Transitions the processor to Handler mode** with the MSP active. 4. **Begins executing the vector at the corresponding exception slot** in the vector table. When the handler ends with `BX LR` or `POP {..., PC}`, the value `EXC_RETURN` flows into PC. The core recognizes it as a return token (not a normal address) and performs the return. **Bit Decoding of EXC_RETURN [5:0]:** | Bit | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | Bit 2 | **SPSEL** | `0` → Unstack from MSP; `1` → Unstack from PSP | | Bit 3 | **MODE** | `0` → Return to Handler mode (tail-chain); `1` → Return to Thread mode | | Bit 4 | **FTYPE** | `0` → Standard FP frame (16 regs); `1` → Extended FP frame (32 regs) | | Bit 5 | **F** | `0` → No FP state stacked; `1` → FP state was stacked | | Bit 6 | **S** | `0` → No FP state; `1` → FP state was saved (lazy stacking) | **Neat Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):** The flow from `BX LR` → PC receives EXC_RETURN → Decode bits → Unstack → Resume Thread. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between MSP and PSP in Cortex-M. Explain when the PSP is used in exception return and why it is preferred in RTOS designs.** #### Model Solution: **MSP vs PSP Comparison:** | Feature | **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** | **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** | |---|---|---| | Default usage | Always used on reset | Available for Thread mode | | Mode usage | **Handler mode** (and Thread by default) | **Thread mode only** | | Typical user | Kernel / exception handlers | Application tasks (RTOS threads) | | Banked | Yes, banked between modes | Yes, separate from MSP | | Control | Always accessible | Controlled via CONTROL register | **When PSP is used in exception return:** If bit 2 (SPSEL) of the EXC_RETURN value is `1`, the processor unstack from PSP rather than MSP. This is typically the case when: - The application thread was running with PSP active (CONTROL[1] = 1). - An RTOS kernel handles the exception and wishes to return back to the **specific task's stack** that was interrupted, not to the kernel's own MSP. **Why PSP is preferred in RTOS designs:** 1. **Stack isolation** — Each task has its own PSP region, preventing corruption of kernel stacks by faulty task code. 2. **Context switch efficiency** — The RTOS can switch tasks by simply updating PSP, no full register save needed. 3. **Memory protection** — Hardware MPU can guard PSP regions per task with distinct permissions. 4. **Debug clarity** — Stack traces distinguish handler stack (MSP) from task stack (PSP). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **(a) [7 Marks] With a clear block diagram, illustrate the stack frame layout on exception return in ARM Cortex-M. How is the frame size affected when FPU lazy stacking is enabled?** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** #### Model Solution: **Stack Frame Layout (Basic — No FPU):** The 8-word (32-byte) frame is pushed in the order shown and popped in reverse on return. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Top of stack (low addr)} &: \text{xPSR (offset 0x1C)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{PC (offset 0x18)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{LR (offset 0x14)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{R12 (offset 0x10)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{R3 (offset 0x0C)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{R2 (offset 0x08)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{R1 (offset 0x04)} \\ \phantom{\text{Top of stack (low addr)} :}& \text{R0 (offset 0x00) - highest address} \end{aligned} $$ **Frame Size with FPU Lazy Stacking:** When lazy stacking is enabled on Cortex-M4F, on entry, the hardware reserves space for FP context but only pushes the **core registers (8 words)** immediately. When the handler executes its first FP instruction, the hardware pushes the remaining **FP context (16 single-precision registers S0–S15) + FPSCR (1 word) = 17 words** onto the previously reserved space. $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial push} &: 8 \text{ words} = 32 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Lazy FP push} &: 17 \text{ words} = 68 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Total frame} &: 25 \text{ words} = 100 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} } $$ On exception return, the S bit (bit 6) of EXC_RETURN signals the hardware to pop both core and FP context in one operation. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of tail-chaining in the NVIC. How does the EXC_RETURN value facilitate tail-chaining and what is the latency benefit?** #### Model Solution: **Tail-Chaining Concept:** When a handler completes and **another exception is already pending**, the NVIC can skip the costly **unstacking + re-stacking** round-trip. Instead, the processor switches directly to the next exception's handler. The EXC_RETURN value with `MODE = 0` (bit 3 cleared) signals this behavior. **How EXC_RETURN facilitates tail-chaining:** 1. Handler returns via `BX LR`. 2. PC receives `EXC_RETURN` with MODE bit = 0. 3. Processor recognizes: "Do not enter Thread mode; do not unstack the current frame." 4. NVIC identifies the highest-priority pending exception. 5. Processor immediately vectors to the new handler (in 6 cycles). 6. The new handler uses the **same MSP stack frame** without re-pushing. **Latency Benefit:** | Operation | Cycles | |---|---| | Standard exception entry (full stack) | **12 cycles** | | Tail-chained entry | **6 cycles** | | **Savings** | **6 cycles per back-to-back IRQ** | In a system processing back-to-back interrupts (e.g., USB + DMA + Timer), tail-chaining can save **tens of microseconds per burst**, drastically reducing worst-case interrupt latency — a key reason ARM Cortex-M is dominant in real-time control. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing EXC_RETURN with a memory address** — It is NOT. It is a magic value the CPU intercepts. Many students lose 2 marks by calling it an "address." > > 2. **Forgetting the unstack order is reverse of stack** — The pop order is `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` — NOT the same as push. Wrong order = 2 mark loss. > > 3. **Bit numbering confusion** — In ARM bit numbering, **bit 0 is LSB**. SPSEL is bit 2, MODE is bit 3. Students often swap these. > > 4. **Saying "EXC_RETURN is loaded into PC"** — The value sits in LR; it is **copied into PC** by `BX LR` or `POP {PC}`. Subtly different! > > 5. **Missing the FPU implications** — On Cortex-M4F, forgetting the S/F/FTYPE bits loses 2–3 marks. --- ## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins — memorize these:** - ✅ **EXC_RETURN** is a 32-bit token in LR, format `0xFFFF_FFxx`, with bits [5:0] carrying mode/stack/FPU info. - ✅ **Bit 2 (SPSEL):** 0 = MSP, 1 = PSP - ✅ **Bit 3 (MODE):** 0 = Handler (tail-chain), 1 = Thread (normal return) - ✅ **Bits 31:4 = `0x1FFFFFF`** — guarantees Thumb2 decode compatibility. - ✅ **8 registers stacked on entry:** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR - ✅ **Unstack order is REVERSE:** xPSR → PC → LR → R12 → R3 → R2 → R1 → R0 - ✅ **Stack frame size (basic):** 8 words = 32 bytes - ✅ **Stack frame size (FPU lazy):** 25 words = 100 bytes - ✅ **Tail-chain latency:** 6 cycles; standard entry: 12 cycles; late-arrival savings: 12 cycles - ✅ **`BX LR` and `POP {PC}`** are the two ways to trigger EXC_RETURN. - ✅ **MSP** is used by Handler mode; **PSP** is used by Thread mode (RTOS tasks). - ✅ **IPSR is cleared** automatically on return → no stale ISR number lingers in Thread mode. - ✅ **APSR flags are restored** from the stacked xPSR → interrupt cannot leak condition flags. - ✅ **EXC_RETURN is not visible in PC after return** — it is consumed and PC gets the real restored address. - ✅ **Higher 6 bits of EXC_RETURN are always `1`s** to make the value decode as a Thumb2 instruction.

topics.title: Latency Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Latency Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **ARM Architecture and Programming**, **Latency** is formally defined as the *number of clock cycles required between issuing an instruction (or a memory access request) and the moment its result becomes available to the dependent instruction*. **Latency Reduction Techniques** constitute the family of microarchitectural and hardware-level design strategies implemented within the ARM Cortex-A, Cortex-R, and Cortex-M cores to **minimize the delay between the initiation of an operation and the production of its output**, thereby increasing instructions-per-cycle (IPC) throughput and overall system performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> Latency Reduction Techniques in ARM processors include: (1) Caches (L1, L2, L3 hierarchy), (2) Branch Target Address Cache (BTAC) and Branch Prediction Buffers, (3) Speculative Execution, (4) Out-of-Order Execution, (5) Register Renaming, (6) Prefetch Buffers, (7) Pipeline Interlocking, and (8) Multi-issue / Superscalar datapaths. All these techniques are aimed at reducing the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** and **stall cycles** in the ARM pipeline.

## 1.2 Fundamental Latency Equation

The foundation of all latency reduction work is grounded in the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** equation:

$$AMAT = Hit\_Time + Miss\_Rate \times Miss\_Penalty$$

Every technique in this module (caches, prefetch, prediction, speculation) is essentially a *lever* that manipulates one or more of these three variables: **Hit Time, Miss Rate, or Miss Penalty**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Library Research Workflow:**
>
> Imagine you are a graduate student writing a thesis. You need information from the library:
> - **No Cache (worst case):** Every single time you need a fact, you walk to the central library 5 km away. Each fact takes 60 minutes to fetch → *High Latency*.
> - **L1 Cache (your desk):** You keep a small notebook of the last 20 facts on your desk. Lookup is instant → *1 cycle hit time*.
> - **L2 Cache (your bookshelf):** Slightly slower but bigger than your desk → *5–10 cycle hit time*.
> - **L3 Cache (department library):** Bigger still, slightly slower → *20–40 cycle hit time*.
> - **Main Memory (central library):** Slowest, largest → *200+ cycle penalty*.
> - **Branch Predictor:** Before going to the library, you *guess* which book you'll need next and fetch it speculatively so it's already on your desk when required.
> - **Prefetch Buffer:** A research assistant who notices patterns in your requests and brings books *before* you ask.
> - **Out-of-Order Execution:** While waiting for one hard-to-find book, you work on a different chapter that doesn't depend on it.

This library metaphor maps **directly** to ARM Cortex-A53/A57/A75 pipeline design, where every cycle lost is a "trip to the library."

## 1.4 Types of Latency in ARM Pipelines

| Latency Type | Description | Typical Magnitude (Cortex-A) |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Latency** | Cycles between fetch and retirement of an instruction | 5–15 cycles |
| **Cache Hit Latency (L1)** | Cycles to access L1 data/instruction cache | **1–4 cycles** |
| **Cache Hit Latency (L2)** | Cycles to access unified L2 cache | **8–20 cycles** |
| **Cache Miss Penalty (DRAM)** | Cycles to fetch from main memory | **200–500 cycles** |
| **Branch Resolution Latency** | Cycles to resolve conditional branch outcome | 5–10 cycles |
| **Pipeline Interlock Stall** | Cycles wasted waiting for a hazard to clear | Variable, 1–N cycles |
| **TLB Miss Penalty** | Cycles to service a TLB miss with a page walk | **30–200 cycles** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Latency Reduction Principle

The entire ARM microarchitecture is fundamentally a **latency-hiding** and **latency-reducing** engine. A bare sequential processor would require *one full instruction latency* to be paid for every instruction in a program. Modern ARM cores (Cortex-A series) achieve **IPC > 1** (instructions per cycle) by overlapping latencies. The two complementary approaches are:

1. **Latency Reduction (True Speed-up):** Make the *fundamental operation* itself faster.
   - Examples: Faster caches, shorter pipelines, deeper branch prediction, register renaming.
2. **Latency Hiding (Throughput-oriented):** Keep the pipeline busy doing *useful work* during unavoidable waits.
   - Examples: Out-of-order execution, speculative execution, prefetching, multi-issue.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Latency Reduction in ARM

### 2.2.1 Pillar 1 — Memory Hierarchy & Caching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cache Fundamentals (KTU High-Yield Topic):**
> A cache exploits two principles: **Temporal Locality** (recently accessed data is likely to be re-used) and **Spatial Locality** (data near a recently accessed address is likely to be accessed soon).

A cache is a small, fast SRAM located close to the CPU that stores a subset of main memory's contents. ARM cores implement a **modified Harvard architecture** with separate L1 instruction (L1i) and L1 data (L1d) caches, often unified L2, and in some designs (Cortex-A75, A76) a per-core L2 plus shared L3.

**AMAT (Average Memory Access Time):**
$$AMAT = Hit\_Time + Miss\_Rate \times Miss\_Penalty$$

**For a multi-level hierarchy (e.g., L1, L2, Main Memory):**
$$AMAT = Hit\_Time_{L1} + Miss\_Rate_{L1} \times (Hit\_Time_{L2} + Miss\_Rate_{L2} \times Miss\_Penalty_{L2})$$

### 2.2.2 Pillar 2 — Branch Prediction

A conditional branch outcome is not known until the **Execute** stage. Without prediction, the pipeline would stall for *N cycles* (where N = branch resolution latency). A branch predictor:

1. **Predicts** the direction (taken vs. not-taken).
2. **Predicts the target address** for indirect/function-pointer branches.
3. Begins **speculatively fetching** from the predicted path.

**Prediction accuracy metric:**
$$Prediction\_Accuracy = \frac{Correct\_Predictions}{Total\_Branches} \times 100\%$$

**Branch misprediction penalty (MIPS per penalty):**
$$Mispred\_Penalty = Pipeline\_Depth - 1 + Branch\_Resolution\_Cycle$$

ARM Cortex-A77, for example, features a **TAGE predictor** (TAgged GEometric history length) achieving >95% accuracy on SPEC benchmarks.

### 2.2.3 Pillar 3 — Speculative Execution

The processor executes instructions *down the predicted path* before knowing the branch outcome. If the prediction was correct, the work is committed. If wrong, all speculative state is **squashed (rolled back)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Speculative execution reduces *effective* latency by transforming a future stall into useful work. The Cortex-A series speculatively issues loads early, *speculating* they will not fault.

### 2.2.4 Pillar 4 — Out-of-Order Execution (OoO)

ARM Cortex-A57, A72, A75, A76, A77, A78, A710, A715, X1 — *all* modern high-performance ARM cores use **out-of-order issue** with **in-order commit (retirement)**.

The core maintains a **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** with 128+ entries. Instructions are:
1. **Decoded** and dispatched to the issue queue.
2. **Issued** to execution units when their operands are *ready* (not when they appear in program order).
3. **Retired** strictly in program order to preserve architectural state.
