This allows the processor to overlap a long-latency load with independent integer work that *appeared later* in the instruction stream.

### 2.2.5 Pillar 5 — Prefetching

A hardware prefetcher detects **memory access patterns** (sequential strides, recurring strides) and issues **speculative load requests** to bring data into the cache *before* the program actually demands it.

Types in ARM:
- **Line prefetcher:** Detects sequential access (e.g., AArch64 `LDP` streaming).
- **Stride prefetcher:** Detects constant-stride access (e.g., array `arr[i]`, `arr[i+1]`, ...).
- **Stream prefetcher (Cortex-A75 onwards):** Issues up to 8 outstanding prefetches per stream.

## 2.3 ARM-Specific Latency Reduction Features

| Feature | ARM Implementation | Latency Saved |
|---|---|---|
| **BTAC** (Branch Target Address Cache) | Caches recent branch target addresses | Saves target-address computation (1–2 cycles) |
| **Return Address Predictor (RAS)** | Predicts return targets for `BL`/`RET` pairs | Saves function-return stalls |
| **Register Renaming** | Maps architectural regs (X0–X30) to large physical pool (e.g., 160+ regs) | Eliminates false WAW/WAR hazards |
| **Micro-op fusion** | Combines paired ops (e.g., compare+branch) | Saves one decode/dispatch slot |
| **NEON / SIMD** | Performs 4–16 ops in parallel per instruction | 4×–16× data parallelism |
| **Cache line size** | Typically **64 bytes** in ARM Cortex-A | Matches DRAM burst; amortizes tag overhead |
| **Write-Through vs. Write-Back** | Cortex-A: Write-Back (with Write-Allocate) | Reduces memory traffic on hits |
| **Critical Word First / Early Restart** | On miss, deliver demanded word *first* | Reduces miss penalty from full-line to ~1 word |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every KTU question on latency reduction will test one of these equations.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AMAT (single-level cache)** | $AMAT = H + MR \times MP$ | Quantifies average memory access delay |
| 2 | **AMAT (multi-level)** | $AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times (H_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times MP_{L2})$ | Hierarchical memory design |
| 3 | **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times Clock\_Period$ | Overall performance impact |
| 4 | **CPI with memory stalls** | $CPI = CPI_{base} + MR_{mem} \times MP \times (Fraction\_of\_mem\_ops)$ | Stalls on cache misses |
| 5 | **Branch Misprediction Penalty** | $Stall_{branch} = Branch\_Frequency \times Mispred\_Rate \times Mispred\_Penalty$ | Branch predictor evaluation |
| 6 | **Speedup from cache** | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{no\_cache}}{T_{cache}}$ | Quantifies cache benefit |
| 7 | **Prediction Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{\#Correct}{\#Total\_Branches} \times 100\%$ | Predictor quality metric |
| 8 | **AMAT for write-allocate** | $AMAT_{write} = Write\_Hit\_Time + Write\_Miss\_Rate \times Mem\_Access\_Time$ | Write policy design |
| 9 | **Miss Rate** | $MR = 1 - Hit\_Rate$ | Used universally |
| 10 | **Effective CPI** | $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + \sum Stall\_Cycles$ | Architectural bottleneck analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in real-world CPU design by companies like **Arm Holdings, Apple Silicon (M-series), Qualcomm (Snapdragon), Samsung (Exynos)**, and in performance verification teams of every chip vendor. A senior ARM performance engineer uses AMAT daily to evaluate cache sizing trade-offs.

## 2.5 The Performance Triangle — A Mental Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every latency reduction technique is a triangle with these three vertices:
>
> **Hit Time ←→ Miss Rate ←→ Miss Penalty**
>
> You cannot optimize all three simultaneously. Optimizing one often *worsens* another:
>
> - **Bigger cache** → lower miss rate, *higher* hit time.
> - **Smaller cache** → faster hit time, *higher* miss rate.
> - **Multi-level cache** → balances all three but adds complexity and coherence overhead.
>
> ARM Cortex-A designs choose point-designs along this trade-off curve. The KTU 2024 syllabus tests this **trade-off reasoning** explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivation: AMAT for a 3-Level Cache Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-style):** An ARM Cortex-A53 has the following memory hierarchy parameters:
> - L1 instruction cache: Hit Time = **1 cycle**, Miss Rate = **5%**
> - L2 unified cache: Hit Time = **12 cycles**, Miss Rate = **20%** *(of L1 misses)*
> - Main Memory: Access Time = **200 cycles**
>
> **(a)** Derive the AMAT formula step-by-step.
> **(b)** Compute the numerical value of AMAT.
> **(c)** If the L1 hit time is increased to 2 cycles, find the new AMAT and comment.

### Step 1: Establish the Recursive AMAT Framework

The most rigorous way to think about a multi-level memory hierarchy is to treat **each level as a "cache" for the level below it**. The AMAT is therefore the *time spent at this level* plus the *time spent at all lower levels if this level misses*.

Let us define:
- $H_i$ = Hit Time at level $i$
- $MR_i$ = Miss Rate at level $i$ (fraction of accesses that miss at level $i$)
- $MP_i$ = Miss Penalty at level $i$ (time to access level $i+1$ or memory on a miss)

For a 2-level system (L1, L2), the AMAT is:
$$AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times AMAT_{L2}$$

For a 3-level system (L1, L2, Memory), we extend:
$$AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times (H_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times H_{Mem})$$

### Step 2: Expand Algebraically

Substituting the symbolic values:
$$AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times H_{L2} + MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} \times H_{Mem}$$

This compact form has three terms:
- **Term 1:** Cost of an L1 hit (the common case).
- **Term 2:** Cost of an L1 miss that *is* caught by L2.
- **Term 3:** Cost of an L1 miss *and* an L2 miss — the dreaded double miss.

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

Using $H_{L1} = 1$, $MR_{L1} = 0.05$, $H_{L2} = 12$, $MR_{L2} = 0.20$, $H_{Mem} = 200$:

$$AMAT = (1) + (0.05) \times (12) + (0.05) \times (0.20) \times (200)$$

Compute each term step by step.

**Term 1:**
$$H_{L1} = 1 \text{ cycle}$$

**Term 2:**
$$0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ cycles}$$

**Term 3:**
$$0.05 \times 0.20 = 0.01$$
$$0.01 \times 200 = 2.0 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 4: Sum All Three Terms

$$AMAT = 1 + 0.6 + 2.0 = 3.6 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 5: Sub-Question (c) — Sensitivity Analysis

If L1 hit time increases to 2 cycles (e.g., due to a deeper L1 or larger associativity):
$$AMAT' = (2) + (0.05) \times (12) + (0.05) \times (0.20) \times (200)$$
$$AMAT' = 2 + 0.6 + 2.0 = 4.6 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A *1-cycle* increase in L1 hit time caused a *28% increase in AMAT* (from 3.6 to 4.6). This is why ARM designs use **small, fast, low-associativity L1 caches** (typically 32–64 KB, 4-way set associative). Designers sacrifice some miss rate to keep the *hit time* as low as possible, because hit time affects **every single access**.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Effective CPI with Cache and Branch Penalties

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A pipelined ARM processor has the following parameters:
> - Ideal CPI (no stalls) = **1.0**
> - Instruction mix: 30% loads/stores, 10% conditional branches
> - Data cache miss rate = **8%**, miss penalty = **100 cycles**
> - Branch predictor accuracy = **90%**, misprediction penalty = **8 cycles**
>
> Derive the effective CPI.

### Step 1: Build the Total Stall Equation

$$CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + Stalls_{cache} + Stalls_{branch}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Cache Stalls

Only **data memory operations** incur cache misses. We need the *fraction of instructions* that are memory operations and the miss rate among them.

$$Stalls_{cache} = (Fraction\_memory) \times (Miss\_Rate) \times (Miss\_Penalty)$$
$$Stalls_{cache} = 0.30 \times 0.08 \times 100$$
$$Stalls_{cache} = 2.4 \text{ cycles per instruction}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Branch Stalls

$$Stalls_{branch} = (Fraction\_branch) \times (Mispred\_Rate) \times (Mispred\_Penalty)$$
$$Stalls_{branch} = 0.10 \times (1 - 0.90) \times 8$$
$$Stalls_{branch} = 0.10 \times 0.10 \times 8 = 0.08 \text{ cycles per instruction}$$

### Step 4: Sum to Find Effective CPI

$$CPI_{eff} = 1.0 + 2.4 + 0.08 = 3.48$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A common KTU exam error is forgetting that **branch misprediction rate = 1 − accuracy**. Many students write 0.90 instead of 0.10, which makes the answer 100% wrong. Always write $(1 - accuracy)$ explicitly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Table (Mandatory KTU Topic)

| Technique | Reduces | Increases | Net Effect | Used in ARM Cortex-A |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase L1 size | Miss Rate | Hit Time | Positive up to ~32–64 KB | Yes (Cortex-A53: 32 KB) |
| Increase associativity | Miss Rate | Hit Time | Mild positive | Yes (4-way common) |
| Add L2 cache | L1 Miss Penalty | Average access latency, area | Strong positive | Yes (all Cortex-A) |
| Pseudo-associative cache | Miss Rate | Effective hit time (on miss) | Conditional positive | Rare in ARM |
| Victim cache | Conflict misses | Slight area/hit time | Positive for small L1 | Some embedded |
| Prefetching | Cache misses | Bandwidth, pollution | Strong positive | Yes (A75+) |
| Multi-level branch predictor | Mispred. rate | Predictor latency | Strong positive | Yes (TAGE in A77+) |
| Deeper speculation | Branch penalty | Recovery cost on miss | Strong positive | Yes (limited in mobile) |
| Wider issue (4-wide → 6-wide) | IPC | Power, complexity | Positive | Yes (A76: 4-wide decode) |
| Register renaming | False dependencies | Renaming unit latency | Strong positive | Yes (A57+) |
| Out-of-order execution | Memory stall impact | Complexity, power | Strong positive | Yes (A57+) |
| Critical Word First | Miss penalty | Slight complexity | Positive | Yes (line fill) |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Branch Predictor Performance Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compare two branch predictors for a workload with 20% branches and 50,000 total instructions:
> - **Predictor A:** 90% accuracy, 2-cycle misprediction penalty
> - **Predictor B:** 95% accuracy, 4-cycle misprediction penalty
>
> Which is better in terms of total branch stalls?

### Step 1: Compute Branch Count
$$N_{branches} = 50{,}000 \times 0.20 = 10{,}000$$

### Step 2: Compute Mispredictions
- Predictor A: $10{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}000$ mispredictions
- Predictor B: $10{,}000 \times 0.05 = 500$ mispredictions

### Step 3: Compute Total Branch Stall Cycles
- Predictor A: $1{,}000 \times 2 = 2{,}000$ cycles
- Predictor B: $500 \times 4 = 2{,}000$ cycles

**Conclusion:** They are *equal* in this scenario. This illustrates the **trade-off principle**: higher accuracy often requires deeper history tracking, which costs more cycles per misprediction recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Modern ARM predictors (Cortex-A77 onwards) use **TAGE** with a 5-cycle misprediction recovery but achieve >97% accuracy, making the trade-off favorable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Latency Reduction in an ARM Cortex-A Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram below:** Block diagram of how latency reduction techniques integrate within a modern ARM Cortex-A out-of-order superscalar pipeline. Mermaid flowchart shows the data path and the *control* path (branch predictor) feeding the front-end, with the memory hierarchy and OoO back-end handling data-side latency.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Instruction Fetch Unit IFU] --> B[Branch Target Address Cache BTAC]
    A --> C[Instruction Cache L1i 32 to 64 KB]
    A --> D[Return Address Stack RAS]
    A --> E[Instruction TLB ITLB]
    B --> F[Branch Predictor TAGE]
    F --> A
    C --> G[Fetch Buffer 8 to 16 entries]
    G --> H[Decoder 4 wide decode]
    H --> I[Micro op Fusion]
    I --> J[Register Renaming Table]
    J --> K[Reorder Buffer ROB 128 to 256 entries]
    K --> L[Issue Queue IQ 60 to 100 entries]
    L --> M1[Int ALU Pipeline]
    L --> M2[FP SIMD NEON Pipeline]
    L --> M3[Load Store Unit LSU]
    L --> M4[Branch Unit]
    M3 --> N[Data Cache L1d 32 to 64 KB]
    M3 --> O[Data TLB DTLB]
    M3 --> P[L2 Cache unified 256 KB to 1 MB per core]
    P --> Q[L3 Cache shared 2 to 8 MB optional]
    Q --> R[Main Memory Controller DRAM]
    K --> S[Retirement Unit in order commit]
    M4 --> F
```

**How to read this diagram for KTU exams:**
- **Front-end (left):** BTAC + TAGE predictor + RAS — these three together minimize *fetch-side* latency.
- **Mid-pipeline (center):** Decoder + Register Renaming + ROB — minimize *decode/dispatch* stalls and false dependencies.
- **Back-end (right):** Issue Queue + multiple execution units + L1d/L2/L3 — minimize *execution and memory* latency.
- **Feedback loop:** M4 (Branch Unit) sends actual outcomes back to F (Branch Predictor) for training and learning.

## 4.2 Latency Hiding vs. Latency Reduction Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Latency_Reduction["LATENCY REDUCTION"]
        LR1[Make operation itself faster]
        LR2[Faster cache]
        LR3[Lower miss rate]
        LR4[Shorter pipeline]
        LR1 --> LR2
        LR1 --> LR3
        LR1 --> LR4
    end
    subgraph Latency_Hiding["LATENCY HIDING"]
        LH1[Keep pipeline busy during wait]
        LH2[Out of order execution]
        LH3[Speculative execution]
        LH4[Prefetching]
        LH1 --> LH2
        LH1 --> LH3
        LH1 --> LH4
    end
    Latency_Reduction --> P[Higher IPC and Lower AMAT]
    Latency_Hiding --> P
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cache Hierarchy & Latency Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Core[ARM CPU Core] --> L1d[L1 Data Cache 1 to 4 cycles]
    Core --> L1i[L1 Instruction Cache 1 to 4 cycles]
    L1d --> L2[L2 Unified Cache 8 to 20 cycles]
    L1i --> L2
    L2 --> L3[L3 Shared Cache 30 to 60 cycles]
    L3 --> DRAM[Main Memory DRAM 200 to 500 cycles]
    L1d --> Prefetch[Hardware Prefetcher]
    Prefetch -.speculatively issues.-> L2
```

## 4.4 State Diagram — Speculative Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IssueSpeculative
    IssueSpeculative --> Executing
    Executing --> OutcomeKnown
    OutcomeKnown --> PredictionCorrect : Commit
    OutcomeKnown --> PredictionWrong : Squash and Rollback
    PredictionWrong --> IssueSpeculative
    PredictionCorrect --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Aid:** Every ARM Cortex-A core since the A57 uses this exact speculative lifecycle. The `Squash and Rollback` transition is what *costs* performance on a misprediction — all in-flight work is discarded. This is why *predictor accuracy* is the single most important predictor design metric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are **2-mark/3-mark short questions** typical of KTU University Exam Part A. They test precise definitions and direct recall.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]` (3 Marks)

**Q: Define Average Memory Access Time (AMAT). Explain how it is used to evaluate the effectiveness of a cache in an ARM processor.**

**Model Answer (verbatim board-exam style):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** *Average Memory Access Time* (AMAT) is the average time the processor takes to access memory, accounting for both hits and misses across the memory hierarchy. It is formally given by:

$$AMAT = Hit\_Time + (Miss\_Rate \times Miss\_Penalty)$$

**Application to ARM (1.5 marks):** In ARM Cortex-A processors, AMAT is used as the primary figure-of-merit to evaluate cache design trade-offs. A lower AMAT indicates a more effective cache hierarchy. For example, the Cortex-A53's L1 32 KB cache achieves an AMAT of ~1–2 cycles in typical workloads, while a poorly designed cache (e.g., direct-mapped with high conflict misses) may show AMAT of 5–10 cycles. ARM designers iteratively tune cache size, associativity, and line size to minimize AMAT subject to power and area constraints.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]` (3 Marks)

**Q: What is Branch Prediction? Explain the role of the Branch Target Address Cache (BTAC) in ARM processors.**

**Model Answer:**

**Branch Prediction (1.5 marks):** Branch prediction is a latency reduction technique where the hardware *guesses* the outcome (taken vs. not-taken) and the target address of a conditional branch *before* the branch is actually resolved. This allows the fetch unit to continue fetching instructions from the predicted path, keeping the pipeline full. Modern ARM Cortex-A cores (e.g., A77) achieve >95% prediction accuracy using TAGE predictors.

**BTAC (1.5 marks):** The Branch Target Address Cache (BTAC) is a small, fully-associative cache that stores the **target addresses** of recently executed branches. When the fetch unit encounters a branch instruction, it consults the BTAC in parallel with the L1i cache. If the branch's PC is found (a "BTAC hit"), the predicted target is immediately available, saving 1–2 cycles that would otherwise be needed to compute the target. If a miss occurs, the branch target is computed via the branch unit and a new entry is installed in the BTAC.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module in the ESE (End Semester Exam) contains a **14-mark question with internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each 14-mark question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. The 7 marks further split as ~5 marks for the analytical content + 2 marks for the diagram/derivation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **Speculative Execution** in ARM processors. Describe how it reduces pipeline latency, and discuss the recovery mechanism used on a misprediction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **3-level cache hierarchy** (L1, L2, L3, Main Memory) in an ARM Cortex-A processor. Derive the AMAT expression for this hierarchy and compute the AMAT given: $H_{L1} = 1$, $MR_{L1} = 0.04$, $H_{L2} = 10$, $MR_{L2} = 0.15$, $H_{L3} = 30$, $MR_{L3} = 0.30$, $H_{Mem} = 250$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Speculative Execution

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
Speculative execution is a latency reduction technique in which the processor executes instructions *down the predicted path of a branch* **before** the branch outcome is known. This converts what would have been a pipeline stall (waiting for the branch to be resolved) into useful computational work. ARM Cortex-A cores (A57, A72, A75, A76, A77, A78, X1, etc.) all use speculative execution extensively. The key idea is that *speculation is a bet* — if the prediction is correct, the work is committed; if wrong, it is rolled back.

**Step 2: How it reduces latency (2 marks)**
In a 5-stage or 13-stage pipeline, a branch is typically resolved in the **Execute (EX)** stage. Without speculation, the next 2–8 instructions (depending on pipeline depth) cannot be fetched until the branch is resolved. With speculation:
- The branch predictor chooses a direction at the **Fetch (IF)** stage.
- Instructions from the predicted path are *fetched, decoded, renamed, dispatched, and executed* before the branch is resolved.
- The results are held in temporary buffers (the Reorder Buffer, ROB) and are *not* committed to the architectural state (registers, memory) until the branch outcome is known.
- The net effect: the branch's *latency cost* is hidden by the work done during speculation.

**Step 3: Recovery on misprediction (2 marks)**
When the branch outcome is known and it is *different* from the prediction:
1. **Flush the pipeline:** All in-flight speculative instructions in the ROB are marked invalid.
2. **Restore architectural state:** The rename map is rewound to the snapshot taken at branch issue.
3. **Squash the speculative state:** No architectural registers or memory locations are updated.
4. **Restart fetch** from the correct target address.
5. **Update the predictor** with the actual outcome to improve future predictions.

The *cost* of misprediction is called the **misprediction penalty**, equal to (pipeline depth − 1) + branch resolution cycle, typically 8–15 cycles in modern ARM cores.

**Step 4: Diagram reference (1 mark)**
Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes (State Diagram — Speculative Execution Lifecycle).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 3-Level Cache AMAT

**Step 1: Block Diagram (2 marks)**
Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes (Block Diagram — Cache Hierarchy & Latency Profile).

The diagram shows the CPU at the top, with L1, L2, L3, and DRAM as successive levels. Each level has a *hit time* and a *miss rate*. A miss at level $i$ is served by level $i+1$.

**Step 2: Derivation of AMAT for 3-Level Cache (2 marks)**
Let $H_i$ be the hit time at level $i$ and $MR_i$ the miss rate at level $i$. The AMAT is the *time spent at L1*, plus the *time spent at L2 conditional on an L1 miss*, plus the *time spent at L3 conditional on both L1 and L2 misses*, plus the *time at memory conditional on all three misses*.

$$AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times \left[ H_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times \left[ H_{L3} + MR_{L3} \times H_{Mem} \right] \right]$$

**Step 3: Numerical Computation (3 marks)**

$$AMAT = 1 + 0.04 \times \left[ 10 + 0.15 \times \left[ 30 + 0.30 \times 250 \right] \right]$$

First, compute the innermost bracket:
$$30 + 0.30 \times 250 = 30 + 75 = 105 \text{ cycles}$$

Next, the middle bracket:
$$10 + 0.15 \times 105 = 10 + 15.75 = 25.75 \text{ cycles}$$

Finally, the full expression:
$$AMAT = 1 + 0.04 \times 25.75 = 1 + 1.03 = 2.03 \text{ cycles}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{AMAT = 2.03 \text{ cycles per memory access}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply miss rates:** Students often write $AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times H_{L2} + H_{L3} + H_{Mem}$ — *wrong*. The miss rate at each level only applies to accesses that *missed* the previous level.
> 2. **Missing parentheses:** The expression is a *nested* product, not a sum. Use parentheses carefully.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Miss rates are dimensionless (fractions), not percentages. Convert 4% → 0.04 before computing.
> 4. **Missing diagram:** A 7-mark sub-part *must* include a labelled diagram to get full marks. KTU 2024 valuations are strict on this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]` (Alternative to Question A)

**Q: (a)** Explain **Out-of-Order Execution (OoO)** in ARM processors with a neat block diagram. How does it differ from in-order execution, and how does it help in reducing the effective CPI? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear diagram, describe the **Branch Target Address Cache (BTAC)** and the **Return Address Stack (RAS)**. A program has 20% conditional branches and 5% function calls. Branch predictor accuracy is 92%, RAS accuracy is 99%. Misprediction penalty is 10 cycles. Calculate the total branch-related stalls per 1000 instructions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Out-of-Order Execution

**Step 1: Definition and In-order vs. Out-of-order (2 marks)**
In an **in-order** pipeline, instructions are issued and completed strictly in program order. A long-latency instruction (e.g., a cache-missing load) **stalls all subsequent instructions**, even if they are independent. In an **out-of-order** pipeline, instructions are dispatched in program order into a **Reservation Station / Issue Queue**, but they are *issued to execution units* as soon as their operands are ready, regardless of program order. The results are tracked in a **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** and *retired* in program order to preserve architectural (programmer-visible) state.

**Step 2: Block Diagram (2 marks)**
The OoO block diagram should show: **Fetch → Decode → Rename → Issue Queue → [Multiple Execution Units: ALU, FPU, LSU, Branch] → ROB → Commit/Retire**. The data path shows instructions entering the IQ and being dispatched to EUs in *any* order, but the *commit* path is strictly in-order. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the integrated block diagram.

**Step 3: How it reduces CPI (2 marks)**
OoO execution **overlaps independent instructions with long-latency memory operations**. Consider the sequence:
```
LDR R0, [R1]    ; Miss — 200 cycles to DRAM
ADD R2, R3, R4  ; Independent — could complete in 1 cycle
SUB R5, R6, R7  ; Independent — could complete in 1 cycle
```
In-order execution stalls the ADD and SUB for 200 cycles. OoO execution issues the ADD and SUB *immediately*, completing them while the LDR is still in flight. The effective CPI contribution of the LDR miss drops from 200 to (200 − work done) ≈ a few cycles.

**Step 4: Trade-offs (1 mark)**
OoO execution adds complexity: register renaming, ROB management, wake-up logic, and replay mechanisms. ARM's Cortex-M class cores (e.g., M0, M3, M4) are *in-order* because they target low-power embedded use cases, while the Cortex-A class uses OoO for high performance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — BTAC, RAS, and Stall Calculation

**Step 1: BTAC Diagram (1.5 marks)**
The BTAC is a small associative structure indexed by the branch instruction's PC. It stores the predicted target address. On a fetch, the BTAC and L1i are accessed in parallel. A BTAC hit provides the predicted target; a miss falls back to the branch unit. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the integrated location of the BTAC.

**Step 2: RAS Diagram and Explanation (1.5 marks)**
The **Return Address Stack (RAS)** is a small hardware stack (typically 8–16 entries deep in ARM) that stores the *return addresses* of `BL` (Branch-with-Link) instructions. When a `BL` is executed, the return address is pushed onto the RAS. When a `RET` (or `PC` load with a return-pattern target) is fetched, the RAS is popped to provide the predicted return target. This is critical because return addresses are *highly predictable* (always the instruction after the `BL`).

**Step 3: Stall Calculation (4 marks)**
Per 1000 instructions:
- Conditional branches: $1000 \times 0.20 = 200$ branches
- Function calls: $1000 \times 0.05 = 50$ calls

Misprediction rates:
- Conditional: $1 - 0.92 = 0.08$
- RAS: $1 - 0.99 = 0.01$

Stall cycles:
$$Stalls_{cond} = 200 \times 0.08 \times 10 = 160 \text{ cycles}$$
$$Stalls_{RAS} = 50 \times 0.01 \times 10 = 5 \text{ cycles}$$

Total branch-related stalls per 1000 instructions:
$$Total = 160 + 5 = \boxed{165 \text{ cycles per 1000 instructions}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts for Question B:**
> 1. **Diagrams are mandatory.** A 7-mark sub-part with no diagram may lose 2–3 marks in KTU 2024 valuations.
> 2. **RAS accuracy is separate from branch predictor accuracy.** A common mistake is to use the same 92% for both. RAS accuracy is typically higher (98–99%) because returns are deterministic.
> 3. **Unit scaling:** Always express miss rates as fractions (0.08, not 8) when multiplying by cycle counts.
> 4. **Stall calculation must be per-instruction or per-1000-instructions, not absolute.** Make the denominator explicit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Latency Reduction Techniques**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Latency Definition:** Cycles between issue and result availability of an instruction.
- **AMAT Equation:** $AMAT = Hit\_Time + Miss\_Rate \times Miss\_Penalty$
- **Multi-level AMAT:** $AMAT = H_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times (H_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times (H_{L3} + MR_{L3} \times H_{Mem}))$
- **CPI with stalls:** $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + Stalls_{cache} + Stalls_{branch}$
- **Branch stall:** $Stalls_{branch} = Branch\_Frac \times (1 - Acc) \times Penalty$
- **Misprediction rate = 1 − Accuracy** (a frequent exam trap).

### The 5 Pillars of Latency Reduction (in order of exam importance)
1. **Memory Hierarchy & Caches** — L1 (split, ~1–4 cycles), L2 (unified, ~8–20 cycles), L3 (shared, ~30–60 cycles), Main Memory (200–500 cycles).
2. **Branch Prediction** — TAGE predictor in modern Cortex-A (A77+); >95% accuracy.
3. **Speculative Execution** — Execute down predicted path; squash on mispredict.
4. **Out-of-Order Execution** — Issue when operands ready; retire in program order.
5. **Prefetching** — Sequential, stride, and stream prefetchers in modern ARM.

### ARM-Specific Features to Remember
- **BTAC** (Branch Target Address Cache): Caches target addresses of recent branches.
- **RAS** (Return Address Stack): 8–16 entries; predicts function return addresses.
- **Register Renaming:** Architectural → Physical mapping; eliminates false WAW/WAR hazards.
- **ROB (Reorder Buffer):** 128–256 entries in modern Cortex-A; in-order commit buffer.
- **Critical Word First / Early Restart:** Reduces miss penalty by delivering the demanded word first on a cache fill.
- **Cache line size:** Typically **64 bytes** in ARM Cortex-A.
- **Write policy:** Write-Back with Write-Allocate is the standard for Cortex-A.

### Trade-off Triangle (Always Mentioned in KTU Answers)
- **Hit Time ↔ Miss Rate ↔ Miss Penalty** — You cannot optimize all three.
- Bigger cache → lower miss rate, higher hit time.
- Higher associativity → lower miss rate, higher hit time.
- Multi-level cache → balances all three, adds coherence complexity.

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Using *miss rate* directly (e.g., 0.04) is correct, but using *miss rate as a percentage* (e.g., 4) is a **unit error** costing 1–2 marks.
- ❌ Forgetting the **multiplicative cascade** in multi-level AMAT.
- ❌ Conflating **BTAC** with the branch predictor — they are *different* structures with different purposes.
- ❌ Saying "out-of-order means random order" — *incorrect*; it means *issue is out of order, but commit is in order*.
- ❌ Forgetting to include a **diagram** in a 7-mark sub-part.

### Numerical Practice Targets (Always Have These Memorized)
- L1 hit time: 1–4 cycles
- L2 hit time: 8–20 cycles
- DRAM access: 200–500 cycles
- Branch misprediction penalty: 8–15 cycles
- Typical L1 cache size: 32 KB (Cortex-A53), 64 KB (Cortex-A77)
- Typical line size: 64 bytes
- Typical TLB miss penalty: 30–200 cycles (depending on page walk depth)

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, always structure your answer in **three clear blocks**: (1) **Definition + Diagram (3 marks)**, (2) **Explanation / Mechanism (5 marks)**, (3) **Numerical / Derivation (3 marks)**, and **Conclusion / Trade-off (1 mark)** and a **warning/recommendation (2 marks)**. KTU examiners award full marks only when the answer is *structured*, *labelled*, and *complete*. Never write essays without headings, formulas in plain text, or diagrams as ASCII art.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priorities and Nested Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Priorities and Nested Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **ARM Cortex-M** processor architecture, **priorities** are numeric values assigned to interrupts and exceptions that govern the order in which the processor services pending events when multiple requests occur simultaneously. **Nested exceptions** refer to the mechanism by which the Cortex-M core allows a higher-priority exception to preempt the currently executing exception handler, save its context on the stack, execute the new handler, and return precisely to the interrupted handler without any software stacking overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Priorities in ARM are governed by the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**. The lower the priority number, the higher the logical priority of the exception. Priority levels are stored in programmable registers and are **sub-priority** aware in some variants (e.g., Cortex-M3/M4/M7).

The Cortex-M architecture supports a configurable number of priority bits (typically **3 to 8 bits**), allowing anywhere from **8 to 256 distinct priority levels**. Reset, NMI, and HardFault possess fixed (negative-priority equivalent) statuses and are **non-maskable** in the conventional sense.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Room"

Imagine a busy hospital Emergency Room (ER) where patients (exceptions) arrive simultaneously:

- **Patients are exceptions.** A heart attack (Reset/HardFault) is a critical, life-threatening case that preempts everything.
- **Triage nurses assign priority numbers.** A lower number means higher urgency (Priority 0 is most urgent).
- **Doctor currently treating Patient A (low priority) hears a code-blue alarm (high priority exception).** The doctor does NOT finish Patient A immediately; instead, she writes a quick note on the patient's chart (stacking the context), puts Patient A on hold, and rushes to Patient B (nesting).
- **After stabilizing Patient B, the doctor returns to Patient A's chart, reads the notes, and resumes treatment** (exception return / unstacking).

This is precisely how **Nested Exceptions** work in ARM Cortex-M. The hardware automatically pushes registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the stack — a process called **stacking** — without any instruction overhead. When returning, an **unstack** restores the previous state.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Embedded Systems

Real-time embedded systems (automotive ECUs, medical devices, motor controllers) demand **deterministic response times**. The NVIC's nested exception mechanism provides:

- **Deterministic interrupt latency** (as low as **12 clock cycles** for entry)
- **No software overhead** for context saving (hardware does it)
- **Predictable preemption** based on priority values

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — "Priority"**
> A priority is a non-negative integer assigned to an interrupt source that determines its **preemption rank** relative to other active exceptions. The **active exception** is the one whose handler is currently executing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — "Nested Exception"**
> A nested exception occurs when an exception handler is itself interrupted by another exception of **higher priority**, requiring a second context save on the stack before servicing the new exception.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of Exception Priority

The ARM Cortex-M exception model rests on three pillars:

1. **Priority Numbering Rule (Inverse Logic)**
   - Priority **0 = highest urgency** (most urgent).
   - Priority **N (max) = lowest urgency** (least urgent).
   - Reset, NMI, and HardFault have **non-configurable** priority and are effectively at priority **−1, −2, −3** (lower number = higher).

2. **Preemption vs. Sub-Priority**
   - **Preemption Priority**: Determines whether an exception can preempt another. A higher-priority (lower number) exception preempts a lower-priority (higher number) one.
   - **Sub-Priority**: Used only when two exceptions of the **same preemption level** arrive simultaneously. The lower sub-priority number wins. Sub-priority does **not** cause preemption.

3. **Priority Grouping (PRIGROUP)**
   - The `AIRCR.PRIGROUP` field partitions the priority bits into `[Preemption : Sub]` fields.
   - Example: With **3 priority bits** and `PRIGROUP = 4` (binary), split could be **1 preemption bit, 2 sub-priority bits**.

## 2.2 The Exception Entry Sequence (Hardware Stacking)

When an exception is accepted, the hardware performs these steps **atomically** (no other code can run in between):

| Step | Hardware Action | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Push Registers | R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR pushed onto current SP |
| 2 | Read Vector | Fetch handler address from Vector Table |
| 3 | Update IPSR | Set IPSR to the active exception number |
| 4 | Load PC | Begin executing the handler |
| 5 | LR Update | LR loaded with special **EXC_RETURN** value |

The total stacking time is **deterministic** — 12 cycles on Cortex-M3/M4 (no FP) or **30 cycles** with lazy stacking on FPU variants.

## 2.3 The Exception Exit Sequence

Triggered by a special return instruction (often a `POP {PC, ...}` or dedicated instruction like `BX LR` where LR contains the EXC_RETURN magic value):

1. Unstack registers from PSP/MSP.
2. Restore IPSR to its previous value.
3. Return to the address stored in the popped PC.
4. NVIC clears the active bit for that exception.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This hardware-stacking model is why ARM Cortex-M is the dominant choice in real-time control loops (BLDC motor control, robotic actuators) where microsecond-level determinism is required.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| Priority Inversion | $P_{\text{logical}} = 2^{N} - 1 - P_{\text{register}}$ | Cortex-M uses inverted logic |
| Number of Priority Levels | $L = 2^{N_{\text{priority\_bits}}}$ | $N_{\text{priority\_bits}}$ = implemented priority bits (3–8) |
| Sub-Priority Field Width | $S = N_{\text{priority\_bits}} - P$ | $P$ = preemption field width |
| Preemption Field Width | $P = N_{\text{priority\_bits}} - S$ | Configurable via AIRCR.PRIGROUP |
| Stacking Time (Cortex-M3/M4) | $T_{\text{stack}} = 12 \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | 12 clock cycles (no FPU) |
| Stacking Time (FPU Lazy) | $T_{\text{stack}} = 30 \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | With floating-point context |
| EXC_RETURN Value (MSP) | $\text{EXC\_RETURN} = 0xFFFFFFF1$ | Return to MSP, Handler mode |
| EXC_RETURN Value (PSP) | $\text{EXC\_RETURN} = 0xFFFFFFF9$ | Return to Process stack |
| EXC_RETURN Magic Bits | Bits [31:4] all `1`s | Used by hardware to detect return |
| Interrupt Latency (Worst) | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{stack}} + T_{\text{fetch}} + T_{\text{handler}}$ | For deterministic analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXC_RETURN Encoding Rule:** The hardware recognizes bits [31:4] = `0xFFFFFFF` as a valid EXC_RETURN. Bits [3:0] encode the return stack and mode.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Distinct Priority Levels

The Cortex-M core implements a configurable priority register width. Let $N$ denote the number of implemented priority bits.

**Step 1:** The priority field is stored in an 8-bit register location within the Interrupt Priority Register (IPR).

**Step 2:** The number of unique priority levels is a power of 2 based on $N$:

$$L = 2^{N}$$

**Step 3:** For example, when the vendor implements **4 priority bits**:

$$L = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ distinct priority levels}$$

**Step 4:** Valid priority values range from $0$ to $2^{N} - 1$. With $N = 4$:

$$P_{\text{valid}} \in \left[ 0, \; 2^{4} - 1 \right] = \left[ 0, \; 15 \right]$$

**Step 5:** Lower number means higher priority, so the *most urgent* is $0$ and the *least urgent* is $15$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sub-Priority vs. Preemption Field Split

Given a total of $N$ priority bits and a `PRIGROUP` value $g$, the split is computed as:

**Step 1:** The preemption field occupies the **upper bits** of the priority byte.

$$P_{\text{preempt\_bits}} = N - g$$

**Step 2:** The sub-priority field occupies the **lower bits**.

$$P_{\text{sub\_bits}} = g$$

**Step 3:** Total bits satisfy conservation:

$$N = (N - g) + g = N \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Worked example: $N = 4$ (4 priority bits), `PRIGROUP = 5`.

$$P_{\text{preempt\_bits}} = 4 - 5$$

Wait — this is a *negative* value. The actual rule is more nuanced. The Cortex-M3 uses the convention where `PRIGROUP` = 7 minus the binary point position. Let us adopt the **STM32 standard**:

$$P_{\text{preempt\_bits}} = \begin{cases} 0, & g \geq N \\ N - g, & g < N \end{cases}$$

For $N = 4$ and $g = 2$ (2 preemption bits, 2 sub-priority bits):

$$P_{\text{preempt\_bits}} = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ bits} \implies 2^{2} = 4 \text{ preemption levels}$$
$$P_{\text{sub\_bits}} = 2 \text{ bits} \implies 2^{2} = 4 \text{ sub-priority levels per preemption level}$$

**Step 5:** The actual priority byte value for a given preemption level $p$ and sub-priority $s$ is:

$$V_{\text{byte}} = (p \ll g) \;\vert\; s$$

Note: we use the semicolon `\vert` (LaTeX math mode) to avoid breaking markdown tables.

**Step 6:** Numerical example — preemption level $1$, sub-priority $2$, $g = 2$:

$$V_{\text{byte}} = (1 \ll 2) + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6$$

In binary: $0000\,0110_2$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Exception Behavior by Priority State

| Current State | New Exception Priority | Preemption? | Stack Operation | Result |
|---------------|------------------------|-------------|-----------------|--------|
| Thread Mode (no exception) | Any exception | Yes (always) | Push 8 registers | New handler executes |
| Handler A (priority 5) | New = priority 3 (higher) | **Yes (Nested)** | Push 8 registers on stack | Handler B runs; A suspended |
| Handler A (priority 5) | New = priority 7 (lower) | **No** | None | New exception **pends** until A finishes |
| Handler A (priority 5) | New = priority 5, sub = 2 | No (same preempt) | None | Sub-priority tie-break; pends if sub is higher number |
| Handler A (priority 5) | New = priority 5, sub = 1 | No (same preempt) | None | A continues; new exception pends |
| Handler A (priority 5) | New = HardFault (priority -1) | **Yes (always)** | Push 8 registers | HardFault handler runs |

## 3.4 Derivation: Stack Frame Contents

When an exception occurs, the hardware pushes **8 words** in a fixed order. Let us derive the frame structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame}[0] &= R0 \\
\text{Frame}[1] &= R1 \\
\text{Frame}[2] &= R2 \\
\text{Frame}[3] &= R3 \\
\text{Frame}[4] &= R12 \\
\text{Frame}[5] &= LR \quad (\text{return address of caller}) \\
\text{Frame}[6] &= PC \quad (\text{saved program counter of interrupted code}) \\
\text{Frame}[7] &= xPSR \quad (\text{status register with IPSR set to exception number})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** The stack pointer is decremented by $8 \times 4 = 32$ bytes before any push (full descending stack convention).

$$SP_{\text{new}} = SP_{\text{old}} - 32$$

**Step 2:** When a *second* exception (nested) preempts, a second 32-byte frame is pushed.

$$SP_{\text{after\_2nd}} = SP_{\text{old}} - 64$$

**Step 3:** On exit, the stack pointer is restored by the same amount.

$$SP_{\text{restored}} = SP_{\text{current}} + 32 \text{ per exception return}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Nested Exception Timing

Suppose a **72 MHz Cortex-M4** is running. Calculate worst-case interrupt latency for a nested case.

**Step 1:** Clock period.

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} = 13.89 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2:** Stacking time (no FPU).

$$T_{\text{stack}} = 12 \cdot T_{\text{clk}} = 12 \times 13.89 \text{ ns} = 166.67 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3:** Vector fetch time.

$$T_{\text{fetch}} = 1 \cdot T_{\text{clk}} = 13.89 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4:** Total latency for first exception.

$$T_{\text{latency, 1st}} = 166.67 + 13.89 = 180.56 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5:** For a *nested* exception (interrupting the first handler), the same stacking happens again, but the processor must also fetch the new vector.

$$T_{\text{latency, nested}} = 180.56 \text{ ns} \quad (\text{same, deterministic})$$

This **determinism** is the engineering reason real-time systems use Cortex-M.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Exception Priority Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Event Trigger: IRQ or Exception] --> nodeB{Is Higher Priority Active?}
    nodeB -- Yes, Higher Priority --> nodeC[Hardware Stacking: Push 8 Regs]
    nodeB -- No, Lower or Equal --> nodeD[Pend: Wait for Current Handler]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Read Vector Table: Fetch Handler Address]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Load PC: Jump to Handler]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Execute Handler Code]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Exception Return Triggered?}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeG
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Hardware Unstack: Pop 8 Regs]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Restore PC: Return to Interrupted Code]
    nodeD --> nodeK{Current Handler Finished?}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeC
    nodeK -- No --> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Nested Exception Preemption

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Thread/Main Code
    participant HandlerA as Handler A (Priority 5)
    participant HandlerB as Handler B (Priority 2)
    participant Stack as Hardware Stack

    Main->>Stack: Push frame: R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR
    Main->>HandlerA: PC loaded with Handler A address
    HandlerA->>HandlerA: Executing instructions...
    Note over HandlerA: IRQ B fires with Priority 2
    HandlerA->>Stack: Push 2nd frame: R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR
    HandlerA->>HandlerB: PC loaded with Handler B address
    HandlerB->>HandlerB: Executing instructions...
    HandlerB->>Stack: Pop frame: restore to Handler A state
    HandlerB->>HandlerA: PC returns to Handler A
    HandlerA->>HandlerA: Continues execution
    HandlerA->>Stack: Pop frame: restore to Main state
    HandlerA->>Main: PC returns to Main code
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: NVIC Priority Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[IRQ Input Lines: 0 to N] --> nodeY[NVIC Priority Comparator]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Active Exception Register]
    nodeZ --> nodeW{New vs Active Priority}
    nodeW -- New is Higher --> nodeV[Trigger Preemption: Stacking]
    nodeW -- New is Lower or Equal --> nodeU[Set Pending Bit]
    nodeV --> nodeT[Vector Table Fetch]
    nodeT --> nodeS[Handler Execution]
    nodeS --> nodeR[EXC_RETURN Detect]
    nodeR --> nodeQ[Unstack and Restore]
    nodeU --> nodeY
```

## 4.4 Stack Frame Layout (Textual Diagram)

```
Higher Address (Stack Bottom)
+-----------------------+
|       (older data)    |
+-----------------------+
|        xPSR   [31]    |  <-- SP after 1st exception
|        PC     [30]    |
|        LR     [29]    |
|        R12    [28]    |
|        R3     [27]    |
|        R2     [26]    |
|        R1     [25]    |
|        R0     [24]    |  <-- SP before 1st exception
+-----------------------+
|        xPSR   [23]    |  <-- SP after 2nd (nested) exception
|        PC     [22]    |
|        LR     [21]    |
|        R12    [20]    |
|        R3     [19]    |
|        R2     [18]    |
|        R1     [17]    |
|        R0     [16]    |  <-- SP before 2nd exception
+-----------------------+
Lower Address (Stack Top - MSP)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Nested Exception" in the context of ARM Cortex-M. Why is hardware stacking critical for nested exception handling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **nested exception** occurs when an exception handler is itself interrupted by a higher-priority exception before it completes execution. Hardware stacking is critical because the ARM Cortex-M NVIC automatically pushes 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the stack in **12 clock cycles** without any software instruction overhead. This **deterministic, zero-overhead stacking** allows the system to handle deeply nested interrupts with predictable latency, which is essential in real-time embedded systems. Without hardware stacking, the additional instructions to save context would make response times non-deterministic.

*(Keywords for full marks: deterministic, 12 cycles, hardware stacking, R0–R3 R12 LR PC xPSR, no software overhead)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: Explain the role of "Preemption Priority" and "Sub-Priority" in the ARM Cortex-M NVIC. How does the PRIGROUP field configure them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Preemption Priority** determines whether an exception can preempt another currently executing exception. A lower numerical preemption value means higher priority. **Sub-Priority** is used only when two exceptions with the same preemption level arrive simultaneously — the one with the lower sub-priority value wins, but it does NOT cause preemption. The **PRIGROUP field** in the AIRCR register partitions the implemented priority bits into preemption and sub-priority fields. For example, with 4 priority bits and PRIGROUP=2, the upper 2 bits form the preemption field (4 levels) and the lower 2 bits form the sub-priority field (4 sub-levels per preemption level). This grouping allows flexible priority management for complex real-time workloads.

*(Keywords: preemption, sub-priority, lower number higher priority, PRIGROUP, partition, AIRCR)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the hardware stacking process during a nested exception in ARM Cortex-M. List the registers pushed onto the stack and their order.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
When an exception is accepted in ARM Cortex-M3/M4, the NVIC automatically performs hardware stacking. In a nested scenario, this happens multiple times without software intervention.

**Registers Pushed (3 marks):**
The hardware pushes **8 words (32 bytes)** onto the active stack pointer (MSP by default) in the following fixed order, from highest to lowest address (full descending stack):

1. **R0**
2. **R1**
3. **R2**
4. **R3**
5. **R12**
6. **LR** (Link Register of interrupted code)
7. **PC** (Return address of interrupted code)
8. **xPSR** (with IPSR updated to the active exception number)

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(See the textual stack diagram in Section 4.4.)*

**Nested Behavior (1 mark):**
For a second (nested) exception, the entire 32-byte frame is pushed **on top** of the first frame. The SP decrements by another 32 bytes, allowing unlimited nesting depth limited only by stack size.

**Conclusion:**
This zero-overhead hardware stacking is the cornerstone of deterministic real-time response in Cortex-M.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: A Cortex-M4F processor runs at 72 MHz. Calculate:**
- **(i)** The deterministic interrupt latency for a single exception (no nesting).
- **(ii)** The deterministic interrupt latency for a nested exception interrupting the first handler.
- **(iii)** Total cycles consumed if the handler executes 50 instructions.

**Model Answer:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- Clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}} = 72 \text{ MHz}$
- Cortex-M4F stacking time (no FP) = 12 cycles
- Vector fetch = 1 cycle

**Step 1: Clock period (1 mark)**

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} = 13.89 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2: Latency for a single exception (2 marks)**

$$T_{\text{latency, single}} = (12 + 1) \times T_{\text{clk}} = 13 \times 13.89 \text{ ns} = 180.56 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Latency for a nested exception (2 marks):**
The nested exception requires the same stacking + vector fetch again:

$$T_{\text{latency, nested}} = 13 \times 13.89 \text{ ns} = 180.56 \text{ ns}$$

This is identical because the hardware stacking is deterministic regardless of nesting depth.

**Step 4: Total cycles including handler (1 mark):**

$$C_{\text{total}} = 12 \text{ (stack)} + 1 \text{ (fetch)} + 50 \text{ (handler)} = 63 \text{ cycles}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 63 \times 13.89 \text{ ns} = 875 \text{ ns}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the priority grouping mechanism in ARM Cortex-M. Given that the processor implements 4 priority bits, derive the priority byte value for an exception with preemption level 2 and sub-priority 1, using PRIGROUP = 2.**

**Model Answer:**

**Priority Grouping (2 marks):**
The AIRCR register's **PRIGROUP field** (3 bits) partitions the implemented priority bits into a preemption field (upper bits) and a sub-priority field (lower bits). The preemption bits determine preemption rights; sub-priority bits resolve simultaneous exceptions of the same preemption level.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Given $N = 4$ priority bits and $g = \text{PRIGROUP} = 2$:

- Preemption field width: $4 - 2 = 2$ bits (4 levels: 0–3)
- Sub-priority field width: $2$ bits (4 sub-levels: 0–3)

The priority byte is constructed as:

$$V_{\text{byte}} = (p \ll g) + s$$

Substituting $p = 2$, $g = 2$, $s = 1$:

$$V_{\text{byte}} = (2 \ll 2) + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9$$

In binary: $0000\,1001_2$.

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
The preemption bits `10` occupy the upper 2 bits, and the sub-priority bits `01` occupy the lower 2 bits. This exception can preempt any handler with a preemption value higher than `10` (i.e., preemption values 2 or 3), but cannot preempt those with values 0 or 1. Among exceptions of the same preemption level, the one with sub-priority 1 takes precedence.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Differentiate between Preemption Priority and Sub-Priority with a state-transition diagram. What happens when two exceptions of identical priority are triggered simultaneously?**

**Model Answer:**

**Differences (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Preemption Priority | Sub-Priority |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| Purpose | Decides preemption rights | Resolves simultaneous exceptions |
| Effect | Can cause nesting (preempt) | NEVER causes preemption |
| Comparison Rule | Lower number = higher priority | Lower number = higher priority |
| Tie Behavior | Triggers nesting or pending | Decides which one runs first |

**State-Transition Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ThreadMode
    ThreadMode --> HandlerX : New exception accepted
    HandlerX --> HandlerY : Higher preemption priority exception arrives
    HandlerY --> HandlerX : Handler Y returns (unstack)
    HandlerX --> ThreadMode : Handler X returns
    HandlerX --> HandlerX : New exception with same preemption, higher sub (pended)
    HandlerX --> HandlerX : New exception with same preemption, lower sub (no effect)
```

**Simultaneous Same-Priority Case (1 mark):**
If two exceptions have **identical** preemption AND sub-priority values, the one with the **lower exception number** (earlier in the vector table) wins. For example, IRQ 3 wins over IRQ 5 if both have the same priority byte. This is a fixed tie-breaking rule in the NVIC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the inverse logic:** Many students incorrectly state that "higher priority number means higher priority." In Cortex-M, the **lower** the number, the **higher** the priority. This is the #1 mark-losing mistake.
> - **Confusing Sub-Priority with Preemption:** Sub-priority does NOT cause nesting. It only resolves ties. Writing "sub-priority also causes preemption" will cost 2–3 marks.
> - **Neglecting EXC_RETURN:** When explaining exception exit, students often forget that the LR contains a special EXC_RETURN magic value (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF1`) which the hardware recognizes.
> - **Ignoring the stack pointer choice:** Cortex-M uses MSP for handler mode by default but can use PSP for thread mode. Forgetting this distinction loses marks in advanced questions.
> - **Wrong units in calculations:** Always use **nanoseconds (ns)** or **microseconds (µs)** for timing and include the clock period derivation explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Determinism:** Hardware stacking takes **12 clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4 (no FPU) — this is the foundation of real-time behavior.
- 🔢 **Priority Logic:** Lower numerical value = higher priority. Priority 0 is the most urgent user-configurable level.
- 🚫 **Fixed-Priority Exceptions:** **Reset, NMI, and HardFault** are non-maskable and have fixed (highest) priorities. They CANNOT be preempted by configurable exceptions.
- 🏗 **Hardware Stacking:** The 8 registers pushed are **R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR** — in this exact order, on the current stack pointer.
- 🔁 **EXC_RETURN Magic Value:** Bits [31:4] = `0xFFFFFFF` indicates a valid exception return. Common values are `0xFFFFFFF1` (return to MSP) and `0xFFFFFFF9` (return to PSP).
- 🪜 **Nesting Depth:** Limited only by available stack memory. Each nested level consumes 32 bytes (no FPU) or 64+ bytes (with FPU).
- 🎚 **PRIGROUP Field:** Splits priority bits into preemption and sub-priority. Configurable via AIRCR register.
- ⏸ **Pending Mechanism:** A lower-priority exception that arrives during a higher-priority handler is **pended** and serviced later.
- 🤝 **Same-Priority Tie-Break:** Lower exception number wins. Sub-priority decides only when preemption levels match.
- ⚡ **FPU Cost:** With FPU enabled, stacking jumps to 30 cycles (lazy) or 32 cycles (eager) — this is critical for deterministic design in DSP applications.
- 🧠 **Vector Table Location:** By default at `0x00000000` (or `0x08000000` in remapped STM32), each entry is 4 bytes containing the handler address.
- 📊 **Latency Formula:** $T_{\text{latency}} = (N_{\text{stack}} + N_{\text{fetch}}) \times T_{\text{clk}}$ where $N_{\text{stack}} = 12$ (typical) and $N_{\text{fetch}} = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** in ARM architecture refers to the coordinated control of shared resource access among multiple processes, threads, or processor cores to prevent race conditions, ensure data consistency, and maintain program correctness. ARM provides hardware-assisted synchronization primitives including **atomic operations**, **exclusive access instructions (`LDREX`/`STREX`)**, **memory barriers (`DMB`, `DSB`, `ISB`)**, and legacy **`SWP`** instruction to support this coordination.

In a multi-tasking or multi-core environment, two or more execution contexts (threads/cores) may attempt to read-modify-write the same memory location simultaneously. Without synchronization, **race conditions** occur, producing non-deterministic, incorrect behavior. Synchronization enforces **mutual exclusion**, ensuring that critical sections of code execute atomically.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The Bathroom Key Hook** 🏢
>
> Imagine a single bathroom in a co-working space with **one key hanging on a hook outside the door**. 
> - **The hook** = the shared memory variable (a `lock`).
> - **The bathroom** = the critical section (shared resource).
> - **Picking up the key** = acquiring the lock (`LDREX`).
> - **Returning the key** = releasing the lock (`STREX`).
> - **Only one person at a time** can take the key — guaranteeing mutual exclusion.
>
> If a second person tries to take the key, they must **wait** (spinlock) until the first person returns it. This is exactly how a thread/CPU synchronizes access to a shared resource in an ARM-based system.

## 1.3 Why Synchronization is Critical in ARM Systems

| Problem | Consequence Without Synchronization |
|---|---|
| **Race Condition** | Two threads write to the same variable; final value is unpredictable |
| **Torn Read/Write** | A 32-bit value is read/written in two 16-bit halves on a 16-bit bus |
| **Memory Reordering** | Compiler/CPU reorders instructions, breaking logical dependencies |
| **Cache Coherency** (Multi-core) | One core's write to cached data is invisible to other cores |

## 1.4 Key ARM Hardware Primitives (Module 4 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Hardware Mechanisms for Synchronization:**
> 1. **Atomicity through Exclusive Access Monitor** — `LDREX`, `STREX`, `LDREXB/H`, `STREXB/H`
> 2. **Legacy Swap** — `SWP`, `SWPB` (deprecated in ARMv8, retained for context)
> 3. **Memory Ordering Barriers** — `DMB`, `DSB`, `ISB`
> 4. **Exclusive Monitor (Local & Global)** — hardware component inside the core
> 5. **Spinlock Implementation Patterns** — using above primitives in software

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM Exclusive Access Mechanism — Core Theory

ARM implements atomic read-modify-write operations **without** locking the bus. Instead, it uses a per-address **exclusive monitor** that tracks exclusive accesses. The flow is a two-instruction handshake:

### Step-by-Step Logical Operation

1. **Load-Exclusive (`LDREX Rt, [Rn]`)**
   - Reads a value from memory address `Rn` into register `Rt`.
   - Marks the address as **exclusive** for the issuing core by setting a tag in the local exclusive monitor.

2. **Modify Operation**
   - The core performs the required computation (e.g., increment, set a bit) on the value held in `Rt`. This is a normal register operation, not a memory access.

3. **Store-Exclusive (`STREX Rd, Rt, [Rn]`)**
   - Attempts to write the modified value back to the **same address** `Rn`.
   - The exclusive monitor checks: *"Did any other master write to this address since the LDREX?"*
     - If **NO** other write occurred → store **succeeds**, `Rd = 0`.
     - If **YES** another write occurred → store **fails**, `Rd ≠ 0`, and the memory is **not** modified.
   - The exclusive tag is cleared regardless of success/failure.

4. **Software Retry Loop**
   - On `STREX` failure (`Rd = 1`), the entire LDREX–modify–STREX sequence is retried until it succeeds. This is the basis of all spinlock implementations.

## 2.2 The Exclusive Monitor — Hardware Component

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Tiers of Exclusive Monitors:**
> - **Local Monitor (per core)**: Tracks exclusive accesses for the issuing core. The store-exclusive check happens here first.
> - **Global Monitor (in bus interconnect)**: For multi-core systems, a global monitor tracks exclusive addresses across cores. If Core 0 holds an exclusive address and Core 1 writes to it, the global monitor invalidates Core 0's exclusive tag.

| Monitor Type | Scope | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Local Monitor | Single core (single-thread) | All multi-core ARM systems |
| Global Monitor | Multi-core (cross-core) | ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore, A15, A53, A57, A72, A73, A75, A76, A78, A710, X1, etc. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Instruction | Syntax | Operation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LDREX` | `LDREX Rt, [Rn]` | Load exclusive word | Begin atomic sequence |
| `LDREXB` | `LDREXB Rt, [Rn]` | Load exclusive byte | 8-bit atomic access |
| `LDREXH` | `LDREXH Rt, [Rn]` | Load exclusive halfword | 16-bit atomic access |
| `LDREXD` | `LDREXD Rt, Rt2, [Rn]` | Load exclusive doubleword | 64-bit atomic access (ARMv7-A) |
| `STREX` | `STREX Rd, Rt, [Rn]` | Store exclusive word; `Rd`=result | Conditional atomic write |
| `STREXB` | `STREXB Rd, Rt, [Rn]` | Store exclusive byte | 8-bit atomic write |
| `STREXH` | `STREXH Rd, Rt, [Rn]` | Store exclusive halfword | 16-bit atomic write |
| `STREXD` | `STREXD Rd, Rt, Rt2, [Rn]` | Store exclusive doubleword | 64-bit atomic write |
| `CLREX` | `CLREX` | Clear exclusive monitor | Force-clear local monitor tag |
| `SWP` | `SWP Rt, Rt2, [Rn]` | Atomic swap word (legacy) | Pre-ARMv6 atomic |
| `SWPB` | `SWPB Rt, Rt2, [Rn]` | Atomic swap byte (legacy) | Pre-ARMv6 atomic |
| `DMB` | `DMB {option}` | Data Memory Barrier | Order memory accesses |
| `DSB` | `DSB {option}` | Data Sync Barrier | Complete pending accesses |
| `ISB` | `ISB {option}` | Instruction Sync Barrier | Flush pipeline |

### Memory Barrier Options

| Option | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `SY` (default) | Full system — orders all accesses |
| `ST` | Store-side only — orders stores |
| `LD` | Load-side only — orders loads |
| `ISH` | Inner Shareable domain |
| `ISHST` | Inner Shareable, store-only |
| `NSH` | Non-shareable domain |

### STREX Return Value (Critical)

| `Rd` Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **`0`** | Store **succeeded** — exclusive access was preserved |
| **`1`** | Store **failed** — exclusivity was lost (retry required) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating System Kernels (Linux, FreeRTOS, RTOS)**: implement mutexes, semaphores, spinlocks using `LDREX`/`STREX` in `arch_spin_lock()` family of functions.
- **Multicore Bootloaders (U-Boot)**: synchronize secondary core bring-up.
- **Bare-metal Firmware**: critical-section guarding of peripheral registers.
- **Hypervisors (KVM, Xen on ARM)**: IPI (Inter-Processor Interrupt) handling synchronization.
- **Device Drivers**: protecting shared DMA descriptors and ring buffers.
- **Real-time Industrial Control**: ensuring deterministic updates to control variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Atomic Increment Using LDREX/STREX

**Problem:** Write an ARM assembly function that atomically increments a 32-bit counter in memory and returns its new value.

### Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1: Set up retry loop entry label**
A loop is required because `STREX` may fail. We use a label `retry` as the loop target.

**Step 2: Perform `LDREX` to read current value**
This loads the memory location into `r1` and tags the address exclusive in the local monitor.

**Step 3: Increment the loaded value**
A standard `ADD` instruction operates on the register copy — no memory access occurs.

**Step 4: Attempt `STREX` to write back**
If exclusive tag is intact, write succeeds and `r2 = 0`. Otherwise, `r2 = 1` and the store is discarded.

**Step 5: Branch on failure**
If `r2 = 1`, branch back to `retry`. If `r2 = 0`, the new value is in `r1`; return it.

### Formal Assembly Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{atomic\_increment:} \\
&\quad \text{retry:} \quad \text{LDREX} \quad r1, [r0] \quad &\text{// Step 1: Load exclusive from address in r0} \\
&\quad \text{ADD} \quad r1, r1, \#1 \quad &\text{// Step 2: Increment the local copy} \\
&\quad \text{STREX} \quad r2, r1, [r0] \quad &\text{// Step 3: Conditionally store, r2=status} \\
&\quad \text{CMP} \quad r2, \#0 \quad &\text{// Step 4: Test STREX result} \\
&\quad \text{BNE} \quad \text{retry} \quad &\text{// Step 5: Retry if r2 = 1 (failure)} \\
&\quad \text{MOV} \quad r0, r1 \quad &\text{// Step 6: Return new value in r0} \\
&\quad \text{BX} \quad \text{lr} \quad &\text{// Step 7: Return to caller}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Note:** The function takes the address of the counter in `r0`. If two cores call this simultaneously, the global monitor guarantees that exactly one STREX succeeds; the other receives `r2 = 1` and retries, re-reading the updated value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spinlock Acquire/Release (Mutex)

**Problem:** Implement an ARM spinlock for kernel-level mutual exclusion.

### Spinlock Data Structure

The lock is a single memory word:
- Value **`0`** → lock is **free**.
- Value **`1`** → lock is **held**.

### Acquire (Lock) Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{spin\_lock:} \\
&\quad \text{try\_again:} \quad \text{LDREX} \quad r1, [r0] \quad &\text{// Load current lock value exclusively} \\
&\quad \text{CMP} \quad r1, \#0 \quad &\text{// Is lock free?} \\
&\quad \text{BNE} \quad \text{try\_again} \quad &\text{// If held, spin and re-read} \\
&\quad \text{STREX} \quad r1, \#1, [r0] \quad &\text{// Try to set lock to 1} \\
&\quad \text{CMP} \quad r1, \#0 \quad &\text{// Did STREX succeed?} \\
&\quad \text{BNE} \quad \text{try\_again} \quad &\text{// Retry if failed (race lost)} \\
&\quad \text{DMB} \quad \text{ISY} \quad &\text{// Ensure subsequent loads observe the lock} \\
&\quad \text{BX} \quad \text{lr} \quad &\text{// Return — lock acquired}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Release (Unlock) Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{spin\_unlock:} \\
&\quad \text{MOV} \quad r1, \#0 \quad &\text{// Prepare release value} \\
&\quad \text{STR} \quad r1, [r0] \quad &\text{// Clear the lock atomically (only owner writes)} \\
&\quad \text{DMB} \quad \text{ISY} \quad &\text{// Ensure prior critical-section writes are visible} \\
&\quad \text{BX} \quad \text{lr} \quad &\text{// Return}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `DMB ISY` after unlock?** The Data Memory Barrier ensures that all writes performed inside the critical section are committed to memory **before** the lock variable is cleared. Without it, another core acquiring the lock might see fresh lock state but stale critical-section data in its own cache.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Atomic Primitive

**Problem:** Implement atomic CAS: if `*ptr == old`, set `*ptr = new`; return success.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{atomic\_cas:} \\
&\quad \text{retry:} \quad \text{LDREX} \quad r2, [r0] \quad &\text{// Load *ptr exclusively into r2} \\
&\quad \text{CMP} \quad r2, r1 \quad &\text{// Compare with expected old value} \\
&\quad \text{BNE} \quad \text{fail} \quad &\text{// Mismatch → return 0 (failure)} \\
&\quad \text{STREX} \quad r3, r2, [r0] \quad &\text{// Try to store (will succeed if no race)} \\
&\quad \text{CMP} \quad r3, \#0 \quad &\text{// Check STREX status} \\
&\quad \text{BNE} \quad \text{retry} \quad &\text{// Retry if exclusivity was lost} \\
&\quad \text{MOV} \quad r0, \#1 \quad &\text{// Return 1 = success} \\
&\quad \text{BX} \quad \text{lr} \quad \\
&\text{fail:} \\
&\quad \text{MOV} \quad r0, \#0 \quad &\text{// Return 0 = failure} \\
&\quad \text{BX} \quad \text{lr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** In practice, the new value (`r2` from caller) is stored via `STREX r3, r_new, [r0]`. The simplified version above shows the comparison logic. The `retry` loop handles contention where another core modifies `*ptr` between LDREX and STREX.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Primitive

| Scenario | Recommended Primitive | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Counter increment | `LDREX` + `ADD` + `STREX` loop | Simple, retry handles contention |
| Mutex/Spinlock | `LDREX` + `CMP` + `STREX` + `DMB` | Needs ordering for correctness |
| Producer-Consumer queue flag | `LDREX` + `STREX` | Single-word atomic flag |
| Linking list insertion | `LDREXD` + `STREXD` | 64-bit pointer + tag pair |
| Order writes across cores | `DMB ISH` | Memory ordering, not atomicity |
| Order writes within core | `DMB ISY` | Full system barrier |
| Legacy code portability (ARMv5) | `SWP` | Old style — discouraged in ARMv7+ |

## 3.5 Failure-Case Analysis of STREX

| Condition | STREX Outcome | `Rd` Value | Software Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| No intervening write | Success | **0** | Proceed past critical section |
| Another core wrote | Failure | **1** | Retry full LDREX-modify-STREX cycle |
| Same core wrote (self-interference) | Failure | **1** | Retry required |
| External DMA wrote | Failure | **1** | Retry required |
| Address misaligned | UNPREDICTABLE | — | Must be naturally aligned |
| Wrong address space | UNPREDICTABLE | — | Must be in normal memory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exclusive Access Monitor Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU0["ARM Core 0"]
    CPU1["ARM Core 1"]
    CPU2["ARM Core 2"]
    CPU3["ARM Core 3"]
    L0["Local Excl Monitor 0"]
    L1["Local Excl Monitor 1"]
    L2["Local Excl Monitor 2"]
    L3["Local Excl Monitor 3"]
    GM["Global Excl Monitor in Bus Interconnect"]
    MEM["Main Memory Lock Variable"]
    CPU0 --> L0
    CPU1 --> L1
    CPU2 --> L2
    CPU3 --> L3
    L0 --> GM
    L1 --> GM
    L2 --> GM
    L3 --> GM
    GM --> MEM
    GM -. "Invalidate tags on remote write" .-> L0
    GM -. "Invalidate tags on remote write" .-> L1
    GM -. "Invalidate tags on remote write" .-> L2
    GM -. "Invalidate tags on remote write" .-> L3
```

## 4.2 LDREX-STREX Handshake Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CoreA as Core 0
    participant Mon as Local Excl Monitor
    participant GM as Global Monitor
    participant Mem as Lock Variable in Memory
    participant CoreB as Core 1
    CoreA->>Mon: LDREX r1, lock_addr
    Mon->>Mon: Tag lock_addr as exclusive
    Mon-->>CoreA: r1 = current value (0=free)
    CoreA->>CoreA: Modify r1 (e.g., set to 1)
    CoreA->>Mon: STREX r2, r1, lock_addr
    Mon->>GM: Is lock_addr still exclusive?
    GM-->>Mon: YES (no remote write occurred)
    Mon->>Mem: Write r1 to lock_addr
    Mon-->>CoreA: r2 = 0 (success)
    Note over CoreA: Lock acquired
    CoreB->>Mon: LDREX r1, lock_addr
    Mon->>Mon: Tag lock_addr as exclusive for Core 1
    Mon-->>CoreB: r1 = 1 (held by Core 0)
    CoreB->>Mon: STREX r2, r1, lock_addr
    Mon->>GM: Is lock_addr still exclusive?
    GM-->>Mon: YES
    Mon-->>CoreB: r2 = 0 (store succeeds)
    Note over CoreB: CoreB mistakenly overwrote
    CoreA->>Mon: LDREX (re-read after release)
    Mon-->>CoreA: r1 = 1 (overwritten by CoreB)
```

## 4.3 Spinlock State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Free
    Free --> Contended: Multiple cores call spin_lock
    Contended --> LDREX_Load: First attempt
    LDREX_Load --> CheckZero: Load value to r1
    CheckZero --> Spin: r1 != 0 (lock held)
    CheckZero --> STREX_Write: r1 == 0 (lock free)
    Spin --> LDREX_Load: Re-attempt after delay
    STREX_Write --> CheckStatus: Write attempt result
    CheckStatus --> Spin: r2 != 0 (failed, retry)
    CheckStatus --> Held: r2 == 0 (success, DMB)
    Held --> CriticalSection: Execute protected code
    CriticalSection --> Release: Call spin_unlock
    Release --> Free: STR 0 to lock + DMB
```

## 4.4 Memory Barrier Functionality (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Core Pipeline Write Buffer"]
    B["DMB Data Memory Barrier"]
    C["L1 Cache"]
    D["L2 Cache"]
    E["Main Memory DRAM"]
    F["Other Cores See Data"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600
    style F fill:#93c47d,stroke:#274e13
```

```mermaid

    L2A["Hardware Primitives"]

    L3C3["ISB"]
    L1 --> L2A
    L1 --> L2B
    L1 --> L2C
    L2A --> L3A1
    L2A --> L3A2
    L2A --> L3A3

```
