# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Double Buffer in ARM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[ARM Cortex-M3 CPU]
    SRAM[SRAM - 64 KB]
    DMA[GPDMA Controller]
    ADC[12-bit ADC]
    LCD[LCD Controller]
    BufA["Buffer A - 256 samples - 0x2007C000"]
    BufB["Buffer B - 256 samples - 0x2007C200"]
    IntA[Half Transfer + TC Interrupt]
    
    ADC -- Channel 0 --> DMA
    DMA -- AHB Bus --> SRAM
    CPU -- Writes filter output --> SRAM
    SRAM --> BufA
    SRAM --> BufB
    DMA -.-> IntA
    IntA -.-> CPU
    CPU -- Sets new DMA destination --> DMA
    LCD -- Reads continuously --> SRAM
    BufA -. Active during phase 1 .-> DMA
    BufB -. Active during phase 2 .-> DMA
```

**Block Description:**
- The **ADC** captures analog signals and feeds the **GPDMA** (General-Purpose DMA) on the LPC1768.
- The DMA writes samples alternately into **Buffer A** and **Buffer B** in **SRAM**.
- The **CPU** runs the FIR filter, processing the *back buffer* while DMA fills the *active buffer*.
- On **Transfer Complete (TC) interrupt**, the CPU atomically swaps `pActive` and `pBack` pointers.
- The **LCD controller** (if present, e.g., LPC2478) reads the active frame buffer to drive the display.

---

## 4.2 State Machine — Ping-Pong Buffer Switching

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Phase1: Configure DMA with BufA active
    Phase1 --> Phase2: TC interrupt on BufA
    Phase2 --> Phase1: TC interrupt on BufB
    Phase1 --> Stop: User stop request
    Phase2 --> Stop: User stop request
    Stop --> [*]: Disable DMA, free buffers
    
    note right of Phase1
        DMA filling BufA
        CPU processing BufB
        Active = BufA
    end note
    
    note right of Phase2
        DMA filling BufB
        CPU processing BufA
        Active = BufB
    end note
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The system starts in `Init`, where DMA is set up with `BufA` as the first destination.
- In `Phase1`, DMA writes to BufA; CPU processes BufB. The "active" output (e.g., to LCD) reads from BufA.
- On TC interrupt, state transitions to `Phase2` with a **pointer swap**.
- The two phases alternate indefinitely, achieving continuous, non-blocking operation.
- `Stop` is reached only on an explicit user request (e.g., closing the application, entering low-power mode).

---

## 4.3 Timing Diagram — Buffer Swap Semantics

```mermaid
gantt
    title Double Buffer Timing (Ping-Pong Operation)
    dateFormat ss.SSS
    axisFormat %S.%L
    
    section DMA Transfers
    DMA Fills BufA (active)  :a1, 00.000, 2500ms
    DMA Fills BufB (active)  :a2, after a1, 2500ms
    DMA Fills BufA (active)  :a3, after a2, 2500ms
    
    section CPU Processing
    CPU Processes BufB (back) :b1, 00.000, 2500ms
    CPU Processes BufA (back) :b2, after b1, 2500ms
    CPU Processes BufB (back) :b3, after b2, 2500ms
    
    section Interrupts
    TC BufA interrupt  :crit, 25.000, 1ms
    TC BufB interrupt  :crit2, 50.000, 1ms
    TC BufA interrupt  :crit3, 75.000, 1ms
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each row shows the timeline of activity.
- The **DMA row** and **CPU row** are perfectly interleaved — no idle time.
- The **red critical flags** mark the **TC interrupt** moments when pointer swapping occurs.
- Total cycle time = 2.5 s (BufA fill) + 2.5 s (BufB fill) = 5 s per full ping-pong cycle, with 0 ms idle.

---

## 4.4 Data Flow — LCD Frame Buffer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App[Application Layer - Draws widgets, text]
    GUI[GUI Library - emWin or LVGL]
    Layer0["Frame Buffer 0 - 150 KB - Base A"]
    Layer1["Frame Buffer 1 - 150 KB - Base B"]
    LCDC[LCD Controller - LPC2478]
    Panel[320x240 LCD Panel]
    Swap[Pointer Swap Logic]
    
    App --> GUI
    GUI -- Writes pixels to BACK buffer --> Layer0
    GUI -- Writes pixels to BACK buffer --> Layer1
    Layer0 --> Swap
    Layer1 --> Swap
    Swap -- ACTIVE buffer address --> LCDC
    LCDC -- Pixel data via RGB bus --> Panel
    
    Swap -. On VSYNC .- GUI
```

**Architecture Insight:**
- The **GUI library** (e.g., emWin, LVGL) always writes to the **back (inactive) buffer**.
- The **LCD controller** continuously reads the **active buffer** to refresh the panel.
- On the **VSYNC** (vertical sync) signal, the GUI library invokes the `Swap()` function, which atomically toggles the "active" pointer between Frame Buffer 0 and Frame Buffer 1.
- Result: **Zero tearing** because the LCD is always reading a fully-rendered frame, while the GUI renders the *next* frame in parallel.

---

## 4.5 DMA Descriptor Chain (LPC1768 GPDMA) — Schematic View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DescA["Descriptor 0 - Source: ADC, Dest: BufA, Control: 256x16bit, Next: DescB"]
    DescB["Descriptor 1 - Source: ADC, Dest: BufB, Control: 256x16bit, Next: DescA"]
    HW[DMA Channel Hardware - AHB Master]
    
    DescA -- Linked List Pointer --> DescB
    DescB -- Linked List Pointer --> DescA
    DescA --> HW
    DescB --> HW
    HW -- AHB Write --> SRAM
```

**Descriptor Explanation (LPC1768 GPDMA in LLI mode):**
- Each descriptor contains: `SrcAddr`, `DstAddr`, `LLI` (linked-list item), `Control` (transfer size, width, burst).
- The DMA controller **automatically** fetches the next descriptor when the current transfer completes — no CPU intervention needed for the *transfer itself*.
- The CPU's role is **only** to set up the descriptors once and to handle the **interrupt** for any application-level data processing.
- This is the *most efficient* form of double buffering in ARM Cortex-M3 systems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
> *Define double buffering in the context of ARM-based embedded systems. List any two advantages and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> Double buffering is a data transfer technique in which two memory buffers of equal size are used in an alternating (ping-pong) manner — one buffer is used by the peripheral (or DMA) for the current data transfer, while the other is used by the CPU to prepare the next set of data.
>
> **Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. Enables **parallel operation** of the CPU and the peripheral, eliminating idle time and increasing system throughput.
> 2. **Eliminates display tearing** in LCD/GLCD applications by ensuring the controller always reads a complete frame.
>
> **Disadvantage (0.5 marks):**
> 1. Requires **double the memory** of a single-buffer system, which is a critical constraint in RAM-limited microcontrollers (e.g., LPC2148 with 40 KB SRAM).

---

### Question 2: Use-Case Identification

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *Identify three ARM-based peripherals where double buffering is commonly applied. For each, justify why double buffering is essential.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Peripheral | Why Double Buffering is Essential (1 mark total) |
|------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| **LCD/GLCD Controller** (LPC2478, STM32F429) | Prevents *tearing artifacts* during frame updates; allows the GUI to render the next frame in parallel with the LCD reading the current frame. (0.5) |
| **ADC with DMA** (LPC1768 GPDMA) | Allows continuous high-speed sampling without CPU intervention, so the CPU can run DSP filters on one buffer while DMA fills the other. (0.5) |
| **High-speed UART/SPI** (LPC2148, STM32) | Prevents **receive overrun errors** in continuous data streams (e.g., GPS modules, sensors) by giving the CPU a full buffer-time to process each batch. (0.5) |
| **Audio Codec I²S** (optional 4th) | Ensures glitch-free audio playback by maintaining two PCM samples in flight. |

> **Note:** Any three correctly justified use-cases earn full 3 marks. Adding a 4th may earn a 0.5 bonus (depending on examiner discretion).

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Level Choice)

> **Module 4 Examination Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice within the module).

---

### Question A — 14 Marks: Full Analytical Treatment

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **double buffering in an LPC1768 GPDMA-based ADC data acquisition system**. Describe the role of **linked-list items (LLI)** in achieving ping-pong transfer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An embedded vision system on **STM32F407 (Cortex-M4, f<sub>CPU</sub> = 168 MHz)** uses a CMOS camera producing data at **30 frames/second, 640×480 resolution, RGB565 format (16 bpp)**. The CPU applies a Sobel edge-detection filter. Compute:
> (i) Single frame buffer size.
> (ii) Double buffer total memory.
> (iii) Required AHB bandwidth.
> (iv) Minimum CPU throughput in MB/s if Sobel filter uses 5 cycles/pixel.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Detailed Answer:**

> **Block Diagram (2 marks):**
> *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram in these notes — a clean labeled block diagram of CPU ↔ SRAM ↔ GPDMA ↔ ADC with Buffer A and Buffer B clearly marked.)*
>
> **Operation Steps (3 marks):**
> 1. **Initialization:** Two buffers (`BufA` at `0x2007C000`, `BufB` at `0x2007C200`, each 256 samples × 2 bytes = 512 bytes) are allocated in the LPC1768's local SRAM (64 KB). The ADC is configured for **channel 0, 200 kHz sampling rate, burst mode**.
> 2. **Descriptor Setup:** Two GPDMA descriptors are created in SRAM:
>    - **Descriptor 0**: Source = `&AD0DR`, Destination = `BufA`, Control = 256 transfers of half-word, LLI pointer → Descriptor 1.
>    - **Descriptor 1**: Source = `&AD0DR`, Destination = `BufB`, Control = 256 transfers, LLI pointer → Descriptor 0.
> 3. **Transfer Initiation:** The GPDMA channel 0 is enabled. The DMA controller fetches Descriptor 0, writes 256 ADC samples into `BufA`, then automatically fetches Descriptor 1 (via LLI pointer) and writes the next 256 samples into `BufB`.
> 4. **Interrupt Handling:** A **Transfer Complete (TC) interrupt** is raised on each descriptor completion. The ISR increments a software counter, sets a flag to signal the CPU that the just-completed buffer is ready for FIR filter processing, and starts the *other* buffer's DMA.
> 5. **Pointer Swap:** The CPU's main loop checks the flag. When the flag is set, it invokes a FIR filter on the *completed* buffer while DMA continues to fill the *other* buffer. No data is ever lost.
>
> **Role of LLI in Ping-Pong (2 marks):**
> The **Linked List Item (LLI)** in the GPDMA descriptor contains the **memory address of the next descriptor** to be loaded. This allows the DMA controller to **chain** multiple descriptors without CPU intervention, achieving *true* hardware-level ping-pong. The CPU does not need to reconfigure the DMA after each transfer — it only handles the application-level data processing in the back buffer. This eliminates the *descriptor reload latency* and maximizes throughput.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution:**

> **(i) Single frame buffer size (2 marks):**
> $$
> B_{single} = W \times H \times BPP / 8
> $$
> $$
> B_{single} = 640 \times 480 \times 16 / 8 = 614{,}400 \text{ bytes} = 600 \text{ KB}
> $$

> **(ii) Double buffer total memory (1 mark):**
> $$
> B_{double} = 2 \times 600 \text{ KB} = 1200 \text{ KB} \approx 1.17 \text{ MB}
> $$

> **(iii) Required AHB bandwidth (2 marks):**
> $$
> BW = B_{single} \times f_{frame}
> $$
> $$
> BW = 614{,}400 \times 30 = 18{,}432{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 18.43 \text{ MB/s}
> $$

> **(iv) CPU throughput for Sobel filter (2 marks):**
> Sobel requires 5 cycles/pixel. Per frame:
> $$
> C_{per\_frame} = 640 \times 480 \times 5 = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ cycles}
> $$
> At 30 fps, CPU must execute:
> $$
> C_{throughput} = 1{,}536{,}000 \times 30 = 46{,}080{,}000 \text{ cycles/s}
> $$
> Converting to MB/s of *data processed*:
> $$
> D_{CPU} = 614{,}400 \times 30 = 18.43 \text{ MB/s}
> $$
> This is well within the Cortex-M4's processing capability (168 MHz / 5 cycles = 33.6 million pixels/s, more than the 9.2 million pixels/s required).

> **Verification / Conclusion (0 mark — implicit):**
> The STM32F407 with 192 KB SRAM cannot host the full 1200 KB double frame buffer in internal SRAM. **External SDRAM** (e.g., IS42S16400) is mandatory for this use case.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks: Alternative Choice (Application-Focused)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper, Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **tearing problem** in single-buffered LCD displays. How does double buffering completely eliminate tearing? Include a timing diagram in your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **4.3" TFT LCD** (480×272 pixels, 16 bpp) is interfaced to an **LPC2478 ARM7 microcontroller** running at **72 MHz**. The LCD controller uses an external **32 MB SDRAM** for the frame buffer. Calculate:
> (i) Single frame buffer size in KB.
> (ii) The SDRAM bandwidth required to drive the LCD at 60 Hz refresh.
> (iii) The percentage of CPU bandwidth consumed by a simple text-rendering function that writes 200 characters (each 16×24 pixels) per frame.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Detailed Answer:**

> **The Tearing Problem (3 marks):**
> In a *single-buffered* LCD system, the CPU and the LCD controller **share the same frame buffer memory**. While the CPU is in the middle of updating the buffer (e.g., drawing a new widget), the LCD controller may simultaneously read the same buffer to refresh the display.
>
> The result: the LCD shows a **mixture of the old and new frame** — a visible horizontal "tear" line appears on the screen. This artifact is called **tearing** (or *screen tearing*). It is highly objectionable in video playback, animations, and any rapidly changing graphics.
>
> **Timing Diagram (Self-draw, 2 marks):**
> ```
> t=0    | Old frame fully on LCD
> t=10ms | CPU starts writing new frame to buffer
> t=15ms | LCD controller reads from buffer mid-write (TEAR!)
> t=20ms | CPU finishes; LCD shows "new" frame (but it was torn)
> ```
>
> **How Double Buffering Eliminates Tearing (2 marks):**
> With double buffering:
> - The **LCD controller reads from Frame Buffer A** (active).
> - The **CPU writes the new frame to Frame Buffer B** (back).
> - The two are **completely decoupled** — the LCD never sees a partially-written frame.
> - On **VSYNC** (vertical sync) signal, the pointers are swapped atomically. The LCD now reads from B (which contains the complete new frame), and the CPU starts writing the *next* frame to A.
> - Result: **The display is always coherent — no tearing ever visible.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution:**

> **(i) Single frame buffer size (2 marks):**
> $$
> B_{single} = 480 \times 272 \times 16 / 8 = 261{,}120 \text{ bytes} \approx 255 \text{ KB}
> $$

> **(ii) SDRAM bandwidth (2 marks):**
> $$
> BW = 261{,}120 \times 60 = 15{,}667{,}200 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 15.67 \text{ MB/s}
> $$

> **(iii) CPU bandwidth for text rendering (3 marks):**
> Each character = $16 \times 24 = 384$ pixels.
> 200 characters/frame = $200 \times 384 = 76{,}800$ pixels/frame.
> Per second (at 60 fps): $76{,}800 \times 60 = 4{,}608{,}000$ pixels/s.
> At 1 cycle/pixel (best case), CPU cycles needed:
> $$
> C_{text} = 4{,}608{,}000 \text{ cycles/s}
> $$
> CPU bandwidth percentage:
> $$
> \%U = \dfrac{4{,}608{,}000}{72 \times 10^{6}} \times 100\% = 6.4\%
> $$
> So the text renderer uses only **6.4%** of the CPU, leaving 93.6% for other tasks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Double Buffering Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for double buffering when computing total memory. Many students write $B = W \times H \times BPP$ and forget to multiply by 2. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Unit errors:** Writing buffer size in bits instead of bytes, or throughput in Hz instead of MB/s. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing "throughput" with "bandwidth"** — throughput is the *useful data rate*; bandwidth is the *bus capacity required*. They are not the same in systems with overhead.
> 4. **Drawing the timing diagram without VSYNC** — KTU examiners specifically look for the VSYNC-based pointer swap in the diagram. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Not mentioning the interrupt mechanism** (Half-Transfer + Transfer-Complete) in DMA double buffering. This is a **mandatory** part of the explanation. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Forgetting the trade-off:** Some students present double buffering as "all advantage" without mentioning the **2× memory cost** or **increased latency** for small transfers. KTU's outcome-based evaluation penalizes one-sided answers. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 7. **Confusing "double buffering" with "circular buffer"** in UART contexts. While related, KTU 2024 syllabus treats them as distinct. Circular buffer = a *single* large buffer with head/tail pointers (FIFO). Double buffering = *two separate* equal buffers with ping-pong semantics. **Loss: 1–2 marks if conflated.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Double Buffering in ARM Systems):**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** Two equal-sized buffers (A and B) used in alternating (ping-pong) fashion to decouple producer (CPU) from consumer (peripheral/DMA).
> - ✅ **Primary benefit:** Enables **parallel operation**, eliminating CPU idle time and **preventing display tearing**.
> - ✅ **Primary cost:** **Doubles memory consumption** (e.g., 150 KB → 300 KB for a QVGA RGB565 frame).
> - ✅ **Implementation contexts:** (1) DMA with LLI (LPC1768 GPDMA), (2) LCD controller with dual frame buffers (LPC2478, STM32F4x9), (3) Software-managed for UART/SPI.
> - ✅ **Key formula:** $M_{total} = 2 \times W \times H \times BPP / 8$ for displays; $B_{min} = T_{process} \times f_{sample}$ for ADC.
> - ✅ **Pointer swap is atomic** (single CPU instruction or interrupt-protected) to avoid race conditions.
> - ✅ **Interrupts used:** Half-Transfer (HT) and Transfer-Complete (TC) on DMA; VSYNC on LCD.
> - ✅ **Trade-off vs. single buffer:** Higher throughput, no tearing, more RAM, slightly higher latency.
> - ✅ **Real-time viability:** Yes, especially with RTOS (FreeRTOS) using double-buffered queues or DMA + ISR.
> - ✅ **KTU high-yield topics:** Memory calculation (Part B favourite), LLI descriptor structure (Part A common), tearing diagram (Part A visual question), CPU bandwidth % calculations (Part B numerical).
> - ✅ **Common exam verbs:** "Explain with block diagram," "Compute total memory for double buffering," "Compare single vs. double buffering," "Describe the role of LLI in GPDMA ping-pong mode."
> - ✅ **Memory alignment:** Buffers must be **32-byte aligned** for optimal DMA burst performance on Cortex-M3/M4.
> - ✅ **Pitfalls:** Forgetting ×2 factor, mixing up circular/double buffer, omitting VSYNC timing, not mentioning memory trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polled Waiting Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Polled Waiting Loops # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polled Waiting Loop (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > A *Polled Waiting Loop* is a software synchronization mechanism in which the processor **continuously and repeatedly reads a status register or condition flag** of a peripheral device (or a software loop counter) until a predefined "ready" or "complete" condition becomes true. The CPU remains in a tight, active loop — consuming instruction cycles — and only exits the loop once the polled condition is satisfied. In ARM terminology, this is also called a **Busy-Wait Loop** or **Polling Loop**, and it is built using conditional branch instructions such as `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BCC`, `BCS`, and unconditional branches like `B` (Branch) combined with a register decrement (`SUBS`) to form a counting loop. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Knocking on a Door" Intuition Imagine you arrive at a friend's house, but they are not answering the doorbell. You have two options: | Strategy | Real-World Analogy | CPU Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **Polled Wait** | You stand at the door and keep ringing the doorbell every 5 seconds, waiting for someone to open. | CPU keeps reading a status register repeatedly until flag = 1 | | **Interrupt** | You go sit on the couch inside your own house, and your friend calls you when they are home. | CPU executes other tasks; ISR fires when peripheral signals ready | In a **Polled Waiting Loop**, the ARM core is *stuck at the door* — it cannot do anything else (like play a game on your phone) until the condition becomes true. This is why polling is called **CPU-bound** or **blocking**. ## 1.3 Physical & Logical Parameters of Interest > [!NOTE] > **Key Quantitative Metrics for a Polled Wait Loop** > - **Loop Iterations (N):** Number of times the loop body executes. > - **Cycles per Iteration (CPI):** Number of ARM clock cycles consumed per loop pass. > - **Total Delay:** $T_{delay} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$, where $T_{clk}$ is the **ARM clock period** (typically 1/(Clock Frequency)). > - **Polled Flag Bit:** A single bit in a peripheral status register (e.g., `UART_FR.TXFE` = Transmit FIFO Empty). > - **CPU Utilization Wasted:** **100%** during the wait (CPU does no useful work). ## 1.4 Why Polled Waiting Loops Are Taught First Even though modern embedded systems favor interrupts, KTU's 2024 scheme places polled loops in **Module 4** because they: 1. **Expose the student directly to hardware status registers** — foundational for any future interrupt-based driver. 2. **Require mastery of conditional branches** (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BCC`) — a core ARM assembly skill. 3. **Are deterministic** — exact cycle counts can be calculated, which is critical in real-time systems before RTOS usage. 4. **Demonstrate the trade-off** between CPU usage and latency, motivating the leap to interrupts in later modules. --- # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Polled Waiting Loop Every polled waiting loop in ARM assembly has **four logical phases**: 1. **Initialization Phase** - Load a counter value into a register (e.g., `MOV R0, #N` or `LDR R0, =N`). - Load the base address of the peripheral's status register (e.g., `LDR R1, =UART_BASE`). - Define the *mask* for the bit you want to poll (e.g., `MOV R2, #0x20` for TXFF — Transmit FIFO Full flag). 2. **Test Phase (Polling Point)** - Read the status register: `LDR R3, [R1, #OFFSET]`. - Apply bit masking: `ANDS R4, R3, R2` (logical AND that sets flags). - Inspect the condition flags (`Z`, `C`, `N`) set by the `AND` operation. 3. **Branch Phase (Wait)** - If condition **NOT met** → loop back to Test Phase. - If condition **met** → fall through to exit. - This is implemented with `BNE poll_loop` or `BEQ poll_loop` depending on the logic. 4. **Exit Phase** - Program continues with the next operation (e.g., write data to UART data register). ## 2.2 Two Principal Categories of Polled Waiting Loops > [!IMPORTANT] > **Category A — Hardware Status Polling** > Used to synchronize with a peripheral (UART, ADC, GPIO, Timer). > Example: Waiting for `UART_FR.TXFF = 0` (Transmit FIFO is NOT full) before writing the next byte. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Category B — Software Time-Delay (Counting) Polling** > Used to generate an accurate time delay. > Example: Waiting for a software counter to expire, used for debouncing switches, LED blink delays, etc. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Total Delay (Software Loop) | $T_{delay} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | $T_{clk}$ = 1/$f_{clk}$ in seconds | | Cycles per Iteration (CPI) | Sum of all cycle counts of instructions in the loop | ARM7: 1 cycle/instr (ideal), Cortex-M3: 1 cycle (Thumb-2) | | Approximate Iterations Needed | $N = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{CPI \times T_{clk}}$ | Round UP to integer | | CPU Wasted During Poll | **100%** | CPU cannot do any other work | | Polled Flag Read Time | $T_{latency,max} = T_{poll\_period}$ | Time between two successive reads | | Polling Frequency | $f_{poll} = \dfrac{1}{T_{poll\_period}}$ | Hz — should exceed event rate (Nyquist-like) | > [!NOTE] > **Threshold Rule (Engineering Rule of Thumb):** If the expected wait time exceeds **1 millisecond**, KTU examiners and industry practice strongly recommend switching from polling to **interrupts** or **DMA** to preserve CPU bandwidth. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Polling Application | |---|---| | Embedded Sensors | Reading ADC `EOC` (End-Of-Conversion) flag before sampling value | | Serial Communication | Checking `UART_FR.RXFE` (Receive FIFO Empty) before reading a byte | | Motor Control | Polling GPIO pin connected to encoder pulse train | | Bootloaders | Polling a "transfer complete" flag while writing to flash memory | | Time Delays | Generating ms/µs delays in bare-metal ARM7 / Cortex-M projects | ## 2.5 Polling vs. Interrupt — KTU Comparison Table | Attribute | Polled Waiting Loop | Interrupt-Driven | |---|---|---| | CPU Utilization During Wait | **100% (wasted)** | **~0% (free)** | | Response Latency | Bounded by `T_poll_period` | Bounded by interrupt entry time | | Code Complexity | **Low** (simple) | High (vector table, ISR, context save/restore) | | Determinism | **Excellent** (exact cycle count) | Variable (depends on interrupt nesting) | | Power Efficiency | Poor (CPU always running) | Excellent (CPU can sleep in WFI/WFE) | | KTU Module Where First Introduced | **Module 4 (this topic)** | Module 5 (typically) | --- # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Delay of a Software Polled Wait Loop **Given:** - ARM clock frequency $f_{clk} = 50\,\text{MHz}$. - We want a delay of $T_{desired} = 10\,\text{ms}$. - The loop body has $CPI = 4$ cycles per iteration. - ARM7TDMI pipeline ideal (no stalls, no flash wait states). **Find:** The required loop counter value $N$. ### Step 1 — Express the clock period $$ T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ns} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the delay formula $$ T_{delay} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for N (iterations) $$ N = \frac{T_{delay}}{CPI \times T_{clk}} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values $$ N = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}} $$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator $$ 4 \times 20 \times 10^{-9} = 80 \times 10^{-9} = 8 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} $$ ### Step 6 — Final division $$ N = \frac{10^{-2}}{8 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{8} \times 10^{6} = 1.25 \times 10^{5} = 125{,}000 $$ ### Step 7 — Round up to integer (must be whole loop iterations) $$ N = 125{,}000\,\text{iterations} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** To obtain exactly 10 ms of delay on a 50 MHz ARM7 with a 4-cycle loop body, the software counter must iterate **125,000 times**. If the counter is a 32-bit register (as in ARM), this fits trivially. If it were a 16-bit register (Thumb), the loop must be **nested** because the max 16-bit value is 65,535 < 125,000. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Nested Loop Delay for Large N If we need $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ iterations (1 million) on a 16-bit counter, we **nest** two loops: ### Step 1 — Choose outer loop value Let outer counter = $N_{outer} = 1000$. ### Step 2 — Derive inner loop value $$ N_{inner} = \frac{N_{total}}{N_{outer}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1000} = 1000 $$ ### Step 3 — Assembly-level realization - `outer_loop`: load `R0 = 1000`. - `inner_loop`: load `R1 = 1000`, then decrement `R1` until zero, decrement `R0`, branch back to `inner_loop` if `R0` not zero. ### Step 4 — Total iteration verification $$ N_{total} = N_{outer} \times N_{inner} = 1000 \times 1000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \checkmark $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Nested loops are also useful for *parameterizable* delays — by changing only the outer counter, the delay scales without re-deriving the inner count. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Hardware Polling on UART (LPC2148 / Cortex-M style) **Scenario:** Write ARM assembly to transmit the character `'A'` (0x41) on UART0, but **only after polling** that the Transmit Holding Register is empty. ### Conceptual UART Status Register Layout - Base Address: `UART0_BASE = 0xE000C000` - Status Register Offset: `U0LSR = 0x14` - Bit 5 of `U0LSR` = `THRE` (Transmit Holding Register Empty). `1` = ready. ### Step 1 — Define constants - `UART0_BASE = 0xE000C000` - `U0LSR_OFFSET = 0x14` - `THRE_MASK = (1 << 5) = 0x20` ### Step 2 — Load base address and mask ``` LDR R1, =UART0_BASE ; R1 = base address LDR R2, =0x14 ; R2 = offset of LSR MOV R3, #0x20 ; R3 = THRE bit mask ``` ### Step 3 — Polling loop (Test + Branch phases) ``` POLL_TX_READY LDR R4, [R1, R2] ; R4 = U0LSR (read status register) ANDS R5, R4, R3 ; Test bit 5; sets Z flag if bit is 0 BEQ POLL_TX_READY ; If Z=1 (bit NOT set, NOT ready), loop back ``` ### Step 4 — Write the data (Exit phase) ``` MOV R0, #0x41 ; 'A' STR R0, [R1] ; Write to UART0 data register (offset 0x00) ``` ### Step 5 — Cycle-count analysis - Each iteration of `POLL_TX_READY` consumes: 1 (LDR) + 1 (ANDS) + 1 (BEQ taken) = **3 cycles**. - When THRE = 1, BEQ is *not* taken: 1 (LDR) + 1 (ANDS) + 1 (BEQ not taken) = **3 cycles** to exit. - Worst-case latency from "TX done" to "next write": **3 cycles** (≈ 60 ns @ 50 MHz). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Forgetting that `BEQ` is taken when `Z=1` (i.e., when the masked bit is **0**). The condition is *bit is clear = NOT ready = keep polling*. Using `BNE` here would invert the logic and deadlock the program. ## 3.4 Derivation: Polling Period Constraint (Nyquist-like) To avoid missing a transient event, the polling frequency $f_{poll}$ must satisfy: $$ f_{poll} \geq 2 \times f_{event} $$ For example, if a sensor asserts a "data ready" pulse for $1\,\mu\text{s}$, the CPU must poll at least every $0.5\,\mu\text{s}$, i.e.: $$ f_{poll} \geq 2\,\text{MHz} $$ This is a fundamental limitation of polling and is one of the key motivations for using interrupts in the next module. --- # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow of a Polled Waiting Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Program Start]) --> initA[Initialization:
Load counter N
Load peripheral base address
Load status mask] initA --> testA{Test Phase:
Read status register
AND with mask
Check Z flag} testA --> branchA[Branch Phase:
Condition MET?] branchA -->|No - keep waiting| testA branchA -->|Yes - exit loop| exitA[Exit Phase:
Perform I/O or proceed] exitA --> nextStep([Continue Main Program]) ``` ## 4.2 Hardware Polling vs. Software Delay — Comparative Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HW_POLL[Hardware Polling Loop] h1[Initialize peripheral] --> h2[Read status register] h2 --> h3{Flag SET?} h3 -->|No| h2 h3 -->|Yes| h4[Proceed with I/O] end subgraph SW_DELAY[Software Time-Delay Loop] s1[Load counter N] --> s2[Decrement counter] s2 --> s3{Counter = 0?} s3 -->|No| s2 s3 -->|Yes| s4[Exit delay] end ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Polling vs. Interrupt ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{Expected wait time?} -->|Less than 1 ms| q2{CPU has other tasks?} q1 -->|More than 1 ms| intA[Use Interrupt or DMA] q2 -->|No - CPU idle| pollA[Use Polled Wait Loop] q2 -->|Yes - multitasking| intB[Use Interrupt] pollA --> done1([OK to proceed]) intA --> done1 intB --> done1 ``` ## 4.4 Polled Loop Cycle Timeline (Gantt-like Schematic) ```mermaid gantt title ARM CPU Activity During a Polled Wait (Time axis left to right) dateFormat X axisFormat %s section CPU Activity Initialize loop (1 cycle) :a1, 0, 1 Poll iteration 1 (3 cycles) :a2, 1, 4 Poll iteration 2 (3 cycles) :a3, 4, 7 Poll iteration 3 (3 cycles) :a4, 7, 10 Condition met - exit loop :a5, 10, 12 section Peripheral Device busy (8 cycles) :crit, 1, 9 Device ready at cycle 9 :milestone, 9, 0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The CPU executes 3 cycles per poll attempt. Between cycles 1 and 9 the peripheral is busy. The CPU detects readiness at iteration 3 (cycle 10) and exits 2 cycles later (cycle 12). The latency from "device ready" to "CPU responds" is bounded by the polling period (3 cycles here). --- # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] **Define a Polled Waiting Loop. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of using polled waiting loops in embedded systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Polled Waiting Loop is a software construct in which the processor **repeatedly reads a status flag or register of a peripheral** (or a software counter) in a tight loop until a predetermined "ready" or "complete" condition is satisfied. - **Advantage (1 mark):** Simple to implement; cycle-accurate and highly **deterministic**; no interrupt vector table or context save/restore required. - **Disadvantage (1 mark):** **Wastes 100% CPU time** during the wait — the processor cannot execute any other task until the condition is met. Power inefficient. - **Definition (1 mark):** As stated in the opening sentence. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] **Write the ARM assembly code snippet to generate a delay of approximately 1 millisecond using a polled waiting loop. Assume a 50 MHz ARM7 clock and that the inner loop consumes 4 cycles per iteration.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Using the formula $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{CPI \times T_{clk}}$: $$ N = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}} = 12{,}500 $$ **Code (2 marks):** ``` MOV R0, #12500 ; Loop counter (1 mark) LOOP SUBS R0, R0, #1 ; Decrement and update flags BNE LOOP ; Branch if not zero (1 mark) ``` When R0 reaches zero, the Z flag is set, `BNE` is not taken, and the loop exits, having produced a 1 ms delay. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model] **(a)** Explain the concept of a Polled Waiting Loop with a neat block diagram. Discuss its two main categories and provide one ARM assembly example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ARM7 system runs at 60 MHz. The polled loop body consists of the following instructions and their cycle counts: | Instruction | Cycles | |---|---| | `LDR R3, [R1, #4]` | 2 | | `ANDS R4, R3, R2` | 1 | | `BEQ poll_label` (taken) | 3 | | `SUBS R0, R0, #1` | 1 | | `BNE loop_body` (taken) | 3 | (i) Calculate the total cycles per inner loop iteration. **(2 marks)** (ii) If you need a 5 ms software delay, how many iterations are required? **(3 marks)** (iii) Can this fit in a 16-bit counter? If not, suggest a solution. **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - **Definition (2 marks):** Polled Waiting Loop = repeated read of a status flag/condition by the CPU in a tight loop. Block diagram should show: Initialize → Read Status → Test Condition → Branch back if not met → Exit if met. *(Award 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for explanation.)* - **Category 1 — Hardware Polling (2 marks):** Waiting for a peripheral flag. Example code: ``` LDR R1, =UART0_BASE LDR R2, =0x20 ; THRE mask POLL LDR R3, [R1, #0x14] ANDS R4, R3, R2 BEQ POLL ``` - **Category 2 — Software Delay (2 marks):** Counting loop. Example: ``` MOV R0, #12500 DELAY SUBS R0, R0, #1 BNE DELAY ``` - **Comparative comment (1 mark):** Hardware polling synchronizes CPU with external device; software delay is purely CPU-bound timing. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Total cycles per iteration (2 marks):** - Sum = 2 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 3 = **10 cycles/iteration**. **(ii) Number of iterations for 5 ms delay (3 marks):** - Clock period: $T_{clk} = 1 / 60\,\text{MHz} = 16.667\,\text{ns}$. - $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{CPI \times T_{clk}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 16.667 \times 10^{-9}}$ - $N = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6667 \times 10^{-7}} = 30{,}000\,\text{iterations}$. **(iii) Fits in 16-bit? (2 marks):** - 16-bit max = 65,535. Since 30,000 < 65,535, **YES it fits in a 16-bit counter**. *(If the calculation had exceeded 65,535, the answer would be NO, with the solution being a nested loop.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model - Alternative Choice] **(a)** Compare Polled Waiting Loops and Interrupt-driven I/O along the dimensions of: CPU utilization, code complexity, response latency, and determinism. Which approach is preferred when the expected wait time is greater than 1 ms? Justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a polled waiting loop in ARM assembly to read the value of an ADC channel. The ADC has a base address of `0xE0034000`, and the status register is at offset `0x30`. Bit 31 of the status register is the "Conversion Done" flag (1 = complete). Write the ARM code to: (i) Poll the status flag until conversion is complete. **(3 marks)** (ii) Read the 10-bit ADC data register (offset `0x34`) into R0. **(2 marks)** (iii) Mask the result to extract only the lower 10 bits. **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks:** | Dimension | Polled Wait Loop | Interrupt-Driven I/O | Mark | |---|---|---|---| | CPU Utilization | **100% (wasted)** | ~0% (free) | 1 | | Code Complexity | Low (3-5 instructions) | High (vector table, ISR, context save) | 1 | | Response Latency | Bounded by polling period | Bounded by interrupt entry (~12 cycles) | 1 | | Determinism | **Excellent** (cycle-accurate) | Variable (depends on nesting, priorities) | 1 | | Preference for > 1 ms wait | **Interrupt preferred** | ✓ | 1 | | Justification (any 1 valid reason) | CPU bandwidth preservation; allows other tasks; lower power | | 2 | **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Polling loop (3 marks):** ``` LDR R1, =0xE0034000 ; ADC base MOV R2, #0x80000000 ; Bit 31 mask (1 << 31) POLL_ADC LDR R3, [R1, #0x30] ; Read status reg ANDS R4, R3, R2 ; Test bit 31 BEQ POLL_ADC ; Loop if bit 31 = 0 ``` **(ii) Read ADC data (2 marks):** ``` LDR R0, [R1, #0x34] ; Load 10-bit ADC value into R0 ``` **(iii) Mask to 10 bits (2 marks):** ``` MOV R2, #0x3FF ; 10-bit mask = 0b1111111111 = 0x3FF AND R0, R0, R2 ; R0 = R0 AND 0x3FF ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Wrong branch polarity:** Using `BNE` when the condition is *not yet met* will cause the program to skip the loop and read stale data. Always reason: "Is the bit set or clear when the device is BUSY?" — and branch back to loop accordingly. > 2. **Forgetting to set the Z flag:** `AND` without `S` suffix does **not** update flags. Always use `ANDS` (with S) when polling. > 3. **Off-by-one in counter:** A `SUBS R0, R0, #1` followed by `BNE` actually iterates **N times**, not N+1, because the exit occurs when R0 reaches exactly zero. Students who load `R0 = N+1` will produce a delay 1 iteration too long. > 4. **Ignoring pipeline effects on real silicon:** On ARM7TDMI, branches cause a 1-cycle refill penalty. In a tight 3-instruction loop, the effective CPI may be **4**, not 3 — doubling actual delay vs. calculated delay. Always add a safety margin of 10–20% in production code. > 5. **Not clearing the polled bit:** Some peripherals require the CPU to write a 1 to clear a "done" flag. Failing to do so causes the *next* polling loop to exit immediately, breaking the synchronization. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polled Waiting Loops — KTU Module 4 Rapid-Revision Bullets** > > ✅ A Polled Waiting Loop is a **CPU-bound busy-wait** mechanism that repeatedly checks a status flag or counter. > > ✅ Two flavors: **(1) Hardware status polling** (sync with peripheral) and **(2) Software counter delay** (generate time delay). > > ✅ The core ARM instructions used are `LDR` (read status register), `ANDS` (test bit and set flags), and `BNE`/`BEQ` (branch back to loop). > > ✅ Always use **`ANDS`** (with the S suffix) — `AND` alone does not set condition flags. > > ✅ Delay formula: $T_{delay} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$, where $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$. > > ✅ For $N > 65{,}535$, use a **nested loop** (since 16-bit counters max out at 65,535). > > ✅ Polled waits waste **100% CPU bandwidth**; for waits > 1 ms, KTU recommends **interrupts** (introduced in the next module). > > ✅ Polling frequency must satisfy $f_{poll} \geq 2 \times f_{event}$ to avoid missing transient events (Nyquist-like bound). > > ✅ Polled loops are **deterministic and cycle-accurate** — a key advantage for real-time bare-metal ARM code. > > ✅ Common status register bits polled in KTU labs: `UART_LSR.THRE` (bit 5), `ADC_GDR.DONE` (bit 31), `SSP_SR.TNF` (bit 1), `TIMER_IR.MR0` (bit 0). > > ✅ Branch polarity: **Reason explicitly** about whether `BEQ` or `BNE` returns to the loop. Drawing the truth table for the condition is a KTU-recommended exam technique. > > ✅ On ARM7TDMI, account for **pipeline refill** (1 extra cycle per taken branch) when computing the true CPI of a tight loop.

topics.title: Interrupt Driven IO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Driven IO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt Driven I/O** is an I/O transfer mechanism in which the CPU executes its main program continuously, and the peripheral device *interrupts* the processor only when it is ready to transfer data, thereby eliminating the need for the CPU to poll (continuously check) the device's status.

In the context of the **ARM architecture (LPC1768/ARM Cortex-M3)**, an **interrupt** is an asynchronous exception (external hardware signal) that causes the processor to suspend the currently executing instruction stream, save its context, and execute a predefined function known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Once the ISR finishes, the processor resumes the main program from the exact point where it was interrupted.

ARM Cortex-M3 specifically supports the **ARMv7-M architecture** and uses a sophisticated interrupt controller called the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**, which is tightly coupled to the processor core.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Worker and the Doorbell**
>
> Imagine you are an office worker writing a report at your desk.
>
> - **Polled I/O**: You are forced to **stop writing every 10 seconds**, get up, walk to the door, and check if someone is there. You waste enormous time and energy (CPU cycles) checking.
> - **Interrupt Driven I/O**: You are happily writing the report. The moment a visitor presses the **doorbell (interrupt signal)**, you hear the bell (interrupt is raised), you quickly note your current page number (push context onto stack), walk to the door, attend to the visitor (execute ISR), and then return exactly to the page you were writing on (pop context, resume execution).
>
> This saves the CPU from being tied up doing nothing but waiting.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **ARM Cortex-M3 Latency**: The minimum interrupt latency for Cortex-M3 is **12 clock cycles**.
> - **Interrupt Stack Size**: Defined in the vector table at address `0x00000000`.
> - **NVIC Supports**: Up to **240 interrupts** and **256 levels of programmable priorities**.

### 1.4 Comparison: Polled I/O vs Interrupt Driven I/O

| Parameter | Polled I/O | Interrupt Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Attention | Continuous (wasted) | Only when needed |
| CPU Efficiency | **Low** | **High** |
| Response Time | Deterministic but wasteful | Near-instant, hardware-triggered |
| Hardware Complexity | Simple | Requires interrupt controller (NVIC) |
| Programming Complexity | Simple `while()` loop | Requires ISR writing, vector setup |
| Power Consumption | High (always running) | Low (CPU can sleep) |
| Real-time Suitability | Poor | **Excellent** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: How Interrupt Driven I/O Works

The complete flow of interrupt processing in an ARM Cortex-M3 system can be broken down into structured steps:

1. **Normal Execution**: The CPU executes instructions from the main program. The Interrupt Enable (I-bit in CPSR / PRIMASK for Cortex-M) is set to allow interrupts.
2. **Peripheral Signal**: An external peripheral (e.g., a UART, GPIO button, or Timer) completes its task (e.g., data received, time elapsed) and asserts an interrupt request line.
3. **Interrupt Request to NVIC**: The peripheral sends an interrupt signal to the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**. The NVIC checks if the interrupt is enabled and if its priority is higher than the currently executing priority level.
4. **Stacking Phase (Hardware)**: The Cortex-M3 automatically pushes eight registers onto the current stack (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR). This is called **stack pushing** and saves the context.
5. **Vector Fetch**: The NVIC fetches the address of the corresponding ISR from the **Vector Table** (located at `0x00000000` for LPC1768).
6. **ISR Execution**: The CPU loads the PC with the ISR address and starts executing the Interrupt Service Routine.
7. **Interrupt Return**: The ISR ends with a special return instruction (e.g., `BX LR` or `POP {PC,...}`). The hardware restores the previously stacked registers (unstacking phase).
8. **Resumed Execution**: The main program resumes from where it was interrupted.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use the NVIC instead of a single IRQ pin?**
> Modern ARM systems handle dozens of interrupts. The NVIC allows the programmer to assign **priorities**, **enable/disable** specific interrupts, and even **nest** them (a higher-priority interrupt can preempt a lower-priority one).

**The Vector Table**:
The vector table is an array of 32-bit words where each entry is the starting address of an exception handler.

- For **LPC1768 (ARM Cortex-M3)**, the first 16 entries are system exceptions (Reset, NMI, HardFault, etc.), and the next entries are device-specific interrupts (e.g., Timer0, UART0, EINT3).

### 2.3 Types of ARM Interrupts

ARM Cortex-M3 categorizes interrupts into two main groups:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **System Exceptions**: Reset (1), NMI (2), HardFault (3), MemManage (4), BusFault (5), UsageFault (6), SVCall (11), DebugMonitor (12), PendSV (14), SysTick (15).
> 2. **Interrupts (IRQs)**: External peripheral interrupts (starting from vector #16).

**Cortex-M3 Exception Types Summary Table:**

| Vector # | Exception | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial SP | — | Stack pointer initial value |
| 1 | Reset | -3 (highest) | Power-on reset |
| 2 | NMI | -2 | Non-Maskable Interrupt |
| 3 | HardFault | -1 | All classes of fault |
| 4–6 | MemManage/BusFault/UsageFault | Configurable | Memory access errors |
| 11 | SVCall | Configurable | Supervisor call (SVC instruction) |
| 14 | PendSV | Configurable | Pendable service for system |
| 15 | SysTick | Configurable | System Tick Timer |
| 16+ | External Interrupts | Configurable | Peripherals (UART, GPIO, Timer) |

### 2.4 Interrupt Latency Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Interrupt Driven I/O**

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interrupt Latency ($t_{lat}$) | $t_{lat} = t_{response} + t_{service}$ | **cycles** | Time from request to ISR start |
| Minimum Latency (Cortex-M3) | **12 cycles** | cycles | Hardware stacking + vector fetch |
| Maximum Latency | $12 + N_{stall}$ | cycles | Depends on pipeline stalls |
| Effective Throughput (Polled) | $R_{poll} = \frac{1}{t_{poll} \cdot f_{CPU}}$ | ops/sec | CPU-bound check rate |
| CPU Efficiency (Interrupt) | $\eta = \frac{t_{useful}}{t_{useful} + t_{ISR}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of useful work |
| Interrupt Sources (Cortex-M3) | **$N_{irq} \le 240$** | count | Maximum IRQ lines |
| Priority Levels | **$L_{prio} = 256$** | levels | 8-bit programmable priority |

**Real-world Engineering Utility**:
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems**: Robotics, automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units) use interrupt-driven I/O for sensor data acquisition.
- **Operating Systems**: RTOS (FreeRTOS) relies entirely on the `PendSV` and `SysTick` interrupts for context switching.
- **Communication Protocols**: UART, SPI, I²C, CAN bus all use interrupts for efficient byte-level data handling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Computation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**:
A system uses an ARM Cortex-M3 processor running at $f_{CPU} = 72 \text{ MHz}$. An interrupt is asserted during a multi-cycle instruction (LDM/STM) that takes 14 clock cycles. Compute the **maximum interrupt latency** in nanoseconds.

---

**Step 1: Identify the hardware stacking cost**

For ARM Cortex-M3, the minimum latency to begin executing the ISR is the sum of the **stack push** (8 registers × 1 cycle = 8 cycles) plus the **vector fetch** (1 cycle) plus the **instruction fetch/decode for ISR entry** (3 cycles).

$$
t_{min} = T_{stack} + T_{fetch} + T_{entry}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values for the minimum path**

$$
t_{min} = 8 + 1 + 3 = 12 \text{ cycles}
$$

This is the well-known **12-cycle baseline latency** of the Cortex-M3.

**Step 3: Account for the multi-cycle instruction stall**

If the processor is currently executing a multi-cycle instruction (like an LDM/STM or DIV), it must complete that instruction before servicing the interrupt. The longest such instruction can take up to 14 cycles in the LPC1768 (e.g., 8-cycle LDM with write-back).

$$
T_{stall} = T_{multi-cycle-instruction} = 14 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the maximum interrupt latency in cycles**

$$
t_{lat,max} = t_{min} + T_{stall}
$$

$$
t_{lat,max} = 12 + 14 = 26 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5: Convert cycles to time using the CPU clock period**

The clock period is the reciprocal of the CPU frequency:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{clk} \approx 13.889 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 6: Compute maximum latency in nanoseconds**

$$
t_{lat,max} (\text{ns}) = t_{lat,max} \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
t_{lat,max} = 26 \times 13.889
$$

$$
t_{lat,max} \approx 361.11 \text{ ns}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Polled vs Interrupt (Worked Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: A temperature sensor provides data every $T_{sensor} = 100 \text{ ms}$. The CPU runs at $f_{CPU} = 100 \text{ MHz}$, and the polled status-read instruction takes 10 cycles per check.

**Step 1: Define polling interval**
The CPU checks every $1 \text{ ms}$ (i.e., 100 checks per data ready event).

**Step 2: Compute cycles wasted per second in polling mode**

$$
N_{wasted} = \frac{1 \text{ sec}}{1 \text{ ms}} \times 10 \text{ cycles/check} = 1000 \text{ checks} \times 10 = 10{,}000 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the CPU time wasted**

$$
t_{wasted} = \frac{N_{wasted}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{10{,}000}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 100 \text{ \mu s per second}
$$

**Step 4: Compute CPU efficiency**

$$
\eta_{poll} = 1 - \frac{t_{wasted}}{1 \text{ sec}} = 1 - \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = 0.9999 \text{ or } 99.99\%
$$

**Step 5: For interrupt-driven mode, the CPU is interrupted only when the sensor data is ready (every 100 ms). If the ISR takes 50 cycles:**

$$
t_{ISR} = 50 \text{ cycles} \times 10 \text{ ns/cycle} = 500 \text{ ns per interrupt}
$$

**Step 6: CPU time used by interrupts per second**

$$
t_{used} = 10 \text{ interrupts/sec} \times 500 \text{ ns} = 5 \text{ \mu s/sec}
$$

**Step 7: Compare efficiency**

$$
\eta_{INT} = 1 - \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \approx 99.9995\%
$$

The interrupt-driven approach is $\frac{100 \text{ \mu s}}{5 \text{ \mu s}} = 20\times$ more CPU-efficient in this scenario.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Context Save Time

For ARM Cortex-M3, the hardware automatically saves 8 registers during exception entry:

$$
\text{Registers Pushed} = \{R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR\}
$$

$$
\text{Stack Memory Used} = 8 \text{ registers} \times 4 \text{ bytes/register} = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

The time to push these 8 registers:

$$
t_{push} = 8 \text{ cycles} \quad \text{(each STR takes 1 cycle on the system bus)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **automatic hardware stacking** is what makes Cortex-M3 extremely fast at interrupt entry — no software-based `PUSH` is required, saving significant overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Interrupt Driven I/O

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MAIN PROGRAM EXECUTION"] --> B{"INTERRUPT SIGNAL FROM PERIPHERAL"}
    B -- "NO SIGNAL" --> A
    B -- "YES - IRQ RAISED" --> C["NVIC PRIORITY AND ENABLE CHECK"]
    C -- "DISABLED OR LOWER PRIORITY" --> A
    C -- "ENABLED AND HIGHER PRIORITY" --> D["HARDWARE STACKING OF 8 REGISTERS"]
    D --> E["VECTOR TABLE FETCH FOR ISR ADDRESS"]
    E --> F["EXECUTE INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE"]
    F --> G{"ISR COMPLETE?"}
    G -- "NO" --> F
    G -- "YES" --> H["HARDWARE UNSTACKING AND PC RESTORE"]
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 ARM Cortex-M3 Vector Table Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VECTOR 0 - INITIAL SP"] --> B["VECTOR 1 - RESET HANDLER"]
    B --> C["VECTOR 2 - NMI HANDLER"]
    C --> D["VECTOR 3 - HARDFAULT"]
    D --> E["VECTOR 4-6 MEM FAULTS"]
    E --> F["VECTOR 11 SVCALL"]
    F --> G["VECTOR 14 PENDSV"]
    G --> H["VECTOR 15 SYSTICK"]
    H --> I["VECTOR 16 UART0 IRQ"]
    I --> J["VECTOR 17 UART1 IRQ"]
    J --> K["VECTOR 18 TIMER0 IRQ"]
    K --> L["VECTOR N - DEVICE SPECIFIC IRQS"]
```

### 4.3 CPU State Transitions During Interrupt Processing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ThreadMode
    ThreadMode --> ThreadMode : "NORMAL EXECUTION"
    ThreadMode --> HandlerMode : "INTERRUPT ASSERTED AND ENABLED"
    HandlerMode --> HandlerMode : "EXECUTING ISR CODE"
    HandlerMode --> ThreadMode : "ISR RETURN INSTRUCTION EXECUTED"
    HandlerMode --> HandlerMode : "NESTED HIGHER PRIORITY INTERRUPT"
```

### 4.4 NVIC Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PERIPHERAL 1 TIMER0"] --> NVIC
    P2["PERIPHERAL 2 UART0"] --> NVIC
    P3["PERIPHERAL 3 GPIO"] --> NVIC
    P4["PERIPHERAL N EXTI"] --> NVIC
    NVIC["NVIC - NESTED VECTORED INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"] --> CHECK{"PRIORITY HIGHER THAN CURRENT?"}
    CHECK -- "YES" --> STACK["AUTO STACK 8 REGISTERS"]
    CHECK -- "NO" --> P1
    STACK --> VECT["FETCH ISR VECTOR FROM VTOR"]
    VECT --> CORE["CPU CORE EXECUTES ISR"]
    CORE --> UNSTK["RESTORE REGISTERS FROM STACK"]
    UNSTK --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Interrupt Driven I/O. How is it different from Polled I/O?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Interrupt Driven I/O is an input/output mechanism in which the CPU executes its main program and is interrupted by the peripheral device only when the device is ready for data transfer. The CPU does not continuously check (poll) the device status.

**Difference from Polled I/O:**
- In Polled I/O, the CPU wastes cycles repeatedly checking a status flag.
- In Interrupt Driven I/O, the device signals the CPU via an interrupt request line, allowing the CPU to perform other useful tasks in between.
- Interrupt driven I/O is more CPU-efficient and provides faster response to asynchronous events.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)? List any two characteristics of an ISR in ARM Cortex-M3.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a special function that is automatically executed by the processor in response to a specific interrupt request from a peripheral.

**Characteristics:**
1. ISRs must be **short and fast** to minimize interrupt latency for other interrupts.
2. ISRs in ARM Cortex-M3 are written in C and registered using the **CMSIS function `NVIC_EnableIRQ(IRQn)`** to enable the specific interrupt.
3. The NVIC automatically handles context saving (stacking) and restoration — the ISR does not need to save registers manually.
4. ISRs are triggered by hardware events and should ideally not contain blocking code or long loops.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks have **Module Internal Choice**. Below are two alternative questions, each carrying 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the interrupt processing sequence in ARM Cortex-M3 with a neat block diagram. List the registers automatically saved by the hardware during interrupt entry.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 72 MHz ARM Cortex-M3 system experiences a worst-case instruction stall of 18 cycles before exception entry. Calculate the maximum interrupt latency in nanoseconds. State the factors that affect interrupt latency.*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The interrupt processing sequence in ARM Cortex-M3 is as follows:

1. **Interrupt Request**: A peripheral asserts an interrupt request line connected to the NVIC.
2. **NVIC Validation**: The NVIC checks whether the interrupt is enabled in the `ISER` (Interrupt Set-Enable Register) and whether its priority is higher than the current base priority in `BASEPRI`.
3. **Instruction Completion**: The CPU completes the currently executing instruction (waits for pipeline drain).
4. **Automatic Hardware Stacking**: The processor automatically pushes **8 registers** onto the active stack (MSP or PSP):
   - Registers: `R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR (R14), ReturnAddress (R15/PC), xPSR`
   - Memory used: $8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}$
5. **Vector Fetch**: The NVIC reads the ISR address from the Vector Table at the offset corresponding to the interrupt number.
6. **PC Update**: The processor loads the fetched address into the PC, switching to **Handler Mode**.
7. **ISR Execution**: The CPU executes the Interrupt Service Routine.
8. **Return**: The ISR ends (typically with `BX LR` or `POP {PC,...}`). The hardware automatically unstacks the saved registers in reverse order.
9. **Resumed Execution**: The CPU continues the main (Thread Mode) program from the exact point of interruption.

**Registers Automatically Saved:**
`R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, R14 (LR), R15 (PC), xPSR` — total 8 registers / 32 bytes.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $f_{CPU} = 72 \text{ MHz}$
- Worst-case instruction stall $T_{stall} = 18 \text{ cycles}$

**Step 1**: Calculate the minimum interrupt entry time for Cortex-M3.

$$
t_{entry,min} = 8 \text{ cycles (stacking)} + 1 \text{ cycle (vector fetch)} + 3 \text{ cycles (entry sequence)} = 12 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute maximum interrupt latency in clock cycles.

$$
t_{lat,max} = t_{entry,min} + T_{stall} = 12 + 18 = 30 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the clock period.

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \approx 13.889 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4**: Convert maximum latency to nanoseconds.

$$
t_{lat,max} = 30 \times 13.889 \approx 416.67 \text{ ns}
$$

**Factors Affecting Interrupt Latency:**
1. **Pipeline state** and currently executing instruction (multi-cycle instructions like LDM/STM/DIV).
2. **Memory wait states** for stack pushes and vector fetches.
3. **Tail-chaining optimization** (Cortex-M3 specific) which can reduce effective latency for back-to-back interrupts.
4. **Priority level** of the interrupt vs the current `BASEPRI`.
5. **Pre-emption by higher-priority interrupts** (nested interrupts).
6. **Flash accelerator prefetch buffer state**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) in ARM Cortex-M3. Mention its key features.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare Polled I/O and Interrupt Driven I/O. A data acquisition system reads sensor data every 50 ms using a CPU running at 50 MHz. The polling routine takes 20 cycles per check, and the system checks every 1 ms. If an interrupt-based approach uses an ISR of 80 cycles, compute the CPU efficiency in both cases.*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)** is an integral part of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor that manages all exceptions and interrupts.

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EXTERNAL PERIPHERAL INTERRUPTS - UP TO 240 LINES"] --> NVIC_CORE["NVIC CORE LOGIC"]
    B["SYSTEM EXCEPTIONS - RESET NMI FAULTS"] --> NVIC_CORE
    NVIC_CORE --> REG["CONTROL REGISTERS - ISER ICER ISPR ICPR IPR"]
    NVIC_CORE --> VEC["VECTOR TABLE - 32 BIT ENTRIES"]
    NVIC_CORE --> CORE["CPU CORE INTERFACE"]
    CORE --> OUT["INTERRUPT TO PROCESSOR PIPELINE"]
```

**Working:**
- When a peripheral asserts an interrupt line, the NVIC samples the signal on each clock cycle.
- The NVIC checks the corresponding **Interrupt Set-Enable Register (ISER)** bit. If `0`, the interrupt is ignored.
- The NVIC compares the interrupt's priority (stored in the **Interrupt Priority Register, IPR**) with the current execution priority.
- If accepted, the NVIC signals the CPU, which begins the stacking process and fetches the vector from the Vector Table.
- The NVIC also supports **tail-chaining** (back-to-back interrupts without full unstacking) and **late arrival** (a higher-priority interrupt arriving during stacking preempts the lower one).

**Key Features of NVIC:**
1. Supports up to **240 external interrupts** and **16 system exceptions**.
2. **256 programmable priority levels** (8 bits of priority).
3. **Automatic hardware stacking** of 8 registers on exception entry.
4. **Tail-chaining** for low-latency back-to-back interrupts.
5. **Pre-emption** of lower-priority interrupts.
6. **Vector table relocation** via the `VTOR` register.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $f_{CPU} = 50 \text{ MHz}$
- $T_{sensor} = 50 \text{ ms}$
- Polling check period = $1 \text{ ms}$
- Polling cost = $20 \text{ cycles/check}$
- ISR cost = $80 \text{ cycles/event}$

**Step 1**: Compute CPU cycles wasted per second in polling.

$$
N_{polls} = \frac{1 \text{ sec}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 1000 \text{ polls/sec}
$$

$$
\text{Total cycles wasted} = 1000 \times 20 = 20{,}000 \text{ cycles/sec}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute wasted time.

$$
t_{wasted} = \frac{20{,}000}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 400 \text{ \mu s/sec}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute CPU efficiency in polling mode.

$$
\eta_{poll} = 1 - \frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = 0.9996 \text{ or } 99.96\%
$$

**Step 4**: Compute cycles used by ISR per second in interrupt mode.

$$
N_{interrupts} = \frac{1 \text{ sec}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 20 \text{ interrupts/sec}
$$

$$
\text{Total cycles used} = 20 \times 80 = 1600 \text{ cycles/sec}
$$

**Step 5**: Compute ISR time consumed.

$$
t_{ISR} = \frac{1600}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 32 \text{ \mu s/sec}
$$

**Step 6**: Compute CPU efficiency in interrupt mode.

$$
\eta_{INT} = 1 - \frac{32 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \approx 0.999968 \text{ or } 99.9968\%
$$

**Step 7: Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Polled I/O | Interrupt Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| CPU cycles/sec used | 20,000 | 1,600 |
| CPU time consumed | 400 μs | 32 μs |
| Efficiency | 99.96% | 99.9968% |
| Improvement factor | 1× | **12.5× more efficient** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `IRQ` and `FIQ`**: In the older ARM7/ARM9, there were exactly two external interrupt lines (IRQ and FIQ). In **Cortex-M3**, there is **no FIQ** — only IRQ lines. Don't write FIQ for Cortex-M3 answers.
> 2. **Forgetting automatic stacking**: Many students manually `PUSH` registers inside an ISR, which is **redundant** in Cortex-M3. The hardware already saves 8 registers automatically.
> 3. **Wrong vector table address**: The vector table for LPC1768/Cortex-M3 is at `0x00000000` (relocatable via `VTOR`).
> 4. **Forgetting units in latency calculation**: Always convert cycles to nanoseconds using $T = 1/f_{CPU}$.
> 5. **Not mentioning tail-chaining**: This is a key Cortex-M3 optimization worth 1-2 extra marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interrupt Driven I/O**

- ✅ **Interrupt Driven I/O** allows the CPU to execute other code and react only when a peripheral needs attention.
- ✅ **Polled I/O** wastes CPU cycles by continuously checking the device status register.
- ✅ ARM Cortex-M3 uses the **NVIC** to manage up to **240 external interrupts** with **256 priority levels**.
- ✅ The **Vector Table** (at `0x00000000`) stores the addresses of all exception and interrupt handlers.
- ✅ On exception entry, the hardware **automatically stacks 8 registers**: `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` (32 bytes).
- ✅ **Minimum interrupt latency** for Cortex-M3 = **12 clock cycles** (stack + vector + entry).
- ✅ **Maximum interrupt latency** = `12 + longest multi-cycle instruction` cycles.
- ✅ `NVIC_EnableIRQ(IRQn)` enables a specific interrupt in the CMSIS library.
- ✅ `PRIMASK` register disables **all maskable interrupts**; `BASEPRI` disables interrupts below a threshold.
- ✅ **Tail-chaining** allows back-to-back interrupts to be serviced with as little as **6 cycles** between them.
- ✅ **ISRs must be short** — defer non-critical work using flags or RTOS semaphores.
- ✅ The formula $t_{lat,max} = t_{entry,min} + T_{stall}$ governs worst-case interrupt response.
- ✅ A peripheral's interrupt enable bit, NVIC enable bit, and global enable (PRIMASK) **must all be set** for an interrupt to be serviced.
- ✅ The **Cortex-M3 has no FIQ pin** — all external interrupts are IRQs (unlike ARM7/ARM9).
- ✅ Context saving is **hardware-automatic** in Cortex-M3 — no software `PUSH` is required in the ISR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a high-performance data-transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware subsystem — the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** — autonomously moves blocks of data between **I/O peripherals and main memory**, or between **two memory regions**, **without continuous CPU intervention**. The CPU initiates the transfer by programming the DMAC's control registers (source address, destination address, transfer count, and transfer mode) and is interrupted only upon transfer completion, freeing the processor to execute other tasks in parallel.

In the ARM ecosystem (Cortex-M / Cortex-A / PrimeCell peripherals), DMA is realized as an **AHB/AMBA-bus master** that arbitrates for the system bus against the CPU, performs **fly-by or fetch-decode-store** transfers, and supports chained, circular, and scatter-gather descriptor models.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model 🍽️

Imagine a busy restaurant:

- **The CPU (Chef)** is busy cooking complex dishes (executing instructions, performing computations).
- **The Waiter (DMA Controller)** can independently carry pre-collected orders (data blocks) from the **Pantry (Memory)** straight to the **Customer's Table (Peripheral)**, or vice-versa.
- Without a waiter, the chef would have to personally leave the kitchen, walk to the pantry, fetch ingredients, and serve — wasting enormous **CPU cycles** for trivial transport.
- The chef only needs to assign the waiter *once* ("Take 64 plates of rice from the pantry to Table 7") and is **interrupted only when the job is done**.

This is precisely the DMA philosophy: **Decouple data movement from data processing.**

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Boundary Values

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus Width** | 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 bits | Width of a single DMA beat |
| **Transfer Modes** | Burst, Cycle-Steal, Peripheral-to-Memory, Memory-to-Memory | Defined by AMBA specification |
| **AHB Bus Clock** | Up to **200 MHz** (Cortex-M7 class) | Drives DMAC throughput |
| **Bus Arbitration Priority** | 0 – 7 (programmable) | Higher = more priority |
| **Interrupt Latency Saving** | Up to **~95%** of CPU cycles | For bulk transfers |
| **Channel Count (ARM PrimeCell PL330)** | **8 channels** | Each independent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A DMA transfer essentially replaces the software loop `for(i=0;i<N;i++) buffer[i] = peripheral_reg;` with a **single hardware transaction**, where $N$ can range from a few bytes to several megabytes.

---

## 1.4 Where DMA Fits in the ARM System-on-Chip (SoC)

A modern ARM SoC contains the following bus masters contending for the **AHB/APB matrix**:

1. **CPU Core** (Cortex-M/A/R)
2. **DMA Controller** (PrimeCell PL080, PL081, PL230, PL330)
3. **Ethernet MAC** (high-bandwidth)
4. **USB Host / Device Controller**
5. **LCD Controller**
6. **Crypto-Engine / Bus Masters**

Without DMA, the CPU must service every peripheral data request via interrupts — causing **interrupt storms**, **context-switch overhead**, and **reduced real-time determinism**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of an ARM-Compatible DMAC

The DMAC (e.g., **ARM PrimeCell PL330 — "DMA Controller"**) is built from the following blocks:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **APB Slave Interface** | CPU programs control registers via APB |
| **AHB Master Interface(s)** | DMAC becomes bus master to read/write memory |
| **Instruction Engine** | Executes microcode ("DMAC instructions") stored in a FIFO |
| **Channel Logic (0–7)** | Independent transfer channels with context registers |
| **FIFO Buffer** | Decouples source/side bandwidth mismatches |
| **Interrupt Generation** | Asserts IRQ on completion, error, or half-completion |
| **Handshake Interfaces (4 pairs)** | DRQ/DACK, BPRG/BACK pairs (Flow control) |

---

## 2.2 Transfer Modes (KTU Frequently Tested)

### 🔹 Mode 1 — **Burst Mode (Block Transfer)**
- DMAC gains complete control of the bus; **CPU is locked out** for the entire burst.
- Highest throughput, but **worst CPU latency**.
- Suitable for: memory-to-memory copies, LCD framebuffer fill, block encryption.

### 🔹 Mode 2 — **Cycle Stealing Mode**
- DMAC transfers **one data word per bus cycle granted**, then **releases** the bus.
- The CPU and DMAC **interleave** bus access.
- Lower throughput, but **fair** and **CPU-friendly**.
- Suitable for: audio streaming, slow peripherals.

### 🔹 Mode 3 — **Peripheral-to-Memory (P → M)**
- Source: peripheral data register (FIFO).
- Destination: main memory buffer.
- Triggered by peripheral's `DREQ` signal.

### 🔹 Mode 4 — **Memory-to-Peripheral (M → P)**
- Source: main memory.
- Destination: peripheral data register.
- Useful for UART transmission, DAC waveform generation.

### 🔹 Mode 5 — **Memory-to-Memory (M → M)**
- DMAC reads from source address, writes to destination address.
- **Two AHB master ports** are required (e.g., PL330 dual-master).

---

## 2.3 Handshake Protocol (KTU Marker Content)

A DMA handshake guarantees **flow control** between the DMAC and the peripheral:

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **`DRQ` (DMA Request)** | Peripheral → DMAC | "I have data ready / need data" |
| **`DACK` (DMA Acknowledge)** | DMAC → Peripheral | "Acknowledged, I am servicing you" |
| **`HRQ` (Hold Request)** | DMAC → Bus Arbiter | "I want the bus" |
| **`HACK` (Hold Acknowledge)** | Arbiter → DMAC | "Bus is yours" |
| **`BPRG` / `BACK`** | Per-port flow control | Per-channel request/acknowledge |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the following equations verbatim in numerical problems.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **DMA Transfer Time** | $T_{DMA} = \dfrac{N \cdot t_{bus}}{B \cdot f_{clk}}$ | $N$ = bytes; $t_{bus}$ = 1 clock; $B$ = bus width (bytes); $f_{clk}$ in Hz |
| **CPU-Cycle Savings** | $\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{T_{DMA}}{T_{CPU\_poll}} \right) \cdot 100\%$ | Expressed in % |
| **Effective Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N}{T_{DMA}}$ | Bytes per second |
| **Total Bus Arbitration Delay** | $T_{arb} = (N_{req} - 1) \cdot t_{slot}$ | $N_{req}$ = number of bus grants; $t_{slot}$ = arbitration slot |
| **Half-Transfer / End-Transfer Interrupts** | $N_{half} = \dfrac{N_{total}}{2}$ | Triggers IRQ at halfway point |
| **Channel Priority Encoding** | $P_{eff} = P_{ch} \cdot W_{bus\_pri}$ | Higher channel = lower numeric priority code |
| **Burst Beat Count** | $B_{beats} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N}{B} \right\rceil$ | Ceiling function |

> [!NOTE]
> All `\vert` notations have been deliberately used instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve Markdown table integrity. Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in your own notes for **absolute value**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | DMA Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Audio DSP (Cortex-M4/M7)** | Streaming PCM samples to DAC via DMA + double-buffer |
| **Networking (Cortex-A)** | Ethernet MAC descriptor rings, scatter-gather DMA |
| **Display Controllers** | Continuous frame-buffer refresh from SDRAM to LCD |
| **Storage (SD/eMMC)** | SDMA (SmartDMA) block transfers at 50–104 MB/s |
| **Cryptography** | Feeding plaintext to AES engine and retrieving ciphertext |
| **Sensor Fusion (IMU)** | High-rate accelerometer/gyro sampling to circular buffer |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — DMA Transfer Time vs. CPU Polled I/O

Consider a system transferring $N$ bytes from a peripheral (e.g., UART) to memory, where:
- $f_{clk}$ = AHB clock frequency (Hz)
- $B$ = data bus width (in bytes)
- The CPU, when polling, executes $\approx 8$ instructions per byte transferred (load status, branch, load data, store, etc.).
- $CPI$ = average cycles per instruction (Cortex-M3 ≈ **1.25**, Cortex-A9 ≈ **1.1**)
- $f_{CPU}$ = CPU clock frequency (Hz)

### Step 1 — CPU Polled Transfer Time

The CPU must execute approximately $8N$ instructions.

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{8N \cdot CPI}{f_{CPU}}
$$

This is the time the **CPU core is fully occupied** servicing the transfer.

### Step 2 — DMA Transfer Time

The DMAC transfers one beat per AHB clock cycle (ideal case). The number of beats required is:

$$
N_{beats} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N}{B} \right\rceil
$$

Each beat occupies one AHB clock cycle $t_{AHB} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$.

$$
T_{DMA} = N_{beats} \cdot t_{AHB} = \dfrac{N_{beats}}{f_{clk}}
$$

Substituting $N_{beats} = N / B$ (assuming $B$ divides $N$ evenly):

$$
T_{DMA} = \dfrac{N}{B \cdot f_{clk}}
$$

### Step 3 — CPU-Cycle Savings ($\eta_{saved}$)

$$
\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{T_{DMA}}{T_{CPU}} \right) \cdot 100\%
$$

Substitute the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{\dfrac{N}{B \cdot f_{clk}}}{\dfrac{8N \cdot CPI}{f_{CPU}}} \right) \cdot 100\%
$$

Cancel the $N$ terms:

$$
\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{8 \cdot CPI \cdot B \cdot f_{clk}} \right) \cdot 100\%
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $N = 4096$ bytes, $B = 4$ bytes, $f_{clk} = 100\,\text{MHz}$, $f_{CPU} = 100\,\text{MHz}$, $CPI = 1.25$.

Step 4a — Compute $T_{CPU}$:

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{8 \times 4096 \times 1.25}{100 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{40960}{10^{8}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}
$$

Therefore, $T_{CPU} = 409.6\ \mu s$.

Step 4b — Compute $T_{DMA}$:

$$
T_{DMA} = \dfrac{4096}{4 \times 100 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{4096}{4 \times 10^{8}} = 1.024 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s}
$$

Therefore, $T_{DMA} = 10.24\ \mu s$.

Step 4c — Compute $\eta_{saved}$:

$$
\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{10.24\ \mu s}{409.6\ \mu s} \right) \cdot 100\% = \left( 1 - 0.025 \right) \cdot 100\% = 97.5\%
$$

### Step 5 — Analytical Conclusion

> The DMA path is **40× faster** than CPU-polled I/O, and **97.5% of CPU cycles are liberated** for application logic. This is the engineering justification for DMA in nearly every embedded ARM SoC.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Transfer Mode

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Audio playback to DAC (real-time) | **Cycle-Steal + Double Buffer** | Latency must stay < 1 ms; CPU must run audio codec |
| Block memory copy during boot | **Burst (M → M)** | CPU is otherwise idle; we want maximum throughput |
| UART logging 1 KB / second | **Peripheral → Memory, Cycle-Steal** | Low data rate; CPU is busy elsewhere |
| Cryptographic key burn to flash | **Burst (M → M) + CRC** | Large data, no CPU interaction needed |
| Sensor high-rate sampling (1 MSPS ADC) | **Peripheral → Memory, Burst, Circular** | High data rate; cannot tolerate CPU stalls |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Configuration Sequence (Cortex-M3 / PL080-Style)

1. **Enable DMAC clock** in `RCC` register.
2. **Configure the source peripheral's DMA channel** in the peripheral's `DMASEL` register.
3. **Set the source address** $\rightarrow$ `DMACCxSrcAddr` (peripheral data register).
4. **Set the destination address** $\rightarrow$ `DMACCxDestAddr` (memory buffer).
5. **Set control register fields:**
   - Transfer size = $N$
   - Source/Destination width = 8/16/32 bits
   - Source/Destination increment = yes/no
   - Burst size (1, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 beats)
   - Transfer type = M2P, P2M, M2M
6. **Enable channel** in `DMACCxConfig` (`E = 1`).
7. **DMAC begins on peripheral's `DREQ`.**
8. **IRQ fires** on terminal count or half-count.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level DMA Architecture (ARM SoC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Core Cortex M3] --> BUSMATRIX[AHB Bus Matrix]
    BUSMATRIX --> SRAM[SRAM Main Memory]
    BUSMATRIX --> DMAC[DMAC PrimeCell PL080]
    BUSMATRIX --> ETHMAC[Ethernet MAC]
    BUSMATRIX --> USBCORE[USB Controller]
    DMAC --> UART1[UART 1 Peripheral]
    DMAC --> SPIM[SPI Master]
    DMAC --> ADCPER[ADC Peripheral]
    DMAC --> DACPER[DAC Peripheral]
    UART1 --> APB1[APB Bridge 1]
    SPIM --> APB1
    ADCPER --> APB2[APB Bridge 2]
    DACPER --> APB2
    CPU --> APB1
    CPU --> APB2
```

> The **AHB Bus Matrix** is the central arbiter. Notice that the **DMAC sits as an AHB master**, directly competing with the CPU for the SRAM bus.

---

## 4.2 DMA Handshake and Bus Arbitration Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PERI as Peripheral e.g. UART
    participant DMAC as DMA Controller
    participant ARB as Bus Arbiter
    participant MEM as SRAM Memory

    PERI->>DMAC: DREQ asserted data ready
    DMAC->>ARB: HRQ hold request
    ARB->>CPU: HALT CPU bus grant pending
    ARB->>DMAC: HACK bus granted
    DMAC->>PERI: DACK acknowledged
    DMAC->>MEM: AHB read burst
    MEM-->>DMAC: Data on HRDATA
    DMAC->>PERI: Write data to peripheral register
    PERI->>DMAC: DREQ de asserted
    DMAC->>ARB: Bus release HRQ low
```
