```mermaid
    ARB->>CPU: Resume CPU bus access
    DMAC->>CPU: IRQ terminal count
```

---

## 4.3 Internal DMAC Block Diagram (PrimeCell PL330)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APBSLV[APB Slave Interface] --> REGS[Control Registers]
    REGS --> SCHED[Instruction Scheduler]
    SCHED --> CH0[Channel 0 Context]
    SCHED --> CH1[Channel 1 Context]
    SCHED --> CH2[Channel 2 Context]
    SCHED --> CH3[Channel 3 Context]
    SCHED --> CH4[Channel 4 Context]
    SCHED --> CH5[Channel 5 Context]
    SCHED --> CH6[Channel 6 Context]
    SCHED --> CH7[Channel 7 Context]
    CH0 --> AHBMA[AHB Master Port A]
    CH4 --> AHBMA
    CH0 --> AHBMB[AHB Master Port B]
    CH4 --> AHBMB
    AHBMA --> BUSARB[Internal Bus Arbiter]
    AHBMB --> BUSARB
    BUSARB --> AHBEXT[External AHB Bus]
    CH0 --> FIFO0[FIFO Buffer 0]
    CH4 --> FIFO4[FIFO Buffer 4]
    AHBEXT --> INTC[Interrupt Controller IRQ Output]
```

---

## 4.4 Transfer Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need data transfer] --> B{Source and Destination}
    B -->|Memory to Memory| C{Bus bandwidth critical}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Burst Mode M to M]
    C -->|No| E[Use Cycle Steal M to M]
    B -->|Peripheral to Memory| F{Real time critical}
    F -->|Yes| G[Cycle Steal with double buffer]
    F -->|No| H[Burst mode P to M]
    B -->|Memory to Peripheral| I{DAC or stream output}
    I -->|Yes| J[Cycle Steal with circular buffer]
    I -->|No| K[Burst M to P]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional mapping avoids attempting physically-rendered drawings (e.g., IC pinouts) and instead represents the **logical flow of decision-making and data ownership**, which is the academically-rigorous way to depict DMA transfer selection in KTU exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Direct Memory Access. List any two advantages.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a data-transfer mechanism in which a dedicated hardware controller (DMAC) autonomously transfers data between I/O devices and memory (or between two memory locations) **without CPU intervention** for each data word.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Higher data throughput** — the DMAC can move data at bus-clock speeds, much faster than CPU-driven polling loops.
2. **CPU offloading** — frees the CPU to perform computational tasks concurrently, improving overall system performance and reducing interrupt overhead.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Burst Mode and Cycle-Stealing Mode of DMA.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Burst Mode | Cycle-Stealing Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus Ownership** | DMAC holds the bus for the **entire block** | DMAC holds the bus for **one data word** at a time |
| **CPU Access** | CPU is **completely blocked** during burst | CPU and DMAC **interleave** bus usage |
| **Throughput** | **Highest** | Lower than burst |
| **Latency Impact** | Poor for real-time CPU tasks | Better latency for real-time tasks |
| **Use Case** | Memory-to-memory copy, bulk fill | Audio streaming, low-rate peripherals |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-4 Question Paper Format:** Answer **either (a) or (b)** from each module. Each carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical **DMA Controller** used in ARM-based systems. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An ARM Cortex-M3 system must transfer **8 KB** of data from a peripheral to SRAM using DMA. The AHB bus width is **32 bits** and the bus clock is **80 MHz**. Calculate:
> - (i) The number of AHB beats required.
> - (ii) The total DMA transfer time.
> - (iii) Compare with CPU polling if the CPU executes **8 instructions/byte** at **80 MHz** with $CPI = 1.25$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Block Diagram:**

The DMAC consists of the following major blocks (as drawn in **Section 4.3**):

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **APB Slave Interface** | Allows the CPU to program the DMAC's control and configuration registers via the APB bus. |
| **Control Registers** | Store source/destination addresses, transfer count, control flags, channel configuration. |
| **Instruction Scheduler** | Fetches microcoded DMA instructions from the FIFO and dispatches them to the correct channel. |
| **Channel Context (0–7)** | Each channel has independent context: `SrcAddr`, `DstAddr`, `Control`, `Config`. |
| **FIFO Buffer** | Buffers data between source and destination when their widths/clocks differ. |
| **AHB Master Port(s)** | Initiate AHB read/write transactions on the system bus as bus masters. |
| **Interrupt Logic** | Generates IRQ on terminal count, half-completion, or error. |

**Working Principle:**

1. The CPU writes the source address, destination address, transfer count, and mode bits into the channel's registers.
2. The peripheral asserts `DREQ` when ready.
3. The DMAC requests the bus via `HRQ`; the arbiter grants via `HACK`.
4. The DMAC reads data from source (AHB master transaction) and writes to destination.
5. On terminal count, the DMAC de-asserts `HRQ`, releases the bus, and fires an IRQ to the CPU.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- $N = 8\ \text{KB} = 8192\ \text{bytes}$
- $B = 32\ \text{bits} = 4\ \text{bytes}$
- $f_{clk} = 80\ \text{MHz} = 80 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$

**(i) Number of AHB Beats:**

$$
N_{beats} = \dfrac{N}{B} = \dfrac{8192}{4} = 2048\ \text{beats}
$$

**(ii) DMA Transfer Time:**

$$
T_{DMA} = \dfrac{N_{beats}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{2048}{80 \times 10^{6}} = 2.56 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s}
$$

Therefore, $T_{DMA} = 25.6\ \mu s$.

**(iii) CPU Polled Transfer Time:**

The CPU executes 8 instructions per byte, totaling $8 \times 8192 = 65536$ instructions.

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{65536 \times CPI}{f_{CPU}} = \dfrac{65536 \times 1.25}{80 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{81920}{80 \times 10^{6}} = 1.024 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1024\ \mu s
$$

**Comparison Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{T_{CPU}}{T_{DMA}} = \dfrac{1024}{25.6} = 40
$$

**CPU Cycle Savings:**

$$
\eta_{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{25.6}{1024} \right) \cdot 100\% = 97.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The DMA path is **40× faster**, freeing up **97.5%** of CPU cycles.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the following DMA transfer modes with suitable diagrams: **(7 Marks)**
>   1. Burst Mode
>   2. Cycle Stealing Mode
>   3. Peripheral-to-Memory
>   4. Memory-to-Peripheral
>
> **(b)** Explain the **bus arbitration** process between the CPU and the DMAC in an ARM SoC. What is the role of `HRQ` and `HACK` signals? When does the CPU get stalled? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**1. Burst Mode:**
The DMAC acquires the system bus and performs the **entire transfer in one continuous burst**. The CPU cannot access the bus during this period. This yields the **highest throughput** but causes the **worst CPU latency**.

**2. Cycle Stealing Mode:**
The DMAC transfers **one word per bus cycle grant** and then releases the bus. The CPU can access the bus between DMAC transfers. This is **fairer** to the CPU but reduces throughput.

**3. Peripheral-to-Memory (P → M):**
Data flows from a peripheral data register (e.g., ADC buffer) into a memory region. The DMAC is triggered by the peripheral's `DREQ`. Typical in sensor data acquisition.

**4. Memory-to-Peripheral (M → P):**
Data flows from memory into a peripheral data register (e.g., UART transmit holding register). Used in waveform generation, display refresh, and audio playback.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Bus Arbitration Process:**

In an ARM SoC, both the CPU and the DMAC are **AHB bus masters**. Only one master can drive the bus at a time. The **bus arbiter** decides which master gets control.

**Sequence:**

1. The DMAC detects a pending `DREQ` from a peripheral.
2. The DMAC asserts **`HRQ` (Hold Request)** to the bus arbiter.
3. The arbiter completes the current CPU bus cycle, then signals the CPU to **release** the bus.
4. The arbiter asserts **`HACK` (Hold Acknowledge)** to the DMAC.
5. The DMAC performs the transfer; the CPU is **stalled** (no instruction fetch/execute).
6. On completion, the DMAC de-asserts `HRQ`; the arbiter returns the bus to the CPU.

**Role of `HRQ` and `HACK`:**

| Signal | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `HRQ` | DMAC → Arbiter | "I need the bus" |
| `HACK` | Arbiter → DMAC | "Bus is yours, CPU is stalled" |

> The CPU is **stalled only during the actual AHB transfer beats**, not during DMAC setup. In cycle-stealing mode, the CPU is stalled for only **one beat at a time**, minimizing the latency impact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**
> - ❌ Writing only "DMA = direct access" — must mention **"without CPU intervention per byte"**.
> - ❌ Confusing **Burst** with **Cycle Steal** — Burst blocks CPU **completely**; Cycle Steal **interleaves**.
> - ❌ Forgetting units in calculations — KTU deducts **½ to 1 mark** for missing $\mu s$ or $ms$.
> - ❌ Confusing `HRQ`/`HACK` (DMAC ↔ Arbiter) with `DREQ`/`DACK` (Peripheral ↔ DMAC).
> - ❌ Skipping the **"CPU is freed"** justification in definitions.
> - ❌ Not mentioning the **AMBA AHB Master** role of DMAC — examiners expect this explicitly.
> - ❌ Failing to compute the **savings ratio $T_{CPU}/T_{DMA}$** in numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **DMA = hardware-driven data movement** between memory ↔ peripheral or memory ↔ memory, **without per-byte CPU intervention**.
- [x] **DMAC** acts as an **AHB Master** in AMBA-based ARM SoCs, arbitrating for the bus against the CPU.
- [x] **Burst Mode** = entire block at once → highest throughput, worst CPU latency.
- [x] **Cycle Steal Mode** = one word at a time → fair, better for real-time.
- [x] **M → M** mode requires **two AHB master ports** (e.g., PrimeCell PL330).
- [x] **Handshake Signals:**
   - `DREQ` / `DACK` → Peripheral ↔ DMAC (flow control).
   - `HRQ` / `HACK` → DMAC ↔ Bus Arbiter (bus ownership).
- [x] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
   - $T_{DMA} = \dfrac{N}{B \cdot f_{clk}}$
   - $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{8N \cdot CPI}{f_{CPU}}$
   - $\eta_{saved} = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{DMA}}{T_{CPU}}\right) \cdot 100\%$
- [x] **ARM PrimeCell PL080/PL330** are the canonical DMAC IPs — expect them in questions.
- [x] **Scatter-Gather** and **Linked-List Descriptors** are advanced features for chained, non-contiguous transfers.
- [x] **Circular Buffers + Double Buffering** = the standard pattern for real-time audio/DSP DMA.
- [x] **CPU is stalled only during the active bus transfer beats** — not during DMAC programming or idle time.
- [x] **Half-transfer IRQ** lets software process the first half of a buffer while the DMAC fills the second half.
- [x] Always specify **bus width (B) and clock frequency ($f_{clk}$)** when solving numericals — KTU grading rewards this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Initialization: Memory Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System Initialization: Memory Layout ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **System Initialization: Memory Layout** refers to the predefined, structured organization of the microprocessor's addressable memory space immediately after reset, defining where the **Stack Pointer (MSP)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, **Vector Table**, **Code (.text)**, **Initialized Data (.data)**, **Zero-Initialized Data (.bss)**, and **Heap** reside in physical memory. In the ARM Cortex-M architecture, this layout is fixed at compile time via the **Linker Script** (`.sct` for Keil / `.ld` for GCC) and enforced at boot by the **Reset Handler**, which copies `.data` from Flash to SRAM, zero-fills `.bss`, and configures the stack pointer using the value stored in the vector table's first entry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEECT524 - Module 4):** > "System Initialization: Memory Layout, Stack, Heap, Data sections (initialized/uninitialized), Vector Table placement, Linker Script fundamentals, Startup code execution flow." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the microcontroller's memory as a **large multi-storey warehouse** the moment a delivery truck (power-on) arrives: | Warehouse Zone | Memory Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Reception desk (ground floor)** | Vector Table (0x00000000) | Where the manager first looks for "emergency contacts" (interrupt handlers) | | **Pantry with food already stocked** | `.data` (initialized) | Variables that arrive with known values from Flash | | **Empty storage racks** | `.bss` (uninitialized) | Variables that must be wiped to zero on startup | | **Office workspace** | `.text` / Code (Flash) | Permanent program instructions | | **Top-floor desk with sticky notes** | Stack (grows downward) | Temporary scratchpad for function calls and local variables | | **Loading dock for big items** | Heap (grows upward) | Dynamic memory allocation (`malloc`) | When the warehouse opens (reset), a **boot loader clerk** (Reset Handler) walks through: ① sets up the desk (stack pointer), ② stocks the pantry (copies `.data`), ③ wipes the empty racks (zeros `.bss`), ④ then the workers (main program) start their shift. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries - **ARM Cortex-M4 Memory Map**: 4 GB unified address space (0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF). - **Typical SRAM Size**: 128 KB to 256 KB. - **Typical Flash Size**: 512 KB to 2 MB. - **Stack Alignment**: 8-byte aligned (mandatory in AAPCS). - **Vector Table Size**: `(Number of IRQs + 16 system exceptions) × 4 bytes`. > [!NOTE] > **Core Takeaway:** The memory layout is **not** decided at runtime — it is **statically defined** by the linker script and **dynamically established** by the startup code during the first microseconds of reset. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Canonical Memory Sections ARM toolchains (ARMCC, GCC, IAR) organize compiled output into the following sections, each with a distinct purpose and lifetime: #### 2.1.1 `.text` (Code / Text Section) - Holds the **machine instructions** of the program. - Stored in **non-volatile Flash** (read-only at runtime). - Marked **read-execute (RX)** in MPU/MPU-less systems. - Includes the **vector table** in most Cortex-M implementations. - *Why?* Code must survive power cycles; Flash is byte-addressable and inexpensive per bit. #### 2.1.2 `.data` (Initialized Data Section) - Contains **global and static variables with explicit non-zero initializers** at compile time. ```c int sensor_calibration = 1500; // goes to .data static float gain = 1.25f; // goes to .data ``` - **Two physical locations**: - **Load Address (LMA)** → Flash (where the initial value is stored). - **Virtual/Run Address (VMA)** → SRAM (where the variable is read/written at runtime). - The startup code **copies** the LMA contents to the VMA on reset. #### 2.1.3 `.bss` (Block Started by Symbol — Uninitialized Data) - Contains **global/static variables initialized to zero** (or uninitialized). ```c int counter; // goes to .bss (implicitly zero) static char buffer[256]; // goes to .bss ``` - Stored **only in SRAM** at runtime; the LMA in Flash is typically **4 bytes of zeros** (or omitted). - The startup code **zero-fills** the entire `.bss` range on reset. #### 2.1.4 `.rodata` (Read-Only Data) - String literals, `const` globals, lookup tables. ```c const char msg[] = "Hello"; // goes to .rodata ``` - Stored in Flash; never copied to SRAM (saves RAM). #### 2.1.5 Stack - **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** memory region for: - Function return addresses (LR → pushed on stack). - Local variables. - Function arguments (in some AAPCS variants). - Context saving during interrupts. - **Grows downward** (toward lower addresses) on ARM. - **Stack Pointer**: Main Stack Pointer (MSP) in Handler mode; Process Stack Pointer (PSP) in Thread mode. - Size is configured in the linker script (e.g., 1 KB to 8 KB). #### 2.1.6 Heap - Used for **dynamic memory allocation** (`malloc`, `calloc`, `new` in C++). - **Grows upward** (toward higher addresses) on ARM. - Often implemented using `malloc()` from a newlib or microlib library. - No fixed size; bounded by `Heap_Start` and `Heap_End` linker symbols. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Total `.bss` Size | $\text{bss\_size} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{sizeof}(var_i)$ | Bytes | Sum of all uninitialized statics/globals | | Total `.data` Size | $\text{data\_size} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{sizeof}(var_i)$ | Bytes | Sum of all non-zero initialized statics/globals | | Stack End (Top) | $\text{Stack\_Top} = \text{SRAM\_Base} + \text{SRAM\_Size} - 1$ | Address | Highest SRAM address | | Stack Overflow Detection | $\text{SP} < \text{Stack\_Limit}$ | — | Trap if violated | | Vector Table Size | $\text{VT\_size} = (16 + N_{IRQ}) \times 4$ | Bytes | $N_{IRQ}$ = number of device IRQs | | Heap Start | $\text{Heap\_Start} = \text{end\_of\_bss} + 1$ | Address | Right after `.bss` | | Heap End | $\text{Heap\_End} = \text{Stack\_Base} - 1$ | Address | Just below stack | | Memory Occupied | $\text{Occupied} = \text{code} + \text{data} + \text{bss} + \text{stack} + \text{heap}$ | Bytes | Must $\leq$ total memory | | Stack Alignment | $\text{SP} \equiv 0 \pmod 8$ | Bytes | AAPCS requirement | | Initial SP Value | $\text{SP}_{init} = \text{VectorTable}[0]$ | Address | First word of vector table | ### 2.3 Why Memory Layout Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Embedded Firmware Determinism** — Predictable boot time for safety-critical systems (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262). 2. **Memory-Mapped I/O** — Knowing exact SRAM boundaries prevents accidental writes to peripheral registers. 3. **Link-Time Optimization** — Layout enables `__attribute__((section(".ccmram")))` for placing hot code in Core-Coupled Memory. 4. **Security (TrustZone)** — SAU (Security Attribution Unit) relies on memory region definitions to enforce NS (Non-Secure) vs S (Secure) partitioning. 5. **RTOS Memory Budgeting** — FreeRTOS `configTOTAL_HEAP_SIZE` and stack depth per task must be carved from the same SRAM pool — overlaps cause silent corruption. > [!NOTE] > **Why a separate "copy" step for `.data`?** > Flash is slower than SRAM. Code that runs frequently (e.g., loop variables) benefits from being in SRAM. However, initial values must persist across resets, so they are stored in Flash (LMA) and copied to SRAM (VMA) at boot. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Build a Complete Memory Map from a Linker Script Consider an STM32F407 (Cortex-M4) with the following linker-defined regions (simplified `STM32F407VGTX_FLASH.ld`): ``` MEMORY { FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1M SRAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K } SECTIONS { .isr_vector : { KEEP(*(.isr_vector)) } >FLASH .text : { *(.text*) *(.rodata*) } >FLASH _sidata = LOADADDR(.data); .data : { _sdata = .; *(.data*) _edata = .; } >SRAM AT> FLASH .bss (NOLOAD) : { _sbss = .; *(.bss*) _ebss = .; } >SRAM ._user_heap_stack : { . = ALIGN(8); PROVIDE ( end = . ); PROVIDE ( _end = . ); . = . + _Min_Heap_Size; . = . + _Min_Stack_Size; . = ALIGN(8); } >SRAM } ``` **Step 1: Identify the FLASH boundaries** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FLASH}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x08000000} \\ \text{FLASH}_{\text{end}} &= \text{FLASH}_{\text{start}} + \text{1 MB} - 1 \\ &= \text{0x08000000} + \text{0x100000} - 1 \\ &= \text{0x080FFFFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify the SRAM boundaries** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRAM}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x20000000} \\ \text{SRAM}_{\text{end}} &= \text{SRAM}_{\text{start}} + \text{128 KB} - 1 \\ &= \text{0x20000000} + \text{0x20000} - 1 \\ &= \text{0x2001FFFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Place the Vector Table** The vector table begins at the very start of FLASH because the Cortex-M4 boots from address `0x00000000` (aliased to `0x08000000` after BOOT0 configuration). $$ \text{VT}_{\text{start}} = \text{0x08000000} $$ Assuming the device has $N_{IRQ} = 82$ IRQs (STM32F407 typical), the vector table size is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VT}_{\text{size}} &= (16 + 82) \times 4 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 98 \times 4 \\ &= 392 \text{ bytes} \\ &= \text{0x188 bytes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \text{VT}_{\text{end}} = \text{0x08000000} + \text{0x188} - 1 = \text{0x08000187} $$ **Step 4: Place the `.text` and `.rodata` sections** The compiler places the rest of the code immediately after the vector table. $$ \text{.text}_{\text{start}} = \text{0x08000188} \quad \text{(aligned to 4 bytes)} $$ Suppose the compiled `.text` + `.rodata` consumes $C$ bytes (read from the `.map` file). Then: $$ \text{.text}_{\text{end}} = \text{0x08000187} + C $$ **Step 5: Define the `.data` section's LMA and VMA** The `.data` section has a **Load Address** (in Flash) and a **Run Address** (in SRAM). The linker sets: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LoadAddr} &= \_\_sidata = \text{address right after } .text \\ \text{RunAddr} &= \_\_sdata = \text{SRAM base (or after stack)} \end{aligned} $$ The startup code performs the copy: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Source} &= \_\_sidata \quad \text{(in Flash, LMA)} \\ \text{Destination} &= \_\_sdata \quad \text{(in SRAM, VMA)} \\ \text{Word count} &= \frac{\_\_edata - \_\_sdata}{4} \end{aligned} $$ This is implemented in C startup as: ```c unsigned long *src = &_sidata; unsigned long *dst = &_sdata; unsigned long *end = &_edata; while (dst < end) { *dst++ = *src++; } ``` **Step 6: Zero-fill the `.bss` section** The startup code iterates from `__sbss` to `__ebss` in SRAM and writes zeros: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Start} &= \_\_sbss \\ \text{End} &= \_\_ebss \\ \text{Bytes} &= \_\_ebss - \_\_sbss \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Set up the Stack and Heap** Assuming `_Min_Heap_Size = 0x200` (512 B) and `_Min_Stack_Size = 0x400` (1 KB): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Heap}_{\text{start}} &= \_\_end \quad \text{(end of .bss)} \\ \text{Heap}_{\text{end}} &= \text{Heap}_{\text{start}} + 0x200 \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{end}} &= \text{Heap}_{\text{end}} + 0x400 \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} &= \text{SRAM}_{\text{end}} = \text{0x2001FFFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8: Initial Stack Pointer (read from Vector Table)** On reset, the hardware reads the first word of the vector table and loads it into MSP: $$ \text{MSP}_{\text{initial}} = \text{Memory}[0x08000000] = \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} $$ **Step 9: Validate the layout** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total SRAM used} &= \text{sizeof(.data)} + \text{sizeof(.bss)} + \text{Heap}_{\text{size}} + \text{Stack}_{\text{size}} \\ \text{Constraint} &: \text{Total SRAM used} \leq 0x20000 \quad \text{(128 KB)} \end{aligned} $$ If violated, the linker emits `region 'SRAM' overflowed`. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Address Calculation **Problem:** A Cortex-M3 system has: - FLASH: 0x00000000 to 0x0007FFFF (512 KB) - SRAM: 0x20000000 to 0x0001FFFF (128 KB) — *(typo correction: 0x20000000 to 0x2001FFFF)* - Vector table: 64 entries × 4 B = 256 B - `.text` + `.rodata`: 120 KB - `.data`: 2 KB - `.bss`: 4 KB - Heap: 8 KB - Stack: 4 KB **Calculate the start address of the stack (top of stack) and verify no memory overflow.** **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VT}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x00000000} + 256 - 1 = \text{0x000000FF} \\ \text{.text}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x000000FF} + 120 \times 1024 = \text{0x000000FF} + \text{0x1E000} = \text{0x0001E0FF} \\ \text{.data}_{\text{LMA}} &= \text{0x0001E100} \\ \text{.data}_{\text{VMA}} &= \text{0x20000000} \\ \text{.data}_{\text{VMA,end}} &= \text{0x20000000} + 2 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x200007FF} \\ \text{.bss}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x20000800} \\ \text{.bss}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x20000800} + 4 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x200017FF} \\ \text{Heap}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x20001800} \\ \text{Heap}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x20001800} + 8 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x200037FF} \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} &= \text{0x20003800} + 4 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x200047FF} \end{aligned} $$ **Overflow check:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRAM}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x2001FFFF} \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} &= \text{0x200047FF} \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} < \text{SRAM}_{\text{end}} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{0x200047FF} < \text{0x2001FFFF} \quad \checkmark \text{ OK} \\ \text{Used SRAM} &= 2 + 4 + 8 + 4 = 18 \text{ KB} \\ \text{Free SRAM} &= 128 - 18 = 110 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Pitfalls | Pitfall | Symptom | Root Cause | Fix | |---|---|---|---| | Stack overflow | HardFault on deep call | Stack size too small in linker | Increase `_Min_Stack_Size` | | Heap/Stack collision | Random data corruption | Heap grows into stack | Use `_sbrk` with bounds check | | `.data` not initialized | Global var starts as 0 instead of 5 | Startup copy missing | Ensure `__main` calls `__scatterload` | | Unaligned SP | UsageFault on first function call | SP not 8-byte aligned | Use `ALIGN(8)` in linker | | Flash overflow | Linker error: region overflowed | Code too large | Optimize, use `-Os`, enable LTO | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Complete ARM Cortex-M Memory Map (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FLASH["FLASH MEMORY (0x08000000 - 0x080FFFFF)"] VT["Vector Table (0x08000000)
Initial SP - Reset_Handler - NMI - HardFault ... IRQ[82]"] TEXT[".text Section
Machine Code Instructions"] RODATA[".rodata Section
String Literals + const Globals"] DATALMA[".data LMA
Initial Values of .data Vars"] end subgraph SRAM["SRAM (0x20000000 - 0x2001FFFF)"] DATAVMA[".data Section VMA
Initialized Global/Static Vars"] BSSVMA[".bss Section
Zero-Initialized Vars"] HEAP["Heap Region
Grows Upward (malloc)"] STACK["Stack Region
Grows Downward (MSP)"] end PERIPH["Peripheral Registers
(0x40000000 - 0x5FFFFFFF)"] SYS["System Control
(0xE0000000 - 0xFFFFFFFF)"] EXT["External Memory
(0x60000000 - 0x9FFFFFFF)"] VT -. "Reads initial SP" .-> STACK TEXT --> DATALMA DATALMA -- "Reset_Handler copies
word-by-word" --> DATAVMA DATAVMA --> BSSVMA BSSVMA -- "Startup zeros out" --> HEAP HEAP -- "Adjacent to" --> STACK PERIPH --- SYS SYS --- EXT ``` ### 4.2 Startup Code Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Power-On / Reset
CPU reads 0x00000000"] --> B["Load MSP from
VectorTable[0]"] B --> C["Set PC = VectorTable[1]
= Reset_Handler address"] C --> D["Reset_Handler Executes"] D --> E["Disable Interrupts
(PRIMASK = 1)"] E --> F["Copy .data LMA to VMA
(__main routine)"] F --> G["Zero-fill .bss region
(__main routine)"] G --> H["Initialize C runtime
(call constructors, FPU init)"] H --> I["Call main()"] I --> J["Enter Scheduler
(if RTOS) or
Idle Loop (bare-metal)"] ``` ### 4.3 Logical Section-to-Memory Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["C/C++ Source File"] --> COMPILE["Compiler (arm-none-eabi-gcc)"] COMPILE --> OBJ[".o Object File
.text .data .bss .rodata"] OBJ --> LINK["Linker (ld) + Linker Script"] LINK --> ELF[".elf Executable"] ELF --> OBJCOPY["objcopy"] OBJCOPY --> BIN[".bin / .hex
Flashed to MCU"] LINK -. "Defines" .-> SYM["Symbols:
_sidata, _sdata, _edata,
_sbss, _ebss, _estack, _end"] SYM --> STARTUP["Startup Code
(startup_stm32f4xx.s)"] STARTUP --> BOOT["Boot Sequence
Copy + Zero + Run"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading tip for KTU board exams:** When a question asks to "draw the memory layout", always show **both** the Flash side (LMA of `.data` + `.text` + Vector Table) and the SRAM side (`.data` VMA + `.bss` + Heap + Stack) on **two parallel columns**, with arrows showing the **copy operation** between them. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]` **Define the term "memory layout" in the context of ARM Cortex-M system initialization. List the major memory sections.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Memory layout refers to the **structured organization of the addressable memory space** of a microcontroller as defined by the **linker script** and established by the **startup code** during system initialization. It specifies the addresses, sizes, and contents of all code, data, and runtime regions. **Major Sections (1.5 marks):** 1. **Vector Table** (in Flash) — contains initial SP and exception handler addresses. 2. **`.text` / Code section** (in Flash) — machine instructions. 3. **`.rodata`** (in Flash) — read-only constants and string literals. 4. **`.data`** (LMA in Flash, VMA in SRAM) — initialized globals/statics. 5. **`.bss`** (in SRAM) — zero-initialized globals/statics. 6. **Heap** (in SRAM) — dynamic memory, grows upward. 7. **Stack** (in SRAM) — LIFO for function calls, grows downward. --- #### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]` **Explain the role of the Reset Handler in initializing the memory layout of an ARM Cortex-M system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Reset Handler** is the first user-defined function executed after reset. Its role in memory layout initialization includes: 1. **Stack Pointer Initialization (1 mark):** Although the hardware auto-loads MSP from `VectorTable[0]`, the Reset Handler may re-initialize or switch to PSP for Thread mode. 2. **`.data` Copy (1 mark):** Copies the contents of the `.data` section from its **Load Memory Address (LMA)** in Flash to its **Virtual Memory Address (VMA)** in SRAM using a `for` loop and the linker symbols `__sidata`, `__sdata`, and `__edata`. 3. **`.bss` Zero-Fill (1 mark):** Iterates from `__sbss` to `__ebss`, writing zeros to all uninitialized global/static variables to guarantee defined startup values. 4. *(Optional bonus point)*: It then calls `__libc_init_array()` to invoke C++ constructors, initializes the FPU, and finally calls `main()`. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete memory layout** of an ARM Cortex-M4 system. Show both the Flash and SRAM regions, and indicate the direction of growth of the stack and heap. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Cortex-M3 system has the following linker-defined regions: - FLASH: 0x00000000, size 256 KB - SRAM: 0x20000000, size 64 KB - Vector Table: 48 entries - `.text` + `.rodata` size: 80 KB - `.data` size: 3 KB - `.bss` size: 5 KB - Heap: 4 KB - Stack: 2 KB **Calculate the start and end addresses of each section, and verify that no memory overflow occurs.** **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. Key points to be mentioned: 1. **(2 marks)** Draw the **two-column diagram** (Flash on left, SRAM on right): - Flash contains: Vector Table, `.text`, `.rodata`, `.data` LMA. - SRAM contains: `.data` VMA, `.bss`, Heap, Stack. 2. **(2 marks)** Label the **direction of growth**: - Stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). - Heap grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). 3. **(2 marks)** Show the **arrow from Flash `.data` to SRAM `.data`** indicating the copy operation by the startup code. 4. **(1 mark)** Mention that the **MSP initial value** is read from the first word of the vector table at reset. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: Vector Table size and boundaries (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VT}_{\text{size}} &= 48 \times 4 = 192 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{VT}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x00000000} \\ \text{VT}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x000000BF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: `.text` + `.rodata` boundaries (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{.text}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x000000C0} \quad \text{(aligned)} \\ \text{.text}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x000000BF} + 80 \times 1024 = \text{0x000000BF} + \text{0x14000} = \text{0x000140BF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: `.data` LMA and VMA (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{.data}_{\text{LMA, start}} &= \text{0x000140C0} \\ \text{.data}_{\text{VMA, start}} &= \text{0x20000000} \\ \text{.data}_{\text{VMA, end}} &= \text{0x20000BFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: `.bss` (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{.bss}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x20000C00} \\ \text{.bss}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x20000C00} + 5 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x20001FFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Heap (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Heap}_{\text{start}} &= \text{0x20002000} \\ \text{Heap}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x20002000} + 4 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x20002FFF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Stack (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} &= \text{0x20003000} + 2 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x200037FF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Overflow verification (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRAM}_{\text{end}} &= \text{0x20000000} + 64 \times 1024 - 1 = \text{0x2000FFFF} \\ \text{Stack}_{\text{top}} &= \text{0x200037FF} \\ \text{0x200037FF} < \text{0x2000FFFF} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{No overflow} \quad \checkmark \\ \text{Used SRAM} &= 3 + 5 + 4 + 2 = 14 \text{ KB} \quad (\approx 22\% \text{ of total}) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The layout is valid; all sections fit within the available Flash (256 KB) and SRAM (64 KB) regions. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **role of the linker script** in defining the memory layout. Describe the key symbols it generates (`__sdata`, `__edata`, `__sbss`, `__ebss`, `__StackTop`) and how the startup code uses them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **Load Memory Address (LMA)** and **Virtual Memory Address (VMA)**. Explain why the `.data` section has two addresses, while `.text` and `.bss` have only one. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Role of Linker Script (2 marks):** The linker script (`.ld` for GCC, `.sct` for Keil) is a text file that tells the linker: - The **physical memory regions** available (their start addresses and sizes) using the `MEMORY` command. - How to **map input sections** from object files (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, etc.) to **output sections** in memory using the `SECTIONS` command. - The **placement order** of sections in memory. 2. **Generated Symbols (3 marks):** | Symbol | Meaning | Location | |---|---|---| | `__sdata` | Start of `.data` in SRAM (VMA) | SRAM | | `__edata` | End of `.data` in SRAM (VMA) | SRAM | | `__sidata` | Load address of `.data` in Flash (LMA) | Flash | | `__sbss` | Start of `.bss` in SRAM | SRAM | | `__ebss` | End of `.bss` in SRAM | SRAM | | `__StackTop` | Initial value of MSP (top of stack) | SRAM | | `__end` | End of `.bss` / start of heap | SRAM | 3. **Startup Code Usage (2 marks):** - **`.data` copy loop:** `src = &__sidata; dst = &__sdata; while (dst < &__edata) *dst++ = *src++;` - **`.bss` zero loop:** `for (p = &__sbss; p < &__ebss; p++) *p = 0;` - **MSP initialization:** `__set_MSP(__StackTop);` (or hardware auto-loads from vector table). - **Heap/Stack boundaries:** `malloc` uses `__end` to `__StackTop` range for `_sbrk()`. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** 1. **Definition of LMA and VMA (2 marks):** - **LMA (Load Memory Address):** The address where the section's **initial bytes are physically stored** in non-volatile memory (Flash/ROM). - **VMA (Virtual Memory Address):** The address where the section's bytes reside **at runtime** (the address used by the CPU when executing instructions or accessing data). 2. **Why `.data` has two addresses (3 marks):** - The `.data` section contains variables that **must have known non-zero initial values** (e.g., `int threshold = 100;`). - These initial values must **persist across power cycles** ⇒ stored in **Flash** (LMA). - However, at runtime, these variables are **frequently read/written** ⇒ must reside in **writable SRAM** (VMA). - Hence, the linker script declares `.data` with `>SRAM AT> FLASH` syntax: ``` .data : { ... } >SRAM AT> FLASH ``` - The startup code performs the **LMA → VMA copy** at reset. 3. **Why `.text` has only one address (1 mark):** - Code is **executed in place (XIP)** directly from Flash. - No need to copy; the LMA and VMA are identical (both in Flash). 4. **Why `.bss` has only one address (1 mark):** - `.bss` variables are **defined to be zero** by the C standard. - There is no useful initial value to preserve in Flash — saving Flash space. - The startup code **zero-fills** `.bss` in SRAM at boot. - Declared with `NOLOAD` to instruct the linker not to reserve Flash space. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing LMA with VMA** — A very common 1–2 mark deduction. Remember: **LMA = where data is *stored*; VMA = where data is *executed/accessed***. State both explicitly. > > 2. **Wrong direction of stack growth** — Students often write that the **stack grows upward**. On ARM (and most architectures), the **stack grows downward** (toward lower addresses). The **heap grows upward**. > > 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1 from end addresses** — End address = Start + Size − 1, not Start + Size. > > 4. **Not mentioning `NOLOAD` for `.bss`** — When drawing the Flash column, students sometimes show `.bss` occupying Flash space. It does not — `.bss` is `NOLOAD` and exists **only in SRAM**. > > 5. **Omitting the Vector Table location** — Always begin the Flash column with the **vector table at 0x00000000** (or 0x08000000 for STM32). The first 4 bytes are the **initial MSP**, not code. > > 6. **Not showing the copy arrow** — The `.data` copy operation from Flash to SRAM is the **central concept** of memory initialization. Drawing an arrow earns full marks; missing it costs 1–2 marks. > > 7. **Using wrong units in calculations** — 1 KB = 1024 bytes = 0x400. Show the conversion explicitly (e.g., 80 KB = 0x14000 bytes). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Memory layout** = pre-defined organization of addressable memory, fixed by the **linker script** and established by the **Reset Handler**. - ✅ **Flash holds**: Vector Table + `.text` + `.rodata` + `.data` LMA. - ✅ **SRAM holds**: `.data` VMA + `.bss` + Heap + Stack. - ✅ **LMA (Load Address)** = storage location (Flash); **VMA (Run Address)** = execution/access location (SRAM). - ✅ `.text` and `.bss` have **only one address**; `.data` has **two**. - ✅ **Stack grows DOWN**; **Heap grows UP**. - ✅ **MSP is loaded from `VectorTable[0]`** automatically by hardware on reset. - ✅ **Reset Handler responsibilities**: (1) Disable interrupts, (2) Copy `.data` LMA → VMA, (3) Zero-fill `.bss`, (4) Call constructors, (5) Call `main()`. - ✅ **Linker symbols to memorize**: `__sdata`, `__edata`, `__sidata`, `__sbss`, `__ebss`, `__StackTop`, `__end`. - ✅ **Vector Table size** = $(16 + N_{IRQ}) \times 4$ bytes. - ✅ **Stack alignment**: 8-byte aligned per AAPCS — use `ALIGN(8)` in linker script. - ✅ **`.bss` is `NOLOAD`** — does not consume Flash space. - ✅ **Total SRAM used** = `.data` + `.bss` + Heap + Stack $\leq$ SRAM size; otherwise, **linker region overflow error**. - ✅ **Typical boot sequence**: Power-On → Load MSP from VT[0] → Jump to Reset_Handler → Copy `.data` → Zero `.bss` → Init C runtime → Call `main()`. - ✅ **Why memory layout matters**: determinism, security (TrustZone), MPU region setup, RTOS task budgeting, and avoiding hard-to-debug corruption. - ✅ **Engineering analogy**: Think of Flash as the "warehouse's permanent blueprint room" and SRAM as the "active workshop floor" where current work happens.

topics.title: CPU and Vector Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPU and Vector Table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The ARM Cortex-M CPU: Formal Definition

The **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of the ARM Cortex-M microcontroller is based on the **ARMv7-M / ARMv8-M** architecture and implements a **Thumb-2 instruction set**, providing an optimal blend of high code density and high performance. The CPU core is a **32-bit, load-store, RISC-style processor** with a unified memory map and integrated **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** for deterministic exception handling.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term "CPU" refers to the **processor core register file** — the set of internal registers that the programmer directly interacts with while writing assembly or analyzing compiler-generated output.

## 1.2 The Vector Table: Formal Definition

The **Vector Table** is a predefined, contiguous block of **32-bit (4-byte) words** stored at the bottom of the system memory map (starting at address **0x0000 0000** by default, but relocatable via the **Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR)**). It contains:
- The **initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** value
- The **Reset vector** (initial Program Counter)
- The addresses of all **exception and interrupt service routines (ISRs)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 PEECT524 syllabus, this topic directly maps to **CO2: "Describe the ARM processor core, registers, and exception model."**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Hospital Emergency System

| Component | Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Registers** | Doctor's personal instruments (stethoscope, pen, clipboard) | Hold immediate values the CPU is working on right now |
| **Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** | Hospital's main reception desk | Tracks the "current" working area for system/handler mode |
| **Process Stack Pointer (PSP)** | Specialty clinic's reception desk | A separate working area for thread-mode user code |
| **Vector Table** | Hospital's emergency contact directory | On any event (fire, NMI, fault), instantly look up *who* to call and *where* to go |
| **NVIC** | Hospital's emergency dispatcher | Decides priority and dispatches the right handler |

Think of the **vector table** as a **pre-indexed phonebook**. When an exception occurs, the CPU doesn't waste time figuring out *what* the handler address is — it simply multiplies the exception number by 4 and reads the entry. Speed is paramount because in embedded real-time systems, every microsecond counts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The vector table is fundamental to understanding exception latency, prioritization, and the Cortex-M tail-chaining optimization.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Boundaries

- **Register width:** **32 bits** for all general-purpose registers
- **Vector table entry size:** **4 bytes** (32 bits) per vector
- **Number of system exceptions:** **16** (including reserved slots)
- **Maximum external interrupts:** **Up to 240** (device-dependent, e.g., STM32F4 has 82)
- **Default vector table base address:** **0x0000 0000**
- **Processor modes:** **2** (Thread, Handler) with **2** access levels (Privileged, Unprivileged)
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cortex-M Register File: The "Brain" of the CPU

The ARM Cortex-M core contains **thirteen 32-bit general-purpose registers (R0–R12)**, plus several special-purpose registers. They are classified as follows:

### 2.1.1 General-Purpose Registers (R0 – R12)

- **R0–R7:** Low registers, accessible by all 16-bit Thumb instructions.
- **R8–R12:** High registers, only accessible by 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions.
- **R0–R3:** Used as **argument/scratch registers** in the AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard).
- **R4–R11:** **Callee-saved registers** — the called function preserves these.
- **R12 (IP):** Intra-procedure call scratch register.

### 2.1.2 Special-Purpose Registers

| Register | Name | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R13 (SP)** | Stack Pointer | Two physical SPs: **MSP** (Main) and **PSP** (Process) | Critical for context switching in RTOS |
| **R14 (LR)** | Link Register | Stores return address after subroutine call (`BL`/`BLX`) | Used for return from exception |
| **R15 (PC)** | Program Counter | Holds address of next instruction to execute | Points to current instruction + 2 (Thumb) |
| **xPSR** | Combined Program Status | Holds **APSR + IPSR + EPSR** | Indicates current execution state |
| **PRIMASK** | Priority Mask | Disables all configurable exceptions | Used in critical sections |
| **FAULTMASK** | Fault Mask | Disables all exceptions except NMI | Used during fault handling |
| **BASEPRI** | Base Priority | Masks all exceptions below a threshold | Granular priority masking |
| **CONTROL** | Control Register | Selects SP (MSP/PSP) and access level (Privileged/Unprivileged) | **Key register for RTOS design** |

### 2.1.3 The Program Status Registers (PSR Family)

The single **xPSR** register is a logical OR combination of three sub-registers:

$$
\text{xPSR} = \text{APSR} \cup \text{IPSR} \cup \text{EPSR}
$$

Where:
- **APSR (Application PSR):** Contains condition flags — **N** (Negative), **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry), **V** (Overflow).
- **IPSR (Interrupt PSR):** Contains the **exception number** of the currently executing ISR (bits [5:0]).
- **EPSR (Execution PSR):** Contains the **Thumb state bit** (T, bit 24) and the **ICI/IT** bits for interruptible-continuable instructions.

## 2.2 Operating Modes and Privilege Levels

The Cortex-M supports two **modes** and two **access levels**:

$$
\text{Mode} \in \{\text{Thread}, \text{Handler}\} \quad,\quad \text{Access} \in \{\text{Privileged}, \text{Unprivileged}\}
$$

- **Thread Mode:** Normal program execution; can use either MSP or PSP; can be Privileged or Unprivileged.
- **Handler Mode:** Exception handler execution; **always Privileged**; always uses **MSP**.
- On reset, the CPU enters **Thread Mode + Privileged** with **MSP** selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two Stack Pointers?** The **MSP** is used for kernel/OS code, while the **PSP** is for application tasks. This separation enables clean RTOS context switches without corrupting the kernel stack.

## 2.3 The Vector Table: Architectural Deep-Dive

### 2.3.1 Structure of the Vector Table

The vector table is an **array of 32-bit function pointers** indexed by the exception number. For the ARMv7-M architecture, the layout is **fixed** for the first 16 entries:

| Vector # | Exception | Offset from Base | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 0 | **Initial MSP** | `0x00` | Initial Main Stack Pointer value on reset |
| 1 | **Reset** | `0x04` | Initial Program Counter — entry point of `Reset_Handler` |
| 2 | **NMI** | `0x08` | Non-Maskable Interrupt |
| 3 | **HardFault** | `0x0C` | Catch-all fault handler |
| 4 | **MemManage** | `0x10` | Memory management fault |
| 5 | **BusFault** | `0x14` | Bus access error |
| 6 | **UsageFault** | `0x18` | Undefined instruction, divide by zero, etc. |
| 7–10 | Reserved | `0x1C`–`0x28` | Must be set to 0 or valid fault handlers |
| 11 | **SVCall** | `0x2C` | Supervisor call (used by `SVC` instruction for OS syscalls) |
| 12 | **DebugMonitor** | `0x30` | Debug monitor exception |
| 13 | Reserved | `0x34` | — |
| 14 | **PendSV** | `0x38` | Pendable service (used for RTOS context switching) |
| 15 | **SysTick** | `0x3C` | System tick timer (used for OS tick) |
| 16+ | **IRQ0, IRQ1, …** | `0x40 + 4 \cdot n` | External device interrupts |

### 2.3.2 The Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR)

The vector table can be relocated at runtime by programming the **VTOR**:

$$
\text{Vector\_Base\_Address} = \text{VTOR}
$$

This is essential for **bootloaders** that must redirect the vector table to the application image, and for **RTOS** kernels that may have multiple process contexts.

### 2.3.3 The Exception Entry Sequence (Stacking)

When an exception is accepted by the NVIC, the CPU performs an **automatic hardware stacking** of 8 registers onto the active stack pointer (MSP in handler mode):

$$
\text{Stack Frame} = \{\text{R0}, \text{R1}, \text{R2}, \text{R3}, \text{R12}, \text{LR}, \text{PC}, \text{xPSR}\}
$$

This **xPSR stacking** preserves the interrupted context so that exception return is deterministic and atomic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Vector entry address | $\text{Addr} = \text{VTOR} + 4 \cdot N$ | Locate ISR handler for exception number $N$ |
| Vector table size | $\text{Size} = 4 \cdot (N_{\text{sys}} + N_{\text{IRQ}})$ | Memory footprint calculation |
| MSP reset value | $\text{MSP}_0 = *(\text{VTOR} + 0)$ | Stack initialization on power-up |
| Reset PC value | $\text{PC}_0 = *(\text{VTOR} + 4)$ | Entry point of firmware |
| xPSR composition | $\text{xPSR} = \text{APSR} \mid \text{IPSR} \mid \text{EPSR}$ | Decoding processor state in debugger |
| LR return value (exc.) | $\text{LR} = 0x\text{FFFF FFFF}_X$ | Used by `BX LR` to return from exception |
| Stack frame size | $8 \text{ words} = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Worst-case interrupt latency calculation |
| PC increment (Thumb) | $\text{PC} = \text{Current} + 2$ | Pipeline behavior in Thumb mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** `LR` in exception return is encoded with a special value pattern (`0xFFFFFFFx`). The lower 4 bits indicate which stack pointer to use and the return mode. This is **not** a normal subroutine return address.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RTOS Design:** Understanding the vector table is mandatory for writing **PendSV** handlers that perform context switches between tasks.
- **Bootloader Development:** Relocating the vector table using **VTOR** is the foundation of secure boot and firmware update mechanisms.
- **Interrupt Latency Calculation:** The deterministic stacking of 8 registers (12 cycles on Cortex-M4) gives engineers a calculable worst-case interrupt response time.
- **Fault Diagnosis:** Reading **IPSR** in a debugger instantly reveals whether the system crashed inside an exception handler and *which* one.
- **Security:** Unprivileged mode + CONTROL register settings enable **MPU-based memory isolation** in safety-critical systems (automotive, medical).
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing the Vector Table Entry Address

### Problem Setup

You are given a Cortex-M4 device where **VTOR = 0x0800 0000** (Flash base on STM32F4). Find the address of the **SysTick** handler in the vector table.

### Given Data

- Exception number for **SysTick:** $N = 15$
- Vector Table Offset: $\text{VTOR} = 0x0800\,0000$
- Each vector entry width: $4$ bytes

### Step 1: Recall the Vector Address Formula

The address of the $N$-th vector entry in the table is given by:

$$
\text{Vector\_Addr}(N) = \text{VTOR} + (4 \times N)
$$

This works because each entry is a 32-bit (4-byte) word, and the table is a flat array indexed by the exception number.

### Step 2: Substitute the Known Values

$$
\text{Vector\_Addr}(15) = 0x08000000 + (4 \times 15)
$$

### Step 3: Perform the Multiplication

$$
4 \times 15 = 60_{10}
$$

Convert $60$ to hexadecimal by repeated division by 16:

$$
60 \div 16 = 3 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 60_{10} = 0x3C
$$

### Step 4: Perform the Hexadecimal Addition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vector\_Addr}(15) &= 0x08000000 + 0x0000003C \\
&= 0x0800003C
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verification via Bit-Shift Form

Because multiplying by 4 is equivalent to left-shifting by 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 \times N &= N \ll 2 \\
15 \ll 2 &= 0b1111 \ll 2 = 0b111100 = 60 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\text{Vector\_Addr}(\text{SysTick}) = 0x0800\,003C}
$$

The CPU will load the **32-bit word at this address** into the **PC** when a SysTick exception is taken.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Interrupt Latency

### Problem Setup

A safety-critical automotive ECU must guarantee that an interrupt is serviced within **200 ns** after the event. Calculate whether a Cortex-M4 @ **168 MHz** (STM32F407) meets this requirement for a fully-stacked exception entry.

### Given Data

- Clock frequency: $f_{\text{clk}} = 168\,\text{MHz}$
- Cycle time: $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$
- Exception entry cycles (full stacking, Cortex-M4): $C_{\text{entry}} = 12$ cycles

### Step 1: Express the Time per Clock Cycle

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{168 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{seconds}
$$

### Step 2: Convert to Nanoseconds

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{clk}} &= \frac{10^{9}}{168 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{ns} \\
&= \frac{1000}{168}\,\text{ns} \\
&\approx 5.952\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Total Interrupt Entry Latency

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = C_{\text{entry}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{latency}} &= 12 \times \frac{1000}{168}\,\text{ns} \\
&= \frac{12000}{168}\,\text{ns} \\
&= 71.43\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compare with the Deadline

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = 71.43\,\text{ns} \;\ll\; T_{\text{deadline}} = 200\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Safety Margin

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{latency}}}{T_{\text{deadline}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= \frac{200 - 71.43}{200} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{128.57}{200} \times 100\% \\
&= 64.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{latency}} \approx 71.43\,\text{ns} \;\text{with a}\; 64.29\%\;\text{safety margin}}
$$

The Cortex-M4 easily meets the 200 ns deadline, with substantial headroom for additional bus accesses and instruction fetches inside the ISR.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Vector Table — Exception Numbers vs. Priorities

| Exception # | Exception Name | Priority (Cortex-M4 default) | Bit Position in NVIC |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Reset | –3 (highest, fixed) | N/A |
| 2 | NMI | –2 (fixed) | N/A |
| 3 | HardFault | –1 (fixed) | N/A |
| 4 | MemManage | Configurable | 0 |
| 5 | BusFault | Configurable | 1 |
| 6 | UsageFault | Configurable | 2 |
| 11 | SVCall | Configurable | 7 |
| 12 | DebugMonitor | Configurable | 8 |
| 14 | PendSV | Configurable | 10 |
| 15 | SysTick | Configurable | 11 |
| 16+ | IRQ0…IRQ$n$ | Configurable | 12+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower priority number = Higher logical priority.** Reset is the most privileged (priority –3), and user-configurable IRQs default to a higher numeric value (lower priority).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Exception Entry Flow

When a peripheral asserts an interrupt:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:}\quad \text{Peripheral sets IRQ pending bit in NVIC.} \\
&\text{Step 2:}\quad \text{NVIC arbitrates against current priority.} \\
&\text{Step 3:}\quad \text{CPU completes current instruction.} \\
&\text{Step 4:}\quad \text{Hardware stacks } \{R0,R1,R2,R3,R12,LR,PC,xPSR\}. \\
&\text{Step 5:}\quad \text{PC} = *(\text{VTOR} + 4 \times N). \\
&\text{Step 6:}\quad \text{LR} = 0x\text{FFFF FFFF}_X \text{ (return marker)}. \\
&\text{Step 7:}\quad \text{IPSR} = N. \\
&\text{Step 8:}\quad \text{Handler mode entered; MSP used.}
\end{aligned}
$$
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cortex-M CPU Register File — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GPR["GENERAL PURPOSE REGISTERS"]
        R0["R0 - ARG0"]
        R1["R1 - ARG1"]
        R2["R2 - ARG2"]
        R3["R3 - ARG3"]
        R4["R4 - VAR1"]
        R5["R5 - VAR2"]
        R6["R6 - VAR3"]
        R7["R7 - VAR4"]
        R8["R8 - V5"]
        R9["R9 - V6"]
        R10["R10 - V7"]
        R11["R11 - V8"]
        R12["R12 - IP"]
    end

    subgraph SPGRP["STACK POINTERS"]
        MSP["R13 - MSP - KERNEL STACK"]
        PSP["R13 - PSP - USER STACK"]
    end

    LR["R14 - LR - LINK REGISTER"]
    PC["R15 - PC - PROGRAM COUNTER"]

    subgraph SPSR["SPECIAL PSR REGISTERS"]
        APSR["APSR - N Z C V FLAGS"]
        IPSR["IPSR - EXCEPTION NUMBER"]
        EPSR["EPSR - THUMB STATE"]
        XPSR["xPSR - COMBINED VIEW"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL REGISTERS"]
        PRIM["PRIMASK"]
        FMASK["FAULTMASK"]
        BPRI["BASEPRI"]
        CTL["CONTROL - SP SEL / PRIV"]
    end

    R0 --> CPUCORE["CPU CORE PIPELINE"]
    R1 --> CPUCORE
    R12 --> CPUCORE
    MSP --> CPUCORE
    PSP --> CPUCORE
    LR --> CPUCORE
    PC --> CPUCORE
    XPSR --> CPUCORE
    CTL --> CPUCORE
    PRIM --> CPUCORE
    FMASK --> CPUCORE
    BPRI --> CPUCORE
```

---

## 4.2 Vector Table Layout — Memory-Map Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["MEMORY MAP - VECTOR TABLE BASE 0x08000000"]
        E0["OFFSET 0x00 - INITIAL MSP VALUE"]
        E1["OFFSET 0x04 - RESET VECTOR - PC ENTRY"]
        E2["OFFSET 0x08 - NMI HANDLER"]
        E3["OFFSET 0x0C - HARDFAULT HANDLER"]
        E4["OFFSET 0x10 - MEMMANAGE FAULT"]
        E5["OFFSET 0x14 - BUSFAULT"]
        E6["OFFSET 0x18 - USAGEFAULT"]
        E11["OFFSET 0x2C - SVCALL"]
        E12["OFFSET 0x30 - DEBUG MONITOR"]
        E14["OFFSET 0x38 - PENDSV - RTOS CONTEXT SWITCH"]
        E15["OFFSET 0x3C - SYSTICK HANDLER"]
        E16["OFFSET 0x40 - EXTERNAL IRQ0"]
        E17["OFFSET 0x44 - EXTERNAL IRQ1"]
        E18["OFFSET 0x48 - EXTERNAL IRQ2"]
        EDOTS["... 4 BYTES PER ENTRY ..."]
        ELAST["OFFSET BASE + 4*N - LAST IRQ"]
    end

    E0 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5 --> E6 --> E11 --> E12 --> E14 --> E15 --> E16 --> E17 --> E18 --> EDOTS --> ELAST

    VTOR["VTOR REGISTER - RELOCATABLE BASE"] -. POINTS TO .-> E0
```

---

## 4.3 Exception Entry & Stacking — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["PERIPHERAL ASSERTS IRQ SIGNAL"]
    NVIC_ARB{"NVIC ARBITRATION - HIGHER PRIORITY?"}
    STACK["HARDWARE STACKING 8 REGISTERS ONTO MSP"]
    READ_VT["READ VECTOR ADDRESS FROM VTOR + 4*N"]
    LOAD_PC["LOAD HANDLER ADDRESS INTO PC"]
    SET_LR["LR = 0xFFFFFFFx RETURN MARKER"]
    SET_MODE["ENTER HANDLER MODE - PRIVILEGED"]
    EXEC["EXECUTE ISR BODY"]
    RET_INSTR["EXCEPTION RETURN - SPECIAL BX LR"]
    UNSTACK["HARDWARE UNSTACKING RESTORES CONTEXT"]
    RESUME["RESUME INTERRUPTED CODE"]

    START --> NVIC_ARB
    NVIC_ARB -- YES --> STACK
    NVIC_ARB -- NO --> WAIT["WAIT / REMAIN IN CURRENT ISR"]
    STACK --> READ_VT
    READ_VT --> LOAD_PC
    LOAD_PC --> SET_LR
    SET_LR --> SET_MODE
    SET_MODE --> EXEC
    EXEC --> RET_INSTR
    RET_INSTR --> UNSTACK
    UNSTACK --> RESUME
```

---

## 4.4 CONTROL Register Bit-Field Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL_BITS["CONTROL REGISTER - 32 BITS"]
        B0["BIT 0 - SPSEL - STACK POINTER SELECT - 0=MSP, 1=PSP"]
        B1["BIT 1 - nPRIV - PRIVILEGE - 0=PRIVILEGED, 1=UNPRIVILEGED"]
        B2["BITS 2-31 - RESERVED - MUST BE ZERO"]
    end

    B0 --> NOTE1["CHOOSES BETWEEN MSP AND PSP IN THREAD MODE"]
    B1 --> NOTE2["IN HANDLER MODE, ALWAYS PRIVILEGED REGARDLESS OF nPRIV"]
    B2 --> NOTE3["WRITING NON-ZERO TO RESERVED BITS CAUSES USAGEFAULT"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VTOR Relocation Use Case:** When a bootloader jumps to an application at address `0x0800 8000`, it must set `SCB->VTOR = 0x0800 8000` **before** the first instruction of the application executes, otherwise the CPU will fetch the Reset vector from the wrong location and the application will crash.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any six registers of the ARM Cortex-M processor and state the function of the Link Register (LR).**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM Cortex-M processor contains 13 general-purpose registers (R0–R12) plus several special-purpose registers. Six important registers are:

1. **R0–R3:** Argument and scratch registers per AAPCS.
2. **R4–R11:** Callee-saved registers for local variables.
3. **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer, with two physical instances: MSP and PSP.
4. **R14 (LR):** Link Register — holds the return address.
5. **R15 (PC):** Program Counter — points to the next instruction.
6. **xPSR:** Combined Program Status Register containing APSR, IPSR, and EPSR.

**Function of LR (R14):** The Link Register holds the **return address** of a subroutine call after a `BL` (Branch with Link) or `BLX` instruction. In exception handling, LR is loaded with a special encoded value `0xFFFF FFFFₓ` that the hardware uses to perform an **exception return** (unstacking). The `x` nibble encodes the stack pointer selection and mode return.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a vector table in the ARM Cortex-M architecture? Where is it located and what does its first entry contain?**

**Model Answer:**

A **vector table** is a predefined array of 32-bit words stored at the base of the system memory map that the CPU uses to locate exception and interrupt handlers.

- **Location:** By default at address `0x0000 0000`. It is **relocatable** at runtime using the **Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR)** to a base such as `0x0800 0000` (Flash) or `0x2000 0000` (SRAM).
- **Structure:** The first entry (offset `0x00`) contains the **initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** value, and the second entry (offset `0x04`) contains the **Reset vector** — the address where the CPU begins execution after power-on or reset.
- The remaining entries contain the addresses of the NMI, HardFault, SVCall, PendSV, SysTick, and all external interrupt handlers.
- Each entry occupies **4 bytes**, and the handler address is computed as: $\text{VTOR} + 4 \times \text{Exception\_Number}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks: Explain the CPU register organization of the ARM Cortex-M processor with a neat block diagram.

**Model Solution:**

The ARM Cortex-M CPU register file consists of:

**1. General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12):**
- **R0–R7 (Low Registers):** Accessible by all 16-bit Thumb instructions. R0–R3 serve as argument/scratch registers in the AAPCS; R4–R11 are callee-saved and used for local variable preservation across function calls.
- **R8–R12 (High Registers):** Accessible only by 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. R12 (IP) is reserved as a scratch register between subroutine calls.

**2. Special-Purpose Registers:**
- **R13 (SP) — Stack Pointer:** Has two physical instances:
  - **MSP (Main Stack Pointer):** Used in Handler mode and on reset; serves the OS kernel.
  - **PSP (Process Stack Pointer):** Used in Thread mode for application code; facilitates RTOS context switching.
- **R14 (LR) — Link Register:** Stores the return address from subroutine calls (`BL`/`BLX`). In exception entry, it is loaded with the special value `0xFFFF FFFFₓ` for exception return.
- **R15 (PC) — Program Counter:** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch. In Thumb mode, PC is current instruction address + 2.
- **xPSR — Combined Program Status Register:** The logical combination of:
  - **APSR (Application PSR):** Holds condition flags — N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow).
  - **IPSR (Interrupt PSR):** Holds the exception number of the current ISR.
  - **EPSR (Execution PSR):** Holds the Thumb state bit (T) and ICI/IT bits.

**3. Interrupt-Masking Registers:**
- **PRIMASK:** 1-bit mask; when set, blocks all configurable exceptions.
- **FAULTMASK:** 1-bit mask; when set, blocks all exceptions except NMI.
- **BASEPRI:** Threshold register; masks all exceptions with priority numerically higher (lower urgency) than its value.

**4. CONTROL Register:**
- **Bit 0 (SPSEL):** Selects SP in Thread mode (0 = MSP, 1 = PSP).
- **Bit 1 (nPRIV):** Sets privilege level in Thread mode (0 = Privileged, 1 = Unprivileged).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOREG["LOW REGISTERS R0-R7"]
        R0["R0 - ARG0 / SCRATCH"]
        R1["R1 - ARG1 / SCRATCH"]
        R2["R2 - ARG2 / SCRATCH"]
        R3["R3 - ARG3 / SCRATCH"]
        R4["R4 - CALLEE SAVED"]
        R5["R5 - CALLEE SAVED"]
        R6["R6 - CALLEE SAVED"]
        R7["R7 - CALLEE SAVED"]
    end

    subgraph HIREG["HIGH REGISTERS R8-R12"]
        R8["R8 - V5"]
        R9["R9 - V6"]
        R10["R10 - V7"]
        R11["R11 - V8"]
        R12["R12 - IP SCRATCH"]
    end

    SPRG["R13 SP - DUAL BANKED - MSP / PSP"]
    LRR["R14 LR - LINK REGISTER"]
    PCR["R15 PC - PROGRAM COUNTER"]
    XPSR["xPSR - APSR + IPSR + EPSR"]
    PRIM["PRIMASK - 1 BIT"]
    FMASK["FAULTMASK - 1 BIT"]
    BPRI["BASEPRI - 8 BITS"]
    CTL["CONTROL - SPSEL / nPRIV"]

    CORE["CPU CORE - THUMB-2 PIPELINE"]

    R0 --> CORE
    R12 --> CORE
    SPRG --> CORE
    LRR --> CORE
    PCR --> CORE
    XPSR --> CORE
    PRIM --> CORE
    FMASK --> CORE
    BPRI --> CORE
    CTL --> CORE
```

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks: Describe the vector table of ARM Cortex-M in detail. Explain the exception entry process.

**Model Solution:**

**Vector Table Structure:**

The vector table is a **contiguous array of 32-bit words** located at a configurable base address (default `0x0000 0000`, typically relocated to Flash start in real devices). The first 16 entries are architecturally fixed for system exceptions, and entries 16 onward hold external interrupts.

The address of any vector $N$ is:

$$
\text{Vector\_Addr}(N) = \text{VTOR} + 4 \times N
$$

| Offset | Vector # | Exception | Purpose |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| `0x00` | 0 | Initial MSP | Reset value of Main Stack Pointer |
| `0x04` | 1 | Reset | Entry point of firmware on reset |
| `0x08` | 2 | NMI | Non-Maskable Interrupt handler |
| `0x0C` | 3 | HardFault | Catch-all fault handler |
| `0x10` | 4 | MemManage | MPU fault |
| `0x14` | 5 | BusFault | Bus error |
| `0x18` | 6 | UsageFault | Undefined instruction, etc. |
| `0x2C` | 11 | SVCall | OS system call |
| `0x38` | 14 | PendSV | RTOS context switch |
| `0x3C` | 15 | SysTick | OS tick |
| `0x40+` | 16+ | IRQ0, IRQ1, … | External peripherals |

**Exception Entry Process:**

1. **Peripheral asserts IRQ:** A peripheral sets its interrupt pending bit in the NVIC.
2. **Priority arbitration:** The NVIC compares the new request's priority with the currently executing exception. If higher, preemption occurs.
3. **Current instruction completes:** The CPU finishes the instruction in progress.
4. **Hardware stacking:** The CPU automatically pushes 8 registers onto the active stack (MSP in handler mode):
   $$\{R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR\}$$
5. **PC load:** The CPU reads the 32-bit handler address from `VTOR + 4 × N` and loads it into the PC.
6. **LR encoding:** LR is set to the special return marker `0xFFFF FFFFₓ`.
7. **IPSR update:** IPSR is set to the exception number.
8. **Mode transition:** The CPU enters **Handler mode** (always Privileged, always using MSP).
9. **ISR execution:** The handler runs; on completion, it triggers exception return via a special `BX LR` or `POP` instruction.
10. **Hardware unstacking:** The 8 registers are automatically restored, and the original program resumes seamlessly.

This **deterministic entry** with known stacking overhead (12 cycles on Cortex-M4) is why ARM Cortex-M is the industry standard for real-time embedded systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks: With neat diagrams, explain the operating modes and access levels of the ARM Cortex-M processor. How does the CONTROL register manage them?

**Model Solution:**

**Operating Modes:**

The Cortex-M supports **two processor modes**:

1. **Thread Mode:** Used for normal program execution. Can run with either Privileged or Unprivileged access and can use either the MSP or PSP.
2. **Handler Mode:** Used during exception handling. **Always Privileged** and **always uses the MSP**. Cannot use the PSP.

**Access Levels (Privilege):**

1. **Privileged Access:** Full access to all memory regions and special registers. The CPU is in this state on reset, in Handler mode, or in Thread mode with `CONTROL.nPRIV = 0`.
2. **Unprivileged Access:** Restricted access — writes to NVIC, system control, and protected memory regions are blocked, triggering a HardFault. Used for application sandboxing.

The state space is the Cartesian product:

$$
\{\text{Thread, Handler}\} \times \{\text{Privileged, Unprivileged}\}
$$

**State Transition Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset: POWER ON
    Reset --> ThreadPriv: CONTROL = 0x0 - MSP - PRIVILEGED
    ThreadPriv --> ThreadUnpriv: Set CONTROL.nPRIV = 1
    ThreadUnpriv --> ThreadPriv: Clear CONTROL.nPRIV - PRIVILEGED ONLY
    ThreadPriv --> HandlerPriv: EXCEPTION TAKEN
    ThreadUnpriv --> HandlerPriv: EXCEPTION TAKEN - AUTO PRIV UPGRADE
    HandlerPriv --> ThreadPriv: EXCEPTION RETURN - LR MODE BITS
    HandlerPriv --> ThreadUnpriv: EXCEPTION RETURN TO UNPRIV - LR MODE BITS
    HandlerPriv --> [*]: RESET
```

**CONTROL Register Management:**

The **CONTROL** register is a 32-bit special register with two active bits:

| Bit | Name | Reset Value | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 0 | **SPSEL** | 0 | Stack Pointer select in Thread mode: `0 = MSP`, `1 = PSP` |
| 1 | **nPRIV** | 0 | Privilege level in Thread mode: `0 = Privileged`, `1 = Unprivileged` |
| 2–31 | Reserved | 0 | Must be written as zero (else UsageFault) |

**Rules:**
- CONTROL is **only writable in Privileged mode**. Unprivileged writes are silently ignored.
- In Handler mode, SPSEL is **forced to 0** (MSP) regardless of CONTROL value.
- A typical RTOS design leaves SPSEL=0 in the kernel (Handler mode always uses MSP) and switches SPSEL=1 when entering user tasks (Thread mode with PSP).

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks: Explain the role of VTOR and compute the address of the **PendSV** handler if VTOR = 0x0800 0000.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR):**

VTOR is a 32-bit system control register at address `0xE000 ED08` in the System Control Block (SCB). It holds the **base address** of the vector table in memory. Its principal functions are:

1. **Relocation of the vector table** from the default address `0x0000 0000` to a different memory region (typically Flash at `0x0800 0000` in STM32 devices, or to SRAM in bootloader scenarios).
2. **Bootloader support:** A bootloader at `0x0800 0000` can set `SCB->VTOR = 0x0800 8000` before jumping to the application, ensuring the CPU reads the application's vector table rather than the bootloader's.
3. **Runtime vector remapping:** Allows RTOS designs to use multiple vector tables or relocate the table to SRAM for faster access.
4. **Alignment requirement:** VTOR must be aligned to a boundary equal to or greater than the vector table size, typically a power of 2 (e.g., 128-byte aligned for 32 vectors).

**Numerical Computation:**

We are given:
- $\text{VTOR} = 0x0800\,0000$
- Exception number for **PendSV:** $N = 14$
- Entry size: $4$ bytes

**Step 1: Apply the vector address formula**

$$
\text{Vector\_Addr}(N) = \text{VTOR} + 4 \times N
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
\text{Vector\_Addr}(14) = 0x08000000 + 4 \times 14
$$

**Step 3: Compute the offset**

$$
4 \times 14 = 56_{10} = 0x38
$$

**Step 4: Add to base**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vector\_Addr}(\text{PendSV}) &= 0x08000000 + 0x00000038 \\
&= 0x08000038
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{\text{PendSV handler address} = 0x0800\,0038}
$$

The CPU will fetch the 32-bit value stored at this address and load it into the PC when a PendSV exception is taken.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting that MSP has two banks:** Many students write "R13 is the Stack Pointer" without distinguishing **MSP** and **PSP**. KTU examiners specifically award partial credit for naming both physical SPs. Always state: *"R13 is banked into MSP and PSP."*
> 
> 2. **Wrong exception numbers:** Students often confuse the order — Initial SP is **vector 0**, Reset is **vector 1**, not the other way around. Memorize the table: `(0) MSP, (1) Reset, (2) NMI, (3) HardFault`.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the LR encoding in exceptions:** Writing "LR stores the return address" in an exception context is incomplete. State explicitly that LR holds the **special value `0xFFFF FFFFₓ`**, which the hardware uses to perform exception return.
> 
> 4. **Off-by-one in vector table math:** The offset is `4 × N`, **not** `4 × (N+1)`. For SysTick (N=15), the offset is `0x3C = 60`, not `0x40`.
> 
> 5. **Confusing priority:** Lower number = higher logical priority. A priority of `0x01` is **more urgent** than `0xFF`. Writing the opposite loses marks in numerical questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CPU and Vector Table**

- ✅ The Cortex-M CPU has **13 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0–R12)**, plus the special **SP (R13)**, **LR (R14)**, and **PC (R15)**.
- ✅ **R13 is banked** into two physical Stack Pointers: **MSP** (kernel) and **PSP** (application).
- ✅ The **xPSR** combines **APSR** (flags), **IPSR** (exception number), and **EPSR** (Thumb state).
- ✅ **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, and BASEPRI** control interrupt enable/disable granularly.
- ✅ The **CONTROL** register's two active bits are **SPSEL** (SP select) and **nPRIV** (privilege).
- ✅ The Cortex-M has **two modes** (Thread, Handler) and **two access levels** (Privileged, Unprivileged).
- ✅ **Handler mode is always Privileged and always uses MSP**, regardless of CONTROL.
- ✅ The **vector table** is a 32-bit-word array starting at the address held in **VTOR** (default `0x0000 0000`).
- ✅ **Vector 0 = Initial MSP**, **Vector 1 = Reset**, **Vector 2 = NMI**, **Vector 3 = HardFault**.
- ✅ The formula for any vector address is: $\text{VTOR} + 4 \times N$.
- ✅ The CPU automatically **stacks 8 registers** {R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR} on exception entry.
- ✅ Exception entry takes **12 cycles** on Cortex-M4 — this is the **deterministic latency** that makes it real-time.
- ✅ On exception entry, **LR = 0xFFFF FFFFₓ** (special return marker, not a normal address).
- ✅ **Lower priority number = Higher logical priority** (priority `0x00` is most urgent, `0xFF` is least).
- ✅ The **VTOR register** allows the vector table to be relocated — essential for bootloaders and RTOS designs.
- ✅ **SysTick** is vector 15, **PendSV** is vector 14, **SVCall** is vector 11 — these are critical for RTOS tick and context switching.
- ✅ The Cortex-M uses **Thumb-2** instructions: PC increments by **2** bytes per instruction in Thumb mode.
- ✅ **Tail-chaining** (a Cortex-M optimization) reduces interrupt-to-interrupt latency to just **6 cycles** when back-to-back exceptions occur.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C Run-Time Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** C Run-Time Environment # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the C Run-Time Environment? The **C Run-Time Environment (RTE)** refers to the underlying software and hardware infrastructure that supports the execution of a C program on a target processor. In the context of ARM architecture, it specifically defines how the compiler, linker, assembler, and the ARM hardware cooperate to manage memory, registers, stack frames, and procedure calls during program execution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The C Run-Time Environment is the collection of registers, memory segments, stack conventions, and procedure call standards (such as AAPCS for ARM) that govern how a compiled C program is loaded into memory, how its functions are invoked, how arguments are passed, how return values are handled, and how local variables and machine state are preserved during execution. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** preparing multiple dishes (functions) simultaneously: - **The Head Chef (CPU)** can only work on one dish at a time, following a recipe card (instruction stream). - **The Order Ticket Board (Stack)** holds pending orders with the most recent one on top — exactly like the function call stack, where the most recently called function executes first. - **Standardized Containers (Registers r0–r12)** are reused for every new dish, just like ARM registers being volatile across procedure calls. - **The Storage Pantry (Heap + Data segments)** holds ingredients that persist across many orders. - **The Recipe Standard (AAPCS)** ensures every chef knows which container holds which spice — a calling convention everyone obeys. Just as a kitchen cannot operate without these conventions, a C program cannot run on ARM without a clearly defined run-time environment. ## 1.3 Why is the C Run-Time Environment Important in ARM? > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU PEECT524 Module 4:** > Understanding the C Run-Time Environment is essential because it bridges the gap between **high-level C constructs** (functions, local variables, recursion) and the **low-level ARM hardware** (registers, memory, stack pointer). Without this knowledge, embedded systems programmers cannot write efficient ARM assembly, debug stack-related crashes, or interface C with assembly routines. ### Key Components of the C Run-Time Environment 1. **Memory Layout / Segments** — Text, Data, BSS, Heap, Stack 2. **ARM Register Conventions** — Role of r0–r15 3. **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)** — Calling rules 4. **Stack Frame / Activation Record** — Function prolog and epilog 5. **Parameter Passing Mechanism** — Registers vs Stack 6. **Return Value Handling** — Convention for return types 7. **Linker & Loader Support** — Runtime startup code ## 1.4 Standard ARM Register Roles (At a Glance) The table below gives a snapshot of the **15 general-purpose registers (r0–r12)** plus the **3 special registers (r13–r15)** that are central to the run-time environment. | Register | Alias | Role in C Run-Time | |----------|-------|--------------------| | r0 | a1 | Argument 1 / Return value (low) | | r1 | a2 | Argument 2 / Return value (high) | | r2 | a3 | Argument 3 | | r3 | a4 | Argument 4 | | r4–r10 | v1–v8 | Variable registers (must be preserved by callee) | | r11 | fp | Frame Pointer (optional in ARM) | | r12 | ip | Intra-procedure scratch | | r13 | sp | **Stack Pointer** — points to top of stack | | r14 | lr | **Link Register** — return address | | r15 | pc | **Program Counter** | > [!IMPORTANT] > Under the **AAPCS standard**, registers **r0–r3 and r12 are caller-saved (volatile)**, while **r4–r11 are callee-saved (non-volatile)**. This is a *contract* between caller and callee and is exam-favorite content in KTU Module 4. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Memory Layout of a C Program at Run-Time When a C program is loaded into ARM memory, the OS/loader arranges it into **five distinct logical segments**.
