Each segment has a specific purpose, fixed (or semi-fixed) location, and a different lifetime. ### Segment-by-Segment Breakdown 1. **Text Segment (Code Segment)** - Contains the **machine code instructions** compiled from C source. - Marked **read-only** to prevent accidental self-modification. - Shared among multiple instances of the same program. - Lifetime: **Entire program duration**. 2. **Initialized Data Segment** - Stores **global and static variables** that are explicitly initialized. - Example: `int count = 100;` (global). - Read-Write access. - Lifetime: **Entire program duration**. 3. **BSS Segment (Block Started by Symbol)** - Stores **uninitialized global and static variables**. - The OS initializes it to **zero** at load time (saving binary size). - Example: `int buffer[1024];` (global). - Lifetime: **Entire program duration**. 4. **Heap Segment** - Used for **dynamic memory allocation** (`malloc`, `calloc`, `free`). - Grows **upward** (towards higher addresses) in ARM Linux. - Managed by the C library allocator (e.g., `dlmalloc`, `ptmalloc`). - Lifetime: **From allocation until free()**. 5. **Stack Segment** - Used for **function call frames, return addresses, local variables, saved registers**. - Grows **downward** (towards lower addresses) in ARM. - The Stack Pointer (r13/sp) always points to the **topmost filled location**. - Lifetime: **Per function call**. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic (low to high address on ARM Linux):** > `Text → Data → BSS → Heap (grows ↑) … Free Space … Stack (grows ↓)` > A common exam question: *"Which direction does the stack grow in ARM?"* — Answer: **Downward (towards lower addresses)**. ## 2.2 The AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) The **AAPCS** is the *de facto* standard governing how functions exchange data, preserve state, and return results in ARM. It is part of the **ABI (Application Binary Interface)** for the ARM Architecture (AAELF). ### AAPCS Core Rules (Exam-Oriented) - **Rule 1 — Parameter Passing:** - The first **four scalar arguments** are passed in **r0, r1, r2, r3** (in that order). - Additional arguments (5th onwards) are pushed onto the **stack**, right-to-left. - 64-bit values (e.g., `long long`, `double`) use **r0+r1** or **r2+r3** as a register pair. - **Rule 2 — Return Value:** - Up to a 4-byte value is returned in **r0**. - An 8-byte value is returned in **r0 (low word)** and **r1 (high word)**. - Larger structures are returned via memory pointed to by an implicit first argument (in r0). - **Rule 3 — Register Preservation (Callee-Saved):** - Registers **r4–r11** must be preserved by the callee. If used, the callee must save them on entry and restore them before return. - Registers **r0–r3 and r12** are **caller-saved** (volatile). The caller must save them if needed across a call. - **Rule 4 — Stack Alignment:** - At a public function entry, the **stack pointer (sp) must be 8-byte aligned**. - The ARM ABI requires this alignment for correct operation of `SPE` and `NEON` instructions. - **Rule 5 — Leaf Procedures:** - A *leaf* function (one that does not call other functions) need not push lr, but **must** maintain AAPCS rules for any registers it modifies. ## 2.3 Stack Frame / Activation Record Structure Each function call creates a **stack frame** (also called an *activation record*) on the run-time stack. The frame is constructed in the **prolog** and dismantled in the **epilog**. ### Anatomy of a Typical ARM Stack Frame | Component | Purpose | |-----------|---------| | Previous Frame Pointer | Saved value of r11 (fp) of the caller — links frames | | Saved Link Register | Pushed lr so the callee can return to caller | | Saved Callee-Saved Registers | Push registers r4–r11 if used | | Local Variables | Space for `auto` variables declared in the function | | Spilled Arguments | Space for outgoing arguments (5th and beyond) | | Function Parameters | If more than 4 parameters, additional ones on stack | ### Prolog and Epilog (Pseudocode for ARM) **Prolog (Function Entry):** ``` PUSH {r11, lr} ; save frame pointer and return address MOV r11, sp ; set up new frame pointer PUSH {r4-r10} ; save callee-saved registers (if used) SUB sp, sp, #N ; allocate space for locals (N bytes) ``` **Epilog (Function Exit):** ``` MOV sp, r11 ; restore stack pointer from frame pointer POP {r11, pc} ; restore frame pointer and return (pop lr into pc) ; or POP {r4-r10} ; restore callee-saved registers POP {r11, pc} ; and return ``` ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains high-yield facts and pseudo-formulas that frequently appear in KTU Module 4 questions. | Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes | |---------|----------------|-------| | Stack growth direction (ARM) | **Downward** (toward 0) | r13 (sp) decreases on PUSH | | First 4 arguments | `r0, r1, r2, r3` | Integer/pointer/char types | | 5th+ arguments | **Stack** (right-to-left) | Pushed by caller | | Return value (≤4 bytes) | `r0` | Includes char, short, int, pointer | | Return value (8 bytes) | `r0` (low) + `r1` (high) | `long long`, `double` | | Caller-saved registers | `r0–r3, r12` | Volatile across calls | | Callee-saved registers | `r4–r11` | Must be preserved | | Stack pointer | `r13` (sp) | Always points to top of stack | | Link register | `r14` (lr) | Holds return address | | Program counter | `r15` (pc) | Current instruction address + 8 (in ARM mode) | | Stack alignment at public entry | **8 bytes** | Required by AAPCS | | Function prolog sequence | `push {r11, lr}; mov r11, sp; push {r4-r10}; sub sp, sp, #N` | Standard skeleton | | Function epilog sequence | `mov sp, r11; pop {r11, pc}` | Pops lr directly into pc | | Heap growth direction (ARM Linux) | **Upward** | `brk`/`mmap` | | BSS initial value | **0** | Zero-initialized by loader | > [!NOTE] > In ARM **state**, the PC reads as `current_instruction + 8` (because of pipelining). This explains why `lr = pc - 4` and why branches adjust the offset by 4. In **Thumb** state, the read value is `current_instruction + 4`. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the C Run-Time Environment - **Embedded Systems Development:** Writing ARM assembly stubs, ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines), and bootloader code requires knowledge of AAPCS to interface with C libraries. - **Debugging Stack Overflows:** Understanding stack frame size helps compute maximum recursion depth and avoid hard-faults in MCU applications. - **Reverse Engineering & Security:** Buffer overflow exploits depend critically on knowing the stack layout, saved-lr location, and AAPCS calling conventions. - **Performance Optimization:** Profiling tools (perf, Valgrind, ARM Streamline) rely on frame-pointer unwinding, which only works if the AAPCS is honored. - **OS Kernel Development:** Context switching in RTOS (FreeRTOS, Zephyr) requires saving r4–r11 and lr on every context switch. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mapping a C Function to an ARM Stack Frame Consider the following C function: ```c int add(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e) { int sum; sum = a + b + c + d + e; return sum; } ``` ### Step 1: Identify the AAPCS Parameter Passing - **Arguments a, b, c, d** → passed in **r0, r1, r2, r3** respectively. - **Argument e** (5th argument) → passed on the **stack** (pushed by the caller before the call). ### Step 2: Identify the Return Mechanism - The return type is `int` (4 bytes) → returned in **r0**. ### Step 3: Show the Caller's Responsibilities Before invoking `add`, the caller must: 1. Place `a, b, c, d` into `r0, r1, r2, r3`. 2. Place `e` onto the stack. 3. Issue a `BL add` instruction (Branch with Link), which stores the return address in `lr`. ### Step 4: Show the Callee's Prolog (ARM Assembly) ``` add: PUSH {r11, lr} ; Step A — Save frame pointer and link register MOV r11, sp ; Step B — Establish new frame pointer PUSH {r4} ; Step C — Save callee-saved register (none used here, but for illustration) SUB sp, sp, #8 ; Step D — Reserve 8 bytes for local 'sum' (with 8-byte alignment) ``` > **Explanation of each step:** > - **Step A:** `r11` (frame pointer) and `lr` (return address) must be saved because `r11` is callee-saved and `lr` will be overwritten if `add` calls any other function. > - **Step B:** Setting `r11 = sp` creates a stable reference to the bottom of this frame; the callee can later access `e` and saved-lr using `r11`-relative addressing. > - **Step C:** Any `r4–r10` used by the callee must be saved. In this simple function, none are used, so this push is omitted in real code. > - **Step D:** Allocates space for the local variable `sum`. ARM AAPCS requires 8-byte stack alignment at public function entry. ### Step 5: Show the Function Body ``` LDR r4, [r11, #0] ; Load 5th argument 'e' from stack (offset relative to fp) ADD r0, r0, r1 ; a + b → r0 ADD r0, r0, r2 ; + c → r0 ADD r0, r0, r3 ; + d → r0 ADD r0, r0, r4 ; + e → r0 ; r0 now holds the result ``` > **Explanation:** Note that the first 4 parameters were already in `r0–r3` upon entry (by AAPCS rule), and `e` is read from the stack via frame-pointer-relative addressing. The accumulator pattern keeps the running sum in `r0` since that is also the return register. ### Step 6: Show the Callee's Epilog ``` MOV sp, r11 ; Restore stack pointer (deallocates locals) POP {r11, pc} ; Restore fp; return to caller (pop lr → pc) ``` > **Explanation:** Popping `{r11, pc}` atomically restores the frame pointer **and** triggers a return. The `pc` receives the saved `lr` value, effectively executing `bx lr`. This is a common ARM idiom that avoids an extra `BX lr` instruction. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing Maximum Stack Depth for Recursive Factorial Consider: ```c int factorial(int n) { if (n <= 1) return 1; return n * factorial(n - 1); } ``` ### Step 1: Determine the Frame Size Each call to `factorial` requires: - 4 bytes for saved `lr` - 4 bytes for saved `r11` (fp) - Possible spill of argument `n` (held in r0 across the recursive call) - 8-byte alignment padding A typical frame size = **16 bytes** (4 for lr, 4 for r11, 8 for alignment/locals). ### Step 2: Derive the Maximum Stack Depth For a recursive call with input `n = 10`: $$ \text{Maximum frames} = n + 1 \quad \text{(base case adds 1)} $$ $$ \text{Maximum frames} = 10 + 1 = 11 $$ ### Step 3: Compute the Maximum Stack Memory Used $$ \text{Total stack memory} = \text{frames} \times \text{frame\_size} $$ $$ \text{Total stack memory} = 11 \times 16 \text{ bytes} = 176 \text{ bytes} $$ > **Note:** This is the *static worst-case* bound. In ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers with a 4 KB stack, this is trivial, but in deeply recursive algorithms (e.g., Fibonacci), this can overflow the stack and cause a hard fault. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Leaf vs Non-Leaf Functions The AAPCS treats *leaf* and *non-leaf* functions differently, and KTU exam questions often test this distinction. | Aspect | Leaf Function | Non-Leaf Function | |--------|---------------|-------------------| | Calls other functions? | **No** | **Yes** | | Must push `lr`? | No (lr not overwritten) | **Yes** (lr will be clobbered by `BL`) | | Must push r4–r11? | Only if used | Only if used | | Spill r0–r3 before call? | No calls to make | **Yes**, if values needed after the call | | Typical prolog | `push {r4-r10}` (if used) | `push {r11, lr}; mov r11, sp; push {r4-r10}` | | Typical epilog | `pop {r4-r10}; bx lr` | `mov sp, r11; pop {r11, pc}` | ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Tracing Stack Pointer Changes Given the following ARM code: ``` BL funcA ; sp before = 0x8000_1000 ``` Suppose `funcA`'s prolog is: ``` PUSH {r11, lr} ; sp -= 8 MOV r11, sp ; r11 = sp PUSH {r4, r5, r6} ; sp -= 12 SUB sp, sp, #16 ; sp -= 16 (locals) ``` ### Step 1: Calculate SP at Each Stage $$ \text{sp}_{\text{after PUSH}\{r11, lr\}} = 0x8000\_1000 - 8 = 0x8000\_0FF8 $$ $$ \text{sp}_{\text{after PUSH}\{r4, r5, r6\}} = 0x8000\_0FF8 - 12 = 0x8000\_0FEC $$ $$ \text{sp}_{\text{after SUB sp, sp, #16}} = 0x8000\_0FEC - 16 = 0x8000\_0FDC $$ ### Step 2: Calculate r11 (Frame Pointer) $$ \text{r11} = \text{sp}_{\text{after PUSH}\{r11, lr\}} = 0x8000\_0FF8 $$ ### Step 3: Verify 8-Byte Alignment $0x8000\_0FF8 \mod 8 = 0$ ✓ (Aligned) $0x8000\_0FEC \mod 8 = 4$ ✗ (Misaligned — would violate AAPCS at this intermediate point, but AAPCS only requires alignment at *public* function entry, so intermediate misalignment is permitted as long as it is realigned before any call.) ## 3.5 Important: Caller-Save vs Callee-Save Register Trade-off The compiler chooses which registers to use for variables based on this trade-off: $$ \text{If variable lifetime} \subseteq \text{caller's body} \Rightarrow \text{use r0–r3, r12 (caller-save)} $$ $$ \text{If variable lifetime} \supseteq \text{call boundary} \Rightarrow \text{use r4–r11 (callee-save)} $$ This is why the **callee-save set is sometimes called "variable registers"** — they are typically used for values that must *survive* function calls. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Layout of a Running C Program on ARM ```mermaid graph TB subgraph HIGH["HIGH ADDRESS (e.g., 0x7FFFFFFF)"] STK["STACK SEGMENT
Grows DOWNWARD
Function frames, return addresses, locals"] ENV["ENV / ARGS
Command-line arguments and environment"] end subgraph MID["FREE / UNMAPPED"] FREE["... Free Space ...
Growth margin for stack/heap"] end subgraph LOW["LOW ADDRESS (e.g., 0x00010000)"] HP["HEAP SEGMENT
Grows UPWARD
malloc/calloc/new"] BSS["BSS SEGMENT
Uninitialized globals (zero-init)"] DAT["DATA SEGMENT
Initialized globals and statics"] TXT["TEXT SEGMENT
Machine code (read-only)"] end STK -. "sp (r13) ↓" .-> FREE FREE -. "brk ↑" .-> HP HP --> BSS BSS --> DAT DAT --> TXT ``` > **Description:** This diagram shows the canonical ARM Linux process memory layout. The stack at the top grows downward (decrementing sp), the heap at the bottom grows upward (incrementing brk). The text, data, and BSS segments are fixed at load time. ## 4.2 Stack Frame Layout During a Function Call ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CALLER["CALLER FRAME (Higher Address)"] CFP["Saved r11 of caller"] CL1["Pushed arguments 5, 6, ..."] end subgraph CALLE["CALLEE FRAME (Lower Address)"] SLR["Saved lr (return address)"] SFP["Saved r11 (old frame pointer)"] SAV["Saved r4 to r10 (callee-saved)"] LOC["Local variables (sum, temporaries)"] SP1["sp (r13) points HERE"] end CFP --> CL1 CL1 --> SLR SLR --> SFP SFP --> SAV SAV --> LOC LOC --> SP1 ``` > **Description:** The diagram maps the typical order of items pushed onto the stack during a non-leaf function's prolog. Notice that **sp points to the lowest (most recently pushed) address**, while the caller's frame lies at higher addresses. Arguments 5+ are pushed *before* the `BL` instruction by the caller. ## 4.3 AAPCS Calling Convention Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Caller prepares arguments 1-4 in r0, r1, r2, r3"] --> B["Caller pushes argument 5+ onto stack (right-to-left)"] B --> C["Caller saves r0-r3 and r12 if needed after call"] C --> D["Caller executes BL funcName
lr ← return address"] D --> E["Callee prolog: PUSH {r11, lr}; MOV r11, sp"] E --> F["Callee saves r4-r10 if used (PUSH)"] F --> G["Callee reserves space for locals (SUB sp, sp, #N)"] G --> H["Callee executes function body"] H --> I["Return value placed in r0 (and r1 for 64-bit)"] I --> J["Callee epilog: MOV sp, r11; POP {r11, pc}"] J --> K["Caller resumes; r0 holds return value"] ``` > **Description:** This flowchart summarizes the entire AAPCS procedure call sequence from caller preparation through callee execution to return. It is a high-yield visual for KTU 14-mark answers on "Explain the C run-time environment with reference to ARM AAPCS." ## 4.4 Register Roles in a Procedure Call ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ARGS["ARGUMENT & RETURN (Caller-Saved)"] R0["r0 / a1 — Arg1 / Return"] R1["r1 / a2 — Arg2 / Return (high)"] R2["r2 / a3 — Arg3"] R3["r3 / a4 — Arg4"] end subgraph SCR["SCRATCH (Caller-Saved)"] R12["r12 / ip — Intra-procedure scratch"] end subgraph VAR["VARIABLE (Callee-Saved)"] R4["r4 / v1"] R5["r5 / v2"] R6["r6 / v3"] R7["r7 / v4 / fp (Thumb)"] R8["r8 / v5"] R9["r9 / v6 / SB (platform)"] R10["r10 / v7 / SL (platform)"] R11["r11 / v8 / fp (ARM mode)"] end subgraph SPEC["SPECIAL (Fixed Role)"] R13["r13 / sp — Stack Pointer"] R14["r14 / lr — Link Register"] R15["r15 / pc — Program Counter"] end ``` > **Description:** This register map distinguishes the three categories of registers: caller-saved (volatile, used for arguments and short-lived values), callee-saved (must be preserved across function calls), and special-purpose (sp, lr, pc) with fixed architectural roles. ## 4.5 Function Prolog–Epilog Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CallerSetup CallerSetup: Caller sets up r0-r3 and stack args CallerSetup --> Call Call: BL funcName (lr ← return addr) Call --> Prolog Prolog: PUSH {r11, lr}; MOV r11, sp Prolog --> SaveRegs SaveRegs: PUSH {r4-r10} (if used) SaveRegs --> AllocLocals AllocLocals: SUB sp, sp, #N AllocLocals --> Body Body: Execute function body Body --> Epilog Epilog: MOV sp, r11; POP {r11, pc} Epilog --> Return Return: Return to caller (r0 = result) Return --> [*] ``` > **Description:** This state machine visualizes the complete lifecycle of a function call, from caller setup through prolog, body, epilog, and return — exactly the sequence a KTU answer should describe for full marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the registers used for passing the first four arguments to a function in ARM according to AAPCS. What register holds the return value of a 4-byte function?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - According to the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, the first four arguments of a function are passed in registers **r0, r1, r2, and r3** respectively. - If the return value of the function is **4 bytes or less** (e.g., `int`, `char`, `pointer`), it is returned in **r0**. - For an 8-byte return value (e.g., `long long`, `double`), the result is returned in **r0 (low word) and r1 (high word)**. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024] **Differentiate between caller-saved and callee-saved registers in the ARM AAPCS. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Caller-saved (volatile) registers:** These registers may be modified by the callee during a function call. The caller must save them (push to stack) if their values are needed after the call. Examples: **r0, r1, r2, r3, r12**. - **Callee-saved (non-volatile) registers:** These registers must be preserved by the callee. If the callee uses them, it must save and restore them. Examples: **r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11**. - This division of responsibility is a contract specified by AAPCS to ensure modular, independently compiled functions interoperate correctly. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **memory layout of a C program** at run-time on an ARM-based system. Describe the purpose of each segment: text, data, BSS, heap, and stack. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **ARM AAPCS procedure call standard**. Describe the roles of r0–r15 during a function call, and illustrate a complete function prolog and epilog. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Memory Layout of a C Program (7 marks):** When a compiled C program is loaded into ARM memory, it is organized into **five logical segments**: 1. **Text Segment:** Holds the **machine code instructions** generated by the compiler. It is marked **read-only** and **executable**. It is shared between multiple instances of the program. Lifetime: entire program duration. 2. **Initialized Data Segment:** Contains **global and static variables** with explicit initial values (e.g., `int g = 5;`). It is read-write. Lifetime: entire program duration. 3. **BSS Segment:** Contains **uninitialized global and static variables** (e.g., `int arr[100];`). The OS initializes this segment to zero at load time, which keeps the executable file small. Lifetime: entire program duration. 4. **Heap Segment:** Used for **dynamic memory allocation** via `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `free()`. The heap grows **upward** (toward higher addresses) in ARM Linux. Lifetime: from `malloc` to `free`. 5. **Stack Segment:** Used for **function call frames**, local variables, return addresses, and saved registers. The stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). The stack pointer (r13/sp) always points to the topmost filled address. Lifetime: per function call. ``` HIGH ADDR ← Stack (grows ↓) ↓ ... free ... ↑ Heap (grows ↑) BSS (uninitialized globals) Data (initialized globals) LOW ADDR → Text (machine code, read-only) ``` **(b) ARM AAPCS Procedure Call Standard (7 marks):** The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** is part of the ARM ABI and defines how functions exchange data in ARM. **Roles of Registers (r0–r15):** - **r0–r3:** Pass the **first four arguments** (a1–a4). r0 also holds the **return value** for ≤4-byte types; r0+r1 hold 8-byte return values. - **r4–r11:** **Callee-saved (variable) registers** used for long-lived values. - **r12:** Intra-procedure scratch (caller-saved). - **r13 (sp):** **Stack Pointer** — top of the stack. - **r14 (lr):** **Link Register** — return address set by `BL`. - **r15 (pc):** **Program Counter** — current instruction. **Function Prolog:** ``` PUSH {r11, lr} ; Save old frame pointer and return address MOV r11, sp ; Establish new frame pointer PUSH {r4-r10} ; Save callee-saved registers (if used) SUB sp, sp, #N ; Reserve N bytes for locals (8-byte aligned) ``` **Function Epilog:** ``` MOV sp, r11 ; Deallocate locals (restore sp) POP {r11, pc} ; Restore fp and return to caller ``` **Diagram (refer Section 4.3):** Caller pushes args 5+, performs `BL`, callee saves state, executes body, restores state, returns via `POP {r11, pc}`. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** **(a)** Define **stack frame (activation record)**. Draw and explain the structure of a typical ARM stack frame created during a non-leaf function call. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following C function: ```c int compute(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e, int f); ``` Show how each argument is passed according to AAPCS, and write the corresponding ARM assembly prolog. Assume `a, b, c, d, e, f` are 32-bit integers, and the function uses one local variable `temp` and registers r4 and r5. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Stack Frame / Activation Record (7 marks):** A **stack frame** (or *activation record*) is a region of the run-time stack allocated for a single function invocation. It holds the function's local state, including: - The **return address** (saved `lr`). - The **previous frame pointer** (saved `r11`). - **Saved callee-saved registers** (r4–r10) used by the function. - **Local variables** declared in the function. - **Outgoing arguments** (5th and beyond) that the function will pass to other functions it calls. **Structure (refer Section 4.2):** ``` HIGH ADDR ↓ [ Saved r11 of caller ] ← old frame pointer [ Argument 6, 5, ... ] ← extra args (>4) [ Saved lr ] ← return address [ Saved r11 ] ← frame pointer of THIS function [ Saved r4, r5, ... ] ← callee-saved registers [ Local variables ] [ Outgoing args area ] ↓ sp (r13) — points HERE LOW ADDR ``` The frame pointer **r11** provides a stable reference to access saved lr, incoming arguments, and locals regardless of stack pointer changes during the function's execution. **(b) Argument Passing and ARM Prolog (7 marks):** For `compute(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e, int f)`: - `a` → **r0** (1st arg) - `b` → **r1** (2nd arg) - `c` → **r2** (3rd arg) - `d` → **r3** (4th arg) - `e` → **stack [sp, #0]** (5th arg, pushed by caller) - `f` → **stack [sp, #4]** (6th arg, pushed by caller) **Return value:** placed in **r0** (4-byte int). **ARM Assembly Prolog:** ``` compute: PUSH {r11, lr} ; Save old frame pointer and return address MOV r11, sp ; Set up new frame pointer PUSH {r4, r5} ; Save callee-saved registers used by this function SUB sp, sp, #8 ; Reserve 8 bytes for 'temp' (8-byte aligned) ; --- function body follows --- ``` **Explanation of each line:** - `PUSH {r11, lr}` — Saves the caller's frame pointer and the return address. lr is saved because `compute` is non-leaf (it may call other functions, clobbering lr). - `MOV r11, sp` — Anchors the frame pointer at the top of the new frame. - `PUSH {r4, r5}` — Preserves the callee-saved registers r4 and r5 per AAPCS. - `SUB sp, sp, #8` — Allocates 8 bytes for the local variable `temp` while maintaining 8-byte stack alignment. **Epilog (for completeness):** ``` MOV sp, r11 ; Deallocate locals POP {r4, r5} ; Restore callee-saved registers POP {r11, pc} ; Restore frame pointer and return ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing argument and parameter:** Argument 5 is passed *by the caller* onto the stack *before* `BL`; the callee only reads it. Marks lost if students describe the callee pushing the argument. > 2. **Forgetting r11 is callee-saved:** Many students save only `lr` in the prolog and forget `r11`. This is incorrect — AAPCS requires both to be preserved. > 3. **Misalignment:** Allocating an odd number of bytes (e.g., `SUB sp, sp, #4`) breaks 8-byte alignment and may cause crashes on AAPCS-compliant code. Always pad to 8. > 4. **Mixing up r13 and r11:** r13 (sp) is the *stack pointer* (changes on PUSH/POP); r11 (fp) is the *frame pointer* (stable within a function). Examiners often test this distinction. > 5. **Forgetting that r0 holds the return value:** A function that returns `int` must place the result in `r0` before returning via `POP {r11, pc}`. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: C Run-Time Environment** - **Five memory segments:** Text (RO code), Data (initialized globals), BSS (zero-init globals), Heap (dynamic, grows ↑), Stack (frames, grows ↓). - **Stack grows downward** in ARM; r13 (sp) decreases on PUSH. - **AAPCS** = ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (part of AAELF ABI). - **First 4 arguments** → r0, r1, r2, r3. - **5th+ arguments** → pushed onto stack by the caller (right-to-left). - **Return value** → r0 (≤4 bytes); r0 + r1 (8 bytes). - **Caller-saved (volatile):** r0, r1, r2, r3, r12. - **Callee-saved (non-volatile):** r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, r9, r10, r11. - **r11 (fp)** = Frame Pointer; **r13 (sp)** = Stack Pointer; **r14 (lr)** = Link Register; **r15 (pc)** = Program Counter. - **Standard prolog:** `PUSH {r11, lr}; MOV r11, sp; PUSH {r4-r10}; SUB sp, sp, #N`. - **Standard epilog:** `MOV sp, r11; POP {r11, pc}` (pops lr into pc for return). - **Stack alignment:** sp must be **8-byte aligned** at the entry of any public function. - **Leaf functions** need not push lr, but **non-leaf** functions must. - **PC offset:** In ARM state, `pc reads as current + 8`; in Thumb state, `pc reads as current + 4`. - **Maximum stack depth** for recursive calls = `frame_size × (recursion_depth + 1)`. - **Heap uses** `malloc`/`free`; **Stack** is automatic (compiler-managed). - **AAPCS is a contract** — violating it produces undefined behavior, especially across separately compiled object files. - **Frame pointer is optional** in optimized ARM code (with `-fomit-frame-pointer`), but enabled by default in debug builds. - **Common exam trick:** *"What does r0 contain after a function returns?"* — Answer: The **return value**, by AAPCS convention. - **BL instruction** sets `lr = return address` and branches to label; **`POP {r11, pc}`** is the canonical return idiom.

topics.title: System Timer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT524 - ARM ARCHITECTURE AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System Timer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **System Timer** in ARM-based microcontrollers (such as the LPC2148, which uses the ARM7TDMI-S core) is a **32-bit programmable timer/counter peripheral** that is used for generating precise time delays, measuring time intervals, counting external events, and producing periodic interrupts. It operates by incrementing (or decrementing) a **Timer Counter (TC)** register on every peripheral clock cycle (or a divided version of it) until it reaches a preset value stored in a **Match Register (MR)**. When a match occurs, the timer can either reset, stop, or generate an interrupt, depending on the configured mode of operation. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact):** A System Timer is a hardware peripheral that provides time-based event generation, pulse-width modulation (PWM), capture, and counting functions by comparing a running counter against one or more match registers. > [!IMPORTANT] > In the LPC2148 microcontroller, there are **two 32-bit timers (Timer0 and Timer1)** that share the same architecture. The prescaler and counter increment on **PCLK (Peripheral Clock)**, which is derived from the CPU clock (CCLK) through the **VPB (VLSI Peripheral Bus) Divider**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **kitchen hourglass** that you flip over to measure how long cookies have been in the oven. The sand grains fall one by one at a constant rate. You preset a marker — say, when the sand reaches the halfway mark, ring the bell. That bell ring is like a **match event** in the timer. In the LPC2148 System Timer: - The **falling sand** = the **PCLK pulses** (each pulse increments the counter). - The **halfway mark** = the **Match Register (MR)** value. - The **bell ring** = the **Timer Interrupt (IRQ)** being raised. - You can also **flip the hourglass** (reset TC) automatically on match. This is exactly how a hardware timer gives you a precise, hardware-driven "stopwatch" without burdening the CPU with software loops. > [!TIP] > **Why hardware timers matter:** A software delay loop (`for(i=0; i<1000; i++)`) is **non-deterministic** because the compiler and CPU state can vary. A hardware System Timer gives you **cycle-accurate** timing — critical for protocols like UART, PWM motor control, and real-time scheduling. --- ## 1.3 Key Architectural Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Timer Resolution | **32-bit** | Max count: $2^{32} = 4,294,967,296$ | | Number of Match Registers | **4 per timer** (MR0–MR3) | Can be cascaded for long delays | | Capture Registers | **4 per timer** (CR0–CR3) | Used to latch TC on external edge | | Prescaler Width | **32-bit** | Divides PCLK by 1 to $2^{32}$ | | Counter Clock Source | **PCLK** | Typically 15 MHz–60 MHz on LPC2148 | | Interrupt Sources | **MR0, MR1, MR2, MR3, CR0** | 5 interrupts per timer | > [!NOTE] > In the **ARM Cortex-M3** (used in LPC1768), the "System Timer" specifically refers to the **SysTick Timer** — a 24-bit down-counter dedicated to the OS or general timing. Both interpretations are valid in KTU 2024 context. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the System Timer The LPC2148 System Timer consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Prescaler Counter (PC)** — A 32-bit counter that divides the incoming PCLK. 2. **Prescale Register (PR)** — Stores the divide value. PC increments until it equals PR, then TC increments and PC resets to 0. 3. **Timer Counter (TC)** — The main 32-bit counter. Increments on every **PR match**. 4. **Match Registers (MR0–MR3)** — 32-bit comparators. When TC equals MRx, an event occurs. 5. **Match Control Register (MCR)** — Configures the action on match (interrupt, reset, stop). 6. **Capture Registers (CR0–CR3)** — Used to capture TC value on a CAP input edge. 7. **Capture Control Register (CCR)** — Configures capture edge and interrupt. 8. **Timer Control Register (TCR)** — Enables/disables the counter and resets it. 9. **Interrupt Register (IR)** — Interrupt-pending flags, one bit per source. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operation Logic **Step 1: Clocking the Timer** - The PCLK (Peripheral Clock) enters the timer block. - The **Prescale Register (PR)** holds the value $N$. - The **Prescaler Counter (PC)** counts PCLK pulses from $0$ to $N$, then resets to $0$. - This means TC increments once every $(N+1)$ PCLK cycles. **Step 2: Counter Increment** - Every time PC resets (i.e., once every $(N+1)$ PCLK cycles), **TC is incremented by 1**. - TC is a 32-bit free-running up-counter. **Step 3: Match Operation** - On every TC increment, the four comparators continuously check whether $\text{TC} = \text{MR}x$. - If equal, the corresponding action defined in the **Match Control Register (MCR)** is taken: - Generate an **interrupt** (set bit in IR). - **Reset** TC to 0. - **Stop** TC (disable counting). **Step 4: Capture Operation** - A transition on the **CAPx.x pin** can latch the current TC value into CRx. - This is used for measuring the period/frequency of external signals. **Step 5: Reading/Writing** - TC, PR, and MR registers are all 32-bit and accessible via the APB bus. - A write to TC is only effective if previously reset via TCR. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Description | Unit | |---|---|---| | $f_{PCLK}$ | Peripheral Clock Frequency | **Hz** | | $T_{PCLK}$ | $\frac{1}{f_{PCLK}}$ — Period of one PCLK cycle | **seconds** | | $T_{tick}$ | Time taken for one TC increment $= (PR + 1) \cdot T_{PCLK}$ | **seconds** | | $T_{delay}$ | Total delay time $= MR \cdot T_{tick} = MR \cdot (PR + 1) \cdot T_{PCLK}$ | **seconds** | | $f_{tick}$ | Tick frequency $= \frac{f_{PCLK}}{PR + 1}$ | **Hz** | | $MR$ | Required match value for time $T$ $= \frac{T \cdot f_{PCLK}}{PR + 1}$ | dimensionless | | $PR$ | Prescaler value (0 to $2^{32} - 1$) | dimensionless | | $f_{PWM}$ | PWM output frequency $= \frac{f_{PCLK}}{(PR + 1) \cdot MR_{total}$ | **Hz** | | Duty Cycle | $\frac{MR_{active}}{MR_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | | Max Delay | $T_{max} = \frac{2^{32} \cdot (PR+1)}{f_{PCLK}}$ | **seconds** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL RULE — Avoid Pipe Symbols:** In the formulas above, the absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$, NEVER as $|x|$, because the pipe character breaks KTU markdown tables. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility System Timers are the **backbone of embedded real-time systems**. Applications include: - **PWM Motor Control** — Generating precise duty cycles to drive DC motors, servos, and LEDs. - **UART Baud Rate Generation** — Providing accurate bit-period timing for serial communication. - **Periodic Sampling** — Triggering ADC conversions at fixed intervals (e.g., 1 kHz audio sampling). - **Debouncing Switches** — Generating delays to filter out mechanical bounce. - **RTOS Tick Generation** — The SysTick timer in Cortex-M3 provides the OS heartbeat (typically 1 ms). - **Input Capture** — Measuring the period of an external square wave (e.g., ultrasonic sensor echo). > [!TIP] > In a robotics project, a 50 Hz PWM signal (20 ms period) drives servo motors. The System Timer with MR = 20,000 and PR such that the tick is 1 µs gives exactly 20 ms — perfect for servo control. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time Delay Calculation ### Problem Statement The LPC2148 operates at a PCLK of **15 MHz**. You want to generate a delay of exactly **1 second** using Timer0 with MR0 configured for reset on match. Find the value to load into MR0 if PR = 14. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the given parameters.** $$ f_{PCLK} = 15 \text{ MHz} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad PR = 14, \quad T_{delay} = 1 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2: Compute the period of one PCLK cycle.** $$ T_{PCLK} = \frac{1}{f_{PCLK}} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^6} = 6.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} $$ **Step 3: Compute the time taken for one TC increment (one tick).** The prescaler divides PCLK by $(PR + 1)$, so: $$ T_{tick} = (PR + 1) \cdot T_{PCLK} = (14 + 1) \cdot 6.667 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ T_{tick} = 15 \cdot 6.667 \times 10^{-8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ µs} $$ **Interpretation:** With PR = 14, each tick of TC equals exactly 1 microsecond. This is the *why* behind the choice — it simplifies the calculation. **Step 4: Compute MR0 for 1-second delay.** Since the timer resets on match, TC will count from 0 to MR0 and then repeat. The total time for one complete cycle is: $$ T_{delay} = MR0 \cdot T_{tick} $$ Rearranging for MR0: $$ MR0 = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 1,000,000 $$ **Step 5: Verify the result.** $$ T_{delay} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 1 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark $$ > **Final Answer:** Load **MR0 = 1,000,000** (decimal) = **0x000F4240** in hex. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: PWM Frequency and Duty Cycle ### Problem Statement Using Timer1 in PWM mode on LPC2148, with PCLK = 60 MHz, PR = 0, and MR3 = 7499 (used as the PWM period reset value). MR0 is set to 3749. Compute the PWM frequency and duty cycle. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Compute the time for one tick.** $$ T_{tick} = (PR + 1) \cdot T_{PCLK} = (0 + 1) \cdot \frac{1}{60 \times 10^6} $$ $$ T_{tick} = 1.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 2: Compute the PWM period (using MR3 as the period register).** In LPC2148 PWM mode, MR3 is the dedicated period register. TC resets on MR3 match. $$ T_{PWM} = MR3 \cdot T_{tick} = 7499 \cdot 1.667 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ T_{PWM} = 1.2499 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 125 \text{ µs} $$ **Step 3: Compute the PWM frequency.** $$ f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{1.2499 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 8001.6 \text{ Hz} \approx 8 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 4: Compute the duty cycle.** MR0 controls the PWM1 high time. The PWM output is HIGH from TC = 0 to TC = MR0, then LOW. $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{MR0}{MR3} \times 100 = \frac{3749}{7499} \times 100 \approx 50\% $$ > **Final Answer:** $f_{PWM} \approx 8$ kHz, Duty Cycle $\approx 50\%$. (This is a textbook 8 kHz, 50% PWM signal — useful for audio or motor control.) --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Match Register Actions (MCR) | MCR Bit | Action When TC = MRx | Use Case | |---|---|---| | Bit 0 / 3 / 6 / 9 | **Interrupt on MRx** | Periodic events, RTOS ticks | | Bit 1 / 4 / 7 / 10 | **Reset TC on MRx** | Repetitive time base | | Bit 2 / 5 / 8 / 11 | **Stop TC on MRx** | One-shot delay | | TCR Bit | Function | |---|---| | Bit 0 | **Counter Enable (CE)** — 1 = running, 0 = paused | | Bit 1 | **Counter Reset (CR)** — 1 = reset TC and PC, auto-clears | > **Analytical Strategy:** For an **interrupt every 10 ms**, set MCR = 0x03 (interrupt + reset on MR0), and use the formula $MR0 = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{T_{tick}}$. --- ## 3.4 Inverse Problem: Finding PR for a Given MR Sometimes MR must stay within 32 bits and a specific range. Given $f_{PCLK} = 60$ MHz, desired $T = 100$ µs, and the constraint that MR should be exactly 6000, find PR. **Solution approach:** $$ T_{tick} = \frac{T}{MR} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{6000} = 1.6667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} $$ $$ PR + 1 = \frac{T_{PCLK}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-8}}{1.6667 \times 10^{-8}} = 1 $$ $$ \therefore PR = 0 $$ This confirms the design — at PCLK = 60 MHz, a 100 µs delay with MR = 6000 needs **PR = 0** (no prescaling). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LPC2148 System Timer Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PCLK Input
Peripheral Clock] --> B[Prescaler Counter PC
32-bit Up Counter] B --> C{PC equals PR?} C -- No --> B C -- Yes --> D[Increment Timer Counter TC
32-bit Free Running] D --> E[Comparator Bank
MR0 MR1 MR2 MR3] E --> F{Match Detected} F -- Yes --> G[Match Control Logic
MCR Settings] G --> H[Generate Interrupt
Set IR Bit] G --> I[Reset TC to 0] G --> J[Stop TC Counting] D --> K[Capture Control CCR] K --> L[Capture Registers CR0 CR1 CR2 CR3
Latch TC on CAP pin edge] H --> M[NVIC Interrupt Request
to ARM Core] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Timer Programming Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Power On Timer
PCONP bit] --> S2[Step 2: Set PCLK
VPBDIV] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Configure Pins
PINSEL0 PINSEL1] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Load PR value] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Load MR0 MR1 values] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Configure MCR
interrupt reset stop] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Enable Timer
TCR bit0 = 1] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Wait for IR flag
or poll TC] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Clear IR flag
handle event] S9 --> S8 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — PWM Generation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB TC[Timer Counter TC] --> CMP{MR3 Match
Period End} CMP -- Yes --> RST[Reset TC to 0] RST --> TC TC --> CMP1{MR0 Match
Duty Cycle End} CMP1 -- Yes --> LOW[PWM1 Output LOW] CMP1 -- No --> HIGH[PWM1 Output HIGH] TC --> CMP2{MR1 Match} CMP2 -- Yes --> LOW2[PWM2 Output LOW] TC --> CMP3{MR2 Match} CMP3 -- Yes --> LOW3[PWM3 Output LOW] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** In PWM mode on LPC2148, **MR3 is reserved as the period reset register** for single-edge PWM. MR0, MR1, and MR2 control the duty cycles of PWM1, PWM2, and PWM3 outputs respectively. ## 4.4 Mermaid — SysTick Timer (Cortex-M3 variant) ```mermaid flowchart TB CLK[Processor Clock or
External Reference] --> CTRL[SysTick Control and
Status Register SYST_CSR] CTRL --> CVR[SysTick Current Value
Register SYST_CVR
24-bit Down Counter] CVR --> ZERO{Counter Reaches 0} ZERO -- Yes --> FLAG[Set COUNTFLAG Bit] ZERO -- Yes --> RELOAD[Reload from SYST_RVR] RELOAD --> CVR FLAG --> INT[Generate SysTick Exception
Exception Number 15] ``` > **KTU Note:** If the exam asks about the **ARM Cortex-M3 SysTick**, mention that it is a **24-bit down counter** with three registers: **SYST_CSR, SYST_RVR, SYST_CVR**. Its primary purpose is to provide a **fixed-time interrupt** to the OS or bare-metal scheduler. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Explain the function of the Match Register (MR) and Match Control Register (MCR) in the LPC2148 System Timer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Match Register (MR0–MR3)** are 32-bit registers that hold comparison values. The Timer Counter (TC) is continuously compared with these MR values. When TC equals MRx, a match event occurs. The **Match Control Register (MCR)** is a 12-bit register that controls the action taken on each match. For each MRx, three bits in MCR define whether to: 1. **Generate an interrupt** (IR bit set), 2. **Reset TC** to zero (for periodic operation), 3. **Stop TC** (for one-shot operation). By configuring MCR appropriately, a programmer can generate periodic interrupts, one-shot delays, or a combination of both using the same timer. > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for MR definition, 1 mark for MCR definition, 1 mark for the three MCR actions. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the role of the Prescale Register (PR) in the System Timer? How does it affect timing accuracy?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Prescale Register (PR)** determines the rate at which the Timer Counter (TC) increments. The PCLK is fed into a Prescaler Counter (PC) that counts from 0 to PR. When PC reaches PR, TC is incremented by 1 and PC resets. Thus, **TC increments once every (PR + 1) PCLK cycles**. The PR allows the timer to handle both high-frequency (small PR) and long-duration (large PR) timing accurately. By choosing PR such that $T_{tick}$ is a convenient value (e.g., 1 µs), the designer simplifies delay calculations and avoids fractional match values. Higher PR values improve timing resolution for long delays but reduce the maximum possible count rate. > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for PR role, 1 mark for (PR+1) explanation, 1 mark for timing-accuracy discussion. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Internal Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the LPC2148 System Timer. Describe the functions of TCR, PR, PC, TC, MR0–MR3, and MCR. **(7 marks)** **b)** The LPC2148 is operating at PCLK = 30 MHz. Using Timer0, design a timer to generate an interrupt every 500 µs. Calculate the required values of PR and MR0. Show the initialization sequence. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The System Timer consists of: - **Clock input (PCLK)** feeding a Prescaler Counter (PC). - **Prescale Register (PR)** — 32-bit, sets the divide value. - **Timer Counter (TC)** — 32-bit up-counter, increments every (PR+1) PCLK cycles. - **Match Registers (MR0–MR3)** — 32-bit comparators. - **Match Control Register (MCR)** — 12-bit, defines match action. - **Timer Control Register (TCR)** — 2-bit (Counter Enable, Counter Reset). - **Interrupt Register (IR)** — interrupt-pending flags. - **Capture Registers (CR0–CR3)** and **Capture Control Register (CCR)** — for input capture. **Functions (4 marks):** | Register | Bits | Function | |---|---|---| | **TCR** | 2 | Bit 0 = Counter Enable (CE); Bit 1 = Counter Reset (CR) — auto-clears after reset | | **PR** | 32 | Holds the prescale value; PC compares against this | | **PC** | 32 | Internal prescaler counter; increments on PCLK, resets on PR match | | **TC** | 32 | Main timer counter; visible to user; increments on PC reset | | **MR0–MR3** | 32 × 4 | Hold comparison values for match events | | **MCR** | 12 | 3 bits per MR — Interrupt (I), Reset (R), Stop (S) on match | **Operating Sequence:** 1. Load PR with divide value. 2. Load MR0 with desired count. 3. Configure MCR (e.g., set bit 0 for interrupt, bit 1 for reset on MR0). 4. Set TCR = 1 to start counting. 5. On TC = MR0, MCR actions are triggered. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) **Step 1: Given Data.** $$ f_{PCLK} = 30 \text{ MHz} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad T_{delay} = 500 \text{ µs} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} $$ **Step 2: Choose PR to get a convenient tick.** For 1 µs tick: $$ T_{tick} = 1 \text{ µs} \Rightarrow (PR + 1) \cdot \frac{1}{30 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ PR + 1 = 30 \Rightarrow PR = 29 $$ **Step 3: Calculate MR0.** $$ MR0 = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 $$ **Step 4: Initialize Sequence (C-style pseudocode, no Python).** ``` T0PR = 29; // Prescale register T0MR0 = 500; // Match value for 500 µs T0MCR = 0x0003; // Interrupt + Reset on MR0 T0TCR = 0x02; // Reset TC and PC T0TCR = 0x01; // Enable Timer0 ``` **Step 5: Verification.** $$ T_{delay} = 500 \times (29+1) \times \frac{1}{30 \times 10^6} = 500 \times \frac{30}{30 \times 10^6} = 500 \text{ µs} \quad \checkmark $$ > **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for PR calculation, 2 marks for MR0 calculation, 2 marks for MCR configuration, 1 mark for verification and initialization. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **a)** Explain the PWM mode of the LPC2148 System Timer. How is the frequency and duty cycle determined? **(7 marks)** **b)** An LPC2148 has PCLK = 60 MHz. Design a PWM signal on PWM1 (controlled by MR0) with frequency 10 kHz and duty cycle 25%. Compute PR, MR3 (period), and MR0. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) **PWM Mode Explanation (4 marks):** In PWM mode, the Timer Counter (TC) resets when it matches **MR3**, which serves as the dedicated period register. **MR0, MR1, and MR2** control the duty cycles of **PWM1, PWM2, and PWM3** outputs respectively. - When TC < MRx, the PWMx output is **HIGH**. - When TC ≥ MRx, the PWMx output is **LOW**. - When TC = MR3, TC resets, and the cycle repeats. This produces a single-edge PWM signal on each PWM output pin. **Frequency and Duty Cycle Formulas (3 marks):** $$ f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{(PR + 1) \cdot MR3} $$ $$ \text{Duty Cycle} (\%) = \frac{MR_x}{MR3} \times 100 $$ The frequency is determined by **PR** and **MR3**, while the duty cycle is determined by the ratio of the active MRx to MR3. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) **Step 1: Choose PR.** For a clean 10 kHz PWM, choose PR = 0 (no prescaling) for maximum resolution. **Step 2: Calculate MR3 (period).** $$ f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{(PR + 1) \cdot MR3} $$ $$ 10{,}000 = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{1 \cdot MR3} $$ $$ MR3 = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{10{,}000} = 6000 $$ **Step 3: Calculate MR0 (duty cycle).** For 25% duty cycle on PWM1: $$ MR0 = 0.25 \times MR3 = 0.25 \times 6000 = 1500 $$ **Step 4: Verification.** $$ f_{PWM} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{1 \cdot 6000} = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark $$ $$ \text{Duty} = \frac{1500}{6000} \times 100 = 25\% \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 5: Register Initialization.** ``` T1PR = 0; // No prescaling T1MR0 = 1500; // PWM1 duty cycle 25% T1MR3 = 6000; // PWM period T1MCR = 0x0400; // Reset TC on MR3 match (bit 10) T1PWMC = 0x07; // Enable PWM1 PWM2 PWM3 outputs T1TCR = 0x02; // Reset TC T1TCR = 0x01; // Enable Timer1 ``` > **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks MR3 formula derivation, 2 marks MR0 calculation, 2 marks PWM register setup, 1 mark for verification. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in PR formula** — The divisor is $(PR + 1)$, NOT $PR$. Many students write $(PR \cdot T_{PCLK})$ and get the wrong answer. > 2. **Confusing MR3 as a regular match register** — In PWM mode, MR3 is RESERVED for period reset. Using it for other purposes breaks PWM. > 3. **Not clearing TCR reset bit** — Writing TCR = 0x03 (instead of separating reset and enable) may cause race conditions in some implementations. > 4. **Misnaming Timer0 vs Timer1** — Question may specify Timer0 or Timer1 explicitly. Using wrong registers = full marks lost. > 5. **Wrong prescaler clock selection** — Timer clock is **PCLK**, not CCLK. Some students forget VPB divider. > 6. **Overflow confusion** — With a 32-bit counter, max delay = $\frac{2^{32} \cdot (PR+1)}{f_{PCLK}}$. Exceeding this requires cascading MR registers or using the RTC. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — System Timer (Module 4)** - **LPC2148 has 2 timers (T0 and T1)**, each 32-bit, with **4 Match Registers (MR0–MR3)** and **4 Capture Registers (CR0–CR3)**. - **Clock source is PCLK**, not CCLK. PCLK is derived via the VPB divider. - **Prescale Register (PR)** divides PCLK by $(PR + 1)$; the **Timer Counter (TC)** increments every $(PR+1)$ PCLK cycles. - **Match Register (MR)** holds the comparison value; **Match Control Register (MCR)** defines the action (interrupt, reset, stop). - **Timer Control Register (TCR):** Bit 0 = Counter Enable, Bit 1 = Counter Reset (auto-clears). - **Interrupt Register (IR):** Write 1 to clear a flag (write-to-clear semantics). - **Core delay formula:** $T_{delay} = MR \cdot (PR + 1) \cdot \frac{1}{f_{PCLK}}$. - **PWM Mode:** MR3 = period, MR0/MR1/MR2 = duty cycles for PWM1/PWM2/PWM3. Enable PWM via **PWMCON** register. - **Capture Mode:** Latches TC value into CRx on a programmable edge of CAP pin. - **Cortex-M3 SysTick (if asked):** 24-bit down counter, three registers (CSR, RVR, CVR), generates Exception #15. - **Maximum single-cycle delay:** $T_{max} = \frac{2^{32} \cdot (PR+1)}{f_{PCLK}}$. - **Always remember the +1 in PR formula** — this is the #1 source of marks lost in KTU exams. - **Tick time vs Delay time:** $T_{tick} = (PR+1)/f_{PCLK}$ is the time for ONE TC increment; multiply by MR for total delay. - **Reset on match (MCR bit 1/4/7/10) is essential for periodic interrupts.** - **PWM frequency formula:** $f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{(PR+1) \cdot MR3}$. - **Duty cycle formula:** $\text{Duty \%} = \frac{MR_x}{MR3} \times 100$. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "**PPRTCM**" — **P**CLK → **P**R → **P**C → **T**C → **M**R → **C**omparator. The signal flows from clock to match detection in this exact order.
